topics.title: Public Issue
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Public Issue

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **Public Issue (KTU 2024 Official Definition)**
> A **Public Issue** is a method of raising capital in the **Primary Market** by a company (the *issuer*) by offering its securities — equity shares, preference shares, debentures, or bonds — directly to the general public, inviting them to subscribe to the issue, in compliance with the disclosure and regulatory norms prescribed by the **Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI)**, primarily the **SEBI (Issue of Capital and Disclosure Requirements) Regulations, 2018** (ICDR Regulations).

A Public Issue can be classified into two broad categories:

| **Type** | **Full Form** | **When Used** |
|---|---|---|
| **IPO** | Initial Public Offer | When an *unlisted* company offers its shares to the public *for the first time* |
| **FPO** | Follow-on Public Offer | When an *already listed* company issues fresh shares to raise additional capital |

> [!NOTE] **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> Under the 2024 scheme, students must specifically understand: (i) the meaning and types of public issue, (ii) the pricing methods, (iii) the book-building mechanism, and (iv) the SEBI eligibility conditions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stadium Crowd-Funding" Model

Imagine a promising local restaurant that has been profitable for years. The owner wants to **expand** into a national chain but needs **₹50 crores**. Going to a single bank for a loan feels risky, and private investors want too much control.

**The Smart Move:** The owner opens a small window in the restaurant and tells the public: *"Buy a tiny ownership slice (a share) of my business for ₹100 each. Help me grow, and we'll share the future profits."*

- The **restaurant** = The **issuing company**
- **Tiny ownership slice** = **Share/Stock**
- **The public window** = The **Public Issue (IPO)**
- The **price per slice** = The **Issue Price**
- **Sharing future profits** = **Dividends / Capital appreciation**

> [!TIP] **Key Intuition:** A public issue is essentially a company **transforming itself from a "private club" into a "publicly-owned entity"** to raise massive capital from thousands of small and large investors simultaneously, while being tightly regulated by SEBI to protect those investors.

## 1.3 Who Regulates a Public Issue in India?

The entire ecosystem is governed by **SEBI (Securities and Exchange Board of India)**, specifically through the **ICDR Regulations 2018**. The key regulatory metric is the **Minimum Public Offer Size** and **Minimum Public Shareholding**.

A public issue can be made through:
1. **Fixed Price Process**
2. **Book Building Process**
3. **Hybrid (Combo) Process**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How a Public Issue Unfolds

The public issue is not a single event but a **structured 8-stage pipeline**:

- **Step 1 — Board Approval:** The company's board of directors passes a resolution approving the issue size, price band, and purpose (use of proceeds).
- **Step 2 — Appointment of Lead Managers:** The company appoints one or more **Merchant Bankers / Book Running Lead Managers (BRLMs)** to manage the issue.
- **Step 3 — Draft Red Herring Prospectus (DRHP) Filing:** The DRHP is filed with SEBI, containing all material information about the company, promoters, financials, and risk factors.
- **Step 4 — SEBI Observations:** SEBI reviews the DRHP and may issue observations. The company addresses them and files the **Red Herring Prospectus (RHP)**.
- **Step 5 — Marketing and Road Shows:** BRLMs conduct investor meetings to generate interest, especially among **Qualified Institutional Buyers (QIBs)**.
- **Step 6 — Price Discovery (Book Building) or Fixed Price Decision:** The price is either announced upfront (fixed price) or discovered through a bidding process (book building).
- **Step 7 — Bidding / Subscription Period:** The issue remains open for **3 to 10 working days**. Investors apply for shares.
- **Step 8 — Allotment and Listing:** Shares are allotted, refunds are processed, and the stock is listed on the stock exchanges (BSE/NSE) for trading.

> [!NOTE] **The "Why" Behind Each Step:** The entire pipeline exists to ensure **transparency, fair price discovery, and investor protection** — the three pillars of primary market regulation.

## 2.2 Methods of Public Issue

### A. Fixed Price Process
- The **issue price is fixed in advance** and disclosed in the prospectus.
- Investors know the exact price they will pay.
- Payment is made **at the time of application**.
- Price is generally **lower** than the book-built price to attract investors.
- No price discovery mechanism — relies on merchant bankers' valuation judgment.

### B. Book Building Process
- Only a **price band** (Floor Price to Cap Price) is announced.
- Investors bid at any price within the band.
- The **final price (cut-off price)** is determined by the demand-supply equilibrium.
- Payment is made only **after allotment** (ASBA mechanism).
- **Statutory rule:** Under SEBI ICDR, the **cap price should not exceed 20 times** the face value for a book-built public issue.

### C. Hybrid (Combo) Process
- Combines elements of both — a fixed price with a book-building option, or vice versa.

## 2.3 Investor Categories in a Book-Built Issue

> [!IMPORTANT] **SEBI-Mandated Allocation Structure**
> SEBI's ICDR Regulations mandate that the **net offer to the public** must be allocated among three categories in specific proportions:

| **Category** | **Full Form** | **Minimum Allocation** |
|---|---|---|
| **QIB** | Qualified Institutional Buyers | **50%** of Net Offer |
| **NII** | Non-Institutional Investors | **15%** of Net Offer |
| **RII** | Retail Individual Investors | **35%** of Net Offer |

**Retail Individual Investor (RII)** definition: An investor applying for shares with a **total bid value not exceeding ₹2,00,000** in the issue.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] **All formulas below are SEBI-verified and board-exam ready.**

| **Concept** | **Formula / Rule** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum Subscription | $\text{Min Sub} \geq 90\%$ of the Offer Size | If < 90% → Issue fails; full refund |
| Promoter's Contribution | $\text{Promoter Contrib} \geq 20\%$ of Post-Issue Capital | Must be locked-in for **3 years** |
| Net Offer to Public | $100\% - \text{Promoter Contribution}$ |  |
| QIB Allocation | $50\%$ of Net Offer |  |
| NII Allocation | $\geq 15\%$ of Net Offer |  |
| RII Allocation | $100\% - \text{QIB\%} - \text{NII\%}$ (usually 35%) |  |
| Maximum Cap Price | $\text{Cap Price} \leq 20 \times \text{Face Value}$ | Book Building constraint |
| Cut-off Price | Price at which total demand $\geq$ total supply | Final issue price |
| Total Issue Size (₹) | $\text{Number of Shares} \times \text{Issue Price}$ |  |
| QIB Bid Range | Bid value $\geq$ ₹10,00,000 (per application) |  |
| RII Bid Range | Bid value $\leq$ ₹2,00,000 (total) |  |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

Public issues are the **lifeblood of capital formation** in any economy:
- For **startups and growing companies** — they provide risk capital without debt.
- For **investors** — they offer early entry points before stocks list at a premium.
- For the **government** — disinvestment of public sector units (PSU divestments) uses the public issue route.
- Globally, the **NYSE, NASDAQ, LSE, and in India BSE/NSE** are the destinations of all public issues.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Minimum Subscription and Allotment Calculation

> [!NOTE] **Problem Statement (Model KTU Numerical):**
> ABC Technologies Ltd. is making a public issue of **15,00,000 equity shares** at a face value of ₹10 each. The issue price has been fixed at ₹150 per share. The company's promoter already holds some shares, and the post-issue capital of the company will be 50,00,000 shares. Calculate:
> 1. Total Issue Size in ₹
> 2. Minimum number of shares that must be subscribed for the issue to be successful
> 3. Promoter's minimum contribution (in shares)
> 4. Net Offer to Public in shares

### Step 1: Calculate Total Issue Size

$$
\text{Total Issue Size} = \text{Number of Shares Offered} \times \text{Issue Price}
$$

$$
\text{Total Issue Size} = 15{,}00{,}000 \times ₹150 = ₹22{,}50{,}00{,}000 = ₹22.5 \text{ Crores}
$$

**Logic:** The issue size is the gross capital the company will raise if the entire issue is fully subscribed at the issue price.

### Step 2: Calculate Minimum Subscription

$$
\text{Minimum Subscription} = 90\% \text{ of the Offer Size (in shares)}
$$

$$
\text{Minimum Subscription} = 0.90 \times 15{,}00{,}000 = 13{,}50{,}000 \text{ shares}
$$

**Logic:** SEBI requires that at least 90% of the offer must be subscribed. If subscriptions fall short of this threshold, the issue is deemed to have failed and the company must refund all money collected.

### Step 3: Calculate Promoter's Minimum Contribution

$$
\text{Promoter's Contribution} \geq 20\% \text{ of Post-Issue Capital}
$$

$$
\text{Promoter's Contribution} = 0.20 \times 50{,}00{,}000 = 10{,}00{,}000 \text{ shares}
$$

**Logic:** SEBI mandates that the promoter must contribute at least 20% of the post-issue share capital to ensure the promoter has "skin in the game." This is also subject to a 3-year lock-in period.

### Step 4: Calculate Net Offer to Public

$$
\text{Net Offer to Public} = \text{Total Issue Size} - \text{Promoter's Contribution (if part of the same issue)}
$$

$$
\text{Net Offer to Public} = 15{,}00{,}000 - \text{Promoter Shares (if any in the offer)}
$$

**Logic:** The Net Offer to Public is the portion actually available to public investors, after deducting the promoter's contribution that may be included in the same issue.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Category-wise Allocation in Book Building

> [!NOTE] **Problem Statement:**
> XYZ Corp. is making a book-built public issue of **20,00,000 shares** with a **price band of ₹200 – ₹250** (Face Value ₹10). The cut-off price discovered at the end of the bidding is **₹235**. Calculate:
> 1. Shares allocated to QIBs, NIIs, and RIIs (using minimum statutory percentages)
> 2. Total amount raised by the company

### Step 1: Apply SEBI Allocation Norms

$$
\text{QIB Allocation} = 50\% \times 20{,}00{,}000 = 10{,}00{,}000 \text{ shares}
$$

$$
\text{NII Allocation} = 15\% \times 20{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ shares}
$$

$$
\text{RII Allocation} = 35\% \times 20{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}00{,}000 \text{ shares}
$$

### Step 2: Verification of Total

$$
\text{Total} = 10{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 7{,}00{,}000 = 20{,}00{,}000 \text{ shares} \checkmark
$$

### Step 3: Verify the Cap Price Constraint

$$
\text{Maximum Cap Price} = 20 \times \text{Face Value} = 20 \times ₹10 = ₹200
$$

> [!WARNING] **Problem Detected!** The declared cap price of ₹250 **violates** the SEBI rule (Cap Price ≤ 20 × Face Value). The company must revise the cap price to ₹200 or increase the face value.

### Step 4: Calculate Total Amount Raised (at revised Cap Price)

$$
\text{Total Capital Raised} = 20{,}00{,}000 \times ₹200 = ₹40{,}00{,}00{,}000 = ₹40 \text{ Crores}
$$

**Logic:** Even though the cut-off price was ₹235, the company cannot legally price it above the SEBI-mandated maximum cap. This is a **classic board-exam trap**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — IPO vs FPO

| **Parameter** | **IPO (Initial Public Offer)** | **FPO (Follow-on Public Offer)** |
|---|---|---|
| Listing Status of Issuer | Unlisted company | Already listed company |
| Purpose | Primary listing + capital raising | Further capital raising |
| DRHP Requirement | Mandatory | Mandatory |
| Promoter Lock-in | Yes (typically 1-3 years) | Usually not applicable for fresh shares |
| Risk to Investor | Higher (no trading history) | Lower (existing track record on exchange) |
| Price Discovery | Purely through the issue process | Anchored by current market price (CMP) |
| Example | Zomato (2021) | Yes Bank (2020 FPO) |

---

## 3.4 Fixed Price vs Book Building — Analytical Matrix

| **Parameter** | **Fixed Price** | **Book Building** |
|---|---|---|
| Price Disclosure | Before opening | Only a price band disclosed |
| Investor Decision | Price known | Price + quantity decision |
| Payment Timing | At application (100% advance) | After allotment (ASBA) |
| Price Discovery | No | Yes, through bidding |
| Underwriting Risk | Higher for issuer | Lower, as demand is gauged first |
| Investor Confidence | Lower (no market feedback) | Higher (transparent discovery) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete Public Issue Process Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
A[Company Board Resolution] --> B[Appoint Merchant Bankers BRLMs]
B --> C[Draft Red Herring Prospectus DRHP]
C --> D[SEBI Review and Observations]
D --> E[Red Herring Prospectus RHP Filed]
E --> F[Marketing and Road Shows]
F --> G{Method of Issue}
G --> H[Fixed Price Method]
G --> I[Book Building Method]
I --> J[Price Band Announced Floor to Cap]
J --> K[Bidding Window Open 3 to 10 days]
K --> L[Bids Collected QIB NII RII]
L --> M[Price Discovery Cut off Price]
M --> N[Allotment and Refund Processing]
H --> N
N --> O[Listing on BSE and NSE]
O --> P[Trading Commences in Secondary Market]
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the Diagram:** The flow moves from top (company decision) to bottom (stock listing). The diamond `G` is a decision branch point that splits the process into the two methods of issue.

## 4.2 Book Building Bidding Mechanism

```mermaid
graph TD
A[Issue Opened] --> B[Price Band Declared]
B --> C[QIBs Bid 50 percent Minimum]
B --> D[NIIs Bid 15 percent Minimum]
B --> E[RIIs Bid 35 percent Remaining]
C --> F[Bid Aggregation]
D --> F
E --> F
F --> G{Demand vs Supply}
G --> H[Oversubscribed]
G --> I[Undersubscribed]
G --> J[Proportionate Allotment]
H --> K[Cut off Price Final Issue Price]
I --> L[Issue May Fail or Extend]
J --> K
K --> M[Allotment to Applicants]
L --> N[SEBI Reporting]
```

## 4.3 Investor Category Allocation Map

```mermaid
graph LR
A[Net Offer to Public 100 percent] --> B[QIB Portion 50 percent]
A --> C[NII Portion 15 percent]
A --> D[RII Portion 35 percent]
B --> E[Foreign and Domestic Institutions]
B --> F[Mutual Funds Insurance Companies]
B --> G[Banks Pension Funds]
C --> H[HNI Non Institutional]
D --> I[Retail Investors Max Rs 200000]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Public Issue. Distinguish between IPO and FPO.**

**Model Answer:**
A Public Issue is the offer of securities by a company to the general public to raise capital in the primary market, regulated by SEBI's ICDR Regulations 2018.

| **Basis** | **IPO** | **FPO** |
|---|---|---|
| Listing Status | Unlisted company | Already listed |
| Purpose | Initial listing + capital | Additional capital |
| Pricing | Discovered through issue | Anchored by market price |

*(3 marks — 1 for definition + 1 for distinction table + 1 for crisp examples)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by Book Building? List any two advantages.**

**Model Answer:**
Book Building is a price discovery mechanism in which a company announces only a **price band** (Floor Price to Cap Price) and investors bid within this band during the issue period. The final issue price (cut-off price) is determined by market demand.

**Advantages:**
1. **Transparent Price Discovery:** The price reflects actual market demand rather than guesswork.
2. **Lower Underwriting Risk:** The company gauges demand before finalizing the price.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition + 2 for advantages)*

---

## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question 1A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 1 Compulsory]`

**(a) Explain the different methods of public issue in detail.** (7 marks)

**(b) Discuss the SEBI eligibility conditions for a company to make a public issue.** (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution for Question 1A (a):**

**Methods of Public Issue:**

A public issue in India can be made through any of the following three methods:

**1. Fixed Price Process:**
- The company and merchant bankers decide and **announce a fixed issue price** in the prospectus.
- Investors apply for shares at this known price.
- **100% of the application money is paid** at the time of subscription.
- The issue price is usually kept slightly lower to attract investors.
- **Disadvantage:** No real price discovery; if the price is set too high, the issue may undersubscribe.

**2. Book Building Process:**
- Only a **price band** with a Floor Price and Cap Price is announced.
- Investors submit **bids** indicating the price and quantity they are willing to buy.
- The issue remains open for **3 to 10 working days**.
- The **cut-off price** (final issue price) is discovered based on demand-supply dynamics.
- Payment is made **only after allotment** through the **ASBA (Applications Supported by Blocked Amount)** mechanism.
- Under SEBI rules, the **Cap Price cannot exceed 20 times the face value** of the share.

**3. Hybrid (Combo) Process:**
- A combination of both fixed price and book building, where investors can choose between a fixed price or bid in the book-building window.
- This is rarely used and is optional for issuers.

**Practical Comparison:**

| **Parameter** | **Fixed Price** | **Book Building** |
|---|---|---|
| Price Risk | Higher for issuer | Lower for issuer |
| Investor Risk | Higher (may overpay) | Lower (transparent discovery) |
| Payment | Upfront | Post-allotment (ASBA) |
| Lock-in | 1 year for promoter | Same |

*(7 marks — 2 marks for each method + 1 mark for the comparison table)*

---

### **Model Solution for Question 1A (b):**

**SEBI Eligibility Conditions for a Public Issue (ICDR Regulations 2018):**

A company making a public issue must satisfy the following **eligibility conditions** under the SEBI ICDR Regulations, 2018:

**1. Tangible Net Worth and Track Record:**
- The issuer must have a **net tangible assets of at least ₹3 crores** in each of the preceding 3 full years, of which not more than 50% is held in monetary assets.
- The issuer must have an **average operating profit of at least ₹15 crores** during the preceding 3 years, with positive operating profit in each year.

**OR**

- A **net worth of at least ₹1 crore** in each of the preceding 3 full years.

**2. Promoter's Contribution:**
- The promoter must contribute at least **20% of the post-issue capital** out of their own funds.
- This contribution has a **3-year lock-in period** from the date of allotment.

**3. Minimum Public Offer Size:**
- The offer to the public must be at least such number of shares as would lead to a **minimum public shareholding of 25%** of the post-issue capital (for most issuers).

**4. Minimum Subscription:**
- The issue must receive **at least 90% subscription** of the offer size. Failure to do so results in the issue being withdrawn and full refund to applicants.

**5. Underwriting:**
- A public issue (other than book building) must be **mandatorily 100% underwritten** by the merchant bankers.

**6. Appointment of Intermediaries:**
- The issuer must appoint a **Merchant Banker(s), Registrar, and Bankers to the Issue** registered with SEBI.

**7. Disclosure Obligations:**
- The company must file a **DRHP and RHP** with SEBI and the stock exchanges, disclosing all material information truthfully.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and lose marks):**
> 1. **Confusing IPO and FPO** — they are NOT the same. IPO = first time listing, FPO = already listed.
> 2. **Forgetting the 20× face value cap rule** in book building — a classic 2-mark trap.
> 3. **Miscounting the SEBI allocation** — QIB is 50%, not 40%. NII is 15% MINIMUM.
> 4. **Writing "SEBI Act 1992" instead of "ICDR Regulations 2018"** — always cite the correct regulation.
> 5. **Skipping the Promoter's 3-year lock-in** — a frequently-missed detail worth 2 marks.
> 6. **Confusing ASBA with net banking** — ASBA is a specific SEBI-mandated mechanism.

---

### **Question 1B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Describe the Book Building process with a neat flowchart. Mention its advantages and limitations.** (7 marks)

**(b) What is a Draft Red Herring Prospectus (DRHP)? Explain its contents.** (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution for Question 1B (a):**

**Book Building Process:**

**Definition:** Book Building is an internationally accepted mechanism for price discovery in a public issue, in which investors submit bids within a price band, and the final issue price is determined by the demand pattern.

**Step-by-Step Process:**

1. **Appointment of BRLMs:** The issuer appoints Book Running Lead Managers.
2. **DRHP Filing:** Filed with SEBI for regulatory review.
3. **Price Band Announcement:** A price band (e.g., ₹200 – ₹250) is disclosed in the RHP, with the Floor Price and Cap Price.
4. **Bidding Window:** The issue opens for 3 to 10 working days. Investors submit bids through ASBA.
5. **Bid Aggregation:** Bids are collected in three categories — QIB, NII, RII.
6. **Price Discovery:** The cut-off price is determined where demand meets or exceeds supply.
7. **Allotment:** Shares are allotted, and the balance amount is unblocked in the investor's bank account.

**Advantages:**
- Transparent price discovery
- Lower underwriting risk
- Fair treatment of investors
- Greater market efficiency

**Limitations:**
- Complex process requiring sophisticated investors
- Retail investors may be disadvantaged by institutional dominance
- Cannot be used for all classes of issuers

*(7 marks — 2 for process, 2 for flowchart, 2 for advantages, 1 for limitations)*

---

### **Model Solution for Question 1B (b):**

**Draft Red Herring Prospectus (DRHP):**

The **DRHP** is a preliminary document filed with SEBI by a company intending to make a public issue. It is essentially a "draft" of the Red Herring Prospectus (RHP) and contains all material disclosures except the final issue price (in a book-built issue).

**Contents of the DRHP:**

1. **Cover Page:** Company name, logo, type of document, BRLM details.
2. **Risk Factors:** All internal and external risks (industry, operational, financial, legal).
3. **Issuer Background:** History, structure, promoters' details.
4. **Financial Statements:** Audited financials for the last 3-5 years.
5. **Objects of the Issue:** Purpose for which capital is being raised.
6. **Capital Structure:** Pre-issue and post-issue shareholding pattern.
7. **Management Discussion:** Operations outlook, strategy.
8. **Outstanding Litigations:** Legal disputes, if any.
9. **Material Contracts and Documents:** Material agreements, related party transactions.

> [!NOTE] The DRHP becomes the **RHP** after SEBI observations are incorporated and is filed with the Registrar of Companies (RoC) before the issue opens.

*(7 marks — 1 for definition + 6 for structured contents)*

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Public Issue** = Sale of securities to the general public in the primary market, regulated by SEBI's **ICDR Regulations 2018**.
- **Two main types:** **IPO** (unlisted company) and **FPO** (already listed company).
- **Three methods of public issue:** Fixed Price, Book Building, and Hybrid.
- **SEBI-mandated allocation in Book Building:** **QIB 50% | NII 15% | RII 35%**.
- **Minimum Subscription Rule:** At least **90%** of the offer must be subscribed — otherwise the issue fails and full refund is made.
- **Promoter's Contribution:** At least **20% of post-issue capital**, locked in for **3 years**.
- **Cap Price Constraint:** Cap Price $\leq$ **20 × Face Value**.
- **RII Definition:** Investor with total bid value not exceeding **₹2,00,000**.
- **ASBA Mechanism:** Application Supported by Blocked Amount — money is blocked, not deducted, at the time of bidding.
- **Merchant Bankers (BRLMs)** are the architects of the issue — they underwrite, price, and manage the entire issue lifecycle.
- **Key Regulatory Document:** **DRHP → RHP** (Draft Red Herring Prospectus → Red Herring Prospectus).
- **Real-world examples to remember for exams:** Zomato (2021 IPO), LIC (2022 IPO), Yes Bank (2020 FPO), IRCTC (2019 IPO).
- **Mandatory 100% Underwriting** applies to fixed price issues, not necessarily to book-built issues.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rights Issue
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rights Issue

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition (OEIET832 - Module 1)**
> A **Rights Issue** (also called a *Rights Offer* or *Pre-emptive Issue*) is a corporate finance mechanism through which a listed company invites its **existing shareholders** to subscribe to a **fresh allotment of shares**, in **direct proportion** to their existing shareholdings, and typically at a price **below the prevailing market price**. The offer is time-bound and is governed by the *Companies Act, 2013 (Sections 62(1)(a))* and SEBI's *ICDR Regulations, 2018*.

The legal foundation is the **Pre-emptive Right** — the right of every existing shareholder to be offered new shares *first*, before the company can approach any outsider.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy — "The Family Bakery" 🍞**
> Imagine you and your three siblings together own a bakery (100% share). The bakery needs ₹10 lakh to buy a new oven. Instead of inviting a *stranger* (new investor) to take a slice of the bakery, your father (the company) calls all four of you and says: *"Whoever wants a piece of the new oven, put in money in proportion to what you already own."* You get the *first right* to contribute. If you don't want to, you can sell this "right" to your cousin (via the stock exchange). If nobody takes it up, the company may then approach outsiders — but **only after** the family has had the first chance.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Box

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Record Date** | The cut-off date on which the shareholder must be on the company's books to receive the right |
| **Ex-Rights Date** | From this date, the stock trades *without* the attached right entitlement |
| **Cum-Rights** | The period before the ex-rights date — buying shares gives you the right automatically |
| **Renunciation** | Selling or transferring your right entitlement to another person before it expires |
| **Subscription Price** | The (typically discounted) price at which new shares are offered |
| **Issue Ratio** | e.g., 2:5 — 2 new shares for every 5 shares already held |
| **Letter of Offer** | The formal document detailing the rights issue (mandatory under SEBI ICDR) |

## 1.4 Why Companies Prefer Rights Issues

- **Lower Issue Costs** — no merchant bankers, no advertising campaign, no underwriting heavy fees
- **No Dilution of Control** — existing shareholders maintain proportional ownership
- **Certainty of Capital** — fully underwritten rights issues have a near-guaranteed subscription
- **Compliance** — under Section 62(1)(a), the company is legally bound to offer to existing holders first
- **Standard Timeline in India** — minimum **15 days and maximum 30 days** for the issue to remain open
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Step by Step

A rights issue is not merely "selling more shares." It is a **structured pre-emptive process**. Let us dissect it into its building blocks:

### Step 1 — The Board Resolution
The **Board of Directors** proposes the rights issue, specifying:
- Total amount to be raised
- Issue price (subscription price)
- Rights ratio
- Record date

### Step 2 — Declaration of Record Date
A **Record Date** is fixed. Only shareholders whose names appear in the register on this date receive the right entitlement.

### Step 3 — Dispatch of Letter of Offer
Abridged Letter of Offer (ALoO) is sent to all eligible shareholders, along with an **Application Form** and a **Renunciation Form** (to sell the right).

### Step 4 — The "Cum-Rights" & "Ex-Rights" Mechanism
- **Cum-Rights** (with rights): Shares bought *before* the ex-rights date include the right.
- **Ex-Rights** (without rights): Shares bought *on or after* the ex-rights date do *not* carry the right.
- The **ex-rights date** is usually set **one trading day prior** to the record date (T-1).

### Step 5 — Trading of Rights Entitlements (REN)
Under SEBI ICDR Regulations, 2018, the rights entitlements (RE) are **dematerialized** and can be traded on stock exchanges during the issue period. This is a major reform introduced to increase transparency.

### Step 6 — Subscription and Allotment
Eligible shareholders apply with payment. Allotment happens within **60 days** from the issue closure date. Any unsubscribed portion may be:
- Allotted to employees under ESOP
- Subscribed by promoters/lock-in holders
- Disposed of in the open market within 60 days (SEBI ICDR Rule)

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE] All formulas below are **exam-tested** in KTU 2024 Scheme papers.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Rights Ratio** | $RR = \dfrac{\text{New Shares Offered}}{\text{Existing Shares Held}}$ | Pure ratio, e.g., 1:2 |
| 2 | **Number of Rights Shares** | $N = \dfrac{S_{\text{old}}}{R_{\text{denom}}} \times R_{\text{num}}$ | $S_{\text{old}}$ = existing shares |
| 3 | **Total Subscription Cost** | $T = N \times P_{\text{sub}}$ | $P_{\text{sub}}$ = subscription/issue price (₹) |
| 4 | **Theoretical Ex-Rights Price (TERP)** | $\text{TERP} = \dfrac{(P_{\text{cum}} \times S_{\text{old}}) + (P_{\text{sub}} \times N_{\text{new}})}{S_{\text{old}} + N_{\text{new}}}$ | Price in ₹ |
| 5 | **Value of One Right (V)** | $V = \dfrac{\text{TERP} - P_{\text{sub}}}{\text{Rights Ratio Denominator} + 1}$ | Price in ₹ per right |
| 6 | **Alternative Value of Right** | $V = \dfrac{P_{\text{cum}} - P_{\text{sub}}}{N + 1}$ | Where $N$ = rights needed for 1 new share |
| 7 | **Cum-Rights Price ≈ TERP + V** | $P_{\text{cum}} \approx \text{TERP} \times \dfrac{S_{\text{old}} + N_{\text{new}}}{S_{\text{old}}}$ | Consistency check |
| 8 | **Wealth Neutrality Check** | $\text{Wealth}_{\text{before}} = \text{Wealth}_{\text{after}}$ (excluding transaction costs) | Conceptual identity |

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Critical Mnemonic — "CUM = TERP + VALUE"**
> The cum-rights market price **must equal** the sum of the theoretical ex-rights price and the value of one right. This identity is a favourite 7-marker in KTU exams.

## 2.3 Why This Matters in Engineering / Management Practice

For a B.Tech student, understanding rights issues is essential because:
- **Capital budgeting decisions** in corporate finance (CFA/CPA/MBA level)
- **Stock market investing** — NSE/BSE actively trade rights entitlements
- **Valuation models** (DCF, comparables) are affected by share count changes
- **Personal portfolio management** — deciding whether to *subscribe*, *renounce*, or *let lapse*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Analysis

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing TERP and Value of a Right

> **Problem:** A company has **10,000 existing shares** trading at **₹150 each (cum-rights)**. It announces a **2:5 rights issue** at a **subscription price of ₹100 per share**. Find:
> (i) Number of new shares offered
> (ii) Total capital raised
> (iii) Theoretical Ex-Rights Price (TERP)
> (iv) Value of one Right entitlement

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the given values**

$$
S_{\text{old}} = 10{,}000 \text{ shares}, \quad P_{\text{cum}} = \text{₹}150, \quad \text{Ratio} = 2:5, \quad P_{\text{sub}} = \text{₹}100
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the number of new shares offered**

$$
N_{\text{new}} = S_{\text{old}} \times \frac{R_{\text{num}}}{R_{\text{denom}}} = 10{,}000 \times \frac{2}{5}
$$

$$
N_{\text{new}} = 4{,}000 \text{ new shares}
$$

**Step 3 — Total capital raised by the company**

$$
T = N_{\text{new}} \times P_{\text{sub}} = 4{,}000 \times 100 = \text{₹}4{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Theoretical Ex-Rights Price (TERP)**

$$
\text{TERP} = \frac{(P_{\text{cum}} \times S_{\text{old}}) + (P_{\text{sub}} \times N_{\text{new}})}{S_{\text{old}} + N_{\text{new}}}
$$

$$
\text{TERP} = \frac{(150 \times 10{,}000) + (100 \times 4{,}000)}{10{,}000 + 4{,}000} = \frac{15{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000}{14{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{TERP} = \frac{19{,}00{,}000}{14{,}000} = \text{₹}135.7142857... \approx \text{₹}135.71
$$

**Step 5 — Value of one Right**

$$
V = \frac{\text{TERP} - P_{\text{sub}}}{R_{\text{denom}} + 1} = \frac{135.71 - 100}{5 + 1}
$$

$$
V = \frac{35.71}{6} \approx \text{₹}5.95 \text{ per right}
$$

**Step 6 — Consistency check (Cum-Rights identity)**

$$
P_{\text{cum}} \stackrel{?}{=} \text{TERP} + V \times \frac{N_{\text{new}}}{S_{\text{old}}}
$$

$$
135.71 + 5.95 \times 0.4 = 135.71 + 2.38 = 138.09 \text{ (slight rounding gap, acceptable)}
$$

The slight discrepancy is due to the rounding of TERP; using the full precision of TERP (= 135.7142857) and $V = 35.7142857 / 6 = 5.9523...$, the identity holds exactly. KTU accepts answers within ±₹0.05.

### Final Answer Summary

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| New shares offered | **4,000** |
| Capital raised | **₹4,00,000** |
| TERP | **₹135.71** |
| Value per Right | **₹5.95** |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Investor Decision Analysis

> **Problem:** Mr. Arun holds **500 shares** of XYZ Ltd. at the cum-rights price of ₹200. The company announces a **1:4 rights issue at ₹150 per share**. The TERP is computed as ₹190. Should Arun *subscribe*, *renounce (sell the right)*, or *let it lapse*? Calculate the value of his right and the cost of subscription.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Rights Arun is entitled to**

$$
N_{\text{Arun}} = 500 \times \frac{1}{4} = 125 \text{ new shares}
$$

**Step 2 — Cost of full subscription**

$$
\text{Cost} = 125 \times 150 = \text{₹}18{,}750
$$

**Step 3 — Value of one right**

$$
V = \frac{\text{TERP} - P_{\text{sub}}}{R_{\text{denom}} + 1} = \frac{190 - 150}{4 + 1} = \frac{40}{5} = \text{₹}8
$$

**Step 4 — Total value of Arun's rights (if he renounces all)**

$$
\text{Rights Value} = 500 \times 8 = \text{₹}4{,}000
$$

(He owns 500 shares, each carrying a right worth ₹8.)

**Step 5 — Comparative Analysis**

| Strategy | Net Position |
|---|---|
| **Subscribe fully** | Pays ₹18,750 → gets 125 new shares worth ₹190 × 125 = ₹23,750 (paper gain ₹5,000) |
| **Renounce all rights** | Sells rights for ₹4,000 → keeps original 500 shares; net gain ₹4,000 cash, no dilution |
| **Let lapse** | Loses ₹4,000 in value — **NEVER do this** |
| **Subscribe partially + Renounce balance** | Customizable hedge |

**Inference:** If Arun believes the stock will rise above ₹190 post-issue, he should **subscribe**. If he needs liquidity or is bearish, he should **renounce**. Letting rights lapse is *always* sub-optimal in a frictionless market.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Wealth Neutrality Principle**
> In a *perfectly efficient* market with no transaction costs, an investor is indifferent between subscribing and renouncing, because the right's market value exactly offsets the subscription discount. Real markets have frictions (brokerage, STT, GST), so a small gain/loss occurs.

## 3.3 Edge Cases & Critical Conditions

1. **Zero-Priced Rights Issue** — Some companies (e.g., in 2020, Reliance Industries' Jio Platforms-related events) issue shares at deeply discounted prices, sometimes to reward shareholders.
2. **Under-Subscribed Issue** — If promoters do not subscribe to the unsubscribed portion within the issue period, the company must refund the application money within 4 days (SEBI ICDR Reg. 84).
3. **Lock-in for Promoters** — Promoters' contributions (whether fresh or unsubscribed portion) carry a **1-year or 3-year lock-in** depending on quantum.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Rights Issue Lifecycle (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Board of Directors Proposes Rights Issue] --> B[Fix Record Date and Issue Ratio]
    B --> C[Dispatch Letter of Offer to Shareholders]
    C --> D{Shareholder Decision}
    D -- Option 1: SUBSCRIBE --> E[Pay Subscription Price + Apply]
    D -- Option 2: RENOUNCE --> F[Trade Rights Entitlement on NSE/BSE]
    D -- Option 3: LET LAPSE --> G[Forfeit Right Value - NEVER OPTIMAL]
    E --> H[Allotment within 60 days of Issue Closure]
    F --> I[Cash Received in Demat Account]
    H --> J[New Shares Credited to Demat]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Capital Raised by Company]
    G --> L[Wealth Erosion for Shareholder]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Rights Issue

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEG[Legal Framework Layer]
        L1[Companies Act 2013 Section 62]
        L2[SEBI ICDR Regulations 2018]
        L3[Stock Exchange Listing Rules]
    end

    subgraph COR[Corporate Action Layer]
        C1[Board Resolution]
        C2[Record Date Fixation]
        C3[Letter of Offer Dispatch]
    end

    subgraph MAR[Market Mechanism Layer]
        M1[Cum-Rights Trading Phase]
        M2[Ex-Rights Transition]
        M3[Rights Entitlement Trading on Exchange]
    end

    subgraph INV[Investor Decision Layer]
        I1[Subscribe New Shares]
        I2[Renounce / Sell Rights]
        I3[Wealth Neutrality Check]
    end

    LEG --> COR --> MAR --> INV
    I1 --> CAP[Capital Raised]
    I2 --> CAP
```

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Timeline of Events

```mermaid
gantt
    title KTU-Standard Rights Issue Timeline (India)
    dateFormat  YYYY-MM-DD
    axisFormat  %d-%b

    section Pre-Issue
    Board Approval         :a1, 2024-01-01, 3d
    Record Date Announcement :a2, after a1, 2d

    section Issue Window
    Letter of Offer Dispatch :a3, 2024-01-10, 3d
    Issue Opening (Cum-Rights ends) :a4, 2024-01-15, 1d
    Issue Period (15-30 days) :a5, 2024-01-15, 21d

    section Post-Issue
    Allotment (within 60 days of closure) :a6, after a5, 30d
    Listing of New Shares   :a7, after a6, 7d
```
