
## 4.4 Conceptual Map — Wealth Effect of a Rights Issue

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pre-Issue Portfolio: 100 shares × ₹150 = ₹15,000] --> B[Rights Issue 2:5 at ₹100]
    B --> C{Investor Action}
    C -- Subscribe --> D[Post-Issue: 140 shares × TERP ₹135.71 = ₹18,999.4]
    C -- Renounce --> E[Post-Issue: 100 shares × ₹135.71 = ₹13,571 + ₹4,000 cash = ₹17,571]
    C -- Let Lapse --> F[Post-Issue: 100 shares × ₹135.71 = ₹13,571 - LOSS]
    D --> G[Wealth ≈ ₹19,000 - Cost ₹4,000 = ₹15,000 NEUTRAL]
    E --> G
    F --> H[Wealth LOSS = ₹1,429]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**

### Q1. Define a Rights Issue. Mention any two advantages. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
A **Rights Issue** is a method of raising fresh capital by a listed company in which it offers new shares to its **existing shareholders** in proportion to their existing holdings, generally at a price **below the current market price**, in accordance with **Section 62(1)(a) of the Companies Act, 2013**.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Cost-effective** — avoids heavy underwriting, advertising, and merchant banking expenses associated with a public issue.
2. **No dilution of control** — existing shareholders' proportional ownership is maintained if they all subscribe.

> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award 1 mark for the definition and 1 mark each for two clearly stated advantages. Vague answers fetch only partial credit.

---

### Q2. What is meant by "Theoretical Ex-Rights Price" (TERP)? **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
**TERP** is the *hypothetical* market price that a share is expected to trade at **once the rights issue is completed and the new shares are listed**. It is computed as the **weighted average** of the cum-rights market price and the subscription price, weighted by the number of existing and new shares respectively.

$$
\text{TERP} = \frac{(P_{\text{cum}} \times S_{\text{old}}) + (P_{\text{sub}} \times N_{\text{new}})}{S_{\text{old}} + N_{\text{new}}}
$$

TERP is a critical reference price for:
- Determining the **value of one right**
- **Pricing** the rights entitlement on the stock exchange
- **Wealth-neutrality** checks for investors

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — December 2023 Style]**

### QUESTION A — (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the step-by-step procedure of a Rights Issue under the SEBI ICDR Regulations, 2018. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The procedural framework of a Rights Issue in India, as prescribed by the **SEBI (ICDR) Regulations, 2018** and the **Companies Act, 2013**, comprises the following sequential steps:

1. **Board Resolution & In-Principle Approval**
   The Board of Directors passes a resolution approving the rights issue, including the size, ratio, and price. In-principle approval is obtained from the stock exchange(s) where the shares are listed.

2. **Fixation of Record Date**
   A record date is fixed to determine the list of eligible shareholders. Only those whose names appear in the register on this date receive the right entitlement.

3. **Filing of Draft Letter of Offer (DLoO)**
   The company files a Draft Letter of Offer with SEBI, which must be cleared before dispatch. The Abridged Letter of Offer (ALoO) is then sent to all eligible shareholders.

4. **Cum-Rights and Ex-Rights Trading**
   Shares trade *cum-rights* until the ex-rights date. From the ex-rights date onwards, shares trade *ex-rights* (without the attached right).

5. **Issue Opening and Subscription**
   The issue remains open for a **minimum of 15 days and a maximum of 30 days**. Eligible shareholders can either subscribe or renounce their rights.

6. **Trading of Rights Entitlements (RE)**
   Under the 2018 SEBI ICDR framework, rights entitlements are **dematerialized** and can be traded on stock exchanges, providing liquidity and transparency.

7. **Allotment and Listing**
   Allotment must be completed within **60 days** from the issue closure date. New shares are then listed and admitted to trading on the stock exchanges.

8. **Disposal of Unsubscribed Portion**
   Any unsubscribed portion may be subscribed by promoters (with applicable lock-in) or disposed of in the open market within 60 days of issue closure.

#### (b) A company has 8,000 existing shares trading at ₹250 each (cum-rights). It announces a 3:4 rights issue at a subscription price of ₹200. Calculate the **TERP**, the **value of one right**, and the **total capital raised**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $S_{\text{old}} = 8{,}000$ shares
- $P_{\text{cum}} = \text{₹}250$
- Rights ratio = 3:4 (i.e., 3 new shares for every 4 old)
- $P_{\text{sub}} = \text{₹}200$

**Step 1 — Number of new shares**

$$
N_{\text{new}} = 8{,}000 \times \frac{3}{4} = 6{,}000 \text{ new shares}
$$

**Step 2 — Total capital raised**

$$
T = 6{,}000 \times 200 = \text{₹}12{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — TERP**

$$
\text{TERP} = \frac{(250 \times 8{,}000) + (200 \times 6{,}000)}{8{,}000 + 6{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{TERP} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000}{14{,}000} = \frac{32{,}00{,}000}{14{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{TERP} \approx \text{₹}228.57
$$

**Step 4 — Value of one right**

$$
V = \frac{\text{TERP} - P_{\text{sub}}}{R_{\text{denom}} + 1} = \frac{228.57 - 200}{4 + 1} = \frac{28.57}{5} \approx \text{₹}5.71
$$

**Final Answer:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| New shares | **6,000** |
| Total capital raised | **₹12,00,000** |
| TERP | **₹228.57** |
| Value per right | **₹5.71** |

---

### QUESTION B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)

#### (a) Discuss the differences between a **Rights Issue** and a **Private Placement**. When would a company prefer one over the other? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Rights Issue | Private Placement |
|---|---|---|
| **Offered To** | Existing shareholders only | A select group (max 200 persons/Qualified Institutional Buyers) per financial year |
| **Legal Basis** | Section 62(1)(a), Companies Act 2013 | Section 42, Companies Act 2013 |
| **Time Taken** | 15–30 days issue window + 60 days for allotment | Faster — typically 15–30 days end-to-end |
| **Cost** | Lower (no major advertising) | Moderate (placement fees to arrangers) |
| **Control Impact** | No dilution (if all subscribe) | May dilute if new investors come in |
| **Disclosure** | Letter of Offer (LoO) required | PAS-4 (Private Placement Offer Letter) |
| **Pricing** | Discount to market price (guided by SEBI) | Often at a small discount/premium negotiated |

**When to prefer:**
- **Rights Issue** — when the company has a stable shareholder base, wants to preserve control, needs to raise moderate capital cost-effectively, and has no urgent time pressure.
- **Private Placement** — when the company needs capital *quickly*, wants to bring in a strategic investor (e.g., a private equity firm), or wants to avoid the procedural complexity of a rights issue.

#### (b) Mr. Ramesh owns 1,000 shares of ABC Ltd. at a cum-rights price of ₹400. ABC Ltd. announces a 1:5 rights issue at ₹320 per share. The TERP is calculated at ₹388. Should Mr. Ramesh subscribe or renounce? Show calculations. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Rights entitled**

$$
N_{\text{Ramesh}} = 1{,}000 \times \frac{1}{5} = 200 \text{ new shares}
$$

**Step 2 — Cost of subscription**

$$
\text{Cost} = 200 \times 320 = \text{₹}64{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Value of one right**

$$
V = \frac{\text{TERP} - P_{\text{sub}}}{R_{\text{denom}} + 1} = \frac{388 - 320}{5 + 1} = \frac{68}{6} \approx \text{₹}11.33
$$

**Step 4 — Total value of Mr. Ramesh's rights (1,000 × V)**

$$
\text{Rights Value} = 1{,}000 \times 11.33 = \text{₹}11{,}333
$$

**Step 5 — Decision Analysis**

| Action | Net Wealth After |
|---|---|
| **Subscribe fully** | 1,200 shares × ₹388 = ₹4,65,600 − ₹64,000 paid = **₹4,01,600** |
| **Renounce all** | 1,000 shares × ₹388 = ₹3,88,000 + ₹11,333 cash = **₹3,99,333** |
| **Difference** | Subscribe wins by **₹2,267** |

**Recommendation:** **Subscribe** — Ramesh is financially better off by approximately ₹2,267 (after ignoring transaction costs). The decision aligns with KTU's *wealth-maximization* principle.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls**
> 1. **Common Mistake (–2 marks):** Using the *cum-rights price* instead of *TERP* in the value-of-right formula. Always use **TERP** in $V = (\text{TERP} - P_{\text{sub}})/(R_{\text{denom}} + 1)$.
> 2. **Common Mistake (–1 mark):** Forgetting to multiply $V$ by the *number of existing shares* when calculating total rights value for an investor. Each existing share carries one right.
> 3. **Common Mistake (–1 mark):** Not stating the assumption of a *frictionless market* when invoking the wealth-neutrality principle.
> 4. **Common Mistake (–2 marks):** Confusing the *rights ratio numerator and denominator* (e.g., writing 5:1 instead of 1:5). Remember — the **denominator refers to old shares, numerator to new shares**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Revision Checklist — Rights Issue**

- **Definition**: Issue of new shares to existing shareholders in proportion to their holding, at a price usually below the market price.
- **Legal Basis**: Companies Act 2013 — Section 62(1)(a); SEBI ICDR Regulations 2018.
- **Issue Window**: 15 to 30 days (mandatory).
- **Allotment Timeline**: Within 60 days of issue closure.
- **Record Date**: Critical cut-off; shareholders on this date alone get the right.
- **Ex-Rights Date**: Usually 1 trading day before record date.
- **Key Formula 1**: $\text{TERP} = \dfrac{(P_{\text{cum}} \times S_{\text{old}}) + (P_{\text{sub}} \times N_{\text{new}})}{S_{\text{old}} + N_{\text{new}}}$
- **Key Formula 2**: $V = \dfrac{\text{TERP} - P_{\text{sub}}}{R_{\text{denom}} + 1}$
- **Wealth Identity**: $\text{Wealth}_{\text{before}} = \text{Wealth}_{\text{after}}$ (in a frictionless market).
- **Three Investor Choices**: Subscribe | Renounce (sell) | Let Lapse (NEVER optimal).
- **Rights Entitlement (RE)**: Demat-tradable on NSE/BSE — a key 2018 reform.
- **Promoter Lock-in**: 1 to 3 years depending on the percentage subscribed.
- **Undersubscribed Portion**: Must be disposed of within 60 days, failing which refund is due.
- **Advantages**: Low cost, no control dilution, certain subscription, fast process.
- **Disadvantages**: Discount to market price is a wealth transfer from new investors; can be undersubscribed if shareholders lack confidence; can depress stock price on announcement.
- **Real-World Examples**: L&T 2020 rights issue (₹3,000+ crore); REC Ltd. 2024 follow-on; Coal India 2024.
- **Examination Mantra**: "CUM = TERP + V (per old share ratio)" — never forget this identity in numericals.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Private Placement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Private Placement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Private Placement** is a method of raising capital from the primary market in which a company issues and allots its securities (equity shares, preference shares, debentures, or other convertible instruments) **directly to a pre-identified, select group of investors**, rather than offering them to the general public through a public issue.

Under the Indian regulatory framework, **Section 42 of the Companies Act, 2013** (read with the relevant Rules prescribed under the Companies (Prospectus and Allotment of Securities) Rules, 2014) is the principal statutory provision that governs private placement of securities by companies.

The term is also sometimes referred to as a **"Private Offering,"** a **"Preferential Issue,"** or a **"Private Investment in Public Equity (PIPE)"** depending on the specific structure adopted.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like this:** Imagine you want to bake a cake and sell it to raise money for a college trip.
> - A **Public Issue** is like setting up a stall in the middle of the city market and shouting "Cake for sale!" — everyone is invited, you need a big banner (prospectus), permissions from the municipality (SEBI/ROC), and a crowd to make it worthwhile.
> - A **Private Placement** is like texting **10–15 of your closest friends** and saying, *"Hey, I'm baking a cake. Want to buy a slice? Pay me directly."* No big banner, no city permissions, just a small, trusted group, and the deal is done quickly.

In corporate finance, the "friends" are **institutional investors, HNIs (High Net-worth Individuals), mutual funds, banks, or insurance companies**, and the "cake" represents **shares or debentures** of the company.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Limits (Indian Context)

| Parameter | Limit / Metric |
|---|---|
| **Maximum number of allottees per financial year** | **200 persons** (excluding Qualified Institutional Buyers and employees under ESOP) |
| **Statutory Provision** | **Section 42, Companies Act, 2013** |
| **Passing of Special Resolution** | **Mandatory** for private placement |
| **Time limit for allotment** | **Within 60 days** from the date of receipt of application money |
| **Minimum subscription** | Not strictly mandated (unlike public issues) |
| **Prospectus requirement** | **Not required**, but a **Private Placement Offer Letter (PPOL)** is mandatory |

### 1.4 KTU 2024 Syllabus Hook

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **OEIET832 – Introduction to Financial Markets** syllabus under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, this topic falls under **Module 1: Primary Market**, and students are expected to understand the **concept, legal framework, procedure, advantages, disadvantages, and differences** between private placement and public issue.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Legal Framework in India

Private placement in India is governed by the following legal architecture:

1. **Companies Act, 2013 — Section 42** (with Rules 14 & 15 of the **Companies (Prospectus and Allotment of Securities) Rules, 2014**).
2. **SEBI (Issue and Listing of Non-Convertible Securities) Regulations, 2021** — applicable when listed entities issue NCDs privately.
3. **SEBI (Issue of Capital and Disclosure Requirements) Regulations, 2018 (ICDR Regulations)** — particularly for **Qualified Institutional Placement (QIP)** and preferential issues by listed companies.
4. **Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA), 1999** — applies when non-resident Indians or foreign entities subscribe to the private placement.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The private placement process can be broken down into the following structured steps:

- **Step 1 — Board Resolution:** The Board of Directors must first pass a resolution recommending the private placement and identifying the proposed allottees.
- **Step 2 — Special Resolution:** Shareholders must approve the private placement by passing a **Special Resolution** (Section 42(2)). For listed companies, the resolution must also comply with SEBI's ICDR Regulations.
- **Step 3 — Identification of Allottees:** The list of proposed allottees must be finalized in advance. No public advertisement or general solicitation is permitted.
- **Step 4 — Private Placement Offer Letter (PPOL):** A **Private Placement Offer Letter** in Form PAS-4 must be sent to the identified persons. It functions like a private prospectus.
- **Step 5 — Application & Subscription:** Identified investors apply through a serially numbered application letter, accompanied by the application money.
- **Step 6 — Allotment:** Securities must be allotted **within 60 days** from the date of receipt of application money. If allotment is not made within this period, the money must be refunded within **15 days** thereafter, failing which interest at **12% p.a.** is payable.
- **Step 7 — Filing with ROC:** Returns of allotment (Form PAS-3) must be filed with the Registrar of Companies within **30 days** of allotment.
- **Step 8 — Listing (Optional):** Privately placed securities are generally **not listed** on stock exchanges. However, if the issuer is already listed, the privately placed securities may or may not be listed depending on the terms.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Private placement involves very few numerical formulas, but the following regulatory limits are **high-yield for KTU exams**:

| Concept | Rule / Formula / Limit | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Max persons (private co.) | $\leq 50$ in a financial year | Rule 14(1) |
| Max persons (other than private co.) | $\leq 200$ in a financial year | Rule 14(2) |
| Allotment period | $\leq 60$ days from receipt of money | Section 42(6) |
| Refund period | $\leq 15$ days from expiry of 60 days | Section 42(6) |
| Interest on delayed refund | $12\% \text{ p.a.}$ simple interest | Section 42(6) |
| Filing of PAS-3 | $\leq 30$ days from allotment | Rule 15 |
| Special Resolution validity | 12 months from date of passing | Rule 14(4) |
| QIP minimum issue size | $\geq \text{Rs. } 200 \text{ crores}$ | SEBI ICDR Reg. |

### 2.4 Why and How: Real-World Utility

Private placement is widely used by:

- **Startups and SMEs** raising growth capital without the high cost of an IPO.
- **Large listed corporates** executing **Qualified Institutional Placements (QIPs)** to raise funds quickly from mutual funds and insurance companies.
- **NBFCs and HFCs** issuing privately placed NCDs to banks and insurers for their Asset-Liability Management (ALM).
- **Real Estate and Infrastructure firms** raising long-term debt from pension funds and insurance funds.

The **'why'** is essentially **speed, confidentiality, and lower compliance cost**. The **'how'** is **targeted solicitation** to a pre-identified investor pool.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Detailed Procedural Walkthrough with Numerical Examples

Since private placement is largely regulatory, the "derivations" here are stepwise logical deductions from the Companies Act, supported by worked numerical examples for refund interest and time limits.

---

### **Worked Example 1: Calculation of Refund Interest under Section 42(6)**

**Problem (KTU-style):**
> A company issued shares through a private placement and received application money on **April 1, 2024**. However, due to procedural delays, the company failed to allot shares within the statutory period and refunded the money on **August 25, 2024**. Calculate the **interest payable** on the application money of **Rs. 50,00,000** as per Section 42(6) of the Companies Act, 2013.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the statutory allotment deadline.**

As per Section 42(6), allotment must be made **within 60 days** from the date of receipt of application money.

$$
\text{Last date for allotment} = \text{April 1, 2024} + 60 \text{ days} = \text{May 31, 2024}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the statutory refund deadline.**

The company has an additional **15 days** to refund the money after the 60-day window expires.

$$
\text{Last date for refund (without interest)} = \text{May 31, 2024} + 15 \text{ days} = \text{June 15, 2024}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the period of delay beyond the 15-day grace period.**

Refund was actually made on **August 25, 2024**.

$$
\text{Total days from receipt of money to refund} = \text{April 1 to August 25} = 146 \text{ days}
$$

$$
\text{Statutory period (60 + 15)} = 75 \text{ days}
$$

$$
\text{Delay period} = 146 - 75 = 71 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the prescribed interest rate of 12% per annum.**

$$
\text{Interest} = P \times R \times \frac{T}{365}
$$

$$
\text{Interest} = 50{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{12}{100} \times \frac{71}{365}
$$

$$
\text{Interest} = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.12 \times 0.19452
$$

$$
\text{Interest} = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.023342
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Interest Payable} = \text{Rs. } 1{,}16{,}712 \text{ (approximately)}}
$$

---

### **Worked Example 2: Verification of the "200 Person" Limit**

**Problem (KTU-style):**
> ABC Ltd. (a public unlisted company) proposes to make a private placement in FY 2024–25. The proposed allottees include: 150 HNIs, 40 Mutual Funds, and 15 employees under ESOP. Is the proposed private placement in compliance with the Companies Act?

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the rule.**

Under Section 42 read with Rule 14(2), the maximum number of allottees in a financial year (for a public company) is **200**, **excluding** Qualified Institutional Buyers (QIBs) and **employees** under ESOP.

**Step 2 — Categorize the proposed allottees.**

| Category | Count | Treatment |
|---|---|---|
| HNIs | 150 | Counted towards the limit of 200 |
| Mutual Funds (QIBs) | 40 | **Excluded** from the 200 count |
| Employees (ESOP) | 15 | **Excluded** from the 200 count |

**Step 3 — Evaluate compliance.**

$$
\text{Effective count} = 150 \text{ (HNIs only)}
$$

Since $150 \leq 200$, the proposal is **in compliance** with Section 42.

$$
\boxed{\text{Compliant — Eligible for Private Placement}}
$$

---

### **Worked Example 3: Identifying the Type of Private Placement**

**Problem (KTU-style):**
> XYZ Ltd., a listed company, wants to raise Rs. 250 crores by issuing shares to domestic mutual funds and insurance companies only. Identify the most appropriate instrument under SEBI's ICDR Regulations.

**Solution:**

Since the issue size is **≥ Rs. 200 crores**, the allotment is to **institutional investors only**, and the issuer is a **listed company**, the appropriate mechanism is a **Qualified Institutional Placement (QIP)** under **Chapter VI of SEBI ICDR Regulations, 2018**.

$$
\boxed{\text{Qualified Institutional Placement (QIP)}}
$$

## 3.2 Comparative Analysis Matrix

| Parameter | Public Issue | Private Placement |
|---|---|---|
| Number of investors | Unlimited | Max **200** (excluding QIBs/employees) |
| Prospectus required | Yes (Red Herring Prospectus) | No (Private Placement Offer Letter in PAS-4) |
| SEBI approval | Mandatory | Generally not required (for unlisted) |
| Cost of issue | High (10–15% of issue size) | Relatively low (2–4%) |
| Time taken | 3–6 months | 1–3 months |
| Confidentiality | Low (public document) | High (confidential) |
| Pricing | Discovered through book-building | Negotiated / fixed by Board |
| Listing | Mandatory | Generally not |
| Liquidity | High | Low |
| Regulatory oversight | SEBI + ROC + Stock Exchange | Primarily ROC under Companies Act |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow of Private Placement under Section 42

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step A: Board of Directors Meeting] --> B[Step B: Board Resolution recommending Private Placement]
    B --> C[Step C: Convene General Meeting]
    C --> D[Step D: Pass Special Resolution by Shareholders]
    D --> E[Step E: Identify Proposed Allottees]
    E --> F[Step F: Prepare Private Placement Offer Letter in Form PAS-4]
    F --> G[Step G: Issue PAS-4 to Identified Allottees]
    G --> H[Step H: Receive Application Money]
    H --> I[Step I: Allotment of Securities within 60 Days]
    I --> J{Allotment Done within 60 Days?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Step K: File Form PAS-3 with ROC within 30 Days]
    J -->|No| L[Step L: Refund Money within 15 Days else pay 12% Interest]
    L --> M[Step M: File PAS-3 even for Non-Allotment]
    K --> N[Step N: End Process]
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Private Placement Issue

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Issuer
        A1[Board of Directors]
        A2[Audit Committee Review]
        A3[Compliance Officer]
    end

    subgraph Intermediaries
        B1[Legal Counsel]
        B2[Merchant Banker if Listed]
        B3[Registrar and Transfer Agent]
    end

    subgraph Regulators
        C1[Registrar of Companies ROC]
        C2[SEBI if Listed]
        C3[RBI if FEMA involved]
    end

    subgraph Investors
        D1[Qualified Institutional Buyers QIBs]
        D2[High Networth Individuals HNIs]
        D3[Employees under ESOP]
    end

    A1 --> A2 --> A3
    A3 --> B1
    A3 --> B2
    A3 --> B3
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> A3
    C2 --> A3
    C3 --> A3
    A3 --> D1
    A3 --> D2
    A3 --> D3
    D1 --> H1[Application Money via ASBA or Cheque]
    D2 --> H1
    D3 --> H1
    H1 --> A3
```

## 4.3 Schematic Decision Tree: Choosing Private Placement vs. Public Issue

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Company Needs Capital]) --> Q1{Issue Size and Investor Base?}
    Q1 -->|Small targeted group below Rs 50 Cr| P1[Private Placement]
    Q1 -->|Large public participation required| P2[Public Issue IPO or FPO]
    Q1 -->|Above Rs 200 Cr only to institutions| P3[Qualified Institutional Placement QIP]
    P1 --> R1[Governed by Section 42 of Companies Act 2013]
    P2 --> R2[Governed by SEBI ICDR Regulations and Companies Act]
    P3 --> R3[Governed by SEBI ICDR Chapter VI]
```

## 4.4 Key Advantages vs. Disadvantages — Visual Summary

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Advantages
        a1[Faster Execution]
        a2[Lower Issue Cost]
        a3[Confidentiality of Deal]
        a4[Negotiated Pricing]
        a5[Less Regulatory Burden]
    end

    subgraph Disadvantages
        b1[Limited Investor Pool]
        b2[Lower Liquidity]
        b3[Risk of Allotment Failure]
        b4[Possibly Higher Coupon for Debt]
        b5[Reputational Risk if Deal Fails]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
> Define private placement. Mention the statutory provision governing it in India.

**Model Answer:**

Private placement is a method of raising funds from the primary market in which a company issues securities **directly to a pre-identified group of investors**, rather than inviting subscriptions from the general public.

In India, private placement is governed by **Section 42 of the Companies Act, 2013**, read with **Rules 14 and 15 of the Companies (Prospectus and Allotment of Securities) Rules, 2014**.

Key characteristics include: maximum of **200 allottees** per financial year (excluding QIBs and employees), no public prospectus, and mandatory issuance of a **Private Placement Offer Letter (Form PAS-4)**.

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
> State any **three differences** between a public issue and a private placement.

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | Public Issue | Private Placement |
|---|---|---|
| 1. | Securities are offered to the general public. | Securities are offered to a select group of identified investors. |
| 2. | Requires a Prospectus / Red Herring Prospectus. | Requires only a Private Placement Offer Letter (Form PAS-4). |
| 3. | SEBI approval is mandatory. | SEBI approval not required for unlisted companies; only ROC filing needed. |

*(Any 3 such differences would carry full 3 marks.)*

---

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[INTERNAL CHOICE — Answer ANY ONE: Question A or Question B]**

---

### 🅰️ **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **legal framework and procedure** for private placement of securities under the Companies Act, 2013. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages** of private placement from the perspective of an issuing company. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — Legal Framework & Procedure:

The legal framework for private placement in India is anchored in **Section 42 of the Companies Act, 2013**, supplemented by Rules 14 and 15 of the Companies (Prospectus and Allotment of Securities) Rules, 2014.

**1. Board Resolution:** The Board of Directors must first pass a resolution at a meeting recommending the private placement and identifying the proposed allottees.

**2. Special Resolution:** Shareholders must approve the issue by a **Special Resolution**. For private companies, an ordinary resolution is sufficient if the Articles permit.

**3. Identification of Allottees:** The list of proposed allottees must be finalized **before** the offer. No advertisement or general solicitation is permitted.

**4. Issuance of Form PAS-4:** A **Private Placement Offer Letter** in Form PAS-4, serially numbered, must be issued to each proposed allottee.

**5. Application Money:** Identified allottees must apply through serially numbered application forms. The application money must be received from **each allottee's own bank account** (to prevent money laundering).

**6. Allotment Time Limit:** Allotment must be made **within 60 days** from the date of receipt of application money. If not, the money must be refunded within **15 days** along with interest at **12% p.a.**

**7. Filing of PAS-3:** A return of allotment in Form PAS-3 must be filed with the Registrar of Companies (ROC) within **30 days** of allotment.

**8. Non-Compliance Penalty:** Failure to comply attracts fines of up to **Rs. 25 lakh** on the company and **Rs. 5 lakh** on every defaulting officer.

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — Advantages & Disadvantages:

**Advantages:**

1. **Speed:** Faster than a public issue — typically completed in 1–3 months.
2. **Lower Cost:** Issue costs are typically **2–4%** of issue size vs. **10–15%** for a public issue.
3. **Confidentiality:** Negotiations remain private; strategic information is not disclosed publicly.
4. **Pricing Flexibility:** The issuer can negotiate price with select investors.
5. **Less Regulatory Burden:** No SEBI approval (for unlisted); reduced compliance time.
6. **Targeted Marketing:** The issue can be tailored to specific investor categories (e.g., long-term insurance funds).

**Disadvantages:**

1. **Limited Investor Base:** Maximum 200 persons — restricts capital-raising scale.
2. **Lower Liquidity:** Privately placed securities are generally not listed, reducing marketability.
3. **Higher Coupon (for Debt):** Private placement of NCDs often carries a **higher interest rate** to compensate for illiquidity.
4. **Concentration Risk:** Heavy reliance on a few investors may expose the issuer to renegotiation pressures.
5. **Reputational Risk:** Failed private placements may signal financial weakness to the market.
6. **Special Resolution Overhead:** Mandatory shareholder approval adds procedural time and cost.

---

### 🅱️ **Question B (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** What is **Qualified Institutional Placement (QIP)**? Explain its features and eligibility conditions. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** A company received application money of **Rs. 75 lakhs** on **March 1, 2024** through private placement. It failed to allot shares within the statutory period and refunded the money on **July 15, 2024**. Calculate the **interest payable** under Section 42(6). **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — QIP:

**Qualified Institutional Placement (QIP)** is a special route under **Chapter VI of SEBI ICDR Regulations, 2018**, allowing listed companies to raise capital by issuing equity shares, fully convertible debentures, or other convertible securities **exclusively to Qualified Institutional Buyers (QIBs)**.

**Features:**

1. **Eligible Issuers:** Only listed entities can issue QIPs.
2. **Minimum Issue Size:** **Rs. 200 crores** (can be lower with shareholder approval if below this threshold).
3. **Investors Only:** Only QIBs — mutual funds, insurance companies, pension funds, foreign portfolio investors, banks, etc. — can subscribe.
4. **Minimum Allottees:** At least **2 allottees** are required.
5. **Pricing Floor:** A floor price is calculated based on the **average of the last 6 months' closing prices** or **2 weeks' average**, whichever is higher.
6. **Lock-in Period:** **6 months** for the issuer's promoters and the QIB subscribers.
7. **No SEBI Approval Required:** QIPs are governed by SEBI regulations, but offer documents do not require SEBI vetting — only stock exchange filing.
8. **Speed:** Issue can be completed in **2–4 weeks**, much faster than an FPO.

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — Interest Calculation:

**Step 1 — Statutory 60-day allotment window.**

$$
\text{Receipt date} = \text{March 1, 2024}
$$

$$
\text{Last allotment date} = \text{March 1, 2024} + 60 \text{ days} = \text{April 30, 2024}
$$

**Step 2 — 15-day refund grace period.**

$$
\text{Last refund date without interest} = \text{April 30, 2024} + 15 \text{ days} = \text{May 15, 2024}
$$

**Step 3 — Total days from receipt to actual refund.**

$$
\text{Refund date} = \text{July 15, 2024}
$$

$$
\text{Total days} = \text{March 1 to July 15} = 136 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 4 — Excess days beyond statutory 75 days (60 + 15).**

$$
\text{Delay} = 136 - 75 = 61 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply 12% p.a. simple interest.**

$$
\text{Interest} = P \times R \times \frac{T}{365}
$$

$$
\text{Interest} = 75{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{12}{100} \times \frac{61}{365}
$$

$$
\text{Interest} = 75{,}00{,}000 \times 0.12 \times 0.16712
$$

$$
\text{Interest} = 75{,}00{,}000 \times 0.020055
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Interest Payable} = \text{Rs. } 1{,}50{,}411 \text{ (approximately)}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Many students forget to **exclude QIBs and ESOP employees** from the 200-person limit. Always clarify who counts.
> - **Mistake 2:** In interest calculations, students often use **360 days** instead of **365 days**. The Companies Act uses **365 days**.
> - **Mistake 3:** Confusing **Form PAS-3** (return of allotment) with **Form PAS-4** (offer letter). PAS-4 is the offer document; PAS-3 is the post-allotment return.
> - **Mistake 4:** Writing "Private Placement is governed by SEBI." For unlisted companies, SEBI has no jurisdiction — it's purely the **Companies Act, 2013 (Section 42)** and the ROC.
> - **Mistake 5:** Forgetting to mention that **private placement is not available to public issues**; confusing it with preferential allotment under SEBI ICDR (which is for listed companies).

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam:**

- ✅ Private placement = **direct issue** to select investors, **not the public**.
- ✅ Statutory authority: **Section 42, Companies Act, 2013**.
- ✅ Max **200 allottees** per financial year (excluding QIBs and ESOP employees).
- ✅ **Special Resolution** is mandatory (for public companies).
- ✅ **PAS-4** = Private Placement Offer Letter (issued to allottees).
- ✅ **PAS-3** = Return of Allotment (filed with ROC within 30 days).
- ✅ Allotment deadline = **60 days** from receipt of application money.
- ✅ Refund deadline = **15 days** after the 60-day window; **12% p.a. interest** beyond.
- ✅ **QIP** = QIB-only route for listed companies, minimum **Rs. 200 crores**, governed by **SEBI ICDR Chapter VI**.
- ✅ **No prospectus, no public advertisement, no SEBI approval** (for unlisted companies).
- ✅ Filing of **Form MGT-14** with ROC is required for the Special Resolution.
- ✅ **Penalty for non-compliance:** Rs. 25 lakh on company + Rs. 5 lakh on every defaulting officer (and imprisonment up to 6 months for repeat offenders).
- ✅ Private placement is **cheaper, faster, and more confidential** but offers **lower liquidity**.
- ✅ **Key corporate users:** NBFCs, HFCs, startups, large listed corporates for QIPs, and infrastructure firms.
- ✅ Distinguish clearly between **Preferential Allotment** (SEBI ICDR, listed companies) and **Private Placement** (Section 42, may be unlisted or listed).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Secondary Market - BSE
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Secondary Market - BSE

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SECONDARY MARKET (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> The **Secondary Market**, also known as the *aftermarket* or *follow-on public offering (FPO) market*, is the financial market where previously issued securities — such as **equity shares, debentures, bonds, and government securities** — are traded among investors. In the Indian context, the two apex stock exchanges are the **Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE)** and the **National Stock Exchange (NSE)**, both regulated by the **Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI)** under the **SEBI Act, 1992**.

The Secondary Market performs the critical function of providing **liquidity, price discovery, and continuous valuation** of securities that were originally sold in the Primary Market. It does **not** create new capital for issuing companies; instead, it facilitates the transfer of ownership from one investor to another at mutually agreed prices determined by market forces of demand and supply.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Used Car Market" Analogy:**
> Imagine a brand-new car is manufactured and sold for the first time by the company to a buyer — this is the **Primary Market**. Now, that buyer decides to sell the car a year later to another person. The place where this resale happens — be it a used car showroom or an online platform — is the **Secondary Market**.
>
> - The car company does **not** get any money in the resale.
> - However, the **existence of a resale market** is what gave the first buyer the *confidence* to buy the car in the first place (because they knew they could exit later).
> - The **price** of the used car is now determined by how much a new buyer is willing to pay the old owner — this is **price discovery**.

In the financial world:
- **Primary Market** = IPO/FPO (Initial/Further Public Offer)
- **Secondary Market** = BSE/NSE trading platforms where shares are bought and sold daily.
- The resale value of your share = **Market Price** (e.g., ₹850 on BSE Sensex).

### 1.3 Key Characteristics of the Secondary Market

| Characteristic | Description |
|---|---|
| **Liquidity** | Investors can convert their securities into cash quickly without major price concession. |
| **Price Discovery** | Real-time market forces continuously determine the fair value of securities. |
| **Market Efficiency** | Information is rapidly incorporated into prices (Weak, Semi-Strong, Strong forms per **Eugene Fama's Efficient Market Hypothesis, 1970**). |
| **No Direct Capital Flow to Issuer** | Money flows from buyer → seller, not buyer → company. |
| **Regulatory Oversight** | Governed by **SEBI (Securities and Exchange Board of India)** under SEBI Act, 1992. |
| **Two Auction Types** | **Open Outcry System** (older) and **Order-Driven Electronic System** (modern, used by BSE since 1995). |

### 1.4 Introduction to BSE (Bombay Stock Exchange)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BOMBAY STOCK EXCHANGE (BSE) — Key Highlights:**
> - **Full Name:** BSE Limited (formerly Bombay Stock Exchange Limited).
> - **Established:** **1875** — the oldest stock exchange in Asia and one of the oldest in the world.
> - **Location:** Dalal Street, Mumbai, Maharashtra, India.
> - **Benchmark Index:** **S&P BSE SENSEX (Sensitive Index)** — comprises **30 constituent stocks** representing major sectors.
> - **Listed Companies:** Over **5,500+ companies** (as of recent SEBI disclosures).
> - **Trading System:** **BOLT (BSE On-Line Trading)** — introduced in 1995, fully screen-based, order-driven.
> - **Trading Hours:** **9:15 AM to 3:30 PM IST**, Monday to Friday (Pre-open session: 9:00–9:15 AM).
> - **Settlement Cycle:** **T+1 rolling settlement** (post SEBI's 2023 mandate).
> - **Global Recognition:** Recognized by the **Government of India** under the **Securities Contracts (Regulation) Act, 1956**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown: How a Secondary Market Transaction Works

The operational flow of a trade on the BSE is governed by SEBI regulations and follows a structured electronic process:

1. **Investor Decision & Order Placement** — The investor places a Buy/Sell order through a **SEBI-registered Stock Broker** (e.g., Zerodha, ICICI Direct) via the broker's trading terminal.
2. **Order Routing** — The broker's terminal routes the order to the **BSE's BOLT (BSE On-Line Trading) system** through leased lines or secured internet.
3. **Order Matching Engine** — BOLT's matching engine pairs Buy and Sell orders based on **Price-Time Priority**:
   - **Price Priority:** Best price gets matched first (highest bid / lowest ask).
   - **Time Priority:** If prices are equal, the order that came first in time is matched first.
4. **Trade Execution** — Once matched, a trade confirmation is generated with a **unique Trade Number** and timestamp.
5. **Trade Confirmation to Broker** — The broker receives trade details and informs the client.
6. **Clearing & Settlement** — The trade is cleared through the **Indian Clearing Corporation Limited (ICCL)** — BSE's clearing arm — and settled on a **T+1 basis** (T = Trade Day, T+1 = next working day).
7. **Securities & Funds Transfer** — Shares move from seller's **Demat Account** to buyer's Demat Account, and funds move from buyer to seller.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept: Demat Account**
> A *Dematerialized Account* holds shares in electronic form, eliminating physical share certificates. It is maintained by **Depositories** — **NSDL (National Securities Depository Limited)** and **CDSL (Central Depository Services Limited)**.

### 2.2 The BSE Sensex: Construction & Methodology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **S&P BSE SENSEX (Sensitive Index):**
> The Sensex is a **free-float market capitalization-weighted index** of **30 well-established and financially sound companies** listed on the BSE. It is the oldest index in India, first compiled in **1986**, with a base year of **1978–79** and a base value of **100**.

**Selection Criteria for Sensex Constituents:**
- Listing history on BSE (minimum track record).
- Trading frequency and volume.
- **Free-float market capitalization** (shares readily available for trading, excluding promoter holdings).
- Industry representation and sectoral balance.
- Financial soundness (no disqualification under SEBI's eligibility norms).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Formulas for Sensex & Market Calculations**

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Free-Float Market Capitalization** | $\text{FFMC} = \text{Total Shares} \times \text{Market Price} \times \text{Free-Float Factor}$ | Factor ∈ [0, 1] |
| 2 | **Sensex Value (Index Formula)** | $\text{Sensex} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{30} \text{FFMC}_{i,t}}{\sum_{i=1}^{30} \text{FFMC}_{i,0}} \times \text{Base Value}$ | $i$ = constituent stock, $t$ = current time, $0$ = base period |
| 3 | **Market Capitalization (Company)** | $\text{MCap} = \text{Total Outstanding Shares} \times \text{Current Market Price}$ | In ₹ (or ₹ Crore) |
| 4 | **Stock Return (Simple)** | $R = \dfrac{P_{1} - P_{0} + D}{P_{0}}$ | $P_1$ = current price, $P_0$ = initial price, $D$ = dividend |
| 5 | **PE Ratio (Price-to-Earnings)** | $\text{PE} = \dfrac{\text{Market Price per Share}}{\text{Earnings per Share (EPS)}}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 6 | **Turnover Ratio** | $\text{TR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Shares Traded in Period}}{\text{Total Outstanding Shares}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | **Bid-Ask Spread** | $\text{Spread} = \text{Ask Price} - \text{Bid Price}$ | In ₹ per share |
| 8 | **Volatility (Std. Dev. of Returns)** | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(R_i - \bar{R})^2}$ | $R_i$ = period return, $\bar{R}$ = mean return |
| 9 | **Beta (Systematic Risk)** | $\beta = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_{\text{stock}}, R_{\text{market}})}{\text{Var}(R_{\text{market}})}$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | **Circuit Breaker Trigger** | Daily price band limits: **±2%, ±5%, ±10%, ±20%** based on index movement | Triggered automatically by BOLT |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TABLE RULE APPLIED:** The vertical pipe symbol `\|` has been replaced with `\vert` in the Spread formula to prevent markdown table breaking. For example, "$\vert$Ask - Bid$\vert$" uses the safe delimiter.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Economic Utility

- **Algorithmic Trading Systems:** High-frequency trading (HFT) platforms rely on the order-matching architecture pioneered by exchanges like BSE.
- **Database Engineering:** Settlement systems require **ACID-compliant distributed databases** (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) to handle millions of daily transactions.
- **Network Engineering:** BOLT requires **low-latency leased-line networks** (sub-millisecond order routing).
- **Financial Economists** use Sensex time-series data to test models like the **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)** and **Arbitrage Pricing Theory (APT)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Sensex Index Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive the Sensex formula from fundamental market principles.

**Step 1 — Defining Market Capitalization**

The market capitalization of company $i$ at time $t$ is the product of its total outstanding shares and current market price.

$$
\text{MCap}_{i,t} = N_i \cdot P_{i,t}
$$

Where:
- $N_i$ = Total number of outstanding shares of company $i$.
- $P_{i,t}$ = Market price per share of company $i$ at time $t$.

**Step 2 — Adjusting for Free-Float**

The BSE Sensex uses the **Free-Float methodology** (adopted in **September 2003**), which excludes promoter- and government-locked shares.

$$
\text{FFMC}_{i,t} = N_i \cdot F_i \cdot P_{i,t}
$$

Where $F_i$ is the **Free-Float Factor** (proportion of shares available for public trading), where $0 \leq F_i \leq 1$.

**Step 3 — Aggregating Across the 30 Constituents**

$$
\text{Total FFMC}_{t} = \sum_{i=1}^{30} N_i \cdot F_i \cdot P_{i,t}
$$

**Step 4 — Anchoring to the Base Period**

To express the index as a number relative to a base, we divide the current aggregate by the base period aggregate (1978–79 for Sensex).

$$
\text{Index}_{t} = \dfrac{\text{Total FFMC}_{t}}{\text{Total FFMC}_{0}} \cdot \text{Base Value}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Sensex Formula**

$$
\boxed{\text{SENSEX}_{t} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{30} (N_i \cdot F_i \cdot P_{i,t})}{\sum_{i=1}^{30} (N_i \cdot F_i \cdot P_{0,\text{base}})} \times 100}
$$

Where the base value **100** corresponds to the 1978–79 base period.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Calculating Sensex for a Simplified 2-Stock Case

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** Consider a hypothetical index with 2 stocks (A and B), with the following data:

| Stock | Shares Outstanding (N) | Free-Float Factor (F) | Current Price (P_t) | Base Price (P_0) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 10,000 | 0.7 | ₹500 | ₹300 |
| B | 5,000 | 0.8 | ₹800 | ₹600 |

**Step 1 — Compute FFMC for each stock at time t**

$$
\text{FFMC}_{A,t} = 10{,}000 \times 0.7 \times 500 = 35{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\text{FFMC}_{B,t} = 5{,}000 \times 0.8 \times 800 = 32{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 2 — Total FFMC at time t**

$$
\text{Total FFMC}_{t} = 35{,}00{,}000 + 32{,}00{,}000 = 67{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute FFMC for each stock at base period**

$$
\text{FFMC}_{A,0} = 10{,}000 \times 0.7 \times 300 = 21{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\text{FFMC}_{B,0} = 5{,}000 \times 0.8 \times 600 = 24{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 4 — Total FFMC at base period**

$$
\text{Total FFMC}_{0} = 21{,}00{,}000 + 24{,}00{,}000 = 45{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Index Formula with Base Value 100**

$$
\text{Index}_{t} = \dfrac{67{,}00{,}000}{45{,}00{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Index}_{t} = 1.4889 \times 100 = 148.89
$$

**Interpretation:** The hypothetical index has risen from 100 (base) to 148.89, indicating a **48.89% appreciation** in the free-float market value of the constituent stocks.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Key Differences — BSE vs. NSE

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparative Matrix for KTU Exam Preparation**

| Parameter | **BSE (Bombay Stock Exchange)** | **NSE (National Stock Exchange)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Established** | 1875 (Asia's oldest) | 1992 |
| **Benchmark Index** | S&P BSE Sensex (30 stocks) | Nifty 50 (50 stocks) |
| **Trading System** | BOLT (since 1995) | NEAT (National Exchange for Automated Trading) |
| **Listed Companies** | 5,500+ | 2,000+ |
| **Clearing House** | ICCL (Indian Clearing Corp. Ltd.) | NCL (National Clearing Ltd.) |
| **Market Capitalization Rank** | 2nd in India (historically) | 1st in India |
| **Settlement** | T+1 (since 2023) | T+1 (since 2023) |
| **Headquarters** | Mumbai | Mumbai |
| **Global Recognition** | SEBI-recognized | SEBI-recognized |

### 3.4 Derivation: Return on Sensex (Index Return)

The percentage return on the Sensex over a period from $t_0$ to $t_1$ is:

$$
R_{\text{Sensex}} = \dfrac{\text{Sensex}_{t_1} - \text{Sensex}_{t_0}}{\text{Sensex}_{t_0}} \times 100\%
$$

**Example:** If Sensex moved from 65,000 to 72,000:

$$
R = \dfrac{72{,}000 - 65{,}000}{65{,}000} \times 100 = \dfrac{7{,}000}{65{,}000} \times 100 \approx 10.77\%
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: BSE Secondary Market Trading Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Investor Client] -->|Places Buy or Sell Order| B[Stock Broker Terminal]
    B -->|Encrypted Order via Leased Line| C[BSE BOLT Trading Engine]
    C -->|Order Validation and Risk Check| D[Order Matching Module]
    D -->|Price Time Priority Match| E[Trade Execution Confirmation]
    E -->|Trade Details| F[ICCL Clearing House]
    F -->|Net Settlement Obligation| G[T Plus 1 Settlement Cycle]
    G -->|Demat Transfer via NSDL or CDSL| H[Buyer Demat Account]
    G -->|Funds Transfer via Bank| I[Seller Bank Account]
    E -->|Real Time Price Data| J[BSE Sensex Index Engine]
    J -->|Weighted Aggregation of 30 Stocks| K[Sensex Live Value]
    K -->|Broadcast to Global Vendors| L[News and Data Feeds]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Sensex Index Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Stock 1 Price Feed] --> B1[Compute FFMC 1]
    A2[Stock 2 Price Feed] --> B2[Compute FFMC 2]
    A3[Stock 30 Price Feed] --> B3[Compute FFMC 30]
    B1 --> C[Sum Total FFMC at Time t]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    D[Base Period FFMC Constant 1978-79] --> E[Division Operation]
    C --> E
    E --> F[Multiply by Base Value 100]
    F --> G[SENSEX Real Time Value]
    G --> H[Dashboard and News Display]
```

### 4.3 Hierarchical Structure: Secondary Market Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Regulator SEBI Securities and Exchange Board of India] --> S1[BSE Bombay Stock Exchange]
    R --> S2[NSE National Stock Exchange]
    S1 --> P1[Trading Engine BOLT]
    S1 --> P2[Clearing House ICCL]
    S1 --> P3[Index S and P BSE Sensex]
    S1 --> M1[Member Brokers]
    M1 --> I1[Retail Investors]
    M1 --> I2[Institutional Investors FIIs DIIs]
    M1 --> I3[High Frequency Traders]
    P2 --> DEP1[Depository NSDL]
    P2 --> DEP2[Depository CDSL]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Secondary Market." List any **two** functions performed by the Secondary Market.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Secondary Market** is a financial marketplace where **already-issued securities** (such as shares, debentures, and bonds) are traded between investors. Unlike the Primary Market, no new securities are created here; ownership is simply transferred.

**Two Key Functions:**

1. **Liquidity Provision:** It allows investors to easily buy and sell their holdings, converting investments into cash without significant price loss. This continuous tradability makes investors more willing to invest in the Primary Market.
2. **Price Discovery:** Through continuous interaction of demand and supply forces, the market determines the **fair market price** of securities in real time.

> **Alternative valid functions:** Continuous valuation, market efficiency, risk redistribution, and efficient capital allocation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*What is the **BSE Sensex**? State the **base year** and **base value** of the Sensex.*

**Model Answer:**

The **S&P BSE Sensex (Sensitive Index)** is the **benchmark index of the Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE)**. It represents the performance of **30 well-established, financially sound, and actively traded companies** listed on the BSE, selected on the basis of **free-float market capitalization**, industry representation, and trading volume.

- **Base Year:** **1978–79**
- **Base Value:** **100**

The Sensex is calculated using the **Free-Float Market Capitalization-weighted methodology** and is widely regarded as a barometer of the Indian equity market and broader economic sentiment.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 | Module 1 Choice Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **structure and operational mechanism** of the **Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE)**. Describe the role of **BOLT** and **ICCL** in the trading process. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the Sensex value using the **Free-Float Market Capitalization** method from the following data, given a base value of **100**:

| Stock | Outstanding Shares | Free-Float Factor | Current Price (₹) | Base Price (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| X | 1,00,000 | 0.6 | 250 | 150 |
| Y | 80,000 | 0.75 | 400 | 300 |
| Z | 50,000 | 0.5 | 600 | 400 |

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**1. Structure of BSE:**

The **Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE)**, established in **1875**, is Asia's oldest stock exchange. It is a **demutualized entity** registered as **BSE Limited** under the Companies Act, 2013. It operates under the regulatory supervision of **SEBI (Securities and Exchange Board of India)** established by the **SEBI Act, 1992**. The BSE is recognized under the **Securities Contracts (Regulation) Act, 1956**.

**2. Operational Mechanism:**

- Investors place orders through **SEBI-registered stock brokers**.
- Orders are routed electronically to the **BSE's BOLT (BSE On-Line Trading)** platform.
- BOLT's **order-matching engine** executes trades based on **Price-Time Priority**.
- Trades are confirmed and sent for **clearing and settlement**.

**3. Role of BOLT:**

**BOLT (BSE On-Line Trading)** is the **fully automated, screen-based, order-driven trading system** introduced by BSE in **1995**. It replaced the old open-outcry system. BOLT ensures:
- Real-time order matching
- Transparent price discovery
- Reduced transaction time and settlement risk
- Efficient dissemination of trade data

**4. Role of ICCL:**

The **Indian Clearing Corporation Limited (ICCL)** is the **clearing and settlement arm** of the BSE. It acts as the **central counterparty (CCP)** to all trades, guaranteeing settlement even if one party defaults. It handles:
- **Trade novation** (becoming counterparty to both buyer and seller)
- **Risk management** (margin collection, exposure monitoring)
- **Net settlement** on a **T+1 rolling basis** (as mandated by SEBI in 2023)

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute Current FFMC for each stock at time t:**

$$
\text{FFMC}_{X,t} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6 \times 250 = 1{,}50{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\text{FFMC}_{Y,t} = 80{,}000 \times 0.75 \times 400 = 2{,}40{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\text{FFMC}_{Z,t} = 50{,}000 \times 0.5 \times 600 = 1{,}50{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 2 — Total FFMC at time t:**

$$
\text{Total FFMC}_{t} = 1{,}50{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}40{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Base FFMC for each stock:**

$$
\text{FFMC}_{X,0} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6 \times 150 = 90{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\text{FFMC}_{Y,0} = 80{,}000 \times 0.75 \times 300 = 1{,}80{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\text{FFMC}_{Z,0} = 50{,}000 \times 0.5 \times 400 = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Base FFMC:**

$$
\text{Total FFMC}_{0} = 90{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}70{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Sensex Formula:**

$$
\text{Sensex} = \dfrac{5{,}40{,}00{,}000}{3{,}70{,}00{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Sensex} = 1.4595 \times 100 = \mathbf{145.95}
$$

**Interpretation:** The index value of 145.95 (above the base of 100) indicates a **45.95% appreciation** in the free-float market value of the constituent stocks since the base period.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 1 Choice Question]**

**(a)** Differentiate between the **Primary Market** and the **Secondary Market** on any **five** parameters. **(5 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Price Discovery** and **Liquidity** in the Secondary Market. Describe the **order-driven trading mechanism** with the concept of **Price-Time Priority**. **(9 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

| Parameter | Primary Market | Secondary Market |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Purpose** | Mobilizes new capital for issuing companies | Provides a platform for trading existing securities |
| **2. Issuer Involvement** | Company directly issues and receives funds | Company is not involved; transactions are between investors |
| **3. Price Determination** | Fixed price (set by issuer/underwriter) | Market-driven (demand-supply forces) |
| **4. Examples** | IPO, FPO, Rights Issue, Bonus Issue | BSE/NSE trading platform for listed shares |
| **5. Frequency of Transactions** | One-time or occasional | Continuous (daily during trading hours) |
| **6. Capital Flow** | Flows from investors → issuing company | Flows from buyer → seller (not to company) |
| **7. Regulation** | SEBI (ICDR Regulations, 2018) | SEBI (Stock Brokers Regulations, LODR, 2015) |

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**1. Price Discovery:**

**Price Discovery** is the process by which a competitive market determines the **fair market price** of a security through the **interaction of buyers' demand and sellers' supply**. The BSE's BOLT system aggregates thousands of buy and sell orders every second, and the equilibrium price at which the maximum quantity is matched becomes the **market-clearing price**. This mechanism ensures that prices reflect all publicly available information — a core tenet of the **Efficient Market Hypothesis (EMH)** proposed by **Eugene Fama (1970)**.

**2. Liquidity:**

**Liquidity** refers to the ease with which a security can be bought or sold in the market **without significantly affecting its price**. Highly liquid markets (like BSE Sensex stocks) have:
- High trading volumes
- Tight bid-ask spreads
- Many active buyers and sellers
- Quick order execution

Liquidity reduces **transaction costs** and **price slippage** for investors, making the market more attractive.

**3. Order-Driven Trading Mechanism:**

In an **order-driven market** (as opposed to a quote-driven/dealer market), investors submit their own orders specifying the price and quantity they wish to trade. There is no market maker quoting prices — instead, the exchange's matching engine directly matches buy and sell orders from the order book.

**4. Price-Time Priority:**

The BSE's BOLT uses a strict **two-tier priority rule** for order matching:

**i. Price Priority:** Orders with the **best price** are matched first.
- A **Buy order at ₹100** is matched before a Buy order at ₹99.
- A **Sell order at ₹95** is matched before a Sell order at ₹96.

**ii. Time Priority:** If two orders have the **same price**, the order that **entered the system earlier** is matched first.

**Example:**
- Buy Order 1: ₹100, 9:15:00 AM (100 shares)
- Buy Order 2: ₹100, 9:15:05 AM (200 shares)
- Sell Order: ₹100, 9:15:10 AM (150 shares)

**Matching Result:** Buy Order 1 receives 100 shares (fully executed), and Sell Order is partially filled (50 shares remaining unsold). Buy Order 2 waits for the next seller.

This system ensures **fairness, transparency, and efficiency** in trade execution.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Primary and Secondary Market functions** — common student error. Remember: *Primary = new capital creation*; *Secondary = liquidity & price discovery only*.
> 2. **Free-Float Factor** is often forgotten in numerical problems — students frequently multiply shares × price without applying $F_i$. This costs **2–3 marks** instantly.
> 3. **Base Value = 100** must be multiplied at the **end** — applying it incorrectly at intermediate steps is a frequent mistake.
> 4. **Price-Time Priority** is a guaranteed 2-mark question. Memorize the order: **Price FIRST, Time SECOND.**
> 5. **T+1 settlement** is the current SEBI-mandated cycle (post 2023). Older T+2 references may cost marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- ✅ **Secondary Market** = trading of *already-issued* securities; does **NOT** create new capital.
- ✅ **BSE Established:** 1875 (Asia's oldest); **NSE Established:** 1992.
- ✅ **BSE Benchmark:** S&P BSE Sensex — **30 stocks**, **Base Year 1978–79**, **Base Value 100**.
- ✅ **NSE Benchmark:** Nifty 50 — **50 stocks**, **Base Year 1995–96**, **Base Value 1000**.
- ✅ **Trading System at BSE:** **BOLT** (BSE On-Line Trading) — introduced 1995, fully electronic, order-driven.
- ✅ **Clearing House at BSE:** **ICCL (Indian Clearing Corporation Limited)** — acts as **Central Counterparty (CCP)**.
- ✅ **Settlement Cycle:** **T+1 rolling settlement** (effective January 2023, per SEBI mandate).
- ✅ **Trading Hours:** 9:15 AM – 3:30 PM IST (Pre-open: 9:00–9:15 AM).
- ✅ **Regulator:** **SEBI** under **SEBI Act, 1992**; exchanges recognized under **SCRA, 1956**.
- ✅ **Order Matching Rule:** **Price Priority → Time Priority**.
- ✅ **Sensex Formula:** $\text{FFMC}_{t}/\text{FFMC}_{0} \times 100$, where $\text{FFMC} = N \times F \times P$.
- ✅ **Functions of Secondary Market:** Liquidity, Price Discovery, Continuous Valuation, Risk Redistribution.
- ✅ **Depositories:** **NSDL** and **CDSL** handle electronic (Demat) share transfers.
- ✅ **Key Index Methodology:** **Free-Float Market Capitalization-Weighted** (adopted by Sensex in 2003).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NASDAQ
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** NASDAQ

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of NASDAQ

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NASDAQ (National Association of Securities Dealers Automated Quotations)** is a fully electronic, screen-based, dealer-oriented stock exchange headquartered in New York City, USA. It is operated by **Nasdaq, Inc.** (formerly the NASDAQ OMX Group) and is globally recognized as the **second-largest stock exchange in the world by market capitalization**, surpassed only by the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE).

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Introduction to Financial Markets*, NASDAQ is classified as a **secondary market** instrument and falls under the category of an **organized, over-the-counter (OTC) evolved exchange** that transitioned from a quotation system to a full-fledged listed exchange in **2006**.

### 1.2 Key Identification Metrics (as per Latest Verified Data)

| Metric | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Founding Year | **1971** (February 8, 1971) |
| Headquarters | New York City, USA |
| Operator | **Nasdaq, Inc.** |
| Trading Platform | Fully Electronic (Screen-Based) |
| Type of Market | Order-Driven + Dealer (Hybrid) |
| Major Indices | **NASDAQ Composite**, **NASDAQ-100**, **NASDAQ Biotechnology** |
| Listing Tier 1 | NASDAQ Global Select Market |
| Listing Tier 2 | NASDAQ Global Market |
| Listing Tier 3 | NASDAQ Capital Market |
| Daily Trading Hours | 09:30 AM – 4:00 PM ET (Pre-market: 4:00 AM – 9:30 AM ET) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of NASDAQ as the "Amazon of Stock Exchanges."** Just as Amazon replaced physical shops with an online catalog of products, NASDAQ replaced the open-outcry trading floor (where humans shout buy/sell orders) with a **computerized network of quotes**. When you walk into a traditional marketplace (NYSE), traders physically meet and trade. NASDAQ is like a giant digital marketplace where buyers and sellers are connected through screens and computer algorithms — no shouting, no hand signals, just data flowing through fiber-optic cables.

> **Plain English Summary:** NASDAQ is essentially a **virtual, computer-based marketplace** where shares of technology giants like Apple, Microsoft, Amazon, Google (Alphabet), Meta, and Tesla are bought and sold. It is **technology-friendly**, **innovation-focused**, and the **preferred home for growth-oriented and tech companies** worldwide.

### 1.4 Historical Evolution Snapshot

```
1971 → Founded as world's first electronic stock market
1987 → Reached 10,000 daily quote volume milestone
2000 → Dot-com bubble peak (NASDAQ Composite hit 5,048.62)
2006 → Became a registered national securities exchange
2007 → Acquired OMX (Swedish-Finnish exchange) → NASDAQ OMX
2015 → Renamed to Nasdaq, Inc.
2023 → Surpassed NYSE briefly in market cap of listed companies
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus:** Examiners frequently test the *year of founding (1971)*, the *fully electronic nature*, the *acronym expansion*, and the *shift from OTC to listed exchange in 2006*. These are **direct 2-mark questions** in the KTU Part A pattern.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Knowledge Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanics — How NASDAQ Works

NASDAQ operates on a **dealer market model** using a sophisticated system of **Market Makers**. The mechanics are as follows:

1. **Quotation System:** NASDAQ maintains a real-time database of buy and sell prices (quotes) for all listed securities, accessible to participants via the **Nasdaq MarketSite** and proprietary terminals.

2. **Market Makers (Dealers):** Each listed stock has multiple registered market makers (e.g., Citadel Securities, Virtu Financial, GTS Securities) who continuously quote **bid prices** (what they will pay to buy) and **ask prices** (what they will sell for). The difference is the **bid-ask spread**.

3. **Order Routing:** Buy/sell orders are routed electronically via the **Small Order Execution System (SOES)** or the **Super Display Book (SDBK)** for large orders.

4. **Trade Execution:** Trades are matched through the **Nasdaq Execution System (NES)** which is built on the **INET platform** (acquired in 2005 from the Island ECN).

5. **Settlement:** Trades clear via the **DTCC (Depository Trust & Clearing Corporation)** on a T+1 basis (post-2024 SEC mandate).

### 2.2 The Three-Tier Listing Structure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> NASDAQ uniquely classifies its listings into three tiers based on financial and liquidity thresholds. This is a **favourite KTU Part B question** (7–14 marks).

| Tier | Market Name | Minimum Financial Criteria | Typical Companies |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tier 1** | **NASDAQ Global Select Market** | Highest: EBITDA ≥ USD 15M, 1.1M public float, 4-year operating history | Apple, Microsoft, Amazon |
| **Tier 2** | **NASDAQ Global Market** | Moderate: USD 75M public float OR total assets ≥ USD 75M | Mid-cap growth firms |
| **Tier 3** | **NASDAQ Capital Market** | Lower: USD 5M stockholders' equity, USD 1M public float | Small-cap / emerging firms |

### 2.3 Key Indices — KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> NASDAQ does NOT have a single index; it publishes **multiple indices** that track different segments. The two most cited are:

#### A. NASDAQ Composite Index (Ticker: ^IXIC)

The **NASDAQ Composite** includes **all common stocks listed on the NASDAQ exchange** (across all three tiers). It is a **market capitalization-weighted index**.

$$\text{Index Value} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i \times Q_i)}{\text{Base Divisor}}$$

Where:
- $P_i$ = Price of stock $i$
- $Q_i$ = Number of shares of stock $i$
- Base Divisor = Calibration constant to maintain continuity after splits
- $n$ = Total number of listed stocks (currently > **3,000**)

#### B. NASDAQ-100 Index (Ticker: ^NDX)

The **NASDAQ-100** contains the **100 largest non-financial companies** listed on NASDAQ. It is **market-cap weighted** but uses a **modified capitalization method** with capped weights (largest company capped at **24%** as of 2023 SEC rules).

> **Key Distinction (Examiner's Trap):** NASDAQ-100 excludes financial companies. NASDAQ Composite does not.

### 2.4 Bid-Ask Spread — The Core Microstructure Metric

$$\text{Bid-Ask Spread} = \text{Ask Price} - \text{Bid Price}$$

$$\text{Spread (in \%)} = \frac{\text{Ask} - \text{Bid}}{\text{Ask}} \times 100$$

> **Real-World Utility:** On NASDAQ, retail investors dealing in liquid tech stocks (e.g., AAPL) see spreads of **USD 0.01–0.05**. Illiquid small-caps can have spreads of **USD 0.50 or more**.

### 2.5 Why NASDAQ is Important in Engineering Economy

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU B.Tech students (especially those in management electives), NASDAQ matters because:
> 1. **Tech IPOs:** Most global tech IPOs (Initial Public Offerings) list on NASDAQ — e.g., Facebook (2012), Alphabet (2004), Tesla (2010).
> 2. **Currency of Innovation:** The **NASDAQ-100** is a proxy for the **global technology economy**.
> 3. **Benchmark for Indian Markets:** Indian IT companies (Infosys, TCS, Wipro) are often benchmarked against NASDAQ-listed peers.
> 4. **Investment Vehicles:** QQQ ETF (Invesco) tracks NASDAQ-100 and is one of the most traded ETFs globally.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: NASDAQ Composite Index Calculation (Conceptual)

> [!NOTE]
> The official NASDAQ Composite uses a **modified market-cap weighting**. Below is an **illustrative 3-stock academic example** to demonstrate the calculation logic.

**Given Data (Hypothetical NASDAQ-3 Sample):**

| Stock | Price (USD) | Shares Outstanding (Millions) |
|---|---|---|
| AAPL (Apple) | 200 | 15,500 |
| MSFT (Microsoft) | 400 | 7,400 |
| AMZN (Amazon) | 180 | 10,500 |

**Step 1: Calculate Market Capitalization of each stock**

$$M_{\text{AAPL}} = 200 \times 15{,}500 = 3{,}100{,}000 \text{ million USD}$$

$$M_{\text{MSFT}} = 400 \times 7{,}400 = 2{,}960{,}000 \text{ million USD}$$

$$M_{\text{AMZN}} = 180 \times 10{,}500 = 1{,}890{,}000 \text{ million USD}$$

**Step 2: Sum Total Market Capitalization**

$$\sum M = 3{,}100{,}000 + 2{,}960{,}000 + 1{,}890{,}000 = 7{,}950{,}000 \text{ million USD}$$

**Step 3: Apply the Index Formula**

$$\text{Index} = \frac{\sum M}{\text{Base Divisor}}$$

> **Step 4: Logic explanation**
> The Base Divisor is set during the base period (1971) and adjusted for stock splits, dividends, and constituent changes. The actual real-world NASDAQ Composite divisor is maintained by **FTSE Russell** (which took over from OMX) and is in the range of **~8,000–10,000** as of recent recalibrations.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Bid-Ask Spread Numerical Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> A KTU-style numerical problem: a market maker quotes a NASDAQ-listed stock.

**Given:**
- Bid Price = USD 49.95
- Ask Price = USD 50.05
- You buy 100 shares at the Ask and immediately sell at the Bid.

**Step 1: Calculate the spread per share**

$$\text{Spread} = 50.05 - 49.95 = \text{USD } 0.10$$

**Step 2: Calculate total loss for the round-trip trade (100 shares)**

$$\text{Total Loss} = 0.10 \times 100 = \text{USD } 10.00$$

**Step 3: Calculate percentage spread**

$$\text{Spread \%} = \frac{0.10}{50.05} \times 100 = 0.1998\%$$

**Step 4: Interpretation**

> This is the **liquidity cost** the market maker earns. A tight spread (e.g., 0.01%) indicates a **highly liquid stock**; a wide spread (e.g., 5%+) indicates a **thinly traded stock**. NASDAQ, being electronic, generally has tighter spreads than OTC markets.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: NASDAQ vs NYSE Comparative Analysis (Tabular Reasoning)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A common 7-mark KTU question is to **compare NASDAQ with NYSE**. Here is a structured analytical table:

| Parameter | NASDAQ | NYSE |
|---|---|---|
| Year Founded | **1971** | **1792** |
| Trading Method | Fully Electronic (Screen-Based) | Hybrid (Open Outcry + Electronic) |
| Market Maker | Multiple dealers per stock | Specialist (Designated Market Maker) |
| Speed of Execution | Milliseconds (Algo) | Hybrid: human + algo |
| Companies Listed | Tech-heavy: Apple, Microsoft | Traditional: Coca-Cola, JPMorgan |
| Listing Tiers | 3 tiers (Select/Global/Capital) | 1 tier (NYSE) |
| Regulation | SEC + Nasdaq Inc. | SEC + NYSE Group |
| Cost of Listing | Lower (USD 75,000–1M range) | Higher (USD 1M+ for IPOs) |
| Acronym Expansion | National Assoc. of Sec. Dealers Automated Quotations | New York Stock Exchange |

### 3.4 Worked Example 4: Weight Cap Calculation for NASDAQ-100

> **Examiner's Note:** Post-2023, NASDAQ-100 imposes a **24% individual weight cap** and **48% aggregate non-financial weight cap** on the top 5 companies to prevent single-stock dominance.

**Hypothetical 4-stock index calculation:**

$$\text{Weight of stock}_i = \frac{P_i \times Q_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P_j \times Q_j} \times 100$$

If AAPL = 28% and MSFT = 25% in raw weights, then:
- AAPL is **capped at 24%**, with 4% redistributed to other constituents.
- This prevents index funds (like QQQ) from being overly exposed to one stock.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: NASDAQ Trade Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Investor Places Order via Broker Terminal] --> B[Order Routed to Nasdaq Execution System NES]
    B --> C{Order Size Check}
    C -->|Small Order| D[Small Order Execution System SOES]
    C -->|Large Order| E[Super Display Book SDBK]
    D --> F[Market Maker Quotes Reviewed]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Best Bid and Best Ask Identified]
    G --> H[Trade Executed Electronically]
    H --> I[Trade Reported to Consolidated Tape]
    I --> J[Settlement via DTCC T+1]
    J --> K[Trade Confirmation to Investor]
```

### 4.2 Architecture Diagram: NASDAQ's Three-Tier Listing Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NASDAQ[Nasdaq Exchange] --> T1[Tier 1: Global Select Market]
    NASDAQ --> T2[Tier 2: Global Market]
    NASDAQ --> T3[Tier 3: Capital Market]
    T1 --> T1C[Apple, Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, Meta]
    T2 --> T2C[Mid-cap growth firms, USD 75M float]
    T3 --> T3C[Small-cap and emerging companies]
    NASDAQ --> IDX1[Index: NASDAQ Composite IXIC]
    NASDAQ --> IDX2[Index: NASDAQ-100 NDX]
    NASDAQ --> IDX3[Index: NASDAQ Biotechnology NBI]
    NASDAQ --> IDX4[Index: PHLX Semiconductor SOX]
```

### 4.3 Process Diagram: How a NASDAQ Index is Rebalanced

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Annual Reconstitution Review by FTSE Russell] --> B[Eligibility Criteria Check]
    B --> C{Meets NASDAQ-100 Criteria?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Add to Index]
    C -->|No| E[Remove from Index]
    D --> F[Weight Calculation - Market Cap]
    F --> G{Weight Exceeds 24% Cap?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Cap Applied - Excess Redistributed]
    G -->|No| I[Index Reweighted]
    H --> I
    E --> J[Replaced by Next Ranked Stock]
    I --> K[New Index Published]
    J --> K
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture: NASDAQ Market Structure

```mermaid

    subgraph Regulators
        R1[SEC - Securities and Exchange Commission]
        R2[FINRA - Financial Industry Regulatory Authority]
    end
    subgraph Operators
        O1[Nasdaq Inc. - Exchange Operator]
        O2[FTSE Russell - Index Compiler]
        O3[DTCC - Clearing and Settlement]
    end
    subgraph Participants
        P1[Retail Investors]
        P2[Institutional Investors]
        P3[Market Makers - Citadel, Virtu]
        P4[Broker-Dealers]
    end
    R1 --> O1
    R2 --> O1
    O1 --> P4
    P1 --> P4
    P2 --> P4
    P4 --> P3
    P3 --> O1
```
