```mermaid
    O1 --> O3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

---

**Q1. Define NASDAQ and expand the acronym. Mention the year it was founded.** [3 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
NASDAQ stands for **National Association of Securities Dealers Automated Quotations**. It is a fully electronic, dealer-based stock exchange headquartered in New York, USA, founded in **1971**. It is currently the second-largest stock exchange in the world by market capitalization and is operated by **Nasdaq, Inc.**

---

**Q2. Differentiate between NASDAQ Composite and NASDAQ-100 index.** [3 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | NASDAQ Composite | NASDAQ-100 |
|---|---|---|
| Constituents | All common stocks listed on NASDAQ (~3,000+) | Top 100 non-financial companies |
| Financial Stocks | Included | Excluded |
| Weighting | Market-cap weighted | Modified market-cap with weight caps |
| Ticker | ^IXIC | ^NDX |
| Use Case | Broad market barometer | Tech-sector proxy |

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**Q3 (a)** Explain the **organizational structure of NASDAQ**. Discuss the **three-tier listing system** in detail with eligibility criteria for each tier. [7 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

NASDAQ is operated by **Nasdaq, Inc.**, a holding company that owns and operates the exchange, regulates market makers, and maintains listed company compliance. It uses a **dealer market** model where multiple market makers compete to provide liquidity.

The **three-tier listing system** is:

**1. NASDAQ Global Select Market (Tier 1):** Highest tier with stringent financial, liquidity, and corporate governance standards. Required: **USD 1.1 million public float**, **EBITDA ≥ USD 15 million** (or revenue ≥ USD 110M for tech firms), and **4 years of operating history**.

**2. NASDAQ Global Market (Tier 2):** Mid-tier for established companies. Requirements: **USD 75 million public float** OR total assets of same amount, OR **USD 50M revenue + USD 50M float**.

**3. NASDAQ Capital Market (Tier 3):** Entry-level tier for smaller, emerging companies. Minimum: **USD 5 million stockholders' equity**, **USD 1 million public float**, and **USD 15 million total assets** OR **USD 50M revenue** (alternative path).

> **Examination Tip:** Students often confuse Tier 2 and Tier 3 thresholds. Memorize the **USD 5M equity** for Capital Market — that is the most tested figure.

---

**Q3 (b)** Describe the **trading mechanism of NASDAQ**. Explain the role of market makers and the **bid-ask spread** in price discovery. [7 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

NASDAQ operates **without a physical trading floor**. All trades are executed electronically through the **Nasdaq Execution System (NES)** built on the **INET platform**.

**Role of Market Makers:**
- Each listed stock has **multiple registered market makers** (broker-dealers) who continuously quote **bid** (buy) and **ask** (sell) prices.
- They provide **liquidity** and **price continuity**.
- Examples: Citadel Securities, Virtu Financial, GTS Securities.

**Bid-Ask Spread Mechanism:**

$$\text{Spread} = P_{\text{ask}} - P_{\text{bid}}$$

The market maker buys at the bid and sells at the ask, profiting from the spread. A **tighter spread** indicates **higher liquidity** and **lower transaction cost** for investors. For example, AAPL typically has a spread of USD 0.01, while a small-cap biotech may have a spread of USD 0.50 or more.

**Order Execution Process:**
1. Order received via broker.
2. Routed to SOES (small orders) or SDBK (large orders).
3. Best bid/ask matched.
4. Trade executed in **milliseconds**.
5. Settled via DTCC on **T+1 basis** (post-2024).

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q4 (a)** What are the **major indices published by NASDAQ**? Explain the methodology of **NASDAQ-100** with a numerical example of weight calculation. [7 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Major NASDAQ indices include:
- **NASDAQ Composite (^IXIC)** — all listed stocks.
- **NASDAQ-100 (^NDX)** — top 100 non-financial.
- **NASDAQ Biotechnology (^NBI)** — biotech sector.
- **PHLX Semiconductor (^SOX)** — semiconductor sector.

**NASDAQ-100 Methodology:**
- **Universe:** Top 100 non-financial companies by market cap.
- **Weighting:** Modified market-cap with **24% individual cap** and **48% aggregate top-5 cap**.
- **Rebalancing:** Annual in December, with quarterly review.
- **Calculation:**

$$\text{Index}_t = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{100} (P_{i,t} \times Q_{i,t})}{\text{Divisor}}$$

**Numerical Example:** Suppose the top 3 stocks are AAPL (USD 3T cap), MSFT (USD 2.8T), AMZN (USD 1.7T). Total weight = USD 7.5T.

$$\text{Weight}_{\text{AAPL}} = \frac{3.0}{7.5} \times 100 = 40\%$$

Since 40% > 24% cap, AAPL weight is **truncated to 24%**, and 16% is redistributed pro-rata to MSFT and AMZN.

---

**Q4 (b)** Compare and contrast **NASDAQ with NYSE** across at least **six parameters**. Why is NASDAQ preferred for tech IPOs? [7 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | NASDAQ | NYSE |
|---|---|---|
| **Founding** | 1971 | 1792 (Buttonwood Agreement) |
| **Trading Style** | Fully electronic | Hybrid (open outcry + electronic) |
| **Market Maker** | Multiple competing dealers | Single Designated Market Maker (DMM) |
| **Listing Cost** | Lower (USD 75K–500K) | Higher (USD 1M+) |
| **Sector Bias** | Technology, biotech, growth | Banking, energy, industrial |
| **Execution Speed** | Milliseconds (algorithm-friendly) | Slightly slower (human element) |
| **Regulation** | SEC + FINRA + Nasdaq Inc. | SEC + FINRA + NYSE Group |

**Why NASDAQ is Preferred for Tech IPOs:**
1. **Lower listing fees** suit high-growth, cash-constrained startups.
2. **Electronic execution** matches the algorithmic nature of tech.
3. **Brand association** with innovation (Apple, Google, Amazon effect).
4. **Tier 1 (Global Select)** offers credibility while allowing flexible requirements.
5. **Faster IPO process** compared to NYSE's traditional procedures.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Acronym Mistake:** Writing "Nasdaq" as "NASDQ" or omitting the "Q" for "Quotations" — costs **0.5–1 mark**.
> 2. **Founding Year Confusion:** NASDAQ was founded in **1971**; NYSE in **1792**. Mixing these costs marks.
> 3. **Index Confusion:** Students often say "NASDAQ has only one index" — wrong. It has **multiple indices** (Composite, 100, Biotech, Semiconductor).
> 4. **Market Maker Misconception:** Some students say "NASDAQ has specialists" — incorrect. NYSE has **DMMs (Designated Market Makers)**; NASDAQ has **multiple competing market makers**.
> 5. **Listing Tiers Mix-up:** Tier 1 = Global Select (highest), Tier 3 = Capital Market (lowest). Reversing this order is a common error.
> 6. **Sketching the Trade Flow:** A simple flow diagram (Investor → Broker → System → Market Maker → Settlement) is mandatory for full marks in 7-mark mechanism questions. Skipping it costs **2 marks**.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Remember High-Yield Points for KTU Board Exam:**

- NASDAQ = **National Association of Securities Dealers Automated Quotations**
- Founded: **1971** (world's first electronic stock exchange)
- Headquartered: **New York City**
- Operated by: **Nasdaq, Inc.**
- **3 Listing Tiers:** Global Select (Tier 1), Global Market (Tier 2), Capital Market (Tier 3)
- **Major Indices:** NASDAQ Composite (^IXIC), NASDAQ-100 (^NDX)
- NASDAQ-100 excludes **financial companies**; Composite does not
- **Weight cap on NASDAQ-100:** 24% individual, 48% top-5 aggregate
- Trading is **fully electronic** — no physical trading floor
- **Multiple market makers** per stock (dealer system)
- **Settlement:** T+1 (post-2024 SEC mandate)
- NASDAQ is the **2nd largest exchange globally** by market cap
- Became a **registered exchange in 2006**
- **Renamed to Nasdaq, Inc.** in 2015 (from NASDAQ OMX Group)
- Major listed firms: Apple, Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, Meta, Tesla, Nvidia
- Key difference from NYSE: NASDAQ is **dealer-based**; NYSE is **auction-based with specialists**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trading and Settlement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Trading and Settlement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Trading — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trading** in a financial market refers to the **buying and selling of financial securities** (equities, derivatives, bonds, currencies, commodities) on a recognized exchange or an over-the-counter (OTC) platform, governed by a transparent set of **rules, order-matching rules, price-time priority**, and **surveillance mechanisms** prescribed by the Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI).

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, trading is not merely a transaction — it is a **structured economic event** that contains four mandatory sub-processes:

1. **Order Entry** — Investor places buy/sell order with a broker.
2. **Order Routing** — Broker routes the order to the exchange.
3. **Order Matching** — Exchange engine matches buy/sell orders.
4. **Trade Confirmation** — Trade is confirmed and forwarded for clearing & settlement.

## 1.2 Settlement — The Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Settlement** is the **final, irrevocable transfer of securities** from the seller's demat account to the buyer's demat account, accompanied by the **corresponding transfer of funds** from the buyer to the seller, completing the trade legally and operationally.

In India, after SEBI's reforms (effective **January 2023**), the equity cash market follows a **T+1 rolling settlement cycle** — meaning trades executed on Day **T** are settled on the next working day (**T+1**).

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Vegetable Market Model

Imagine a regulated vegetable mandi (market):

- **Trading** is like shouting out "I want 10 kg tomatoes at ₹30/kg" and another vendor shouting "I have 10 kg at ₹30" — the moment both **agree on price and quantity**, a **trade happens**.
- **Settlement** is the **actual exchange** — the vendor hands over the tomatoes and the buyer hands over the cash. Until this physical handover, the deal is only a promise, not a closed deal.

Similarly, in capital markets:

- **Trade execution** = matching of buy & sell orders at an agreed price.
- **Settlement** = actual movement of shares and money.

## 1.4 Key Entities in Trading & Settlement

| Entity | Role |
|---|---|
| **BSE / NSE** | Stock exchanges — provide trading platform |
| **SEBI** | Market regulator |
| **NSCCL** (National Securities Clearing Corporation Ltd) | Clearing & settlement for NSE trades |
| **ICCL** (Indian Clearing Corporation Ltd) | Clearing house for BSE |
| **Depositories (NSDL / CDSL)** | Hold securities in **electronic (demat) form** |
| **Trading Member / Stock Broker** | Intermediary executing orders |
| **Custodian** | Holds funds/securities on behalf of FPIs |

## 1.5 Order Types — The Building Blocks of Trading

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Understanding **order types** is critical for KTU exams — this is a high-frequency short-answer topic.

- **Market Order** — Executed immediately at the best available price.
- **Limit Order** — Executed only at a specified price or better.
- **Stop-Loss Order** — Becomes a market order once a trigger price is hit.
- **Good-Till-Cancelled (GTC)** — Remains active until cancelled.
- **Immediate-or-Cancel (IOC)** — Must execute immediately, partially or fully; rest cancelled.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Trade Life Cycle — Step-by-Step Logic

The complete **trade life cycle** in Indian securities markets can be broken down into **eight sequential stages**:

1. **Investor Decision** — Client decides to buy/sell a security.
2. **Order Placement** — Client places order with the broker (online/offline).
3. **Order Validation by Broker** — Broker checks KYC, margin, and risk limits.
4. **Order Transmission to Exchange** — Sent via the broker's trading terminal to NSE/BSE.
5. **Order Matching** — Exchange's matching engine uses **price-time priority**.
6. **Trade Execution** — Trade is generated with a unique **trade number**.
7. **Trade Reporting to Clearing Corporation** — NSCCL/ICCL receives the trade.
8. **Clearing & Settlement** — Net positions are computed and settled on T+1.

> [!NOTE]
> **Price-Time Priority** means: among all buy orders, the highest price gets matched first; among all sell orders, the lowest price gets matched first. If prices are equal, the order that came **earlier in time** gets matched first.

## 2.2 Clearing — The Risk Management Layer

**Clearing** is the process that sits *between* trade execution and settlement. Its jobs are:

- **Novation** — The clearing corporation becomes the **central counterparty (CCP)** to every trade, replacing the original buyer and seller. This eliminates counterparty risk.
- **Netting** — Calculates the **net obligation** of each member. If a member has 10 buy trades and 7 sell trades of the same stock, only **3 net units** are owed.
- **Margin Computation** — Calculates initial margin, exposure margin, and mark-to-market margin.
- **Risk Management** — Maintains a **Settlement Guarantee Fund (SGF)** and **Core SGF** to absorb defaults.

## 2.3 Settlement Cycle Evolution in India

| Period | Settlement Cycle |
|---|---|
| Pre-2001 | Weekly / Fortnightly settlement |
| 2001–2002 | T+3 rolling settlement |
| 2002–2003 | T+2 rolling settlement |
| 2003–2022 | T+2 rolling settlement (extended era) |
| **Jan 2023 onwards** | **T+1 rolling settlement** (current) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **T+1** was introduced in a phased manner starting **January 27, 2022** for select stocks, and made **universal** from **January 20, 2023** by SEBI.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Net Settlement Quantity | $Q_{net} = \sum Q_{buy} - \sum Q_{sell}$ | Computes delivery obligation per scrip |
| Net Settlement Value | $V_{net} = Q_{net} \times P_{settle}$ | Cash leg of the transaction |
| Pay-in / Pay-out | Funds \& securities delivered on **T+1** by a fixed time | Operational deadline |
| Mark-to-Market (MTM) | $MTM = (P_{today} - P_{trade}) \times Q$ | Daily settlement of derivative contracts |
| Initial Margin (SPAN) | $IM = \max(Loss_{portfolio})$ at 99% VaR | Risk-based margin for F\&O |
| Settlement Price | $P_{settle} = \frac{\sum P_{i} \times Q_{i}}{\sum Q_{i}}$ (volume-weighted) | Closing / settlement price |
| Auction Price Penalty | $P_{penalty} = P_{settlement} \pm k\% \cdot P_{settlement}$ | Charged on shortages in delivery |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing tables in markdown, **never** use the pipe character `\vert` inside cells — use LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the table structure.

## 2.5 Why Trading & Settlement Matter in Engineering / Management Contexts

Even though this is a finance course, engineering students must appreciate that **modern trading systems** are essentially **high-performance distributed computing problems**:

- The NSE matching engine handles **millions of orders per second** with **microsecond latency**.
- Risk systems process real-time **portfolio-level VaR computations**.
- Depositories (NSDL/CDSL) manage **Demat accounts** as **distributed ledgers**.
- The shift from T+2 to T+1 required massive **technology upgradation** across the entire stack.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Worked Example — Net Settlement Calculation

### Problem
A trader executes the following trades in RELIANCE on Day **T**:

| Trade | Side | Quantity | Price (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Buy | 100 | 2,400 |
| 2 | Sell | 60 | 2,405 |
| 3 | Buy | 50 | 2,398 |
| 4 | Sell | 40 | 2,402 |
| 5 | Buy | 75 | 2,410 |

Compute the **net obligation** and **net settlement value**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute total buy quantity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{buy} &= Q_1 + Q_3 + Q_5 \\
Q_{buy} &= 100 + 50 + 75 \\
Q_{buy} &= 225 \text{ shares}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute total sell quantity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{sell} &= Q_2 + Q_4 \\
Q_{sell} &= 60 + 40 \\
Q_{sell} &= 100 \text{ shares}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute net quantity obligation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{net} &= Q_{buy} - Q_{sell} \\
Q_{net} &= 225 - 100 \\
Q_{net} &= 125 \text{ shares (to be delivered to the trader)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $Q_{net} > 0$, the trader must **receive 125 shares** on settlement day.

**Step 4: Compute gross trade values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{buy,gross} &= (100 \times 2400) + (50 \times 2398) + (75 \times 2410) \\
V_{buy,gross} &= 240000 + 119900 + 180750 \\
V_{buy,gross} &= 540650 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{sell,gross} &= (60 \times 2405) + (40 \times 2402) \\
V_{sell,gross} &= 144300 + 96080 \\
V_{sell,gross} &= 240380 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute net cash obligation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{net,cash} &= V_{buy,gross} - V_{sell,gross} \\
V_{net,cash} &= 540650 - 240380 \\
V_{net,cash} &= 300270 \text{ ₹ (cash to be paid by the trader)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Settlement Statement for the Trader:**

| Obligation | Direction | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Shares | Receive 125 RELIANCE shares | 125 |
| Cash | Pay ₹3,00,270 | ₹3,00,270 |
| Settlement Day | T+1 (next working day) | — |

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — T+1 vs T+2 vs T+5 Settlement

| Parameter | T+5 (Old Era) | T+2 (Pre-2023) | T+1 (Current) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capital Lock-up | Highest | Moderate | Lowest |
| Counterparty Risk Exposure | Very High | Moderate | Very Low |
| Liquidity Reuse Speed | Slow | Faster | Fastest |
| Operational Cost for Brokers | High | Medium | Low (after tech upgrade) |
| Margin Requirement | High | Reduced | Further Reduced |
| Global Benchmark Status | Outdated | Acceptable | Aligned with global best practice |
| Implementation Date (India) | Pre-2002 | 2003–2022 | **January 2023** |

## 3.3 Derivative Settlement — Daily MTM Walk-through

**Concept:** In Futures \& Options (F\&O), settlement is **two-fold**:

1. **Daily Mark-to-Market (MTM)** — Every day, profits/losses are settled in **cash**.
2. **Final Settlement on Expiry** — Options may be settled in cash; futures in cash.

### MTM Formula Derivation

For a long futures position:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTM_{day} &= (P_{close,today} - P_{close,yesterday}) \times Q \\
MTM_{cum} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} MTM_{i} \\
\text{Total Profit} &= MTM_{cum} + (P_{final} - P_{initial}) \times Q
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:**

A trader buys 1 lot of NIFTY futures (Qty = 25) at ₹20,000.

| Day | Closing Price (₹) | Daily MTM (₹) | Cumulative (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Day 0 (Entry) | 20,000 | — | 0 |
| Day 1 | 20,150 | 25 × 150 = **+3,750** | +3,750 |
| Day 2 | 19,980 | 25 × (-170) = **-4,250** | -500 |
| Day 3 | 20,100 | 25 × 120 = **+3,000** | +2,500 |

Each day's MTM is **settled in cash by the end of Day T+1** via the clearing corporation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete Trading & Settlement Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Investor places order with Broker] --> B{Broker validates order}
    B -->|KYC OK, Margin OK| C[Order routed to Exchange NSE or BSE]
    B -->|Rejected| B1[Order rejected to client]
    C --> D[Exchange Matching Engine]
    D --> E{Price Time Priority Match Found?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Trade Generated Trade Number Assigned]
    E -->|No| G[Order placed in Order Book]
    G -->|Price moves| D
    F --> H[Trade reported to Clearing Corp NSCCL or ICCL]
    H --> I[Clearing: Novation and Netting]
    I --> J[Margin Computation and Risk Check]
    J --> K[Member Pay-in on T plus 1 by 10 30 AM]
    K --> L[Clearing Corp Pay-out to Members]
    L --> M[Securities delivered to Buyer Demat via NSDL or CDSL]
    L --> N[Funds credited to Seller Bank Account]
    M --> O[Trade Fully Settled on T plus 1]
    N --> O
```

## 4.2 Entity Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Regulator_Layer
        R1[SEBI Market Regulator]
    end

    subgraph Trading_Layer
        T1[NSE Trading Platform]
        T2[BSE Trading Platform]
    end

    subgraph Clearing_Layer
        C1[NSCCL Clearing House]
        C2[ICCL Clearing House]
    end

    subgraph Settlement_Layer
        S1[NSDL Depository]
        S2[CDSL Depository]
        S3[Bank for Cash Settlement]
    end

    R1 --> T1
    R1 --> T2
    R1 --> C1
    R1 --> C2
    R1 --> S1
    R1 --> S2

    T1 --> C1
    T2 --> C2

    C1 --> S1
    C1 --> S3
    C2 --> S2
    C2 --> S3
```

## 4.3 Order Matching Logic — Price-Time Priority

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[New Buy or Sell Order arrives] --> check1{Is there a matching opposite order?}
    check1 -->|No| place[Place in Order Book]
    check1 -->|Yes| pricecheck{Higher buy price vs Lower sell price?}
    pricecheck -->|Match| timecheck{Which order came first in time?}
    pricecheck -->|No match| place
    timecheck -->|First order| execute[Execute Trade]
    timecheck -->|Second order remains| place
    execute --> notify[Generate Trade Confirmation]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**What is meant by rolling settlement? State the current settlement cycle applicable to the Indian equity cash market.**

**Model Answer:**

> Rolling settlement is a system in which trades executed on any given trading day are settled on a **fixed number of subsequent working days**, with the settlement cycle continuing every day (rolling forward). Unlike the older periodic settlement system (weekly/fortnightly), rolling settlement ensures continuous settlement of all trades.

> As per SEBI's directive effective **January 20, 2023**, the Indian equity cash market follows a **T+1 rolling settlement** — meaning trades executed on Day **T** are mandatorily settled on the **next working day (T+1)**.

> *Key benefits:* reduced counterparty risk, faster liquidity reuse, lower margin requirement, and better alignment with global best practices.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Explain the role of a Clearing Corporation in the settlement process.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Clearing Corporation** (e.g., NSCCL for NSE, ICCL for BSE) acts as the **Central Counterparty (CCP)** between every buyer and seller. Its functions include:

> 1. **Novation** — It substitutes itself as the counterparty to every trade, eliminating default risk between the original parties.
> 2. **Netting** — It computes the **net obligation** of each member by offsetting buys against sells in the same scrip.
> 3. **Margin Collection** — It collects initial margin, exposure margin, and MTM margin from members.
> 4. **Settlement Guarantee** — It guarantees settlement through its **Settlement Guarantee Fund (SGF)** and **Core SGF**, even if a member defaults.
> 5. **Funds \& Securities Pay-in/Pay-out** — It coordinates the actual movement of money and shares.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B contains an **internal choice**. Below are **Question A** and **Question B** — students answer **either** one full set.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **complete trade life cycle** in the Indian securities market, listing all entities involved and the **functions of each entity**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **T+1, T+2, and weekly settlement** systems. Discuss the **advantages of T+1 settlement** from the perspective of investors, brokers, and the market regulator. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Trade Life Cycle:**

The Indian securities market trade life cycle consists of the following **eight sequential stages**:

1. **Investor Decision & Order Placement:** The client decides to buy/sell and places the order through a registered stock broker — via internet trading platform, telephone, or physical order slip.

2. **Broker Validation:** The trading member verifies the client's KYC compliance, available margin, and risk limits before accepting the order.

3. **Order Routing:** The broker enters the order into his **Trading Member Terminal (TMT)**, which routes the order to the relevant exchange (NSE/BSE) through a secure **VSAT / Leased Line** network.

4. **Order Matching at Exchange:** The exchange's **Matching Engine** receives the order and matches it using the **Price-Time Priority** rule. Each order gets a unique **Order Number**.

5. **Trade Execution:** Upon a successful match, a **Trade** is generated with a unique **Trade Number** containing details — symbol, quantity, price, buyer/seller member ID, and trade timestamp.

6. **Trade Reporting to Clearing Corp:** The trade is instantaneously forwarded to the **Clearing Corporation (NSCCL/ICCL)**.

7. **Clearing & Margin Computation:** The clearing corporation computes **net positions**, **MTM**, and **margin obligations** for each member. **Novation** is executed — the CCP becomes counterparty.

8. **Settlement on T+1:** Members complete **Pay-in** of funds and securities by a fixed cutoff (typically 10:30 AM on T+1). The clearing corporation then executes **Pay-out** — securities delivered to buyer's demat account, funds credited to seller's bank account.

**Entities & Their Roles:**

- **Investor / Client** — Originator of the trade.
- **Stock Broker / Trading Member** — Intermediary executing the trade.
- **Exchange (NSE / BSE)** — Provides trading platform and matching engine.
- **Clearing Corporation (NSCCL / ICCL)** — CCP, netting, risk management.
- **Depositories (NSDL / CDSL)** — Hold securities electronically in demat accounts.
- **Settlement Banks** — Handle cash leg of the settlement.
- **SEBI** — Regulator overseeing the entire process.

**(b) T+1 vs T+2 vs Weekly Settlement:**

| Parameter | Weekly | T+2 | T+1 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Settlement Days | 5–7 days | 2 days | 1 day |
| Risk Window | Very High | Moderate | Lowest |
| Capital Lock-up | Highest | Medium | Lowest |
| Implementation Era | Pre-2001 | 2003–2022 | 2023–present |

**Advantages of T+1 Settlement:**

*For Investors:*
- Faster access to shares purchased — can be sold the next day.
- Reduced opportunity cost; quicker liquidity reuse.
- Lower margin blocked with broker.

*For Brokers:*
- Lower settlement risk exposure.
- Reduced margin requirements.
- Operational efficiency — fewer unsettled trades in books.

*For Regulator (SEBI):*
- Lower systemic risk in the market.
- Stronger alignment with **global best practice** (US, UK, Europe follow T+1 or T+2).
- Enhanced market integrity and investor protection.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — where students lose marks:**
> 1. Confusing **clearing** with **settlement** — they are *different* stages. Clearing = risk management + netting; Settlement = actual delivery of shares & funds.
> 2. Writing T+2 as the *current* cycle — it is **T+1** now.
> 3. Forgetting to mention **Depositories (NSDL/CDSL)** in the trade life cycle.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** What is **Novation**? How does it eliminate counterparty risk in the settlement process? Explain the **margin system** followed by Indian clearing corporations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A trader executes the following trades in **TCS** stock on Day T:

| Trade | Buy / Sell | Quantity | Price (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Buy | 200 | 3,800 |
| 2 | Buy | 150 | 3,810 |
| 3 | Sell | 100 | 3,820 |
| 4 | Sell | 120 | 3,795 |
| 5 | Buy | 80 | 3,805 |

Compute: **(i)** Net share obligation, **(ii)** Net cash obligation, **(iii)** When and how settlement will occur. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Novation & Margin System:**

**Novation** is the legal process by which the **Clearing Corporation interposes itself** between the original buyer and seller of every trade. After novation:

- The original contract between Buyer M1 and Seller M2 is replaced.
- Two new contracts are created: Buyer ↔ CCP and Seller ↔ CCP.
- The CCP becomes the **guarantor** of every trade.

This eliminates **counterparty risk** because even if the original buyer or seller defaults, the CCP ensures the other party receives its shares/funds.

**Margin System in India:**

The Indian clearing corporations (NSCCL/ICCL) follow a **risk-based, multi-tier margin system**:

1. **Initial Margin (IM)** — Collected up-front on all open positions. Computed using **SPAN (Standard Portfolio Analysis of Risk)** or **VaR-based** methodology to cover 99% of potential 1-day losses.
2. **Extreme Loss Margin (ELM)** — Additional margin (typically 1.5%–3%) to cover extreme market movements.
3. **Mark-to-Market (MTM) Margin** — Settled **daily in cash** based on daily price movement.
4. **Delivery Margin** — Charged on the net open position intended for delivery.
5. **Concentration Margin** — Additional margin on highly concentrated positions.

Margins can be paid in **cash, bank guarantees, fixed deposit receipts, or government securities**.

**(b) Numerical Solution for TCS Trades:**

**Step 1: Total Buy Quantity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{buy} &= 200 + 150 + 80 = 430 \text{ shares}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Total Sell Quantity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{sell} &= 100 + 120 = 220 \text{ shares}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Net Share Obligation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{net} &= Q_{buy} - Q_{sell} = 430 - 220 = 210 \text{ shares}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $Q_{net} > 0$, the trader **receives 210 TCS shares** in his demat account on settlement day.

**Step 4: Total Buy Value**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{buy} &= (200 \times 3800) + (150 \times 3810) + (80 \times 3805) \\
V_{buy} &= 760000 + 571500 + 304400 \\
V_{buy} &= 1635900 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Total Sell Value**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{sell} &= (100 \times 3820) + (120 \times 3795) \\
V_{sell} &= 382000 + 455400 \\
V_{sell} &= 837400 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Net Cash Obligation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{net,cash} &= V_{buy} - V_{sell} \\
V_{net,cash} &= 1635900 - 837400 \\
V_{net,cash} &= 798500 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

The trader must **pay ₹7,98,500** to receive 210 TCS shares.

**Step 7: Settlement Schedule**

- **Trade Day T:** All 5 trades executed.
- **T+1 (next working day):** Settlement occurs.
  - By **10:30 AM** — Pay-in (member delivers funds + 210 shares).
  - By **2:00 PM** — Pay-out (clearing corporation delivers 210 shares to demat, credits ₹7,98,500 to seller's bank account).
- **Settlement Type:** Rolling T+1 (compulsory).
- **Mode:** Electronic — securities via **NSDL/CDSL**, funds via **settlement bank (RBI-approved)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — where students lose marks in numerical questions:**
> 1. Forgetting to **net** quantities and values separately.
> 2. Not clearly stating **direction** of obligation (pay/receive).
> 3. Missing the **time and method** of settlement (T+1, electronic, demat + bank transfer).
> 4. Confusing share prices — using **trade price** vs **settlement price** incorrectly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Trading & Settlement**

- **Trading** = buy/sell orders matched on exchange using **Price-Time Priority**.
- **Order types**: Market, Limit, Stop-Loss, GTC, IOC, Bracket, Cover.
- **Clearing Corporation** acts as **Central Counterparty (CCP)** through **Novation**.
- **Netting** reduces multiple trades to a **single net obligation** per scrip per member.
- **Current Indian equity settlement cycle = T+1** (effective Jan 20, 2023).
- **T+1 settlement** was implemented in a phased manner starting Jan 27, 2022.
- **Settlement** = delivery of securities to demat (NSDL/CDSL) + credit of funds to bank.
- **Two clearing corporations** in India: **NSCCL** (for NSE) and **ICCL** (for BSE).
- **Margin system**: Initial Margin, ELM, MTM, Delivery Margin, Concentration Margin.
- **SPAN margin** = Value-at-Risk based 99% confidence, 1-day horizon.
- **Settlement Guarantee Fund (SGF)** = backstop in case of member default.
- **Trade Life Cycle** has **8 stages**: Investor → Broker → Exchange → Match → Trade → Clearing → Pay-in → Pay-out.
- **Derivatives** are settled via **daily MTM in cash** + final cash/expiry settlement.
- **Auction mechanism** is used for delivery shortages; penalty = **% of settlement price**.
- **Corporate actions** (dividends, splits) — buyer on **record date** gets the benefit, not the original seller.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open Outcry System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Open Outcry System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

The **Open Outcry System** is a traditional, floor-based method of price discovery and trade execution employed by organized exchanges (such as commodity futures exchanges and certain stock exchanges) in which traders—physically gathered in a designated area called a **"trading pit"** or **"ring"**—verbally announce their bids (buy prices) and offers (sell prices) through shouting, and concurrently use a standardized set of **hand signals** to communicate order quantity, price, and contract month to other participants in the pit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme, the Open Outcry System is studied as a *historical and comparative benchmark* against modern **Electronic Trading Systems (ETS)**. It is foundational for understanding the evolution of market microstructure.

The transactions are then executed under a strict set of exchange-defined **auction rules** (typically *double-auction* or *continuous double-auction mechanics*) that govern priority—**price priority followed by time priority**—for order matching.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **fish auction at a coastal harbor**:
- Buyers and sellers crowd into a circular space.
- The auctioneer shouts a starting price.
- Buyers shout higher numbers ("500!", "510!") and sellers counter ("520!").
- Hands fly up to signal quantity (e.g., ten fingers = 10 crates).
- The moment two traders agree on price and quantity, a deal is struck on the spot, recorded by clerks nearby.

The Open Outcry System works **exactly** like this — but for standardized financial contracts (futures, options) instead of fish. The "pit" is the trading arena; the "shouts" are bids/asks; the "hand signals" replace verbal confirmation of size and month to cut through the deafening noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric to Remember:** The world’s most famous open-outcry venue, the **Chicago Board of Trade (CBOT)** — founded in **1848** — operated exclusively on this system for over **160 years** before fully transitioning to electronic trading in **2015** (Globex platform).

### Core Terminology You Must Know

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Trading Pit / Ring** | Physical octagonal or circular stepped area on exchange floor |
| **Floor Trader (Local)** | Independent trader working for own account |
| **Broker / Floor Broker** | Executes orders on behalf of clients |
| **Hand Signals** | Finger gestures for price (palm), month (fingers), quantity (repeats) |
| **Outcry** | The collective verbal shouting of bids/offers |
| **Pit Committee** | Exchange officials overseeing fair practice |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Six Logical Steps

The Open Outcry System operates through a deterministic sequence:

1. **Physical Aggregation**: Buyers and sellers gather around a specific pit designated for a particular contract (e.g., wheat futures, T-bonds).
2. **Public Quotation**: A trader (often the *market maker* or *local*) shouts out the prevailing bid and offer — e.g., *"Bid 102.05, offered 102.10!"*
3. **Hand Signal Communication**: Simultaneously, traders flash standardized hand signs to indicate *quantity* and *delivery month*, because verbal communication alone is impossible amid the noise.
4. **Outcry & Counter-Outcry**: Other participants either improve the quote (raise bid / lower offer) or accept it by shouting *"Mine!"* or similar affirmation.
5. **Trade Confirmation**: A verbal agreement is binding. Exchange clerks ("runners" or "board boys") record the trade on card slips which are then entered into the clearing system.
6. **Clearing & Settlement**: The exchange's clearinghouse (e.g., **CME Group Clearing**, **LCH**) becomes the counterparty to both sides (a *novation*), guaranteeing performance.

### 2.2 Why the System Worked — The "Why" Behind the Mechanism

- **Why shouting?** Liquidity in futures markets requires *immediate price discovery* with many participants responding to the same quote simultaneously.
- **Why hand signals?** With multiple traders shouting in the same pit, verbal-only communication leads to ambiguity. Hand signals create a *parallel visual channel*.
- **Why a pit?** The circular/stepped geometry ensures that all participants can **see** each other's signals and hear each other's quotes — a primitive form of *high-bandwidth parallel processing*.
- **Why a clearinghouse?** Counterparty risk must be eliminated for the market to function. Novation transfers risk from the trader to the clearinghouse.

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since Open Outcry is a *qualitative / structural* topic, the "formulas" below represent the **decision rules, signals, and comparative metrics** used in KTU exam answers — not algebraic equations.

| Element | Rule / Standard | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Order Matching Priority** | **Price → Time** | Highest bid / Lowest offer matched first; then earliest time |
| **Bid (Bid Price)** | Price at which a trader **buys** | Symbol: $P_b$ |
| **Ask (Offer Price)** | Price at which a trader **sells** | Symbol: $P_a$ |
| **Bid-Ask Spread** | $\text{Spread} = P_a - P_b$ | Must be **> 0** in a normal market |
| **Hand Signal: Price** | **Palm facing in/out** | Fingers indicate price increments |
| **Hand Signal: Month** | **Fingers on other hand** | Jan = 1, Feb = 2 ... Dec = 12 |
| **Hand Signal: Quantity** | **Repeated gestures** | Each full motion = number of contracts |
| **Auction Type** | Continuous Double Auction (CDA) | Both buyers and sellers can initiate |
| **Clearing Mechanism** | **Novation** | Clearinghouse becomes CCP (Central Counterparty) |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility & Modern Relevance

Although largely replaced by **electronic limit order books (LOB)** today, Open Outcry's *principles* still underpin modern markets:

- **Pre-opening call auctions** in NSE/BSE use a *single-price auction* — a descendant of open-outcry price discovery.
- The **CME Group** still maintains *pits* for certain agricultural contracts (e.g., live cattle, pork bellies) as of the early 2020s for **liquidity & hedging convenience**.
- Open Outcry provided *visual market depth* (you could *see* buyer/seller aggression by body language) — modern **order flow heatmaps** in trading software attempt to replicate this.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Since Open Outcry is fundamentally a *qualitative market-microstructure* concept, the analytical derivations below focus on (a) **bid-ask spread derivation**, (b) **order priority ranking**, and (c) **comparative transaction cost analysis** — the quantitative anchors examiners expect.

### 3.1 Derivation: Bid-Ask Spread in a Continuous Double Auction

The quoted spread under an Open Outcry system is driven by three classical components:

$$
\text{Spread} = \underbrace{\alpha}_{\text{adverse selection}} + \underbrace{\beta}_{\text{order processing}} + \underbrace{\gamma}_{\text{inventory cost}}
$$

**Step-by-step logical expansion:**

**Step 1 — Adverse Selection Component ($\alpha$)**

This compensates the market maker for trading against better-informed participants (insiders, arbitrageurs). If the probability of trading with an informed trader is $\pi$ and the expected price movement against the market maker is $\Delta P$:

$$
\alpha = \pi \cdot \Delta P
$$

> *Logic:* Higher informed-trader activity (e.g., during major news releases) widens the spread.

**Step 2 — Order Processing Cost ($\beta$)**

This covers the **fixed costs** of running a pit operation — exchange fees, clearing fees, floor staff salaries, communications. For a floor broker, this is approximately:

$$
\beta = \frac{C_{\text{fixed}}}{Q}
$$

where $C_{\text{fixed}}$ is total fixed cost per trading session and $Q$ is the number of contracts handled.

> *Logic:* Higher fixed costs per unit of volume → wider spread required.

**Step 3 — Inventory Cost ($\gamma$)**

The market maker holds net inventory $I_t$ (positive = long, negative = short). Holding cost accrues at financing rate $r$ and suffers price variance $\sigma^2$:

$$
\gamma = r \cdot \vert I_t \vert + \frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 \cdot I_t^2 \cdot \Delta t
$$

> *Logic:* Larger / longer-held inventories demand wider spreads to compensate risk.

**Final Combined Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Spread} = \pi \cdot \Delta P + \frac{C_{\text{fixed}}}{Q} + r \cdot \vert I_t \vert + \frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 \cdot I_t^2 \cdot \Delta t}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Order Priority Resolution Example

Imagine five simultaneous orders shouted in a pit for the *same contract*:

| Order # | Trader Type | Side | Price | Time Stamp | Priority Rank |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Local | Buy | 102.10 | 09:30:01.100 | **1 (Matched)** |
| 2 | Broker | Buy | 102.10 | 09:30:01.250 | 2 |
| 3 | Broker | Buy | 102.05 | 09:30:01.150 | 3 |
| 4 | Local | Sell | 102.10 | 09:30:01.080 | **Counterparty** |
| 5 | Local | Buy | 102.15 | 09:30:01.300 | 4 |

**Resolution logic (explicit):**

1. Match #1 (Buy 102.10) ↔ #4 (Sell 102.10) — prices equal → match candidate.
2. Time priority: #1 (09:30:01.100) vs #4 (09:30:01.080) — both sides agree.
3. Trade executes at **102.10**, removing both orders from the book.
4. Order #2 (102.10) remains unmatched (waiting for a seller at 102.10 or lower).
5. Order #3 (102.05) is *inferior* to #2 — wait until sellers offer at 102.05 or lower.
6. Order #5 (102.15) is the new best bid — improves market.

### 3.3 Comparative Cost Analysis — Pit vs Electronic

$$
\text{Total Transaction Cost (TTC)} = \underbrace{\text{Spread}}_{\text{explicit}} + \underbrace{\text{Commission}}_{\text{explicit}} + \underbrace{\text{Delay Cost}}_{\text{implicit}} + \underbrace{\text{Market Impact}}_{\text{implicit}}
$$

| Cost Component | Open Outcry Pit | Electronic Limit Order Book |
|---|---|---|
| **Spread** | Moderate (liquidity-dependent) | Often **tighter** (more passive liquidity) |
| **Commission** | Higher (floor + clearing fees) | Lower (automated clearing) |
| **Delay Cost** | Low (immediate verbal execution) | Negligible (microsecond matching) |
| **Market Impact** | Higher for large orders (visible pit) | Algorithmic slicing possible |
| **Error Rate** | Higher (misheard shouts / wrong hand signals) | Near-zero (deterministic) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Floor Layout of an Open Outcry Trading Pit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["Pit Boundary - Octagonal Stepped Arena"]
    node2["Lower Ring - Active Locals and Market Makers"]
    node3["Upper Ring - Floor Brokers Handling Client Orders"]
    node4["Pit Committee Supervisor - Rules Enforcement"]
    node5["Runners and Board Boys - Slip Recording"]
    node6["Clearing House Gateway - Trade Reporting"]
    node7["Bid Shout - Voice Channel"]
    node8["Ask Shout - Voice Channel"]
    node9["Hand Signal - Price Palm In Out"]
    node10["Hand Signal - Month Fingers 1 to 12"]
    node11["Hand Signal - Quantity Repeated Gestures"]
    node12["Trade Card Slip - Contract Details"]
    node13["Novation and Central Counterparty CCP"]

    node1 --> node2
    node1 --> node3
    node1 --> node4
    node1 --> node5
    node2 --> node7
    node2 --> node8
    node2 --> node9
    node2 --> node10
    node2 --> node11
    node3 --> node7
    node3 --> node8
    node5 --> node12
    node12 --> node6
    node6 --> node13
    node9 --> node12
    node10 --> node12
    node11 --> node12
```

### 4.2 Order-Matching Flow inside the Pit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Start - Trader Enters Pit"]
    stepB["Identify Contract and Pit"]
    stepC["Announce Bid or Offer Verbally"]
    stepD["Flash Hand Signals for Quantity and Month"]
    stepE["Counterparty Improves or Accepts"]
    stepF{"Is Price Mutually Accepted?"}
    stepG["Reject - Return to Step C"]
    stepH["Trade Confirmed - Verbal Binding"]
    stepI["Runner Records on Trade Slip"]
    stepJ["Submit Slip to Exchange Clearing"]
    stepK["Clearinghouse Performs Novation"]
    stepL["End - Trade Settled as CCP Obligation"]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF -->|"No"| stepG
    stepG --> stepC
    stepF -->|"Yes"| stepH
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ
    stepJ --> stepK
    stepK --> stepL
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture — Communication Channels in Open Outcry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ch1["Verbal Channel - Shouting Bids and Offers"]
    ch2["Visual Channel - Hand Signals for Size Month"]
    ch3["Spatial Channel - Body Positioning in Pit"]
    ch4["Audit Channel - Trade Card Slip Documentation"]
    ch5["Clearing Channel - Novation through CCP"]
    ch6["Bid Ask Spread Formation"]
    ch7["Priority Resolution - Price then Time"]
    ch8["Final Trade Execution"]

    ch1 --> ch6
    ch2 --> ch6
    ch3 --> ch6
    ch6 --> ch7
    ch7 --> ch8
    ch8 --> ch4
    ch4 --> ch5
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **spatial channel** (where you stand in the pit relative to the active traders) is an under-appreciated feature: experienced locals positioned themselves where they could hear and see the most orders — an informal *latency optimization* in a human system.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define the Open Outcry System. Mention any two exchanges that historically used it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** The Open Outcry System is a floor-based, verbal-auction method of trading in which traders physically congregate in a designated "trading pit" and communicate their buy/sell interest by shouting bids and offers, supplemented by standardized hand signals indicating price, contract month, and quantity.
- **Examples (1 mark):** (i) **Chicago Board of Trade (CBOT)** for agricultural futures; (ii) **Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME)** for live cattle and pork belly futures.

---

#### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]`
**What is a "pit" in an exchange? Why are hand signals essential in a pit?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Pit (1.5 marks):** A *pit* (or *ring*) is the physical, octagonal/stepped arena on the floor of an exchange where traders gather to conduct Open Outcry trading of a specific contract.
- **Hand Signals (1.5 marks):** Hand signals are essential because (i) the noise level in a busy pit makes verbal-only communication error-prone, and (ii) signals convey *quantity* and *delivery month* simultaneously with price, allowing rapid parallel communication with multiple counterparties.

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain in detail the operational mechanism of the Open Outcry System. Describe the six sequential steps involved in trade execution under this system. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Physical Aggregation (1 mark):** Buyers, sellers, locals, and floor brokers gather around the designated pit for a specific contract. The stepped/circular geometry ensures visibility and audibility.

2. **Public Quotation (1 mark):** A market maker or local announces the prevailing bid-offer — e.g., *"Bid 102.05, offered 102.10."* This serves as the *reference quote* for the round.

3. **Hand Signal Communication (1 mark):** Because of high ambient noise, traders use standardized hand signals: palm direction for price increments, finger count for contract month, repeated gestures for quantity.

4. **Outcry and Counter-Outcry (1 mark):** Other participants respond by either improving the quote (raising bid / lowering offer) or accepting the standing quote by shouting *"Mine!"* or similar affirmation.

5. **Trade Confirmation (1 mark):** A verbal agreement is immediately binding under exchange rules. No written contract is needed at this stage; the verbal agreement creates the legal obligation.

6. **Slip Recording & Clearing (2 marks):** A nearby clerk ("runner" or "board boy") records the trade details on a card slip — contract, price, quantity, parties. The slip is relayed to the exchange's clearinghouse, which performs **novation** by stepping in as the central counterparty (CCP) to both sides, eliminating bilateral counterparty risk.

---

**(b) Compare the Open Outcry System with the modern Electronic Trading System (ETS). Highlight any four points of difference. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Dimension | Open Outcry | Electronic Trading System |
|---|---|---|
| **Mode of Communication** | Verbal shouts + hand signals (1 mark) | Digital messages over network (1 mark) |
| **Order Matching** | Human resolution in pit (1 mark) | Algorithmic matching engine (1 mark) |
| **Transaction Cost** | Higher (floor staff, clearing) (0.5 mark) | Lower (automation) (0.5 mark) |
| **Speed** | Seconds (1 mark) | Microseconds (1 mark) |
| **Transparency** | Limited to pit participants (0.5 mark) | Full order-book visibility (0.5 mark) |
| **Error Rate** | Higher (mishearing/mis-signaling) (0.5 mark) | Near-zero (deterministic) (0.5 mark) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often write only *advantages* of ETS and forget to acknowledge that **Open Outcry still offers benefits** — e.g., (i) visible body language gives *informal* market depth cues, (ii) human judgment for illiquid contracts, (iii) immediate human negotiation for block trades. Losing the *balanced comparison* point can cost **2 marks** in Part (b).

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]`

**(a) What is the role of a clearinghouse in an Open Outcry exchange? Explain the concept of novation with a suitable diagram. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of the Clearinghouse (3 marks):**
- Acts as the **Central Counterparty (CCP)** to every trade executed on the exchange.
- **Guarantees settlement** — the buyer need not worry about the seller's default, and vice versa.
- Performs **margin collection**, daily **mark-to-market (MTM)** settlement, and final delivery or cash settlement.
- Examples: **CME Clearing**, **LCH Ltd**, **ICE Clear Europe**.

**Novation — Concept (2 marks):**
> **Novation** is the legal process by which the clearinghouse *replaces* the original buyer and seller in a trade, becoming the buyer to every seller and the seller to every buyer. The original bilateral contract between two traders is extinguished and replaced by two new contracts — one between the original buyer and the CCP, and one between the original seller and the CCP.

**Diagrammatic Explanation (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    preA["Before Novation - Trader A and Trader B Contract Directly"]
    nov["Novation Event - Clearinghouse Steps In"]
    postA["After Novation - Contract A to Clearinghouse"]
    postB["After Novation - Contract B to Clearinghouse"]

    preA --> nov
    nov --> postA
    nov --> postB
```

**Worked numerical illustration (additional clarity, not mandatory):**

Suppose Trader A buys 10 Wheat futures contracts at ₹2,500/quintal from Trader B.
- **Before novation:** A owes B ₹25,00,000 (notional).
- **After novation:** A owes the **CCP** ₹25,00,000, and the **CCP** owes B ₹25,00,000. A and B no longer have any direct obligation to each other.

---

**(b) Discuss the three main components that determine the bid-ask spread in a dealer/auction market. Why is the spread considered a "cost" to the trader? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**The Three Components (5 marks):**

1. **Adverse Selection Cost ($\alpha$) (2 marks):**
   - Compensation for trading with *informed* participants who possess superior information about the asset's true value.
   - Formula representation: $\alpha = \pi \cdot \Delta P$, where $\pi$ = probability of informed trading, $\Delta P$ = expected adverse price move.

2. **Order Processing Cost ($\beta$) (1.5 marks):**
   - Fixed operational costs (exchange fees, salaries, technology, clearing) divided by trading volume.
   - $\beta = \frac{C_{\text{fixed}}}{Q}$

3. **Inventory Cost ($\gamma$) (1.5 marks):**
   - Cost of holding net long/short inventory due to financing and price risk.
   - $\gamma = r \cdot \vert I_t \vert + \frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 \cdot I_t^2 \cdot \Delta t$

**Why Spread is a Cost (2 marks):**
- A **market order** to buy immediately is filled at the *ask*, but the *true mid-price* is lower — the difference is an *implicit cost* to the buyer.
- Similarly, a market sell hits the *bid*, which is below the mid — again a cost.
- Over many trades, the spread accumulates as a real economic cost, especially for **high-frequency traders** and **large institutional orders**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Numerical Mistakes:** A very common error in Part B answers is writing the inventory cost as $r \cdot I_t$ (without absolute value or the variance term). The full inventory-holding cost must include **both** the linear financing component **and** the quadratic risk component. Examiners explicitly check this — missing either term costs **1 mark**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Open Outcry** = *floor-based verbal + hand-signal auction* trading.
- ✅ **Pit / Ring** = physical arena; **locals** = proprietary traders; **floor brokers** = client-order agents.
- ✅ **Hand Signals** = price (palm), month (fingers 1–12), quantity (repeated motions).
- ✅ **Order Matching Priority** = **Price first, then Time** (Price-Time priority rule).
- ✅ **Clearinghouse** performs **novation** → becomes Central Counterparty (CCP) → eliminates bilateral default risk.
- ✅ **Bid-Ask Spread** = $P_a - P_b$ (ask > bid in normal market).
- ✅ **Spread Components**: adverse selection + order processing + inventory cost.
- ✅ **CDA** = Continuous Double Auction — both sides can initiate quotes.
- ✅ **Historical Landmark**: CBOT (1848) → fully electronic (Globex) in 2015; CME still retains pits for select agricultural contracts.
- ✅ **Modern Descendants**: pre-opening call auctions, open-outcry principles embedded in modern limit-order-book design.
- ✅ **Examiner's Keyword Triggers**: "novation," "price-time priority," "central counterparty," "bid-ask spread components," "continuous double auction" — use these exact phrases for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Screen-Based System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Screen-Based System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Screen-Based System** (also called an **Electronic Trading System** or **Order-Driven Market System**) is a fully automated, computer-networked trading mechanism in which all buy and sell orders for securities are entered, displayed, matched, and executed electronically on a centralized digital platform — without any physical trading floor, open-outcry, or face-to-face interaction between traders.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *OEIET832 – Introduction to Financial Markets*, the screen-based system is defined as:

> *"An order-driven, screen-based trading mechanism wherein orders from market participants across geographically dispersed locations are routed through trading terminals to a central exchange server, where they are matched automatically using a predefined price-time priority algorithm."*

The two most prominent global examples are:
- **NSE (National Stock Exchange of India)** — pioneered screen-based trading in India in **1994**
- **BSE (Bombay Stock Exchange)** — migrated from open-outcry to its **BOLT + now** (New Generation Trading System)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A screen-based system is the **foundation** of all modern secondary-market trading. The KTU examiner expects you to compare it with the older *Quote-Driven / Floor-Based System* and list its **advantages and disadvantages** as a high-weightage topic.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a giant digital noticeboard in a town square. Instead of people shouting "I will buy apples at ₹10!" across a noisy marketplace, **everyone types their buy/sell requests into a computer**. The screen instantly shows **all live buy and sell prices** (called the **Order Book**). A computer brain then automatically pairs the highest buyer with the lowest seller, just like a matchmaker, **within microseconds**.

> **Real-World Analogy — "Swiggy/Zomato for Stocks"**:  
> Think of a food delivery app. You see live menus (orders), place your order (buy/sell), and the app matches you with the restaurant. You never speak to the chef. The **screen-based system is exactly this** — but for stocks. The exchange acts as the matching engine.

---

### 1.3 Core Characteristics (High-Yield KTU Definitions)

| Characteristic | Description |
|---|---|
| **Anonymity** | Identity of buyer/seller is hidden; only price & quantity visible |
| **Transparency** | Live order book visible to all participants simultaneously |
| **Automation** | No human intervention in matching; algorithmic execution |
| **Centralization** | Single central order book & matching engine |
| **Speed** | Order execution typically in **microseconds to milliseconds** |
| **Geographical Reach** | Pan-India (or global) access via VSAT/Leased Line/Internet |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric for KTU:** The latency (response time) of modern screen-based systems like NSE is **less than 200 microseconds** per order round-trip.

---

### 1.4 Key Terminology (Must-Know for KTU)

- **Order Book**: The live, sorted list of all unmatched buy and sell orders for a particular scrip.
- **Bid Price**: The highest price a buyer is willing to pay.
- **Ask Price (Offer)**: The lowest price a seller is willing to accept.
- **Bid-Ask Spread**: Difference between the best bid and best ask.
- **Matching Engine**: The software algorithm that pairs buy and sell orders.
- **Order Matching Priority**: **Price-Time Priority** — best price first; if same price, then earliest order first.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture — How a Screen-Based System Works

The screen-based system operates through a **6-stage pipeline**:

1. **Order Origination**: A trader at a broker's terminal decides to buy/sell a scrip.
2. **Order Routing**: The order travels through the broker's server to the exchange via secure leased lines (e.g., **1 Gbps MPLS**).
3. **Pre-Trade Risk Checks**: System validates margins, circuit limits, and price bands.
4. **Order Placement in Order Book**: Order enters the **Central Order Book** sorted by price-time priority.
5. **Matching Engine Execution**: If a counter-order at a matching price exists, a trade is executed automatically.
6. **Trade Confirmation & Settlement**: Trade confirmation is sent back; settlement occurs on **T+1 day** (as per SEBI 2024 norms).

### 2.2 The Price-Time Priority Algorithm (Core KTU Concept)

This is the **heart of the screen-based system** and is heavily tested.

$$\text{Matching Priority} = \text{Price Priority} \; \gg \; \text{Time Priority}$$

**The Logic (Decision Tree):**

- **Step 1 — Price Check**: Among all pending buy orders, the one with the **highest price** gets matched first. Among all pending sell orders, the one with the **lowest price** gets matched first.
- **Step 2 — Time Check (Tie-Breaker)**: If multiple orders exist at the same price, the **earliest placed order** is matched first (FIFO — First In, First Out).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bid-Ask Spread | $S = P_{ask} - P_{bid}$ | ₹ (currency) | Measures market liquidity |
| Spread Percentage | $S\% = \dfrac{P_{ask} - P_{bid}}{P_{ask}} \times 100$ | % | Cross-stock comparison |
| Effective Spread | $S_{eff} = 2 \times \vert P_{trade} - P_{mid} \vert$ | ₹ | Actual realized trading cost |
| Mid-Price | $P_{mid} = \dfrac{P_{bid} + P_{ask}}{2}$ | ₹ | Reference price for quoting |
| Order Latency | $t_{latency} = t_{exec} - t_{submit}$ | microseconds | System speed benchmark |
| Market Depth | $D = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i$ | units (shares) | Total quantity visible at top 5 bid/ask levels |
| Price Impact | $PI = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta Q}$ | ₹ per unit share | Price change per unit quantity traded |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` (NOT the pipe symbol `|`) inside LaTeX to prevent markdown table breakage in your exam answer scripts.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Financial Utility

Screen-based systems are vital in:
- **Algorithmic Trading**: HFT (High-Frequency Trading) firms exploit sub-millisecond latency.
- **Market Surveillance**: SEBI uses real-time data feeds to detect insider trading & price manipulation.
- **Global Market Integration**: Enables seamless cross-border trading (e.g., SGX-NSE IFSC-SGX Connect).
- **Reduced Transaction Costs**: Lower brokerage due to automation.
- **Disaster Recovery**: Modern exchanges have **DR (Disaster Recovery) sites** in geographically separated locations for business continuity.

### 2.5 Comparison: Screen-Based vs. Floor-Based System (KTU Hot Question)

| Parameter | Screen-Based System | Floor-Based (Open Outcry) System |
|---|---|---|
| **Mode** | Electronic, automated | Manual, human-mediated |
| **Transparency** | High (live order book) | Low (only ring members see) |
| **Speed** | Microseconds | Seconds to minutes |
| **Geographic Reach** | Pan-India/Global | Limited to physical floor |
| **Anonymity** | Anonymous | Face-to-face, no anonymity |
| **Error Rate** | Very low | Higher (manual errors) |
| **Cost** | Low per trade | High (staff, infrastructure) |
| **Examples** | NSE, NASDAQ, NYSE Arca | Old BSE ring, traditional commodity pits |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Order Matching Using Price-Time Priority

**Problem Statement:**  
The following orders are sitting in the order book for **Scrip X (₹)** in chronological arrival order:

| Order # | Type | Price (₹) | Quantity | Time Stamp |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Buy | 105 | 200 | 09:30:01 |
| 2 | Buy | 107 | 150 | 09:30:02 |
| 3 | Sell | 104 | 100 | 09:30:03 |
| 4 | Sell | 106 | 250 | 09:30:04 |
| 5 | Buy | 107 | 100 | 09:30:05 |
| 6 | Sell | 107 | 150 | 09:30:06 |

**Now, a new SELL order arrives at 09:30:07: Sell 300 shares @ ₹106.**

**Step 1 — Identify the incoming order type**  
Incoming = **Sell** @ ₹106, Quantity = 300.

**Step 2 — Apply Price Priority (Find Best Buy ≥ ₹106)**  
Sort all BUY orders by price (highest first), then by time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Buy Ladder (sorted by Price-Time Priority)}: \\
\text{(a) Buy @ ₹107 — Order #2, Time 09:30:02, Qty = 150} \\
\text{(b) Buy @ ₹107 — Order #5, Time 09:30:05, Qty = 100} \\
\text{(c) Buy @ ₹105 — Order #1, Time 09:30:01, Qty = 200}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Execute Match for ₹106 Sell Order**

We must match ₹106 Sell against Buy orders with price ≥ ₹106 (i.e., the ₹107 ladder).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Match 1: Sell 300 vs Buy #2 (₹107, Qty 150)} \\
\text{Trade 1: 150 shares @ ₹107, Time = 09:30:07} \\
\text{Remaining Sell = 300 - 150 = 150 shares}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Match 2: Remaining Sell 150 vs Buy #5 (₹107, Qty 100)} \\
\text{Trade 2: 100 shares @ ₹107, Time = 09:30:07} \\
\text{Remaining Sell = 150 - 100 = 50 shares}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Check for Further Matches at ₹106**  
Next Buy is at ₹105, which is **less than ₹106** → **No match possible**.  
Remaining 50 shares sit in the order book as a new sell at ₹106.

**Final Answer:**  
- **Trade 1**: 150 shares executed @ ₹107 (against Order #2)  
- **Trade 2**: 100 shares executed @ ₹107 (against Order #5)  
- **Unmatched**: 50 shares remain in sell book @ ₹106

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Bid-Ask Spread Calculation

**Given:**  
$P_{bid} = ₹499.50$ and $P_{ask} = ₹500.50$

**Step 1 — Compute Absolute Spread**

$$
S = P_{ask} - P_{bid} = 500.50 - 499.50 = ₹1.00
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Mid-Price**

$$
P_{mid} = \frac{P_{bid} + P_{ask}}{2} = \frac{499.50 + 500.50}{2} = ₹500.00
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Spread Percentage**

$$
S\% = \frac{S}{P_{ask}} \times 100 = \frac{1.00}{500.50} \times 100 = 0.1998\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A spread of **< 0.5%** indicates a **highly liquid scrip** — typically large-cap blue-chip stocks.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Market Depth Calculation

**Top 5 levels of Buy Ladder (in ₹, shares):**

| Level | Price | Quantity |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 500 |
| 2 | 99 | 800 |
| 3 | 98 | 1200 |
| 4 | 97 | 600 |
| 5 | 96 | 400 |

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \sum_{i=1}^{5} Q_i \\
&= 500 + 800 + 1200 + 600 + 400 \\
&= 3500 \text{ shares}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**: A market depth of 3500 shares suggests **moderate liquidity** at this stock.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Advantages vs. Disadvantages of Screen-Based System

| Aspect | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|---|
| **Speed** | Sub-millisecond execution | Technology dependency; latency arbitrage risks |
| **Transparency** | Real-time order book for all | Front-running by HFT (high-frequency traders) |
| **Cost** | Lower transaction costs | High initial IT infrastructure cost |
| **Accessibility** | Pan-India reach; remote trading | Digital divide; rural investors face barriers |
| **Efficiency** | Automated matching; price discovery | System failures (e.g., 2021 NSE halt) can halt entire market |
| **Scalability** | Handles millions of orders/day | Requires constant upgradation |
| **Audit Trail** | Every order logged — regulatory ease | Cyber-security risks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture Flow of a Screen-Based Trading System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trader at Broker Terminal] --> B[Broker Server]
    B --> C[Pre-Trade Risk Check Module]
    C --> D{Valid Order?}
    D -- No --> E[Reject with Error Code]
    D -- Yes --> F[Exchange Gateway / Leased Line]
    F --> G[Central Order Book]
    G --> H{Matching Engine}
    H -- Match Found --> I[Trade Execution at Price-Time Priority]
    H -- No Match --> J[Order Queued in Order Book]
    I --> K[Trade Confirmation to Trader]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Clearing & Settlement on T+1]
    L --> M[Funds and Securities Delivery]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style G fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style H fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style I fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style M fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000
```

### 4.2 Price-Time Priority Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Order Arrives] --> B{Order Type?}
    B -- BUY --> C[Place in Buy Ladder]
    B -- SELL --> D[Place in Sell Ladder]
    C --> E{Sell Order Available at or below Buy Price?}
    D --> F{Buy Order Available at or above Sell Price?}
    E -- YES --> G[Match by Price Priority]
    F -- YES --> G
    E -- NO --> H[Order Waits in Buy Queue]
    F -- NO --> I[Order Waits in Sell Queue]
    G --> J{Same Price Orders?}
    J -- YES --> K[Match by Time Priority FIFO]
    J -- NO --> L[Execute Trade]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Generate Trade Confirmation]
    H --> N[Order Visible in Order Book]
    I --> N
    N --> O[Wait for Counterparty]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000
    style G fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000
    style L fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram of Trading Layers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L1[Layer 1: Trading Participants]
        T1[Retail Investors] --> T2[Brokers and Sub-Brokers]
        T2 --> T3[Institutional Investors]
    end

    subgraph L2[Layer 2: Broker Infrastructure]
        T2 --> B1[Broker Terminal Software]
        B1 --> B2[Order Management System OMS]
    end

    subgraph L3[Layer 3: Exchange Core]
        B2 --> X1[Exchange Gateway]
        X1 --> X2[Matching Engine]
        X2 --> X3[Order Book Database]
        X2 --> X4[Trade Confirmation Engine]
    end

    subgraph L4[Layer 4: Post-Trade]
        X4 --> C1[Clearing Corporation NSCCL]
        C1 --> C2[Bank for Funds Settlement]
        C1 --> C3[Depository NSDL or CDSL for Securities]

    style L1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000
    style L2 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000
    style L3 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#000
    style L4 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000
```

### 4.4 Order Book Visualization (Bid-Ask Ladder)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BL[BUY LADDER Bid Side]
        B5[Buy 100 shares at 95]
        B4[Buy 200 shares at 96]
        B3[Buy 800 shares at 97]
        B2[Buy 500 shares at 98]
        B1[Buy 150 shares at 99 Best Bid]
    end

    subgraph SL[SELL LADDER Ask Side]
        A1[Sell 100 shares at 100 Best Ask]
        A2[Sell 300 shares at 101]
        A3[Sell 600 shares at 102]
        A4[Sell 400 shares at 103]
        A5[Sell 250 shares at 104]
    end

    B1 -. Spread = 1 .-> A1

    style B1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style A1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**Define a screen-based trading system. List any two advantages.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A screen-based trading system is an **electronic, order-driven platform** where buy and sell orders are entered through computer terminals and matched automatically by a central matching engine using **price-time priority**, without any physical trading floor.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Transparency** — The live order book is visible to all participants, ensuring fair price discovery.
2. **Speed and Efficiency** — Orders are matched in microseconds, reducing transaction time and cost.

*(Mark Distribution: Definition = 1M, Two Advantages = 1M each)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**  
**What is meant by 'price-time priority' in a screen-based system?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Price-time priority is the matching algorithm used in screen-based systems with two hierarchical rules:
1. **Price Priority**: Orders with the best price (highest bid / lowest ask) are matched first.
2. **Time Priority**: Among orders at the same price, the order placed **earliest** is matched first (FIFO).

This ensures a fair, deterministic, and transparent order matching process.

*(Mark Distribution: Price rule = 1M, Time rule = 1M, FIFO explanation = 1M)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style]*

**Q. (a)** Explain the architecture and working of a screen-based trading system with a neat block diagram. Discuss its key features. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
A screen-based trading system is a fully automated electronic mechanism where orders are matched via a centralized matching engine, eliminating the need for a physical trading floor.

**Architecture / Working (4 Marks):**
The system operates through these stages:
1. The trader places an order via a **broker terminal**.
2. The order passes through **pre-trade risk checks** (margin validation, price band verification).
3. The order is routed via **secure leased lines/VPN** to the exchange gateway.
4. It enters the **central order book**, sorted by price-time priority.
5. The **matching engine** compares the incoming order with opposite-side orders.
6. If a match is found, a **trade is executed**; otherwise, the order rests in the queue.
7. **Trade confirmation** is sent to both parties, followed by **clearing and settlement on T+1**.

**Key Features (2 Marks):**
- Transparency (real-time order book)
- Anonymity
- High speed (sub-millisecond)
- Pan-India accessibility
- Low transaction cost

---

**Q. (b)** Compare the screen-based system with the traditional floor-based (open-outcry) system on any **seven parameters**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Tabular Comparison — 7 Parameters × 1 Mark each):**

| Parameter | Screen-Based System | Floor-Based System |
|---|---|---|
| **Mode of Trading** | Electronic, automated | Manual, open-outcry |
| **Transparency** | High — order book visible | Low — limited to ring |
| **Speed** | Microseconds | Seconds to minutes |
| **Anonymity** | Anonymous | Open, face-to-face |
| **Geographic Reach** | Pan-India / Global | Limited to floor |
| **Transaction Cost** | Low | High |
| **Error Rate** | Minimal | Higher human errors |

*(Closing remark — 0 Marks but recommended: "Hence, screen-based systems are globally preferred.")*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**Q. (a)** Explain the **price-time priority** algorithm used in screen-based trading systems. Illustrate with an example involving at least 6 orders. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Price-time priority is a two-tier matching rule:
- **Price Priority** — best price gets matched first.
- **Time Priority** — earlier orders get matched first when prices are equal (FIFO).

**Example Illustration (4 Marks):**

*Order Book for Scrip ABC:*

| Order # | Side | Price | Qty | Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Buy | ₹50 | 100 | 10:00:01 |
| 2 | Sell | ₹49 | 50 | 10:00:02 |
| 3 | Buy | ₹50 | 100 | 10:00:03 |
| 4 | Sell | ₹50 | 80 | 10:00:04 |
| 5 | Sell | ₹50 | 120 | 10:00:05 |
| 6 | Buy | ₹51 | 200 | 10:00:06 |

**Execution Trace:**
- Order #6 (Buy 200 @ ₹51) is best priced → matched first.
- Match 1: vs Order #2 (Sell @ ₹49) → Trade 50 @ ₹49 (lowest sell).
- Match 2: Remaining 150 @ ₹51 vs Order #4 (Sell 80 @ ₹50) → Trade 80 @ ₹50.
- Match 3: Remaining 70 @ ₹51 vs Order #5 (Sell 120 @ ₹50) → Trade 70 @ ₹50.
- Total trades executed: 200 shares. Remaining 50 sell at ₹50 stays in book.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
This demonstrates how **price first, then time** governs order matching.

---

**Q. (b)** Discuss **any five advantages and three disadvantages** of the screen-based trading system. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Five Advantages (5 × 1 = 5 Marks):**
1. **Transparency** — Live order book visible.
2. **Speed** — Sub-millisecond execution.
3. **Lower Costs** — Reduced brokerage and operational overheads.
4. **Accessibility** — Remote trading from anywhere in India.
5. **Audit Trail** — Every order electronically logged for SEBI compliance.

**Three Disadvantages (2 Marks):**
1. **Technology Risk** — System failures can halt markets (e.g., 2021 NSE outage).
2. **Digital Divide** — Rural investors with poor internet connectivity are disadvantaged.
3. **Cyber Security Threats** — Vulnerable to hacking and data breaches.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse "screen-based" with "OTCE" or "Bullion" markets** — these are different. The KTU expects the definition in the **equity/derivatives cash market** context.
> 2. **Always write "Price-Time Priority" in that exact order** — writing "Time-Price Priority" will lose you 1 mark.
> 3. **Don't forget the diagram in 14-mark questions** — a missing block diagram = -2 marks minimum.
> 4. **Mention T+1 settlement** explicitly (post-2023 SEBI circular). Writing T+2 will be marked outdated.
> 5. **Do NOT claim screen-based systems are zero-risk** — the examiner wants balanced answers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] A **screen-based system** is an **electronic, order-driven** trading platform.
- [ ] Key examples: **NSE (1994)**, **BSE (BOLT)**, **NASDAQ**.
- [ ] Core component = **Matching Engine**.
- [ ] Core algorithm = **Price-Time Priority** (Price first, then Time).
- [ ] **Bid-Ask Spread** measures liquidity; lower = better.
- [ ] Settlement cycle (current) = **T+1** (SEBI 2024).
- [ ] Order book = live, sorted list of unmatched orders.
- [ ] Advantages: Transparency, Speed, Cost, Access, Audit.
- [ ] Disadvantages: Tech Risk, Digital Divide, Cyber Threats.
- [ ] Compare with floor-based system: 7 parameters minimum.
- [ ] Always include a **block diagram** in long-answer questions.
- [ ] Remember: **Anonymity + Transparency** are the twin pillars.
- [ ] Key formulas: $S = P_{ask} - P_{bid}$, $P_{mid} = (P_{bid}+P_{ask})/2$, $S\% = S/P_{ask} \times 100$.
- [ ] Market Depth = sum of top 5 bid/ask quantities.
- [ ] Latency benchmark: **< 200 microseconds** for modern systems.
- [ ] KTU expects **Indian context** (NSE/SEBI), not just generic global examples.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rolling Settlement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rolling Settlement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 1 — ROLLING SETTLEMENT

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OEIET832 / Module 1)**
> **Rolling Settlement** is a standardized market mechanism, mandated by the Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI), wherein every trade executed on a recognized stock exchange is compulsorily settled on a **fixed, pre-determined number of trading days** after the trade date, on a continuous (rolling) day-to-day basis, without batching trades into weekly or fortnightly account periods.

In simpler words: in the old days, all trades done in a whole week were clubbed together and settled on a fixed day. In **rolling settlement**, each trade gets its own independent settlement clock that starts the moment the trade is executed.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Pizza Delivery Clock"

Imagine two pizza shops:
- **Old Shop (Account Period Settlement)**: You order pizzas every day, but the shop only delivers them all together **every Friday**. The shop bundles Monday's, Tuesday's, Wednesday's orders and sends them on Friday. If Monday's pizza goes bad, the whole Friday delivery suffers, and you carry the risk of stale food for 4 days.
