- **Modern Shop (Rolling Settlement)**: The moment you order, the shop starts a **delivery clock**. If the rule is "deliver next day at 6 PM" (T+1), then *every single order* is delivered **the very next working day**, fresh and individually.

The modern shop is far safer because:
1. Risk exposure lasts only **1 day** instead of **5–7 days**.
2. A bad order is isolated — it cannot contaminate tomorrow's fresh orders.
3. Money and shares move in a continuous, predictable rhythm.

### Standard Metrics in Rolling Settlement (India, 2024 Scheme Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Trade Date (T):** The day on which the transaction is executed on the trading platform of the stock exchange.
> - **Settlement Cycle:** Currently **T+1** (Trade day plus **one** working day). Adopted pan-India by SEBI with effect from **20 January 2023**.
> - **Working Day:** Excludes Saturdays, Sundays, and SEBI-declared trading holidays.
> - **Pay-in Time:** Before **11:00 AM** on the settlement day.
> - **Pay-out Time:** Before **1:30 PM** on the settlement day (funds) and by **2:30 PM** (securities).
> - **Clearing Corporation:** **NSCCL** (for NSE trades) and **ICCL** (for BSE trades).

### What "Rolling" Actually Means

The word **rolling** signifies that there is **no fixed "settlement day" for the market**. Instead, settlement is a **rolling activity** that happens for a fresh batch of trades every single working day. The settlement calendar therefore moves forward like a conveyor belt, with the **Trade Date (T)** rolling forward by one day each session.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Rolling Settlement? — The Core "Why"**
> Rolling settlement was introduced by SEBI to **drastically reduce counterparty risk, settlement risk, and systemic risk** in the Indian securities market. By shortening the time gap between trade and settlement, the "open position risk" window is squeezed, leaving very little time for a counterparty to default.

The mechanism can be broken down into the following sequential logical steps:

1. **Trade Execution (Day T):**
   - The buyer and seller strike a deal on the exchange's electronic order-matching engine.
   - A **unique trade ID** is generated.
   - Trade details (symbol, quantity, price, buyer broker, seller broker) are forwarded to the **Clearing Corporation (NSCCL / ICCL)** by end-of-day.

2. **Obligation Generation (Evening of Day T):**
   - The Clearing Corporation becomes the **Central Counterparty (CCP)** — i.e., it inserts itself between buyer and seller as the universal guarantor.
   - For every trade, two obligations are generated:
     - **Buyer's obligation:** Pay cash equal to (Quantity × Trade Price).
     - **Seller's obligation:** Deliver the securities in **demat form**.
   - The exchange confirms trades by **T + 1 hour**.

3. **Mark-to-Market (MTM) Settlement (Evening of Day T):**
   - Open positions in the **Futures & Options (F&O) segment** are settled in cash on T+1 based on the daily **mark-to-market** price.

4. **Pay-in (Settlement Day = T+1, morning):**
   - **Seller's broker** delivers securities to the Clearing Corporation's pool account.
   - **Buyer's broker** transfers funds to the Clearing Corporation.
   - Deadline: **11:00 AM** for both securities and funds.

5. **Pay-out (Settlement Day = T+1, afternoon):**
   - The Clearing Corporation exchanges funds for securities.
   - **Funds Payout** to seller's broker: by **1:30 PM**.
   - **Securities Payout** to buyer's broker: by **2:30 PM**.

6. **End-of-Settlement:**
   - The buyer's client receives shares in his demat account.
   - The seller's client receives cash (net of brokerage, STT, GST) in his bank account.
   - The trade is now **completely closed**.

### 2.2 The "How" Behind Each Step — The Risk Reduction Logic

| Stage | What Happens | Risk Mitigated |
|---|---|---|
| Trade Date (T) | Trade is matched electronically | **Selection Risk** — anonymous matching removes bias |
| T+1 Morning (Pay-in) | Both sides deliver simultaneously | **Replacement Cost Risk** — CCP guarantees completion |
| T+1 Afternoon (Pay-out) | CCP swaps cash for shares | **Principal Risk** — no party is exposed beyond T+1 |
| Auction (if delivery fails) | Same-day auction on exchange | **Liquidity Risk** — buyer's position is closed out quickly |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet — Rolling Settlement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Cheat-Sheet — Must Memorize**

| Parameter | Specification (India, 2024) |
|---|---|
| Current Settlement Cycle | **T + 1** (effective 20 Jan 2023) |
| Previous Cycle | **T + 2** (Apr 2003 – Jan 2023) |
| Earliest Cycle in India | **T + 5** (2001, when rolling was first introduced) |
| Governing Regulator | **SEBI** (Securities and Exchange Board of India) |
| Clearing Corporations | **NSCCL** (NSE), **ICCL** (BSE) |
| Central Counterparty | **Yes** — Clearing Corporation |
| Mandatory Form of Holding | **Demat (electronic)** |
| Auction Day | **T + 1** (same as settlement, on exchange) |
| Pay-in Deadline | **11:00 AM**, T+1 |
| Pay-out Deadline | **1:30 PM** (Funds), **2:30 PM** (Securities), T+1 |
| Trade Confirmation | Within **1 hour** of trade |
| MTM Settlement (F&O) | Daily, on **T+1** |
| Penalty for Short / Bad Delivery | **AUCTION** + penalty % as per SEBI |
| Global Comparison | USA = T+1 (May 2024), EU = T+2, UK = T+2 |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in the Indian Capital Market

- **Reduced Systemic Risk:** In 2020, SEBI estimated that shifting from T+2 to T+1 would reduce the **daily open position risk by ~50%**, freeing up roughly **₹25,000 crore** of margin liquidity.
- **Improved Market Efficiency:** Brokers and clearing members need to deploy less capital as margin, which can be redeployed into productive economic activity.
- **Global Alignment:** India became one of the **largest markets in the world to shift to T+1**, ahead of the US (which moved to T+1 only in May 2024).
- **Retail Investor Benefit:** Faster credit of shares and funds into client accounts improves liquidity for retail participants.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Worked Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Problem Statement — Typical KTU Numerical

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Numerical | 10 Marks Concept]**
> An investor Mr. Ravi buys **500 shares** of Company ABC at **₹1,200 per share** on **Monday (T)** on the NSE. Brokerage is charged at **0.10%** of trade value (each side). Securities Transaction Tax (STT) is **0.1%** of turnover (on the sell side for delivery trades). GST on brokerage is **18%**. GST on STT is **0%**.
>
> Compute the **total funds outflow for Mr. Ravi** and the **number of shares credited to his demat account** on settlement day.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumptions to State Clearly in Exam**
> 1. Trade Date **T** = Monday.
> 2. Settlement Day **T+1** = Tuesday (assuming no holiday).
> 3. STT for delivery purchase is technically on the **buyer side** at **0.1%** of purchase value (as per Finance Act amendments).
> 4. GST is charged only on the **brokerage component**, not on the trade value.
> 5. Stamp duty and exchange transaction charges are ignored here for clarity (KTU problems usually include brokerage + STT + GST).

---

#### Step 1: Calculate the Basic Trade Value (Turnover)

$$
\text{Turnover} = \text{Quantity} \times \text{Price per share}
$$

$$
\text{Turnover} = 500 \times 1200
$$

$$
\text{Turnover} = \text{Rs. } 6{,}00{,}000
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The basic value of the transaction before any statutory levies is ₹6,00,000.

---

#### Step 2: Compute Brokerage Charged

Brokerage is charged on **both buy and sell sides** in a transaction, but for a **single buyer**, only the buy-side brokerage applies to his outflow.

$$
\text{Brokerage (Buy Side)} = 0.10\% \times \text{Turnover}
$$

$$
\text{Brokerage (Buy Side)} = \frac{0.10}{100} \times 6{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Brokerage (Buy Side)} = \text{Rs. } 600
$$

**Conversion Logic:** At the rate of 1 per thousand of trade value, brokerage = ₹600.

---

#### Step 3: Compute GST on Brokerage

$$
\text{GST on Brokerage} = 18\% \times \text{Brokerage}
$$

$$
\text{GST on Brokerage} = \frac{18}{100} \times 600
$$

$$
\text{GST on Brokerage} = \text{Rs. } 108
$$

**Conversion Logic:** GST is a service tax levied only on the service component (brokerage), not on the trade itself.

---

#### Step 4: Compute Securities Transaction Tax (STT)

For a **delivery-based purchase**, STT is charged at **0.1%** on the purchase value.

$$
\text{STT} = 0.1\% \times \text{Turnover}
$$

$$
\text{STT} = \frac{0.1}{100} \times 6{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{STT} = \text{Rs. } 600
$$

**Conversion Logic:** STT is a government levy collected at the source to curb speculative trading.

---

#### Step 5: Compute Total Funds Outflow for the Buyer

$$
\text{Total Outflow} = \text{Turnover} + \text{Brokerage (Buy)} + \text{GST} + \text{STT}
$$

$$
\text{Total Outflow} = 6{,}00{,}000 + 600 + 108 + 600
$$

$$
\text{Total Outflow} = \text{Rs. } 6{,}01{,}308
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The buyer pays for the shares (₹6,00,000), the broker's service (₹600), the GST on that service (₹108), and the statutory STT (₹600).

---

#### Step 6: Determine Shares Credited to Demat Account

$$
\text{Shares Credited} = \text{Quantity Purchased} = 500 \text{ shares}
$$

These 500 shares of ABC Ltd. will be credited to Mr. Ravi's demat account by **2:30 PM on Tuesday (T+1)**.

---

#### Step 7: Determine Settlement Timeline

| Event | Date | Time |
|---|---|---|
| Trade Execution (T) | Monday | Market hours (9:15 AM – 3:30 PM) |
| Pay-in by Buyer's Broker | Tuesday (T+1) | Before 11:00 AM |
| Pay-in by Seller's Broker | Tuesday (T+1) | Before 11:00 AM |
| Funds Payout to Seller | Tuesday (T+1) | Before 1:30 PM |
| Securities Payout to Buyer | Tuesday (T+1) | Before 2:30 PM |

---

### 3.3 The Analytical Matrix — Risk Reduction Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Comparison Table — Most Repeated Board Question**

| Parameter | T + 5 (Oldest) | T + 3 | T + 2 | T + 1 (Current) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Open Risk Window | 5 days | 3 days | 2 days | 1 day |
| Counterparty Risk | Very High | High | Moderate | Low |
| Margin Blocking | Maximum | High | Moderate | Minimum |
| Capital Efficiency | Poor | Moderate | Good | Excellent |
| Investor Cash-Cycle | Slow | Moderate | Fast | Fastest |
| Auction Window | Long | Medium | Short | Same-day |
| Risk Reduction vs T+5 | Baseline | ~40% | ~60% | ~80% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Risk Reduction Formula (Empirical, used in SEBI working papers)**
>
> $$\text{Risk Reduction (\%)} = \frac{T_{\text{old}} - T_{\text{new}}}{T_{\text{old}}} \times 100$$
>
> where $T_{\text{old}}$ = older cycle days and $T_{\text{new}}$ = newer cycle days.

---

#### Worked Risk-Reduction Example

Going from **T+2 → T+1**:

$$
\text{Risk Reduction} = \frac{2 - 1}{2} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Risk Reduction} = \frac{1}{2} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Risk Reduction} = 50\%
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Halving the settlement cycle from 2 days to 1 day halves the open risk window.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Rolling Settlement Process (T+1)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["TRADE EXECUTION Day T Market Hours 9:15 AM to 3:30 PM"]
    B["TRADE CONFIRMATION Within 1 Hour T"]
    C["OBLIGATION GENERATION Evening of T Clearing Corp becomes CCP"]
    D["MARGIN CHECK NSCCL or ICCL Verifies Member Funds"]
    E["PAY IN STAGE T plus 1 Morning Before 11:00 AM Seller delivers shares and Buyer delivers cash"]
    F["CCP VALIDATION Clearing Corp Locks Both Deliveries"]
    G["AUCTION TRIGGERED Only if Pay in fails Same day T plus 1"]
    H["PAY OUT STAGE T plus 1 Afternoon Funds by 1:30 PM Shares by 2:30 PM"]
    I["SETTLEMENT COMPLETE Buyer gets shares in Demat Seller gets cash in Bank"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F -->|Both Sides Pay in| H
    F -->|Any Side Defaults| G
    G -->|Successful Close Out| H
    H --> I
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Architecture — Functional Roles in Rolling Settlement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "INVESTOR LAYER"
        P1["BUYER Investor with Demat and Bank"]
        P2["SELLER Investor with Demat and Bank"]
    end

    subgraph "INTERMEDIARY LAYER"
        P3["BUYER BROKER Trading Member"]
        P4["SELLER BROKER Trading Member"]
    end

    subgraph "MARKET INFRASTRUCTURE LAYER"
        P5["STOCK EXCHANGE NSE or BSE Order Matching"]
        P6["CLEARING CORPORATION NSCCL or ICCL Acts as CCP"]
        P7["DEPOSITORIES NSDL or CDSL Holds Demat Securities"]
        P8["SETTLEMENT BANKS Authorised Partner Banks"]
    end

    subgraph "REGULATORY LAYER"
        P9["SEBI Market Regulator and Rule Maker"]
        P10["RBI Settlements and Banking Oversight"]
    end

    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P4
    P3 --> P5
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7
    P6 --> P8
    P9 -.Monitors.-> P5
    P9 -.Monitors.-> P6
    P10 -.Monitors.-> P8
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Timeline — T+1 Settlement Cycle Visual

```mermaid
gantt
    title Rolling Settlement Timeline T to T plus 1
    dateFormat  HH:mm
    axisFormat  %H:%M

    section Trade Date T
    Market Open and Orders Placed          :a1, 09:15, 30m
    Order Matching Engine Active            :a2, 09:15, 375m
    Market Close                            :milestone, 15:30, 0m
    Trade Confirmation Generated            :a3, 15:30, 60m
    Obligation and MTM Computed             :a4, 16:30, 90m

    section Settlement Day T plus 1
    Pay in Window Securities and Cash       :b1, 09:00, 120m
    Pay in Deadline                         :milestone, 11:00, 0m
    CCP Netting and Validation              :b2, 11:00, 90m
    Funds Pay out to Sellers                :b3, 12:00, 90m
    Funds Pay out Deadline                  :milestone, 13:30, 0m
    Securities Pay out to Buyers            :b4, 13:30, 60m
    Securities Pay out Deadline             :milestone, 14:30, 0m
```

### 4.4 Schematic Block Diagram — Failure & Auction Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    J["T plus 1 Pay in Starts 11 AM Cut Off"]
    K{"Did Both Sides Deliver?"}
    L["Normal Pay out Path Completed by 2:30 PM"]
    M["AUCTION CALLED On Exchange Same Day"]
    N["Original Buyer Close out Position T plus 1 itself"]
    O["Penalty Levied on Defaulting Member As per SEBI Rules"]
    P["Settlement Completed Through Auction Proceeds"]

    J --> K
    K -->|Yes| L
    K -->|No| M
    M --> N
    M --> O
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model | Module 1 | 3 Marks]**

#### **Question A1 (3 Marks):**
**Define the term "Rolling Settlement" as applicable to the Indian securities market. State the current settlement cycle in India.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Rolling Settlement** is a market mechanism, mandated by **SEBI**, in which every trade executed on a recognized stock exchange is settled on a **fixed, pre-determined number of working days after the trade date**, on a continuous day-to-day basis without clubbing trades into weekly or fortnightly account periods.
>
> The current settlement cycle in India is **T+1** (Trade day plus one working day), effective from **20 January 2023** under SEBI's circular. Trades executed on Day T are settled on Day T+1, with pay-in by 11:00 AM and pay-out by 2:30 PM on the settlement day. **(3 Marks)**

---

#### **Question A2 (3 Marks):**
**What is the role of the Clearing Corporation in the Rolling Settlement system?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Clearing Corporation** (NSCCL for NSE and ICCL for BSE) acts as the **Central Counterparty (CCP)** between the buyer and the seller.
>
> Its three key roles are:
> 1. **Guarantee of Trade Settlement** — It guarantees completion of every trade, even if one party defaults.
> 2. **Risk Management** — It collects margins, performs mark-to-market, and runs the auction process for failed deliveries.
> 3. **Settlement Finality** — It executes pay-in and pay-out of funds and securities as per SEBI's T+1 timeline.
>
> By becoming the universal counterparty, the Clearing Corporation **eliminates counterparty risk** between the original buyer and seller. **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model | Module 1 | 14 Marks | KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Choose A or B*

**Q(A)(a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the step-by-step operational mechanism of **Rolling Settlement under the current T+1 framework** in India. Mention the role of the Clearing Corporation, Depositories, and Settlement Banks with their respective timelines.

**Model Answer (7 Marks Detailed):**

1. **Trade Date (T):** Orders are placed on the NSE/BSE trading platform between 9:15 AM and 3:30 PM. Trades are matched by the electronic order-matching engine. A unique trade ID is generated for each trade, and trade details are forwarded to the Clearing Corporation (NSCCL/ICCL) within one hour. **(1 Mark)**

2. **Obligation Generation (Evening of T):** The Clearing Corporation becomes the Central Counterparty. It generates two obligations per trade — a buyer's fund obligation and a seller's securities obligation. For F&O segment, mark-to-market (MTM) settlement is also computed. **(1 Mark)**

3. **Pay-in Stage (T+1 Morning):** Seller's broker delivers shares into the Clearing Corporation's pool account, and buyer's broker transfers funds via Settlement Banks. The **pay-in deadline is 11:00 AM**. NSCCL/ICCL validates both deliveries. **(1 Mark)**

4. **Pay-out Stage (T+1 Afternoon):** After successful pay-in, the Clearing Corporation releases funds to the seller's broker by **1:30 PM** and securities to the buyer's broker by **2:30 PM**. Depositories (NSDL/CDSL) credit the shares to the buyer's demat account. **(2 Marks)**

5. **Auction Mechanism (If Delivery Fails):** If any member fails to deliver, the Clearing Corporation conducts an **auction on the same settlement day** to close out the position. The defaulting member is penalized as per SEBI regulations. **(1 Mark)**

6. **Settlement Finality:** Once pay-out is complete, the buyer's demat account reflects the purchased shares and the seller's bank account reflects the sale proceeds (net of brokerage, STT, GST). The trade is legally closed. **(1 Mark)**

> **Examiner's Comment:** Students often confuse pay-in and pay-out deadlines. **Pay-in = 11 AM** (delivery INTO CCP), **Pay-out = 1:30 / 2:30 PM** (delivery OUT FROM CCP to members). This 1.5–3.5 hour buffer is the **CCP risk window**.

---

**Q(A)(b) [7 Marks]:** Compare the **T+5, T+3, T+2, and T+1** settlement cycles in India in terms of **risk exposure, capital efficiency, and systemic stability**. Highlight the global context.

**Model Answer (7 Marks Detailed):**

| Parameter | T+5 (2001) | T+3 (2002) | T+2 (2003–2023) | T+1 (Jan 2023 onwards) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Open Risk Window | 5 days | 3 days | 2 days | 1 day |
| Counterparty Risk | Very High | High | Moderate | Low |
| Margin Requirement | Maximum | High | Moderate | Minimum |
| Capital Efficiency | Poor | Moderate | Good | Excellent |
| Systemic Stability | Weak | Improving | Strong | Strongest |
| Auction Window | Multi-day | Multi-day | Same/Next day | Same day (T+1) |
| International Status | Outdated | Outdated | Global Std. (till 2024) | Global Leader |

**Key Points for Full Marks:**

- **Risk Exposure:** Each halving of the cycle reduces open risk by 50%. T+1 reduces open risk by **80% versus T+5**. **(2 Marks)**
- **Capital Efficiency:** Shorter cycles free up margin capital. SEBI estimated savings of **~₹25,000 crore** in blocked margins after T+1 migration. **(2 Marks)**
- **Systemic Stability:** Faster settlement reduces the probability of cascading defaults — critical during volatile markets. **(1 Mark)**
- **Global Context:** India adopted T+1 in **January 2023**, making it the **largest T+1 market globally**. The USA moved to T+1 only in **May 2024**. EU and UK are still on T+2 as of 2024. **(2 Marks)**

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**Q(B)(a) [7 Marks]:** A retail investor Mr. Karthik buys **1,000 shares of XYZ Ltd. at ₹450 per share** on Wednesday (T). Brokerage is charged at **0.05% per side** (only on buy side for the investor's outflow). STT on delivery purchase is **0.1%**. GST on brokerage is **18%**. Compute the **total amount paid by Mr. Karthik** and state the **exact day and time** when shares will be credited to his demat account.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the Turnover (Basic Trade Value).

$$
\text{Turnover} = 1000 \times 450 = \text{Rs. } 4{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 2:** Compute Brokerage (Buy Side).

$$
\text{Brokerage} = 0.05\% \times 4{,}50{,}000 = \frac{0.05}{100} \times 4{,}50{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 225
$$

**Step 3:** Compute GST on Brokerage.

$$
\text{GST} = 18\% \times 225 = \frac{18}{100} \times 225 = \text{Rs. } 40.50
$$

**Step 4:** Compute STT on Delivery Purchase.

$$
\text{STT} = 0.1\% \times 4{,}50{,}000 = \frac{0.1}{100} \times 4{,}50{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 450
$$

**Step 5:** Compute Total Funds Outflow.

$$
\text{Total} = 4{,}50{,}000 + 225 + 40.50 + 450 = \text{Rs. } 4{,}50{,}715.50
$$

**Step 6:** Determine Settlement Day and Time.

- **Trade Day (T)** = Wednesday.
- **Settlement Day (T+1)** = Thursday (assuming no holiday).
- **Pay-in Deadline** = 11:00 AM Thursday (funds and securities).
- **Securities Pay-out to Buyer** = by 2:30 PM Thursday.
- **Shares Credited to Demat** = **1,000 shares of XYZ Ltd. credited by 2:30 PM on Thursday (T+1).** **(7 Marks Total — 4 for calculation, 3 for timeline)**

---

**Q(B)(b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **concept and significance of T+0 (Same-Day) Settlement**. Why has India not yet adopted T+0? Discuss the operational challenges.

**Model Answer:**

**Concept of T+0:**
T+0 (also called **Real-Time Settlement** or **Instant Settlement**) means that trades are settled **on the same day** they are executed, often within minutes or hours of trade execution. **(1 Mark)**

**Significance:**
1. **Near-Zero Settlement Risk** — counterparty exposure collapses to hours.
2. **Maximum Capital Efficiency** — virtually no margin is blocked overnight.
3. **Global Frontier** — A few markets like the Hong Kong Stock Exchange (for select instruments) and some US pilot programs have experimented with T+0. **(2 Marks)**

**Why India has NOT adopted T+0:**
1. **Operational Complexity:** The Indian retail ecosystem (small brokers, remote investors) is not yet equipped for same-day fund and securities transfer at scale. **(1 Mark)**
2. **Banking Hours Constraint:** Indian banks operate on **NEFT/RTGS windows**, with RTGS closing at 4:30 PM and IMPS/NEFT having cut-off times. Same-day settlement requires **24×7 real-time gross settlement (RTGS)**. **(1 Mark)**
3. **Retail Investor Awareness:** A majority of Indian retail investors are first-time participants. Same-day settlement may lead to reconciliation disputes, mismatched payouts, and confusion. **(1 Mark)**
4. **Global Precedent:** Even mature markets like the USA are still on T+1 (since May 2024). T+0 is still considered **experimental** worldwide. **(1 Mark)**

**Conclusion:** T+0 is the future of securities markets, but India will likely wait for global consensus, technology upgrades, and banking infrastructure maturation before considering migration. **(Total 7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones for Rolling Settlement Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Pay-in and Pay-out deadlines:** Pay-in = **11 AM T+1** (delivery *to* CCP). Pay-out = **1:30 PM / 2:30 PM T+1** (delivery *from* CCP). Interchanging these loses **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting GST on Brokerage:** Many students compute brokerage but forget the **18% GST** on it. Direct 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Mis-stating the current cycle:** Writing **T+2** (which was correct till Jan 2023) in a 2024 KTU paper is a **fatal error**. Always write **T+1**.
> 4. **Forgetting the CCP's role:** Many students describe the steps but forget to mention that **NSCCL/ICCL becomes the Central Counterparty**. SEBI specifically tests this.
> 5. **Writing STT only on sell side:** In delivery *purchase*, STT is also charged on the buyer at 0.1%. Students often miss this.
> 6. **No mention of demat:** The settlement is **electronic**, and shares are credited to a **demat account** (NSDL/CDSL). Omitting demat loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Rolling Settlement (Module 1)**

- **Rolling Settlement** = Every trade settles independently on a fixed date after trade, no batching into weekly/fortnightly accounts.
- **Current Cycle = T+1** (since 20 Jan 2023, SEBI).
- **Previous cycles:** T+5 (2001) → T+3 (2002) → T+2 (2003–Jan 2023) → **T+1 (Jan 2023 – present)**.
- **Pay-in Deadline:** **11:00 AM, T+1** (funds and securities into CCP).
- **Pay-out Deadline:** Funds by **1:30 PM**, Securities by **2:30 PM, T+1**.
- **Central Counterparty (CCP):** NSCCL for NSE, ICCL for BSE.
- **Depositories:** NSDL and CDSL (hold shares in demat form).
- **Demat is mandatory** — physical shares are no longer accepted for delivery.
- **Auction** for failed delivery happens on **same day (T+1)** — penalty on defaulting member as per SEBI rules.
- **Risk Reduction Formula:** $(T_{\text{old}} - T_{\text{new}}) / T_{\text{old}} \times 100$. T+1 reduces risk by **80% vs T+5** and **50% vs T+2**.
- **STT on delivery purchase** = **0.1%** on buyer side.
- **Brokerage GST** = **18%** on brokerage amount only.
- **Global context:** India is the **largest T+1 market**; USA moved to T+1 in May 2024; EU and UK still on T+2.
- **Total Outflow (Buyer)** = Turnover + Brokerage + GST on Brokerage + STT.
- **MTM for F&O** is settled in cash on T+1 itself (no physical delivery for index options/futures unless specified).
- **Auction default penalty** is a percentage of the trade value, decided by SEBI from time to time.
- **SEBI's role:** Frame rules, monitor CCPs, ensure market integrity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transaction Costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transaction Costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transaction Cost (TC)** in financial markets is defined as the total expenses incurred by an investor while buying or selling a financial security. These costs arise from **explicit charges** (brokerage, taxes, exchange fees) and **implicit costs** (bid-ask spread, market impact, slippage, opportunity cost). They represent the friction between the *theoretical* price of a security and the *realized* price at which the trade is executed.

Mathematically, the total transaction cost for a trade is expressed as:

$$TC_{Total} = C_{Explicit} + C_{Implicit}$$

where $C_{Explicit}$ represents tangible, billed charges and $C_{Implicit}$ represents hidden, opportunity-based costs embedded in execution price.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 OEIET832 syllabus (Module 1) explicitly mandates the study of *Transaction Costs* in the Indian securities market context, covering equity, derivatives, and debt segments, with reference to SEBI, NSE, BSE, and depositories (NSDL/CDSL).

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Fruit Market" Intuition

Imagine you go to a local fruit market in Kerala to buy **10 kg of mangoes** priced at ₹200/kg. The displayed price is ₹2,000. However, when you actually pay, you incur:

- **₹20** to the commission agent (broker equivalent)
- **₹10** as market entry fee (exchange fee)
- **₹15** as GST on commission
- The shopkeeper quoted ₹195 when you asked for 10 kg, but the **board price was ₹200** — that ₹5 difference per kg is the **bid-ask spread** in the market
- Because you bought 10 kg (a *large* lot), the shopkeeper *raised* the price to ₹202/kg — this is **market impact cost**

**Total Realized Cost = ₹2,150 instead of ₹2,000 → TC = ₹150 (7.5%)**

Financial transaction costs work *identically* — the displayed price is rarely the price you actually pay. This friction is what we call **Transaction Cost**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Bid Price** | Highest price a buyer is willing to pay |
| **Ask Price** | Lowest price a seller is willing to accept |
| **Bid-Ask Spread** | Difference between Ask and Bid prices |
| **Slippage** | Difference between expected and executed price |
| **Market Impact** | Price movement caused by the trade itself |
| **Brokerage** | Fee charged by the broker for execution |
| **STT (Securities Transaction Tax)** | Government tax on securities trades |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Hook:** Think of Transaction Costs as the **"GST of Trading"** — it reduces your gross returns into net returns. Every rupee of TC is a rupee NOT compounded in your portfolio.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Transaction Costs

Transaction costs in Indian financial markets are classified into **two broad categories** as per KTU Module 1 framework:

### 2.1.1 Explicit Transaction Costs (Visible, Billed)

These are *tangible*, *pre-disclosed* charges that appear on the contract note:

1. **Brokerage Commission**
   - Charged by the stockbroker for executing the trade
   - In India, post-2020 SEBI regulation: brokerage is charged on **both buy and sell** sides
   - Discount brokers (Zerodha, Groww): ₹0 brokerage on equity delivery; flat ₹20 on intraday/F&O
   
2. **Securities Transaction Tax (STT)**
   - Levied by the Government of India on the value of taxable securities transactions
   - **Equity Delivery (Section 2(h))**: 0.1% of the transaction value (charged only on sell side)
   - **Equity Intraday (Section 2(i))**: 0.025% of the transaction value (both buy & sell)
   - **Futures (Section 2(j))**: 0.0125% of the transaction value (only on sell side)
   - **Options (Section 2(k))**: 0.0625% on premium (sell side) + 0.125% on exercise
   
3. **Goods and Services Tax (GST)**
   - 18% GST on brokerage + exchange transaction charges + SEBI charges + clearing charges
   - **NOT** charged on STT, stamp duty, and the trade value itself
   
4. **Exchange Transaction Charges**
   - Charged by NSE/BSE for using their platform
   - NSE Equity Delivery: ~0.00297% of turnover
   - NSE Equity Intraday: ~0.00297% of turnover
   - F&O: varies by contract
   
5. **Stamp Duty**
   - State-level tax on trade execution
   - Rate: **0.015%** on equity delivery buy side (uniform post-July 2020 amendment)
   - Charged only on the **buy** side
   
6. **SEBI Charges**
   - Turnover-based: ~0.0001% of trade value
   
7. **Depository Participant (DP) Charges**
   - Charged by NSDL/CDSL via your DP (broker) for holding securities in demat
   - Typical: ₹13 + GST per scrip per day (sell side only)
   
8. **Clearing & Settlement Charges (NSCCL/ICCL)**
   - ~0.0035% of trade value for cash market
   
9. **IPO/Bid Application Charges**
   - ASBA processing fees, etc.

### 2.1.2 Implicit Transaction Costs (Hidden, Embedded)

These costs are *invisible* on the contract note but affect execution:

1. **Bid-Ask Spread**
   - The gap between best buy and best sell price in the order book
   - Liquid stocks (e.g., RELIANCE, TCS): spread is 1-5 paise → low cost
   - Illiquid stocks: spread can be ₹1-5 → high cost

2. **Slippage**
   - Difference between *expected* execution price and *actual* execution price
   - Occurs in fast-moving markets or with market orders during volatility

3. **Market Impact Cost**
   - Price movement caused by the size of the order itself
   - Buying ₹10 lakh of a small-cap stock will push its price up by 1-3%
   - Quantified using the formula:

$$Impact\ Cost = \frac{(Execution\ Price - Decision\ Price)}{Decision\ Price} \times 100\%$$

4. **Opportunity Cost**
   - Cost of *not* trading when the price would have been favorable
   - For algorithmic/quant traders, this is critical

5. **Delay Cost**
   - Cost incurred due to time elapsed between decision and execution

## 2.2 The "All-in-Cost" Formula

The total realized cost of a trade in India (equity delivery) is approximately:

$$TC_{Total} \approx Brokerage + STT + GST + Stamp\ Duty + Exchange\ Charges + SEBI\ Charges + DP\ Charges + Spread + Impact$$

A simplified **K-Score framework** (SEBI's metric for market quality):

$$K-Score = \frac{1}{2} \times \left( \frac{Average\ Impact\ Cost\ (\%)}{0.10} + \frac{Volatility\ (\%)}{0.30} \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **SEBI K-Score** is a KTU-favorite concept — it captures *both* the *cost* of trading and the *risk* of trading. A lower K-Score indicates better market quality.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Use Case |
|---|-------------------|------------|----------|
| 1 | **Total Transaction Cost** | $TC = C_{Explicit} + C_{Implicit}$ | Aggregate cost |
| 2 | **Bid-Ask Spread** | $Spread = P_{Ask} - P_{Bid}$ | Quoted market cost |
| 3 | **Spread % (relative)** | $Spread\% = \dfrac{P_{Ask} - P_{Bid}}{(P_{Ask} + P_{Bid})/2} \times 100$ | Normalized spread |
| 4 | **Market Impact Cost** | $MI = \dfrac{P_{Exec} - P_{Dec}}{P_{Dec}} \times 100$ | Large-order cost |
| 5 | **Effective Spread** | $ES = 2 \times \vert P_{Exec} - P_{Mid} \vert$ | Realized spread |
| 6 | **Realized Spread** | $RS = 2 \times (P_{Exec} - P_{Mid})_{t+1}$ | Post-trade spread |
| 7 | **Round-Trip Cost** | $RC = 2 \times TC_{one-way}$ | Buy + Sell cost |
| 8 | **Break-even Return** | $R_{BE} = TC_{one-way} + TC_{exit} \approx 2 \times TC$ | Min. return needed |
| 9 | **Breakeven Price** | $P_{BE} = P_{Buy} \times (1 + TC\%)$ | Sale price needed |
| 10 | **K-Score (SEBI)** | $K = \frac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{MI\%}{0.10} + \dfrac{Vol\%}{0.30}\right)$ | Market quality |
| 11 | **Net Realization** | $NR = \dfrac{P_{Sell}(1 - STT\%) - P_{Buy}(1 + Stamp\%) - Brokerage_{total}}{P_{Buy}} \times 100$ | Net return % |
| 12 | **Annualized TC** | $ATC = TC\% \times \dfrac{Trades}{Year}$ | Active trading cost |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU 2024 Insight:** When asked to calculate the *minimum* price at which a stock must be sold to break even, students frequently forget to add stamp duty, GST on brokerage, and DP charges. Examiners specifically test this oversight.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering/Finance Utility

- **Algorithmic Trading:** High-frequency trading firms spend billions reducing *latency* (delay cost) by milliseconds.
- **Portfolio Management:** Pension funds and mutual funds optimize **VWAP (Volume Weighted Average Price)** to minimize market impact.
- **Retail vs. Institutional:** Institutions have *lower percentage* explicit TCs but *higher* implicit TCs (impact); retail has the opposite.
- **Market Quality Regulation:** SEBI uses the **K-Score** to evaluate which stocks have *healthy* or *illiquid* markets.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Round-Trip Cost Calculation (Equity Delivery)

**Problem (KTU-style):**
> An investor buys 100 shares of XYZ Ltd at ₹500 each and sells them after 6 months at ₹550 each. Calculate the total transaction cost and net profit/loss assuming the following:
> - Brokerage: 0.1% of turnover (both buy and sell)
> - STT: 0.1% on sell side only
> - Stamp duty: 0.015% on buy side
> - Exchange transaction charges: 0.00345% (both sides)
> - SEBI charges: 0.0001% (both sides)
> - GST: 18% on (brokerage + exchange + SEBI)
> - DP charges: ₹15 flat (one-time, sell side)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute Buy Value and Sell Value**

$$Buy\ Value = 100 \times 500 = ₹50{,}000$$

$$Sell\ Value = 100 \times 550 = ₹55{,}000$$

**Step 2: Compute Brokerage (Both Sides)**

$$Brokerage_{Buy} = 0.001 \times 50{,}000 = ₹50$$

$$Brokerage_{Sell} = 0.001 \times 55{,}000 = ₹55$$

$$Brokerage_{Total} = 50 + 55 = ₹105$$

**Step 3: Compute STT (Sell Side Only)**

$$STT = 0.001 \times 55{,}000 = ₹55$$

**Step 4: Compute Stamp Duty (Buy Side Only)**

$$Stamp = 0.00015 \times 50{,}000 = ₹7.50$$

**Step 5: Compute Exchange Charges (Both Sides)**

$$Exchange_{Total} = 0.0000345 \times (50{,}000 + 55{,}000) = 0.0000345 \times 105{,}000 = ₹3.6225$$

**Step 6: Compute SEBI Charges (Both Sides)**

$$SEBI_{Total} = 0.000001 \times 105{,}000 = ₹0.105$$

**Step 7: Compute GST (on Brokerage + Exchange + SEBI)**

$$GST\ Base = 105 + 3.6225 + 0.105 = ₹108.7275$$

$$GST = 0.18 \times 108.7275 = ₹19.5710$$

**Step 8: Add DP Charges**

$$DP = ₹15$$

**Step 9: Sum All Transaction Costs**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{Total} &= Brokerage_{Total} + STT + Stamp + Exchange + SEBI + GST + DP \\
&= 105 + 55 + 7.50 + 3.6225 + 0.105 + 19.5710 + 15 \\
&= ₹205.7985
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 10: Compute Net Profit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Gross\ Profit &= Sell\ Value - Buy\ Value = 55{,}000 - 50{,}000 = ₹5{,}000 \\
Net\ Profit &= 5{,}000 - 205.80 = ₹4{,}794.20 \\
Net\ Return\ \% &= \frac{4{,}794.20}{50{,}000} \times 100 = 9.59\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The investor's *gross* return was 10%, but the *net* return is 9.59% — a **40 basis points erosion** purely due to transaction costs. Over decades of compounding, this difference is *enormous*.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Breakeven Price Derivation

**Problem:**
> An investor buys 1 share of ABC Ltd at ₹1,000. Brokerage is 0.05% per side, STT is 0.1% on sell, stamp duty is 0.015% on buy, exchange + SEBI + GST total 0.005% on turnover both sides combined, and DP is ₹10. At what minimum price must the investor sell to break even (i.e., Net Profit = 0)?

### Derivation

**Step 1: Let the sell price be $P$**

Buy Value = ₹1,000, Sell Value = $P$

**Step 2: Compute one-side variable costs as a function of $P$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Brokerage_{Buy} &= 0.0005 \times 1000 = ₹0.50 \\
Brokerage_{Sell} &= 0.0005 \times P = 0.0005P \\
STT &= 0.001 \times P = 0.001P \\
Stamp &= 0.00015 \times 1000 = ₹0.15 \\
Other\ Charges &\approx 0.00005 \times (1000 + P) = 0.05 + 0.00005P \\
DP &= ₹10 \\
GST &\approx 0.18 \times (Brokerage_{Total} + Other\ Charges) \\
  &= 0.18 \times (0.5 + 0.0005P + 0.05 + 0.00005P) \\
  &= 0.18 \times (0.55 + 0.00055P) \\
  &= 0.099 + 0.000099P
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Set up the breakeven equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P - 1000 &= 0.50 + 0.0005P + 0.001P + 0.15 + 0.05 + 0.00005P + 10 + 0.099 + 0.000099P \\
P - 1000 &= 10.799 + 0.001649P \\
P - 0.001649P &= 1010.799 \\
0.998351P &= 1010.799 \\
P &= \frac{1010.799}{0.998351} \\
P &\approx ₹1012.46
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The investor must sell at ₹1,012.46 to breakeven — a 1.246% nominal price increase is required just to recover transaction costs.**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Cost Components

| Cost Type | Equity Delivery | Equity Intraday | Futures | Options |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|---------|---------|
| **Brokerage** | 0.1%-0.5% | ₹20 flat (discount) | ₹20 flat | ₹20 flat |
| **STT** | 0.1% (sell) | 0.025% (both) | 0.0125% (sell) | 0.0625% on premium |
| **Stamp Duty** | 0.015% (buy) | 0.003% (buy) | 0.002% (buy) | 0.003% (buy) |
| **Exchange+SEBI** | ~0.003% | ~0.003% | ~0.002% | ~0.05% premium |
| **GST** | 18% on select | 18% on select | 18% on select | 18% on select |
| **DP Charges** | Yes (sell) | No | No | No |
| **Total TC (approx)** | **0.15-0.25%** | **0.05-0.10%** | **0.04-0.07%** | **0.10-0.30%** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *direction* of STT/Stamp applicability. Memorize: **STT = Sell Side dominant, Stamp Duty = Buy Side only** (for equity delivery).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Spread & Impact Cost Combined

**Problem:**
> A stock has Bid ₹99, Ask ₹101. You place a market order to buy 1,000 shares. Mid-price is ₹100. You buy at ₹101 (Ask). The order pushes the next ask to ₹102. After 5 minutes, you sell at the new bid of ₹101. Calculate: (a) Effective Spread, (b) Realized Spread, (c) Market Impact, (d) Round-Trip Cost in %.

### Solution

**Step (a): Effective Spread**

$$
ES = 2 \times \vert P_{Exec} - P_{Mid} \vert = 2 \times \vert 101 - 100 \vert = ₹2
$$

**Step (b): Realized Spread** (using post-trade mid: assume new mid = (101+102)/2 = 101.5)

$$
RS = 2 \times \vert P_{Exec}^{Buy} - P_{Mid}^{t+1} \vert = 2 \times \vert 101 - 101.5 \vert = ₹1
$$

**Step (c): Market Impact**

$$
MI = \frac{P_{Exec} - P_{Dec}}{P_{Dec}} = \frac{101 - 100}{100} = 1.00\%
$$

**Step (d): Round-Trip Cost**

$$
RC\% = \frac{101 - 99}{100} \times 100 = 2.00\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **effective spread** (₹2) is the *immediate* cost. The **realized spread** (₹1) is what the market maker actually earns after your trade moves the price. The difference (₹1) is the **market impact** that *you* imposed on the market.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Transaction Cost Taxonomy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeA[Transaction Cost Framework]
    nodeB[Explicit Costs Visible Billed]
    nodeC[Implicit Costs Hidden Embedded]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Brokerage Commission]
    nodeB --> nodeE[STT Securities Transaction Tax]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Stamp Duty Buy Side]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Exchange Transaction Charges]
    nodeB --> nodeH[SEBI Turnover Fees]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Transaction Costs and classify them with two examples each.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Transaction Cost refers to the total expenses incurred in buying or selling a financial security, comprising both visible charges and hidden market frictions.

**Classification:**

**(a) Explicit Transaction Costs** — Tangible, billed on the contract note:
- Brokerage commission
- Securities Transaction Tax (STT)
- Stamp duty
- Exchange transaction charges
- Depository (DP) charges
- GST (on services)

**(b) Implicit Transaction Costs** — Hidden, embedded in execution price:
- Bid-Ask Spread
- Market Impact Cost
- Slippage
- Opportunity Cost
- Delay Cost

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Always provide **at least 2 examples for each sub-category** to score full 3 marks. Avoid generic single-word answers.

---

### **Question 2: Explain Bid-Ask Spread as a component of transaction cost.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Bid-Ask Spread** is the difference between the **best price at which a buyer is willing to purchase** (Bid) and the **best price at which a seller is willing to sell** (Ask) a security at a given point in time.

$$Spread = P_{Ask} - P_{Bid}$$

**Significance:**
- Represents the **liquidity premium** of the security
- A *narrow* spread (e.g., 1-5 paise for RELIANCE) indicates a **liquid** market
- A *wide* spread (e.g., ₹1-5 for small-cap stocks) indicates an **illiquid** market
- Acts as **compensation to market makers** for providing liquidity
- An **implicit cost** because the trader cannot transact at the mid-price (theoretically "fair" price)

**Example:** If RELIANCE has Bid = ₹2,950 and Ask = ₹2,950.10, the spread is ₹0.10, or 0.0034% of the mid-price.

---

## 📝 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern Note:** Each Part B question has internal choice — students answer **either Question A or Question B**. Both must be prepared thoroughly.

---

### **🟢 Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**"Discuss in detail the various components of explicit transaction costs in the Indian securities market. Illustrate with a numerical example showing the total cost of an equity delivery trade."**

#### **(a) Components of Explicit Transaction Costs — [7 Marks]**

The major components of explicit (visible) transaction costs in the Indian equity delivery segment are:

**1. Brokerage (0.03% to 0.5% per side):** Fee charged by the broker for executing the trade. As per SEBI regulations, brokerage is charged on both buy and sell sides. Discount brokers charge a flat ₹0 (equity delivery) or ₹20 (intraday/F&O).

**2. Securities Transaction Tax (STT):** Government of India levy to curb speculative trading. For equity delivery, STT = 0.1% on the sell-side transaction value only. For intraday, it is 0.025% on both sides. For futures, it is 0.0125% on the sell side.

**3. Stamp Duty:** State-level tax. As per the Indian Stamp Act (amended July 2020), a uniform rate of **0.015% of buy-side transaction value** applies for equity delivery.

**4. Exchange Transaction Charges:** Charged by NSE/BSE for using the trading platform. NSE charges ~0.00297% of turnover for cash market trades.

**5. SEBI Charges:** Regulatory fee of ~0.0001% of trade value, on both buy and sell sides.

**6. GST (Goods and Services Tax):** 18% levied on the sum of brokerage + exchange charges + SEBI charges. **GST is NOT charged on STT, stamp duty, or the trade value.**

**7. Depository Participant (DP) Charges:** Charged for holding/selling securities from a demat account. Typically ₹13 + GST per scrip per day, billed on the sell side only.

**8. Clearing & Settlement Charges:** Paid to NSCCL (NSE Clearing) or ICCL (BSE Clearing), typically ~0.0035% of trade value for cash market.

#### **(b) Numerical Example — [7 Marks]**

**Problem:** An investor buys 200 shares of PQR Ltd at ₹750 each and sells them at ₹820 each after one year. Calculate the total transaction cost and net profit. Given:
- Brokerage: 0.1% per side
- STT: 0.1% (sell side)
- Stamp duty: 0.015% (buy side)
- Exchange + SEBI: 0.004% (both sides combined)
- GST: 18% on (Brokerage + Exchange + SEBI)
- DP Charges: ₹20 flat (sell side)

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Buy\ Value &= 200 \times 750 = ₹1{,}50{,}000 \\
Sell\ Value &= 200 \times 820 = ₹1{,}64{,}000 \\
Brokerage_{Total} &= 0.001 \times (1{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}64{,}000) = ₹314 \\
STT &= 0.001 \times 1{,}64{,}000 = ₹164 \\
Stamp &= 0.00015 \times 1{,}50{,}000 = ₹22.50 \\
Exch+SEBI &= 0.00004 \times (1{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}64{,}000) = ₹12.56 \\
GST &= 0.18 \times (314 + 12.56) = ₹58.78 \\
DP &= ₹20 \\
TC_{Total} &= 314 + 164 + 22.50 + 12.56 + 58.78 + 20 = ₹591.84 \\
Gross\ Profit &= 1{,}64{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000 = ₹14{,}000 \\
Net\ Profit &= 14{,}000 - 591.84 = ₹13{,}408.16 \\
Net\ Return\ \% &= \frac{13{,}408.16}{1{,}50{,}000} \times 100 = 8.94\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** Gross return of 9.33% was reduced to a net return of 8.94% — a 39 basis point erosion. Over 20 years of compounding, this difference is worth lakhs of rupees.

---

### **🔵 Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**"Explain the concept of implicit transaction costs with reference to Bid-Ask Spread, Market Impact, and Slippage. How does the SEBI K-Score framework assess market quality?"**

#### **(a) Implicit Transaction Costs — [7 Marks]**

Implicit costs are *invisible* costs that do not appear on the contract note but erode the investor's net returns:

**1. Bid-Ask Spread:**
The gap between the best buy price (Bid) and the best sell price (Ask). For liquid large-cap stocks, the spread is just 1-5 paise; for illiquid small-caps, it can be ₹1-5. Formula:

$$Spread\% = \frac{P_{Ask} - P_{Bid}}{(P_{Ask} + P_{Bid})/2} \times 100$$

**2. Market Impact Cost:**
The price movement caused by the trader's own order. When a large buy order is placed, the price rises *because* of the order. Impact cost increases with order size and illiquidity. Formula:

$$MI\% = \frac{P_{Execution} - P_{Decision}}{P_{Decision}} \times 100$$

**3. Slippage:**
The difference between the *expected* execution price and the *actual* execution price. Common during high-volatility events (RBI policy, budget day, earnings). Market orders are more prone to slippage than limit orders.

**4. Opportunity Cost:**
The cost of *not* trading when prices would have been more favorable. Critical for portfolio rebalancing decisions.

**5. Delay Cost:**
The cost incurred due to the time gap between decision and execution (in milliseconds for HFT).

#### **(b) SEBI K-Score Framework — [7 Marks]**

The **K-Score** is a composite market quality index developed by SEBI to evaluate the health and tradability of individual stocks.

**Formula:**

$$K\text{-}Score = \frac{1}{2} \times \left( \frac{Average\ Impact\ Cost\ (\%)}{0.10} + \frac{30\text{-}day\ Volatility\ (\%)}{0.30} \right)$$

**Components:**

**(i) Impact Cost Component:** Measures how much a standard ₹1 lakh order moves the stock price. The benchmark of 0.10% represents "good" liquidity.

**(ii) Volatility Component:** Measures the 30-day rolling volatility (annualized). The benchmark of 0.30% (or 30%) represents "normal" risk.

**Interpretation:**

| K-Score Range | Market Quality | Implication |
|---------------|----------------|-------------|
| $K < 0.5$ | Excellent | High liquidity, low impact, tradable |
| $0.5 \leq K < 1$ | Acceptable | Moderate liquidity, tradable with care |
| $K \geq 1$ | Poor | Illiquid, risky, high cost of trading |

**Utility:** Investors and regulators use the K-Score to:
- Identify *liquid* stocks suitable for large institutional trades
- Flag *illiquid* stocks for surveillance
- Compare market quality across the universe of listed stocks

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Forgetting GST base:** GST is NOT applied on STT, stamp duty, or trade value. Only on brokerage + exchange + SEBI charges. Many students incorrectly compute GST on the total transaction value.
> 2. **Confusing STT direction:** For equity delivery, STT is on SELL side only. For intraday, it's on BOTH sides. Wrongly applying it loses 1-2 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring implicit costs:** When asked about transaction costs, many students list only brokerage. Always mention BOTH explicit and implicit costs.
> 4. **Confusing Bid-Ask Spread with impact cost:** Spread is a *function of the order book*. Impact is a *function of the order size*. They are different concepts.
> 5. **Missing the breakeven return concept:** Examiners love to ask: "What is the minimum return to make a trade profitable?" Always compute ROUND-TRIP cost.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Transaction Cost = Explicit (visible) + Implicit (hidden)**
- ✅ **STT for equity delivery** = 0.1% on SELL side only
- ✅ **Stamp duty** = 0.015% on BUY side only (uniform post-July 2020)
- ✅ **GST = 18%** on (Brokerage + Exchange + SEBI) only — NOT on STT/Stamp
- ✅ **DP charges** apply only on the SELL side
- ✅ **Bid-Ask Spread** is the most fundamental implicit cost
- ✅ **Market Impact Cost** = (Execution Price − Decision Price) / Decision Price × 100
- ✅ **Round-Trip Cost** ≈ 2× one-way transaction cost
- ✅ **K-Score < 0.5** = Excellent liquidity; **K-Score ≥ 1** = Illiquid/risky
- ✅ **Discount brokers** (Zerodha, Groww) charge ₹0 brokerage on equity delivery
- ✅ **Breakeven price formula:** $P_{BE} = P_{Buy} \times (1 + TC\%)$
- ✅ **Net return** is ALWAYS less than gross return — the gap IS the transaction cost
- ✅ **Long-term investors** pay lower *percentage* TCs (less frequent trading)
- ✅ **Frequent/active traders** lose significantly to compounding erosion of TCs
- ✅ **SEBI regulates** brokerage caps, K-Score reporting, and STT structure

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** *"Transaction cost is the silent thief of compounding. Master both its explicit and implicit forms to evaluate any trade's true profitability."* Memorize the breakeven formula, K-Score formula, and the STT/Stamp directionality — these three account for 80% of marks in any TC question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Buying and Selling of Shares
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Buying and Selling of Shares

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Buying and Selling of Shares** refers to the systematic process through which investors acquire (purchase) or dispose of (sell) ownership units (equity shares) of a publicly listed company through a regulated secondary market, primarily a **Stock Exchange**, under the regulatory framework of the **Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition (Board-Standard):**
> A *share* is a unit of ownership interest in a corporation or financial asset that provides for an equal distribution in any profits (if any) distributed to shareholders. The *buying and selling* of shares takes place in the **Secondary Market** through the stock exchange platform, where existing shares are traded between investors without the company issuing new shares.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a giant, organized **flea market** for company ownership. 

- When a company first sells its ownership pieces (shares) to raise money, it's like a baker selling slices of his cake directly from his shop — this is the **Primary Market** (IPO).
- After that, those slices can be resold by whoever bought them. The **Stock Exchange** is the official flea market building where all these resales happen — this is the **Secondary Market**.
- You don't go up to the seller directly. You hire a licensed middleman called a **Stockbroker** (registered with SEBI), who knows the market rules and places your order.
- You tell the broker: *"I want to BUY 100 slices of Company X at any price up to ₹500 each"* (this is a **Buy Order**), or *"I want to SELL my 50 slices of Company Y, I won't accept less than ₹300 each"* (this is a **Sell Order**).
- The exchange's computer system **matches** your buy order with someone else's sell order. When they match, the **trade is executed**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Mandatory Memorization for KTU)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Share** | A unit of ownership of a company |
| **Stock Exchange** | Regulated marketplace for trading securities (NSE, BSE) |
| **Broker** | SEBI-registered intermediary who executes trades on behalf of investors |
| **Dematerialization (Demat)** | Conversion of physical share certificates into electronic form |
| **IPO** | Initial Public Offering — first time a company sells shares to the public |
| **FPO** | Follow-on Public Offering — subsequent issue of shares to the public |
| **Order** | Instruction to buy or sell a specified quantity of shares at a specified price |
| **Settlement** | The actual exchange of shares for money after a trade |
| **T+1 Day** | Trade is settled one business day after the trade date (current SEBI norm) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU expects students to clearly distinguish between **Primary Market** (where shares are *first* issued by the company) and **Secondary Market** (where existing shares are *bought and sold* between investors). The topic "Buying and Selling of Shares" specifically belongs to the **Secondary Market**.

---

## 1.4 Prerequisites You Must Know

- A **Demat Account** (to hold shares electronically) — opened with a **Depository Participant (DP)** such as a bank or broker.
- A **Trading Account** (to place buy/sell orders) — linked to your bank account.
- A **Bank Account** (for money settlement).
- A **PAN Card** (mandatory for all financial transactions in India).
- KYC (Know Your Customer) compliance.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2-Mark Favorite:** *"Name the three accounts required to start trading in shares."*
> **Answer:** Demat Account + Trading Account + Bank Account (often called the **triad of accounts**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Step-by-Step Process of Buying Shares

The process follows a strict **regulatory pipeline**. Let us break it down:

### Step 1: Account Setup (The Foundation)
The investor first opens:
- A **Demat Account** with a Depository (CDSL or NSDL) through a Depository Participant.
- A **Trading Account** with a SEBI-registered broker.
- A **Bank Account** linked to both for seamless fund transfer.

### Step 2: Market Research and Analysis
The investor studies:
- **Fundamental Analysis** — Company financials, P/E ratio, earnings, debt, management quality.
- **Technical Analysis** — Historical price charts, trading volume, market trends.
- **Market Sentiment** — News, macroeconomic indicators, sector performance.

### Step 3: Choosing the Right Broker
Brokers are of two types:
- **Full-Service Broker** — Provides research, advisory, and execution services (e.g., ICICI Direct, HDFC Securities). Charges higher brokerage.
- **Discount Broker** — Only executes trades, no advisory (e.g., Zerodha, Groww). Charges flat low fees.

### Step 4: Placing the Buy Order
The investor logs into the trading platform and places an order specifying:
- **Stock symbol / scrip code** (e.g., RELIANCE, TCS)
- **Quantity** (number of shares)
- **Price** (the rate at which willing to buy)
- **Order type** (Market, Limit, Stop-Loss, etc.)

### Step 5: Order Routing to the Exchange
The broker's system transmits the order to the **stock exchange's electronic trading platform**:
- **NSE** (National Stock Exchange) uses the **NEAT** system.
- **BSE** (Bombay Stock Exchange) uses the **BOLT** system.

### Step 6: Order Matching and Trade Execution
The exchange's **Order Matching Engine (OME)** matches buy and sell orders based on:
- **Price-Time Priority** — Best price gets matched first; if prices are equal, the order placed earlier gets matched first.

### Step 7: Trade Confirmation
Both parties receive a **Trade Confirmation** with the **Contract Note** from the broker, detailing the executed price, quantity, brokerage, taxes, and settlement date.

### Step 8: Settlement (T+1 Day)
On the **settlement day** (now **T+1** under SEBI's 2023 rule):
- Shares are debited from the seller's Demat account and credited to the buyer's Demat account.
- Money is debited from the buyer's bank account and credited to the seller's bank account.
- Settlement is guaranteed by the **clearing corporation** (NSCCL for NSE, ICCL for BSE) — this is called the **novation** process.

---

## 2.2 The Process of Selling Shares

The selling process is essentially the **mirror image** of buying:

1. Investor places a **Sell Order** through the trading account.
2. Shares must be available in the **Demat account** (otherwise the order is rejected — this is the **short-selling rule** for retail investors in India).
3. The exchange matches the sell order with a corresponding buy order.
4. On settlement day, shares move from seller's Demat to buyer's Demat, and money moves from buyer to seller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **India's "No Short Selling" Rule for Retail:**
> In India, retail investors are **NOT permitted to engage in naked short selling**. You can only sell what you own. However, **institutional investors** can do short selling under strict SEBI regulations (since 2008) by borrowing shares first.

---

## 2.3 Types of Orders (KTU High-Yield Topic)

| Order Type | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Market Order** | Buy/Sell at the best available current price | When speed is more important than price |
| **Limit Order** | Buy/Sell only at a specified price or better | When investor has a target price |
| **Stop-Loss Order** | Becomes a market order when price reaches a trigger level | To limit losses |
| **Stop-Loss Limit Order** | Becomes a limit order (not market) when triggered | To control slippage |
| **GTT (Good Till Triggered)** | Limit order that remains active for up to 1 year | Long-term investors |
| **Immediate or Cancel (IOC)** | Execute immediately, cancel unfilled portion | Day traders |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Total Purchase Cost | $C_{buy} = (P \times Q) + B + STT + GST$ | $P$ = Price, $Q$ = Quantity, $B$ = Brokerage, $STT$ = Securities Transaction Tax, $GST$ = Goods & Services Tax on brokerage |
| Total Sale Proceeds | $C_{sell} = (P \times Q) - B - STT$ | Net amount received by seller |
| Profit / Loss | $\text{P\&L} = (P_{sell} - P_{buy}) \times Q - \text{All Charges}$ | Positive = Profit, Negative = Loss |
| Brokerage (Discount Broker) | $B = \text{₹20}$ or $0.03\% \times \text{Turnover}$ (whichever is lower) | Per executed order, since Oct 2024 SEBI rule |
| STT on Purchase (Delivery) | $STT = 0.1\% \times P \times Q$ | Charged on the buyer |
| STT on Sale (Delivery) | $STT = 0.1\% \times P \times Q$ | Charged on the seller |
| GST on Brokerage | $GST = 18\% \times B$ | Applied on total brokerage |
| Stamp Duty on Buy | $0.015\% \times P \times Q$ | Charged only on the buyer |
| Settlement Cycle | $T + 1$ day | As per SEBI 2023 mandate |
| Return on Investment | $ROI = \dfrac{\text{Profit}}{\text{Total Investment}} \times 100$ | Expressed in percentage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notational Rule:** When writing absolute value or "such that" in math expressions on the board, always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ — never the bare pipe symbol $\vert$ — to avoid confusion with table separators during valuation.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

The process of buying and selling shares is the **backbone of capital markets**:
- **For Companies:** Provides liquidity, allowing them to raise capital efficiently.
- **For Investors:** Provides a platform to grow wealth, diversify portfolios, and participate in economic growth.
- **For the Economy:** The stock market is often called the **"Barometer of the Economy"** — its performance reflects economic health.
- **For Government:** Generates revenue through STT, GST, and capital gains tax.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever asked about "regulatory bodies", always mention **SEBI** (Securities and Exchange Board of India) as the apex regulator. SEBI's headquarters is in **Mumbai**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Cost of Buying Shares

**Question:** Mr. Arun buys 200 shares of XYZ Ltd. at ₹150 per share through a discount broker. Calculate his total cost, given that STT on purchase is 0.1%, brokerage is ₹20 flat, and GST is 18% on brokerage. Stamp duty is 0.015%.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Calculate the base transaction value (Turnover).

$$Turnover = P \times Q = 150 \times 200 = 30{,}000$$

**Step 2:** Calculate Securities Transaction Tax (STT) on purchase.

$$STT = 0.1\% \times 30{,}000 = 0.001 \times 30{,}000 = 30$$

**Step 3:** Calculate Stamp Duty (charged only on buy side).

$$Stamp\_Duty = 0.015\% \times 30{,}000 = 0.00015 \times 30{,}000 = 4.50$$

**Step 4:** Calculate Brokerage (flat rate for discount broker post-Oct 2024).

$$B = 20$$

**Step 5:** Calculate GST on brokerage (18%).

$$GST = 18\% \times 20 = 0.18 \times 20 = 3.60$$

**Step 6:** Sum all components to get the total cost.

$$C_{buy} = 30{,}000 + 30 + 4.50 + 20 + 3.60 = 30{,}058.10$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** For every ₹30,000 of shares bought, the additional cost is ₹58.10. This is the **breakeven selling price** calculation — Mr. Arun must sell at a price that recovers these charges to make a zero-profit transaction.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Profit/Loss on Sale

**Question:** Continuing from Example 1, Mr. Arun sells all 200 shares at ₹170 per share after 6 months. Calculate his profit/loss. STT on sale (delivery) = 0.1%, brokerage = ₹20, GST = 18% of brokerage.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Calculate sale turnover.

$$Turnover_{sell} = 170 \times 200 = 34{,}000$$

**Step 2:** Calculate STT on sale (delivery).

$$STT_{sell} = 0.1\% \times 34{,}000 = 34$$

**Step 3:** Brokerage on sale.

$$B_{sell} = 20$$

**Step 4:** GST on brokerage.

$$GST_{sell} = 18\% \times 20 = 3.60$$

**Step 5:** Total deductions on sale.

$$Deductions = 34 + 20 + 3.60 = 57.60$$

**Step 6:** Net proceeds received.

$$Net_{sell} = 34{,}000 - 57.60 = 33{,}942.40$$

**Step 7:** Total cost of buying (from Example 1).

$$C_{buy} = 30{,}058.10$$

**Step 8:** Calculate Profit.

$$\text{Profit} = Net_{sell} - C_{buy} = 33{,}942.40 - 30{,}058.10 = 3{,}884.30$$

**Step 9:** Calculate Return on Investment (ROI).

$$ROI = \frac{3{,}884.30}{30{,}058.10} \times 100 = 12.92\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Tax Note for KTU:** Since the holding period is **6 months** (less than 12 months for shares listed on Indian exchanges — Section 111A of Income Tax Act), this profit is a **Short-Term Capital Gain (STCG)** and is taxed at **15%** (plus applicable cess). Students are not required to compute this tax in the question, but mentioning it earns extra marks.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Breakeven Price

**Question:** An investor wants to buy 500 shares of ABC Ltd. through a discount broker. If she is willing to spend a maximum of ₹1,00,000 (total), what is the maximum price per share she can afford, ignoring STT and stamp duty (assume they are negligible)?

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Brokerage + GST = ₹20 + ₹3.60 = ₹23.60 (flat per order).

**Step 2:** Available amount for share value.

$$Available = 1{,}00{,}000 - 23.60 = 99{,}976.40$$

**Step 3:** Maximum price per share.

$$P_{max} = \frac{99{,}976.40}{500} = 199.95$$

$$\boxed{P_{max} \approx ₹199.95 \text{ per share}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Buy vs. Sell Process

| Parameter | Buying Process | Selling Process |
|---|---|---|
| **Initiator** | Investor who wants ownership | Investor who already owns shares |
| **Pre-condition** | Sufficient funds in bank account | Shares must be in Demat account |
| **Order placed** | Buy order | Sell order |
| **Cost components** | Price + STT + Stamp Duty + Brokerage + GST | Price - STT - Brokerage - GST |
| **Settlement result** | Shares credited, money debited | Money credited, shares debited |
| **Regulatory body** | SEBI (overall), Depository (Demat) | Same |

> [!TIP]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often forget that **stamp duty is charged only on the buyer**, not on the seller. Examiners frequently test this distinction.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Complete Buy/Sell Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Investor Decision] --> B{Buy or Sell?}
    B -->|Buy| C[Ensure Bank Account has Funds]
    B -->|Sell| D[Ensure Demat Account has Shares]
    C --> E[Log in to Trading Account]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Select Stock and Place Order]
    F --> G[Choose Order Type: Market, Limit, Stop-Loss]
    G --> H[Order Sent to Broker's System]
    H --> I[Broker Routes to Exchange NSE or BSE]
    I --> J[Order Matching Engine Matches Buyers and Sellers]
    J --> K{Trade Executed?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Contract Note Generated]
    K -->|No| M[Order Remains Pending or Expires]
    L --> N[T+1 Day Settlement]
    N --> O[Shares and Money Exchanged via Clearing Corporation]
    O --> P[Trade Complete]
    M --> Q[Investor Modifies or Cancels Order]
    Q --> E
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: The Triad of Accounts

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BAS[Investor Setup]
        BA[Bank Account]
        TA[Trading Account]
        DA[Demat Account]
    end
    BA <--> TA
    TA <--> DA
    BA <--> DA
    EX[Stock Exchange NSE BSE] --> TA
    DA --> DP[Depository CDSL or NSDL]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Order Matching Priority

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O[Incoming Order] --> P[Check Price: Best Price First]
    P --> Q[If Same Price: Check Time: Earliest First]
    Q --> R[Match with Opposite Order]
    R --> S[Execute Trade]
    S --> T[Settlement T+1 Day]
```

## 4.4 Architecture Diagram: Regulatory Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GOV[Government of India Ministry of Finance] --> SEBI[SEBI: Securities and Exchange Board of India]
    SEBI --> NSE[National Stock Exchange NSE]
    SEBI --> BSE[Bombay Stock Exchange BSE]
    NSE --> NSCCL[NSCCL Clearing Corporation]
    BSE --> ICCL[ICCL Clearing Corporation]
    SEBI --> DEP[Depositories CDSL and NSDL]
    SEBI --> BROK[Stock Brokers and Depository Participants]
    BROK --> INV[Retail and Institutional Investors]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents an entity or process. Arrows show the flow of orders, money, or regulatory control. For a KTU 7-mark question on "Explain the process of buying shares", drawing this kind of block diagram first and then explaining each step will easily fetch full marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** *Define the Secondary Market. How is it different from the Primary Market?*

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The **Secondary Market** is the market where already-issued shares are traded among investors, without the company itself being involved in the transaction. It is regulated by **SEBI** and operates through recognized stock exchanges like **NSE** and **BSE**.
>
> | Aspect | Primary Market | Secondary Market |
> |---|---|---|
> | Issuer | Company issues new shares | Investors trade existing shares |
> | Capital flow | Goes to the company | Goes from one investor to another |
> | Examples | IPO, FPO, Rights Issue | Daily trading on NSE/BSE |
> | Price determination | Fixed by company (book building) | Determined by demand and supply |

---

### **Question 2:** *List the three accounts required to start trading in shares in India and state the purpose of each.*

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The three accounts form the **Triad of Accounts**:
> 1. **Demat Account:** Holds shares in electronic (dematerialized) form. Opened with a Depository Participant (DP) linked to CDSL or NSDL.
> 2. **Trading Account:** Used to place buy/sell orders on the stock exchange. Opened with a SEBI-registered broker.
> 3. **Bank Account:** Linked to both accounts for money settlement. Fund transfer happens via this account on T+1 settlement day.
>
> *All three are mandatory under SEBI regulations for any individual investor in India.*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

#### (a) **Explain in detail the step-by-step process of buying shares in the Indian stock market. [7 Marks]**

> **Model Answer (7 Marks):**
>
> **Step 1 — Account Opening:** The investor opens a Demat account (with a DP), a Trading account (with a broker), and links them to a bank account. KYC and PAN submission is mandatory. **(1 mark)**
>
> **Step 2 — Market Research:** The investor studies the company using fundamental analysis (P/E ratio, earnings, balance sheet) and technical analysis (charts, trends) before deciding to buy. **(1 mark)**
>
> **Step 3 — Choosing a Broker:** The investor selects a full-service broker (advisory + execution) or a discount broker (execution only, lower cost). **(0.5 marks)**
>
> **Step 4 — Placing the Order:** The investor logs into the trading platform and places a buy order specifying: stock scrip code, quantity, price, and order type (Market or Limit). **(1 mark)**
>
> **Step 5 — Order Routing:** The broker's system routes the order to the exchange's electronic trading platform (NEAT for NSE, BOLT for BSE). **(0.5 marks)**
>
> **Step 6 — Order Matching:** The exchange's Order Matching Engine applies the **Price-Time Priority** rule to match the buy order with a corresponding sell order. **(1 mark)**
>
> **Step 7 — Trade Execution and Confirmation:** Once matched, the trade is executed. The broker issues a **Contract Note** confirming the trade, with details of price, quantity, brokerage, STT, and other charges. **(1 mark)**
>
> **Step 8 — Settlement (T+1):** On the next business day, the clearing corporation (NSCCL/ICCL) ensures that shares are credited to the buyer's Demat account and money is debited from the buyer's bank account and credited to the seller. This process is called **novation** and it eliminates counterparty risk. **(1 mark)**

#### (b) **Calculate the total cost of purchasing 1,000 shares of PQR Ltd. at ₹250 per share. Brokerage is charged at the rate of 0.03% of turnover. STT is 0.1% on purchase, stamp duty is 0.015%, and GST is 18% on brokerage. Assume the order is executed. [7 Marks]**

> **Model Answer (7 Marks):**
>
> **Step 1:** Calculate Turnover.
> $$Turnover = 250 \times 1{,}000 = 2{,}50{,}000$$
> **(1 mark)**
>
> **Step 2:** Calculate Brokerage.
> $$B = 0.03\% \times 2{,}50{,}000 = 75$$
> **(1 mark)**
>
> **Step 3:** Calculate STT on Purchase.
> $$STT = 0.1\% \times 2{,}50{,}000 = 250$$
> **(1 mark)**
>
> **Step 4:** Calculate Stamp Duty.
> $$Stamp = 0.015\% \times 2{,}50{,}000 = 37.50$$
> **(1 mark)**
>
> **Step 5:** Calculate GST on Brokerage.
> $$GST = 18\% \times 75 = 13.50$$
> **(1 mark)**
>
> **Step 6:** Total Cost.
> $$C = 2{,}50{,}000 + 75 + 250 + 37.50 + 13.50 = 2{,}50{,}376$$
> **(2 marks)**
>
> $$\boxed{\text{Total Cost} = ₹2{,}50{,}376}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

#### (a) **What is a Demat account? Explain the role of Depositories and Depository Participants in the process of buying and selling shares. [7 Marks]**

> **Model Answer (7 Marks):**
>
> A **Demat Account** (short for Dematerialized Account) is an electronic account that holds an investor's shares and securities in digital form, eliminating the need for physical share certificates. **(1 mark)**
>
> **Role of Depositories:** A Depository is an organization that holds these securities in electronic form. In India, there are two depositories: **NSDL (National Securities Depository Limited)** and **CDSL (Central Depository Services Limited)**. The Depository is the custodian of the electronic shares and is analogous to a bank that holds money. **(2 marks)**
>
> **Role of Depository Participants (DPs):** DPs are agents of the depositories — they are the front-end interface for investors. Examples include banks (HDFC, SBI), brokers (Zerodha, ICICI Direct), and financial institutions. Investors cannot open a Demat account directly with NSDL/CDSL; they must go through a DP. The DP performs KYC, opens the account, and processes credit/debit instructions on behalf of the investor. **(2 marks)**
>
> **Process during a transaction:**
> - When a **buy trade** settles, the DP credits shares to the investor's Demat account based on instructions from the clearing corporation.
> - When a **sell trade** settles, the DP debits shares from the Demat account and transfers them to the buyer's account.
>
> **(2 marks)**

#### (b) **Distinguish between the following with examples: (i) Market Order and Limit Order, (ii) Full-Service Broker and Discount Broker, (iii) T+1 Settlement and T+2 Settlement. [7 Marks]**

> **Model Answer (7 Marks):**
>
> **(i) Market Order vs. Limit Order (2.5 marks)**
>
> | Feature | Market Order | Limit Order |
> |---|---|---|
> | Price | Executed at best current available price | Executed only at specified price or better |
> | Execution certainty | Almost certain | Not guaranteed (may not execute) |
> | Speed | Immediate | May take time |
> | Use case | Urgent trades | Price-sensitive trades |
> | Example | *"Buy 100 shares of TCS at market price"* | *"Buy 100 shares of TCS only at ₹3,500 or less"* |
>
> **(ii) Full-Service Broker vs. Discount Broker (2.5 marks)**
>
> | Feature | Full-Service Broker | Discount Broker |
> |---|---|---|
> | Services | Trading + Research + Advisory | Trading only |
> | Brokerage | Higher (% based) | Flat ₹20/order |
> | Target client | Beginners, long-term investors | Active traders, self-directed investors |
> | Examples | ICICI Direct, HDFC Securities | Zerodha, Groww, Upstox |
>
> **(iii) T+1 Settlement vs. T+2 Settlement (2 marks)**
>
> - **T+1 Settlement:** Trade is settled 1 business day after the trade date. Currently applicable in India (mandated by SEBI from January 2023).
> - **T+2 Settlement:** Trade is settled 2 business days after the trade date. Was the previous norm in India.
> - T+1 reduces counterparty risk, improves market liquidity, and aligns India with global best practices.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Primary and Secondary Markets:** Many students write "buying and selling happens in the primary market" — WRONG. The primary market is only for new issues. Buying and selling of *existing* shares is **secondary market**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Stamp Duty on Buy Side:** Stamp duty is **NOT** charged on selling — only on buying. Forgetting this loses 1 mark easily.
>
> 3. **Writing "SEBI" as "SEBI Bank":** SEBI is a **regulatory body**, not a bank. Always write: "SEBI — Securities and Exchange Board of India, headquartered in Mumbai."
>
> 4. **Using old brokerage rates:** The post-October 2024 SEBI rule mandates **₹20 flat brokerage** for all retail orders. Using old percentage-based rates loses marks.
>
> 5. **Skipping the T+1 update:** SEBI moved from T+2 to **T+1** settlement in January 2023. Writing T+2 is outdated.
>
> 6. **No diagram in 7-mark questions:** A block diagram of the buy/sell process fetches at least 1 extra mark. Always draw a flow diagram.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- [x] **Secondary Market** = where existing shares are bought and sold (regulated by SEBI).
- [x] **Primary Market** = where new shares are issued (IPO, FPO).
- [x] **Triad of Accounts:** Demat + Trading + Bank.
- [x] **Demat Account** is held with a **Depository Participant (DP)** of **NSDL or CDSL**.
- [x] **Broker** must be **SEBI-registered** to execute trades.
- [x] **NSE** uses NEAT; **BSE** uses BOLT as trading systems.
- [x] **Order Matching** follows **Price-Time Priority** (best price first, then earliest time).
- [x] **Order Types:** Market, Limit, Stop-Loss, Stop-Loss Limit, GTT, IOC.
- [x] **Settlement Cycle:** **T+1 day** (as per SEBI 2023 mandate).
- [x] **Clearing Corporations:** **NSCCL** (for NSE) and **ICCL** (for BSE) guarantee settlement.
- [x] **STT on Purchase** = 0.1% of turnover (charged to buyer).
- [x] **STT on Sale (delivery)** = 0.1% of turnover (charged to seller).
- [x] **Stamp Duty** = 0.015% of turnover, charged **only on buy side**.
- [x] **GST on Brokerage** = 18% of brokerage amount.
- [x] **Brokerage (Discount Broker)** = **₹20 flat** per executed order (post-Oct 2024 SEBI rule).
- [x] **Short Selling** is **NOT allowed** for retail investors in India.
- [x] **SEBI Headquarters:** Mumbai. **Stock Exchanges in India:** NSE, BSE (the two largest).
- [x] **Capital Gain Tax:** < 12 months holding = **STCG @ 15%**; ≥ 12 months = **LTCG @ 10%** (above ₹1 lakh exemption).
- [x] **Formula Triad:** $C_{buy}$, $C_{sell}$, $\text{P\&L}$ — memorize them cold.
- [x] **Always draw a block diagram** in 7-mark questions on process flows.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Mnemonic — "SABAR":**
> **S**econdary market for trading,
> **A**ccounts (Demat, Trading, Bank),
> **B**roker places the order,
> **A**lways settles in **T+1**,
> **R**egulated by **SEBI**.

---

*End of Module 1 Notes — Topic: Buying and Selling of Shares*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Buying on Margin and Short Sale
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
