> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Buying on Margin and Short Sale

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Buying on Margin

### Formal Academic Definition
**Buying on Margin** is a leveraged trading strategy in which an investor purchases securities by paying a portion of the total purchase price in cash (called the *equity* or *margin deposit*) and borrowing the remaining balance from a registered broker-dealer, using the purchased securities themselves as collateral for the loan. The borrowed funds accrue interest at the broker's call money rate, and the investor's equity in the position fluctuates daily with market price movements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Buying on margin is a core topic in Indian Capital Markets and forms the basis for understanding leverage, margin trading facility (MTF) offered by Indian stock exchanges (NSE/BSE), and SEBI's regulatory framework on margin obligations.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Home Loan" Parallel
Think of buying a stock on margin exactly like buying a house with a home loan:
- **The Down Payment** is your *initial margin* (say 20–50% of house value).
- **The Bank Loan** is what the *broker lends you* against your stock collateral.
- **The House itself** is the *collateral* — if you stop paying the loan, the bank seizes the house and sells it.
- **EMI / Interest** is the *interest charged by the broker* on borrowed funds.
- If house prices fall below the loan amount, the bank issues a **margin call** — just like your broker does when your stock value drops.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Vocabulary Anchor:**
> - **Margin Account** → A special brokerage account that allows borrowing.
> - **Initial Margin** → Minimum % of own funds required at purchase (regulated by SEBI/Exchange).
> - **Maintenance Margin** → Minimum % of equity you must maintain as the price moves.
> - **Margin Call** → Broker's demand to deposit more cash or sell securities when equity falls below maintenance margin.

---

## 1.2 Short Sale (Short Selling)

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Short Sale** (or *Short Selling*) is an inverse trading strategy in which an investor *sells securities they do not currently own* by borrowing the shares from a broker's inventory (or another client's account), with the legal obligation to **buy back (cover) the shares at a later date** and return them to the lender. The short seller profits if the security's price **declines** between the sale and the buy-back.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Borrowed Umbrella" Trick
Imagine you **borrow your friend's umbrella** (which you don't own) on a sunny morning and **sell it in the market for ₹500**. Later in the afternoon, when the price drops because it starts looking cloudy and demand falls, you **buy the same umbrella back for ₹300** and return it to your friend. Your profit = ₹500 − ₹300 = **₹200**. That, in essence, is short selling — **selling high first, buying back low later**, with the borrowed asset returned to its original owner.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Risk Asymmetry:** Unlike buying on margin (where maximum loss = your investment), a short sale has **theoretically UNLIMITED LOSS POTENTIAL** because a stock's price can rise infinitely, forcing you to buy back at any price.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Vocabulary Anchor:**
> - **Short Seller** → The trader who initiates the short sale.
> - **Short Position** → The outstanding obligation to return borrowed shares.
> - **Cover / Buy to Cover** → The act of repurchasing the borrowed shares to close the short.
> - **Short Interest** → Total number of shares currently shorted in the market.
> - **Short Squeeze** → A rapid price spike forcing short sellers to cover at losses.
> - **Divident Liability** → Short sellers must pay any dividends issued during the borrowing period to the lender.
</br>

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Buying on Margin — Operational Mechanics (Step-by-Step)

### Step 1: Opening a Margin Account
The investor registers with a broker and signs a **margin agreement** (analogous to SEBI's *Margin Trading Facility* or MTF agreement in India). The broker checks creditworthiness and risk appetite.

### Step 2: Determining the Marginable Purchase
Assume a stock trades at **₹100 per share**, and the investor wants to buy **1,000 shares**.

$$\text{Total Purchase Value} = 1{,}000 \times ₹100 = ₹1{,}00{,}000$$

### Step 3: Calculating Own Funds vs Borrowed Funds
Suppose the **initial margin requirement = 50%** (a standard illustrative figure; in India, SEBI mandates equity intraday margins and exchange-set MTF margins).

$$\text{Own Funds (Equity)} = 50\% \times ₹1{,}00{,}000 = ₹50{,}000$$

$$\text{Borrowed from Broker} = 50\% \times ₹1{,}00{,}000 = ₹50{,}000$$

### Step 4: Daily Mark-to-Market (MTM) & Equity Recalculation
Stock prices fluctuate daily. The broker recalculates the **investor's equity** each day:

$$\text{Equity} = \text{Market Value of Securities} - \text{Amount Borrowed}$$

### Step 5: The Margin Call Trigger
If equity falls below the **maintenance margin** (typically 25–30% of market value), the broker issues a **margin call** demanding the investor deposit more cash.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why margin calls exist:** They protect the broker. If the stock price crashes and you walk away, the broker must still be able to recover the loan by selling your collateral. Without margin calls, brokers would absorb all the downside risk.

---

## 2.2 Short Sale — Operational Mechanics (Step-by-Step)

### Step 1: Locate Borrowable Shares
The broker confirms shares are available in its **securities lending pool** (often from other clients' margin accounts, institutional investors, or the broker's own inventory).

### Step 2: Execute the Short Sale
The investor sells the borrowed shares in the open market. The proceeds (cash) are deposited into the investor's account but are typically **frozen as collateral** — they cannot be withdrawn freely.

### Step 3: Maintain Collateral / Margin
Regulators (SEBI in India, Reg T in the US) require the short seller to post margin — typically **150% of the short sale value** in the US (50% initial + 100% of the loan value), though Indian short selling has its own rules.

### Step 4: Cover the Short
When (and if) the price falls, the short seller buys back the shares at the lower price and returns them to the lender, pocketing the difference (minus borrow fees, commissions, and any dividend payments owed).

### Step 5: Unlimited Loss Scenario
If the price rises instead, the short seller suffers a loss. Because there is **no upper bound** on stock prices, the loss is theoretically unlimited.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory formulas for the KTU ESE — memorize these.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Margin Percentage | $\text{Margin \%} = \dfrac{\text{Equity}}{\text{Market Value of Securities}} \times 100$ | Must exceed maintenance margin |
| 2 | Buying on Margin – Equity | $\text{Equity} = \text{Market Value} - \text{Loaned Amount}$ | Mark-to-market daily |
| 3 | Margin Call Trigger Price (Long) | $P_{\text{call}} = \dfrac{\text{Loan}}{\text{Number of Shares} \times (1 - \text{Maintenance Margin \%})}$ | Solve for the price that triggers the call |
| 4 | Short Sale Proceeds (Gross) | $\text{Gross Proceeds} = \text{Shares Borrowed} \times \text{Sale Price}$ | Cash received from market sale |
| 5 | Short Sale Profit / Loss | $P/L = (\text{Sale Price} - \text{Buy-back Price}) \times \text{Shares} - \text{Borrow Fees} - \text{Dividends Paid}$ | Subtracted for net P/L |
| 6 | Short Sale Margin Requirement | $\text{Required Collateral} = \text{Short Proceeds} \times \text{Margin \%}$ | SEBI/Reg-T rules apply |
| 7 | Leverage Multiplier | $\text{Leverage} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Initial Margin \%}}$ | e.g., 50% margin = 2× leverage |
| 8 | Return on Invested Capital (Margin) | $R_{\text{margin}} = \dfrac{\text{Price Change \%}}{\text{Initial Margin \%}}$ | Amplified return |
| 9 | Maximum Loss (Long Margin) | $\text{Max Loss} = \text{Initial Equity Invested} + \text{Accrued Interest}$ | Capped at equity |
| 10 | Maximum Loss (Short Sale) | **UNLIMITED** | Price can rise indefinitely |

</br>

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Buying on Margin with Margin Call

### Problem Setup
An investor buys **200 shares of XYZ Ltd.** at **₹500 per share** using a margin account. The initial margin requirement is **50%**, the maintenance margin is **30%**, and the broker's interest rate on the borrowed amount is **8% per annum**. After 1 year, the stock price changes to a new value. Determine the investor's ROI under different price scenarios and the price that triggers a margin call.

### Step 1: Compute Initial Investment Components

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Purchase Value} &= 200 \times ₹500 = ₹1{,}00{,}000 \\[6pt]
\text{Investor's Own Equity} &= 50\% \times ₹1{,}00{,}000 = ₹50{,}000 \\[6pt]
\text{Amount Borrowed} &= ₹1{,}00{,}000 - ₹50{,}000 = ₹50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Derive the Margin Call Price

The broker issues a margin call when:

$$
\text{Equity} = \text{Market Value} - \text{Loan} < 30\% \times \text{Market Value}
$$

Let $P$ = price per share triggering the call, and $N = 200$ shares.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N \cdot P - \text{Loan} &= 0.30 \times N \cdot P \\[6pt]
200 \cdot P - 50{,}000 &= 0.30 \times 200 \cdot P \\[6pt]
200P - 60P &= 50{,}000 \\[6pt]
140P &= 50{,}000 \\[6pt]
P_{\text{call}} &= \dfrac{50{,}000}{140} = ₹357.14
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** If XYZ Ltd. falls **below ₹357.14 per share**, the broker will demand additional funds (a margin call).

### Step 3: ROI under Price Scenarios

**Scenario A — Price rises to ₹600 after 1 year:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ending Market Value} &= 200 \times ₹600 = ₹1{,}20{,}000 \\[6pt]
\text{Ending Equity} &= ₹1{,}20{,}000 - ₹50{,}000 = ₹70{,}000 \\[6pt]
\text{Interest Paid} &= 8\% \times ₹50{,}000 = ₹4{,}000 \\[6pt]
\text{Net Profit} &= ₹70{,}000 - ₹50{,}000 - ₹4{,}000 = ₹16{,}000 \\[6pt]
\text{ROI} &= \dfrac{16{,}000}{50{,}000} \times 100 = \mathbf{32\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Scenario B — Price falls to ₹400 after 1 year (above margin call):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ending Market Value} &= 200 \times ₹400 = ₹80{,}000 \\[6pt]
\text{Ending Equity} &= ₹80{,}000 - ₹50{,}000 = ₹30{,}000 \\[6pt]
\text{Interest Paid} &= 8\% \times ₹50{,}000 = ₹4{,}000 \\[6pt]
\text{Net Loss} &= ₹30{,}000 - ₹50{,}000 - ₹4{,}000 = -₹24{,}000 \\[6pt]
\text{ROI} &= \dfrac{-24{,}000}{50{,}000} \times 100 = \mathbf{-48\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison — Without leverage (all-cash purchase):**

- At ₹600 → Profit = (₹600 − ₹500) × 200 = ₹20,000 → ROI = **20%**
- At ₹400 → Loss = (₹400 − ₹500) × 200 = −₹20,000 → ROI = **−20%**

> **Insight:** Margin **amplifies both gains and losses by the leverage factor (2×)**. The 20% stock gain became 32% (less than 2× because of interest), and the −20% loss became −48% (more than 2× because of interest).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Short Sale Profit/Loss Mechanics

### Problem Setup
An investor short-sells **500 shares of ABC Corp.** at **₹250 per share**. The broker charges a **borrow fee of 3% per annum** on the value of the short position. The investor covers the short after **6 months**. Compute P/L under three price scenarios: ₹200, ₹250, and ₹350.

### Step 1: Initial Cash Inflow

$$
\text{Initial Sale Proceeds} = 500 \times ₹250 = ₹1{,}25{,}000
$$

### Step 2: Borrow Fee Accrual (6 months)

$$
\text{Borrow Fee} = 3\% \times ₹1{,}25{,}000 \times \dfrac{6}{12} = ₹1{,}875
$$

### Step 3: P/L for Each Scenario

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(a) Price drops to ₹200 (Profitable):} \\[4pt]
\text{Buy-back Cost} &= 500 \times ₹200 = ₹1{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Gross Profit} &= ₹1{,}25{,}000 - ₹1{,}00{,}000 = ₹25{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Net Profit} &= ₹25{,}000 - ₹1{,}875 = \mathbf{₹23{,}125} \\[10pt]
\textbf{(b) Price unchanged at ₹250 (Break-even before fees):} \\[4pt]
\text{Net P/L} &= 0 - ₹1{,}875 = \mathbf{-₹1{,}875} \quad \text{(Loss equal to borrow fee)} \\[10pt]
\textbf{(c) Price rises to ₹350 (Loss):} \\[4pt]
\text{Buy-back Cost} &= 500 \times ₹350 = ₹1{,}75{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Gross Loss} &= ₹1{,}25{,}000 - ₹1{,}75{,}000 = -₹50{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Net Loss} &= -₹50{,}000 - ₹1{,}875 = \mathbf{-₹51{,}875}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Dividend Liability Reminder:** If ABC Corp. paid a dividend of, say, ₹5 per share during the 6 months, the short seller would owe ₹5 × 500 = ₹2,500 to the lender of the shares — further reducing net P/L.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Buying on Margin (Long) | Short Sale |
|-----------|--------------------------|-------------|
| Market View | Bullish (price will rise) | Bearish (price will fall) |
| Cash Flow at Entry | Outflow (own funds invested) | Inflow (proceeds from sale) |
| Max Loss | Limited to own equity + interest | **Theoretically unlimited** |
| Max Gain | Limited to full price of stock | Limited to full price (price → 0) |
| Leverage Source | Borrowed funds from broker | Borrowed shares from broker |
| Margin Trigger | Maintenance margin breach | Mark-to-market margin call |
| Cost of Carry | Interest on loan | Borrow fees + dividend liability |
| SEBI/Reg Position | Permitted (MTF framework) | Permitted (with intraday/settlement rules) |

</br>

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Buying on Margin — Functional Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    invA[Investor] -->|1. Opens Margin Account| brkA[Broker Dealer]
    brkA -->|2. Approves Credit Limit| accA[Margin Account Created]
    accA -->|3. Investor pays 50 percent| depA[Own Equity Deposit]
    accA -->|4. Broker lends 50 percent| loanA[Loan Disbursed]
    depA --> loanA
    loanA -->|5. Combined funds used| buyA[Buy Securities on Exchange]
    buyA -->|6. Securities held as collateral| colA[Collateral Pool]
    colA -->|7. Daily MTM valuation| mtmA[Equity Recalculation]
    mtmA -->|8. Check maintenance margin| chkA{Margin OK?}
    chkA -->|Yes| holdA[Hold Position]
    chkA -->|No| callA[Margin Call Issued]
    callA -->|9a. Deposit more cash| depB[Additional Funds]
    callA -->|9b. Sell securities| sellA[Forced Liquidation]
    holdA -->|10. Price rises| profA[Realized Profit]
    holdA -->|11. Price falls| lossA[Realized Loss]
    profA --> closeA[Position Closed]
    lossA --> closeA
```

## 4.2 Short Sale — Functional Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    invB[Short Seller] -->|1. Identify Bearish Stock| locB[Locate Borrowable Shares]
    locB -->|2. Borrow from broker inventory| borB[Shares Borrowed]
    borB -->|3. Sell in open market| sB[Sell Order Executed]
    sB -->|4. Receive sale proceeds| cashB[Sale Proceeds Frozen as Collateral]
    cashB -->|5. Daily MTM| mtB[Collateral Recalculation]
    mtB -->|6. Check margin requirement| chkB{Margin OK?}
    chkB -->|Yes| waitB[Await Price Decline]
    chkB -->|No| calB[Margin Call Deposit More Cash]
    waitB -->|7a. Price falls| buyB[Buy to Cover at Lower Price]
    waitB -->|7b. Price rises| lossB[Realized Loss Unlimited]
    buyB -->|8. Return shares to lender| retB[Shares Returned]
    retB -->|9. Pay borrow fees and dividends| feeB[Net Profit Realized]
    lossB --> feeB
    calB --> feeB
```

## 4.3 Margin Lifecycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> AccountOpened
    AccountOpened --> PositionActive: Buy with margin
    PositionActive --> Healthy: Equity above maintenance
    PositionActive --> MarginCall: Equity below maintenance
    Healthy --> PositionActive: Daily MTM
    MarginCall --> Healthy: Investor deposits cash
    MarginCall --> Liquidated: Investor fails to deposit
    Liquidated --> [*]
    PositionActive --> Closed: Investor voluntarily closes
    Closed --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid for the Diagram:** The broker monitors the position every day. As long as the investor's equity stays above the maintenance margin threshold, the position is "healthy." The instant equity falls below, the state transitions to a margin call. If unresolved, forced liquidation occurs.

</br>

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, 3 marks]**
> **Define buying on margin. What is the role of the maintenance margin in a margin account?**

### Model Answer (For Examiner's Reference)

**Buying on margin** is a leveraged investment strategy where an investor purchases securities by paying a portion of the purchase price in cash and borrowing the balance from a broker, using the securities as collateral. The investor's own contribution is called the *initial margin*, and the broker charges interest on the borrowed portion.

The **maintenance margin** is the minimum percentage of equity (own funds as a proportion of total market value) that the investor must maintain in the account at all times after the initial purchase. Its role is **risk containment for the broker** — if the market value of the securities falls such that the investor's equity percentage drops below the maintenance threshold, the broker issues a **margin call**, requiring the investor to either deposit additional cash or liquidate part of the position. This protects the broker from loan default risk in a falling market.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, 3 marks]**
> **Explain the concept of a short sale. Why is the maximum potential loss in a short sale considered unlimited?**

### Model Answer (For Examiner's Reference)

A **short sale** is a strategy in which an investor sells securities that he or she does not own, by first borrowing the shares from a broker. The investor later buys back the shares (called "covering the short") at a hopefully lower price to return them to the lender, profiting from the price decline.

The **maximum potential loss is unlimited** because while a stock's price can only fall to **zero** (capping the gain at the full sale price), there is **no upper limit** on how high a stock's price can rise. If the price rises sharply after the short sale, the short seller is forced to buy back at a much higher price, and since the price escalation is theoretically unbounded, the loss is also theoretically unbounded. This asymmetric risk profile is why regulators impose strict margin requirements on short sellers and why short selling is considered a high-risk strategy suitable primarily for sophisticated investors.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Module 1, 14 marks]**
>
> **(a)** An investor purchases **400 shares** of a stock at **₹250 per share** on margin. The initial margin requirement is **40%** and the maintenance margin is **25%**. The broker charges **10% interest per annum** on the borrowed amount. **(7 marks)**
>   - **(i)** Calculate the amount of own funds and the loan amount.
>   - **(ii)** At what price per share will a margin call be triggered?
>   - **(iii)** If after one year the stock price is **₹300**, compute the investor's Return on Investment (ROI).
>
> **(b)** Distinguish between **buying on margin** and **short selling** with respect to: market view, profit potential, loss potential, and margin requirements. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Sub-parts (i), (ii), (iii)**

**(i) Own funds and loan amount:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Purchase Value} &= 400 \times ₹250 = ₹1{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Own Funds} &= 40\% \times ₹1{,}00{,}000 = \mathbf{₹40{,}000} \\[4pt]
\text{Loan Amount} &= ₹1{,}00{,}000 - ₹40{,}000 = \mathbf{₹60{,}000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Margin Call Price:**

Let $P$ = price at which margin call is triggered.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Equity} &= 400P - 60{,}000 \\
\text{Maintenance Equity Required} &= 25\% \times 400P = 100P \\
\text{At margin call: } 400P - 60{,}000 &= 100P \\[4pt]
300P &= 60{,}000 \\[4pt]
P &= \mathbf{₹200 \text{ per share}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) ROI at ₹300 after 1 year:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ending Market Value} &= 400 \times ₹300 = ₹1{,}20{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Ending Equity} &= ₹1{,}20{,}000 - ₹60{,}000 = ₹60{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Interest Paid} &= 10\% \times ₹60{,}000 = ₹6{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Net Profit} &= ₹60{,}000 - ₹40{,}000 - ₹6{,}000 = ₹14{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{ROI} &= \dfrac{14{,}000}{40{,}000} \times 100 = \mathbf{35\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Distinction Table**

| Parameter | Buying on Margin | Short Selling |
|-----------|------------------|----------------|
| Market View | Bullish — expects price rise | Bearish — expects price fall |
| Profit Potential | Unlimited (in theory) | Capped at full price (when price → 0) |
| Loss Potential | Capped at own equity + interest | **Theoretically unlimited** |
| Margin Requirement | Initial + maintenance margin (long) | Initial + maintenance margin (short) |
| Cash Flow | Outflow at entry | Inflow at entry (frozen proceeds) |
| Cost of Carry | Interest on loan | Borrow fees + dividend liability |

> Each distinction carries ~1.75 marks. Use a **table** for clarity — examiners award full marks for crisp tabular comparisons.

---

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam — Module 1, 14 marks — INTERNAL CHOICE]**
>
> **(a)** An investor short-sells **600 shares** of a stock at **₹400 per share**. The initial margin requirement for short sales is **50%**, and the maintenance margin is **30%**. The borrow fee is **4% per annum**. The investor covers the short after **9 months** when the price falls to **₹320**. **(7 marks)**
>   - **(i)** Calculate the initial margin deposit required.
>   - **(ii)** At what price per share will a margin call be triggered on the short position?
>   - **(iii)** Compute the net profit/loss on the transaction, including the borrow fee.
>
> **(b)** Explain the **mechanics of a short squeeze** with a suitable example. How does it impact short sellers? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Sub-parts (i), (ii), (iii)**

**(i) Initial margin deposit:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Short Sale Proceeds} &= 600 \times ₹400 = ₹2{,}40{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Initial Margin (50\%)} &= 0.50 \times ₹2{,}40{,}000 = \mathbf{₹1{,}20{,}000} \\[4pt]
\text{Total Account Value} &= ₹2{,}40{,}000 + ₹1{,}20{,}000 = ₹3{,}60{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Margin call price on the short position:**

For a short position, equity = total account value − (current share price × shares). Let $P$ = buy-back price that triggers the call.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Equity} &= 600P_{\text{cover}} \quad \text{(since loan is 600 shares held short)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Standard formulation:** Equity in a short account = Account Value − (Cover Price × Shares). The maintenance margin requires Equity ≥ 30% of current market value (i.e., 30% × 600 × $P$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
3{,}60{,}000 - 600P &= 0.30 \times 600P \\[4pt]
3{,}60{,}000 - 600P &= 180P \\[4pt]
3{,}60{,}000 &= 780P \\[4pt]
P &= \mathbf{₹461.54 \text{ per share}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** If the share price rises **above ₹461.54**, a margin call is triggered.

**(iii) Net P/L at cover price ₹320:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sale Proceeds} &= 600 \times ₹400 = ₹2{,}40{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Buy-back Cost} &= 600 \times ₹320 = ₹1{,}92{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Gross Profit} &= ₹2{,}40{,}000 - ₹1{,}92{,}000 = ₹48{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Borrow Fee (9 months)} &= 4\% \times ₹2{,}40{,}000 \times \dfrac{9}{12} = ₹7{,}200 \\[4pt]
\text{Net Profit} &= ₹48{,}000 - ₹7{,}200 = \mathbf{₹40{,}800}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Short Squeeze**

A **short squeeze** is a rapid and sharp increase in a stock's price that forces short sellers to buy back shares to cover their positions, which in turn drives the price even higher, triggering further covering — a self-reinforcing upward spiral.

**Example:** Suppose XYZ stock has 10 million shares sold short (high short interest). If an unexpected positive announcement (e.g., a takeover bid) causes the price to rise 15% in a single day, short sellers start rushing to cover. Their buy orders further push the price up, forcing other shorts to cover, and so on. The price can double in hours — famously seen in the **GameStop (GME) saga of January 2021**, where retail investors coordinated buying, causing hedge funds with massive short positions to incur billions in losses.

**Impact on short sellers:**
- Forced covering at high prices → **massive losses**.
- Margin calls from brokers due to deteriorating equity.
- Cascading panic covering creates a **death spiral** for the short thesis.
- Demonstrates the **unlimited loss potential** of short positions and the importance of **stop-loss discipline** and **position sizing** when shorting.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Zones**
>
> 1. **Confusing "margin" with "profit."** Margin is the *equity portion*, not profit. Many students write "margin = profit earned" → deduct 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract interest / borrow fees** in the final ROI calculation. KTU examiners specifically look for the *net* figure. Missing it → lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong sign convention for short P/L.** Always: Profit = Sale Price − Buy-back Price. A common error is reversing this. → Lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Not stating the margin-call condition explicitly.** Examiners award 1 mark solely for the algebraic setup $Equity = Maintenance\ \% \times Market\ Value$. Skip it → lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Ignoring dividend liability in short sales.** In a 7-mark short sale problem, mentioning "dividend payable to lender" earns a bonus 0.5 mark.
> 6. **Writing "unlimited profit" for short sales.** WRONG. Short sale profit is capped at the full stock price. Only **loss** is unlimited. Examiners will deduct 1 mark for this reversal.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1: Buying on Margin & Short Sale**

- **Buying on margin** = paying part in cash + borrowing the rest from the broker; the securities act as collateral.
- **Initial margin** = % paid by investor at purchase. **Maintenance margin** = % that must be maintained subsequently.
- **Margin call** is triggered when *Equity ÷ Market Value* falls below the maintenance margin threshold.
- **Margin Call Price (Long)** formula: $P = \dfrac{\text{Loan}}{N \times (1 - \text{Maintenance Margin \%})}$.
- **Leverage = 1 ÷ Initial Margin %.** A 50% margin = 2× leverage, which amplifies both gains and losses.
- **Cost of carry on margin** = interest on the loan. Always subtract this before computing ROI.
- **Short sale** = sell first, buy back later, return the borrowed shares. Profit from price decline.
- **Maximum loss in short sale = unlimited** (price can rise indefinitely). **Maximum gain = capped at full price** (price can fall to zero).
- **Margin call price (Short):** Solve $Account\ Value - N \cdot P = 0.30 \times N \cdot P$ for $P$.
- **Cost of carry on short sale** = borrow fees + any dividends the lender is owed during the borrowing period.
- **Short interest** = total shares currently shorted; high short interest increases the risk of a **short squeeze**.
- **Short squeeze** = rapid price rise forcing covering; can cause catastrophic losses (e.g., GameStop 2021).
- **SEBI regulations** in India govern both margin trading (MTF) and short selling with specific settlement and margin rules.
- **Mark-to-market (MTM)** happens daily for both positions — the account is revalued every trading day.
- **Forced liquidation** by the broker is the last resort if margin calls are unmet.
- **Comparison: Long vs Short** — opposite market views, opposite cash flows, opposite loss asymmetries.
- **Key KTU answer trick:** Always state the margin-call *condition* as an equation first, then solve — examiners reward structured algebra.
- **Memorize the 10 formulas** in the KTU Formula Sheet above — they cover ~80% of all Module 1 numerical questions.

</br>

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stock Market Quotations and Indices: Individual Stock Quotation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stock Market Quotations and Indices: Individual Stock Quotation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stock Market Quotation** refers to the real-time, formalized, and standardized display of price and volume information relating to securities (stocks, bonds, derivatives) actively traded on a recognized stock exchange. It is the most fundamental unit of market communication, providing investors with the current **bid price**, **ask (offer) price**, **last traded price**, and trading volume of a particular financial instrument.

An **Individual Stock Quotation** is the specific data record published for a single publicly listed company's equity share. It consolidates critical trading data — including the **Open, High, Low, Close (OHLC)** prices, traded volume, day range, 52-week range, market capitalization, Price-to-Earnings (P/E) ratio, Earnings Per Share (EPS), and dividend yield — into a single standardized line item, often referred to in market parlance as a **"ticker quote."**

In the Indian context, individual stock quotations for NSE-listed stocks follow the standard **NSE Symbol Format** (e.g., `RELIANCE`, `TCS`, `INFY`), while on the BSE, the **Scrip Code** (e.g., `500325` for Reliance) is used.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Digital Price Tag**
> Imagine walking into a giant supermarket. Every product — milk, bread, soap — has a small digital price tag that constantly updates. The tag shows: the **current price**, the **highest** someone paid today, the **lowest** someone paid today, the **opening** price of the day, and **how many units** have been sold. Now imagine that the "products" on the shelves are actually ownership slices (shares) of real companies like Apple, Tata Motors, or Infosys. A **stock market quotation** is exactly this: a real-time digital price tag for a tiny piece of a company.

The "supermarket" is the **stock exchange** (NSE/BSE), the "products" are **shares**, and the "digital price tag" is the **stock quotation**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Anchor

| Term | Layman Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Ticker Symbol** | The unique short code (e.g., `TCS`) that identifies a company on the exchange |
| **Last Traded Price (LTP)** | The price at which the most recent trade was executed |
| **Volume** | The total number of shares that changed hands during the trading session |
| **Bid Price** | The highest price a buyer is willing to pay at that instant |
| **Ask Price** | The lowest price a seller is willing to accept at that instant |
| **OHLC** | Open, High, Low, Close — the four canonical price data points of a session |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Individual Stock Quotation

A standard individual stock quotation, as displayed on platforms like **Yahoo Finance, NSE India, BSE India, Moneycontrol, or Bloomberg Terminal**, is built from the following layered data points:

### Layer 1 — Identity Layer
- **Ticker / Symbol** (e.g., `RELIANCE`)
- **Company Name** (Reliance Industries Limited)
- **Exchange of Listing** (NSE / BSE)
- **ISIN** (International Securities Identification Number — 12-character unique global ID)

### Layer 2 — Price Layer (OHLC)
- **Open Price (O):** The price of the first trade of the trading day.
- **High Price (H):** The highest price at which the stock traded during the session.
- **Low Price (L):** The lowest price at which the stock traded during the session.
- **Close Price (C):** The last traded price of the day (regular session close at **3:30 PM IST** for Indian markets).
- **Previous Close (PC):** The official closing price of the immediately preceding trading day.
- **Day's Range:** Displayed as `Low – High` (e.g., `₹2,485.00 – ₹2,510.50`).

### Layer 3 — Volume Layer
- **Volume:** Total number of shares traded during the day.
- **Average Volume:** Typically the 30-day rolling average.
- **Bid Size / Ask Size:** The number of shares being offered at the best bid and best ask.

### Layer 4 — Valuation Layer
- **Market Capitalization (Market Cap)**
- **Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio**
- **Earnings Per Share (EPS)**
- **Dividend Yield**
- **52-Week High / 52-Week Low**

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are routinely tested in KTU ESE and serve as the analytical backbone of any stock quotation question.

| # | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering/Finance Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Market Cap} = \text{LTP} \times \text{Total Outstanding Shares}$ | LTP in ₹, Shares in crore units, Market Cap in ₹ crore | Measures total market value of equity; basis for index weightage |
| 2 | $\text{EPS} = \dfrac{\text{Net Profit After Tax (PAT)}}{\text{Total Outstanding Shares}}$ | PAT in ₹, Shares in crore, EPS in ₹/share | Profit allocated to each equity share |
| 3 | $\text{P/E Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{LTP}}{\text{EPS}}$ | Both terms in ₹, ratio is dimensionless | Premium the market pays for ₹1 of earnings |
| 4 | $\text{Dividend Yield (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Dividend per Share}}{\text{LTP}} \times 100$ | DPS in ₹, LTP in ₹, output in % | Income return on a share, ignoring capital gain |
| 5 | $\text{Bid-Ask Spread} = \text{Ask Price} - \text{Bid Price}$ | Both in ₹ per share | Liquidity proxy — lower spread = higher liquidity |
| 6 | $\text{Spread \%} = \dfrac{\text{Ask} - \text{Bid}}{\text{Ask}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless, output in % | Normalized transaction cost benchmark |
| 7 | $\text{Day's Change (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{LTP} - \text{Previous Close}}{\text{Previous Close}} \times 100$ | Both in ₹, output in % | Day-on-day price performance indicator |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stock Quote Golden Rule:** Every line on a stock quotation is **verifiable** from either (a) the day's order book snapshot, or (b) the company's latest published financial statements.

## 2.3 Why These Metrics Matter

- **OHLC** enables **technical analysis** (candlestick charting, support/resistance levels).
- **Volume** confirms the **conviction** behind a price move — a price rise on high volume is more credible than one on thin volume.
- **P/E Ratio** allows **relative valuation** — comparison across companies in the same sector.
- **Market Cap** determines **index inclusion weight** in free-float-adjusted indices like **NIFTY 50** or **S&P BSE Sensex**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Analytical Example: Reading a Live Stock Quote

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample Quotation Displayed for "ABC Technologies Ltd." on NSE**
>
> | Field | Value |
> |---|---|
> | Symbol | ABCTECH |
> | Previous Close | ₹480.00 |
> | Open | ₹485.00 |
> | Day's High | ₹498.00 |
> | Day's Low | ₹482.50 |
> | Last Traded Price (LTP) | ₹495.00 |
> | Volume Traded | 12,50,000 shares |
> | Bid Price / Bid Size | ₹494.90 / 5,000 |
> | Ask Price / Ask Size | ₹495.10 / 4,500 |
> | 52-Week High / Low | ₹610.00 / ₹410.00 |
> | Total Outstanding Shares | 10 crore |
> | Annual EPS | ₹24.00 |
> | Annual Dividend per Share | ₹6.00 |

We will now derive **six core quotation-derived metrics** from this snapshot.

---

### 3.1.1 Day's Absolute Price Change

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Day's Change (\text{₹})} &= \text{LTP} - \text{Previous Close} \\
&= 495.00 - 480.00 \\
&= +\text{₹}15.00
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The stock is **up ₹15** in absolute terms compared to yesterday's close.

---

### 3.1.2 Day's Percentage Change

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Day's Change (\%)} &= \frac{\text{LTP} - \text{Previous Close}}{\text{Previous Close}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{15.00}{480.00} \times 100 \\
&= 3.125\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** ABCTECH has appreciated by **3.125%** in today's session.

---

### 3.1.3 Intraday Range Span (Volatility Indicator)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Day's Range} &= \text{Day's High} - \text{Day's Low} \\
&= 498.00 - 482.50 \\
&= \text{₹}15.50
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Range as \% of Open} &= \frac{15.50}{485.00} \times 100 \\
&= 3.196\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Intraday volatility is **3.20%** of the opening price — a moderately active session.

---

### 3.1.4 Market Capitalization

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Market Cap} &= \text{LTP} \times \text{Total Outstanding Shares} \\
&= 495.00 \times 10,00,00,000 \\
&= 4,950,00,00,000 \\
&= \text{₹}4,950 \text{ crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The total market value of ABCTECH's equity is **₹4,950 crore**, placing it firmly in the **large-cap** category (typically > ₹20,000 crore is large-cap; ₹5,000–₹20,000 crore is mid-cap).

---

### 3.1.5 Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{P/E Ratio} &= \frac{\text{LTP}}{\text{EPS}} \\
&= \frac{495.00}{24.00} \\
&= 20.625 \text{ times}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Investors are willing to pay **₹20.63 for every ₹1 of ABCTECH's annual earnings**. This is a reasonable P/E for a growth technology stock (peer sector average ≈ 22–25x).

---

### 3.1.6 Dividend Yield

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Dividend Yield (\%)} &= \frac{\text{Annual DPS}}{\text{LTP}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{6.00}{495.00} \times 100 \\
&= 1.212\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A pure income-investor holding ABCTECH at ₹495 earns a **1.21% cash return per year** in dividends, before any capital appreciation.

---

### 3.1.7 Bid-Ask Spread Analysis

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Spread (\text{₹})} &= \text{Ask Price} - \text{Bid Price} \\
&= 495.10 - 494.90 \\
&= \text{₹}0.20
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Spread (\%)} &= \frac{0.20}{495.10} \times 100 \\
&= 0.0404\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A **20-paise spread on a near-₹500 stock** signals **excellent liquidity**. For a market order, the implicit transaction cost is only 0.04% of the trade value.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix Summary

| Derived Metric | Computed Value | Investor Inference |
|---|---|---|
| Day's Change | +₹15.00 (+3.125%) | Bullish session |
| Intraday Range | ₹15.50 (3.20%) | Moderate volatility |
| Market Cap | ₹4,950 crore | Mid-cap stock |
| P/E Ratio | 20.625x | Fairly valued vs. sector |
| Dividend Yield | 1.212% | Modest income |
| Bid-Ask Spread | ₹0.20 (0.04%) | Highly liquid |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Anatomy of a Single Stock Quotation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Individual Stock Quotation]:::root
    A --> B[Identity Layer]:::layer1
    A --> C[Price Layer OHLC]:::layer2
    A --> D[Volume Layer]:::layer3
    A --> E[Valuation Layer]:::layer4
    A --> F[Liquidity Layer]:::layer5

    B --> B1[Ticker Symbol]
    B --> B2[Company Name]
    B --> B3[ISIN Code]
    B --> B4[Exchange Tag NSE BSE]

    C --> C1[Open Price]
    C --> C2[High Price]
    C --> C3[Low Price]
    C --> C4[Close Price]
    C --> C5[Previous Close]
    C --> C6[52 Week High Low]

    D --> D1[Day Volume]
    D --> D2[Average Volume 30D]
    D --> D3[Delivery Volume]

    E --> E1[Market Cap]
    E --> E2[Earnings Per Share EPS]
    E --> E3[Price to Earnings P E]
    E --> E4[Dividend Yield]
    E --> E5[Book Value]

    F --> F1[Bid Price]
    F --> F2[Ask Price]
    F --> F3[Bid Size]
    F --> F4[Ask Size]

    classDef root fill:#1f3a68,stroke:#0b1f3a,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef layer1 fill:#f9d77e,stroke:#7a5a00,color:#1a1a1a
    classDef layer2 fill:#a3d8f4,stroke:#0b4a75,color:#0b1f3a
    classDef layer3 fill:#b5e7a0,stroke:#2e5e1f,color:#1a1a1a
    classDef layer4 fill:#f4a3a3,stroke:#7a1f1f,color:#1a1a1a
    classDef layer5 fill:#d4b3f4,stroke:#4a1f7a,color:#1a1a1a
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The diagram above partitions a single stock quotation into **five logical data layers**. In a typical Bloomberg Terminal screen or NSE quote page, these layers are stacked vertically — Identity at the top, Liquidity at the bottom.

---

## 4.2 Process Flow — Lifecycle of a Stock Quotation Update

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Investor Places Order]:::step --> P2[Order Routed to Exchange]:::step
    P2 --> P3[Order Book Updated]:::step
    P3 --> P4{Match Found?}:::decision
    P4 -- Yes --> P5[Trade Executed]:::step
    P4 -- No --> P6[Order Stays in Book]:::step
    P5 --> P7[LTP Updated on Quote]:::step
    P5 --> P8[Volume Incremented]:::step
    P5 --> P9[OHLC Recalculated]:::step
    P7 --> P10[Broadcast to Data Vendors]:::step
    P8 --> P10
    P9 --> P10
    P10 --> P11[Display on Terminal and Mobile App]:::output

    classDef step fill:#cfe8f5,stroke:#0b4a75,color:#0b1f3a
    classDef decision fill:#fff2a8,stroke:#7a5a00,color:#1a1a1a
    classDef output fill:#b5e7a0,stroke:#2e5e1f,color:#1a1a1a
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Parallel:** This is identical in structure to a **distributed publish-subscribe system** in computer engineering — the exchange is the publisher, the data vendors (Bloomberg, Reuters, NSE feed) are brokers, and the trader's terminal is the subscriber. Each trade event triggers a **fan-out broadcast** that updates every layer of the quotation in real-time.

---

## 4.3 Conceptual Map — Quotation Data → Investor Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Stock Quotation Snapshot]:::input --> R1[Technical Analyst Reads]:::user
    Q --> R2[Fundamental Analyst Reads]:::user
    Q --> R3[Day Trader Reads]:::user

    R1 --> D1[Charts Support Resistance Trend]:::out
    R2 --> D2[P E EPS Dividend Yield Based Valuation]:::out
    R3 --> D3[Bid Ask Spread Volume for Entry Exit]:::out

    D1 --> F[Final Investment Decision Buy Sell Hold]:::final
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F

    classDef input fill:#1f3a68,stroke:#0b1f3a,color:#ffffff
    classDef user fill:#f9d77e,stroke:#7a5a00,color:#1a1a1a
    classDef out fill:#a3d8f4,stroke:#0b4a75,color:#0b1f3a
    classDef final fill:#b5e7a0,stroke:#2e5e1f,color:#1a1a1a
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define an Individual Stock Quotation. List its essential components.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **Individual Stock Quotation** is the standardized, real-time display of trading information for a single company's equity share on a stock exchange. It consolidates the security's identity and trading data into a single updatable record, refreshed continuously during market hours.

**Essential Components (any 4 = 3 marks):**
1. **Ticker Symbol / Scrip Code** — unique identifier (e.g., `RELIANCE`).
2. **OHLC Prices** — Open, High, Low, Close of the session.
3. **Last Traded Price (LTP)** — most recent execution price.
4. **Volume Traded** — total shares exchanged during the day.
5. **Bid and Ask Prices** — best buy and sell quotes in the order book.
6. **52-Week High and Low** — annual price extremes.
7. **Valuation Ratios** — P/E, EPS, Dividend Yield, Market Cap.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between the Bid Price and the Ask Price. Why is the spread between them important?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Bid Price | Ask Price (Offer) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Highest price a buyer is willing to pay | Lowest price a seller is willing to accept |
| **Order Book Side** | Buy side | Sell side |
| **Perspective** | From the buyer | From the seller |

**Importance of the Spread (any 2 = 1 mark):**
- **Liquity Indicator:** A narrow spread indicates high liquidity and a deep order book.
- **Transaction Cost:** The spread represents the implicit cost of an immediate market order.
- **Market Maker Profit:** Dealers and market makers earn the spread as their compensation.
- **Information Signal:** Wide spreads may signal uncertainty or impending news.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions in this module carry **14 marks**, with sub-parts typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The two alternatives (A & B) below are independent — students answer only **one** complete set.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**The following is the trading quote of "PQR Pharmaceuticals Ltd." as on the NSE:**

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Previous Close | ₹1,200.00 |
| Open | ₹1,215.00 |
| Day's High | ₹1,248.00 |
| Day's Low | ₹1,198.00 |
| Last Traded Price (LTP) | ₹1,235.00 |
| Volume | 8,00,000 shares |
| Bid Price / Ask Price | ₹1,234.50 / ₹1,235.40 |
| Total Outstanding Shares | 5 crore |
| Annual EPS | ₹48.00 |
| Annual Dividend per Share | ₹12.00 |

**(a)** Compute the **Day's Percentage Change**, **Market Capitalization**, and the **P/E Ratio** of the stock. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **Dividend Yield** and the **Bid-Ask Spread (%)**. Interpret what the spread tells you about the stock's liquidity. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Day's Percentage Change**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Day's Change (\%)} &= \frac{\text{LTP} - \text{Previous Close}}{\text{Previous Close}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1235.00 - 1200.00}{1200.00} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{35.00}{1200.00} \times 100 \\
&= 2.917\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verdict:** PQR is up **2.92%** in the session — a bullish move. **[2 Marks]**

**Step 2: Market Capitalization**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Market Cap} &= \text{LTP} \times \text{Total Outstanding Shares} \\
&= 1235.00 \times 5,00,00,000 \\
&= 6,175,00,00,000 \\
&= \text{₹}6,175 \text{ crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verdict:** Mid-cap stock (₹5,000–₹20,000 crore band). **[2 Marks]**

**Step 3: P/E Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{P/E Ratio} &= \frac{\text{LTP}}{\text{EPS}} \\
&= \frac{1235.00}{48.00} \\
&= 25.73 \text{ times}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verdict:** Market is paying ₹25.73 per ₹1 of earnings — typical for a pharma major. **[3 Marks]**

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 4: Dividend Yield**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Dividend Yield (\%)} &= \frac{\text{Annual DPS}}{\text{LTP}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{12.00}{1235.00} \times 100 \\
&= 0.972\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verdict:** Income return of **0.97% per annum** — modest. **[2 Marks]**

**Step 5: Bid-Ask Spread**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Spread (\text{₹})} &= \text{Ask} - \text{Bid} \\
&= 1235.40 - 1234.50 \\
&= \text{₹}0.90
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Spread (\%)} &= \frac{0.90}{1235.40} \times 100 \\
&= 0.0728\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verdict:** An **0.07% spread** is exceptionally tight, indicating **very high liquidity** in the order book. **[3 Marks]**

**Interpretation on Liquidity (1 Mark):**
A spread under **0.1%** on a stock above ₹1,000 signals deep institutional and retail participation, narrow order-book depth gaps, and minimal slippage for market orders. PQR is highly liquid and suitable for both short-term traders and long-term investors.

**Liquidity Conclusion:** PQR is **liquid enough for large block trades** without significant market impact. **[1 Mark]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Set** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**"LMN Auto Ltd." is listed on both NSE and BSE. Its recent quote on the NSE is tabulated below:**

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Ticker | LMNAUTO |
| Previous Close | ₹850.00 |
| Open | ₹860.00 |
| Day's High | ₹885.00 |
| Day's Low | ₹855.00 |
| LTP | ₹878.00 |
| Volume | 25,00,000 shares |
| 30-Day Average Volume | 10,00,000 shares |
| Bid / Ask | ₹877.80 / ₹878.20 |
| Total Outstanding Shares | 2 crore |
| Annual EPS | ₹35.00 |
| Annual Dividend per Share | ₹7.00 |

**(a)** Compute the **Intraday Range (%)**, **Market Cap**, and **P/E Ratio**. Comment on whether the current P/E is high or low for a typical auto-sector stock. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **Dividend Yield** and **Bid-Ask Spread (%)**. Comment on the volume activity by comparing today's volume with the 30-day average. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Intraday Range (%)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Range} &= \text{Day's High} - \text{Day's Low} \\
&= 885.00 - 855.00 = \text{₹}30.00
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Range (\% of Open)} &= \frac{30.00}{860.00} \times 100 = 3.488\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**[2 Marks]**

**Step 2: Market Cap**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Market Cap} &= 878.00 \times 2,00,00,000 = 1,756,00,00,000 = \text{₹}1,756 \text{ crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

**[2 Marks]** — small-cap to mid-cap stock.

**Step 3: P/E Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{P/E} &= \frac{878.00}{35.00} = 25.09 \text{ times}
\end{aligned}
$$

**[2 Marks]**

**Comment (1 Mark):**
The auto sector's average P/E on the NSE typically falls in the **18–22x** range. LMNAUTO at **25.09x** is **trading at a premium**, indicating either strong growth expectations or sector-specific optimism from the market.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 4: Dividend Yield**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Dividend Yield} &= \frac{7.00}{878.00} \times 100 = 0.797\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**[2 Marks]**

**Step 5: Bid-Ask Spread**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Spread} &= 878.20 - 877.80 = \text{₹}0.40 \\
\text{Spread \%} &= \frac{0.40}{878.20} \times 100 = 0.0455\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**[2 Marks]**

**Volume Activity Comment (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Volume Multiple} &= \frac{\text{Today's Volume}}{\text{30-Day Average Volume}} = \frac{25,00,000}{10,00,000} = 2.5\times
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Today's volume is **2.5 times the 30-day average**, which is a strong signal of **abnormal trading interest**. Such a volume spike often accompanies a **significant corporate announcement, earnings release, or sector-wide news event**. Combined with the bullish 3.27% price rise, the move has **high conviction** behind it.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Stock Quotation Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion in Market Cap:** Always state the final answer in **₹ crore** or **₹ lakh** explicitly. Writing `49,50,00,00,000` without the unit is treated as an incomplete answer and docked **1 mark**.
>
> 2. **Sign of Day's Change:** If the LTP is *below* the previous close, the change is **negative**. Students often forget the negative sign and write only the magnitude — full credit is **not** awarded.
>
> 3. **P/E Ratio Interpretation:** Computing the P/E correctly is only **3 marks**. The remaining **2 marks** are for the *qualitative comment* (high/low/expensive/cheap). Students who skip the comment lose marks despite the math being correct.
>
> 4. **Bid-Ask Spread denominator:** Always divide the spread by the **Ask price**, not by the LTP or the Bid. Using the wrong denominator leads to an inflated/deflated percentage and is a **common error**.
>
> 5. **Confusing OHLC with 52-Week Range:** The 52-week high/low is a **separate data field** from the day's high/low. Examiners often test this distinction — know the difference.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the KTU ESE:**
>
> - An **Individual Stock Quotation** is a real-time, standardized data record for one listed equity — comprising identity, price, volume, valuation, and liquidity layers.
> - **OHLC** stands for **Open, High, Low, Close** — the four canonical price points of any trading session.
> - **Market Cap = LTP × Outstanding Shares** — the cornerstone metric that drives index inclusion.
> - **P/E Ratio = LTP / EPS** — measures how much the market is willing to pay per rupee of earnings.
> - **Dividend Yield = (Annual DPS / LTP) × 100** — the pure income component of a stock's return.
> - **Bid-Ask Spread** is the difference between the best buy price and best sell price; a **narrow spread = high liquidity**.
> - **Volume spike vs. 30-day average** is a powerful technical confirmation signal.
> - In India, **NSE** uses symbols (e.g., `TCS`); **BSE** uses scrip codes (e.g., `532540`).
> - A stock quote is **live data** during market hours (9:15 AM – 3:30 PM IST) and is **frozen** at the closing bell into a single **end-of-day EOD quote**.
> - The **52-Week High/Low** field is an *annual* range, distinct from the *intraday* day's High/Low.
> - Always present percentage values with **two decimal places** in KTU answers; a bare `3%` loses the precision mark.
> - **Negative sign rule:** A "change" of `-2.5%` must always carry the minus sign — KTU examiners are strict on this.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Stock Market Indices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Stock Market Indices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Stock Market Index** is a statistical measure that reflects the composite value of a group of selected stocks representing a particular market segment, sector, or the overall equity market. It is constructed by aggregating the prices (or market capitalizations) of a predefined basket of securities using a standardized mathematical methodology, allowing investors, analysts, and regulators to gauge market performance, track economic sentiment, and benchmark portfolio returns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** An index is essentially a *proxy* or *barometer* of the market. It does **not** represent the entire market but rather a *representative sample* of stocks whose collective movement is taken as an indicator of broader market behavior.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **fever thermometer** in a hospital. A single thermometer does not measure every cell in a patient's body, yet it gives doctors a reliable indication of the body's overall temperature. Similarly, a stock market index does not measure every listed company's performance; instead, it tracks a carefully chosen set of "thermometer stocks" whose combined movement signals whether the entire market is moving up (bullish), down (bearish), or sideways (neutral).

**Another analogy — The Cricket Team Captain:**
Think of a cricket team where the captain's performance is often used as an indicator of the team's form. While the captain alone does not win matches, his batting average, strike rate, and leadership serve as a *summary metric* for the team's performance. A stock market index functions identically — it summarizes the collective performance of its constituent stocks into a single, easily trackable number.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** The number itself (e.g., Nifty = 22,500) is less important than the *direction* and *percentage change* of the index. Investors track percentage movements, not absolute values.

### 1.3 Purpose and Significance

- **Benchmarking:** Mutual funds and portfolio managers compare their performance against indices (e.g., "the fund beat the Nifty by 3%").
- **Market Sentiment Indicator:** Rising indices reflect optimism; falling indices reflect pessimism.
- **Economic Barometer:** Indices are often called the "mirror of the economy."
- **Investment Vehicles:** Index funds and ETFs (Exchange Traded Funds) allow passive investing by replicating an index.
- **Derivatives Underlying:** Futures and options are written on indices (e.g., Nifty futures, Bank Nifty options).

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Indices

The following parameters are critical to understanding any index:

- **Base Date:** The reference date when the index value is set to a baseline (usually 100 or 1000).
- **Base Value:** The initial numerical value of the index on the base date.
- **Free-Float Market Capitalization:** The portion of a company's shares available for public trading (excluding promoter holdings).
- **Constituent Stocks:** The specific companies included in the index.
- **Rebalancing Frequency:** How often the index reviews and re-adjusts its constituents (typically semi-annually).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard **base value** for most Indian indices is **1000**, while many global indices like the **S&P 500** use a base value of **10** and the **Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA)** historically used simple price totals.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Stock Market Indices — The Master Taxonomy

Stock market indices can be classified along several dimensions. Understanding this taxonomy is **critical** for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.

#### 2.1.1 Based on Coverage (Breadth of Market)

**A. Broad Market Indices**
- Represent the overall market or a significant portion of it.
- **Examples:** BSE Sensex (30 stocks), NSE Nifty 50 (50 stocks), S&P 500 (500 stocks), Dow Jones Industrial Average (30 stocks), NASDAQ Composite.

**B. Sectoral Indices**
- Track specific industry sectors.
- **Examples:**
  - **Nifty Bank** (banking sector)
  - **Nifty IT** (information technology)
  - **Nifty Auto** (automobile)
  - **Nifty Pharma** (pharmaceuticals)
  - **BSE Healthcare**, **BSE Metal**.

**C. Market Capitalization-Based Indices**
- **Large-Cap Indices:** Nifty 100, BSE 100
- **Mid-Cap Indices:** Nifty Midcap 100, BSE Midcap
- **Small-Cap Indices:** Nifty Smallcap 100, BSE Smallcap

#### 2.1.2 Based on Construction Methodology

**A. Price-Weighted Index**
- Each stock's influence is proportional to its absolute share price (not market cap).
- A high-priced stock like ₹5000 has more weight than ₹100 stock, regardless of company size.
- **Example:** Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA), Nikkei 225.

**B. Market Capitalization-Weighted (Cap-Weighted) Index**
- Each stock's weight is proportional to its total market capitalization.
- A company with ₹10 lakh crore market cap has more influence than a company with ₹10,000 crore market cap.
- **Example:** S&P 500, Nifty 50, BSE Sensex (in its modern free-float form).

**C. Equal-Weighted Index**
- Every constituent has the same weight, regardless of price or market cap.
- Requires frequent rebalancing to maintain equal weights.
- **Example:** S&P 500 Equal Weight Index, Nifty 50 Equal Weight Index.

**D. Free-Float Market Capitalization Weighted Index**
- Most widely used modern methodology.
- Only the publicly tradeable portion of shares (free float) is considered, not promoter holdings.
- **Example:** Nifty 50, BSE Sensex, S&P 500.

#### 2.1.3 Based on Geographic Scope

- **National Indices:** Sensex (India), S&P 500 (USA), FTSE 100 (UK), DAX (Germany), Nikkei 225 (Japan), Hang Seng (Hong Kong).
- **Global Indices:** MSCI World Index, MSCI Emerging Markets Index, FTSE All-World Index.

#### 2.1.4 Based on Theme / Strategy

- **Thematic Indices:** Track emerging themes (e.g., Nifty CPSE, Nifty Commodities).
- **Strategy Indices:** Apply filters like low volatility, momentum, or quality (e.g., Nifty 50 Value 20, S&P 500 Low Volatility Index).
- **Sustainability / ESG Indices:** Focus on Environmental, Social, and Governance criteria (e.g., Nifty 100 ESG Index, S&P 500 ESG Index).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **high-yield** for KTU examinations. Memorize the formulas, their variable meanings, and the units carefully.

#### Table 1: Core Index Calculation Formulas

| **Formula Name** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Variables & Meaning** | **Used By** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Price-Weighted Index** | $I_t = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{i,t}}{D_t}$ | $P_{i,t}$ = price of stock $i$ at time $t$; $N$ = number of stocks; $D_t$ = divisor | DJIA, Nikkei 225 |
| **Market-Cap Weighted Index** | $I_t = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (P_{i,t} \times Q_{i,t})}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (P_{i,0} \times Q_{i,0})} \times I_0$ | $Q_{i,t}$ = shares outstanding; $I_0$ = base value | S&P 500 (older form) |
| **Free-Float Cap Weighted** | $I_t = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (P_{i,t} \times Q^{ff}_{i,t})}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (P_{i,0} \times Q^{ff}_{i,0})} \times I_0$ | $Q^{ff}_{i,t}$ = free-float shares | Nifty 50, Sensex, S&P 500 |
| **Equal-Weighted Index** | $I_t = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{P_{i,t}}{P_{i,0}} \times I_0$ | Each stock weighted equally | S&P 500 EW |
| **Geometric Mean Index** | $I_t = \left(\prod_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{P_{i,t}}{P_{i,0}}\right)^{1/N} \times I_0$ | Geometric average of returns | Value Line Composite |
| **Laspeyres Index** | $I_t = \dfrac{\sum P_{i,t} Q_{i,0}}{\sum P_{i,0} Q_{i,0}} \times I_0$ | Uses base-period quantities | Inflation indices |
| **Paasche Index** | $I_t = \dfrac{\sum P_{i,t} Q_{i,t}}{\sum P_{i,0} Q_{i,t}} \times I_0$ | Uses current-period quantities | Trade-weighted indices |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note for KTU Students:** When writing absolute value or modulus in your exam scripts, **always** use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ notation, not the plain pipe symbol $\vert$, as the latter may confuse valuation software or readers.

| **Index** | **Country** | **Methodology** | **No. of Stocks** | **Base Year/Value** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| BSE Sensex | India | Free-float cap weighted | 30 | 1978-79, Base = 100 |
| Nifty 50 | India | Free-float cap weighted | 50 | 1995, Base = 1000 |
| BSE 500 | India | Free-float cap weighted | 500 | 1999, Base = 1000 |

| MSCI World | Global | Free-float cap weighted | 1500+ | 1969, Base = 100 |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Economic Utility

While this course is about financial markets, the underlying *engineering principles* of index construction are deeply mathematical:

- **Signal Processing Analogy:** An index is like a *weighted moving average* filter in electrical engineering — it smooths out noise from individual stock movements to reveal the underlying "market signal."
- **Portfolio Theory (Modern Portfolio Theory):** The Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) uses an index as the "market portfolio" to calculate beta ($\beta$), which is the sensitivity of a stock's returns to index returns.
- **Risk Management:** Value at Risk (VaR) calculations in banks use index data to model systemic risk.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU Part B 14-mark answers, always structure your response as: **(1) Definition → (2) Classification with examples → (3) Methodology / Formula → (4) Real-world significance → (5) Limitations**. This mirrors the KTU 2024 marking scheme.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Price-Weighted Index (Dow Jones Method)

#### 3.1.1 Conceptual Foundation

In a **price-weighted index**, the weight of each stock is determined by its **absolute share price**, not by the company's size. A stock trading at ₹5,000 has 5 times the weight of a stock trading at ₹1,000.

#### 3.1.2 Derivation

Let us consider an index with $N$ constituent stocks at time $t$.

**Step 1:** Define the sum of prices at time $t$:

$$S_t = \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{i,t}$$

where $P_{i,t}$ is the price of the $i$-th stock at time $t$.

**Step 2:** The raw price-weighted index value is:

$$I_t^{\text{raw}} = \dfrac{S_t}{N}$$

**Step 3:** The **divisor** $D_t$ is introduced to account for corporate actions (stock splits, bonus issues, spin-offs). A divisor is a number, typically less than $N$, that adjusts the index to prevent artificial jumps caused by non-market events.

**Step 4:** The final price-weighted index formula becomes:

$$I_t = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{i,t}}{D_t}$$

**Step 5:** When a stock split occurs (e.g., 2-for-1), the share price halves. To prevent the index from artificially falling, the divisor is also reduced proportionally:

$$D_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{old}} \times \dfrac{\text{Sum of prices after split}}{\text{Sum of prices before split}}$$

#### 3.1.3 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A price-weighted index has 3 stocks with the following prices: Stock A = ₹300, Stock B = ₹200, Stock C = ₹500. The divisor is 3. Calculate the index value.

**Solution:**

$$\text{Sum of prices} = P_A + P_B + P_C = 300 + 200 + 500 = 1000$$

$$I = \dfrac{1000}{3} = 333.33$$

**Now, suppose Stock A undergoes a 2-for-1 stock split.** Its price drops to ₹150.

New sum of prices (after split) = $150 + 200 + 500 = 850$.

Adjusted divisor to maintain index continuity:

$$D_{\text{new}} = 3 \times \dfrac{850}{1000} = 3 \times 0.85 = 2.55$$

New index value:

$$I_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{850}{2.55} = 333.33 \text{ (unchanged)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical observation:** The divisor adjustment ensures that a corporate action (like a stock split) does not cause the index to move. Only genuine market price changes should move the index.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Free-Float Market Capitalization Weighted Index (Nifty 50 / Sensex Method)

#### 3.2.1 Conceptual Foundation

In a **free-float market capitalization-weighted index**, the weight of each stock is determined by its *free-float market capitalization* — that is, the market cap calculated only on the shares available for public trading (excluding promoter, government, and locked-in holdings).

#### 3.2.2 Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the free-float market capitalization of stock $i$ at time $t$:

$$MC_{i,t}^{\text{ff}} = P_{i,t} \times Q_{i,t}^{\text{ff}}$$

where $Q_{i,t}^{\text{ff}}$ is the number of free-float shares.

**Step 2:** Sum the free-float market caps of all $N$ constituents:

$$M_t^{\text{ff}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (P_{i,t} \times Q_{i,t}^{\text{ff}})$$

**Step 3:** Similarly, the base-period free-float market cap is:

$$M_0^{\text{ff}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (P_{i,0} \times Q_{i,0}^{\text{ff}})$$

**Step 4:** The free-float cap-weighted index at time $t$ is:

$$I_t = \dfrac{M_t^{\text{ff}}}{M_0^{\text{ff}}} \times I_0$$

**Step 5:** The weight of each stock $i$ in the index is:

$$w_{i,t} = \dfrac{P_{i,t} \times Q_{i,t}^{\text{ff}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} (P_{j,t} \times Q_{j,t}^{\text{ff}})}$$

#### 3.2.3 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** An index has 3 stocks with the following data:

| **Stock** | **Price (₹)** | **Total Shares (Cr)** | **Free-Float Factor** | **Base Price (₹)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 100 | 50 | 0.80 | 90 |
| B | 200 | 30 | 0.70 | 180 |
| C | 50 | 100 | 0.90 | 60 |

The base index value $I_0 = 1000$. Calculate the current index value.

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate free-float shares for each stock.

$$Q_A^{\text{ff}} = 50 \times 0.80 = 40 \text{ Cr}$$
$$Q_B^{\text{ff}} = 30 \times 0.70 = 21 \text{ Cr}$$
$$Q_C^{\text{ff}} = 100 \times 0.90 = 90 \text{ Cr}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate current free-float market cap for each stock.

$$MC_A^{\text{ff}} = 100 \times 40 = 4000 \text{ Cr}$$
$$MC_B^{\text{ff}} = 200 \times 21 = 4200 \text{ Cr}$$
$$MC_C^{\text{ff}} = 50 \times 90 = 4500 \text{ Cr}$$

**Step 3:** Sum the current free-float market caps.

$$M_t^{\text{ff}} = 4000 + 4200 + 4500 = 12700 \text{ Cr}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate base-period free-float market caps.

$$MC_{A,0}^{\text{ff}} = 90 \times 40 = 3600 \text{ Cr}$$
$$MC_{B,0}^{\text{ff}} = 180 \times 21 = 3780 \text{ Cr}$$
$$MC_{C,0}^{\text{ff}} = 60 \times 90 = 5400 \text{ Cr}$$

**Step 5:** Sum the base-period free-float market caps.

$$M_0^{\text{ff}} = 3600 + 3780 + 5400 = 12780 \text{ Cr}$$

**Step 6:** Apply the index formula.

$$I_t = \dfrac{12700}{12780} \times 1000$$

$$I_t = 0.99374 \times 1000 = 993.74$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The index has fallen from 1000 to 993.74, indicating a slight decline (~0.63%) in market sentiment. This is purely a *calculated* result, derived step by step from the free-float market cap movements.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Index Type

| **Index Type** | **Weighting Basis** | **Sensitivity to Mega-Caps** | **Rebalancing Need** | **Best Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Price-Weighted | Absolute share price | Medium-High (high-priced stocks dominate) | Frequent (for splits) | Historical legacy indices like DJIA |
| Market-Cap Weighted | Total market cap | Very High (largest company dominates) | Low | Benchmarking large-cap portfolios |
| Free-Float Cap Weighted | Tradeable market cap | High but moderated by float | Low | Modern Indian indices (Nifty, Sensex) |
| Equal-Weighted | Equal weight per stock | None | Very High | Active portfolio strategies, factor investing |
| Thematic | Custom criterion | Depends on theme | Moderate | Niche investments (ESG, commodities) |
| Sectoral | Sector-specific | High within sector | Moderate | Sector rotation strategies |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *price-weighted* with *market-cap weighted*. Remember: **Price-weighted = absolute price matters. Cap-weighted = company size matters.** The DJIA is price-weighted despite the DJIA being dominated by large companies — this is a *coincidence* caused by the fact that large companies often have high share prices.
