## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

> **Differentiate between a price-weighted index and a market capitalization-weighted index. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Basis** | **Price-Weighted Index** | **Market-Cap Weighted Index** |
|---|---|---|
| Weighting Criterion | Based on absolute share price of each stock | Based on total market capitalization of each stock |
| Influence | High-priced stocks have more influence regardless of company size | Larger companies have more influence |
| Corporate Action Handling | Divisor is adjusted for stock splits, etc. | Index divisor reflects market cap changes |
| Example | Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) | S&P 500 (old methodology), Nifty 50 |

A **price-weighted index** gives more weight to stocks with higher absolute prices. For example, in the DJIA, a stock priced at ₹4000 has twice the weight of a stock at ₹2000, regardless of the company's size.

A **market-cap weighted index** gives more weight to larger companies. For example, in the S&P 500, Apple Inc. (with a market cap of over \$3 trillion) has a much larger influence than a small-cap stock with a market cap of \$1 billion.

*(1 mark for each correct differentiation row, 1 mark for examples.)*

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

> **What is a sectoral index? Give two examples from the Indian market and explain its utility.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **sectoral index** is a stock market index that tracks the performance of companies operating in a specific industry or sector of the economy. Unlike broad market indices that cover multiple sectors, a sectoral index focuses on one segment, allowing investors to measure and invest in the performance of that particular industry.

**Two examples:**
1. **Nifty Bank** — Tracks the banking sector, including major banks like HDFC Bank, ICICI Bank, SBI, and Kotak Mahindra Bank.
2. **Nifty IT** — Tracks information technology companies like TCS, Infosys, Wipro, and HCL Technologies.

**Utility:**
- Helps investors make sector-specific investment decisions.
- Useful for sector rotation strategies (shifting investments between sectors based on economic cycles).
- Serves as an underlying for sector-specific derivatives and ETFs.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for utility.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

> **a) Explain the different types of stock market indices with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**
>
> **b) Calculate the value of a free-float market capitalization-weighted index given the following data. Use base index value = 1000. (7 Marks)**

**Data for part (b):**

| **Stock** | **Current Price (₹)** | **Base Price (₹)** | **Total Shares (Cr)** | **Free-Float Factor** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| X | 250 | 200 | 100 | 0.75 |
| Y | 180 | 200 | 50 | 0.80 |
| Z | 400 | 350 | 40 | 0.60 |

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Types of Stock Market Indices (7 Marks):**

Stock market indices can be classified into the following types:

**1. Broad Market Indices (1 Mark):**
These represent the overall market and are used as primary benchmarks. Examples: BSE Sensex (30 stocks), NSE Nifty 50 (50 stocks), S&P 500 (500 stocks), Dow Jones Industrial Average (30 stocks).

**2. Sectoral Indices (1 Mark):**
These track specific industry sectors. Examples: Nifty Bank (banking), Nifty IT (technology), Nifty Auto (automobile), Nifty Pharma (pharmaceuticals), BSE Metal.

**3. Market Capitalization-Based Indices (1 Mark):**
Categorized by company size. Examples: Nifty 100 (large-cap), Nifty Midcap 100 (mid-cap), Nifty Smallcap 100 (small-cap), BSE 500.

**4. Methodology-Based Classification (2 Marks):**
- **Price-Weighted:** DJIA, Nikkei 225.
- **Market Cap Weighted:** S&P 500 (historical form).
- **Free-Float Cap Weighted:** Nifty 50, BSE Sensex, modern S&P 500.
- **Equal-Weighted:** S&P 500 Equal Weight Index.

**5. Global and Thematic Indices (1 Mark):**
- **Global:** MSCI World, FTSE All-World.
- **Thematic/ESG:** Nifty 100 ESG, S&P 500 ESG.

**6. Strategic Indices (1 Mark):**
These apply filters like value, momentum, or low volatility. Examples: Nifty 50 Value 20, Nifty Low Volatility 50, S&P 500 Low Volatility Index.

---

**Part (b) — Free-Float Cap Weighted Index Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Calculate free-float shares for each stock.

$$Q_X^{\text{ff}} = 100 \times 0.75 = 75 \text{ Cr}$$
$$Q_Y^{\text{ff}} = 50 \times 0.80 = 40 \text{ Cr}$$
$$Q_Z^{\text{ff}} = 40 \times 0.60 = 24 \text{ Cr}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Calculate current free-float market cap.

$$MC_X^{\text{ff}} = 250 \times 75 = 18750 \text{ Cr}$$
$$MC_Y^{\text{ff}} = 180 \times 40 = 7200 \text{ Cr}$$
$$MC_Z^{\text{ff}} = 400 \times 24 = 9600 \text{ Cr}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Sum current free-float market cap.

$$M_t^{\text{ff}} = 18750 + 7200 + 9600 = 35550 \text{ Cr}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Calculate base-period free-float market cap.

$$MC_{X,0}^{\text{ff}} = 200 \times 75 = 15000 \text{ Cr}$$
$$MC_{Y,0}^{\text{ff}} = 200 \times 40 = 8000 \text{ Cr}$$
$$MC_{Z,0}^{\text{ff}} = 350 \times 24 = 8400 \text{ Cr}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Sum base-period free-float market cap.

$$M_0^{\text{ff}} = 15000 + 8000 + 8400 = 31400 \text{ Cr}$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Apply the index formula.

$$I_t = \dfrac{35550}{31400} \times 1000$$

$$I_t = 1.13216 \times 1000 = 1132.16$$

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Interpretation.

The index has risen from 1000 to **1132.16**, a gain of **13.22%**, indicating overall positive market sentiment in the constituent stocks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

> **a) Describe broad market indices and sectoral indices in detail with Indian and global examples. (7 Marks)**
>
> **b) Discuss the various methodologies used in constructing stock market indices with their advantages and limitations. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Broad Market and Sectoral Indices (7 Marks):**

**Broad Market Indices (3.5 Marks):**

A broad market index is designed to represent the overall behavior of the equity market. It includes stocks from multiple sectors and industries, providing a comprehensive view of market performance.

**Characteristics:**
- Diversified across sectors.
- Used as the primary benchmark for measuring portfolio performance.
- Serves as underlying for derivatives and index funds.

**Indian Examples:**
- **BSE Sensex (Sensitive Index):** Comprises 30 stocks listed on BSE, free-float cap weighted. Base year 1978-79, base value 100.
- **NSE Nifty 50:** Comprises 50 stocks, free-float cap weighted. Base year 1995, base value 1000.
- **BSE 500:** Broader index with 500 stocks.

**Global Examples:**
- **Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA):** 30 large-cap US stocks, price-weighted.
- **S&P 500:** 500 large-cap US stocks, free-float cap weighted.
- **NASDAQ Composite:** Over 3000 stocks listed on NASDAQ, heavily tech-weighted.
- **FTSE 100 (UK):** Top 100 companies on London Stock Exchange by market cap.
- **Nikkei 225 (Japan):** 225 large-cap Japanese stocks, price-weighted.
- **DAX (Germany):** 40 major German companies, performance-weighted (includes dividends).

**Sectoral Indices (3.5 Marks):**

A sectoral index tracks a specific industry or sector of the economy, enabling investors to measure and gain exposure to that particular segment.

**Characteristics:**
- Focused on one sector; high concentration risk.
- Used for sector rotation strategies.
- Helps in understanding economic cycles (e.g., IT booms, banking reforms).

**Indian Examples:**
- **Nifty Bank:** 12 banking sector stocks. Base 2000, value 1000.
- **Nifty IT:** Major IT companies (TCS, Infosys, Wipro, HCL).
- **Nifty Auto:** Automobile sector (Maruti, M&M, Tata Motors).
- **Nifty Pharma:** Pharmaceutical companies (Sun Pharma, Dr. Reddy's).
- **BSE Healthcare, BSE Metal, BSE FMCG.**

**Global Examples:**
- **S&P 500 Financials Sector Index.**
- **NASDAQ-100 Technology Sector Index.**
- **Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX).**
- **FTSE 350 Mining Index.**

---

**Part (b) — Index Construction Methodologies (7 Marks):**

**1. Price-Weighted Methodology (1.5 Marks):**
Each stock is weighted by its absolute share price.
- **Formula:** $I_t = \dfrac{\sum P_{i,t}}{D_t}$
- **Example:** DJIA, Nikkei 225.
- **Advantage:** Simple to understand and calculate.
- **Limitation:** A high-priced but small company gets disproportionate weight.

**2. Market Capitalization-Weighted Methodology (1.5 Marks):**
Each stock is weighted by its total market cap.
- **Formula:** $I_t = \dfrac{\sum (P_{i,t} \times Q_{i,t})}{\sum (P_{i,0} \times Q_{i,0})} \times I_0$
- **Advantage:** Reflects the true economic significance of companies.
- **Limitation:** Overweighting of mega-caps leads to concentration risk.

**3. Free-Float Market Capitalization-Weighted (1.5 Marks):**
The most widely used modern method. Only tradeable shares are considered.
- **Formula:** $I_t = \dfrac{\sum (P_{i,t} \times Q^{ff}_{i,t})}{\sum (P_{i,0} \times Q^{ff}_{i,0})} \times I_0$
- **Example:** Nifty 50, Sensex, S&P 500.
- **Advantage:** Removes distortion from promoter holdings.
- **Limitation:** Still skewed toward large free-float companies.

**4. Equal-Weighted Methodology (1 Mark):**
All stocks get equal weight regardless of price or size.
- **Advantage:** Provides diversification benefit; prevents mega-cap dominance.
- **Limitation:** Requires frequent rebalancing, increasing transaction costs.

**5. Geometric Mean Methodology (0.5 Mark):**
Uses geometric mean of price relatives.
- **Example:** Value Line Composite Index.
- **Advantage:** Less affected by extreme values.
- **Limitation:** Computationally complex.

**6. Other Methodologies (1 Mark):**
- **Capped Weighting:** Caps maximum weight of any single stock (used in some Sensex methodologies).
- **Modified Cap Weighting:** Adjusts for liquidity, volatility.
- **Fundamentally-Weighted:** Weights by earnings, book value, dividends (e.g., FTSE RAFI).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "free-float market cap" with "total market cap" (Loss: 1-2 marks).** Always subtract promoter and locked-in holdings. Apply the *free-float factor* explicitly in your solution.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by base value $I_0$ (Loss: 1 mark).** Many students compute the ratio $\dfrac{M_t}{M_0}$ but forget to multiply by 1000 (or the base value). This gives an incorrect index value.
>
> 3. **Writing "DJIA is cap-weighted" (Loss: 1-2 marks).** DJIA is **price-weighted**, not cap-weighted. This is a very common error.
>
> 4. **Not adjusting the divisor in price-weighted problems (Loss: 2 marks).** If a question mentions a stock split in a price-weighted index, you MUST adjust the divisor. Showing both old and new divisors earns full marks.
>
> 5. **Confusing sectoral indices with thematic indices (Loss: 1 mark).** Sectoral = industry-based (Nifty Bank). Thematic = theme-based (Nifty 100 ESG). They are different.
>
> 6. **Writing answers without examples (Loss: 1-2 marks).** For KTU 14-mark answers, examiners explicitly look for "examples" and "real-world applications." Always include at least 2-3 examples per answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Stock Market Index** = a statistical measure of a basket of stocks representing a market or sector.
- **Primary Classification:** Broad Market, Sectoral, Market-Cap Based, Global, Thematic, Strategy, ESG.
- **Methodologies:** Price-Weighted (DJIA), Cap-Weighted, Free-Float Cap-Weighted (Nifty, Sensex), Equal-Weighted, Geometric Mean.
- **Major Indian Indices:** Sensex (30, BSE), Nifty 50 (NSE), Bank Nifty, Nifty IT.
- **Major Global Indices:** DJIA (price-weighted), S&P 500 (free-float cap weighted), NASDAQ Composite, FTSE 100, Nikkei 225 (price-weighted), DAX, Hang Seng.
- **Formula Memorization (Critical):**
  - Price-Weighted: $I_t = \dfrac{\sum P_{i,t}}{D_t}$
  - Free-Float Cap Weighted: $I_t = \dfrac{\sum (P_{i,t} \times Q^{ff}_{i,t})}{\sum (P_{i,0} \times Q^{ff}_{i,0})} \times I_0$
- **Divisor Adjustment** is essential whenever there is a stock split, bonus issue, or index constituent change in a price-weighted index.
- **Free-Float Factor** reflects the proportion of shares available for public trading; it is critical in Indian indices like Nifty 50 and Sensex.
- **Base Value:** Indian indices use 1000 as base; DJIA historically used a price sum; S&P 500 uses 10.
- **Index as Barometer:** Indices reflect market sentiment, not absolute market value.
- **Use of Indices:** Benchmarking, passive investing (index funds/ETFs), derivatives underlying, economic indicator.
- **Rebalancing:** Indices review and adjust constituents periodically (semi-annually for Nifty 50).

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When a 14-mark question asks "explain the types of stock market indices," structure your answer as: *Definition → Classification categories → 2-3 examples per category → Real-world significance → Limitations*. This structure has consistently scored top marks in KTU valuation patterns.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stock Market Indices in India - S&P CNX Nifty
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stock Market Indices in India - S&P CNX Nifty

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Stock Market Index?

A **Stock Market Index** is a statistical measure that reflects the composite value of a group of representative stocks selected from the broader market. It serves as a **barometer** of market sentiment, sectoral performance, and overall economic health. By tracking a curated basket of securities, an index provides investors with a single, simplified number that summarizes the directional movement of the market or a specific segment of it.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 1 Definition**
> An index is essentially a *weighted average* of prices of selected securities, calculated and published by an index provider, used as a benchmark to gauge market performance and as a passive investment vehicle.

## 1.2 What is the S&P CNX Nifty?

The **S&P CNX Nifty** (commonly referred to as the **Nifty 50**) is the flagship broad-market benchmark index of the **National Stock Exchange of India (NSE)**. The nomenclature decodes as follows:

- **S&P** → Standard & Poor's (global index provider)
- **CNX** → "Complete National eXchange" — a joint venture brand between NSE and CRISIL
- **Nifty** → A portmanteau of **"N"** (for National) and **"Fifty"** (number of constituent stocks)

It represents the weighted performance of **50 actively traded large-cap companies** listed on the NSE, spanning **12 broad industry sectors** of the Indian economy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official Base Date and Base Value (Verifiable from NSE Records)**
> - **Base Date:** November 3, 1995
> - **Base Period:** November 3, 1995 to January 2, 1996
> - **Base Value:** **1000** (index starts at 1000 on base date)
> - **Calculation Method:** Free-float market capitalization weighted
> - **Index Sponsor:** NSE Indices Limited (a subsidiary of NSE)

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Report Card of India's Top 50 Companies"

Imagine the Indian stock market is a **classroom of 5,000 listed companies**. Just as a teacher cannot grade every student daily to gauge class performance, a **benchmarker** picks the **top 50 best-performing, most-traded students** (companies) and prepares a **single composite report card number (the Index Value)** that tells the world how the class as a whole is doing on any given day.

- If these 50 companies go up → the **Nifty goes up** → the market is bullish
- If these 50 companies fall → the **Nifty falls** → the market is bearish
- The **Nifty is essentially the report card** of Corporate India's elite

This is exactly why mutual funds, FIIs, and retail investors use the Nifty as a **performance benchmark** — if their portfolio beats the Nifty's return, they have outperformed the market.

## 1.4 Key Parties Involved in the Nifty

| Party | Role |
|---|---|
| **NSE (National Stock Exchange)** | Hosts the index, ensures real-time dissemination |
| **NSE Indices Limited** | Subsidiary of NSE; manages index calculation, governance, and rebalancing |
| **S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC** | Global index methodology partner; provides international standards |
| **CRISIL** | Original co-creator (now advisory role); ensures analytical credibility |

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU exam question: *"What does CNX stand for?"* — The full form is **"Complete National eXchange"**, reflecting the digital, electronic nature of NSE's trading platform — the first in India to operate on a fully automated screen-based system.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Was the Nifty Created? — The 'Why'

Before the Nifty's launch in **1995**, India had the **BSE Sensex** (since 1986) as a primary benchmark, but it was based on price-weighted methodology and represented only 30 companies. The NSE launched the Nifty with three core objectives:

1. **Provide a more representative sample** (50 stocks vs. 30)
2. **Adopt free-float market capitalization methodology** (more accurate than price-weighted)
3. **Enable passive investment products** like index funds and exchange-traded funds (ETFs)

## 2.2 Selection Criteria — How a Stock Enters the Nifty 50

The NSE Indices team uses a robust, semi-annual selection process. To be eligible for inclusion, a stock must satisfy the following:

### 2.2.1 Eligibility Filters (Mandatory)
- Must be **domiciled in India** and listed on the NSE
- Must have a **minimum trading history** on NSE
- Must rank within the **top companies by market capitalization**
- Must have a **minimum average daily traded value** (liquidity)
- Must have a minimum **Impact Cost** threshold (a measure of liquidity reflecting the cost of executing large orders)

### 2.2.2 The 12 Sectors Represented in the Nifty

The index is structured to ensure **sectoral balance**, drawing companies from the following 12 broad industries:

1. Financial Services
2. Information Technology
3. Oil, Gas & Consumable Fuels
4. Automobile and Auto Components
5. Healthcare
6. Consumer Goods (FMCG)
7. Construction Materials
8. Metals & Mining
9. Telecommunication
10. Power
11. Services
12. Realty (Real Estate)

## 2.3 The Core Index Calculation — The 'How'

The Nifty uses the **Free-Float Market Capitalization Weighted Method**. This is a crucial conceptual difference from the price-weighted BSE Sensex.

### Why Free-Float?
Promoter holdings, government stakes, and locked-in shares are **excluded** because they are not available for daily trading. Only the **shares actually available to the public for trading** (free-float) influence the index — this gives a more accurate picture of actual market dynamics.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Index Value (Nifty)** | $I_t = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{50} (P_{i,t} \times FF_{i,t})}{\sum_{i=1}^{50} (P_{i,b} \times FF_{i,b})} \times I_b$ | Master Formula |
| **Market Cap (Free-Float)** | $MC_{i,t} = P_{i,t} \times FF_{i,t}$ | $P$ = Price, $FF$ = Free-float shares |
| **Total Free-Float Market Cap** | $MC_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{50} MC_{i,t}$ | Aggregated across all 50 stocks |
| **Impact Cost** | $IC = \dfrac{\text{Average Buy Price} - \text{Average Sell Price}}{\text{Average Mid Price}} \times 100$ | Liquidity metric in % |
| **Base Index Value** | $I_b = 1000$ | Constant |
| **Base Market Cap** | $MC_b$ | Calculated on base date Nov 3, 1995 |

**Where:**
- $I_t$ = Index value at time $t$
- $P_{i,t}$ = Traded price of stock $i$ at time $t$
- $FF_{i,t}$ = Free-float shares of stock $i$ at time $t$
- $P_{i,b}$ = Traded price of stock $i$ on base date
- $FF_{i,b}$ = Free-float shares of stock $i$ on base date
- $I_b$ = Base index value (= 1000)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sectors are capped at 33% weight** as per SEBI norms to prevent any single sector from dominating the index, ensuring diversification.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility (for the Engineer-CEO)

Even though this is a finance course, the underlying **systems engineering** principles are immense:
- **Real-time data pipelines** compute the Nifty every 15 seconds using distributed systems
- **Algorithmic rebalancing** uses weighted scoring functions
- **Statistical sampling** techniques (similar to engineering quality control) determine constituent stocks

The Nifty infrastructure is, in essence, a **large-scale distributed computing problem** wrapped in a financial application.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Calculating Nifty Value

**Problem:** Suppose the Nifty 50 has 2 simplified constituent stocks with the following data:

| Stock | Free-Float Shares (Crore) | Price on Base Date (₹) | Current Price (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reliance | 100 | 200 | 280 |
| TCS | 50 | 1000 | 1500 |

Compute the new Nifty value given base value = 1000.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Compute Base Period Free-Float Market Capitalization**

$$MC_b = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_{i,b} \times FF_{i,b})$$

$$MC_b = (200 \times 100) + (1000 \times 50)$$

$$MC_b = 20{,}000 + 50{,}000$$

$$MC_b = 70{,}000 \text{ Crore (in base period ₹)}$$

**Step 2: Compute Current Free-Float Market Capitalization**

$$MC_t = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_{i,t} \times FF_{i,t})$$

$$MC_t = (280 \times 100) + (1500 \times 50)$$

$$MC_t = 28{,}000 + 75{,}000$$

$$MC_t = 1{,}03{,}000 \text{ Crore (in current ₹)}$$

**Step 3: Apply the Nifty Formula**

$$I_t = \dfrac{MC_t}{MC_b} \times I_b$$

$$I_t = \dfrac{1{,}03{,}000}{70{,}000} \times 1000$$

$$I_t = 1.4714 \times 1000$$

$$I_t = 1471.4$$

### Interpretation
The Nifty value moved from **1000 → 1471.4**, indicating a **47.14% rise** in aggregate free-float market capitalization of the constituent stocks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Price Changes:** The current market capitalization uses **current** share prices, but free-float shares may also be updated by the index committee. In the simplified example above, we assume free-float shares remained constant.

## 3.2 Derivation of Percentage Change in Nifty

Let $I_{t-1}$ be the previous index value and $I_t$ be the current value. The percentage change is:

$$\Delta I \% = \dfrac{I_t - I_{t-1}}{I_{t-1}} \times 100$$

Substituting the master formula:

$$\Delta I \% = \left(\dfrac{MC_t / MC_b}{MC_{t-1} / MC_b} - 1\right) \times 100$$

$$\Delta I \% = \left(\dfrac{MC_t - MC_{t-1}}{MC_{t-1}}\right) \times 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This proves an elegant result:** The percentage change in the Nifty is **numerically equal to the percentage change in the aggregate free-float market capitalization** of the 50 stocks. The base period values cancel out.

## 3.3 Impact Cost — The Liquidity Derivative

Impact Cost is one of the most important selection criteria. It measures the **slippage** a trader faces when executing a market order of a fixed size. The formula:

$$IC = \dfrac{P_{actual} - P_{ideal}}{P_{ideal}} \times 100$$

Where:
- $P_{actual}$ = Volume-weighted average execution price
- $P_{ideal}$ = The last quoted price at the time of order entry

A **lower impact cost** indicates **higher liquidity**, which is the hallmark of a Nifty-eligible stock. SEBI's 2018 guideline mandated a **0.50% impact cost for the top 100 stocks** on NSE.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Nifty vs. Sensex

| Parameter | S&P CNX Nifty | BSE Sensex |
|---|---|---|
| **Exchange** | NSE | BSE |
| **Number of Stocks** | **50** | **30** |
| **Base Year** | **1995** | 1978-79 |
| **Base Value** | **1000** | 100 |
| **Methodology** | Free-float market cap weighted | Initially price-weighted, now free-float weighted |
| **Sectors Covered** | 12 broad sectors | Various sectors |
| **Re-balancing** | Semi-annual | Periodic |
| **Computation** | Every 15 seconds | Every 15 seconds |
| **Index Provider** | NSE Indices Ltd + S&P | BSE Ltd |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Nifty Index Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Market Data Feed] --> B[Real-Time Price Engine]
    C[Corporate Actions Module] --> B
    D[Free-Float Database] --> B
    B --> E[Aggregate Free-Float Market Cap Calculator]
    F[Base Market Cap Constant MCb] --> G[Index Formula Engine]
    E --> G
    H[Base Index Value Ib = 1000] --> G
    G --> I[Nifty 50 Index Value Output]
    I --> J[Broadcast to Trading Terminals]
    I --> K[Index Mutual Funds NAV Update]
    I --> L[Derivatives Pricing Nifty Futures]
    J --> M[Investor Decision Making]
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Nifty Constituent Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[All NSE Listed Stocks] --> S2{Domiciled in India?}
    S2 -->|No| S3[Excluded]
    S2 -->|Yes| S4{Trading History Met?}
    S4 -->|No| S3
    S4 -->|Yes| S5{Ranks in Top by Market Cap?}
    S5 -->|No| S3
    S5 -->|Yes| S6{Liquidity Impact Cost Met?}
    S6 -->|No| S3
    S6 -->|Yes| S7[Eligible Stock Pool]
    S7 --> S8[Apply Sector Capping 33% Rule]
    S8 --> S9[Final 50 Constituents of Nifty]
    S9 --> S10[Semi-Annual Review Cycle]
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Free-Float Market Cap Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Stock_i_Inputs
        P1[Last Traded Price P_it]
        F1[Free-Float Shares FF_it]
    end
    subgraph Multiplication_Unit
        M1[Price x Free-Float Shares]
    end
    subgraph Aggregation_Unit
        A1[Sum Across 50 Stocks]
    end
    subgraph Index_Output
        O1[Index Value It]
    end
    P1 --> M1
    F1 --> M1
    M1 --> A1
    A1 --> O1
```

## 4.4 Sectoral Composition of the Nifty (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N[Nifty 50 Total Index Weight] --> SF[Financial Services Sector]
    N --> IT[Information Technology Sector]
    N --> OG[Oil and Gas Sector]
    N --> AU[Automobile Sector]
    N --> HC[Healthcare Sector]
    N --> CG[Consumer Goods FMCG]
    N --> CM[Construction Materials]
    N --> MM[Metals and Mining]
    N --> TC[Telecommunication]
    N --> PW[Power Sector]
    N --> SV[Services Sector]
    N --> RT[Realty Sector]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualization Insight:** Financial Services and IT typically dominate the Nifty's weight, often together accounting for over 50% of the index. This is why any macroeconomic news impacting these two sectors causes the largest Nifty movements.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a stock market index. List any two major stock market indices in India.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
A stock market index is a statistical measure that reflects the overall movement of a group of representative stocks from a particular market segment, calculated using a standardized methodology. It serves as a benchmark to gauge market performance and is widely used for comparing the performance of investment portfolios.

**Two major Indian indices:**
1. **S&P CNX Nifty** (NSE, 50 stocks, free-float market cap weighted, base value 1000)
2. **BSE Sensex** (BSE, 30 stocks, free-float market cap weighted, base value 100)

### Question 2: What is the full form of CNX in the S&P CNX Nifty index? What does the word Nifty signify?
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
- **CNX** stands for **"Complete National eXchange"**, a brand identity reflecting NSE's fully automated, screen-based electronic trading platform — the first of its kind in India.
- **Nifty** is derived from a combination of **"N"** (representing National) and **"Fifty"** (the number of constituent stocks). The index represents the top 50 large-cap, highly liquid companies listed on the NSE, drawn from 12 broad sectors of the Indian economy.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Study of S&P CNX Nifty

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Question]**

**Part (a)** — *7 marks*

**Q:** Explain the S&P CNX Nifty index in detail. Discuss its origin, methodology, and the selection criteria for constituent stocks.

**Model Answer:**

The S&P CNX Nifty, commonly known as the **Nifty 50**, is the benchmark index of the National Stock Exchange of India. It was launched in **1995** to provide a more representative and modern alternative to the existing BSE Sensex.

**Key Features:**
- Constituent count: **50** large-cap stocks
- Base date: **November 3, 1995**
- Base value: **1000**
- Methodology: **Free-float market capitalization weighted**
- Rebalancing: **Semi-annually** (March and September)
- Sectors covered: **12 broad sectors**
- Sectoral cap: **33% maximum** weightage to prevent concentration

**Selection Criteria:**
A stock must be domiciled in India, listed on NSE, have a minimum trading history, rank in the top tier by market capitalization, meet a minimum daily traded value (liquidity), and satisfy impact cost thresholds below 0.50%.

The index is calculated by NSE Indices Limited (a wholly-owned subsidiary of NSE) in technical partnership with S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, ensuring global standards of governance and methodology.

**Part (b)** — *7 marks*

**Q:** Calculate the new Nifty value using the following data. Base value = 1000.

| Stock | Free-Float Shares (Lakhs) | Base Price (₹) | Current Price (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 200 | 100 | 150 |
| B | 100 | 500 | 600 |
| C | 50 | 800 | 1000 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Base Market Cap**

$$MC_b = (100 \times 200) + (500 \times 100) + (800 \times 50)$$

$$MC_b = 20{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 40{,}000$$

$$MC_b = 1{,}10{,}000$$

**Step 2: Compute Current Market Cap**

$$MC_t = (150 \times 200) + (600 \times 100) + (1000 \times 50)$$

$$MC_t = 30{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 50{,}000$$

$$MC_t = 1{,}40{,}000$$

**Step 3: Apply the Index Formula**

$$I_t = \dfrac{1{,}40{,}000}{1{,}10{,}000} \times 1000$$

$$I_t = 1.2727 \times 1000$$

$$\boxed{I_t = 1272.7}$$

**Interpretation:** The Nifty moved from 1000 to 1272.7, representing a **27.27% appreciation** in aggregate free-float market capitalization.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**

**Part (a)** — *7 marks*

**Q:** Compare the S&P CNX Nifty and the BSE Sensex across at least 6 parameters. Why is the Nifty considered more representative of the Indian economy?

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | S&P CNX Nifty | BSE Sensex |
|---|---|---|
| **Exchange** | NSE | BSE |
| **No. of Stocks** | 50 | 30 |
| **Base Year** | 1995 | 1978-79 |
| **Base Value** | 1000 | 100 |
| **Methodology** | Free-float market cap | Free-float market cap |
| **Sectors** | 12 broad sectors | Multi-sector |
| **Rebalancing** | Semi-annual | Periodic |

**Why Nifty is More Representative:**
1. **Larger sample size (50 vs 30):** Captures a wider cross-section of the economy
2. **Free-float methodology:** Accurately reflects tradeable market value
3. **Sector balance (12 sectors):** Prevents over-representation of any single industry
4. **Semi-annual rigorous rebalancing:** Keeps the index current with market realities
5. **Higher liquidity:** NSE's volume dominance makes Nifty the most-traded benchmark globally for India

**Part (b)** — *7 marks*

**Q:** A portfolio manager observes that the Nifty 50 moved from 18,500 to 18,870 over a quarter. What is the percentage change in the index? If an investor had ₹10,00,000 invested in a Nifty-indexed mutual fund, what would be the new value?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Percentage Change**

$$\Delta I \% = \dfrac{I_t - I_{t-1}}{I_{t-1}} \times 100$$

$$\Delta I \% = \dfrac{18{,}870 - 18{,}500}{18{,}500} \times 100$$

$$\Delta I \% = \dfrac{370}{18{,}500} \times 100$$

$$\Delta I \% = 2.0\%$$

**Step 2: Compute New Portfolio Value**

$$V_{new} = V_{old} \times \left(1 + \dfrac{\Delta I}{100}\right)$$

$$V_{new} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 1.02$$

$$V_{new} = 10{,}20{,}000$$

**Final Answer:** The index appreciated by **2%** and the portfolio grew from ₹10,00,000 to **₹10,20,000**, a gain of ₹20,000.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing the base values:** Nifty base value is **1000**; Sensex base value is **100**. Marks are deducted if swapped.
> 2. **Forgetting the free-float adjustment:** Students often use **total shares outstanding** instead of free-float shares, which is conceptually wrong.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing up free-float shares (Crore / Lakh) with prices (₹) without proper aggregation leads to magnitude errors.
> 4. **Methodology confusion:** Saying Nifty is "price-weighted" is a common error — it is **free-float market cap weighted**.
> 5. **Skipping steps in numericals:** KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks per logical step. Skipping the base market cap calculation directly to the answer leads to deduction.
> 6. **Writing "S&P CNX" without explaining the full form:** Examiners expect **"Complete National eXchange"** spelled out for full credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Quick-Recall Bullet Points**

- **S&P CNX Nifty = Standard & Poor's + Complete National eXchange + N(ational) + Fifty**
- **Base Date:** November 3, 1995 | **Base Value:** 1000
- **Stocks:** 50 large-cap, highly liquid companies
- **Sectors:** 12 broad sectors of the Indian economy
- **Sector Cap Rule:** Maximum 33% weightage per sector
- **Methodology:** Free-float market capitalization weighted
- **Index Manager:** NSE Indices Limited (subsidiary of NSE)
- **Index Partner:** S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC
- **Rebalancing:** Semi-annually (March and September)
- **Computation Frequency:** Every 15 seconds during market hours
- **Master Formula:** $I_t = \dfrac{\sum (P_{i,t} \times FF_{i,t})}{\sum (P_{i,b} \times FF_{i,b})} \times 1000$
- **Free-float shares** = Total shares − Locked-in shares (promoter, government, etc.)
- **Impact Cost** is the key liquidity metric; threshold typically 0.50% for top stocks
- **Why Free-Float?** It reflects the *actual* investable universe, ignoring strategic non-traded holdings
- **Percentage change in Nifty** = Percentage change in aggregate free-float market cap (base values cancel out)
- **Difference from Sensex:** Nifty is broader (50 vs 30), newer (1995 vs 1978), and base value is 1000 vs 100
- **Use Cases:** Benchmarking, passive funds (Nifty ETFs), derivatives (Nifty futures and options), sentiment indicator
- **Examination Tip:** Always write the full form of CNX at least once in your answer for maximum credit
- **Mnemonic to remember base year:** "**N**ifty **1995**" — *N for Ninety-Five*
- **Common sectors carrying highest weight:** Financial Services, IT, Oil & Gas
- **Why Nifty is preferred over Sensex globally:** Higher liquidity, NSE's electronic trading volume dominance, and modern free-float methodology
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensex
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sensex

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SENSEX** (acronym for **S**ensitive **In**dex of the **B**ombay Stock E**x**change) is the benchmark stock market index of the **Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE)** in India. It comprises **30 well-established, financially sound, and actively traded companies** that are representative of various industrial sectors of the Indian economy.

The Sensex was **first published on January 2, 1986**, with the base year taken as **1978-79** and a base index value of **100**. It is managed and published by **BSE Indices Pvt. Ltd.** (formerly known as the BSE Index Committee), a subsidiary of the BSE.

The current methodology, revised in **September 2003**, uses the **free-float market capitalization** method of index calculation, making it compliant with global standards set by MSCI (Morgan Stanley Capital International) and FTSE.

| Parameter | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Full Name** | Bombay Stock Exchange Sensitive Index |
| **Exchange** | Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE), Mumbai |
| **Number of Constituents** | 30 Companies |
| **Launch Date** | January 2, 1986 |
| **Base Year** | 1978-79 (Fiscal Year basis) |
| **Base Value** | 100 |
| **Index Publisher** | BSE Indices Pvt. Ltd. (formerly BSE Index Committee) |
| **Calculation Method** | Free-Float Market Capitalization (Post-Sept 2003) |
| **Review Frequency** | Semi-Annual (every 6 months) |

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Classroom Analogy** — Imagine your classroom has 30 top-performing students. If you want a quick report-card of the entire class, you wouldn't calculate every student's marks. Instead, you'd just look at how these 30 "star" students performed. If most of them scored well, you can confidently say the class is doing great. The Sensex does exactly this — it is a **"thermometer"** measuring the temperature (health) of the Indian stock market and, by extension, the Indian economy. The 30 companies are the "thermometer's mercury," and their collective price movement tells us whether the market is **bullish (rising/fever)** or **bearish (falling/cold)**.

**Why exactly 30 companies?** Because BSE has over **5,000+ listed companies**. Tracking all of them daily is impractical. A well-chosen sample of **30 industry leaders** provides a statistically reliable snapshot. This is similar to how a **pulse oximeter** doesn't measure all blood in the body — it just measures a fingertip to estimate overall health.

## The 30 Sensex Constituents (As of January 2026)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Sensex composition is reviewed **semi-annually** in **March and September**. Below is the **current set of 30 companies** (post the September 2025 reconstitution):

| # | Company | Sector | # | Company | Sector |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Adani Enterprises | Diversified | 16 | HCL Technologies | IT |
| 2 | Adani Ports & SEZ | Infrastructure | 17 | HDFC Bank | Banking |
| 3 | Asian Paints | Consumer Goods | 18 | Hindustan Unilever | FMCG |
| 4 | Axis Bank | Banking | 19 | ICICI Bank | Banking |
| 5 | Bajaj Finance | Financial Services | 20 | IndusInd Bank | Banking |
| 6 | Bajaj Finserv | Financial Services | 21 | Infosys | IT |
| 7 | Bharti Airtel | Telecom | 22 | ITC | Diversified |
| 8 | Dr. Reddy's Labs | Pharma | 23 | Kotak Mahindra Bank | Banking |
| 9 | HUL | FMCG | 24 | Larsen & Toubro | Engineering |
| 10 | Larsen & Toubro | Engineering | 25 | Mahindra & Mahindra | Auto |
| 11 | Maruti Suzuki | Auto | 26 | Nestle India | FMCG |
| 12 | NTPC | Power | 27 | Power Grid | Power |
| 13 | Oil & Natural Gas Corp | Oil & Gas | 28 | Reliance Industries | Energy/Retail |
| 14 | State Bank of India | Banking | 29 | Sun Pharmaceutical | Pharma |
| 15 | Tata Consultancy Services (TCS) | IT | 30 | Tata Motors | Auto |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## The Free-Float Market Capitalization Methodology

The Sensex uses a **modified market-cap weighted index formula**. Let's break it down step-by-step:

### Step 1: Identify All 30 Constituent Companies
Only BSE-listed companies that satisfy the **eligibility criteria** (history, market cap, liquidity, trading frequency, financials) are included.

### Step 2: Determine Free-Float Factor
The **Free-Float Factor (FF)** represents the proportion of a company's shares that are **readily available for trading** in the market. It excludes:
- **Promoter holding**
- **Strategic stakes** (e.g., government holdings)
- **Locked-in shares**
- **Shares held by promoters under lock-in**

$$\text{FF Factor} = \frac{\text{Total Listed Shares} - \text{Locked-in / Non-Float Shares}}{\text{Total Listed Shares}}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Free-Float Market Capitalization

$$\text{FFMC}_i = P_i \times S_i \times FF_i$$

Where:
- $P_i$ = Current market price of company $i$
- $S_i$ = Total number of issued shares of company $i$
- $FF_i$ = Free-float factor of company $i$

### Step 4: Apply the Sensex Formula

$$\boxed{\text{Sensex} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{30} (P_i \times S_i \times FF_i)}{\text{Base Market Capitalization}} \times \text{Base Index Value}}$$

The **Base Market Capitalization** is the total free-float market cap of all 30 companies as on the **base date**. It serves as the **denominator (divisor)** and is kept **constant** unless a corporate action (stock split, rights issue, etc.) requires a divisor adjustment.

### Step 5: Index Maintenance
Whenever a constituent undergoes a corporate action (bonus, split, merger), the **divisor is adjusted** so that the index value remains continuous and no artificial jumps occur.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{FFMC}_i = P_i \times S_i \times FF_i$ | Free-Float Market Cap of company $i$ | ₹ Crores |
| $\text{Sensex} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{30} \text{FFMC}_i}{\text{Base MC}} \times 100$ | Sensex Index Value | Index Points |
| $FF_i = \frac{\text{Free-Float Shares}}{\text{Total Issued Shares}}$ | Free-Float Factor | Decimal (0 to 1) |
| $\text{Index Return} = \frac{P_t - P_{t-1}}{P_{t-1}} \times 100$ | Percentage change in index | Percentage (%) |
| $\text{P/E Ratio} = \frac{\text{Index Value}}{\text{Earnings per Share}}$ | Valuation metric | Multiple |
| $\text{Annualized Return} = \left(1 + R_{\text{total}}\right)^{1/n} - 1$ | CAGR formula | Decimal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip**: In KTU exams, the most commonly asked question is the **Sensex formula and the free-float concept**. Always remember: **Divisor adjustment** is the heart of index maintenance — it keeps the index **comparable over time** even when constituent shares undergo splits or bonuses.

## Real-World Engineering & Economic Utility

While Sensex is a financial concept, its **data analytics infrastructure** is built on:
- **High-Frequency Trading (HFT) systems** requiring real-time data processing (sub-millisecond latency)
- **Distributed computing clusters** for index calculation across millions of trades
- **Algorithmic trading systems** that use Sensex futures and options for hedging
- **Information Retrieval Systems** (Bloomberg, Reuters) that disseminate Sensex data globally
- **Cybersecurity protocols** to prevent market manipulation via index spoofing

The Sensex also drives:
- **Passive investment products** (ETFs, Index Funds) — billions in AUM
- **Economic policy decisions** by RBI and the Government
- **Foreign portfolio investor (FPI) sentiment** indicators
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Worked Example: Sensex Calculation for a Mini-Index of 3 Companies

Let's create a **mini-index** with 3 companies (A, B, C) to understand how the Sensex formula is applied.

### Given Data

| Company | Current Price ($P_i$) | Total Shares ($S_i$) | Free-Float Factor ($FF_i$) | Promoter Hold |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A (Reliance-like) | ₹2,500 | 6,76,00,000 (6.76 Cr) | 0.50 | 50% |
| B (TCS-like) | ₹3,800 | 1,25,00,000 (1.25 Cr) | 0.70 | 30% |
| C (HDFC Bank-like) | ₹1,600 | 7,65,00,000 (7.65 Cr) | 0.65 | 35% |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate Free-Float Market Cap of Each Company**

For Company A:
$$\text{FFMC}_A = 2,500 \times 6,76,00,000 \times 0.50$$

Breaking down the multiplication:
$$\text{FFMC}_A = 2,500 \times 6,76,00,000 \times 0.50$$

$$= 2,500 \times 3,38,00,000 \quad \text{(since } 0.50 \times 6,76,00,000 = 3,38,00,000\text{)}$$

$$= 8,45,00,00,00,000 \quad \text{₹}$$

$$= ₹84,500 \text{ Crores}$$

For Company B:
$$\text{FFMC}_B = 3,800 \times 1,25,00,000 \times 0.70$$

$$= 3,800 \times 87,50,000 \quad \text{(since } 0.70 \times 1,25,00,000 = 87,50,000\text{)}$$

$$= 3,32,50,00,00,000 \quad \text{₹}$$

$$= ₹33,250 \text{ Crores}$$

For Company C:
$$\text{FFMC}_C = 1,600 \times 7,65,00,000 \times 0.65$$

$$= 1,600 \times 4,97,25,000 \quad \text{(since } 0.65 \times 7,65,00,000 = 4,97,25,000\text{)}$$

$$= 7,95,60,00,00,000 \quad \text{₹}$$

$$= ₹79,560 \text{ Crores}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Total Free-Float Market Capitalization**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{3} \text{FFMC}_i = 84,500 + 33,250 + 79,560$$

$$= ₹1,97,310 \text{ Crores}$$

**Step 3: Assume Base Market Capitalization and Calculate Index**

For simplicity, let us assume the **Base MC = ₹1,000 Crores** and **Base Index = 100**.

$$\text{Mini-Index} = \frac{1,97,310}{1,000} \times 100$$

$$= 1,97,310 \times 0.1$$

$$= 19,731 \text{ Index Points}$$

### Verification of the Method

> [!NOTE]
> If the prices of all 3 companies increase by 10%, the new index would be:
> $$\text{New Index} = \frac{1,97,310 \times 1.10}{1,000} \times 100 = 21,704.1 \text{ points}$$
> This represents exactly a **10% rise**, validating the proportional behavior of a market-cap weighted index.

## Analytical Matrix: Sensex vs. Nifty 50

| Parameter | Sensex (BSE) | Nifty 50 (NSE) |
|---|---|---|
| Full Name | Sensitive Index | National Stock Exchange Fifty |
| Exchange | BSE | NSE |
| Number of Companies | 30 | 50 |
| Base Year | 1978-79 | 1995 |
| Base Value | 100 | 1,000 |
| Methodology | Free-Float Market Cap | Free-Float Market Cap |
| Review Frequency | Semi-Annual | Semi-Annual |
| Index Provider | BSE Indices Pvt. Ltd. | NSE Indices Ltd. (Nifty) |
| Benchmark Status | BSE's primary index | NSE's primary index |
| Lower Boundary | None | None |
| Upper Boundary | None | None |

> [!NOTE]
> **Correlation Insight**: The Sensex and Nifty have a correlation of approximately **0.95+** — meaning they move in tandem about 95% of the time. This is because they have significant overlap in their top constituents (Reliance, TCS, HDFC Bank, Infosys, ICICI Bank, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
