# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Sensex. What is its base year and base value?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Sensex** (Sensitive Index) is the benchmark stock market index of the **Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE)**, comprising **30 well-established and financially sound companies** that are representative of the Indian economy.
- **Base Year**: 1978-79
- **Base Value**: 100
- **Launched**: January 2, 1986
- **Maintained by**: BSE Indices Pvt. Ltd.
- **Methodology**: Free-float market capitalization

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for base year, 1 for base value and management)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is meant by Free-Float Factor in Sensex calculation? Why is it used?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Free-Float Factor (FF)** is the ratio of a company's shares that are readily available for trading in the market to its total issued shares. It excludes **promoter holdings, government stakes, and locked-in shares**.

$$\text{FF} = \frac{\text{Free-Float Shares}}{\text{Total Issued Shares}}$$

**Why it is used:**
- Provides a more accurate reflection of **actual investable shares**
- Aligns Indian indices with **global standards** (MSCI, FTSE)
- Prevents distortion by **non-tradeable holdings**
- Better represents **true market sentiment**

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for significance)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **methodology of Sensex calculation** with the relevant formula. Discuss the role of the **divisor adjustment** in index maintenance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Sensex Calculation Methodology:**

The Sensex is calculated using the **Free-Float Market Capitalization** method, which was adopted in **September 2003** to align with global standards.

**Step 1: Free-Float Factor Determination**
Each of the 30 constituent companies has a specific free-float factor that represents the proportion of shares available for public trading.

**Step 2: Free-Float Market Capitalization**

$$\text{FFMC}_i = P_i \times S_i \times FF_i$$

**Step 3: Sensex Formula**

$$\text{Sensex} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{30} \text{FFMC}_i}{\text{Base MC}} \times 100$$

**Role of Divisor Adjustment:**

The **divisor (Base MC)** is a constant value that ensures the Sensex remains comparable over time. When any constituent company undergoes a **corporate action** such as:
- **Stock Split** (e.g., 1:5 split → price drops 5x)
- **Bonus Issue** (e.g., 1:1 bonus → shares double)
- **Rights Issue**
- **Merger / Demerger**

The divisor is mathematically adjusted so that the **Sensex value does not show artificial jumps**. This maintains **continuity of the index series** and allows for fair historical comparison.

**Example:** If Company X splits 1:2, its price halves but shares double. The FFMC remains unchanged mathematically, but to prevent any calculation drift, the divisor is recalibrated.

*(7 marks: 3 for methodology, 2 for formula, 2 for divisor adjustment)*

---

**(b)** Compare **Sensex and Nifty 50** on at least **six parameters**. Why is the Sensex considered a **barometer of the Indian economy**? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Sensex | Nifty 50 |
|---|---|---|
| Exchange | BSE | NSE |
| Number of Companies | 30 | 50 |
| Base Year | 1978-79 | 1995 |
| Base Value | 100 | 1,000 |
| Methodology | Free-Float Market Cap | Free-Float Market Cap |
| Coverage | Narrower, top-heavy | Broader sectoral spread |
| Review Frequency | Semi-Annual | Semi-Annual |
| Index Provider | BSE Indices Pvt. Ltd. | NSE Indices Ltd. |

**Sensex as a Barometer of the Indian Economy:**

1. **Sectoral Representation**: The 30 companies span across banking, IT, energy, FMCG, pharma, auto — mirroring key sectors of the Indian economy.

2. **Investor Confidence Indicator**: A rising Sensex reflects **bullish sentiment**, attracting domestic and foreign investments; a falling Sensex signals **economic stress**.

3. **FPI Sentiment Gauge**: Foreign Portfolio Investors (FPIs) use Sensex trends as a **macro signal** for India allocation decisions.

4. **Wealth Effect**: Sensex movement influences **household wealth perception**, affecting consumption and savings patterns.

5. **Policy Feedback**: The Government and RBI monitor Sensex behavior to assess the **impact of monetary and fiscal policies**.

6. **Global Benchmarking**: Sensex is the most-watched Indian index globally and is featured in **MSCI Emerging Markets Index**, influencing global capital flows.

*(7 marks: 3 for comparison table, 4 for barometer explanation)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** What are the **eligibility criteria for a company to be included in the Sensex**? Explain the **process of semi-annual reconstitution**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Eligibility Criteria for Sensex Inclusion:**

1. **Listing History**: The company must be listed on BSE for at least **6 months**.

2. **Market Capitalization**: Must rank among the **top 100 companies** by market cap on BSE.

3. **Liquidity**: The stock must be **traded on at least 90% of the trading days** in the previous 6 months.

4. **Trading Volume**: Should have an **average daily traded value** that ensures sufficient liquidity.

5. **Financial Health**: Must have **positive net worth** in the immediately preceding financial year and should not have a negative net worth in the last 3 years.

6. **No History of Suspension**: The stock should not have been **suspended from trading** for any regulatory reasons.

7. **Free-Float**: Should have a **minimum free-float market cap** to ensure adequate public participation.

**Process of Semi-Annual Reconstitution:**

The Sensex is reconstituted **twice a year** — in **March and September**.

1. **Data Cut-off Date**: BSE Indices Pvt. Ltd. sets a cut-off date (typically the last trading day of February/August).

2. **Eligibility Review**: All listed BSE companies are screened against the eligibility criteria.

3. **Sectoral Balance**: The Index Committee ensures that the final 30 represent **diverse industry sectors** — preventing over-representation of any single sector.

4. **Replacement Decision**: If new companies qualify and existing constituents fail, the committee decides on additions and deletions.

5. **Buffer Zone**: Companies ranked **21st to 30th** in eligibility serve as a **buffer** — they are typically not removed unless replaced by a significantly higher-ranked company.

6. **Public Notice**: Changes are announced in advance, with a **notice period** before they take effect (typically 2-3 weeks).

7. **Implementation Date**: Changes become effective from a specific date in **March/September**, and the index divisor is recalibrated.

*(7 marks: 4 for eligibility, 3 for reconstitution process)*

---

**(b)** A mini-index consists of 3 companies with the following data:

| Company | Price (₹) | Total Shares | Free-Float Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| P | 1,000 | 5,00,000 | 0.60 |
| Q | 2,500 | 2,00,000 | 0.80 |
| R | 800 | 10,00,000 | 0.50 |

If the **Base Market Capitalization is ₹1,500 Crores** and **Base Index Value is 100**, calculate the index value. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Calculate Free-Float Market Cap (FFMC) for each company**

For Company P:
$$\text{FFMC}_P = 1,000 \times 5,00,000 \times 0.60$$

$$= 1,000 \times 3,00,000$$

$$= 3,00,00,000 = ₹30 \text{ Crores}$$

For Company Q:
$$\text{FFMC}_Q = 2,500 \times 2,00,000 \times 0.80$$

$$= 2,500 \times 1,60,000$$

$$= 4,00,00,00,000 = ₹400 \text{ Crores}$$

For Company R:
$$\text{FFMC}_R = 800 \times 10,00,000 \times 0.50$$

$$= 800 \times 5,00,000$$

$$= 4,00,00,000 = ₹40 \text{ Crores}$$

**Step 2: Total Free-Float Market Capitalization**

$$\sum \text{FFMC} = 30 + 400 + 40 = 470 \text{ Crores}$$

**Step 3: Apply Sensex Formula**

$$\text{Index} = \frac{470}{1,500} \times 100$$

$$= 0.3133 \times 100$$

$$= 31.33 \text{ Index Points}$$

**Interpretation:** Since the index value (31.33) is less than the base value (100), the constituent companies have, on average, performed below the base period levels. This indicates a **bearish phase** in this mini-index.

*(7 marks: 1 for setup, 3 for FFMC calculations, 1 for summation, 2 for index formula and interpretation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Students often confuse **Total Market Cap** with **Free-Float Market Cap**. The Sensex uses ONLY free-float. **Loses 2 marks** if free-float factor is omitted.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Forgetting to apply the **base value (100)** multiplier in the formula. Always write the complete formula: $\frac{\sum \text{FFMC}}{\text{Base MC}} \times \text{Base Index}$.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Not converting shares to crores consistently. Always state units clearly to avoid valuation deductions.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: For semi-annual reconstitution, students forget the **buffer zone concept** — frequently tested in 14-mark questions.
> - **Common Mistake 5**: Mixing up **BSE Sensex** with **S&P BSE indices** (there are multiple BSE indices — Sensex is just one).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ Sensex = **Sensitive Index** of BSE, comprising **30 companies**
- ✅ Base year = **1978-79**, Base value = **100**
- ✅ Launched on **January 2, 1986**
- ✅ Managed by **BSE Indices Pvt. Ltd.** (a BSE subsidiary)
- ✅ Methodology: **Free-Float Market Capitalization** (since September 2003)
- ✅ Formula: $\text{Sensex} = \frac{\sum \text{FFMC}_i}{\text{Base MC}} \times 100$
- ✅ FFMC = $P_i \times S_i \times FF_i$
- ✅ Free-float factor excludes **promoter holding, government stake, locked-in shares**
- ✅ **Divisor adjustment** is essential during corporate actions (splits, bonuses)
- ✅ Review is **semi-annual** (March and September)
- ✅ Sectors represented: Banking, IT, Energy, FMCG, Auto, Pharma, Power
- ✅ Sensex is the **oldest** stock market index in India
- ✅ Sensex vs Nifty: **30 vs 50 companies**; correlation ~**0.95**
- ✅ Buffer zone concept: Companies ranked 21-30 act as replacement pool
- ✅ Sensex serves as a **barometer of the Indian economy** and affects FPI flows
- ✅ ETF and Index Funds passively track the Sensex
- ✅ Top 3 constituents by weight (as of 2026): **Reliance Industries, HDFC Bank, TCS**
- ✅ **Key Difference**: Sensex uses **free-float** (NOT full market cap) — this is the most important conceptual point
- ✅ Remember the **3-step calculation**: FFMC → Sum → Divide by Base MC × 100
- ✅ Sensex is **NOT** the same as BSE's total market cap — it is a sample-based index
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition of Mutual Fund
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Definition of Mutual Fund

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DEFINITION — MUTUAL FUND**
> A **Mutual Fund** is a professionally managed, pooled investment vehicle that collects money from a large number of individual investors who share a common financial goal, and invests those pooled funds in a diversified portfolio of securities such as equities, bonds, money market instruments, and/or other assets, in accordance with the investment objectives disclosed in its offer document. The fund is operated, managed, and administered by a registered **Asset Management Company (AMC)** under the strict regulatory supervision of the **Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI)** and the framework prescribed by the **Association of Mutual Funds in India (AMFI)**.

In simpler, exam-ready phrasing:

> A mutual fund is a **trust** that pools savings from many investors and invests them in securities, with each investor holding units proportional to the amount invested.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bus Journey" Intuition

Imagine **100 friends** want to travel from Kerala to Delhi, but none of them can afford to hire a private luxury bus alone.

| Real-World Element | Mutual Fund Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The 100 friends | Individual retail investors (Unit holders) |
| Their combined ticket money | **Pooled Corpus / AUM** |
| The hired bus with a driver | The **Mutual Fund Scheme** managed by an AMC |
| The professional driver | **Fund Manager** (Chartered / MBA qualified) |
| The travel agency guaranteeing the trip | **SEBI + AMFI** (Regulators) |
| Each friend's share of the bus | **Units** held by the investor |
| The bus's current resale value | **Net Asset Value (NAV)** |

Each friend gets a ticket (unit). When the journey ends or midway, the ticket's worth depends on the bus's *current* condition — exactly how a mutual fund unit's value (NAV) fluctuates daily with the underlying portfolio.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Synonyms (Board-Examiner Favourites):**
> - Pooled Investment Vehicle
> - Collective Investment Scheme
> - Trust-based Investment Vehicle
> - Open-ended / Closed-ended Fund

## 1.3 Key Parties Involved in a Mutual Fund (Mandatory to Memorise)

- **Sponsor** — The person/company who initiates the fund (e.g., HDFC, ICICI, SBI).
- **Trustee** — Holds the fund's assets on behalf of unit holders (typically a Board of Trustees / Trustee Company registered with SEBI).
- **Asset Management Company (AMC)** — The investment manager; manages the portfolio (e.g., HDFC AMC, ICICI Prudential AMC).
- **Custodian** — Safekeeps the securities and assets (e.g., HDFC Bank, Citibank N.A.).
- **Registrar & Transfer Agent (RTA)** — Handles investor records, KYC, and unit allotment (e.g., CAMS, KFintech).
- **Unit Holder / Investor** — The end beneficiary who purchases units.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How a Mutual Fund Works (Bullet Logic)

A mutual fund is **not** a financial product by itself — it is a **legal structure / vehicle** that delivers financial products to investors. The lifecycle is:

1. **Pooling (Collection of Funds):** The AMC invites the public to invest a minimum amount (often **₹500** for SIP, **₹1,000** lump-sum for open-ended equity schemes) under a Scheme Information Document (SID) and Key Information Memorandum (KIM).
2. **Investment Deployment:** The Fund Manager deploys the pooled money into a diversified basket (equity, debt, hybrid, gold, etc.) aligned with the scheme's stated **Investment Objective** (Growth, Income, Liquidity, Tax-saving).
3. **Unit Allotment:** Each investor receives **Units** at the prevailing NAV on the day of purchase.
4. **NAV Computation:** At the close of every business day, the total value of assets minus liabilities is divided by the number of outstanding units to arrive at the per-unit price.
5. **Income Distribution / Growth:** Gains may be distributed as **Dividends (IDCW)** or retained for capital appreciation.
6. **Redemption:** Open-ended funds allow exit anytime at the prevailing NAV; closed-ended funds have a fixed maturity.

## 2.2 Why Mutual Funds Exist — The "Three E's" Framework

- **E1 — Expertise:** Retail investors lack time and skill to research markets; the AMC provides a qualified Fund Manager.
- **E2 — Economies of Scale:** Pooled money negotiates lower brokerage, custody, and transaction costs.
- **E3 — Easy Diversification:** Small amounts gain access to a basket of 40–100+ securities, reducing **unsystematic risk**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite Phrasing:**
> "A mutual fund provides the twin benefits of **professional management** and **diversification** to the small investor at a low cost."

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Term | Meaning | Unit / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{NAV}$ | $\text{NAV} = \dfrac{\text{Total Assets} - \text{Total Liabilities}}{\text{Outstanding Units}}$ | Per-unit market price of the fund | ₹ per unit; calculated daily at **₹999.9999** precision |
| $AUM$ | $\text{AUM} = \sum (\text{Units} \times \text{NAV})$ | Total corpus managed | ₹ Crore |
| $ER$ | $\text{Expense Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Scheme Expenses}}{\text{AUM}}$ | Recurring fee charged by AMC | % p.a.; SEBI cap: **2.50%** for equity, **2.25%** for debt |
| $R_{p}$ | $R_{p} = \dfrac{(\text{NAV}_{t} - \text{NAV}_{0}) + D}{\text{NAV}_{0}}$ | Point-to-point simple return (incl. dividend $D$) | % |
| $CAGR$ | $\text{CAGR} = \left(\dfrac{\text{NAV}_{end}}{\text{NAV}_{begin}}\right)^{1/n} - 1$ | Compounded Annual Growth Rate over $n$ years | % |
| $U_a$ | $U_a = \dfrac{I_a}{\text{NAV}_{day}}$ | Units allotted on investment $I_a$ | Number of units (decimal up to 4 places) |
| $R_v$ | $R_v = U_a \times \text{NAV}_{redemption}$ | Redemption value | ₹ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Career Utility

Although this is a management elective, B.Tech students benefit because:
- **Start-up Funding Knowledge:** Mutual funds are a major institutional investor channel; tech founders must understand investor categories.
- **Personal Finance:** First salary investment planning (SIPs in equity funds).
- **Corporate Treasury:** IT/Manufacturing firms park short-term surplus in **liquid mutual funds** rather than savings accounts.
- **FinTech Domain:** Robo-advisors, MF Utility platform, and SEBI-registered online distributors (like Groww, Zerodha Coin) are direct applications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Demonstration

## 3.1 Derivation of the Net Asset Value (NAV) Formula

The NAV equation is not derived from first principles of finance but from a **balance-sheet identity**. Starting from the **Trust's Point of View**:

$$\text{Value of Fund (Equity of Unit holders)} = \text{Market Value of Assets} - \text{Outside Liabilities}$$

Since each unit represents an equal claim on this net equity, dividing by the number of units gives the per-unit claim:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NAV per unit} &= \frac{\text{Equity of Unit holders}}{\text{Number of Outstanding Units}} \\[6pt]
\therefore \quad \text{NAV} &= \frac{\text{Market Value of Investments} + \text{Cash} + \text{Receivables} - \text{Expenses Payable} - \text{Loans (if any)}}{\text{Total Units Outstanding}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Assets minus liabilities = Net Equity; dividing equity by total units yields price per unit — the *same logic* used in equity share valuation under the **Net Asset Method**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Question)

**Problem:**
An equity mutual fund scheme reports the following at the close of 31st March 2024:
- Market value of equity shares held: ₹ **85,00,00,000**
- Cash and bank balance: ₹ **3,25,00,000**
- Accrued interest receivable: ₹ **48,00,000**
- Expenses payable (audit, management): ₹ **92,00,000**
- Outstanding units in the books: **5,00,00,000 units**

Compute the **NAV per unit** and the **redemption value** for an investor holding **2,500 units**.

### Step 1 — Compute Total Assets
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Assets} &= \text{Equity Shares} + \text{Cash} + \text{Receivables} \\
&= 85,00,00,000 + 3,25,00,000 + 48,00,000 \\
&= 88,73,00,000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Total Liabilities
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Liabilities} &= \text{Expenses Payable} = 92,00,000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Net Asset Value (Aggregate)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Assets} &= 88,73,00,000 - 92,00,000 = 87,81,00,000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — NAV per Unit
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NAV} &= \frac{87,81,00,000}{5,00,00,000} \\
&= 17.5620 \text{ ₹ per unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Numerator is in ₹ (rupees); denominator is in units; therefore result is ₹/unit — consistent with SEBI's **4-decimal rounding rule** (₹17.5620).

### Step 5 — Redemption Value for 2,500 Units
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Redemption Value} &= 2{,}500 \times 17.5620 \\
&= 43{,}905.00 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Convention:** Always quote NAV to **four decimal places** and redemption value to **two decimal places (paise)**. Marks are deducted if precision conventions are ignored.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mutual Fund vs. Direct Stock Investment

| Parameter | Direct Equity Investment | Mutual Fund Investment |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum Capital | High (1 share × premium) | Low (₹500 via SIP) |
| Diversification | Manual; costly for small investors | Automatic (basket of 50–100 stocks) |
| Professional Management | Self-managed | AMC-managed Fund Manager |
| Research Burden | High | Low (delegated to AMC) |
| Transaction Cost | Brokerage + STT + GST | Expense Ratio only |
| Liquidity | Sale on exchange (T+1) | Redemption at NAV (T+1 to T+3) |
| Regulatory Oversight | SEBI (listed companies) | SEBI + AMFI + Trustee |
| Transparency | Quarterly results | Daily NAV disclosure |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Architecture of a Mutual Fund

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["INVESTOR A"] --> P1["POOL OF FUNDS"]
    A2["INVESTOR B"] --> P1
    A3["INVESTOR C"] --> P1
    A4["INSTITUTIONAL INVESTOR"] --> P1
    P1 --> T1["TRUST BOARD OF TRUSTEES"]
    T1 --> AMC1["ASSET MANAGEMENT COMPANY"]
    AMC1 --> FM1["FUND MANAGER"]
    AMC1 --> RA1["RESEARCH ANALYST TEAM"]
    FM1 --> INV1["EQUITY SECURITIES"]
    FM1 --> INV2["DEBT INSTRUMENTS"]
    FM1 --> INV3["MONEY MARKET INSTRUMENTS"]
    FM1 --> INV4["GOLD ETFs AND HYBRID ASSETS"]
    INV1 --> NAV1["DAILY NAV COMPUTATION"]
    INV2 --> NAV1
    INV3 --> NAV1
    INV4 --> NAV1
    NAV1 --> U1["UNITS ALLOTTED TO INVESTOR"]
    NAV1 --> R1["REDEMPTION AT NAV"]
    U1 --> R1
    R1 --> A1
    R1 --> A2
    R1 --> A3
    R1 --> A4
    REG1["SEBI AND AMFI REGULATOR"] -.-> AMC1
    REG1 -.-> T1
    CUS1["CUSTODIAN BANK"] -.-> NAV1
    RTA1["REGISTRAR AND TRANSFER AGENT CAMS OR KFIN"] -.-> U1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Investor's Journey Through a Mutual Fund

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Inv as Investor
    participant AMC as Asset Management Company
    participant FM as Fund Manager
    participant Reg as SEBI and AMFI
    participant Nav as NAV Calculation Engine

    Inv->>AMC: Submit KYC and Application Money
    AMC->>Reg: File Scheme Information Document
    Reg-->>AMC: Approval and Registration
    AMC->>FM: Deploy pooled corpus
    FM->>FM: Construct diversified portfolio
    FM->>Nav: Mark portfolio to market daily
    Nav-->>Inv: Publish NAV per unit
    Inv->>AMC: Place redemption request
    AMC->>Nav: Multiply units by exit NAV
    Nav-->>Inv: Disburse redemption proceeds
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Pie Chart — Typical Asset Allocation of a Balanced Hybrid Fund

```mermaid
pie title Indicative Asset Allocation of Balanced Hybrid Fund
    "Indian Equities Large Cap" : 45
    "Indian Equities Mid Cap" : 15
    "Government Securities" : 20
    "Corporate Bonds AA Rated" : 12
    "Cash and Treasury Bills" : 5
    "Gold ETF Units" : 3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a Mutual Fund. Mention any two features.
**Model Answer (10–12 lines expected):**
A Mutual Fund is a trust that pools money from a large number of investors who share a common financial goal, and invests the corpus in diversified securities such as equities, bonds, and money market instruments under the professional management of an Asset Management Company regulated by SEBI.

**Two Features:**
1. **Professional Management** — Investment decisions are taken by qualified Fund Managers supported by a research team, eliminating the need for the investor to monitor markets daily.
2. **Diversification** — A small investment of ₹500 gives exposure to 50–100 securities, reducing unsystematic risk.
3. **Liquidity** — Open-ended schemes allow redemption at the prevailing NAV on any business day.
4. **Affordability** — Small denominations (SIP of ₹500) make it accessible to retail investors.

---

### Q2. What is NAV? Why is it called the 'price' of a mutual fund unit?
**Model Answer:**
**NAV (Net Asset Value)** is the per-unit market value of a mutual fund scheme's net assets, computed daily as:

$$\text{NAV} = \frac{\text{Total Assets} - \text{Total Liabilities}}{\text{Outstanding Units}}$$

It is called the 'price' of a unit because open-ended mutual fund units are bought and sold (allotted and redeemed) at the NAV — there is no secondary market exchange price. NAV moves daily based on the market value of the underlying portfolio.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) Explain in detail the structure of a Mutual Fund in India, highlighting the roles of the Sponsor, Trustee, AMC, Custodian, and RTA.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The structure of a Mutual Fund in India is a **three-tier legal framework** prescribed under the SEBI (Mutual Funds) Regulations, 1996.

1. **Sponsor** — The promoter entity (e.g., HDFC Ltd, SBI, ICICI Bank) who sets up the fund and appoints the Trustee. The Sponsor must contribute a minimum of **40% of the net worth of the AMC** (currently ₹50 crore minimum net worth for AMCs).

2. **Board of Trustees** — A Trust registered under the Indian Trusts Act, 1882, holding the fund's property for the benefit of unit holders. At least **two-thirds** of trustees must be independent. Their duty is to ensure the AMC acts in the interest of investors.

3. **Asset Management Company (AMC)** — A company registered with SEBI that manages the investment portfolio. The AMC charges an **expense ratio** (capped by SEBI slabs based on AUM). The AMC employs the **Fund Manager** who takes buy/sell decisions.

4. **Custodian** — A SEBI-registered entity (typically a scheduled commercial bank like HDFC Bank, Citibank) that holds the securities in safe custody on behalf of the fund, preventing misappropriation.

5. **Registrar and Transfer Agent (RTA)** — Handles investor servicing — KYC, PAN validation, unit allotment, redemption processing, statement dispatch. Examples: **CAMS (Computer Age Management Services)** and **KFin Technologies**.

**Flow of Funds:** Investor → AMC (via RTA) → Custodian (for safekeeping) → Fund Manager (for investment decisions) → NAV declared daily → Investor (units / redemption).

---

**(b) Compute the NAV per unit of a debt-oriented mutual fund scheme from the following data:** **(7 Marks)**

| Particulars | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|
| Market value of government securities | 42,00,00,000 |
| Market value of corporate bonds | 18,50,00,000 |
| Cash at bank | 1,10,00,000 |
| Interest accrued but not received | 65,00,000 |
| Expenses payable (audit, broker) | 28,00,000 |
| Units outstanding | 6,20,00,000 units |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Total Assets**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&= 42,00,00,000 + 18,50,00,000 + 1,10,00,000 + 65,00,000 \\
&= 62,25,00,000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Total Liabilities**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&= 28,00,000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Net Assets**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&= 62,25,00,000 - 28,00,000 \\
&= 61,97,00,000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: NAV per unit**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NAV} &= \frac{61,97,00,000}{6,20,00,000} \\
&= 9.9951 \text{ ₹ per unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Each unit carries a NAV of **₹9.9951** as on the valuation date.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) "A Mutual Fund is the ideal investment vehicle for a small retail investor." Discuss this statement with reference to professional management, diversification, liquidity, and affordability.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The statement is justified for retail investors because:

1. **Professional Management (2 marks):** Small investors lack the time, expertise, and tools to research 500+ listed companies. A SEBI-registered Fund Manager (often a CFA / MBA Finance) performs fundamental and technical analysis, sector allocation, and risk management on their behalf — for an expense ratio of as low as 0.5%–1.5% p.a.

2. **Diversification (2 marks):** ₹500 invested in a single stock gives exposure to one company. The same ₹500 in an Index Fund gives exposure to **50 (Nifty) or 500 (Sensex)** stocks simultaneously — instantly reducing **firm-specific risk** to negligible levels.

3. **Liquidity (1.5 marks):** Open-ended equity funds allow redemption at NAV on any business day with proceeds credited in **T+1 to T+3** working days — superior to many small-savings instruments.

4. **Affordability & Flexibility (1.5 marks):** SIPs starting at ₹500 monthly allow **rupee-cost averaging**, a benefit impossible in direct real estate or gold.

Hence, a mutual fund combines safety, returns, liquidity, and convenience — suiting the salaried middle-class investor.

---

**(b) Differentiate between Open-Ended and Closed-Ended Mutual Fund schemes. Which one is preferred by retail investors and why?** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Open-Ended Fund | Closed-Ended Fund |
|---|---|---|
| Subscription / Redemption | Continuous — any business day | Only at NFO initially; at maturity thereafter |
| Fund Size | Variable (unlimited) | Fixed at NFO |
| NAV-Based Pricing | Yes, at daily NAV | Listed on stock exchange; trades at premium/discount to NAV |
| Liquidity | High (intrinsic) | Limited (depends on exchange volume) |
| Listing | Not listed | Listed on NSE / BSE |
| Suitability | Long-term SIP investors | Specific tenure investors seeking fixed maturity |

**Preference:** Retail investors prefer **Open-Ended** schemes because of (i) anytime entry/exit, (ii) SIP facility enabling rupee-cost averaging, (iii) absence of market price risk, and (iv) tax efficiency through flexible holding periods.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Board Examiner's Perspective):**
> 1. **Quoting NAV without units** — Always write "₹ per unit"; examiners deduct 0.5 mark if the unit is omitted.
> 2. **Wrong precision** — NAV must be in **4 decimals** (e.g., ₹17.5620), not 2. Using 2 decimals costs 1 mark in numericals.
> 3. **Confusing NAV with Offer Price** — In NFOs, offer price is ₹10 (face value); NAV begins from there. Fresh issue ≠ NAV.
> 4. **Missing SEBI / AMFI mention** — Any mutual fund definition without SEBI regulation is incomplete; lose up to 2 marks.
> 5. **Listing parties but not stating their duties** — Simply naming "Trustee, AMC, Custodian" without explaining *what they do* yields only 50% marks.
> 6. **Confusing Mutual Fund with ETF / PMS / AIF** — ETFs are *exchange-traded* and trade like shares; PMS requires minimum ₹50 lakh; AIF minimum ₹1 crore. Mutual funds start at ₹500.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **One-line definition:** A mutual fund is a trust-managed pooled investment vehicle regulated by SEBI.
- **Legal form:** Trust — governed by the Indian Trusts Act, 1882 + SEBI (Mutual Funds) Regulations, 1996.
- **Three pillars:** Sponsor, Trustee, AMC.
- **Supporting pillars:** Custodian (asset safety), RTA (investor records).
- **Daily NAV formula:** $\text{NAV} = (\text{Assets} - \text{Liabilities}) / \text{Outstanding Units}$.
- **Expense Ratio cap:** 2.50% (equity) | 2.25% (debt) by SEBI.
- **Minimum Investment:** ₹500 SIP / ₹1,000 lump-sum (typical open-ended equity scheme).
- **Tax treatment (equity):** LTCG > ₹1 lakh @ 10%; STCG @ 15%.
- **Tax treatment (debt):** Slab rate (post-April 2023, no indexation for debt MFs).
- **Categories:** Equity, Debt, Hybrid, Solution-oriented, Index/ETF, Fund of Fund.
- **Plans:** Direct Plan (lower expense, no commission) vs Regular Plan (broker-assisted, higher expense).
- **Options:** Growth (auto reinvest) vs IDCW (Dividend payout / reinvest).
- **Regulators to memorise:** SEBI + AMFI + RBI (for liquid schemes).
- **Key Service Providers:** CAMS, KFintech, Sundaram Fasttrack (legacy), Franklin Templeton (AMC example).
- **Golden line for any definition answer:** *"Professional management of diversified portfolios at an affordable cost under SEBI regulation."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types and Classification of Mutual Funds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types and Classification of Mutual Funds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — TYPES AND CLASSIFICATION OF MUTUAL FUNDS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
A **Mutual Fund** is a trust that pools money from a group of investors sharing a common financial goal, and invests the collected corpus in securities such as equities, debt instruments, money market instruments, hybrid instruments, or a combination thereof, in accordance with a pre-defined investment objective. The fund is managed by a professional **Asset Management Company (AMC)** and the price at which investors can buy or sell units is determined by the **Net Asset Value (NAV)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** Under the OEIET832 syllabus, mutual fund classification is examined primarily along **four canonical axes**: (1) Structural Classification, (2) Asset Class / Investment Objective, (3) Operational Mechanics (Load, Entry/Exit), and (4) Specialty / Thematic Orientation. Students are expected to draw clear comparison tables in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Corporate Bus to Dalal Street"
> [!NOTE]
> **The Bus Analogy:**
> Imagine a 40-seater Volvo bus leaving Ernakulam for a trip to Sabarimala. Individually, none of the 40 pilgrims can afford a private luxury coach, but together they pool ₹50 each (total ₹2,000) and hire the bus comfortably.
> - The **bus** = the Mutual Fund scheme.
> - The **₹50 ticket** = a mutual fund **unit**.
> - The **driver** (who decides the route, pit-stops, and rest areas) = the **Fund Manager** of the AMC.
> - The **pilgrims** = the **unit-holders** (investors).
> - The **fairness of sharing cost** = the **NAV** (per-unit value).
>
> If at the end of the trip the bus collected tolls and donations worth more than it cost, every pilgrim gets a proportional share of the surplus. If it suffered losses, every pilgrim shares the loss. This proportional, pooled, professionally-managed structure is *exactly* what a mutual fund is.

### 1.3 Why Classification Matters
With **47+ Asset Management Companies** and **1,200+ schemes** registered with SEBI in India, an investor cannot evaluate every scheme. **Classification** acts as a *pre-filtering mechanism* that:
- Aligns the scheme's risk-reward profile with the investor's risk appetite.
- Ensures **regulatory consistency** under SEBI's Mutual Fund Regulations, 1996.
- Enables **apples-to-apples comparison** between competing schemes.
- Determines **tax treatment** (e.g., equity-oriented funds vs. non-equity funds under Section 80C / LTCG framework).

The key classification parameters are:
1. **Structure** — Open-ended, Close-ended, Interval.
2. **Investment Objective** — Growth, Income, Balanced / Hybrid.
3. **Asset Class** — Equity, Debt, Hybrid, Money Market, Multi-Asset.
4. **Specialty** — Index, Sector, Thematic, Fund of Funds, ETFs, Liquid.
5. **Operational** — Load vs No-Load, Direct vs Regular Plan.
6. **Capital Deployment** — Diversified vs Concentrated.
7. **Management Style** — Active vs Passive.
8. **Geographic Focus** — Domestic vs International / Global.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** In a 14-mark ESE question, the examiner expects the answer to begin with a *definition* of the classification axis, followed by *sub-types*, *features*, *advantages*, and *disadvantages*. Memorize the comparison grid.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Axis 1 — Structural Classification

### 2.1.1 Open-Ended Funds
- Available for subscription and redemption **on a continuous basis** at NAV-related prices.
- **No fixed maturity date**; the fund issues and redeems units on demand.
- Highly **liquid** — investors can enter or exit on any business day.
- NAV is computed **daily** (cut-off time 3:00 PM under SEBI's Uniform Cut-off Timings).
- Best suited for investors seeking **flexibility**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** SBI Bluechip Fund, HDFC Flexi Cap Fund, Axis Liquid Fund.

### 2.1.2 Close-Ended Funds
- Capital is raised through an **NFO (New Fund Offer)** and the fund has a **fixed maturity period** (typically 3–5 years, extendable up to 7 years).
- Units are **not redeemable** before maturity. They trade on stock exchanges (BSE/NSE) like equity shares.
- Price on the exchange may trade at a **premium or discount** to NAV depending on demand-supply.
- Suitable for investors with **long-term horizon** and who can tolerate illiquidity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** Nippon India Fixed Horizon Fund Series, ICICI Prudential Fixed Maturity Plan.

### 2.1.3 Interval Funds
- A **hybrid** of open-ended and close-ended structures.
- The fund remains close-ended but the **AMC declares specific "interval windows"** (typically every quarter for 2–3 days) during which investors may buy or sell units directly from the fund at NAV.
- Combines liquidity windows with disciplined portfolio management.

### 2.1.4 Comparison Grid

| Feature | Open-Ended | Close-Ended | Interval |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maturity | None | Fixed (3–7 yrs) | Fixed, with windows |
| Liquidity | Daily at NAV | Stock exchange only | NAV-based in declared windows |
| Price Mechanism | NAV ± load | Market price (premium/discount) | NAV ± load |
| Unit Issuance | Continuous | Only during NFO | During declared windows |
| SEBI Listing | Not required | Mandatory | Not mandatory |

## 2.2 Classification Axis 2 — By Investment Objective

### 2.2.1 Growth / Equity-Oriented Funds
- Invest ≥ **65%** of corpus in equities (SEBI's threshold for equity taxation).
- Objective: **Capital appreciation** through long-term equity participation.
- High risk–high return; volatility aligned with equity markets.
- Sub-categories:
  - **Multi Cap Fund** — ≥ 25% each in large, mid, small cap; balance flexibly.
  - **Large Cap Fund** — ≥ 80% in large-cap (top 1–100 by market cap).
  - **Mid Cap Fund** — ≥ 65% in mid-cap (101–250 by market cap).
  - **Small Cap Fund** — ≥ 65% in small-cap (251+ by market cap).
  - **Flexi Cap Fund** — ≥ 65% in equity across market caps.

### 2.2.2 Income / Debt-Oriented Funds
- Invest predominantly in **fixed-income securities** (G-Secs, corporate bonds, T-Bills, CDs, CPs).
- Objective: **Stable income with capital preservation**.
- Risk depends on **duration** and **credit quality** of the portfolio.
- Sub-categories: Liquid, Ultra Short Duration, Short Duration, Medium Duration, Long Duration, Gilt, Corporate Bond, Credit Risk, Banking & PSU.

> [!NOTE]
> **SEBI Categorization, 2017:** Debt funds are now classified strictly by ** Macaulay Duration**, replacing the older "income, short-term, gilt" labels. Students must know the new nomenclature.

### 2.2.3 Balanced / Hybrid Funds
- Invest in a **mix of equity and debt** to balance risk and return.
- Under SEBI's 2017 re-categorization, hybrid funds include: Conservative Hybrid, Balanced Hybrid, Aggressive Hybrid, Dynamic Asset Allocation (Balanced Advantage), Multi Asset Allocation, Equity Savings, Arbitrage.

### 2.2.4 Liquid / Money Market Funds
- Invest in **instruments with maturity ≤ 91 days**.
- Provide high liquidity with minimal credit risk.
- Ideal for parking **surplus cash for 1 day to 3 months**.

## 2.3 Classification Axis 3 — By Specialty

| Specialty Fund Type | Description | Risk |
|---|---|---|
| Index Fund | Passively tracks a specific index (Nifty 50, Sensex) | Market risk |
| ETF (Exchange Traded Fund) | Trades on exchange like a stock; tracks an index | Market + liquidity |
| Sector Fund | Concentrates in one sector (IT, Pharma, Banking) | High concentration risk |
| Thematic Fund | Invests around a theme (ESG, Infrastructure, Digital India) | Theme-specific risk |
| Fund of Funds (FoF) | Invests in other mutual fund schemes | Underlying fund risk |
| Gold Fund | Invests in gold bullion or gold-related securities | Commodity risk |
| International Fund | Invests in foreign equities (e.g., US Nasdaq, S&P 500) | Currency + country risk |

## 2.4 Classification Axis 4 — Operational / Commercial Classification

### 2.4.1 Load vs No-Load Funds
- **Load** = a fee charged at the time of purchase (Entry Load — now banned since 2009) or sale (Exit Load).
- **Exit Load** = a percentage deducted from NAV at the time of redemption. Motive: to discourage premature withdrawal and protect fund performance.
- **No-Load Funds** charge no exit load, but may have an **Expense Ratio**.

### 2.4.2 Direct Plan vs Regular Plan
- **Direct Plan** = Investor buys directly from the AMC; **no distributor commission**; lower expense ratio (by ~0.5% to 1.5%).
- **Regular Plan** = Bought through a distributor/agent; AMC pays trail commission to distributor; higher expense ratio.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Must-Know:** SEBI (2013) mandated all AMCs to offer both Direct and Regular Plans with **separate NAVs** under the same scheme. The difference in NAV reflects the commission waiver.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $NAV = \dfrac{\text{Total Assets} - \text{Total Liabilities}}{\text{Number of Outstanding Units}}$ | Per-unit value of the fund | ₹ per unit, daily |
| 2 | $R_t = \dfrac{NAV_t - NAV_{t-1} + D_t}{NAV_{t-1}}$ | Simple holding period return | Decimal / % |
| 3 | $CAGR = \left(\dfrac{NAV_{\text{end}}}{NAV_{\text{start}}}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1$ | Compounded annual growth rate | % per annum |
| 4 | $\text{Expense Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Total Annual Operating Expenses}}{\text{Average AUM}}$ | Annualized cost of running the fund | % (0.1% – 2.5%) |
| 5 | $\text{Sharpe Ratio} = \dfrac{R_p - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ | Excess return per unit of total risk | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{Treynor Ratio} = \dfrac{R_p - R_f}{\beta_p}$ | Excess return per unit of systematic risk | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\alpha = R_p - \left[R_f + \beta_p (R_m - R_f)\right]$ | Jensen's alpha — fund manager's value-add | Decimal / % |
| 8 | $\text{Asset Allocation} = \dfrac{\text{Value in Asset Class } i}{\text{Total AUM}} \times 100$ | Portfolio composition check | % (must align with objective) |
| 9 | $\text{Modified Duration} = \dfrac{\text{Macaulay Duration}}{1 + YTM}$ | Interest rate sensitivity of bond fund | Years |
| 10 | $\text{SEBI Equity Threshold} = \geq 65\%$ | Minimum equity exposure to be classified as equity-oriented fund | % |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Table Symbol Rule:** Whenever writing formulas inside a markdown table, **never** use the bare pipe `\|` character to denote absolute value. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to prevent table-breaking.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of This Classification System

- **Investor Suitability:** A 22-year-old engineering graduate in Trivandrum choosing between a Liquid Fund and a Mid Cap Fund is a *risk-misallocation* problem — the classification system guides the suitability match.
- **Portfolio Construction:** SEBI's Risk-o-Meter (introduced 2020) maps each scheme into 6 risk buckets, directly derived from its objective and asset class.
- **Tax Computation:** A fund's classification as equity-oriented vs non-equity determines whether the **15% Short-Term Capital Gains (STCG)** or **10% LTCG (above ₹1 lakh)** applies.
- **Regulatory Reporting:** AMCs must declare a fund's category at the time of NFO; mis-categorization attracts SEBI penalty.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Net Asset Value (NAV) of a Mutual Fund

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement:** The NAV of a mutual fund scheme on a given business day is the market value of its assets minus its liabilities, divided by the total number of units outstanding.

### Step 3.1.1 — Identify the Components

Let the fund hold $N$ different securities. On day $t$:
- $P_{i,t}$ = market price of security $i$ on day $t$.
- $Q_{i,t}$ = quantity of security $i$ held by the fund.
- $L_t$ = total liabilities on day $t$ (expenses payable, borrowings, etc.).
- $U_t$ = number of outstanding units on day $t$.

### Step 3.1.2 — Compute Market Value of Total Assets (MV)

The total market value of the portfolio is the sum of the market values of all individual securities held:

$$
MV_t = \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{i,t} \cdot Q_{i,t}
$$

**Explanation:** Each holding contributes its market value (price × quantity). This sum reflects the realizable worth of the fund's investments at current market prices.

### Step 3.1.3 — Compute Net Assets (NA)

$$
NA_t = MV_t - L_t = \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{i,t} \cdot Q_{i,t} - L_t
$$

**Explanation:** We subtract liabilities because, in a wind-up scenario, liabilities must first be settled before unit-holders receive any proceeds. Hence the residual is what unit-holders *own*.

### Step 3.1.4 — Divide by Outstanding Units

$$
NAV_t = \frac{NA_t}{U_t} = \frac{\left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{i,t} \cdot Q_{i,t}\right) - L_t}{U_t}
$$

**Explanation:** Since the fund is collectively owned by unit-holders in proportion to units held, dividing the net assets by the unit count gives the per-unit claim — the NAV.

### Step 3.1.5 — Numerical Illustration

Suppose a fund holds 10,000 shares of Reliance Industries at ₹2,500 each, 50,000 shares of Infosys at ₹1,400 each, and ₹20 lakh in cash. Liabilities = ₹5 lakh. Outstanding units = 5 lakh.

Compute market value of equity:
$$
MV_{\text{equity}} = (10{,}000 \times 2{,}500) + (50{,}000 \times 1{,}400) = 2.5 \text{ Cr} + 7.0 \text{ Cr} = 9.5 \text{ Cr}
$$

Add cash:
$$
\text{Total Assets} = 9.5 \text{ Cr} + 0.20 \text{ Cr} = 9.70 \text{ Cr}
$$

Subtract liabilities:
$$
\text{Net Assets} = 9.70 \text{ Cr} - 0.05 \text{ Cr} = 9.65 \text{ Cr}
$$

Compute NAV:
$$
NAV = \frac{9{,}65{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = 193.00
$$

> [!TIP]
> The unit-holder is effectively told: *“Your single unit represents ₹193 of the underlying pool today.”*

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) of a Mutual Fund

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement:** CAGR measures the smoothed annual rate of return assuming profits are reinvested and growth is compounded.

### Step 3.2.1 — Define the Total Growth Multiple

Let the investment grow from $V_0$ (initial NAV or value) to $V_n$ over $n$ years. The total growth multiple is:
$$
G = \frac{V_n}{V_0}
$$

**Explanation:** This ratio tells us by what factor the initial capital has grown.

### Step 3.2.2 — Decompose into n Annual Steps

If the investment grows by a constant rate $r$ compounded annually for $n$ years:
$$
V_n = V_0 \cdot (1 + r)^n
$$

**Explanation:** This is the standard compound interest equation applied to NAV growth.

### Step 3.2.3 — Solve for r

Divide both sides by $V_0$:
$$
\frac{V_n}{V_0} = (1 + r)^n
$$

Take the n-th root of both sides:
$$
\left(\frac{V_n}{V_0}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}} = 1 + r
$$

Isolate r:
$$
r = \left(\frac{V_n}{V_0}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1
$$

Therefore:
$$
CAGR = \left(\frac{NAV_{\text{end}}}{NAV_{\text{start}}}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1
$$

**Explanation:** CAGR flattens the volatile path of yearly returns into one comparable annualized rate — extremely useful for benchmarking two funds with different histories.

### Step 3.2.4 — Numerical Illustration

Initial NAV (Jan 2020) = ₹100. Final NAV (Jan 2025) = ₹215. n = 5 years.

$$
CAGR = \left(\frac{215}{100}\right)^{\frac{1}{5}} - 1
$$

$$
= (2.15)^{0.2} - 1
$$

$$
= 1.1655 - 1 = 0.1655 = 16.55\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fund delivered an *equivalent* annual return of **16.55%** compounded over 5 years, even if the actual year-wise returns were 5%, 30%, -10%, 25%, and 35% respectively.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sharpe Ratio (Risk-Adjusted Performance)

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement:** Sharpe Ratio quantifies how much **excess return per unit of total risk** a fund delivers over the risk-free rate.

### Step 3.3.1 — Set Up Variables

- $R_p$ = average annualized return of the fund.
- $R_f$ = risk-free rate (typically 91-day T-Bill yield).
- $\sigma_p$ = standard deviation of fund returns (total risk).

### Step 3.3.2 — Compute Excess Return

$$
\text{Excess Return} = R_p - R_f
$$

**Explanation:** This is the reward above and beyond the risk-free alternative — the *true* compensation for bearing risk.

### Step 3.3.3 — Normalize by Risk

To compare two funds with different volatilities, divide excess return by risk:
$$
\text{Sharpe} = \frac{R_p - R_f}{\sigma_p}
$$

**Explanation:** A higher Sharpe Ratio means the fund is delivering *more reward per unit of pain (volatility)*. It is the universal yardstick for risk-adjusted performance.

### Step 3.3.4 — Numerical Illustration

| Fund | $R_p$ | $R_f$ | $\sigma_p$ | Sharpe |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A (Aggressive) | 18% | 6% | 12% | (18−6)/12 = 1.00 |
| B (Balanced) | 14% | 6% | 5% | (14−6)/5 = 1.60 |

**Interpretation:** Fund B is *superior* on a risk-adjusted basis despite having lower absolute return, because it delivers more reward per unit of risk.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — A vs B vs C

| Criterion | Open-Ended Equity | Close-Ended Equity | Liquid Fund |
|---|---|---|---|
| Liquidity | Daily | Lock-in till maturity | Daily (T+1 settlement) |
| Risk | High (equity) | High (equity) | Very Low (≤91 days) |
| Expected Return | 12–15% CAGR | 12–15% CAGR | 6–7% p.a. |
| Tax (held > 1 yr) | LTCG 10% (> ₹1L) | LTCG 10% (> ₹1L) | Slab rate (non-equity) |
| NAV Disclosure | Daily | Daily + exchange price | Daily |
| Best Suited For | Long-term wealth | Disciplined long-term | Emergency fund / cash park |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** A common KTU ESE mistake is writing *"Close-ended funds are illiquid and therefore bad."* This is **incorrect** — illiquidity is a *feature* that allows the fund manager to invest in less-liquid securities (e.g., unlisted bonds, deep-value equities) without redemption pressure, often producing superior long-term returns.
