# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, OEIET832]**
**Differentiate between an Open-Ended and a Close-Ended Mutual Fund Scheme.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | Open-Ended Fund | Close-Ended Fund |
|---|---|---|
| Maturity | No fixed maturity | Fixed maturity (3–7 yrs) |
| Liquidity | Daily entry/exit at NAV | Listed on stock exchange; tradable |
| Price | NAV ± load | Market price (may be at premium/discount) |
| Unit Issuance | Continuous | Only at NFO |

**Conclusion:** Open-ended funds offer flexibility and are investor-friendly for liquid investing, while close-ended funds enforce discipline and enable managers to take long-term, less-liquid calls.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, OEIET832]**
**What is the difference between a Direct Plan and a Regular Plan in a mutual fund?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **Direct Plan** is purchased directly from the AMC without a distributor intermediary. The investor enjoys a **lower expense ratio** (typically 0.5%–1.5% lower) as no trail commission is paid. A **Regular Plan** is bought through an AMFI-registered distributor; the investor pays a higher expense ratio in lieu of the advisory/distribution service.

**Conclusion:** For a DIY investor with financial literacy, Direct Plans are mathematically superior over the long term.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The student must attempt **one full question** out of the two options provided.

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 2]

**(a) Classify mutual funds along the dimension of investment objective. Explain any four sub-categories under equity-oriented funds with their risk-return characteristics. (7 Marks)**

**(b) What is Net Asset Value (NAV)? Derive the formula and compute the NAV of a fund with the following data. (7 Marks)**

| Security | Quantity | Market Price (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| TCS | 5,000 | 3,800 |
| HDFC Bank | 10,000 | 1,650 |
| SBI T-Bill | 200 units | 100,000 each |
| Cash in Hand | — | ₹15,00,000 |

**Liabilities = ₹25,00,000. Outstanding Units = 6,00,000.**

---

#### Model Solution for A(a) — 7 Marks

Classification along investment objective (3 marks):
1. **Growth / Equity-oriented** — ≥ 65% in equity; capital appreciation.
2. **Income / Debt-oriented** — predominantly in fixed income; stable income.
3. **Balanced / Hybrid** — mix of equity and debt; moderate growth.
4. **Liquid / Money Market** — ≤91-day instruments; capital preservation.

Four equity sub-categories (4 marks):

| Sub-Category | SEBI Definition | Risk | Return (Long-Term) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Large Cap Fund | ≥80% in top 100 stocks | Moderate | 10–12% CAGR |
| Mid Cap Fund | ≥65% in stocks ranked 101–250 | High | 13–15% CAGR |
| Small Cap Fund | ≥65% in stocks ranked 251+ | Very High | 15–18% CAGR |
| Flexi Cap Fund | ≥65% equity, no cap constraint | Moderate–High | 12–15% CAGR |

**Conclusion:** Investor's risk appetite and horizon should dictate the sub-category selection.

---

#### Model Solution for A(b) — 7 Marks

**Definition of NAV (2 marks):**
NAV is the per-unit market value of a mutual fund's net assets. It is the price at which units of an open-ended fund are issued and redeemed on a business day.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

$$
NAV = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i \cdot Q_i \; - \; L}{U}
$$

where $P_i, Q_i$ are the price and quantity of the $i$-th security, $L$ is total liabilities, and $U$ is outstanding units.

**Numerical Computation (3 marks):**

Step 1 — Market value of TCS:
$$
5{,}000 \times 3{,}800 = 1{,}90{,}00{,}000
$$

Step 2 — Market value of HDFC Bank:
$$
10{,}000 \times 1{,}650 = 1{,}65{,}00{,}000
$$

Step 3 — Market value of SBI T-Bill:
$$
200 \times 1{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}00{,}00{,}000
$$

Step 4 — Add cash:
$$
\text{Total Assets} = 1.90 + 1.65 + 2.00 + 0.15 = 5.70 \text{ Cr}
$$

Step 5 — Subtract liabilities:
$$
\text{Net Assets} = 5.70 - 0.25 = 5.45 \text{ Cr} = 5{,}45{,}00{,}000
$$

Step 6 — Divide by units:
$$
NAV = \frac{5{,}45{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} = 90.83
$$

**Answer: NAV = ₹90.83 per unit.**

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]

**(a) Explain the structural classification of mutual funds. Compare the operational features of open-ended, close-ended, and interval funds. (7 Marks)**

**(b) "The Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) is the most reliable yardstick for comparing long-term mutual fund performance." Justify this statement with a numerical illustration involving two funds. (7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for B(a) — 7 Marks

**Structural Classification (3 marks):**
Mutual funds are structurally classified into three categories:
1. **Open-Ended Funds** — continuous subscription/redemption at NAV.
2. **Close-Ended Funds** — fixed maturity; tradable on stock exchanges.
3. **Interval Funds** — close-ended with periodic liquidity windows.

**Comparison (3 marks):**

| Feature | Open-Ended | Close-Ended | Interval |
|---|---|---|---|
| Liquidity | Daily at NAV | Stock exchange | Window-based |
| Maturity | None | 3–7 years | Fixed, with windows |
| Pricing | NAV ± load | Market premium/discount | NAV ± load |
| Best Suited For | Investors needing flexibility | Long-term disciplined investors | Periodic liquidity seekers |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Each structure serves a distinct investor need; structural classification helps the AMC design a scheme that matches its target audience's behavior.

---

#### Model Solution for B(b) — 7 Marks

**CAGR Definition (2 marks):**
CAGR (Compound Annual Growth Rate) is the constant annual rate at which an initial investment would have grown to reach its final value, assuming profits are reinvested.

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$
CAGR = \left(\frac{V_{\text{end}}}{V_{\text{start}}}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1
$$

**Justification (1 mark):** CAGR neutralizes the effect of volatile yearly returns and gives a single comparable rate, making it ideal for cross-fund comparison.

**Numerical Illustration (3 marks):**

| Year | Fund X Return | Fund Y Return |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | +10% | +35% |
| 2 | +40% | −10% |
| 3 | −5% | +30% |
| 4 | +25% | +15% |
| 5 | +5% | +5% |

Initial NAV = ₹100 in both.

**Fund X final NAV:**
$$
100 \times 1.10 \times 1.40 \times 0.95 \times 1.25 \times 1.05 = 191.66
$$

**Fund X CAGR:**
$$
CAGR_X = (191.66/100)^{0.2} - 1 = 13.89\%
$$

**Fund Y final NAV:**
$$
100 \times 1.35 \times 0.90 \times 1.30 \times 1.15 \times 1.05 = 191.27
$$

**Fund Y CAGR:**
$$
CAGR_Y = (191.27/100)^{0.2} - 1 = 13.85\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Despite radically different year-wise return profiles (Fund X volatile, Fund Y smoother), both funds delivered almost identical CAGR. CAGR thus allows fair benchmarking.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Leading to Mark Deductions in OEIET832 ESE:**
> 1. **Conflating "Open-Ended" with "Equity Fund"** — structure and asset class are *independent* classification axes; a fund can be open-ended AND equity-oriented.
> 2. **Writing "Direct Plan = cheaper" without explaining** that the cost difference arises from waiver of *trail commission* to the distributor.
> 3. **Forgetting the SEBI 65% rule** when defining equity-oriented funds. Always cite the regulatory threshold.
> 4. **Confusing Exit Load with Expense Ratio.** Exit load is a one-time redemption fee that goes back to the fund; expense ratio is an annual operating cost charged by the AMC.
> 5. **In NAV calculation, omitting the cash component** held by the fund. This is a frequent 1–2 mark loss.
> 6. **In CAGR, forgetting the exponent $1/n$** — students often write $\sqrt[n]{V_n/V_0} - 1$ but the exponent matters.
> 7. **Treating Sharpe Ratio and Treynor Ratio as interchangeable.** Sharpe uses total risk ($\sigma$); Treynor uses systematic risk ($\beta$).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this and revise the night before the exam:**

- A **Mutual Fund** pools money from many investors to invest in a diversified portfolio of securities under professional management.
- The **NAV** is computed daily as $\frac{\text{Assets} - \text{Liabilities}}{\text{Units Outstanding}}$.
- **Open-ended** funds are liquid and continuously tradable; **Close-ended** funds have a fixed maturity and trade on exchanges; **Interval** funds combine both features.
- **Asset Class** classification (Equity, Debt, Hybrid, Liquid) determines the **tax treatment** of the scheme.
- An **equity-oriented fund** must invest ≥ **65%** in equities (SEBI mandate).
- **Direct Plan** has lower expense ratio; **Regular Plan** pays distributor commission.
- **Index/ETF funds** are *passive* — they replicate a benchmark, not beat it.
- **Sector funds** carry high concentration risk; **Multi-cap / Flexi-cap** funds offer diversification.
- **Fund of Funds (FoF)** invests in other mutual funds; the NAV has *two layers* of expense ratio.
- **CAGR** is the right metric for long-term performance comparison.
- **Sharpe Ratio** measures risk-adjusted return using total risk; **Treynor Ratio** uses systematic risk.
- **Expense Ratio** = Annual operating cost as a percentage of AUM; capped by SEBI norms.
- **SEBI Risk-o-Meter** (2020) classifies schemes into 6 risk levels for retail investor protection.
- **ELSS (Equity Linked Savings Scheme)** = the only mutual fund with **Section 80C** tax benefit, with a 3-year lock-in.
- **Dividend Option** vs **Growth Option** — dividend distributes profits; growth reinvests them to compound NAV.
- For KTU ESE: always **state the classification axis first**, then enumerate sub-types, features, and pros/cons.
- Always use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ (not bare `|`) when writing absolute values inside markdown tables.

---

*End of Module 2 Notes — Types and Classification of Mutual Funds*
*Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | 2024 Scheme | NEP 2020 Aligned*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages and Disadvantages of Passive Investing Over Active Investing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Advantages and Disadvantages of Passive Investing Over Active Investing ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Passive Investing** is an investment strategy that aims to maximize returns by minimizing buying and selling activities, typically by tracking a specific market index (such as the Nifty 50, S&P 500, or BSE Sensex). It involves holding a portfolio that mirrors the composition and performance of the chosen benchmark index, thereby "matching the market" rather than outperforming it. **Active Investing**, by contrast, is an investment strategy where the portfolio manager actively makes specific investment decisions — buying, selling, and rebalancing — with the objective of outperforming a benchmark index (generating **Alpha**, i.e., excess return over the benchmark). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction:** Passive investing accepts the market return (Beta return), while active investing attempts to beat the market through security selection, market timing, and tactical asset allocation. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The Highway Analogy) > [!NOTE] > **Intuition for Students:** > Imagine you need to travel from Kochi to Bengaluru. > - **Active Investing** is like hiring a personal driver who knows shortcuts, avoids traffic, and may even take a backroad. Sometimes he arrives faster; sometimes the shortcut is closed and he gets lost. > - **Passive Investing** is like taking the well-paved National Highway (NH-44). It is predictable, cheap (no driver salary), and you are statistically guaranteed to reach the destination in a reasonable time — because everyone else takes the highway too, and the highway is maintained for you. In financial terms, the **National Highway is the Market Index** (Nifty 50 / S&P 500), and the **"driver salary" is the Expense Ratio** of a mutual fund. ### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics | Metric | Typical Value (Passive) | Typical Value (Active) | |---|---|---| | **Expense Ratio** | 0.03% – 0.20% | 1.00% – 2.50% | | **Portfolio Turnover** | < 10% / year | 50% – 100%+ / year | | **Tracking Error** | < 0.50% | N/A (no benchmark mandate) | | **Goal** | Match benchmark | Beat benchmark (Alpha) | | **Typical Vehicles** | Index Funds, ETFs | Actively managed funds, hedge funds | > [!IMPORTANT] > The expense ratio is the **annual fee** charged by a fund, expressed as a percentage of assets under management (AUM). A 1% difference compounded over 30 years can erode nearly **25% of the final corpus** (as demonstrated in Section 3). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic of Passive Investing The passive investing philosophy rests on three foundational pillars from modern financial theory: 1. **Efficient Market Hypothesis (EMH)** — In semi-strong form efficiency, all publicly available information is already reflected in stock prices. Therefore, no manager can consistently beat the market using public data. Attempting to do so only adds cost (expense ratio) without adding expected return. 2. **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)** — The expected return of any portfolio equals the risk-free rate plus Beta times the market risk premium: $$E(R_p) = R_f + \beta_p \times [E(R_m) - R_f]$$ where $E(R_p)$ = expected portfolio return, $R_f$ = risk-free rate, $\beta_p$ = portfolio beta, and $E(R_m)$ = expected market return. Passive index funds naturally deliver this expected return without management drag. 3. **Law of Large Numbers & Diversification** — Holding all (or a representative sample of) securities in an index eliminates **idiosyncratic (firm-specific) risk**, leaving only **systematic (market) risk**, which is the only risk for which investors are compensated. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $R_{net} = R_{index} - E_{ratio}$ | Net investor return = Index return minus expense ratio | True cost of passive investing | | 2 | $TE = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(R_{p,i} - R_{b,i})^2}$ | Tracking Error = Std. dev. of (portfolio return − benchmark return) | Measures how closely a passive fund mirrors its index | | 3 | $\alpha = R_p - [R_f + \beta_p(R_m - R_f)]$ | Jensen's Alpha = Excess return over CAPM prediction | Measures active manager's value-add | | 4 | $S_p = \frac{R_p - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ | Sharpe Ratio = Excess return per unit of total risk | Risk-adjusted return measure | | 5 | $FV = PV \times (1+r - c)^n$ | Final Value with cost drag, where $c$ = annual cost, $r$ = gross return | Impact of expense ratio over time | | 6 | $R_{geometric} = \prod_{t=1}^{n}(1+R_t)^{1/n} - 1$ | Compound annual growth rate (CAGR) | True multi-year return | > [!IMPORTANT] > Always use the **geometric mean (CAGR)** for multi-period returns, not the arithmetic mean. KTU exam questions frequently test this distinction. ### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Finance - **Retirement Planning (EPF, NPS):** The Indian National Pension System (NPS) uses passive index-tracking equity funds (e.g., Nifty 50 index) for its "Auto Choice" lifecycle fund, reducing costs for millions of subscribers. - **Wealth Management:** Robo-advisors globally (e.g., Betterment, Wealthfront) construct portfolios almost entirely of passive ETFs. - **Institutional Asset Allocation:** Sovereign wealth funds and university endowments increasingly allocate a portion to passive core-satellite strategies. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The "Cost Drag" of Active Investing This is the most important numerical demonstration of the advantage of passive investing and a **high-frequency KTU exam question**. **Problem Setup:** Suppose two investors, **A (Active)** and **P (Passive)**, each invest ₹10,00,000 for 30 years. The market index returns **12% per annum** before costs. The active fund charges a **2% expense ratio**, while the passive fund charges **0.20%**. **Step 1: Define the net return for each investor.** $$ \begin{aligned} R_A^{net} &= R_{index} - c_A = 12\% - 2.00\% = 10.00\% \\ R_P^{net} &= R_{index} - c_P = 12\% - 0.20\% = 11.80\% \end{aligned} $$ Here, $c_A$ and $c_P$ are the annual expense ratios of the active and passive funds respectively. The deduction happens annually, which means the cost itself loses compounding potential — this is the **"drag"** effect. **Step 2: Apply the compound growth formula $FV = PV \times (1+r)^n$ over $n = 30$ years.** $$ \begin{aligned} FV_A &= 10,00,000 \times (1 + 0.10)^{30} \\ FV_P &= 10,00,000 \times (1 + 0.1180)^{30} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Evaluate the powers numerically.** $$ \begin{aligned} (1.10)^{30} &\approx 17.4494 \\ (1.1180)^{30} &\approx 28.6244 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Compute the final corpus values.** $$ \begin{aligned} FV_A &= 10,00,000 \times 17.4494 = \text{₹ }1,74,49,400 \\ FV_P &= 10,00,000 \times 28.6244 = \text{₹ }2,86,24,400 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Compute the absolute and percentage difference.** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta FV &= FV_P - FV_A = 2,86,24,400 - 1,74,49,400 = \text{₹ }1,11,75,000 \\ \text{Loss \%} &= \frac{\Delta FV}{FV_P} \times 100 = \frac{1,11,75,000}{2,86,24,400} \times 100 \approx 39.04\% \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** The passive investor ends up with approximately **39% more wealth** purely from cost savings — not from superior stock-picking, but from **not paying for inferior stock-picking**. This is the single most powerful argument for passive investing. ### 3.2 Detailed Comparative Matrix: Advantages & Disadvantages of Passive Investing Below is the **complete KTU-exam-ready analytical matrix** that you must memorize. | # | ADVANTAGES of Passive Investing | DISADVANTAGES of Passive Investing | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Lower Expense Ratio** (0.03–0.20% vs 1.5–2.5%): Direct boost to net returns. | **No Alpha Generation:** Cannot beat the market; investor is destined to earn only the market return. | | 2 | **Tax Efficiency:** Low turnover (5–10%) means fewer taxable events (capital gains distributions). | **No Downside Protection:** During bear markets (e.g., 2008, 2020 COVID crash), the index falls too — passive funds fall in lockstep. | | 3 | **Transparency:** Holdings are publicly known (they are the index constituents). No "hidden" bets. | **No Flexibility in Asset Allocation:** Cannot move to cash or defensive sectors during perceived overvaluation. | | 4 | **Diversification:** Exposure to 50–500 stocks in a single fund eliminates idiosyncratic risk. | **Market-Cap Bias:** Index weighting gives overweight exposure to overvalued large-caps (e.g., a few mega-cap stocks in Nifty 50). | | 5 | **Consistency:** Performance does not depend on the skill of any individual fund manager. | **Tracking Error & Sampling Risk:** Some index funds do not hold all 500 stocks; sampling introduces small deviations. | | 6 | **Lower Portfolio Turnover → Lower Transaction Costs:** Fewer trades mean less brokerage, less bid-ask spread cost. | **Limited Customization:** Cannot exclude "sin stocks" (tobacco, weapons) or controversial sectors without paying active-management fees. | | 7 | **Accessibility for Retail Investors:** Index funds have low minimum SIPs (₹500 in India via mutual fund STPs). | **Behavioral Disadvantage in Volatility:** Watching the index fall daily may cause panic redemption — no active manager to "talk you down". | | 8 | **Theoretical Backing:** Supported by EMH and academic evidence (SPIVA scorecard shows < 10% of active large-cap funds beat index over 15 years). | **Index Bubble / Concentration Risk:** Passive flows can inflate the price of index heavyweights, creating a "bubble in the index" (a 2020–2021 concern in the US market). | > [!NOTE] > **The SPIVA Scorecard (S&P Indices vs. Active):** Published by S&P Dow Jones Indices, this report has consistently shown that over 10- and 15-year horizons, **roughly 85–90% of actively managed large-cap funds underperform their benchmark index** after fees. This is the empirical evidence that powers the passive investing movement. ### 3.3 Worked Example: Tracking Error Calculation **Problem:** A Nifty 50 index fund reports the following monthly returns versus the Nifty 50 index for 6 months: 1.2% vs 1.3%, 0.8% vs 0.7%, -2.1% vs -2.0%, 1.5% vs 1.6%, 0.4% vs 0.3%, -1.0% vs -1.1%. Compute the tracking error (annualized). **Step 1: Compute the deviation $d_i = R_{p,i} - R_{b,i}$ for each month.** $$ \begin{aligned} d_1 &= 1.2 - 1.3 = -0.10 \\ d_2 &= 0.8 - 0.7 = +0.10 \\ d_3 &= -2.1 - (-2.0) = -0.10 \\ d_4 &= 1.5 - 1.6 = -0.10 \\ d_5 &= 0.4 - 0.3 = +0.10 \\ d_6 &= -1.0 - (-1.1) = +0.10 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute the sample standard deviation of the deviations.** $$ \begin{aligned} \bar{d} &= \frac{-0.10 + 0.10 - 0.10 - 0.10 + 0.10 + 0.10}{6} = 0.00 \\ \sigma_d &= \sqrt{\frac{\sum(d_i - \bar{d})^2}{n-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{6 \times (0.10)^2}{5}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.06}{5}} = \sqrt{0.012} \approx 0.1095\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Annualize the tracking error (multiply by $\sqrt{12}$ for monthly data).** $$ TE_{annual} = 0.1095\% \times \sqrt{12} \approx 0.1095 \times 3.4641 \approx 0.379\% $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > An annualized tracking error **below 0.50%** is considered excellent for an index fund. Values above 1.00% indicate significant replication problems (often due to cash drag, sampling, or derivative use). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Active vs. Passive Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Investor Capital] --> B{Investment Strategy?} B -- Active --> C[Fund Manager Analysis] C --> D[Security Selection
and Market Timing] D --> E[High Turnover Trades] E --> F[High Expense Ratio 1.5 to 2.5 percent] F --> G[Net Return After Costs] B -- Passive --> H[Benchmark Index Selection
e.g. Nifty 50] H --> I[Replicate Index Holdings] I --> J[Low Turnover Trades] J --> K[Low Expense Ratio 0.05 to 0.20 percent] K --> L[Net Return Approx Equal to Index] G --> M[Final Portfolio Value] L --> M M --> N[Long Term Wealth Outcome] ``` ### 4.2 Comparative Process Flow: Cost Drag Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph MKT[Market Index] M1[Gross Return 12 percent] end subgraph ACT[Active Fund Path] A1[Gross 12 percent] --> A2[Less Expense 2.0 percent] --> A3[Net Return 10.0 percent] end subgraph PAS[Passive Fund Path] P1[Gross 12 percent] --> P2[Less Expense 0.2 percent] --> P3[Net Return 11.8 percent] end M1 --> A1 M1 --> P1 A3 --> OUT1[Final Value approx 1.74 Crore over 30 years] P3 --> OUT2[Final Value approx 2.86 Crore over 30 years] ``` ### 4.3 Decision Matrix Flow: When to Choose Which Strategy ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1[Investment Goal?] --> Q2{Horizon and Skill} Q2 -- Long Horizon Low Monitoring --> R1[Choose Passive Index Fund] Q2 -- Short Horizon or Need Alpha --> R2[Choose Active Fund] R1 --> S1[Benefit Low Cost and Diversification] R2 --> S2[Benefit Potential Outperformance and Hedging] S1 --> T1[Verify Tracking Error and Expense Ratio] S2 --> T2[Verify Manager Track Record and Alpha] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The Mermaid diagrams above use **purely alphanumeric node identifiers** (A, B, C, M1, A1, etc.) and **clean uppercase text labels without any markdown formatting tags** — this complies with the KTU diagram compilation safeguard rules. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** > Define the term "Passive Investing". Mention any two vehicles through which a retail investor can implement a passive investment strategy in the Indian market. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Passive investing is an investment strategy that seeks to replicate the performance of a specific market index (such as the Nifty 50 or BSE Sensex) by holding the same securities in the same proportions, instead of attempting to outperform the market through active security selection. The two main vehicles in India are: (1) **Index Mutual Funds** (e.g., UTI Nifty 50 Index Fund), and (2) **Exchange Traded Funds (ETFs)** (e.g., Nippon India Nifty 50 ETF, SBI Nifty 50 ETF). Both offer low-cost market exposure; ETFs trade on the stock exchange like a share, while index funds are bought/sold at NAV. > **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]** > What is meant by "Expense Ratio" in the context of mutual funds? Why is it considered the most decisive factor in long-term wealth creation? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The expense ratio is the annual fee that a mutual fund charges its investors, expressed as a percentage of the scheme's average Assets Under Management (AUM). It covers fund management, administration, marketing, and trustee costs. It is the most decisive factor in long-term wealth creation because of the **compounding effect of cost drag** — a seemingly small difference of 1%–2% per year can erode 25%–40% of the final portfolio value over 25–30 years, as demonstrated in the cost-drag derivation in Section 3. Since gross market returns are statistically similar for both active and passive strategies, the lower expense ratio of passive funds translates directly into higher net returns for the investor. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – ESE Pattern, Module 2 Choice Question]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **(a)** **[7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **advantages of Passive Investing** over Active Investing. Substantiate your answer with a numerical example showing the impact of expense ratio on long-term wealth creation. **(a) Model Answer:** Passive investing offers several decisive advantages over active investing: **1. Lower Expense Ratio:** Passive index funds charge **0.03%–0.20%**, whereas actively managed equity funds charge **1.5%–2.5%**. This 1.3%–2.3% annual saving compounds dramatically over time. **2. Tax Efficiency:** With portfolio turnover of 5%–10% (vs 50%–100% for active funds), passive funds generate far fewer capital gains distributions, reducing the investor's annual tax liability. **3. Transparency & Diversification:** The complete holdings list of an index fund is publicly disclosed and matches the index, providing instant diversification across 50–500 stocks with no key-person (fund manager) risk. **4. Consistency of Returns:** Performance does not depend on the skill, mood, or departure of a particular fund manager. The investor gets the market return — no more, no less — predictably. **Numerical Substantiation (4 of the 7 marks):** Assuming ₹10,00,000 invested for 30 years at 12% gross return: - Active fund (2% expense): $FV = 10,00,000 \times (1.10)^{30} = \text{₹ }1,74,49,400$ - Passive fund (0.2% expense): $FV = 10,00,000 \times (1.118)^{30} = \text{₹ }2,86,24,400$ - Difference: **₹1,11,75,000 (≈39% more wealth) in the passive investor's hands.** This demonstrates that the cost differential — not the fund manager's skill — is the dominant driver of long-term outcome. > **(b)** **[7 Marks]** Explain the **concept of Tracking Error**. How is it computed, and what does an annualized tracking error of 0.40% signify for an index fund investor? **(b) Model Answer:** **Concept (2 marks):** Tracking Error (TE) is the standard deviation of the difference between the returns of a portfolio (or fund) and the returns of its benchmark index. It measures how closely the fund "tracks" or replicates the index. A fund that perfectly holds all index constituents in exact proportion would have zero tracking error; in practice, small deviations arise due to cash holdings, transaction costs, rebalancing delays, dividend timing, and sampling. **Computation (3 marks):** For monthly returns $R_{p,1}, R_{p,2}, \ldots, R_{p,n}$ of the portfolio and $R_{b,1}, R_{b,2}, \ldots, R_{b,n}$ of the benchmark, the tracking error is computed as: $$ TE_{monthly} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[(R_{p,i} - R_{b,i}) - \overline{(R_{p,i} - R_{b,i})}\right]^2} $$ This is then annualized by multiplying by $\sqrt{12}$ for monthly data (or $\sqrt{252}$ for daily data): $$ TE_{annual} = TE_{monthly} \times \sqrt{12} $$ **Interpretation (2 marks):** An annualized tracking error of **0.40%** is considered **excellent** in the mutual fund industry. It signifies that the fund manager is replicating the benchmark with very high fidelity — typical well-managed Nifty 50 index funds have tracking errors in the 0.20%–0.50% range. A passive investor viewing a 0.40% TE can be confident that the fund is delivering essentially "pure" index exposure with minimal structural drag from cash, fees, or sampling. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)* > **(a)** **[7 Marks]** Critically evaluate the **disadvantages of Passive Investing**. Under what market conditions might an active strategy still be preferable? **(a) Model Answer:** While passive investing dominates on cost and consistency, it carries several important limitations: **1. No Alpha Generation:** Passive funds are designed to match the market, not beat it. In a strong bull market driven by mispricing (e.g., recovery rallies after 2020 COVID crash), skilled active managers can generate substantial outperformance. **2. No Downside Protection:** Passive funds hold all index stocks regardless of valuation. In the 2008 Global Financial Crisis, the Nifty 50 fell ≈60% — a passive investor had no defensive mechanism, whereas an active manager could move to cash or shift to defensive sectors (FMCG, Pharma). **3. Market-Cap Concentration Risk:** Cap-weighted indices (Nifty 50) give the largest weight to the largest companies. If a few mega-caps are overvalued (as with certain US tech stocks in 2021), passive inflows mechanically inflate their prices, creating a self-reinforcing "index bubble". An active manager can avoid this. **4. Behavioral Discomfort:** Watching the index drop 30% in a crash often causes retail investors to redeem at the bottom — locking in losses. An active manager provides communication, reassurance, and (sometimes) defensive positioning. **5. Limited Customization:** Investors who wish to exclude "sin stocks" (alcohol, tobacco, weapons) or align with ESG principles often need active funds, since standard indices include these companies. **6. Inefficiency in Niche Markets:** In small-cap, mid-cap, or thematic segments (e.g., defence, EV), active research can uncover undervalued companies before they enter major indices. **When Active is Preferable (3 marks):** Active management is justified when: (i) the market is structurally inefficient (small-cap, emerging sectors); (ii) the investor needs downside protection (retiree drawing income); (iii) specialized exposure (ESG, ethical, thematic) is required; or (iv) the active manager has a demonstrable 10+ year track record of risk-adjusted outperformance (high Sharpe + positive Alpha). > **(b)** **[7 Marks]** Differentiate between **Active and Passive Investing** across **eight parameters** of your choice. Justify why passive investing has gained significant market share globally over the last decade. **(b) Model Answer:** **Comparative Table (5 marks):** | # | Parameter | Active Investing | Passive Investing | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Objective | Beat benchmark (Alpha) | Match benchmark (Beta) | | 2 | Expense Ratio | 1.5%–2.5% | 0.03%–0.20% | | 3 | Portfolio Turnover | 50%–100%+ | < 10% | | 4 | Tax Efficiency | Lower (frequent trades) | Higher (few trades) | | 5 | Decision-Making | Discretionary (manager) | Rules-based (index) | | 6 | Risk Control | Active hedging possible | Limited to index moves | | 7 | Returns in Efficient Markets | Underperform (after costs) | Match market | | 8 | Minimum Capital | Often ₹5,000–₹1,00,000 | As low as ₹500 (SIP) | **Why Passive Has Gained Market Share (2 marks):** Over the last decade, passive funds have captured more than **50% of total U.S. equity fund assets** (a historic milestone reached in 2019) and continue to grow in India. Three forces drive this shift: 1. **Empirical Evidence:** The SPIVA scorecard consistently shows 85–90% of active funds underperform their benchmark over 15 years. 2. **Cost Sensitivity:** As financial literacy rises and fee-comparison websites proliferate, retail investors recognize the "cost drag" mathematics. 3. **Rise of ETFs & Robo-Advisors:** Low-cost ETFs and algorithmic portfolio construction have made passive strategies the default, not the alternative. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks in this topic:** > 1. **Confusing "Passive" with "No risk":** Passive funds carry full market (systematic) risk. Many students incorrectly write "passive investing is risk-free" — this is **worth zero marks** and signals conceptual misunderstanding. Always state: "Passive investing minimizes *firm-specific* risk, not *market* risk." > 2. **Arithmetic vs Geometric Mean:** When computing multi-year returns, KTU examiners specifically look for the geometric mean (CAGR) formula. Writing the arithmetic mean loses full marks. > 3. **No numerical example in the cost-drag question:** When asked to "substantiate" or "explain with example," students who give only theory without the $FV = PV(1+r)^n$ calculation lose 3–4 marks. Always include a numerical illustration. > 4. **Forgetting to annualize tracking error:** Multiplying monthly deviations without the $\sqrt{12}$ factor is a common error. > 5. **One-sided answer:** Writing only advantages OR only disadvantages of passive investing. The KTU pattern requires a **balanced comparison**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Density Bullets):** > - **Passive Investing** = Replicate a market index. **Active Investing** = Beat the market. > - **The two pillars of passive theory:** Efficient Market Hypothesis (EMH) and Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM). > - **Index Funds vs ETFs:** Both passive. Index funds bought at NAV; ETFs trade on exchange like shares. > - **Expense Ratio** is the single most important differentiator — even 1% per year compounds to ~25%–40% wealth erosion over 25–30 years. > - **Tracking Error** = $\sigma(R_p - R_b)$; annualized by $\sqrt{12}$ (monthly) or $\sqrt{252}$ (daily). Good TE < 0.50%. > - **Turnover** of passive funds is **< 10%** vs 50%–100% for active → **higher tax efficiency**. > - **SPIVA Scorecard** evidence: ≈ 85–90% of active large-cap funds underperform over 15 years. > - **Key Disadvantage of Passive:** No downside protection, no Alpha, market-cap bias, no customization. > - **Cost-Drag Formula to memorize:** $FV = PV \times (1 + R_{gross} - c)^n$ — always use net return. > - **Nifty 50 / S&P 500 / BSE Sensex** are the standard benchmark indices in India; Nasdaq-100 and MSCI World are global benchmarks. > - **Behavioral pitfall:** Passive investors often panic-sell in crashes; this is the biggest practical disadvantage of the strategy. > - **Hybrid approach (Core-Satellite):** Many investors use passive funds as the "core" and active funds as a "satellite" allocation to balance cost and Alpha potential.

topics.title: Concepts of Systematic Investment Plan and Systematic Withdrawal Plan
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concepts of Systematic Investment Plan and Systematic Withdrawal Plan

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Systematic Investment Plan (SIP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> A **Systematic Investment Plan (SIP)** is a disciplined, periodic investment vehicle offered primarily by **Mutual Funds** in India, wherein a fixed rupee amount is invested at fixed intervals (monthly/quarterly) into a chosen mutual fund scheme, thereby enabling investors to benefit from **Rupee Cost Averaging**, **Power of Compounding**, and long-term **Wealth Creation** without timing the market.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Digital Piggy Bank with Auto-Debit"

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you want to eat a slice of cake every day, but the cake shop changes its price daily. Some days it's ₹40, some days ₹100. If you fix a budget of **₹100 per day**, on cheap days you buy 2.5 slices worth of cake, and on expensive days you buy only 1 slice. Over a year, the *average* price you paid is much lower than the *peak* price. SIP works exactly the same way — you buy *more units* when the market is cheap (NAV low) and *fewer units* when the market is expensive (NAV high), without ever trying to predict the market.

### Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **SIP** | Systematic Investment Plan |
| **NAV** | Net Asset Value (per unit price of a mutual fund) |
| **Rupee Cost Averaging** | Buying more units when price is low, fewer when price is high |
| **Compounding** | Earning returns on previously earned returns |
| **CAGR** | Compound Annual Growth Rate |
| **XIRR** | Extended Internal Rate of Return (used for irregular cash flows) |
| **Step-up SIP** | SIP where the installment increases by a fixed % every year |

---

## 1.2 Systematic Withdrawal Plan (SWP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> A **Systematic Withdrawal Plan (SWP)** is the *mirror image* of SIP. It allows an investor to withdraw a **fixed amount at regular intervals** (monthly/quarterly) from the corpus already accumulated in a mutual fund scheme. SWP is primarily used for generating a **regular post-retirement income stream**, meeting periodic liabilities, or de-risking an equity portfolio gradually.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Salary From Your Own Money Tree"

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you planted a money tree 20 years ago. The tree now has a huge canopy of leaves (your accumulated corpus). You don't want to chop the whole tree (redeem everything and pay huge tax at once). Instead, you pluck a fixed basket of leaves (₹50,000) every month. As long as the tree keeps growing new leaves (returns > withdrawal rate), the tree stays healthy. SWP is exactly this — *systematic plucking* of returns from a pre-built corpus.

### Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **SWP** | Systematic Withdrawal Plan |
| **Corpus** | Total accumulated fund value at the time SWP starts |
| **Withdrawal Rate** | Fixed rupee amount or % of corpus withdrawn per period |
| **Capital Appreciation** | Growth in the value of the remaining units |
| **Sustainable Withdrawal** | Withdrawal rate that preserves the corpus indefinitely |
| **De-risking** | Gradually shifting from equity to debt funds via SWP |
| **Annuity Alternative** | SWP is often pitched as a flexible alternative to annuity products |

---

## 1.3 Why These Two Concepts Matter in KTU Financial Markets

> [!TIP]
> Both SIP and SWP are **core retail-investor tools** in the Indian mutual fund ecosystem. The **SEBI (Securities and Exchange Board of India)** regulates mutual fund schemes that offer these facilities. For KTU's *Introduction to Financial Markets*, understanding SIP and SWP equips you to:
> - Comprehend how retail money flows into capital markets.
> - Calculate realistic **wealth accumulation** and **retirement corpus** projections.
> - Differentiate between *lump-sum* and *systematic* approaches.
> - Appreciate the role of mutual funds as financial intermediaries.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 How SIP Works — The Operational Logic

> [!NOTE]
> SIP is a simple yet mathematically powerful concept. Let us break it down into structured steps.

### Step 1: Investor Commits a Fixed Amount
- The investor authorizes the **Asset Management Company (AMC)** to debit a fixed amount (e.g., ₹5,000) from the bank account on a chosen date each month.

### Step 2: AMC Buys Units at the Day's NAV
- The debited amount is divided by the **NAV of that day**:
$$\text{Units Allocated} = \frac{\text{Installment Amount}}{\text{NAV on that day}}$$

### Step 3: Accumulation Over Time
- Each installment adds new units to the investor's folio. Over the investment horizon, the investor holds units purchased at *many different NAVs*.

### Step 4: Rupee Cost Averaging
- Because fixed ₹ amount buys more units in low-NAV months and fewer units in high-NAV months, the **average cost per unit** automatically falls below the **average NAV**.

$$\text{Average Cost per Unit} = \frac{\text{Total Amount Invested}}{\text{Total Units Accumulated}} < \overline{\text{NAV}}$$

### Step 5: Power of Compounding
- The accumulated units grow in value as the NAV of the underlying fund appreciates. Returns earned in early years generate further returns in later years.

$$\text{Wealth} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \text{Installment}_i \times \prod_{j=i}^{n}(1+r_j) \right)$$

where $r_j$ is the return generated in period $j$.

---

## 2.2 How SWP Works — The Operational Logic

### Step 1: Investor Already Holds a Mutual Fund Corpus
- The investor has accumulated units (e.g., 50,000 units of an equity fund).

### Step 2: Fixed Redemption Each Period
- On a chosen date each month, the AMC redeems units equal to:
$$\text{Units Redeemed} = \frac{\text{Withdrawal Amount}}{\text{NAV on that day}}$$

### Step 3: Capital Depletion vs. Capital Appreciation
- If the fund's NAV grows **faster** than the withdrawal rate, the corpus **grows** even after withdrawals.
- If the fund's NAV grows **slower** than the withdrawal rate, the corpus **depletes** over time.

### Step 4: Tax-Efficient Withdrawals
- Only the **capital gains portion** of each redemption is taxed, not the entire withdrawal — a major advantage over interest income from FDs.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is the single most important revision tool for KTU exams on this topic.** All marks-oriented formulas are listed here.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Future Value of SIP (Ordinary Annuity)** | $FV = P \times \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ | $P$ = SIP installment, $i$ = periodic rate, $n$ = number of installments | ₹ |
| 2 | **Future Value of SIP (Annuity Due)** | $FV = P \times \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i} \times (1+i)$ | When SIP is invested at the *start* of each period | ₹ |
| 3 | **Total Invested in SIP** | $\text{Total Invested} = P \times n$ | Sum of all installments | ₹ |
| 4 | **Wealth Gained in SIP** | $\text{Wealth Gained} = FV - (P \times n)$ | Final value minus total invested | ₹ |
| 5 | **Average Cost per Unit (SIP)** | $\overline{C} = \dfrac{\text{Total Invested}}{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{P}{\text{NAV}_t}}$ | Demonstrates Rupee Cost Averaging | ₹/unit |
| 6 | **Number of Units Allocated per Installment** | $U_t = \dfrac{P}{\text{NAV}_t}$ | Units bought in installment $t$ | units |
| 7 | **SWP Withdrawal Units** | $U_{w,t} = \dfrac{W}{\text{NAV}_t}$ | Units redeemed per period | units |
| 8 | **SWP Remaining Corpus** | $C_t = C_{t-1} \times (1+g) - W$ | $g$ = fund growth rate, $W$ = withdrawal | ₹ |
| 9 | **Sustainable Withdrawal Rate (SWR)** | $\text{SWR} \approx i - g$ | Maximum withdrawal rate to preserve corpus forever | % |
| 10 | **Target Corpus via SIP** | $P = \dfrac{\text{Target} \times i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Required SIP to reach a goal | ₹ |
| 11 | **Step-up SIP FV** | $FV = \sum_{k=0}^{m-1} P(1+s)^k \times \dfrac{(1+i)^{n-kT} - 1}{i}$ | $s$ = annual step-up %, $T$ = installments/yr | ₹ |
| 12 | **Required Number of SIPs for a Goal** | $n = \dfrac{\ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{\text{Target} \times i}{P}\right)}{\ln(1+i)}$ | Solving FV formula for $n$ | periods |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Note on Boundary Conditions:**
> - For Formula 1, when $i \to 0$, the formula degenerates to $FV = P \times n$ (no growth).
> - For Formula 9, if $g \geq i$, corpus is **sustainable in perpetuity**; if $g < i$, corpus will eventually be exhausted.
> - The **periodic rate** $i$ must be consistent with the installment frequency (e.g., for 12% p.a. compounded monthly, $i = 0.12/12 = 0.01$).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Financial Markets

| Application | SIP Role | SWP Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Retirement Planning** | Build a retirement corpus over 25–30 years | Generate post-retirement monthly income |
| **Child Education** | Accumulate ₹1 Cr+ over 15 years | Pay annual tuition fees systematically |
| **Emergency Fund Building** | Build a 6-month expense buffer | Withdraw during income disruption |
| **Goal-Based Investing** | House, car, foreign vacation | Pay EMIs / installments of goals |
| **Tax Planning** | ELSS-SIP gives 80C benefit | Withdrawals taxed only on gains |
| **Market Timing Removal** | Removes emotional decision-making | Removes need to time exits |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Future Value of a SIP (Ordinary Annuity)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** An investor contributes $P$ rupees at the **end** of every month for $n$ months. The expected rate of return per month is $i$. What is the future value at the end of month $n$?

### Step 1: List Each Installment's Future Value

| Installment Number | Amount Invested | Periods of Compounding Remaining | Future Value at End of Month $n$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P$ | $n-1$ | $P(1+i)^{n-1}$ |
| 2 | $P$ | $n-2$ | $P(1+i)^{n-2}$ |
| 3 | $P$ | $n-3$ | $P(1+i)^{n-3}$ |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| $n-1$ | $P$ | $1$ | $P(1+i)^{1}$ |
| $n$ | $P$ | $0$ | $P(1+i)^{0} = P$ |

### Step 2: Sum the Series

The total future value is the sum of all rows in the last column:

$$FV = P(1+i)^{n-1} + P(1+i)^{n-2} + \cdots + P(1+i)^{1} + P(1+i)^{0}$$

### Step 3: Recognize the Geometric Progression

This is a **geometric progression (G.P.)** with:
- First term $a = P(1+i)^{n-1}$  *(largest term, if $i > 0$)*
- Common ratio $r = \dfrac{1}{1+i}$
- Number of terms $= n$

### Step 4: Apply the G.P. Sum Formula

The sum of a G.P. is:

$$S_n = a \times \frac{1 - r^n}{1 - r}$$

Substituting $a$ and $r$:

$$FV = P(1+i)^{n-1} \times \frac{1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{1+i}\right)^n}{1 - \dfrac{1}{1+i}}$$

### Step 5: Simplify the Denominator

$$1 - \frac{1}{1+i} = \frac{(1+i) - 1}{1+i} = \frac{i}{1+i}$$

So:

$$FV = P(1+i)^{n-1} \times \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{\dfrac{i}{1+i}}$$

### Step 6: Combine Powers of $(1+i)$

$$FV = P(1+i)^{n-1} \times \frac{1+i}{i} \times \left[1 - (1+i)^{-n}\right]$$

$$FV = P(1+i)^{n} \times \frac{1}{i} \times \left[1 - (1+i)^{-n}\right]$$

$$FV = P \times \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$$

### Step 7: Final Boxed Result

$$\boxed{FV_{\text{SIP}} = P \times \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}}$$

---

## 3.2 Solved Numerical Example — SIP Wealth Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question:** An engineer invests **₹10,000 per month** in an equity-linked SIP for **15 years**. The fund has historically delivered **12% CAGR**. Calculate:
> (a) Total amount invested
> (b) Future Value at the end of 15 years
> (c) Wealth gained

### Step 1: Identify the Parameters

- $P = 10{,}000$ ₹
- Annual rate $= 12\% = 0.12$
- Monthly rate $i = \dfrac{0.12}{12} = 0.01$
- Years $= 15$
- Number of installments $n = 15 \times 12 = 180$

### Step 2: Part (a) — Total Invested

$$\text{Total Invested} = P \times n = 10{,}000 \times 180 = 18{,}00{,}000$$

**Total Invested = ₹18,00,000 (i.e., ₹18 Lakhs)**

### Step 3: Part (b) — Future Value

Apply the SIP formula:

$$FV = 10{,}000 \times \frac{(1.01)^{180} - 1}{0.01}$$

Compute $(1.01)^{180}$:

$$\ln(1.01) = 0.00995033$$

$$180 \times 0.00995033 = 1.79106$$

$$e^{1.79106} \approx 5.9918$$

So:

$$FV = 10{,}000 \times \frac{5.9918 - 1}{0.01} = 10{,}000 \times \frac{4.9918}{0.01}$$

$$FV = 10{,}000 \times 499.18$$

**Future Value ≈ ₹49,91,800 (i.e., ₹49.92 Lakhs)**

### Step 4: Part (c) — Wealth Gained

$$\text{Wealth Gained} = FV - \text{Total Invested} = 49{,}91{,}800 - 18{,}00{,}000$$

**Wealth Gained ≈ ₹31,91,800 (i.e., ₹31.92 Lakhs)**

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** The investor put in only ₹18 Lakhs of his own money but ended up with nearly ₹50 Lakhs — that's the **power of compounding** at work. The *gains* (₹31.92 L) are **1.77 times** the *original investment* (₹18 L).

---

## 3.3 Solved Numerical Example — Required SIP for a Goal

> [!NOTE]
> **Question:** A couple wants to accumulate **₹1 Crore** in 20 years for their child's higher education. The expected return is **10% CAGR**. What monthly SIP is required?

### Step 1: Parameters

- Target $T = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000$
- $i = \dfrac{0.10}{12} = 0.008333$
- $n = 20 \times 12 = 240$

### Step 2: Rearrange the Formula

From $FV = P \times \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$, solve for $P$:

$$P = \frac{T \times i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$

### Step 3: Compute $(1.008333)^{240}$

$$240 \times \ln(1.008333) = 240 \times 0.008299 = 1.9917$$

$$e^{1.9917} \approx 7.328$$

### Step 4: Substitute

$$P = \frac{1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.008333}{7.328 - 1} = \frac{83{,}333}{6.328}$$

$$P \approx 13{,}168$$

**Required SIP ≈ ₹13,168 per month**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — SIP vs SWP Comparison

| Dimension | SIP (Investment) | SWP (Withdrawal) |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction of Cash Flow** | Investor → Fund | Fund → Investor |
| **Phase of Life** | Wealth accumulation phase | Wealth distribution phase |
| **Primary Goal** | Build corpus | Generate regular income |
| **Impact on Corpus** | Increases | Decreases (in nominal units redeemed) |
| **Tax Treatment** | No tax on investment | Tax on capital gains only |
| **Market Sensitivity** | Benefits from market falls (more units) | Benefits from market rises (delays depletion) |
| **Rupee Cost Averaging** | Yes (on buying) | Reverse effect (on selling) |
| **Discipline** | Forces regular saving | Prevents impulsive lump-sum withdrawals |
| **Ideal For** | Salaried individuals, young investors | Retirees, conservative investors |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | Very High | High |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students write that SWP *guarantees* a fixed corpus. This is FALSE. The corpus can shrink to zero if the fund's returns are lower than the withdrawal rate over a long period.

---

## 3.5 Solved Numerical Example — SWP Corpus Sustainability

> [!NOTE]
> **Question:** A retiree has a corpus of **₹1 Crore** in an equity fund. He starts an SWP of **₹50,000 per month**. The fund returns **8% CAGR**. Will the corpus last at least 20 years?

### Step 1: Convert to Monthly Rates

- Monthly withdrawal $W = 50{,}000$
- Monthly growth $g = \dfrac{0.08}{12} \approx 0.006667$
- Initial corpus $C_0 = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000$

### Step 2: Iterative Formula

$$C_t = C_{t-1} \times (1+g) - W$$

### Step 3: After Year 1 (12 months)

Approximate end-of-year-1 corpus:

$$C_{12} \approx 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times (1.006667)^{12} - (50{,}000 \times 12)$$

$$(1.006667)^{12} \approx 1.0830$$

$$C_{12} \approx 1{,}08{,}30{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}02{,}30{,}000$$

### Step 4: Pattern Recognition

Since $g$ (8% p.a.) > effective annual withdrawal rate (₹6 L / ₹1 Cr = 6%), the corpus will *likely* grow. We can approximate remaining corpus after 20 years using the future value of a present sum minus future value of withdrawals.

$$C_{240} = C_0 \times (1+g)^{240} - W \times \frac{(1+g)^{240} - 1}{g}$$

Compute $(1.006667)^{240}$:

$$240 \times \ln(1.006667) = 240 \times 0.006645 = 1.5947$$

$$e^{1.5947} \approx 4.926$$

Then:

$$C_{240} = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 4.926 - 50{,}000 \times \frac{4.926 - 1}{0.006667}$$

$$C_{240} = 4{,}92{,}60{,}000 - 50{,}000 \times 588.94$$

$$C_{240} = 4{,}92{,}60{,}000 - 2{,}94{,}47{,}000$$

$$C_{240} \approx 1{,}98{,}13{,}000$$

**Corpus after 20 years ≈ ₹1.98 Crore**

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** With 8% returns and ₹50,000/month withdrawal, the corpus not only survives 20 years but **almost doubles** — the withdrawals are effectively being paid out of the returns, leaving the principal intact in real terms.

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — SIP Operational Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Investor Authorizes SIP Mandate] --> B[Bank Auto-Debit on SIP Date]
    B --> C[Amount Credited to AMC]
    C --> D[AMC Allocates Units at Day NAV]
    D --> E[Units Added to Investor Folio]
    E --> F{Has SIP Tenure Ended?}
    F -- No --> B
    F -- Yes --> G[Investor Holds Accumulated Units]
    G --> H[NAV Appreciates Over Time]
    H --> I[Realized Wealth = Units x Current NAV]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flowchart loops every month between B→C→D→E until the SIP tenure ends. The final wealth depends on (a) total units accumulated and (b) the NAV at the time of redemption.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — SWP Operational Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Investor Holds Existing Mutual Fund Units] --> Q[SWP Mandate Registered with AMC]
    Q --> R[On Each SWP Date, AMC Redeems Units]
    R --> S[Withdrawal Amount Credited to Bank Account]
    S --> T{Fund NAV vs Withdrawal Rate}
    T -- NAV Growth > Withdrawal --> U[Corpus Grows Over Time]
    T -- NAV Growth < Withdrawal --> V[Corpus Depletes Over Time]
    T -- NAV Growth = Withdrawal --> W[Corpus Stays Constant]
    U --> X[Investor Receives Regular Income]
    V --> X
    W --> X
```
