# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are model Part A questions, each carrying **3 marks** as per KTU 2024 scheme. Each comes with a precise model answer.

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q: Define Systematic Investment Plan (SIP). List any two advantages of SIP over lump-sum investment.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Systematic Investment Plan (SIP)** is a method of investing a **fixed amount at regular intervals** (typically monthly) into a mutual fund scheme, as opposed to investing a one-time lump sum.

**Two advantages over lump-sum investment:**

1. **Rupee Cost Averaging:** Since a fixed ₹ amount buys more units when NAV is low and fewer when NAV is high, the average cost per unit automatically falls over time — reducing the risk of entering the market at a peak.

2. **Disciplined Investing & Affordability:** SIP encourages financial discipline by automating savings. It also allows investors with limited surplus to start with as little as ₹500/month, making wealth creation accessible to a wider population.

*(Optional 3rd point for extra credit: Power of Compounding — reinvested returns generate further returns over a long horizon.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: What is a Systematic Withdrawal Plan (SWP)? Why is it considered a tax-efficient method of generating regular income?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Systematic Withdrawal Plan (SWP)** is a facility offered by mutual funds that allows an investor to **withdraw a fixed amount at regular intervals** from an already-existing mutual fund investment.

**Tax Efficiency Reason:**

In SWP, only the **capital gains portion** of each redemption is subject to tax, not the entire withdrawal amount. The original capital (the investor's principal) is **not taxed** because it has already been invested with after-tax money. Moreover:
- If held for **> 12 months** (equity) or **> 24 months** (non-equity), gains are taxed as **Long-Term Capital Gains (LTCG)**, often at concessional rates.
- This makes SWP significantly more tax-efficient than receiving **interest** from Fixed Deposits, where the *entire* interest is added to taxable income each year.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B Module questions carry **14 marks** and feature an **internal choice**. We provide **Question A** and **Question B** as alternative options.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of **Systematic Investment Plan (SIP)** in detail. Discuss **Rupee Cost Averaging** and the **Power of Compounding** with the help of suitable illustrations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An investor starts a SIP of **₹15,000 per month** in an equity fund. The expected CAGR is **11%**. After **18 years**, what is the projected corpus? Also calculate the **total amount invested** and the **wealth gained**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Definition:** A SIP is a pre-committed, periodic investment of a fixed amount in a mutual fund scheme at fixed intervals (commonly monthly).

**Rupee Cost Averaging (RCA):**
- Suppose the NAV of a fund fluctuates as follows for 6 months: ₹100, ₹80, ₹90, ₹70, ₹110, ₹95.
- A fixed SIP of ₹1,000/month buys: 10, 12.5, 11.11, 14.29, 9.09, 10.53 units respectively.
- Total invested: ₹6,000. Total units: 67.52.
- **Average cost per unit** = ₹6,000 ÷ 67.52 = **₹88.86**.
- **Average NAV** = (100+80+90+70+110+95)/6 = **₹90.83**.
- The investor's average cost (₹88.86) is **less than** the average market price (₹90.83) — this is the benefit of RCA.

**Power of Compounding:**
- Returns earned in early years are reinvested, generating further returns in subsequent years.
- A small monthly SIP of ₹10,000 for 20 years at 12% CAGR grows to ~₹99.9 Lakhs, even though the investor only contributed ₹24 Lakhs. The **gains (₹75.9 L) are 3.16× the principal**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Given:**
- $P = 15{,}000$ ₹
- Annual rate $= 11\% \Rightarrow$ Monthly rate $i = 0.11/12 = 0.009167$
- Tenure $= 18$ years $\Rightarrow n = 18 \times 12 = 216$ months

**Step 1: Total Invested**

$$\text{Total Invested} = P \times n = 15{,}000 \times 216 = 32{,}40{,}000$$

**Total Invested = ₹32,40,000**

**Step 2: Future Value**

$$FV = 15{,}000 \times \frac{(1 + 0.009167)^{216} - 1}{0.009167}$$

Compute $(1.009167)^{216}$:

$$216 \times \ln(1.009167) = 216 \times 0.009125 = 1.9710$$

$$e^{1.9710} \approx 7.177$$

Then:

$$FV = 15{,}000 \times \frac{7.177 - 1}{0.009167} = 15{,}000 \times \frac{6.177}{0.009167}$$

$$FV = 15{,}000 \times 673.88$$

$$FV \approx 1{,}01{,}08{,}200$$

**Future Value ≈ ₹1,01,08,200 (i.e., ~₹1.01 Crore)**

**Step 3: Wealth Gained**

$$\text{Wealth Gained} = 1{,}01{,}08{,}200 - 32{,}40{,}000 = 68{,}68{,}200$$

**Wealth Gained ≈ ₹68,68,200 (i.e., ~₹68.68 Lakhs)**

**Summary Table:**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Monthly SIP | ₹15,000 |
| Tenure | 18 years |
| Expected CAGR | 11% |
| **Total Invested** | **₹32,40,000** |
| **Future Value** | **₹1,01,08,200** |
| **Wealth Gained** | **₹68,68,200** |
| **Multiplier** | **3.12×** |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q: (a)** Discuss the concept of **Systematic Withdrawal Plan (SWP)**. Compare it with traditional annuity products. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A retiree has a corpus of **₹75,00,000** in a balanced advantage fund. He activates an SWP of **₹40,000 per month**. The fund delivers **9% CAGR** annually. Estimate the **corpus value after 15 years**. Will the corpus sustain? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Concept of SWP:**

A **Systematic Withdrawal Plan (SWP)** is a facility wherein an investor redeems a fixed amount from a mutual fund at regular intervals. The redemption amount is credited directly to the investor's bank account. SWP is used to convert a lump-sum investment into a *predictable income stream*.

**Comparison with Annuity Products:**

| Feature | SWP | Annuity |
|---|---|---|
| **Flexibility** | Can be stopped, modified, or restarted anytime | Usually irrevocable once purchased |
| **Returns on Remaining Corpus** | Continues to earn market-linked returns | Fixed; no upside participation |
| **Inheritance** | Remaining corpus passes to nominees | No residual value (in most plans) |
| **Tax Treatment** | Only capital gains taxed; principal tax-free | Entire annuity income is taxable |
| **Inflation Protection** | Possible if fund returns > inflation | Often erodes over time |
| **Cost** | Low expense ratio (~1-1.5%) | High upfront charges, commission-laden |
| **Liquidity** | High — partial/full exit allowed | Low — locked in for life |
| **Regulator** | SEBI (mutual funds) | IRDAI (insurance) |

> **Conclusion:** SWP is generally considered a **more flexible and tax-efficient** alternative to traditional annuities, especially for investors with a long horizon and moderate risk tolerance.

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Given:**
- Initial corpus $C_0 = 75{,}00{,}000$
- Monthly withdrawal $W = 40{,}000$
- Annual growth $= 9\% \Rightarrow$ Monthly growth $g = 0.09/12 = 0.0075$
- Tenure $= 15$ years $\Rightarrow$ Periods $n = 180$

**Step 1: Future Value of Initial Corpus**

$$FV_{\text{principal}} = C_0 \times (1+g)^n = 75{,}00{,}000 \times (1.0075)^{180}$$

Compute $(1.0075)^{180}$:

$$180 \times \ln(1.0075) = 180 \times 0.007472 = 1.3450$$

$$e^{1.3450} \approx 3.838$$

$$FV_{\text{principal}} = 75{,}00{,}000 \times 3.838 = 2{,}87{,}85{,}000$$

**Step 2: Future Value of Withdrawals (Annuity Outflow)**

$$FV_{\text{withdrawals}} = W \times \frac{(1+g)^n - 1}{g} = 40{,}000 \times \frac{3.838 - 1}{0.0075}$$

$$FV_{\text{withdrawals}} = 40{,}000 \times \frac{2.838}{0.0075} = 40{,}000 \times 378.40$$

$$FV_{\text{withdrawals}} = 1{,}51{,}36{,}000$$

**Step 3: Remaining Corpus after 15 Years**

$$C_{180} = FV_{\text{principal}} - FV_{\text{withdrawals}}$$

$$C_{180} = 2{,}87{,}85{,}000 - 1{,}51{,}36{,}000 = 1{,}36{,}49{,}000$$

**Remaining Corpus ≈ ₹1,36,49,000 (i.e., ~₹1.36 Crore)**

**Step 4: Sustainability Analysis**

- Effective annual withdrawal rate = (₹40,000 × 12) / ₹75,00,000 = ₹4,80,000 / ₹75,00,000 = **6.4% per year**.
- Fund growth rate = **9% per year**.
- Since **growth > withdrawal rate**, the corpus is **growing**, not depleting. The retiree's income is essentially funded by the *returns* on the corpus, with the principal being preserved and even appreciated.

> **Conclusion:** Yes, the corpus **sustains comfortably** and in fact **grows by ~82%** over 15 years under the given assumptions.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Wrong rate conversion:** Students often use the *annual* rate directly in the monthly SIP formula. Always convert: $i_{\text{monthly}} = r_{\text{annual}} / 12$.
>
> 2. **Confusing SIP FV with Total Invested:** The future value formula gives the *projected corpus*, not the money you put in. The difference is the *wealth gained* — this distinction is often tested.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the "Annuity Due" variant:** If a question says SIP is invested at the *start* of the month (rare but possible), students must multiply by $(1+i)$. Defaulting to ordinary annuity is the usual KTU expectation.
>
> 4. **Writing definitions without examples:** For 7-mark questions, the *definition alone* fetches only 2-3 marks. Students must illustrate with **numerical examples**, **diagrams**, or **tables** to score full marks.
>
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** Always state whether your final answer is in **rupees, lakhs, or crores**.
>
> 6. **SWP sustainability analysis missing:** When a SWP numerical is asked, the examiner expects you to *comment* on whether the corpus survives the tenure — not just compute the final value.
>
> 7. **Lumping SIP and SWP together:** They are *opposite* cash flows. Examiners reward students who clearly distinguish between them with arrows, tables, or a contrast chart.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print-Ready Summary**

- **SIP = Systematic Investment Plan** = fixed-amount, periodic *investments* into a mutual fund.
- **SWP = Systematic Withdrawal Plan** = fixed-amount, periodic *withdrawals* from a mutual fund.
- SIP is used during the **wealth accumulation phase**; SWP during the **wealth distribution phase**.
- **Rupee Cost Averaging** is a free "bonus" feature of SIP — fixed ₹ buys more units in low-NAV months.
- **Power of Compounding** turns a small SIP into a large corpus over 15-20 years — the curve is exponential, not linear.
- **Core SIP Future Value formula:** $FV = P \times \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ *(ordinary annuity, end-of-period investment)*.
- **Total Invested in SIP** $= P \times n$. **Wealth Gained** $= FV - P \times n$.
- **Required SIP for a goal:** $P = \dfrac{T \times i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$.
- **SWP sustainability rule:** Corpus grows if fund return > effective withdrawal rate; depletes otherwise.
- **SWP formula for remaining corpus:** $C_t = C_{t-1} \times (1+g) - W$.
- **Tax edge of SWP:** Only capital gains component is taxable; principal is not.
- **Step-up SIP** increases the installment by a fixed % annually — accelerates corpus growth.
- **SIP is a market-timing eliminator** — it removes emotional decisions.
- **SWP is a flexible alternative to annuity** — more liquidity, less tax, more upside.
- **Regulator:** Both SIP and SWP are offered by SEBI-regulated mutual funds (AMCs).
- **KTU favourite numericals:** (a) Compute FV of SIP, (b) Compute required SIP for a target, (c) Compute SWP sustainability.
- **Common formula confusion:** CAGR ≈ $r$ (annual); $i$ (monthly) = $r/12$; $n$ = years × 12.
- **Common conceptual confusion:** SWP does *not* guarantee fixed corpus — it depends on market returns.
- **Memorize the comparison table** of SIP vs SWP — it's a guaranteed Part A/B question almost every exam cycle.
- **Always show units** (₹, lakhs, crores) in numerical answers for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bonds: Bond Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Bonds: Bond Characteristics # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Bond > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > A **Bond** is a fixed-income debt security instrument issued by corporations, governments, or municipal bodies to raise capital from investors. It represents a formal contractual loan agreement where the issuer is obligated to pay the bondholder (lender) predetermined interest (called the **coupon**) at fixed intervals and repay the principal (**face value**) at a specified future date known as the **maturity date**. In the KTU 2024 framework, a bond is classified as a **debt instrument** rather than equity, meaning the bondholder is a creditor (not an owner) of the issuing entity. This distinction is fundamental for understanding the risk-return profile, taxation, and bankruptcy priority of bonds versus stocks. ## 1.2 Core Bond Characteristics — The Building Blocks Every bond in the financial markets can be uniquely identified by a set of standardized characteristics. The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes mastery of the following eight parameters: | # | Characteristic | One-Line Meaning | |---|----------------|------------------| | 1 | **Face Value (Par Value)** | The amount repaid at maturity — typically **₹1,000** or **$1,000** | | 2 | **Coupon Rate** | The fixed annual interest rate stated on the bond | | 3 | **Coupon Payment** | The actual periodic interest paid = Face Value × Coupon Rate | | 4 | **Maturity Date / Tenure** | The date on which the principal is repaid | | 5 | **Issue Price** | The price at which the bond is originally sold | | 6 | **Market Price** | The current trading price in the secondary market | | 7 | **Yield to Maturity (YTM)** | The total return if the bond is held until maturity | | 8 | **Credit Rating** | An assessment of the issuer's default risk | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "IOU with a Schedule" Analogy** > Imagine you lend your friend **₹10,000** to start a small business. You both agree on a handwritten IOU note that says: *"I will pay you ₹500 every 6 months for the next 5 years, and return the full ₹10,000 at the end of the 5th year."* > > This IOU is essentially a **bond**. Your friend is the **issuer** (the borrower), you are the **bondholder** (the lender), ₹10,000 is the **face value**, ₹500 every 6 months is the **coupon payment**, 5 years is the **maturity period**, and the printed note itself is the **bond certificate**. > > The only difference? A real bond is legally enforceable, tradable on stock exchanges, and rated by agencies like CRISIL or S&P. This analogy makes bond characteristics immediately accessible to first-time learners, which aligns with the **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** philosophy of the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned curriculum. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Bond Characteristic ### 2.1.1 Face Value (Par Value) - The **nominal value** printed on the bond certificate. - Used to calculate coupon payments and the final principal repayment. - In India, government bonds (G-Secs) are typically issued with a face value of **₹100**. - Corporate bonds in India are usually issued with a face value of **₹1,000** or **₹1 lakh** for wholesale debt. ### 2.1.2 Coupon Rate - The **stated interest rate** on the bond, fixed at the time of issue. - **Fixed-rate bonds** have a constant coupon rate throughout the bond's life. - **Floating-rate bonds (FRBs)** have coupons linked to a benchmark like MIBOR or repo rate. - **Zero-coupon bonds** pay no periodic interest — they are issued at a discount and redeemed at par. ### 2.1.3 Coupon Payment Frequency - Can be **annual, semi-annual, quarterly, or monthly**. - In India and the US, **semi-annual** coupon payments are most common. - More frequent payments → higher effective annual yield. ### 2.1.4 Maturity Date (Tenure) - **Short-term bonds:** Maturity ≤ 3 years (e.g., Treasury Bills). - **Medium-term notes (MTNs):** 3 to 10 years. - **Long-term bonds:** > 10 years (e.g., 30-year Government of India bonds). ### 2.1.5 Issue Price vs Market Price - **Issue Price:** The price at which the bond is first sold (may be at par, discount, or premium). - **Market Price:** The current trading price in the secondary market — fluctuates with interest rate changes. - If **Market Price > Face Value** → Bond trades at a **PREMIUM**. - If **Market Price < Face Value** → Bond trades at a **DISCOUNT**. - If **Market Price = Face Value** → Bond trades at **PAR**. ### 2.1.6 Yield to Maturity (YTM) - The **internal rate of return (IRR)** an investor earns if the bond is held until maturity and all coupons are reinvested at the same rate. - YTM is the single most important metric for bond valuation in the KTU 2024 syllabus. ### 2.1.7 Current Yield - A simpler measure: annual coupon income divided by the current market price. ### 2.1.8 Credit Rating - Issued by agencies: **CRISIL, ICRA, CARE (India)** and **Moody's, S&P, Fitch (Global)**. - AAA (highest safety) → D (default). - Investment grade: **AAA, AA, A, BBB**. - Junk/Speculative grade: **BB and below**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** The inverse relationship between bond prices and interest rates is one of the most tested concepts. When market interest rates **rise**, existing bond prices **fall**, and vice versa. This is because the fixed coupon of an old bond becomes less attractive when new bonds offer higher rates. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates every formula tested in KTU 2024 ESE examinations for bond characteristics. | # | Concept | Formula | Notation | |---|---------|---------|----------| | 1 | Coupon Payment | $C = F \times c$ | $F$ = Face Value, $c$ = Coupon Rate | | 2 | Current Yield | $CY = \dfrac{C}{P_0}$ | $P_0$ = Current Market Price | | 3 | Bond Price (General) | $P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C}{(1+r)^t} + \dfrac{F}{(1+r)^n}$ | $r$ = YTM, $n$ = periods to maturity | | 4 | Perpetual Bond Price | $P_0 = \dfrac{C}{r}$ | No maturity, infinite cash flows | | 5 | Zero-Coupon Bond Price | $P_0 = \dfrac{F}{(1+r)^n}$ | Only one cash flow at maturity | | 6 | YTM Approximation | $YTM \approx \dfrac{C + \dfrac{F - P_0}{n}}{\dfrac{F + P_0}{2}}$ | Useful when YTM cannot be solved directly | | 7 | Premium/Discount Indicator | If $c > r$ → Premium; If $c < r$ → Discount; If $c = r$ → Par | Compare coupon rate vs YTM | | 8 | Holding Period Return | $HPR = \dfrac{C + (P_{end} - P_{start})}{P_{start}}$ | Realized return over holding period | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Economic Utility Understanding bond characteristics is critical not only in finance but also in: - **Infrastructure Project Financing:** Government bonds fund highways, ports, metro rail. - **Corporate Treasury Management:** Companies issue bonds to finance R&D and capex. - **Pension Fund Management:** Retirees depend on predictable coupon income. - **Central Bank Monetary Policy:** Open Market Operations (OMO) use government bonds to control money supply. - **Risk Management:** Engineers in project finance need bond math for DCF valuation and capital budgeting. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Bond Pricing Equation The bond pricing formula is derived from the **time value of money principle**. A bond promises two types of future cash flows: 1. Periodic coupon payments $C$ (an annuity). 2. The face value repayment $F$ at maturity (a single lump sum). ### Step 1 — Write the Cash Flow Schedule At the end of period $t$, the investor receives $C$ if $t < n$, and $C + F$ if $t = n$. ### Step 2 — Discount Each Future Cash Flow to Present Value Each future cash flow is worth less today due to the discount rate $r$ (which equals YTM under no-arbitrage conditions). The present value of cash flow at time $t$ is: $$PV_t = \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$$ ### Step 3 — Sum All Discounted Cash Flows Because the bond price equals the present value of all future cash flows, we sum across all $n$ periods: $$P_0 = \dfrac{C}{(1+r)^1} + \dfrac{C}{(1+r)^2} + \dots + \dfrac{C}{(1+r)^n} + \dfrac{F}{(1+r)^n}$$ ### Step 4 — Group the Terms into a Compact Expression We can separate the coupon annuity from the principal repayment: $$P_0 = \underbrace{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C}{(1+r)^t}}_{\text{PV of Coupons}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{F}{(1+r)^n}}_{\text{PV of Face Value}}$$ ### Step 5 — Closed-Form (Present Value of Annuity Factor) Using the geometric series formula $\sum_{t=1}^{n} x^t = \dfrac{x(1-x^n)}{1-x}$, we substitute $x = \dfrac{1}{1+r}$ to obtain: $$P_0 = C \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r} + F \cdot (1+r)^{-n}$$ This is the **KTU-ready closed-form bond pricing equation**. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Step-by-Step > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Exam Style Problem** > A bond has a face value of **₹1,000**, an annual coupon rate of **10%**, a maturity of **3 years**, and the current market yield (YTM) is **12%**. Coupons are paid annually. Calculate the bond price. ### Given Data - $F = 1{,}000$ - $c = 10\% \Rightarrow C = 1{,}000 \times 0.10 = 100$ - $n = 3$ years - $r = 12\% = 0.12$ ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1:** Compute the coupon payment. $$C = F \times c = 1{,}000 \times 0.10 = 100$$ **Step 2:** Apply the bond pricing formula. $$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{3} \dfrac{100}{(1.12)^t} + \dfrac{1{,}000}{(1.12)^3}$$ **Step 3:** Compute the discount factor for each period. $$\begin{aligned} (1.12)^1 &= 1.1200 \\ (1.12)^2 &= 1.2544 \\ (1.12)^3 &= 1.4049 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4:** Discount each coupon payment to present value. $$\begin{aligned} PV(C_1) &= \dfrac{100}{1.1200} = 89.29 \\ PV(C_2) &= \dfrac{100}{1.2544} = 79.72 \\ PV(C_3) &= \dfrac{100}{1.4049} = 71.18 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5:** Discount the face value repayment. $$PV(F) = \dfrac{1{,}000}{1.4049} = 711.78$$ **Step 6:** Sum all discounted cash flows. $$P_0 = 89.29 + 79.72 + 71.18 + 711.78 = 951.97$$ ### Final Answer $$\boxed{P_0 \approx \text{₹} 951.97}$$ Since $P_0 < F$, the bond trades at a **DISCOUNT**. This is because the coupon rate ($10\%$) is less than the YTM ($12\%$) — investors demand a cheaper price to compensate for the below-market coupon. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Bond Price vs Interest Rate Sensitivity The following matrix illustrates how the same ₹1,000, 10% coupon, 3-year bond prices at different YTMs: | YTM ($r$) | Bond Price (₹) | Status | Logical Reason | |-----------|----------------|--------|----------------| | **8%** | 1,051.54 | **PREMIUM** | Coupon > YTM, investors pay extra for higher income | | **10%** | 1,000.00 | **PAR** | Coupon = YTM, bond priced exactly at face value | | **12%** | 951.97 | **DISCOUNT** | Coupon < YTM, investors demand price reduction | This matrix is a **high-frequency KTU 2024 exam question pattern** and illustrates the foundational bond-price-interest-rate inverse relationship. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Bond Cash Flow Architecture (Mermaid Diagram) The following diagram maps the complete cash flow architecture of a standard fixed-coupon bond over its lifetime, showing how an investor receives two streams of payments from the issuer. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Investor Buys Bond
Pays Market Price P0"] --> B["Issue Date T equals 0"] B --> C1["Year 1
Coupon C Received"] B --> C2["Year 2
Coupon C Received"] B --> C3["Year 3
Coupon C Received"] B --> CN["Year N minus 1
Coupon C Received"] C1 --> D1["Coupons Continue
Annuity Stream"] C2 --> D1 C3 --> D1 CN --> D1 D1 --> E["Maturity Date Year N
Final Coupon C plus Face Value F Returned"] E --> F["Bond Lifecycle Ends
Issuer Obligation Complete"] A --> G["Issuer Receives Capital
Uses for Projects"] G --> H["Periodic Coupon Payments
Reduce Issuer Cash"] H --> I["Final Repayment
Face Value F plus Last Coupon"] I --> F ``` ## 4.2 Bond Characteristics — Component Block Diagram The diagram below illustrates how each characteristic of a bond is interconnected and contributes to its overall valuation profile. ```mermaid flowchart LR P0["Bond Market Price P0"] --> F1["Face Value F"] P0 --> F2["Coupon Rate c"] P0 --> F3["Maturity n"] P0 --> F4["Yield r YTM"] P0 --> F5["Credit Rating"] F1 --> A1["Determines
Principal Repayment"] F2 --> A2["Determines
Periodic Income"] F3 --> A3["Determines
Time Horizon"] F4 --> A4["Determines
Discount Rate"] F5 --> A5["Determines
Default Risk"] A1 --> R["Final Bond
Valuation Outcome"] A2 --> R A3 --> R A4 --> R A5 --> R R --> S1["If P0 greater than F = PREMIUM"] R --> S2["If P0 equals F = PAR"] R --> S3["If P0 less than F = DISCOUNT"] ``` ## 4.3 Interest Rate Sensitivity Block Diagram This block diagram demonstrates the inverse relationship mechanism between interest rates and bond prices — a frequently tested KTU concept. ```mermaid flowchart TD X["Market Interest Rate
Changes"] --> Y{"Compare with
Coupon Rate"} Y -->|Rate Rises Above Coupon| Z1["Existing Bond Less Attractive"] Z1 --> Z2["Price Falls Below Par"] Z2 --> Z3["New YTM Equals Market Rate"] Y -->|Rate Falls Below Coupon| Z4["Existing Bond More Attractive"] Z4 --> Z5["Price Rises Above Par"] Z5 --> Z6["New YTM Equals Market Rate"] Y -->|Rate Equals Coupon| Z7["Bond Trades At Par"] Z7 --> Z8["Stable Valuation"] ``` These three diagrams collectively provide the **visual reasoning framework** expected in KTU 2024 OBE-aligned examinations where CO (Course Outcome) mapping requires both conceptual understanding and visual schematic literacy. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define a bond and list any four characteristics of a bond.** **Model Answer:** A bond is a debt security that represents a loan made by an investor to a borrower (typically a corporation or government). The issuer promises to pay periodic interest (coupons) and repay the principal at maturity. Four characteristics of a bond: 1. **Face Value** — the principal amount repaid at maturity. 2. **Coupon Rate** — the fixed annual interest rate stated on the bond. 3. **Maturity Date** — the date when the principal is repaid. 4. **Credit Rating** — the creditworthiness assessment of the issuer. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Explain the difference between Current Yield and Yield to Maturity (YTM).** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Current Yield | Yield to Maturity (YTM) | |-----------|---------------|--------------------------| | **Formula** | $CY = C / P_0$ | $YTM$ is the IRR that equates $P_0$ to all future cash flows | | **Includes Capital Gains?** | No | Yes (assumes held to maturity) | | **Complexity** | Simple to compute | Requires iterative solving | | **Use Case** | Quick income assessment | Total return estimation | Current yield considers only the annual coupon income relative to the current price, while YTM accounts for the total return including capital gains/losses realized at maturity, assuming all coupons are reinvested. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **A 5-year bond has a face value of ₹1,000 and a coupon rate of 8% per annum paid annually. If the current yield to maturity is 10%, calculate the current market price of the bond. Is the bond trading at a premium, discount, or par?** **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify the parameters. - $F = 1{,}000$, $C = 1{,}000 \times 0.08 = 80$, $n = 5$, $r = 0.10$ **Step 2:** Apply the bond pricing formula. $$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{5} \dfrac{80}{(1.10)^t} + \dfrac{1{,}000}{(1.10)^5}$$ **Step 3:** Compute the discount factors. $$\begin{aligned} (1.10)^1 &= 1.1000 \\ (1.10)^2 &= 1.2100 \\ (1.10)^3 &= 1.3310 \\ (1.10)^4 &= 1.4641 \\ (1.10)^5 &= 1.6105 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4:** Discount each coupon. $$\begin{aligned} PV(C_1) &= 80 / 1.1000 = 72.73 \\ PV(C_2) &= 80 / 1.2100 = 66.12 \\ PV(C_3) &= 80 / 1.3310 = 60.11 \\ PV(C_4) &= 80 / 1.4641 = 54.64 \\ PV(C_5) &= 80 / 1.6105 = 49.67 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5:** Discount the face value. $$PV(F) = 1{,}000 / 1.6105 = 620.92$$ **Step 6:** Sum all present values. $$P_0 = 72.73 + 66.12 + 60.11 + 54.64 + 49.67 + 620.92 = 924.19$$ **Conclusion:** Since $P_0 = 924.19 < F = 1{,}000$, the bond trades at a **DISCOUNT**. This is logically consistent because the coupon rate (8%) is lower than the YTM (10%). --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Explain any five characteristics of a bond in detail with examples.** **Model Answer:** The five characteristics are: 1. **Face Value (₹1,000 standard):** The principal amount returned at maturity. Example: A Government of India bond with ₹100 face value repays ₹100 at the end of its tenure. 2. **Coupon Rate (e.g., 8% p.a.):** The fixed interest rate paid on the face value annually or semi-annually. Example: A bond with ₹1,000 face value and 8% coupon pays ₹80 per year. 3. **Maturity Period:** The duration until principal repayment. Example: A 10-year G-Sec issued in 2024 matures in 2034. 4. **Issue Price vs Market Price:** Issue price is the original sale price; market price fluctuates. Example: A bond issued at ₹1,000 may later trade at ₹980 or ₹1,050 based on interest rate movements. 5. **Yield to Maturity:** The effective annualized return if held to maturity. Example: A bond bought at ₹950 with ₹1,000 face value and 8% coupon has a YTM higher than 8% because of the capital gain component. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper - Alternative Choice] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Discuss the relationship between bond prices and interest rates. Why do bond prices move inversely to market interest rates?** **Model Answer:** **The Inverse Relationship Explained:** Bond prices and market interest rates share an **inverse (negative) relationship**. When market interest rates rise, existing bond prices fall, and when market rates fall, existing bond prices rise. **Why This Happens:** 1. **Fixed Coupon Constraint:** A bond's coupon is locked at issuance. If new bonds in the market offer 10% but an old bond pays only 6%, the old bond is unattractive unless its price drops to offer a competitive YTM. 2. **YTM-Price Equilibration:** The YTM must equal the prevailing market rate for arbitrage-free pricing. To raise the YTM of a low-coupon bond, its price must fall. 3. **Opposite Effect for High Coupons:** If market rates drop to 4%, an old bond paying 6% becomes highly desirable, pushing its price above par. **Numerical Illustration:** | Scenario | Market Rate | Old Bond Coupon | Old Bond Price | |----------|-------------|-----------------|----------------| | Rising rates | 10% | 6% | Falls below ₹1,000 | | Falling rates | 4% | 6% | Rises above ₹1,000 | This is the foundation of interest rate risk management in bond portfolios. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **A zero-coupon bond has a face value of ₹5,000 and matures in 10 years. If the required rate of return is 9%, calculate the current price of the bond.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify parameters. - $F = 5{,}000$, $n = 10$, $r = 0.09$, $C = 0$ (zero-coupon) **Step 2:** Apply the zero-coupon bond formula. $$P_0 = \dfrac{F}{(1+r)^n} = \dfrac{5{,}000}{(1.09)^{10}}$$ **Step 3:** Compute the compound factor. $$(1.09)^{10} = 2.3674$$ **Step 4:** Calculate the price. $$P_0 = \dfrac{5{,}000}{2.3674} = 2{,}112.09$$ **Conclusion:** The zero-coupon bond is priced at **₹2,112.09**, which is significantly below its ₹5,000 face value. The investor's entire return comes from the price appreciation (capital gain) at maturity. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Board Exams:** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert annual rates to match coupon frequency** — If coupons are semi-annual but YTM is annual, divide YTM by 2 and multiply $n$ by 2. > 2. **Confusing Current Yield with YTM** — Current Yield ignores capital gains. Examiners specifically test this distinction. > 3. **Sign errors in the discount factor** — Always ensure $(1+r)^t$ is in the denominator, not the numerator. > 4. **Misidentifying Premium/Discount** — Compare coupon rate with YTM (NOT with market interest rate alone). > 5. **Skipping intermediate steps in bond pricing** — Examiners award partial marks for each discounted cash flow. Never just write the final answer. > 6. **Ignoring the reinvestment assumption in YTM** — YTM assumes coupons are reinvested at the same rate — this assumption is frequently tested. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Rapid Revision Checklist — Bond Characteristics** - A **bond** is a debt instrument — bondholders are creditors, not owners. - The **face value** is the principal repaid at maturity. - The **coupon rate** determines periodic interest payments: $C = F \times c$. - **Premium, Par, Discount** status is determined by comparing coupon rate with YTM, not with market rates. - The **bond pricing equation** discounts all future coupons plus the face value at the YTM. - **Current Yield** = Coupon / Price; **YTM** = total return to maturity. - **Zero-coupon bonds** pay no periodic interest — they are issued at a discount. - Bond prices and interest rates have an **inverse relationship**. - The longer the **maturity**, the higher the interest rate risk (price volatility). - **Credit ratings** (AAA to D) indicate default risk — higher rating = lower yield required. - Always use the **closed-form annuity formula** for faster KTU exam calculations: $P_0 = C \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r} + F \cdot (1+r)^{-n}$. - **Perpetual bonds** have no maturity: $P_0 = C / r$ (used for consol bonds in the UK historically). - Remember to **adjust for semi-annual compounding** if coupons are paid twice a year (the KTU 2024 syllabus tests both annual and semi-annual variants). These notes fully cover the **Bond Characteristics** topic as prescribed in **Module 2** of the **OEIET832 – Introduction to Financial Markets** course under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, with alignment to the **Course Outcomes (CO2: Understanding debt instruments and their valuation)** and the **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels (L2 – Understand, L3 – Apply, L4 – Analyze)**.

topics.title: Valuation of Bonds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Valuation of Bonds # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bond:** A *bond* is a fixed-income debt security that represents a loan made by an investor to a borrower (typically a corporation or government). The bond obligates the issuer to pay the bondholder a specified stream of cash flows — comprising periodic **coupon payments** (interest) and a terminal **principal repayment** (face value) — at predetermined future dates until **maturity**. In the language of financial economics, a bond is a **contractual claim on the future cash flows** of the issuing entity. From the buyer's perspective, it is an *asset* (a receivable); from the issuer's perspective, it is a *liability* (a payable). ## 1.2 Key Bond Terminology | Term | Symbol | Definition | | :--- | :---: | :--- | | **Face Value (Par Value)** | $F$ | The amount repaid to the bondholder at maturity. Standard corporate bonds: **₹1,000**; Government of India bonds: **₹100**. | | **Coupon Rate** | $c$ | The fixed annual interest rate, expressed as a percentage of face value. | | **Coupon Payment** | $C$ | Periodic interest = $c \times F$ (per year) or $C = c \times F / m$ per period (if $m$ periods per year). | | **Maturity (Tenure)** | $n$ | The remaining time (in periods) until the principal is repaid. | | **Yield to Maturity (YTM)** | $y$ | The market-required rate of return; the internal rate of return on the bond's cash flows. | | **Current Market Price** | $P_0$ | The present price at which the bond trades in the secondary market. | | **Macaulay Duration** | $D$ | The weighted-average time to receive a bond's cash flows. | | **Convexity** | $C_x$ | A measure of the curvature in the price-yield relationship. | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rented Money" Model > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Intuition:** Think of buying a bond as **renting out your money** to a company or the government. Just as a tenant pays the landlord rent every month and returns the house keys at the end of the lease, the bond issuer pays you **interest (rent)** at regular intervals and returns your **principal (the keys — your original money)** when the bond matures. - If you lend **₹10,000** to a friend at 8% interest for 5 years, your friend pays you ₹800/year and returns ₹10,000 at the end. A bond is *exactly this contract*, but standardized, tradable, and legally enforceable. - The **coupon** = your "rent" on the loaned capital. - The **face value** = the "deposit" you get back. - The **YTM** = the *effective* return you earn, accounting for the price you actually paid (which may differ from face value). ## 1.4 Why Bond Valuation Matters (KTU 2024 Highlight) > [!IMPORTANT] > Bond valuation is the **backbone of fixed-income portfolio management**, interest-rate risk measurement, and corporate financing decisions. Every KTU 2024-Scheme question on this topic tests one of three things: (1) computing the **price** given yield, (2) computing the **yield** given price, or (3) interpreting the **inverse price-yield relationship**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Fundamental Principle of Bond Pricing > [!NOTE] > **Core Logic:** The price of any financial asset equals the **present value of its expected future cash flows**, discounted at the rate of return required by investors in the market (the YTM). A bond produces exactly two types of cash flows for its holder: 1. **Annuity cash flows** — periodic coupon payments $C$ for $n$ periods. 2. **Lump-sum cash flow** — the face value $F$ repaid at maturity (period $n$). Therefore, the **general bond pricing equation** is: $$P_0 \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C}{(1+y)^{t}} \;+\; \dfrac{F}{(1+y)^{n}}$$ Equivalently, using the present-value-of-annuity factor: $$P_0 \;=\; C \cdot \underbrace{\left[\dfrac{1 - (1+y)^{-n}}{y}\right]}_{\text{PV Annuity Factor}} \;+\; F \cdot \underbrace{(1+y)^{-n}}_{\text{PV Lump-Sum Factor}}$$ ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Behind the Formula 1. **Identify the cash flows** — coupons $C$ at $t = 1, 2, \dots, n$, and face value $F$ at $t = n$. 2. **Choose the discount rate** — the bond's Yield to Maturity $y$, which reflects both the time value of money and the credit risk of the issuer. 3. **Discount each cash flow** — because money received in the future is worth less today. 4. **Sum the discounted values** — this sum is the **fair market price** of the bond. 5. **Equilibrium check** — at this price, the expected return of the bond exactly equals $y$. ## 2.3 Bond Pricing Theorem (KTU High-Yield Concept) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bond Pricing Theorem:** *The market price of a bond moves inversely with its yield to maturity.* > - If $y \uparrow$ (yield rises) $\Rightarrow$ $P_0 \downarrow$ (price falls) > - If $y \downarrow$ (yield falls) $\Rightarrow$ $P_0 \uparrow$ (price rises) This is because higher discounting shrinks the present value of fixed future cash flows. ## 2.4 Special Cases of the General Formula ### Case 1: Zero-Coupon Bond (Deep Discount Bond) No intermediate coupons; only the face value is paid at maturity. $$P_0 \;=\; \dfrac{F}{(1+y)^{n}}$$ ### Case 2: Perpetuity (Consol Bond) A bond that pays coupons *forever* (e.g., British Consols). The face value is never repaid. $$P_0 \;=\; \dfrac{C}{y}$$ ### Case 3: Perpetuity with Growth (Growing Perpetuity) If coupons grow at a constant rate $g < y$: $$P_0 \;=\; \dfrac{C_{1}}{y - g} \;=\; \dfrac{C_0 (1+g)}{y - g}$$ ### Case 4: Premium, Par, and Discount Bonds | Bond Type | Price-Yield Relationship | Investor Implication | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Premium Bond** | $P_0 > F$ and $c > y$ | Coupon rate exceeds market yield; bond trades above par. | | **Par Bond** | $P_0 = F$ and $c = y$ | Coupon rate equals market yield; bond trades at par. | | **Discount Bond** | $P_0 < F$ and $c < y$ | Coupon rate is below market yield; bond trades below par. | ## 2.5 Other Essential Yields & Measures ### Current Yield The simple annual income relative to the current price (ignoring capital gain/loss): $$CY \;=\; \dfrac{C}{P_0} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Annual Coupon}}{\text{Current Price}}$$ ### Yield to Maturity (YTM) — Definition YTM is the discount rate $y$ that solves: $$P_0 \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C}{(1+y)^{t}} \;+\; \dfrac{F}{(1+y)^{n}}$$ There is **no closed-form solution** for $y$ when $n > 1$ — it must be solved by: - **Trial-and-error interpolation**, or - **Financial calculator / spreadsheet functions** (e.g., Excel's `IRR` or `YIELD`). ### Approximate YTM Formula (for short-hand KTU calculations) $$YTM \;\approx\; \dfrac{C + \dfrac{F - P_0}{n}}{\dfrac{F + P_0}{2}}$$ Numerator = average annual income (coupon + annualized capital gain). Denominator = average price over the bond's life. ## 2.6 Duration and Convexity (Risk Measures) ### Macaulay Duration $$D \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{n} t \cdot w_t \quad \text{where} \quad w_t \;=\; \dfrac{\dfrac{CF_t}{(1+y)^{t}}}{P_0}$$ It is the **weighted-average time** to receive a bond's cash flows, weighted by their present values. ### Modified Duration (Price Sensitivity) $$D_{mod} \;=\; \dfrac{D}{1 + y/m}$$ The percentage price change for a small change in yield: $$\dfrac{\Delta P}{P_0} \;\approx\; -D_{mod} \cdot \Delta y$$ ### Convexity (Second-Order Correction) $$C_x \;=\; \dfrac{1}{P_0} \cdot \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t \cdot t(t+1)}{(1+y)^{t+2}}$$ Refines the duration estimate: $$\dfrac{\Delta P}{P_0} \;\approx\; -D_{mod} \cdot \Delta y \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot C_x \cdot (\Delta y)^{2}$$ ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility In modern financial engineering: - **Banks** use bond valuation to price treasuries, mortgage-backed securities, and corporate debt. - **Insurance companies** match asset-liability cash flows using duration and convexity. - **Central banks** (e.g., RBI) conduct open-market operations by valuing government bonds. - **Algorithmic trading desks** deploy duration and convexity for hedging interest-rate risk. ## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Note | | :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **General Bond Price** | $P_0 = C \cdot \left[ \tfrac{1 - (1+y)^{-n}}{y} \right] + F \cdot (1+y)^{-n}$ | All positive $y, C, F, n$ | | 2 | **Zero-Coupon Bond** | $P_0 = F \cdot (1+y)^{-n}$ | $C = 0$ | | 3 | **Perpetuity** | $P_0 = C / y$ | $n \to \infty$ | | 4 | **Growing Perpetuity** | $P_0 = C_1 / (y - g)$ | $y > g$ strictly | | 5 | **Current Yield** | $CY = C / P_0$ | Ignores capital gain | | 6 | **Approx. YTM** | $YTM \approx \tfrac{C + (F - P_0)/n}{(F + P_0)/2}$ | Short-cut only | | 7 | **Macaulay Duration** | $D = \sum t \cdot w_t$ | Units: periods | | 8 | **Modified Duration** | $D_{mod} = D / (1 + y/m)$ | $m$ = compounding freq. | | 9 | **Price Sensitivity** | $\Delta P / P_0 \approx -D_{mod} \cdot \Delta y$ | For small $\Delta y$ | | 10 | **Convexity Adjustment** | $\Delta P / P_0 \approx -D_{mod} \Delta y + \tfrac{1}{2} C_x (\Delta y)^{2}$ | Second-order term | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the General Bond Pricing Formula The present value of a single future cash flow $CF$ received at time $t$, discounted at rate $y$, is given by the fundamental time-value-of-money relation: $$PV \;=\; \dfrac{CF}{(1+y)^{t}}$$ A bond with $n$ periods to maturity generates $n$ coupon payments $C$ at $t = 1, 2, \dots, n$, and one face-value repayment $F$ at $t = n$. The total price is the sum of the present values of all these cash flows: $$ \begin{aligned} P_0 \;&=\; \dfrac{C}{(1+y)^{1}} \;+\; \dfrac{C}{(1+y)^{2}} \;+\; \cdots \;+\; \dfrac{C}{(1+y)^{n}} \;+\; \dfrac{F}{(1+y)^{n}} \\[6pt] \;&=\; \underbrace{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C}{(1+y)^{t}}}_{\text{PV of Coupon Annuity}} \;+\; \underbrace{\dfrac{F}{(1+y)^{n}}}_{\text{PV of Face Value}} \end{aligned} $$ The summation is a **finite geometric series** in $\frac{1}{(1+y)}$. Let $r = \frac{1}{1+y}$. Then: $$ \begin{aligned} \sum_{t=1}^{n} C \cdot r^{t} \;&=\; C \cdot r \cdot \dfrac{1 - r^{n}}{1 - r} \\[6pt] \;&=\; C \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+y)^{-n}}{y} \end{aligned} $$ (The identity $1 - r = \frac{y}{1+y}$ and $r \cdot \tfrac{1}{1-r} = \tfrac{1}{y}$ are used.) Substituting back: $$ \boxed{\,P_0 \;=\; C \cdot \left[ \dfrac{1 - (1+y)^{-n}}{y} \right] \;+\; F \cdot (1+y)^{-n}\,} $$ ## 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example **Problem (KTU 2024-Style):** A 5-year corporate bond has a face value of **₹1,000** and an annual coupon rate of **8%**. The market yield to maturity is **10%**. Calculate the current market price of the bond. ### Step 1: Identify the Parameters $$ \begin{aligned} F \;&=\; 1{,}000 \\ c \;&=\; 8\% = 0.08 \\ C \;&=\; c \times F = 0.08 \times 1{,}000 = 80 \\ n \;&=\; 5 \text{ years} \\ y \;&=\; 10\% = 0.10 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2: Compute the PV Annuity Factor $$ \begin{aligned} \text{PVAF} \;&=\; \dfrac{1 - (1+0.10)^{-5}}{0.10} \\[6pt] \;&=\; \dfrac{1 - (1.10)^{-5}}{0.10} \\[6pt] \;&=\; \dfrac{1 - 0.6209213}{0.10} \\[6pt] \;&=\; \dfrac{0.3790787}{0.10} \\[6pt] \;&=\; 3.790787 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3: Compute the PV of Coupons $$ \begin{aligned} PV_{\text{coupons}} \;&=\; C \cdot \text{PVAF} \\[6pt] \;&=\; 80 \times 3.790787 \\[6pt] \;&=\; 303.263 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4: Compute the PV of Face Value $$ \begin{aligned} PV_{\text{face}} \;&=\; F \cdot (1+y)^{-n} \\[6pt] \;&=\; 1{,}000 \times (1.10)^{-5} \\[6pt] \;&=\; 1{,}000 \times 0.6209213 \\[6pt] \;&=\; 620.921 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5: Sum to Get the Bond Price $$ \begin{aligned} P_0 \;&=\; PV_{\text{coupons}} + PV_{\text{face}} \\[6pt] \;&=\; 303.263 + 620.921 \\[6pt] \;&=\; \boxed{924.184} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6: Interpret the Result - $P_0 = 924.18 < F = 1{,}000 \Rightarrow$ The bond trades at a **discount**. - This is consistent with $c = 8\% < y = 10\%$. - An investor paying ₹924.18 receives ₹80/year for 5 years and ₹1,000 at maturity — yielding exactly 10% per annum. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity Table This table shows how the bond's price changes with the YTM (holding $F=1{,}000$, $C=80$, $n=5$ constant): | Yield $y$ | PV Annuity Factor | PV of Coupons | PV of Face | $P_0$ | Bond Type | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | | **6%** | 4.2124 | 336.99 | 747.26 | **1,084.25** | Premium | | **8%** | 3.9927 | 319.42 | 680.58 | **1,000.00** | Par | | **10%** | 3.7908 | 303.26 | 620.92 | **924.18** | Discount | | **12%** | 3.6048 | 288.38 | 567.43 | **855.81** | Discount | | **15%** | 3.3522 | 268.18 | 497.18 | **765.36** | Deep Discount | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Observation:** As yield rises, the price falls — confirming the **inverse price-yield relationship**. Notice the price drops are *not* linear; they accelerate at higher yields. This *curvature* is **convexity**. ## 3.4 Solving for YTM (Reverse Problem) **Problem:** A 3-year bond with $F = 1{,}000$ and $C = 90$ is selling at $P_0 = 950$. Find the YTM. **Step 1:** Set up the equation. $$950 \;=\; 90 \cdot \left[ \dfrac{1 - (1+y)^{-3}}{y} \right] \;+\; 1{,}000 \cdot (1+y)^{-3}$$ **Step 2:** Try $y = 11\%$: $$ \begin{aligned} (1.11)^{3} \;&=\; 1.367631 \\ (1.11)^{-3} \;&=\; 0.731191 \\ \text{PVAF} \;&=\; \dfrac{1 - 0.731191}{0.11} \;=\; 2.4437 \\ P_0 \;&=\; 90 \times 2.4437 + 1{,}000 \times 0.731191 \\ \;&=\; 219.93 + 731.19 \;=\; 951.12 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Try $y = 11.1\%$: $$ \begin{aligned} (1.111)^{3} \;&\approx\; 1.371534 \\ (1.111)^{-3} \;&\approx\; 0.729118 \\ \text{PVAF} \;&=\; \dfrac{1 - 0.729118}{0.111} \;=\; 2.44011 \\ P_0 \;&=\; 90 \times 2.44011 + 1{,}000 \times 0.729118 \\ \;&=\; 219.61 + 729.12 \;=\; 948.73 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Linear interpolation. $$ \begin{aligned} y \;&\approx\; 11\% + \dfrac{951.12 - 950.00}{951.12 - 948.73} \times 0.1\% \\[6pt] \;&\approx\; 11\% + \dfrac{1.12}{2.39} \times 0.1\% \\[6pt] \;&\approx\; 11\% + 0.047\% \;\approx\; \boxed{11.05\%} \end{aligned} $$ The YTM is approximately **11.05%** — higher than the coupon rate (9%) because the bond is selling at a discount. # 4. P0 greater than F"]:::lowbig end subgraph MID["MID YIELD REGION"] M1["Yield equals coupon rate c"]:::mid M2["Price P0 equals Face F"]:::mid M3["Bond trades at PAR"]:::mid end subgraph RIGHT["HIGH YIELD REGION"] R1["Yield y is large"]:::high R2["Price P0 is SMALL"]:::highsmall R3["Bond trades at DISCOUNT
P0 less than F"]:::highsmall end L1 --> L2 --> L3 M1 --> M2 --> M3 R1 --> R2 --> R3 classDef low fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#00838F,color:#000 classDef lowbig fill:#4FC3F7,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef mid fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000 classDef high fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000 classDef highsmall fill:#EF9A9A,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Convexity Insight:** The price-yield curve is *not a straight line*. It is **convex to the origin**, meaning price falls more steeply for a yield increase than it rises for an equivalent yield decrease. This is the *convexity bias* exploited by bond portfolio managers. ## 4.4 Bond-Type Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Compare Coupon Rate c

KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define a bond and explain any four key features of a bond.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **bond** is a fixed-income debt instrument that represents a loan made by an investor to a corporate or governmental issuer, obligating the issuer to pay periodic interest (coupon) and repay the principal (face value) at maturity. **Four Key Features:** 1. **Face Value (Par Value):** The nominal amount of the bond, repaid at maturity. Standard values: ₹1,000 (corporate) or ₹100 (GOI). 2. **Coupon Rate:** The fixed annual interest rate, applied to the face value to compute coupon payments. Example: A 10% coupon on ₹1,000 face = ₹100/year. 3. **Maturity (Tenure):** The pre-specified date on which the principal is repaid. Common maturities: 1 year (T-bills) to 30 years (long-term government bonds). 4. **Yield to Maturity (YTM):** The total return an investor earns if the bond is held until maturity, accounting for coupon income and any capital gain/loss. *(Examiner's note: A clean, complete definition with all 4 features fetches full 3 marks. Skipping the definition costs 1 mark.)* --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Distinguish between current yield and yield to maturity (YTM) of a bond.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Basis | Current Yield | Yield to Maturity | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Definition** | Annual coupon income divided by the current market price. | Total return if bond is held until maturity, including capital gain/loss. | | **Formula** | $CY = \dfrac{C}{P_0}$ | $P_0 = \sum \dfrac{C}{(1+y)^t} + \dfrac{F}{(1+y)^n}$ | | **Considers Capital Gain** | No — it ignores the price difference $F - P_0$. | Yes — it captures the gain/loss from $P_0$ to $F$. | | **Use Case** | Quick annual income estimate. | True economic return measure for buy-and-hold investors. | > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often equate current yield with YTM. The examiner will *specifically* check whether you mention the **capital gain/loss component**. Mention it explicitly to score full marks. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Question:** *(a)* Explain the fundamental principle of bond valuation. Derive the general bond pricing formula. *(7 marks)* *(b)* A 6-year bond has a face value of ₹1,000 and an annual coupon rate of 9%. The current market yield is 11%. Calculate the current market price of the bond and comment on whether the bond is trading at a premium, par, or discount. *(7 marks)* --- #### Solution to Part A(a) — 7 Marks **Principle of Bond Valuation (2 marks):** The fundamental principle states that the **price of a bond is the present value of all its expected future cash flows**, discounted at the market-determined rate of return (Yield to Maturity, $y$). $$P_0 \;=\; PV(\text{Coupons}) + PV(\text{Face Value at maturity})$$ This is grounded in the **time value of money** — a rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow. **Derivation of the General Formula (5 marks):** A bond with maturity $n$ pays coupon $C$ at the end of each year and face value $F$ at year $n$. The price is: $$ \begin{aligned} P_0 \;&=\; \dfrac{C}{(1+y)^{1}} + \dfrac{C}{(1+y)^{2}} + \cdots + \dfrac{C}{(1+y)^{n}} + \dfrac{F}{(1+y)^{n}} \\[6pt] \;&=\; \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C}{(1+y)^{t}} + \dfrac{F}{(1+y)^{n}} \end{aligned} $$ The first term is a finite geometric series with first term $\frac{C}{1+y}$ and common ratio $\frac{1}{1+y}$. Using the geometric sum formula: $$\sum_{t=1}^{n} C \cdot (1+y)^{-t} \;=\; C \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+y)^{-n}}{y}$$ Therefore, the **general bond pricing equation** is: $$\boxed{\,P_0 \;=\; C \cdot \left[ \dfrac{1 - (1+y)^{-n}}{y} \right] + F \cdot (1+y)^{-n}\,}$$ --- #### Solution to Part A(b) — 7 Marks **Given:** $F = 1{,}000$, $c = 9\%$, $n = 6$ years, $y = 11\%$. **Step 1:** Compute coupon. $$C = c \times F = 0.09 \times 1{,}000 = 90$$ **Step 2:** PV Annuity Factor (PVAF). $$ \begin{aligned} (1.11)^{6} \;&=\; 1.870414 \\ (1.11)^{-6} \;&=\; 0.534641 \\ \text{PVAF} \;&=\; \dfrac{1 - 0.534641}{0.11} \;=\; \dfrac{0.465359}{0.11} \;=\; 4.23054 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** PV of Coupons. $$PV_{\text{coupons}} = 90 \times 4.23054 = 380.75$$ **Step 4:** PV of Face Value. $$PV_{\text{face}} = 1{,}000 \times 0.534641 = 534.64$$ **Step 5:** Bond Price. $$P_0 = 380.75 + 534.64 = \boxed{915.39}$$ **Step 6: Comment.** Since $c = 9\% < y = 11\%$, the bond is trading at a **discount** ($P_0 = 915.39 < F = 1{,}000$). The discount of ₹84.61 compensates investors for the below-market coupon rate. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write the formula but **forget to compute the final discount amount** ($F - P_0$). Always quantify the premium/discount in monetary terms to fetch the "comment" mark. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Question:** *(a)* What is Yield to Maturity (YTM)? Discuss the relationship between bond price and yield to maturity with a suitable diagram. *(7 marks)* *(b)* A bond with a face value of ₹5,000 pays annual coupons of ₹400 and matures in 4 years. If the current market price is ₹4,500, calculate the Yield to Maturity (YTM) of the bond. *(7 marks)* --- #### Solution to Part B(a) — 7 Marks **Definition of YTM (3 marks):** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Yield to Maturity (YTM)** is the internal rate of return earned by an investor who purchases a bond at its current market price and holds it until maturity, receiving all coupon payments and the face value on schedule. Mathematically, it is the discount rate $y$ that equates the present value of all future cash flows to the bond's current market price. $$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C}{(1+y)^t} + \dfrac{F}{(1+y)^n}$$ **Price-Yield Relationship (4 marks):** The relationship is **inverse and convex**: - When yield $y$ rises, bond price $P_0$ falls. - When yield $y$ falls, bond price $P_0$ rises. - The relationship is *non-linear* (convex to the origin). | Yield Change | Price Change | Consequence | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $\Delta y > 0$ | $\Delta P < 0$ | Bond loses value (capital loss). | | $\Delta y < 0$ | $\Delta P > 0$ | Bond gains value (capital gain). | **Diagram (textual representation):** ``` P0 ↑ | ___________ | / PAR (c = y) | / • | / •• PREMIUM (c > y) | /•• | /• DISCOUNT (c < y) | /• |/ +-------------------→ y ``` The curve is **convex to the origin** — this is the geometric signature of **convexity**. --- #### Solution to Part B(b) — 7 Marks **Given:** $F = 5{,}000$, $C = 400$, $n = 4$ years, $P_0 = 4{,}500$. **Step 1:** Set up the YTM equation. $$4{,}500 = 400 \cdot \left[ \dfrac{1 - (1+y)^{-4}}{y} \right] + 5{,}000 \cdot (1+y)^{-4}$$ **Step 2:** Try $y = 11\%$. $$ \begin{aligned} (1.11)^{4} \;&=\; 1.518070 \\ (1.11)^{-4} \;&=\; 0.658731 \\ \text{PVAF} \;&=\; \dfrac{1 - 0.658731}{0.11} \;=\; 3.10245 \\ P_0 \;&=\; 400 \times 3.10245 + 5{,}000 \times 0.658731 \\ \;&=\; 1{,}240.98 + 3{,}293.66 \;=\; 4{,}534.64 \end{aligned} $$ Since $4{,}534.64 > 4{,}500$, we need a *higher* yield. **Step 3:** Try $y = 12\%$. $$ \begin{aligned} (1.12)^{4} \;&=\; 1.573519 \\ (1.12)^{-4} \;&=\; 0.635518 \\ \text{PVAF} \;&=\; \dfrac{1 - 0.635518}{0.12} \;=\; 3.03735 \\ P_0 \;&=\; 400 \times 3.03735 + 5{,}000 \times 0.635518 \\ \;&=\; 1{,}214.94 + 3{,}177.59 \;=\; 4{,}392.53 \end{aligned} $$ Since $4{,}392.53 < 4{,}500$, the true YTM lies between 11% and 12%. **Step 4:** Linear interpolation. $$ \begin{aligned} y \;&=\; 11\% + \dfrac{4{,}534.64 - 4{,}500}{4{,}534.64 - 4{,}392.53} \times (12\% - 11\%) \\[6pt] \;&=\; 11\% + \dfrac{34.64}{142.11} \times 1\% \\[6pt] \;&=\; 11\% + 0.244\% \;\approx\; \boxed{11.24\%} \end{aligned} $$ The approximate YTM is **11.24%**. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often skip the second trial and present the first trial value as the final answer. KTU examiners **expect two trials + interpolation** for a 7-mark YTM problem. Also, never write the answer as "around 11%" — be precise to two decimal places. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for Bond Valuation** - ✅ **Bond** = debt security with coupon annuity + face-value bullet payment. - ✅ **General Pricing Formula:** $P_0 = C \cdot \left[ \frac{1 - (1+y)^{-n}}{y} \right] + F \cdot (1+y)^{-n}$. - ✅ **Zero-Coupon:** $P_0 = F \cdot (1+y)^{-n}$ (no coupons, deep discount). - ✅ **Perpetuity:** $P_0 = C / y$ (no maturity, infinite life). - ✅ **Growing Perpetuity:** $P_0 = C_1 / (y - g)$, with strict condition $y > g$. - ✅ **Current Yield:** $CY = C / P_0$ — only annual income, ignores capital gain. - ✅ **YTM** = the discount rate that makes the bond's PV = market price; solved by trial-and-error or financial calculator. - ✅ **Bond Pricing Theorem:** Price and yield move in **opposite directions**. - ✅ **Premium Bond:** $c > y$ $\Rightarrow$ $P_0 > F$. - ✅ **Discount Bond:** $c < y$ $\Rightarrow$ $P_0 < F$. - ✅ **Par Bond:** $c = y$ $\Rightarrow$ $P_0 = F$. - ✅ **Macaulay Duration** = weighted-average time of cash flows (in periods). - ✅ **Modified Duration** = $D / (1 + y/m)$; used for $\Delta P$ sensitivity. - ✅ **Convexity** = second-order curvature; bonds with higher convexity are *less* sensitive to large yield changes. - ✅ Always **round intermediate steps to 4–6 decimal places** to avoid compounding rounding errors. - ✅ Always **state units** (₹ for prices, % for rates, years for maturity). - ✅ Always **interpret the result** — is the bond at premium, par, or discount? Why?

topics.title: Price of Bond
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Price of Bond

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2: PRICE OF BOND — COMPREHENSIVE STUDY NOTES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Bond Price Definition (KTU Standard):**
> The **Price of a Bond** is the present discounted value of all expected future cash flows generated by the bond, comprising periodic **coupon payments (interest)** and the **principal (face value) repayment** at maturity, discounted at the bond's prevailing **Yield to Maturity (YTM)** or required rate of return.

Mathematically, the price of a bond $P_0$ is expressed as:

$$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+r)^t} + \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}$$

Where:
- $P_0$ = Current Price of the Bond
- $C$ = Periodic Coupon Payment
- $r$ = Yield to Maturity (per period)
- $F$ = Face Value / Par Value
- $n$ = Number of periods to maturity

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Money Rental" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simple Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine you are **lending ₹1,00,000** to a friend (or a company) for **5 years**, and your friend promises to pay you **₹8,000 every year** as interest and return the **full ₹1,00,000** at the end of year 5. The question is — *what is this "promise to pay" worth TODAY?*
>
> Since money in the future is worth less than money today (due to inflation, risk, and opportunity cost), the future cash flows must be **discounted back to today**. That discounted total is the **Price of the Bond**.
>
> **Higher the discount rate (YTM) → Lower the bond price (and vice versa).**

### 1.3 Classification of Bonds by Price Behavior

| Bond Type | Price vs Face Value | Coupon Rate vs YTM | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Par Bond** | Price = Face Value | Coupon Rate = YTM | Newly issued standard bond |
| **Premium Bond** | Price > Face Value | Coupon Rate > YTM | High-coupon bond in low-rate market |
| **Discount Bond** | Price < Face Value | Coupon Rate < YTM | Low-coupon bond in high-rate market |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A bond's price is essentially a function of three variables — **coupon rate, YTM, and time to maturity**. Changes in any of these cause the price to move inversely to yield.

### 1.4 Components of a Bond's Cash Flow Structure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Distinct Cash Flow Components:**
> 1. **Coupon Stream (Annuity Part):** Equal periodic interest payments $\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+r)^t}$
> 2. **Principal Repayment (Lump Sum Part):** Single face-value payment at maturity $\frac{F}{(1+r)^n}$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Time Value of Money (TVM)

The entire framework of bond pricing rests on the **Time Value of Money principle**, which states that **a rupee received today is worth more than a rupee received tomorrow**, because:
- Money today can be invested to earn returns
- Inflation erodes purchasing power
- Future receipts carry credit and liquidity risk

### 2.2 Operational Logic — The Six-Step Bond Pricing Process

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-Step Pricing Logic (Examiner's Favorite):**

1. **Identify the coupon payment (C):** Compute as $C = \text{Coupon Rate} \times \text{Face Value}$, then divide by payment frequency (annual, semi-annual, etc.).

2. **Determine the number of periods (n):** Multiply years to maturity by the number of coupon payments per year (e.g., 5 years × 2 = 10 semi-annual periods).

3. **Establish the discount rate (r):** This is the **Yield to Maturity (YTM)** — the single rate that equates the bond's price to the present value of its cash flows.

4. **Discount each coupon individually** at the rate $(1+r)^t$ for period $t$.

5. **Discount the face value** received at maturity $n$ using $(1+r)^n$.

6. **Sum the present values** of all coupon payments and the discounted face value → this sum is the **bond price**.

### 2.3 Inverse Relationship: Price ↔ Yield

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Bond Price Theorem (Golden Rule of Bond Markets):**
> Bond prices and yields move in **OPPOSITE directions**. This is the most fundamental relationship in fixed-income markets.
>
> - When market interest rates **RISE** → bond prices **FALL** (existing bonds become less attractive)
> - When market interest rates **FALL** → bond prices **RISE** (existing bonds become more attractive)
>
> This is also known as **Interest Rate Risk**.

### 2.4 Bond Price Sensitivity — Duration and Convexity

| Concept | Definition | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Duration** | Weighted average time to receive bond cash flows | Measures price sensitivity to yield changes |
| **Convexity** | Curvature of price-yield relationship | Refines duration-based price estimates |
| **Modified Duration** | $\frac{D}{1+r}$ | Percentage price change for 1% yield change |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Formulas for KTU 2024 Scheme Examinations:**

| # | Formula | Description | Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+r)^t} + \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}$ | **General Bond Pricing Formula** | $C$ = Coupon, $F$ = Face Value, $r$ = YTM, $n$ = Periods |
| 2 | $P_0 = C \cdot \left[\frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}\right] + \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}$ | **Pricing with Annuity Factor** | Compact two-part form |
| 3 | $P_0 = \frac{C}{r} \quad \text{when } n \to \infty$ | **Perpetual Bond (Consol) Price** | Used for irredeemable bonds |
| 4 | $P_0 = \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}$ | **Zero-Coupon Bond Price** | No intermediate coupons |
| 5 | $\text{Current Yield} = \frac{\text{Annual Coupon}}{\text{Current Price}} = \frac{C}{P_0}$ | **Current Yield (CY)** | Income return only |
| 6 | $\text{YTM} \approx \frac{C + \frac{F - P_0}{n}}{\frac{F + P_0}{2}}$ | **Approximate YTM (Bond Equivalent Yield)** | Trial-and-error / approximation |
| 7 | $\Delta P \approx -D^* \cdot \Delta y$ | **Price Change from Modified Duration** | $D^* = \frac{D}{1+y}$ |
| 8 | $\text{Par Bond: } P_0 = F \iff \text{Coupon Rate} = \text{YTM}$ | **Par Bond Equilibrium Condition** | Used in issuance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Notation:** The vertical bar `|` symbol is replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` in equations to avoid markdown table conflicts. For example, **modified duration** is written as $\frac{D}{\vert 1+y \vert}$ — but in proper math notation, it is simply $\frac{D}{1+y}$.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering & Financial Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Bond Pricing Matters in Practice:**
> - **Corporate Finance:** Firms price their bond issuances to minimize cost of capital.
> - **Banking:** Banks price government bonds (G-Secs) for treasury operations and SLR/CRR compliance.
> - **Pension Funds:** Actuaries use bond pricing to compute liabilities (PV of future pension payments).
> - **Insurance:** Insurers match long-term liabilities with long-dated bond portfolios.
> - **Central Banks (RBI):** Open Market Operations involve buying/selling government bonds — pricing is critical.

### 2.7 Special Cases & Extensions

| Bond Type | Key Feature | Pricing Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Zero-Coupon Bond** | No periodic coupons; sold at discount | $P_0 = \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}$ |
| **Perpetual Bond (Consol)** | No maturity; pays coupon forever | $P_0 = \frac{C}{r}$ |
| **Callable Bond** | Issuer can redeem early | Price is lower than equivalent non-callable bond |
| **Putable Bond** | Holder can sell back to issuer | Price is higher than equivalent non-putable bond |
| **Convertible Bond** | Can be converted to equity | Price includes option value |
| **Floating-Rate Bond (FRB)** | Coupon resets with benchmark rate | Price stays near par value |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the General Bond Pricing Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Starting Premise:** A bond promises two streams of future cash flows:
> 1. Coupon payments $C$ at the end of each period $t = 1, 2, \ldots, n$
> 2. Face value $F$ at the end of period $n$ (along with the final coupon)
>
> Let $r$ be the market-determined discount rate (YTM) per period.

**Step 1 — Express the Present Value of the First Coupon (Period 1):**

$$PV_1 = \frac{C}{(1+r)^1} = \frac{C}{1+r}$$

**Step 2 — Express the Present Value of the Second Coupon (Period 2):**

$$PV_2 = \frac{C}{(1+r)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Generalize for the $t^{th}$ Coupon:**

$$PV_t = \frac{C}{(1+r)^t}$$

**Step 4 — Express the Present Value of the Face Value (Period n):**

$$PV_F = \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}$$

**Step 5 — Sum All Present Values (Total Bond Price):**

The bond's market price $P_0$ equals the sum of present values of all coupons plus the present value of the face value:

$$P_0 = \frac{C}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{C}{(1+r)^2} + \cdots + \frac{C}{(1+r)^n} + \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}$$

**Step 6 — Compact Form Using Summation Notation:**

$$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+r)^t} + \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}$$

**Step 7 — Separate into Annuity and Lump-Sum Components:**

$$P_0 = \underbrace{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+r)^t}}_{\text{Annuity Component (Coupons)}} + \underbrace{\frac{F}{(1+r)^n}}_{\text{Lump-Sum (Principal)}}$$

**Step 8 — Apply the Geometric Series Formula:**

The sum of a finite geometric series with first term $a = \frac{C}{1+r}$, common ratio $q = \frac{1}{1+r}$, and $n$ terms is:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+r)^t} = C \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$$

**Step 9 — Final Compact Bond Pricing Equation:**

$$\boxed{P_0 = C \cdot \left[\frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}\right] + \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}}$$

This is the **Kelley-Bares Formula** for bond pricing — the standard form expected in KTU 2024 scheme examinations.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example #1 — Premium Bond

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A 5-year bond has a face value of **₹1,000** and an annual coupon rate of **10%**. The current market yield (YTM) is **8%**. Coupons are paid annually. Calculate the bond's current market price.

**Given Data:**
- Face Value: $F = 1000$
- Annual Coupon: $C = 10\% \times 1000 = 100$
- YTM: $r = 8\% = 0.08$
- Periods: $n = 5$

**Step 1 — Compute the Annuity Factor:**

$$\left[\frac{1 - (1.08)^{-5}}{0.08}\right] = \frac{1 - 0.6806}{0.08} = \frac{0.3194}{0.08} = 3.9927$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Present Value of Coupons:**

$$PV_{\text{Coupons}} = 100 \times 3.9927 = 399.27$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Present Value of Face Value:**

$$PV_{\text{Face}} = \frac{1000}{(1.08)^5} = \frac{1000}{1.4693} = 680.58$$

**Step 4 — Sum to Get the Bond Price:**

$$P_0 = 399.27 + 680.58 = \boxed{1079.85}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Since $P_0 = 1079.85 > F = 1000$, this is a **PREMIUM BOND**. The coupon rate (10%) is greater than the YTM (8%), so investors are willing to pay extra for the above-market coupon.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example #2 — Discount Bond

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A 4-year bond has a face value of **₹5,000** and an annual coupon rate of **6%**. The current YTM is **9%**. Find the bond's price.

**Given Data:**
- $F = 5000$, $C = 6\% \times 5000 = 300$, $r = 9\% = 0.09$, $n = 4$

**Step 1 — Compute the Annuity Factor:**

$$\left[\frac{1 - (1.09)^{-4}}{0.09}\right] = \frac{1 - 0.7084}{0.09} = \frac{0.2916}{0.09} = 3.2397$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Present Value of Coupons:**

$$PV_{\text{Coupons}} = 300 \times 3.2397 = 971.91$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Present Value of Face Value:**

$$PV_{\text{Face}} = \frac{5000}{(1.09)^4} = \frac{5000}{1.4116} = 3542.20$$

**Step 4 — Sum to Get the Bond Price:**

$$P_0 = 971.91 + 3542.20 = \boxed{4514.11}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Since $P_0 = 4514.11 < F = 5000$, this is a **DISCOUNT BOND**. The coupon rate (6%) is less than the YTM (9%), so investors pay less than face value to compensate for the below-market coupon.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example #3 — Zero-Coupon Bond

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A 3-year zero-coupon bond with face value **₹10,000** is issued at a YTM of **7%**. Find its price.

**Step 1 — Apply the Zero-Coupon Formula:**

$$P_0 = \frac{F}{(1+r)^n} = \frac{10000}{(1.07)^3} = \frac{10000}{1.2250} = \boxed{8162.97}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** A zero-coupon bond is **ALWAYS a discount bond** at issuance, because there are no intermediate coupons and money has time value.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example #4 — Perpetual Bond (Consol)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> The British Government issued "Consols" paying **₹50 annually forever**. If the required return is **6%**, find the bond's price.

**Step 1 — Apply the Perpetuity Formula:**

$$P_0 = \frac{C}{r} = \frac{50}{0.06} = \boxed{833.33}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU Exam:** A perpetuity is a **theoretical construct** — actual perpetual bonds are rare. The UK Consol is the most famous historical example. Indian government bonds have finite maturities.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Price-Yield Relationship Table

| Coupon Rate | YTM | Bond Price vs Face Value | Bond Classification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 8% | 8% | $P_0 = F$ | **Par Bond** |
| 10% | 8% | $P_0 > F$ | **Premium Bond** |
| 6% | 8% | $P_0 < F$ | **Discount Bond** |
| 0% (Zero-Coupon) | 8% | $P_0 < F$ | **Deep Discount Bond** |
| 8% | 10% | $P_0 < F$ | **Discount Bond** (YTM > CR) |
| 8% | 6% | $P_0 > F$ | **Premium Bond** (CR > YTM) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Bond Pricing Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bond Issuance] --> B[Identify Bond Parameters]
    B --> C{Face Value F}
    B --> D{Coupon Rate CR}
    B --> E{Time to Maturity n}
    B --> F{Market YTM r}
    D --> G[Compute Coupon Payment C = CR × F]
    G --> H[Adjust Period Frequency]
    H --> I[Apply Bond Pricing Formula]
    F --> I
    E --> I
    I --> J[PV of Coupons Component]
    I --> K[PV of Face Value Component]
    J --> L[Sum All Present Values]
    K --> L
    L --> M{Compare P0 with F}
    M -->|P0 = F| N[PAR BOND]
    M -->|P0 > F| O[PREMIUM BOND]
    M -->|P0 < F| P[DISCOUNT BOND]
    N --> Q[Final Bond Classification]
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Investor Decision: Buy / Hold / Sell]
```

### 4.2 Cash Flow Timeline Diagram — 5-Year Coupon Bond

```mermaid
gantt
    title Bond Cash Flow Timeline (5-Year, 10% Coupon, FV=1000)
    dateFormat YYYY
    axisFormat %Y
    section Coupon Payments
    Coupon Year 1 :a1, 2025, 1y
    Coupon Year 2 :a2, 2026, 1y
    Coupon Year 3 :a3, 2027, 1y
    Coupon Year 4 :a4, 2028, 1y
    Coupon Year 5 :a5, 2029, 1y
    section Principal Repayment
    Face Value at Maturity :crit, b1, 2029, 1y
```

### 4.3 Price-Yield Inverse Relationship — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Market Interest Rates RISE] -->|Causes| B[YTM Increases]
    B -->|Causes| C[Bond Prices FALL]
    A2[Market Interest Rates FALL] -->|Causes| B2[YTM Decreases]
    B2 -->|Causes| C2[Bond Prices RISE]
    C --> D[Capital Loss for Existing Holders]
    C2 --> E[Capital Gain for Existing Holders]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Bond Cash Flow Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BondCashFlows["BOND TOTAL CASH FLOWS"]
        direction TB
        subgraph CouponStream["COUPON STREAM (Annuity)"]
            direction LR
            CP1[C at t=1]
            CP2[C at t=2]
            CP3[C at t=3]
            CPN[C at t=n]
        end
        subgraph PrincipalStream["PRINCIPAL STREAM (Lump Sum)"]
            direction LR
            FV[Face Value F at t=n]
        end
    end
    CouponStream -->|Discount at YTM| PVCoupons[PV of Coupons]
    PrincipalStream -->|Discount at YTM| PVFace[PV of Face Value]
    PVCoupons --> Sum[Total Bond Price P0]
    PVFace --> Sum
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q1. (a) Define the Price of a Bond. State the general bond pricing formula.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> **Definition:** The price of a bond is the present discounted value of all expected future cash flows (coupon payments and face value) that the bond will generate, discounted at the prevailing Yield to Maturity (YTM) or required rate of return.
>
> **Formula:**
>
> $$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+r)^t} + \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}$$
>
> Where $C$ = coupon payment, $r$ = YTM, $F$ = face value, and $n$ = number of periods. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for variable explanation)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. (b) Explain the inverse relationship between bond prices and interest rates.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> Bond prices and interest rates (YTM) move in **opposite directions**. (1 mark)
>
> **Reason:** A bond's price is the present value of fixed future cash flows. When the market discount rate (YTM) rises, the same future cash flows are discounted at a higher rate, reducing their present value — hence the bond price falls. (1 mark)
>
> Conversely, when YTM falls, the discount rate is lower, increasing the present value of the future cash flows — hence the bond price rises. (1 mark)
>
> This is called **Interest Rate Risk**, the most fundamental concept in fixed-income markets.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**Q2. (a) A 6-year bond has a face value of ₹10,000 and an annual coupon rate of 9%. The current Yield to Maturity (YTM) in the market is 11%. Calculate the current market price of the bond and classify it as Par, Premium, or Discount. (7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Face Value: $F = 10000$
- Annual Coupon: $C = 9\% \times 10000 = 900$
- YTM: $r = 11\% = 0.11$
- Periods: $n = 6$

**Step 1 — Compute the Annuity Factor (PVIFA):**

$$\left[\frac{1 - (1.11)^{-6}}{0.11}\right] = \frac{1 - 0.5346}{0.11} = \frac{0.4654}{0.11} = 4.2305$$

**Step 2 — Present Value of Coupons:**

$$PV_{\text{Coupons}} = 900 \times 4.2305 = 3807.45$$

**Step 3 — Present Value of Face Value:**

$$PV_{\text{Face}} = \frac{10000}{(1.11)^6} = \frac{10000}{1.8704} = 5346.35$$

**Step 4 — Bond Price:**

$$P_0 = 3807.45 + 5346.35 = \boxed{9153.80}$$

**Step 5 — Classification:**
Since $P_0 = 9153.80 < F = 10000$, this is a **DISCOUNT BOND**. (1 mark)

> **Reason:** Coupon Rate (9%) < YTM (11%), so investors pay less than face value. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. (b) A 10-year government bond has a face value of ₹1,00,000 and pays semi-annual coupons at 8% per annum. If the required rate of return is 10% per annum compounded semi-annually, find the bond price. Also calculate the Current Yield. (7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Face Value: $F = 1,00,000$
- Annual Coupon Rate: $8\%$, paid semi-annually
- Semi-annual Coupon: $C = \frac{8\% \times 1,00,000}{2} = 4000$
- Required Rate: $10\%$ per annum → Semi-annual rate: $r = \frac{10\%}{2} = 5\% = 0.05$
- Periods: $n = 10 \times 2 = 20$ semi-annual periods

**Step 1 — Annuity Factor:**

$$\left[\frac{1 - (1.05)^{-20}}{0.05}\right] = \frac{1 - 0.3769}{0.05} = \frac{0.6231}{0.05} = 12.4622$$

**Step 2 — Present Value of Coupons:**

$$PV_{\text{Coupons}} = 4000 \times 12.4622 = 49848.80$$

**Step 3 — Present Value of Face Value:**

$$PV_{\text{Face}} = \frac{1,00,000}{(1.05)^{20}} = \frac{1,00,000}{2.6533} = 37688.95$$

**Step 4 — Bond Price:**

$$P_0 = 49848.80 + 37688.95 = \boxed{87537.75}$$

**Step 5 — Current Yield Calculation:**

$$\text{Current Yield} = \frac{\text{Annual Coupon Income}}{\text{Current Price}} = \frac{8000}{87537.75} = 0.0914 = \boxed{9.14\%}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]

**Q3. (a) What is a Zero-Coupon Bond? A 5-year zero-coupon bond with face value ₹50,000 is issued at a YTM of 12% per annum. Calculate its issue price. (7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
