**Definition (1 Mark):** A **Zero-Coupon Bond** is a debt security that does not pay periodic interest (coupons) but is issued at a discount to its face value. The investor's return comes entirely from the difference between the purchase price and the face value received at maturity.

**Given Data:**
- Face Value: $F = 50,000$
- YTM: $r = 12\% = 0.12$
- Periods: $n = 5$

**Step 1 — Apply Zero-Coupon Pricing Formula:**

$$P_0 = \frac{F}{(1+r)^n} = \frac{50000}{(1.12)^5}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $(1.12)^5$:**

$$(1.12)^5 = 1.7623$$

**Step 3 — Final Calculation:**

$$P_0 = \frac{50000}{1.7623} = \boxed{28372.20}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 Mark):**
The investor pays ₹28,372.20 today and receives ₹50,000 after 5 years, earning an annualized return of 12% (the YTM). The discount amount is ₹21,627.80.

---

**Q3. (b) What is a Perpetual Bond? A consol bond pays ₹100 annually forever. If the required rate of return is 8%, calculate the price of the bond. If the required return suddenly rises to 10%, what is the new price? Comment on the change. (7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 Mark):** A **Perpetual Bond (Consol)** is a bond with **no maturity date** that pays a fixed coupon indefinitely. The price is computed using the perpetuity formula.

**Case 1: Required Return = 8%**

**Step 1 — Apply Perpetuity Formula:**

$$P_0 = \frac{C}{r} = \frac{100}{0.08} = \boxed{1250.00}$$

**Case 2: Required Return = 10%**

**Step 1 — Apply Perpetuity Formula:**

$$P_0 = \frac{C}{r} = \frac{100}{0.10} = \boxed{1000.00}$$

**Comment (3 Marks):**
- When the required return rises from 8% to 10%, the bond price **falls from ₹1250 to ₹1000**, a drop of **₹250 (20% decline)**. (1 mark)
- This illustrates the **inverse relationship** between bond price and yield. (1 mark)
- Perpetual bonds have the **highest interest rate sensitivity** (infinite duration) because all cash flows are at infinite maturity and no principal is ever returned. (1 mark)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU EXAMS:**
>
> 1. **Frequency Mismatch Error (Most Common!):** Students often forget to **halve the rate and double the periods** for semi-annual bonds. If a bond pays semi-annual coupons at 10% annual, then $r = 5\%$ per period and $n = 2 \times \text{years}$. **This single error loses 3-5 marks!**
>
> 2. **Coupon Calculation Error:** Confusing *coupon rate* (percentage) with *coupon payment* (₹ amount). Coupon Payment = Coupon Rate × Face Value. Always show this step explicitly.
>
> 3. **Formula Substitution Mistakes:** In $(1+r)^{-n}$, ensure the **negative exponent** is properly placed. Many students write $(1+r)^n$ in the numerator by mistake.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Face Value Component:** Students sometimes price only the coupons and forget to discount the face value. The face value component is **worth 2-3 marks** in any pricing problem.
>
> 5. **No Classification Justification:** For Premium/Discount questions, KTU examiners expect **2-3 lines of reasoning** explaining *why* the bond is premium/discount (coupon rate vs YTM comparison).
>
> 6. **Rounding Errors:** Carry at least **4 decimal places** in intermediate calculations. Rounding to whole numbers in intermediate steps causes cascading errors.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **📌 HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST (Read Before Exam):**

- ✅ **Bond Price = PV(Coupons) + PV(Face Value)** — remember these are the **two components**.
- ✅ **General Formula:** $P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+r)^t} + \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}$
- ✅ **Compact Formula:** $P_0 = C \cdot \left[\frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}\right] + \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}$
- ✅ **Zero-Coupon Formula:** $P_0 = \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}$ — no coupon stream.
- ✅ **Perpetuity Formula:** $P_0 = \frac{C}{r}$ — for bonds with no maturity.
- ✅ **Par Bond Condition:** $P_0 = F$ when Coupon Rate = YTM.
- ✅ **Premium Bond:** $P_0 > F$ when Coupon Rate > YTM.
- ✅ **Discount Bond:** $P_0 < F$ when Coupon Rate < YTM.
- ✅ **Inverse Relationship:** Price ↑ ⇒ YTM ↓ and Price ↓ ⇒ YTM ↑.
- ✅ **Current Yield:** $\frac{\text{Annual Coupon}}{\text{Current Price}}$ — measures income return only.
- ✅ **Semi-Annual Adjustment:** Divide annual rate by 2; multiply years by 2.
- ✅ **Annuity Factor (PVIFA):** $\frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$ — memorize this for speed.
- ✅ **Lump-Sum Factor (PVIF):** $\frac{1}{(1+r)^n}$ — memorize this too.
- ✅ **The four determinants of bond price:** Coupon Rate, YTM, Time to Maturity, Face Value.
- ✅ **The single most tested concept:** Classifying bonds as Par, Premium, or Discount and computing their price.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 FINAL EXAM TIP:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme, bond pricing problems are typically asked for **7-14 marks**. Practice at least **5 numerical problems** covering all bond types (par, premium, discount, zero-coupon, perpetuity). Pay special attention to **semi-annual compounding** and the **classification reasoning** — these are guaranteed marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Yield
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Yield

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **YIELD (Definition - KTU 2024 Standard):**
> **Yield** is the *return* an investor earns on an investment over a specific period, expressed as a **percentage of the investment's current market price (or face value)**, rather than its nominal (coupon) rate. It is the single most important metric used to compare the profitability of bonds, stocks, and other fixed-income securities in primary and secondary markets.

In financial markets, yield captures the **total return** from an instrument, encompassing:
1. **Coupon/Interest Income** (regular periodic payments)
2. **Capital Gain/Loss** (the difference between purchase price and face value/Redemption value, held to maturity)

$$\text{General Yield Concept} = \frac{\text{Annual Income} + \text{Capital Gain Component}}{\text{Price Paid}}$$

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Mango Tree Farmer"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Mango Tree Investment):**
> Imagine you buy a mango tree from your neighbour for **₹1,000**. Every year, the tree gives you mangoes worth **₹80** (the *coupon/dividend*). After 5 years, the tree's wood is worth **₹1,100** (the *face/Redemption value*).
>
> - **The ₹80 annual mango income** is the **Current Yield** portion.
> - The **extra ₹100 wood appreciation** spread over 5 years is the **Capital Gain Yield**.
> - The **combined effective return** if you held it for 5 years is the **Yield to Maturity (YTM)**.
>
> So even though the tree "gave" you ₹80/year, the *real* rate of return on your ₹1,000 investment depends on *when* and *how* you exit — that's what yield measures.

---

## 1.3 Why "Yield" Matters More Than "Coupon Rate"

| Metric | What It Tells You | What It Misses |
|---|---|---|
| **Coupon Rate (₹)** | Fixed income promised *on the face value* | The actual price you paid in the market |
| **Current Yield (CY)** | Annual income vs. current market price | Capital gain/loss component |
| **Yield to Maturity (YTM)** | Total return if held till maturity | Reinvestment risk |
| **Yield to Call (YTC)** | Total return if called at first call date | Loss of future coupons |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** A bond selling at a **discount** has YTM > Coupon Rate, and a bond selling at a **premium** has YTM < Coupon Rate. This inverse relationship is the *price-yield theorem*.

---

## 1.4 Standard Units & Reference Constants

- **Yield is always expressed in % per annum (p.a.)**
- **Standard face value** assumed in KTU problems: **₹100** or **₹1,000** (unless otherwise stated)
- **Coupon frequency**: Usually **Annual (1x)** or **Semi-Annual (2x)** in KTU numerical problems
- **Compounding convention**: Bond yields are conventionally quoted on an **annualized basis** with **semi-annual compounding** in international markets, but KTU problems typically use **annual compounding** for simplicity.

---

## 1.5 Types of Yield — Quick Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Yield Family" in KTU Module 2:**
> 1. **Current Yield (CY)** — Annual coupon ÷ Current Market Price
> 2. **Yield to Maturity (YTM)** — Total return if held till maturity
> 3. **Yield to Call (YTC)** — Total return if bond is called before maturity
> 4. **Nominal Yield** — Stated/coupon rate (unadjusted)
> 5. **Real Yield** — Nominal Yield minus inflation (Fisher Equation)
> 6. **Yield Curve** — Graphical plot of yield vs. time to maturity

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Conceptual Breakdown

### A. Current Yield (CY)

**Why it exists:** The coupon rate is fixed at issue, but market prices fluctuate daily. Current yield updates the income stream to the *price actually paid*.

**How it works:**
1. Identify the **annual coupon income** (C) on the bond.
2. Identify the **current market price** (P) — what you pay *today* to buy the bond.
3. Divide C by P and multiply by 100.

### B. Yield to Maturity (YTM)

**Why it matters:** A bond has multiple cash flows (annual coupons + final redemption). YTM is the *single discount rate* that equates the present value of **all** future cash flows to the **current market price**.

**How it works:**
1. List every future cash flow: Coupon at end of Year 1, 2, ..., n, and Face Value at end of Year n.
2. Apply the unknown discount rate `y` and equate to market price.
3. Solve for `y` (usually via trial-and-error / financial calculator / Excel `RATE` function).
4. YTM assumes **all coupons are reinvested at the same rate y** (reinvestment assumption).

### C. Yield to Call (YTC)

**Why it exists:** Many corporate bonds have a **call option** favoring the issuer. The issuer may redeem the bond *before* maturity if interest rates fall. YTC protects the investor against this risk.

**How it works:**
- Same logic as YTM, but:
  - Use **Call Price (CP)** instead of Face Value.
  - Use **Call Date (tc)** instead of Maturity (n).

### D. Real Yield vs. Nominal Yield (Fisher Equation)

**Why it exists:** A 7% return when inflation is 5% is *not* the same as 7% return when inflation is 2%. The **real yield** measures *purchasing power gain*.

$$\text{Real Yield} = \frac{1 + \text{Nominal Yield}}{1 + \text{Inflation Rate}} - 1$$

The simpler approximation:
$$\text{Real Yield} \approx \text{Nominal Yield} - \text{Inflation Rate}$$

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are board-exam grade and use the convention:**
> - $C$ = Annual Coupon (in ₹)
> - $F$ = Face Value / Par Value / Redemption Value (in ₹)
> - $P$ = Current Market Price (in ₹)
> - $n$ = Number of years to maturity
> - $CP$ = Call Price (in ₹)
> - $t_c$ = Years to first call date
> - $y$ = Yield (in decimal, e.g., 0.08 for 8%)

| # | Yield Type | Formula | Applicable When |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Current Yield (CY)** | $CY = \dfrac{C}{P} \times 100$ | Annual income vs. price |
| 2 | **YTM (Approximation Formula)** | $YTM \approx \dfrac{C + \dfrac{F - P}{n}}{\dfrac{F + P}{2}} \times 100$ | Quick estimate (KTU favorite) |
| 3 | **YTM (Exact, Annual Compounding)** | $P = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C}{(1+y)^t} + \dfrac{F}{(1+y)^n}$ | Precise calculation |
| 4 | **YTC (Approximation)** | $YTC \approx \dfrac{C + \dfrac{CP - P}{t_c}}{\dfrac{CP + P}{2}} \times 100$ | Callable bond |
| 5 | **Real Yield (Fisher)** | $r = \dfrac{1 + \text{Nominal}}{1 + \text{Inflation}} - 1$ | Inflation-adjusted |
| 6 | **Approximate Real Yield** | $r \approx i - \pi$ | Quick estimate |
| 7 | **Holding Period Return (HPR)** | $HPR = \dfrac{C + (P_{sell} - P_{buy})}{P_{buy}} \times 100$ | Held less than full term |
| 8 | **Premium/Discount Flag** | If $P > F \Rightarrow YTM < C/F$ | Premium bond |
| 9 | **Premium/Discount Flag** | If $P < F \Rightarrow YTM > C/F$ | Discount bond |
| 10 | **At Par Condition** | If $P = F \Rightarrow YTM = C/F$ | Par bond |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Typographical Note:** In the YTM approximation formula above, the term $\dfrac{F + P}{2}$ represents the *average* of face and market price — this is the denominator for the "Average Price" concept. Do NOT confuse it with the absolute value operator.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

While this is a finance course, the **yield concept** has engineering parallels:
- **DC Machine Efficiency** (η) = Output Power / Input Power × 100 → Analogous to *Current Yield* (income / invested amount).
- **Process Yield** in manufacturing = (Good Units / Total Units) × 100 → Same percentage framework.
- **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** in project finance = *Mathematically identical* to YTM equation. YTM is, in fact, a special case of IRR for fixed-income securities.

Thus, yield is fundamentally a **rate-of-return** concept that bridges finance and project appraisal.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Current Yield Formula

**Starting Premise:** A bondholder receives an annual coupon `C` and pays a market price `P`. Yield must express the *coupon income as a percentage of the price actually paid*.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Yield} &= \frac{\text{Annual Return Component}}{\text{Price Paid}} \times 100 \\
\text{Annual Return Component} &= C \quad \text{(just the coupon for CY)} \\
\therefore \quad CY &= \frac{C}{P} \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**
- Bond face value $F = ₹1{,}000$, coupon rate = 10% p.a. $\Rightarrow C = ₹100$
- Current market price $P = ₹950$
- **CY** = $(100 / 950) \times 100 = \mathbf{10.53\%}$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the YTM Approximation Formula

**Starting Premise:** The YTM is essentially an *Internal Rate of Return*. We need to consider both:
1. Annual coupon income: $C$
2. Capital gain (or loss) component spread over $n$ years: $\dfrac{F - P}{n}$
3. Average price invested over the life: $\dfrac{F + P}{2}$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{YTM}_{\text{approx}} &= \frac{\text{Annual Income} + \text{Annualized Capital Gain}}{\text{Average Invested Amount}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{C + \dfrac{F - P}{n}}{\dfrac{F + P}{2}} \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

**Step-by-step numerical walkthrough:**
Given: $F = ₹1{,}000$, $C = ₹100$ (10% coupon), $P = ₹950$, $n = 5$ years.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 100 + \frac{1000 - 950}{5} \\
&= 100 + \frac{50}{5} \\
&= 100 + 10 = 110 \\[6pt]
\text{Denominator} &= \frac{1000 + 950}{2} = \frac{1950}{2} = 975 \\[6pt]
\therefore \text{YTM}_{\text{approx}} &= \frac{110}{975} \times 100 = 11.28\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification (Logical Sanity Check):**
- Since $P < F$ (discount bond), YTM **must be greater** than the coupon rate of 10%. ✓
- 11.28% > 10% — **Consistent**. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation of YTM Exact Equation (Annuity + Lump Sum)

**Starting Premise:** The present value of all future cash flows must equal the market price $P$.

The bond pays:
- Coupon $C$ at the end of years 1, 2, 3, ..., $n$ (an *ordinary annuity*)
- Face value $F$ at the end of year $n$ (a *lump sum*)

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= \underbrace{\frac{C}{(1+y)^1} + \frac{C}{(1+y)^2} + \cdots + \frac{C}{(1+y)^n}}_{\text{PV of Coupon Annuity}} + \underbrace{\frac{F}{(1+y)^n}}_{\text{PV of Face Value}} \\
P &= C \cdot \left[\frac{1 - (1+y)^{-n}}{y}\right] + \frac{F}{(1+y)^n}
\end{aligned}$$

This equation is **solved for `y` by iteration** (trial-and-error), which is exactly what a financial calculator's `YTM` button or Excel's `=RATE(n, C, -P, F)` function does.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Real Yield (Fisher Equation)

**Starting Premise:** If your money grew by `(1 + i)` nominally, and prices grew by `(1 + π)`, then your *purchasing power* grew by the ratio of the two.

$$\begin{aligned}
1 + r &= \frac{1 + \text{Nominal Yield (i)}}{1 + \text{Inflation Rate (π)}} \\
r &= \frac{1 + i}{1 + \pi} - 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Approximation derivation (using binomial expansion):**
$$\begin{aligned}
r &= (1 + i) \cdot (1 + \pi)^{-1} - 1 \\
&\approx (1 + i)(1 - \pi) - 1 \quad \text{(when π is small)} \\
&\approx 1 + i - \pi - i\pi - 1 \\
&\approx i - \pi \quad \text{(dropping the small } i\pi \text{ cross term)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Numerical Example:**
- Nominal yield $i = 8\%$, inflation $\pi = 3\%$
- **Exact:** $r = (1.08 / 1.03) - 1 = 0.0485 = 4.85\%$
- **Approx:** $r = 8 - 3 = 5\%$ (close enough!)

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing All Yield Types

| Scenario | C | F | P | CY | YTM (approx) | YTC | Real Yield (if π=4%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Discount Bond** | 100 | 1000 | 950 | 10.53% | 11.28% | 12.00%* | 7.28% |
| **Par Bond** | 100 | 1000 | 1000 | 10.00% | 10.00% | — | 6.00% |
| **Premium Bond** | 100 | 1000 | 1050 | 9.52% | 8.78% | — | 4.78% |

*YTC is meaningful only for callable bonds with a call price typically > face value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation Matrix Rule:** The order of magnitude is universally:
> **Discount Bond:** YTM > CY > Coupon Rate
> **Par Bond:** YTM = CY = Coupon Rate
> **Premium Bond:** Coupon Rate > CY > YTM

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Yield Family Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[YIELD UNIVERSE] --> nodeB[NOMINAL YIELD STATED COUPON RATE]
    nodeA --> nodeC[INVESTOR YIELDS]
    nodeA --> nodeD[INFLATION ADJUSTED YIELDS]

    nodeB --> nodeB1[FIXED COUPON RATE]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[FLOATING RATE TIED TO BENCHMARK]

    nodeC --> nodeC1[CURRENT YIELD CY]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[YIELD TO MATURITY YTM]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[YIELD TO CALL YTC]
    nodeC --> nodeC4[HOLDING PERIOD RETURN HPR]

    nodeC1 --> formula1["C over P times 100"]
    nodeC2 --> formula2["C plus F minus P over n over F plus P over 2 times 100"]
    nodeC3 --> formula3["C plus CP minus P over tc over CP plus P over 2 times 100"]
    nodeC4 --> formula4["C plus Psell minus Pbuy over Pbuy times 100"]

    nodeD --> nodeD1[REAL YIELD via FISHER EQUATION]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[APPROX REAL YIELD i minus pi]

    nodeD1 --> fisherA["one plus i over one plus pi minus one"]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow: Decision Tree for Solving a Yield Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[START: GIVEN A BOND PROBLEM] --> q1{WHAT IS ASKED?}

    q1 -->|Annual income vs price| pathA[COMPUTE CURRENT YIELD]
    q1 -->|Total return till maturity| pathB[COMPUTE YTM]
    q1 -->|Total return till call| pathC[COMPUTE YTC]
    q1 -->|Inflation adjusted return| pathD[APPLY FISHER EQUATION]

    pathA --> fA["CY equals C divided by P times 100"]
    pathB --> fB1{CALCULATION TOOL?}
    fB1 -->|Approximation| fB2["YTM approx formula"]
    fB1 -->|Exact iteration| fB3["Use RATE function or trial and error"]

    pathC --> fC["YTC approx formula with CP and tc"]
    pathD --> fD1{INFLATION GIVEN?}
    fD1 -->|Yes exact| fD2["r equals one plus i over one plus pi minus one"]
    fD1 -->|Yes approx| fD3["r equals i minus pi"]
    fD1 -->|No| fD4[ASSUME INFLATION equals zero or given separately]

    fA --> end[END: YIELD VALUE IN PERCENT]
    fB2 --> end
    fB3 --> end
    fC --> end
    fD2 --> end
    fD3 --> end
    fD4 --> end
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: The Price-Yield Inverse Relationship

```mermaid
graph LR
    priceHigh[PRICE GOES UP] --> yieldDown[YIELD GOES DOWN]
    yieldDown --> bondType[PREMIUM BOND]
    bondType --> flag1[CY less than YTM less than Coupon]

    priceLow[PRICE GOES DOWN] --> yieldUp[YIELD GOES UP]
    yieldUp --> bondType2[DISCOUNT BOND]
    bondType2 --> flag2[Coupon less than CY less than YTM]

    pricePar[PRICE EQUALS FACE VALUE] --> yieldEq[YIELD EQUALS COUPON RATE]
    yieldEq --> bondType3[PAR BOND]
    bondType3 --> flag3[Coupon equals CY equals YTM]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for Students:** This is the **Price-Yield Theorem** — the single most-tested relationship in KTU Module 2. Memorize the three triangles: Premium (Coupon > CY > YTM), Discount (YTM > CY > Coupon), Par (Coupon = CY = YTM).

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How Yield is Computed in Practice

```mermaid
graph TB
    inputBlock[INPUT DATA: FACE F, COUPON C, PRICE P, MATURITY n] --> processBlock[PROCESSING ENGINE]

    subgraph processBlock [YIELD CALCULATION ENGINE]
        direction TB
        step1[STEP 1: IDENTIFY BOND TYPE PAR PREMIUM DISCOUNT] --> step2[STEP 2: CHOOSE FORMULA APPROX OR EXACT]
        step2 --> step3[STEP 3: APPLY NUMERICAL SUBSTITUTION]
        step3 --> step4[STEP 4: SOLVE ITERATIVELY IF EXACT]
    end

    processBlock --> outputBlock[OUTPUT: YIELD IN PERCENT PER ANNUM]

    outputBlock --> usage1[INVESTMENT COMPARISON]
    outputBlock --> usage2[PORTFOLIO DECISIONS]
    outputBlock --> usage3[VALUATION MODELS]
    outputBlock --> usage4[RISK ASSESSMENT]

    style processBlock fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style outputBlock fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Differentiate between Current Yield and Yield to Maturity (YTM).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Current Yield (CY) | Yield to Maturity (YTM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Annual coupon income as a percentage of current market price | Total return (coupons + capital gain) if held till maturity, expressed as a rate |
| **Formula** | $CY = (C / P) \times 100$ | $YTM = \dfrac{C + \dfrac{F-P}{n}}{\dfrac{F+P}{2}} \times 100$ |
| **Capital Gain** | Ignored | Included (spread over `n` years) |
| **Use Case** | Quick income comparison | Total return assessment |

(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for distinction, 1 for formula)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Explain the Fisher Equation for real yield calculation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Fisher Equation**, given by economist Irving Fisher, relates the *nominal interest rate (i)*, the *real interest rate (r)*, and the *expected inflation rate (π)* as:

$$1 + r = \frac{1 + i}{1 + \pi}$$

Solving for real yield:
$$r = \frac{1 + i}{1 + \pi} - 1$$

The approximation $r \approx i - \pi$ is used when inflation is small. It helps investors determine the **actual purchasing-power gain** from an investment after stripping out the inflationary erosion of money. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice Option**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** A ₹1,000 face value bond pays a 10% annual coupon and has 5 years to maturity. If the current market price is ₹950, calculate:
>   (i) Current Yield
>   (ii) Approximate Yield to Maturity (YTM) using the average price formula
>   (iii) Comment on whether this is a premium, discount, or par bond. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** Define Yield to Call (YTC). A 5-year bond with a 9% annual coupon (Face Value ₹1,000) is callable at ₹1,050 at the end of year 3. If the current market price is ₹980, calculate the approximate YTC. **(7 marks)**

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- Face Value $F = ₹1{,}000$
- Annual Coupon Rate = 10% $\Rightarrow C = ₹100$
- Market Price $P = ₹950$
- Maturity $n = 5$ years

**(i) Current Yield [2 marks]:**

$$CY = \frac{C}{P} \times 100 = \frac{100}{950} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{CY = 10.53\%}$$

**(ii) Approximate YTM [4 marks]:**

$$YTM = \frac{C + \dfrac{F - P}{n}}{\dfrac{F + P}{2}} \times 100$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 100 + \frac{1000 - 950}{5} \\
&= 100 + \frac{50}{5} = 100 + 10 = 110 \\[6pt]
\text{Denominator} &= \frac{1000 + 950}{2} = 975 \\[6pt]
YTM &= \frac{110}{975} \times 100 = 11.282\%
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{YTM \approx 11.28\%}$$

**(iii) Bond Type Comment [1 mark]:**

Since $P = ₹950 < F = ₹1{,}000$, the bond is selling at a **DISCOUNT**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** YTM (11.28%) > Coupon Rate (10%) ✓ — *Consistent with the Price-Yield Theorem for a discount bond.*

---

#### **Part (b) — [7 marks]**

**Definition [2 marks]:**
**Yield to Call (YTC)** is the total return an investor earns if a callable bond is *redeemed by the issuer* at the *first call date*, assuming the bond is bought at the current market price and all coupons are received until the call.

**Given:**
- Face Value $F = ₹1{,}000$
- Annual Coupon Rate = 9% $\Rightarrow C = ₹90$
- Call Price $CP = ₹1{,}050$
- Years to Call $t_c = 3$ years
- Current Price $P = ₹980$

**Approximate YTC [5 marks]:**

$$\begin{aligned}
YTC &= \frac{C + \dfrac{CP - P}{t_c}}{\dfrac{CP + P}{2}} \times 100 \\
\text{Numerator} &= 90 + \frac{1050 - 980}{3} \\
&= 90 + \frac{70}{3} \\
&= 90 + 23.33 = 113.33 \\[6pt]
\text{Denominator} &= \frac{1050 + 980}{2} = \frac{2030}{2} = 1015 \\[6pt]
YTC &= \frac{113.33}{1015} \times 100 = 11.166\%
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{YTC \approx 11.17\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The YTC (11.17%) is **higher** than the YTM because the issuer paid a ₹50 premium on early redemption. This makes the bond *risky* for the issuer (likely to be called) but *attractive* for the buyer.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice Option**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Explain the relationship between bond price and yield. Using a numerical example, show how a price decrease from ₹1,000 to ₹950 (for a 10% coupon bond with 5 years maturity) changes the YTM. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** The nominal yield on a government bond is 7.5% and the expected inflation rate is 4%. Calculate:
>   (i) The exact real yield using the Fisher Equation.
>   (ii) The approximate real yield.
>   (iii) Comment on the difference between the two. **(7 marks)**

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — [7 marks]**

**Theoretical Explanation [3 marks]:**
The **Price-Yield Relationship** is *inverse* — as bond price rises, yield falls, and vice-versa. This is because yield is the return earned on the price *paid*, so a higher price spreads the same coupon over a larger base, reducing the percentage return.

**Numerical Demonstration [4 marks]:**

**Case 1: At Par (P = F = ₹1,000):**
$$CY = YTM = \text{Coupon Rate} = 10\%$$

**Case 2: At Discount (P = ₹950, n = 5):**

$$\begin{aligned}
YTM &= \frac{100 + \frac{1000 - 950}{5}}{\frac{1000 + 950}{2}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{110}{975} \times 100 = 11.28\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Scenario | Price | YTM |
|---|---|---|
| At Par | ₹1,000 | 10.00% |
| At Discount | ₹950 | 11.28% |

> A **price drop of 5%** led to a **YTM increase of 1.28 percentage points** — confirming the inverse relationship.

---

#### **Part (b) — [7 marks]**

**Given:** Nominal yield $i = 7.5\% = 0.075$, Inflation $\pi = 4\% = 0.04$.

**(i) Exact Real Yield using Fisher Equation [3 marks]:**

$$\begin{aligned}
1 + r &= \frac{1 + i}{1 + \pi} = \frac{1.075}{1.04} \\
1 + r &= 1.03365 \\
r &= 0.03365
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{r_{\text{exact}} = 3.37\%}$$

**(ii) Approximate Real Yield [2 marks]:**

$$r_{\text{approx}} = i - \pi = 7.5\% - 4\% = 3.5\%$$

$$\boxed{r_{\text{approx}} = 3.50\%}$$

**(iii) Comment on the Difference [2 marks]:**
The approximate formula gives 3.50% while the exact formula gives 3.37% — a difference of **0.13 percentage points** (13 basis points). The approximation $i - \pi$ *overstates* the real yield because it ignores the cross-term $i\pi$. The exact Fisher Equation is mathematically more rigorous and is the preferred method when inflation is high (>5%) or when financial precision is required.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Yield Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by 100** — Students compute `C/P` as a decimal (0.1053) and forget to convert to percentage. **Always end with × 100.**
>
> 2. **Using F instead of P in Current Yield** — Current Yield uses *Price Paid (P)*, NOT Face Value. Many students mistakenly use $C/F$.
>
> 3. **Confusing CY and YTM formulas** — CY = $C/P$ (no average), YTM = average-price approximation formula. The denominator for YTM is $(F+P)/2$, not just $P$.
>
> 4. **Sign error in capital gain** — If $P > F$ (premium), the capital gain is *negative* (loss). Use $F - P$, NOT $P - F$. Result will naturally come out negative.
>
> 5. **Not labeling units** — Write "11.28% p.a." — not just "11.28". Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing unit.
>
> 6. **Mixing YTM and YTC** — In a callable bond, calculate YTM *only if asked*. The default for a callable bond in KTU is **YTC**, not YTM.
>
> 7. **Premium/Discount confusion** — Always check: $P > F$ (premium) vs $P < F$ (discount) vs $P = F$ (par). State the type explicitly for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — YIELD (Module 2)**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** Yield = Return on investment as a % of price paid, encompassing both income and capital gain.
>
> ✅ **Current Yield (CY) Formula:** $CY = (C / P) \times 100$ — annual income vs. price only.
>
> ✅ **YTM Approximation Formula:** $YTM = \dfrac{C + \dfrac{F - P}{n}}{\dfrac{F + P}{2}} \times 100$ — uses **average price** as denominator.
>
> ✅ **YTM Exact Formula:** $P = C \cdot \left[\dfrac{1 - (1+y)^{-n}}{y}\right] + \dfrac{F}{(1+y)^n}$ — solved iteratively for `y`.
>
> ✅ **YTC Formula:** Same as YTM but substitute **Call Price (CP)** for **F** and **years to call ($t_c$)** for **n**.
>
> ✅ **Fisher Equation (Real Yield):** $r = \dfrac{1+i}{1+\pi} - 1$ ; Approximation: $r \approx i - \pi$.
>
> ✅ **Price-Yield Theorem:** Price ↑ ⇒ Yield ↓ ; Price ↓ ⇒ Yield ↑. **Always inverse.**
>
> ✅ **Bond Type Rules:**
>     - **Discount Bond** ($P < F$): YTM > Coupon Rate
>     - **Premium Bond** ($P > F$): YTM < Coupon Rate
>     - **Par Bond** ($P = F$): YTM = Coupon Rate
>
> ✅ **Yield Order Hierarchy:**
>     - **Discount:** YTM > CY > Coupon
>     - **Premium:** Coupon > CY > YTM
>     - **Par:** Coupon = CY = YTM
>
> ✅ **Standard KTU Conventions:** Face Value ₹1,000, Annual Compounding, Annual Coupon, Yields in % p.a.
>
> ✅ **Always label:** Mention bond type (premium/discount/par) for full marks; write units as % p.a.
>
> ✅ **YTC > YTM Rule:** For a callable bond, YTC is usually higher than YTM because the issuer pays a call premium — this creates *reinvestment risk* for the bondholder.
>
> ✅ **Real Yield Sanity Check:** Real yield must be LESS than nominal yield (assuming positive inflation). If your answer shows otherwise, recalculate.
>
> ✅ **Approximation Validity:** The YTM approximation formula is most accurate when $P$ is close to $F$ and $n$ is small. For long-dated bonds, the exact (iterative) method is preferred.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bond Yield Measures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Bond Yield Measures # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **bond yield measure** is a quantitative metric that expresses the *rate of return* an investor earns on a bond investment, normalized for time and price, under specific assumptions about the holding period, reinvestment of coupons, and terminal sale (or redemption) value. Unlike the bond's *coupon rate* (which is fixed at issuance) or its *price* (which fluctuates daily in the secondary market), a yield measure provides a **standardized, comparable performance indicator** across bonds of different coupons, maturities, and credit qualities. The most prominent yield measures in the KTU 2024 OEIET832 syllabus are: 1. **Current Yield (CY)** 2. **Yield to Maturity (YTM)** 3. **Yield to Call (YTC)** 4. **Realized Compound Yield (RCY) / Holding Period Yield** 5. **Bond Equivalent Yield (BEY)** 6. **Effective Annual Yield (EAY)** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** Yield is *not* the same as coupon rate. The coupon rate is a *face-value* percentage fixed at issuance, while yield is a *market-driven* return measure that reflects the bond's current price, remaining cash flows, and assumed holding period. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) Imagine you bought a **rental flat for ₹50 Lakhs** that throws off a fixed **₹3 Lakhs per year** in rent (a "coupon"). You would naturally ask: *"What is my effective rate of return on the ₹50 Lakhs I tied up?"* That answer is the **current yield** — it ignores the fact that you'll eventually get your capital back when you sell the flat. Now, suppose you also expect the flat to *appreciate* from ₹50L to ₹55L over 5 years. The **yield to maturity (YTM)** answers: *"What single, constant annual rate equates today's price to all my future rents + the future sale price?"* It's the IRR (Internal Rate of Return) of the bond. > [!NOTE] > **The Bond Yield Family — Quick Mental Map:** > - **Current Yield** → "Return from coupons alone, relative to today's price." > - **YTM** → "Total return if I hold this bond till it matures and reinvest every coupon at the same rate." > - **YTC** → "Same as YTM, but the company *calls* (retires) the bond early at a pre-agreed price." > - **Realized Yield** → "What I actually earned over a specific past period." > - **BEY / EAY** → "Standardization tools to compare bonds paying semi-annual vs. annual coupons." ## 1.3 Bond Pricing Recap (Foundation) The **present value (PV)** of a bond is: $$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+y)^t} + \frac{F}{(1+y)^n}$$ where $C$ is the periodic coupon, $F$ is the face value, $n$ is the number of periods, and $y$ is the **discount rate per period**. The yield is precisely the discount rate $y$ that makes the PV of the bond's cash flows equal to its observed market price $P_0$. Three critical relationships govern bond pricing: - If $C < y \cdot F$ → **Discount Bond** ($P_0 < F$); YTM > Coupon Rate - If $C = y \cdot F$ → **Par Bond** ($P_0 = F$); YTM = Coupon Rate - If $C > y \cdot F$ → **Premium Bond** ($P_0 > F$); YTM < Coupon Rate # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Logical Breakdown of Each Yield Measure ### A. Current Yield (CY) The **simplest** yield measure. It answers: *"If I buy this bond today at market price $P_0$ and hold it for exactly one year (collecting one coupon but ignoring any price change), what is my income return?"* - **Why it matters:** Quick screening tool for income-oriented investors. - **Limitation:** Ignores capital gains/losses and time value of future coupons. ### B. Yield to Maturity (YTM) The **most important** yield measure in academia and practice. It is the **internal rate of return (IRR)** of the bond — the single discount rate $y$ that equates the present value of all remaining cash flows (coupons + face value) to the current market price. - **Why it matters:** Standard benchmark for comparing bonds of different coupons and maturities. - **Implicit assumption:** All coupons are reinvested at the YTM itself (often unrealistic). ### C. Yield to Call (YTC) Bonds with a **call option** allow the issuer to retire the bond early at a pre-specified **call price** (typically > face value in early years, declining to face value at the call date). YTC solves the same IRR equation, but with: - Periods running only until the **first call date** $T_c$ (or whichever call date gives the lowest YTC — that is the *worst-case* yield for the investor). - Terminal cash flow replaced by the **call price** $F_c$ instead of face value $F$. ### D. Realized Compound Yield (RCY) / Holding Period Yield The **actual historical return** earned over a specific past period, accounting for: - Coupon income received - Sale price at the end of the period - **Compounding** of reinvested coupons (not just simple averaging) ### E. Bond Equivalent Yield (BEY) Most U.S. corporate and Treasury bonds pay **semi-annual coupons**. To compare them with annual-pay bonds (e.g., many Eurobonds), analysts compute BEY by simply doubling the semi-annual yield: $$BEY = 2 \times y_{\text{semi-annual}}$$ ### F. Effective Annual Yield (EAY) BEY ignores compounding *within* the year. EAY incorporates it: $$EAY = \left(1 + \frac{y_{\text{semi-annual}}}{2}\right)^2 - 1$$ For annual-pay bonds, $EAY = y$ directly. **EAY > BEY** always. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | **Measure** | **Formula** | **Key Inputs** | **Use Case** | |---|---|---|---| | Current Yield (CY) | $\displaystyle CY = \frac{C}{P_0}$ | Annual coupon $C$, Price $P_0$ | Quick income screening | | YTM (Implicit) | $\displaystyle P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+y)^t} + \frac{F}{(1+y)^n}$ | Solve for $y$ | Full holding-period IRR | | YTM (Approximation) | $\displaystyle YTM \approx \frac{C + \frac{F - P_0}{n}}{\frac{F + P_0}{2}}$ | $C, F, P_0, n$ | Quick estimate (Bond-lab formula) | | Yield to Call (YTC) | $\displaystyle P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{T_c} \frac{C}{(1+y_c)^t} + \frac{F_c}{(1+y_c)^{T_c}}$ | Solve for $y_c$ | Callable bonds | | Holding Period Return (Simple) | $\displaystyle HPR = \frac{C_t + (P_1 - P_0)}{P_0}$ | Coupons + price change | Past period evaluation | | Realized Compound Yield | $\displaystyle RCY = \left(\frac{C \cdot s_{\overline{n}\vert i} + P_n}{P_0}\right)^{1/n} - 1$ | Reinvested coupons | Realistic historical return | | Bond Equivalent Yield | $\displaystyle BEY = 2 \cdot y_{\text{semi}}$ | Semi-annual yield | Standardize U.S. bonds | | Effective Annual Yield | $\displaystyle EAY = \left(1 + \frac{y_{\text{semi}}}{2}\right)^{2} - 1$ | Semi-annual yield | True annualized comparison | > [!NOTE] > **Notation Tip:** In $s_{\overline{n}\vert i}$ (the annuity-immediate factor), the vertical bar is part of standard actuarial notation — not a markdown table separator. When written inside a table cell, prefer the explicit fraction form $\frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ to avoid rendering issues. ## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Yield Measures - **Portfolio Managers** use YTM as the primary metric for bond immunization and duration matching. - **Credit Analysts** track YTM *spreads* (YTM minus a risk-free benchmark like the 10-year G-Sec yield) to price credit risk. - **Treasury Issuers** monitor their **Weighted Average Yield** at auctions to gauge borrowing costs. - **Retail Investors** rely on Current Yield and BEY for apples-to-apples comparison of FDs vs. bonds. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Current Yield (CY) **Starting Point:** Income return on a single year, no capital gains/losses considered. $$\text{Income Return} = \frac{\text{Annual Coupon Income}}{\text{Invested Capital}} = \frac{C}{P_0}$$ This is essentially the **dividend yield formula** applied to bonds. There is no closed-form derivation beyond this definition, but the intuition is identical to the "rental flat" analogy in §1.2. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: YTM via Bond-Lab Approximation The **exact YTM** has no closed-form algebraic solution (it requires iterative methods like Newton-Raphson). However, the following **approximation** is widely used in KTU numerical problems and on the CFA/FRM exams: $$YTM \approx \frac{C + \frac{F - P_0}{n}}{\frac{F + P_0}{2}}$$ **Why this works (intuitive derivation):** The numerator is the **average annual income** (coupon $C$ + capital gain amortized linearly over $n$ years: $\frac{F-P_0}{n}$). The denominator is the **average capital invested** (average of price paid $P_0$ and final redemption $F$). Thus, the approximation is just a simple **arithmetic mean return** on average invested capital. ## 3.3 Worked Example 1 (Current Yield + YTM Approximation) > **[KTU Numerical Pattern — Dec 2024 Style]** > A 10-year bond with **Face Value ₹1,000**, **Annual Coupon Rate 8%**, is trading at **₹950**. Calculate (a) Current Yield, (b) Approximate YTM, and (c) identify the bond type. ### Given: - $F = 1{,}000$ - Coupon rate = 8% → $C = 80$ per year - $P_0 = 950$ - $n = 10$ years ### (a) Current Yield: $$CY = \frac{C}{P_0} = \frac{80}{950} = 0.08421 = \mathbf{8.42\%}$$ ### (b) Approximate YTM: $$YTM \approx \frac{C + \frac{F - P_0}{n}}{\frac{F + P_0}{2}}$$ Substituting: $$YTM \approx \frac{80 + \frac{1{,}000 - 950}{10}}{\frac{1{,}000 + 950}{2}}$$ $$YTM \approx \frac{80 + \frac{50}{10}}{\frac{1{,}950}{2}}$$ $$YTM \approx \frac{80 + 5}{975}$$ $$YTM \approx \frac{85}{975} = 0.08718 = \mathbf{8.72\%}$$ ### (c) Bond Type: Since $P_0 = 950 < F = 1{,}000$, the bond trades at a **discount**. YTM (8.72%) > Coupon Rate (8%). ✓ Consistent with the discount-bond rule. ## 3.4 Worked Example 2 (Exact YTM — Semi-Annual Compounding) > **Pattern (commonly asked in KTU 14-mark problems):** > A 5-year bond, **Face Value ₹1,000, Coupon 10% per annum paid semi-annually**, is selling for **₹1,080**. Find the exact YTM (compounded semi-annually). ### Given: - $F = 1{,}000$, $C_{\text{semi}} = 50$ (i.e., $10\%/2 \times 1{,}000$) - $n = 10$ semi-annual periods - $P_0 = 1{,}080$ ### Equation: $$1{,}080 = \sum_{t=1}^{10} \frac{50}{(1+y)^t} + \frac{1{,}000}{(1+y)^{10}}$$ This is a **10th-degree polynomial** in $(1+y)$; we need numerical methods. By trial (or financial calculator): - Try $y = 4\%$ (semi-annual): PV of coupons = $50 \times \frac{1 - 1.04^{-10}}{0.04} = 50 \times 8.1109 = 405.54$; PV of face = $\frac{1{,}000}{1.04^{10}} = 675.56$; Total = **1,081.10** ≈ 1,080 ✓ So $y_{\text{semi}} \approx 4\%$ (more precisely 4.02%). **BEY** = $2 \times 4.02\% = \mathbf{8.04\%}$ **EAY** = $(1.0402)^2 - 1 = 1.0820 - 1 = \mathbf{8.20\%}$ > [!NOTE] > **Always convert to the stated compounding period first.** $y_{\text{semi}} = 4.02\% \rightarrow$ BEY = 8.04% → EAY = 8.20%. The relationship is always: $EAY > BEY > y_{\text{semi}}$. ## 3.5 Worked Example 3 (Yield to Call) > A 10-year, **8% annual coupon** bond is callable at **₹1,050** at the end of **year 5**. Current price **₹980**. Find YTC. ### Given: - $C = 80$, $F_c = 1{,}050$, $P_0 = 980$, $T_c = 5$ years ### Equation: $$980 = \sum_{t=1}^{5} \frac{80}{(1+y_c)^t} + \frac{1{,}050}{(1+y_c)^5}$$ Trial: $y_c = 9\%$ → PV coupons = $80 \times \frac{1 - 1.09^{-5}}{0.09} = 80 \times 3.8897 = 311.18$; PV call = $\frac{1{,}050}{1.09^5} = 682.00$; Total = **993.18** (too high). Trial: $y_c = 9.5\%$ → PV coupons = $80 \times 3.7796 = 302.37$; PV call = $\frac{1{,}050}{1.095^5} = 666.12$; Total = **968.49** (too low). Linear interpolation: $y_c \approx 9\% + \frac{993.18 - 980}{993.18 - 968.49} \times 0.5\% = 9\% + 0.267\% = \mathbf{9.27\%}$ This bond is **less attractive to investors** (YTC 9.27% looks fine, but the *worst-case* YTC — minimum over all call dates — is the relevant investor metric). ## 3.6 Worked Example 4 (Realized Compound Yield) > Mr. Sharma bought a 10-year, **₹1,000, 6% bond** at par 5 years ago. He reinvested coupons at **5% per annum**. He sells it now for **₹980**. Find the Realized Compound Yield. ### Step 1: Accumulated value of reinvested coupons (annuity): $AV = C \cdot s_{\overline{5}\vert 0.05} = 60 \times \frac{1.05^5 - 1}{0.05} = 60 \times 5.5256 = 331.54$ ### Step 2: Total terminal value: $TV = 331.54 + 980 = 1{,}311.54$ ### Step 3: Solve for $r$ (5-year realized compound yield): $$1{,}000 \cdot (1+r)^5 = 1{,}311.54 \implies (1+r)^5 = 1.31154$$ $$1+r = 1.31154^{0.2} = 1.0559 \implies r = \mathbf{5.59\%}$$ This is **lower** than the original YTM (≈6%), illustrating how **coupon reinvestment risk** can drag realized returns below promised yields. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Taxonomy of Bond Yield Measures (Mermaid Concept Map) ```mermaid graph TD A[Parent Concept: Bond Return Measures] --> B[Income-Based Measures] A --> C[Total-Return Measures] A --> D[Standardization Measures] B --> B1[Current Yield CY = C / P0] B1 --> B1a[Ignores capital gain] B1 --> B1b[Ignores time value] C --> C1[Yield to Maturity YTM] C --> C2[Yield to Call YTC] C --> C3[Realized Compound Yield RCY] C1 --> C1a[Coupon reinvested at YTM] C1 --> C1b[Hold till maturity] C2 --> C2a[Issuer retires early] C2 --> C2b[Call price replaces face value] C3 --> C3a[Actual past return] C3 --> C3b[Reinvested at observed rates] D --> D1[Bond Equivalent Yield BEY] D --> D2[Effective Annual Yield EAY] D1 --> D1a[BEY equals 2 x semi-annual YTM] D2 --> D2a[EAY equals compound annualization] D2a --> D2b[EAY always greater than BEY] ``` ## 4.2 Decision Flow — Which Yield to Use? ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Need to measure bond return] --> q1{Holding period known?} q1 -->|Yes, past period| RCY[Use Realized Compound Yield] q1 -->|No, planning to hold| q2{Will bond be called early?} q2 -->|Yes, likely| YTC[Compute YTC for all call dates
Choose the minimum] q2 -->|No / Unlikely| YTM[Compute YTM] YTM --> q3{Purpose of comparison?} q3 -->|Compare with annual-pay bonds| EAY[Convert to EAY] q3 -->|Quick income estimate| CY[Compute Current Yield] style start fill:#ffd54f,stroke:#333 style YTM fill:#81c784,stroke:#1b5e20 style YTC fill:#ff8a65,stroke:#bf360c style EAY fill:#4fc3f7,stroke:#01579b style CY fill:#ba68c8,stroke:#4a148c style RCY fill:#f06292,stroke:#880e4f ``` ## 4.3 Relationship Diagram — Coupon Rate vs. Current Yield vs. YTM ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Discount["DISCOUNT BOND: Price below Face"] D1[Coupon Rate 8%] D2[Current Yield 8.42%] D3[YTM 8.72%] D1 -->|plus capital gain| D2 D2 -->|plus time-value effect| D3 end subgraph Par["PAR BOND: Price equals Face"] P1[Coupon Rate 8%] P2[Current Yield 8%] P3[YTM 8%] P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 end subgraph Premium["PREMIUM BOND: Price above Face"] PM1[Coupon Rate 8%] PM2[Current Yield 7.41%] PM3[YTM 7.27%] PM1 -->|minus capital loss| PM2 PM2 -->|minus time-value effect| PM3 end ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Universal Rule (worth memorizing for KTU):** For any bond, the order is > - **Discount bond:** YTM > CY > Coupon Rate > - **Par bond:** YTM = CY = Coupon Rate > - **Premium bond:** Coupon Rate > CY > YTM # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define Yield to Maturity (YTM). Why is it considered the most comprehensive yield measure for a bond?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **YTM** is the internal rate of return earned by an investor who buys a bond at its current market price $P_0$ and holds it until maturity, receiving all scheduled coupon payments and the face value at maturity, with all coupons reinvested at the YTM rate itself. (2 marks) It is considered comprehensive because: (1) it incorporates the **time value of money** for all future cash flows; (2) it captures both **income return** (coupons) and **capital gain/loss** (difference between $P_0$ and $F$); and (3) it provides a **single comparable rate** across bonds of different coupons and maturities. (1 mark) ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Distinguish between Current Yield and Yield to Maturity with a suitable example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Current Yield (CY)** measures only the annual coupon income relative to the bond's current market price: $CY = C/P_0$. It ignores capital gains/losses and the timing of future cash flows. (1.5 marks) **Yield to Maturity (YTM)** is the discount rate that equates the present value of all future coupons plus the face value to the current price; it accounts for capital gains, time value of money, and reinvestment. (1.5 marks) **Example:** A bond with face value ₹1,000, 10% annual coupon, trading at ₹950 has CY = 10.53% but YTM ≈ 10.92% (slightly higher because of the capital gain at maturity). --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]` **(a)** A 6-year bond has a face value of **₹1,000** and an **annual coupon rate of 9%**. It is currently selling at **₹960**. - (i) Calculate the **Current Yield**. (2 marks) - (ii) Calculate the **approximate Yield to Maturity** using the Bond-lab formula. (4 marks) - (iii) Is this bond a premium, discount, or par bond? Justify. (1 mark) **(b)** Explain the concepts of **Yield to Call (YTC)** and **Realized Compound Yield (RCY)**. Under what circumstances is YTC more relevant than YTM? How does coupon reinvestment risk affect RCY? (7 marks) #### Model Solution (Question A) **(a)(i) Current Yield:** $$CY = \frac{C}{P_0} = \frac{0.09 \times 1{,}000}{960} = \frac{90}{960} = 0.09375 = \mathbf{9.375\%} \approx 9.38\%$$ **(a)(ii) Approximate YTM:** $$YTM \approx \frac{C + \frac{F - P_0}{n}}{\frac{F + P_0}{2}} = \frac{90 + \frac{1{,}000 - 960}{6}}{\frac{1{,}000 + 960}{2}}$$ $$YTM \approx \frac{90 + \frac{40}{6}}{\frac{1{,}960}{2}} = \frac{90 + 6.667}{980} = \frac{96.667}{980}$$ $$YTM \approx 0.09864 = \mathbf{9.86\%}$$ **(a)(iii) Classification:** Since $P_0 = 960 < F = 1{,}000$, this is a **discount bond**. This is consistent with YTM (9.86%) > Coupon Rate (9%) > Current Yield (9.38%) ... wait, here CY (9.38%) > Coupon Rate (9%) but < YTM (9.86%). The rule YTM > CY > Coupon holds. ✓ **(b) YTC vs. RCY:** **YTC (Yield to Call):** Applicable to *callable bonds* — bonds with an embedded option allowing the issuer to retire them before maturity at a pre-agreed call price $F_c$ (usually > $F$ in early call years). The YTC is the IRR over the period to the first call date $T_c$: $$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{T_c} \frac{C}{(1+y_c)^t} + \frac{F_c}{(1+y_c)^{T_c}}$$ YTC is more relevant than YTM when: - Interest rates have *fallen* below the coupon rate, making refinancing attractive to the issuer; - The bond is trading at a *premium* (callable bonds rarely trade much above par); - The bond is *near* its first call date. Investors focus on the **worst-case YTC** — the minimum YTC across all possible call dates (Texas Instruments BA II Plus method). **RCY (Realized Compound Yield):** The *actual* annual compound rate earned by an investor over a historical holding period, accounting for **reinvested coupon income** at observed market rates (not the YTM): $$RCY = \left(\frac{\text{Accumulated coupons} + \text{Sale Price}}{P_0}\right)^{1/n} - 1$$ **Coupon reinvestment risk:** YTM assumes coupons are reinvested at the YTM rate, which is rarely achievable in practice. If market rates fall, reinvested coupons earn less, and the **realized** yield will be **lower** than the originally promised YTM. The longer the holding period and the larger the coupon, the more severe this drag. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]` **(a)** What is the **Bond Equivalent Yield (BEY)**? A 3-year bond with **face value ₹1,000** and **12% annual coupon paid semi-annually** is quoted at a yield of **10% per annum (compounded semi-annually)**. Calculate (i) the **current price** of the bond, (ii) the **BEY**, and (iii) the **Effective Annual Yield (EAY)**. (7 marks) **(b)** Discuss the **relationship between coupon rate, current yield, and YTM** for premium, par, and discount bonds. Why does this relationship exist, and how does it relate to the *time-value* of future coupons? (7 marks) #### Model Solution (Question B) **(a) BEY, Price, and EAY:** Given: $F = 1{,}000$, $C_{\text{semi}} = 60$ (i.e., $12\%/2 \times 1{,}000$), $n = 6$ semi-annual periods, $y_{\text{semi}} = 5\%$. **(i) Current Price:** $$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{6} \frac{60}{1.05^t} + \frac{1{,}000}{1.05^6}$$ PV of coupons = $60 \times \frac{1 - 1.05^{-6}}{0.05} = 60 \times 5.0757 = 304.54$ PV of face = $\frac{1{,}000}{1.05^6} = \frac{1{,}000}{1.3401} = 746.22$ $$P_0 = 304.54 + 746.22 = \mathbf{₹1{,}050.76}$$ **(ii) Bond Equivalent Yield:** $$BEY = 2 \times y_{\text{semi}} = 2 \times 5\% = \mathbf{10\%}$$ **(iii) Effective Annual Yield:** $$EAY = (1 + 0.05)^2 - 1 = 1.1025 - 1 = 0.1025 = \mathbf{10.25\%}$$ The bond is a **premium bond** (Price > Face) because Coupon Rate (12%) > YTM (10%). **(b) Relationship Discussion:** | **Bond Type** | **Price vs Face** | **Inequality Chain** | **Economic Intuition** | |---|---|---|---| | **Discount** | $P_0 < F$ | YTM > CY > Coupon | Coupon "feels small" relative to price; investor is compensated by **capital gain** at maturity | | **Par** | $P_0 = F$ | YTM = CY = Coupon | Bond issued/reset so that all three measures align | | **Premium** | $P_0 > F$ | Coupon > CY > YTM | Coupon "feels large" relative to price; investor suffers a **capital loss** as price converges to face at maturity | **Why does CY ≠ YTM in general?** CY = $C / P_0$ is a *simple income ratio* on the *current* price, ignoring: - The **capital gain/loss** built into the bond's convergence to face value; - The **time value** of coupons received in future years (which are worth less than $C / n$ each year under compounding). YTM incorporates both effects by solving the full IRR equation. The difference $(YTM - CY)$ is larger for: - Bonds with **longer maturities** (more capital gain/loss to amortize); - Bonds with **larger discount/premium** (more pronounced capital adjustment). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:** > 1. **Confusing BEY and EAY:** BEY = $2 \times y_{\text{semi}}$ (simple doubling). EAY = $(1 + y_{\text{semi}}/2)^2 - 1$ (compounded). Many students write BEY when asked for EAY and lose 2-3 marks. > 2. **YTM unit mismatch:** If the coupon is paid *semi-annually*, the equation must be solved in *semi-annual* terms. The final answer must specify whether it is per-period, BEY, or EAY. Saying "YTM = 5%" for a semi-annual bond is *incomplete*. > 3. **Approximation vs. Exact YTM:** The Bond-lab approximation gives a value that may differ from the exact IRR by 5–15 basis points. If the question says *"calculate the YTM"* without "approximate", attempt the exact IRR via trial/interpolation, or state clearly that you are using the approximation. > 4. **YTC minimum rule:** When the question gives multiple call dates, compute YTC for *each* and report the **worst-case minimum** — not the maximum. > 5. **Forgetting reinvestment in RCY:** The realized compound yield formula requires the **accumulated value** of all coupons (compounded at the actual reinvestment rate), not just their simple sum. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist - ✅ **Current Yield (CY)** = $C / P_0$ → only captures income return; **always between coupon rate and YTM** (for non-par bonds). - ✅ **Yield to Maturity (YTM)** = IRR of the bond, solved from $P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+y)^t} + \frac{F}{(1+y)^n}$. - ✅ **Bond-lab YTM approximation** = $\frac{C + (F - P_0)/n}{(F + P_0)/2}$ — quick estimate, no calculator needed. - ✅ **Yield to Call (YTC)** = same as YTM but terminal cash flow is the **call price** at the **call date**; **investors use the worst-case (minimum) YTC**. - ✅ **Realized Compound Yield (RCY)** uses **observed reinvestment rates**, not YTM; coupon reinvestment risk is a real concern. - ✅ **BEY** = $2 \times y_{\text{semi-annual}}$ (linear scaling for comparison). - ✅ **EAY** = $(1 + y_{\text{semi}}/2)^2 - 1$ (compounded); **always EAY > BEY > $y_{\text{semi}}$**. - ✅ **Three-way rule:** Discount → YTM > CY > Coupon; Premium → Coupon > CY > YTM; Par → all equal. - ✅ **Compounding period discipline:** Always identify the period (annual, semi-annual, quarterly) before plugging into formulas. - ✅ **Worst-case YTC principle:** When issuers have multiple call options, the relevant YTC for the investor is the **lowest** YTC across all call dates. - ✅ **YTM > Coupon Rate** ↔ Discount bond; **YTM < Coupon Rate** ↔ Premium bond. - ✅ **Capital gain/loss effect** on yield: Discount bonds have positive capital gain → YTM pulled up; Premium bonds have capital loss → YTM pulled down.

topics.title: Yield to Maturity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Yield to Maturity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Yield to Maturity (YTM)** is the **internal rate of return (IRR)** earned by an investor who purchases a bond at its current market price and holds it until maturity, receiving all scheduled coupon payments and the face value repayment at the end. In equilibrium, YTM is precisely the discount rate that equates the present value of a bond's future cash flows (coupons + redemption) to its prevailing market price.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim from Standard Texts):**
> *"The yield to maturity of a bond is the single discount rate that, when applied to all of the bond's future cash flows (interest payments and principal repayment), makes the present value of those cash flows equal to the bond's current market price."*

### 1.2 The General Bond Pricing Equation

The fundamental relationship upon which YTM rests is:

$$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+y)^t} + \frac{F}{(1+y)^n}$$

Where every symbol must be understood precisely:

- $P_0$ = Current **market price** of the bond (₹)
- $C$ = Periodic **coupon payment** in ₹ = (Coupon Rate × Face Value) / Number of Coupons per Year
- $F$ = **Face value / Par value / Redemption value** of the bond (₹)
- $n$ = Number of **remaining coupon periods** until maturity
- $y$ = **Yield to maturity** per compounding period (expressed as a decimal)

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hike Back to Base Camp"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (The Mountain Hike Analogy):**
> Imagine a mountaineer (the investor) starting at a mountain's mid-camp (today's bond price $P_0$). Along the way up to the summit (maturity), they collect small rest-station supplies at every checkpoint (the periodic coupons $C$). Upon reaching the summit, they receive the final grand prize (the face value $F$). The **average annual altitude gain percentage** they achieved over the entire trek is the YTM. Even if some rest stations gave them extra snacks (high coupons) or they had to climb through fog (purchased at a discount), YTM captures the **single, consistent percentage pace** at which their investment grew every year to reach the promised summit payoff.

### 1.4 Bond Pricing Categories Based on YTM

| Market Condition | Coupon Rate vs YTM | Bond Price vs Par | Investor Experience |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Premium Bond** | Coupon Rate > YTM | $P_0 > F$ | Price falls toward par over time |
| **Par Bond** | Coupon Rate = YTM | $P_0 = F$ | Price stays flat at face value |
| **Discount Bond** | Coupon Rate < YTM | $P_0 < F$ | Price rises toward par over time |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** **"CUP"** — Coupon Up, Price Up (when yields fall, prices rise, and vice versa). This is the **inverse price-yield relationship**, a frequently tested KTU concept.

### 1.5 Why YTM Matters — Engineering & Financial Perspective

For a B.Tech student of any discipline, understanding YTM is essential because:

1. **Capital Budgeting Comparison**: YTM lets an investor compare a bond's return against the Internal Rate of Return (IRR) of a competing engineering/IT project.
2. **Cost of Debt**: Corporations use YTM to determine the after-tax cost of debt for Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC) calculations.
3. **Risk Benchmarking**: YTM embeds **default risk, interest-rate risk, and liquidity risk premiums** — making it a single-number summary of a bond's risk-reward.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations — Why YTM is the "True" Yield

Unlike the **current yield** (which only considers the annual coupon divided by price), YTM accounts for **three economic components** simultaneously:

1. **Coupon Income** — the periodic interest received.
2. **Capital Gain/Loss** — the difference between purchase price and face value realized at maturity.
3. **Time Value of Money** — proper discounting of each future cash flow.

### 2.2 The Three Yields Hierarchy

Let $CR$ = Coupon Rate, $CY$ = Current Yield, $YTM$ = Yield to Maturity.

- **Coupon Rate** = $\dfrac{C}{F}$ — fixed at issuance, never changes.
- **Current Yield** = $\dfrac{C}{P_0}$ — ignores capital gain/loss.
- **YTM** = Includes capital gain/loss + time-value adjustment.

The deterministic ranking, depending on price:

- If bond is at a **DISCOUNT** ($P_0 < F$): $CR < CY < YTM$
- If bond is at a **PREMIUM** ($P_0 > F$): $YTM < CY < CR$
- If bond is at **PAR** ($P_0 = F$): $CR = CY = YTM$

### 2.3 Assumption Stack Behind YTM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** KTU questions often require stating assumptions. Always list the following:
> 1. The bond is held until **exact maturity**.
> 2. All coupons are **reinvested at the same rate as YTM**.
> 3. Coupon payments are made at **regular intervals**.
> 4. The issuer **does not default** (full creditworthiness assumed).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C}{(1+y)^t} + \dfrac{F}{(1+y)^n}$ | Fundamental bond pricing identity | Find $P_0$ given $y$, or vice-versa |
| 2 | $CY = \dfrac{C}{P_0}$ | Current Yield | Quick yield indicator ignoring capital gain |
| 3 | $YTM \approx \dfrac{C + \dfrac{F - P_0}{n}}{\dfrac{F + P_0}{2}}$ | **Linear Approximation Formula** | Quick YTM estimate for MCQs (3-mark) |
| 4 | $\text{Realized Compound Yield} = (1 + YTM)^{1/m} - 1$ | Compounding to annual rate | Where $m$ = periods per year |
| 5 | $C = \dfrac{\text{Coupon Rate} \times F}{m}$ | Periodic coupon | Convert annual coupon rate to periodic payment |
| 6 | $P_0 > F \iff y < \text{Coupon Rate}$ | Premium rule | Identify bond type |
| 7 | $P_0 < F \iff y > \text{Coupon Rate}$ | Discount rule | Identify bond type |
| 8 | $P_0 = F \iff y = \text{Coupon Rate}$ | Par rule | Identify bond type |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Project Appraisal**: A civil engineering firm evaluating a 10-year infra bond uses YTM to decide whether the bond's expected return beats the firm's MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return).
- **Loan Pricing**: Banks compute the YTM on government securities to determine the benchmark lending rate (the MCLR in India is anchored to G-Sec yields).
- **Risk Management**: The **duration** of a bond (a sensitivity measure) is derived using YTM as the discount rate.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Bond Pricing Identity

The price of a bond is the present value of its contractual future cash flows. Each coupon $C$ occurs at the end of periods $t = 1, 2, 3, \dots, n$. The face value $F$ is paid at the end of period $n$ along with the final coupon.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0 &= \underbrace{\frac{C}{(1+y)^1}}_{\text{Period 1 coupon}} + \underbrace{\frac{C}{(1+y)^2}}_{\text{Period 2 coupon}} + \dots + \underbrace{\frac{C}{(1+y)^n}}_{\text{Period n coupon}} + \underbrace{\frac{F}{(1+y)^n}}_{\text{Redemption}} \\[6pt]
P_0 &= \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+y)^t} + \frac{F}{(1+y)^n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $y$ is the unique discount rate that exactly equates the right-hand side (PV of all future cash flows) with the observed left-hand side (market price $P_0$).

### 3.2 Derivation of the Approximation Formula

The approximation formula is derived by assuming that the bond's cash flows behave like an **annuity of average magnitude**, then dividing by the average invested capital.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Approximate Capital Gain per year} &= \frac{F - P_0}{n} \\[4pt]
\text{Total Average Annual Return} &= \text{Coupon} + \text{Capital Gain component} \\[4pt]
&= C + \frac{F - P_0}{n} \\[6pt]
\text{Average Invested Capital} &= \frac{F + P_0}{2} \\[6pt]
\therefore \quad YTM &\approx \frac{C + \dfrac{F - P_0}{n}}{\dfrac{F + P_0}{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This formula assumes a **straight-line amortization** of the premium or discount, which is an approximation. The actual YTM (computed by trial-and-error or financial calculator) is mathematically exact.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Pattern

**Problem:** A 5-year bond has a face value of **₹1,000**, an annual coupon rate of **10%** (paid yearly), and is currently trading at **₹950**. Compute the YTM.

**Step 1: Identify the parameters**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \text{₹1,000} \\
CR &= 10\% \text{ annually} \\
C &= CR \times F = 0.10 \times 1000 = \text{₹100} \\
P_0 &= \text{₹950} \\
n &= 5 \text{ years} \\
y &= ?
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Set up the bond pricing equation**

$$
950 = \frac{100}{(1+y)^1} + \frac{100}{(1+y)^2} + \frac{100}{(1+y)^3} + \frac{100}{(1+y)^4} + \frac{1100}{(1+y)^5}
$$

**Step 3: Trial-and-error approach — Test $y = 11\%$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= \frac{100}{1.11} + \frac{100}{(1.11)^2} + \frac{100}{(1.11)^3} + \frac{100}{(1.11)^4} + \frac{1100}{(1.11)^5} \\
&= 90.09 + 81.17 + 73.13 + 65.88 + 682.01 \\
&= \text{₹992.28}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $992.28 > 950$, the assumed yield of 11% is **too low**. We need a higher yield to bring the price down.

**Step 4: Test $y = 12\%$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= \frac{100}{1.12} + \frac{100}{(1.12)^2} + \frac{100}{(1.12)^3} + \frac{100}{(1.12)^4} + \frac{1100}{(1.12)^5} \\
&= 89.29 + 79.72 + 71.18 + 63.55 + 623.17 \\
&= \text{₹926.91}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $926.91 < 950$, the assumed yield of 12% is **too high**.

**Step 5: Linear Interpolation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= y_L + \frac{P_L - P_0}{P_L - P_H} \times (y_H - y_L) \\
y &= 11\% + \frac{992.28 - 950}{992.28 - 926.91} \times (12\% - 11\%) \\
y &= 11\% + \frac{42.28}{65.37} \times 1\% \\
y &= 11\% + 0.6467\% \\
y &\approx \mathbf{11.65\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Verify using the Approximation Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
YTM &\approx \frac{C + \dfrac{F - P_0}{n}}{\dfrac{F + P_0}{2}} \\
&= \frac{100 + \dfrac{1000 - 950}{5}}{\dfrac{1000 + 950}{2}} \\
&= \frac{100 + 10}{975} \\
&= \frac{110}{975} \\
&\approx \mathbf{11.28\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The approximate YTM (11.28%) is close to the exact YTM (11.65%). The small difference arises because the approximation uses straight-line amortization, whereas the exact value accounts for compounding.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Discount, Par, and Premium Scenarios

| Scenario | Price $P_0$ | Coupon Rate | YTM | Capital Gain/Loss | Sign of $(F-P_0)/n$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Discount Bond** | ₹900 | 8% | > 8% (say 11.65%) | Gain (₹100 over 5 yrs) | Positive |
| **Par Bond** | ₹1000 | 10% | = 10% | None | Zero |
| **Premium Bond** | ₹1100 | 12% | < 12% (say 10.91%) | Loss (₹100 over 5 yrs) | Negative |

### 3.5 Comprehensive Example — Premium Bond

**Problem:** A bond with $F = \text{₹1000}$, annual coupon rate = **12%**, $n = 3$ years, current price $P_0 = \text{₹1050}$. Compute YTM.

**Trial $y = 10\%$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= \frac{120}{1.10} + \frac{120}{(1.10)^2} + \frac{1120}{(1.10)^3} \\
&= 109.09 + 99.17 + 842.97 \\
&= \text{₹1051.23}
\end{aligned}
$$

Very close to ₹1050. So YTM $\approx$ **10.04%** by interpolation. Since the bond is at a premium ($P_0 > F$), YTM (10.04%) is correctly **less than** the coupon rate (12%).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — YTM Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Bond Inputs] --> B[Collect F, Coupon Rate, P0, n]
    B --> C{Is P0 equal to F?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Par Bond: YTM equals Coupon Rate]
    C -- No --> E{Is P0 greater than F?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Premium Bond: YTM is less than Coupon Rate]
    E -- No --> G[Discount Bond: YTM is greater than Coupon Rate]
    F --> H[Apply Trial and Error Method]
    G --> H[Apply Trial and Error Method]
    D --> I[Final YTM Value]
    H --> J{Trial Price matches P0?}
    J -- Yes --> I
    J -- No --> K[Adjust Yield Estimate]
    K --> H
    I --> L[Report YTM to Investor]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — YTM as Bond Risk-Return Aggregator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs ["Bond Inputs"]
        faceV[Face Value F]
        coupon[Coupon Rate]
        priceNow[Current Price P0]
        timeN[Time to Maturity n]
    end

    subgraph YTMEngine ["YTM Calculation Engine"]
        engine[Bond Pricing Identity]
        approx[Approximation Formula]
        trial[Trial and Error Solver]
    end

    subgraph Outputs ["Yield Outputs"]
        outYTM[Yield to Maturity YTM]
        outCY[Current Yield]
        capGain[Capital Gain Component]
    end

    subgraph Risk ["Risk Embedding"]
        defaultR[Default Risk Premium]
        intR[Interest Rate Risk]
        liqR[Liquidity Risk Premium]
    end

    faceV --> engine
    coupon --> engine
    priceNow --> engine
    timeN --> engine
    engine --> outYTM
    faceV --> approx
    coupon --> approx
    priceNow --> approx
    timeN --> approx
    approx --> outYTM
    engine --> trial
    trial --> outYTM
    outYTM --> outCY
    outYTM --> capGain
    defaultR --> outYTM
    intR --> outYTM
    liqR --> outYTM
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Relationship Between Three Yields

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Yield Comparison for a Bond] --> ctype{What is the Bond Type?}
    ctype -- Premium P0 greater than F --> prem[YTM is less than Current Yield is less than Coupon Rate]
    ctype -- Par P0 equals F --> par[All three yields are equal]
    ctype -- Discount P0 less than F --> disc[Coupon Rate is less than Current Yield is less than YTM]
    prem --> end1[Investor experiences Capital Loss]
    par --> end2[Investor experiences No Capital Change]
    disc --> end3[Investor experiences Capital Gain]
```

### 4.4 Cash Flow Timeline (Visual Block Representation)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Bond Cash Flow Timeline (5-Year Bond, 10% Coupon)
    dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD
    axisFormat %Y
    section Coupon Payments
    Coupon Year 1 :a1, 2025-01-01, 365d
    Coupon Year 2 :a2, 2025-01-01, 365d
    Coupon Year 3 :a3, 2025-01-01, 365d
    Coupon Year 4 :a4, 2025-01-01, 365d
    Coupon Year 5 :a5, 2025-01-01, 365d
    section Redemption
    Face Value F :milestone, 2025-01-01, 0d
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
**Define Yield to Maturity (YTM). What does it represent to a bond investor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Yield to Maturity (YTM) is the total annualized return an investor earns if a bond is purchased at its current market price and held until the bond's maturity date, with all coupon payments reinvested at the same rate. Mathematically, it is the discount rate $y$ that satisfies:

$$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+y)^t} + \frac{F}{(1+y)^n}$$

YTM represents the bond's **internal rate of return (IRR)** and is the most comprehensive measure of a bond's profitability because it accounts for coupon income, capital gain or loss at maturity, and the time value of money. *(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for investor interpretation.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between Current Yield and Yield to Maturity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Current Yield | Yield to Maturity |
|---|---|---|
| **Formula** | $CY = C / P_0$ | IRR from full cash flow equation |
| **Accounts for Capital Gain/Loss** | No | Yes |
| **Time Value of Money** | Not applied | Fully applied |
| **Use Case** | Quick yield snapshot | Comprehensive total return measure |

Current yield only considers the annual coupon relative to the current price, while YTM considers all coupons, the redemption value, and discounts them appropriately. Hence, for a discount bond, **YTM > Current Yield**, and for a premium bond, **YTM < Current Yield**. *(3 marks — table distinction 2 marks, conceptual inference 1 mark.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and is internally divided into sub-parts (typically (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks). Choose **ONE** of the two alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Pattern]*

**(a)** A 6-year bond has a face value of **₹1,000** and an annual coupon rate of **9%**. The bond is currently selling at **₹920**. Compute the Yield to Maturity using:
   - (i) The approximation formula.
   - (ii) Trial-and-error with linear interpolation.

**(b)** Suppose the same bond is now priced at **₹1,050**. Determine the new YTM using the approximation formula. Comment on why the YTM has changed.

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Sub-part (i): Approximation Formula**

Given: $F = \text{₹1000}$, $CR = 9\%$, $C = \text{₹90}$, $P_0 = \text{₹920}$, $n = 6$ years.

$$
\begin{aligned}
YTM &\approx \frac{C + \dfrac{F - P_0}{n}}{\dfrac{F + P_0}{2}} \\
&= \frac{90 + \dfrac{1000 - 920}{6}}{\dfrac{1000 + 920}{2}} \\
&= \frac{90 + \dfrac{80}{6}}{960} \\
&= \frac{90 + 13.33}{960} \\
&= \frac{103.33}{960} \\
&\approx 0.1076 = \mathbf{10.76\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (a) — Sub-part (ii): Trial-and-Error**

Equation to solve:

$$
920 = \frac{90}{(1+y)^1} + \frac{90}{(1+y)^2} + \dots + \frac{90}{(1+y)^6} + \frac{1000}{(1+y)^6}
$$

**Trial $y = 10\%$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= 90 \times \left[\frac{1 - (1.10)^{-6}}{0.10}\right] + \frac{1000}{(1.10)^6} \\
&= 90 \times 4.3553 + 564.47 \\
&= 391.98 + 564.47 = \text{₹956.45}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since ₹956.45 > ₹920, the yield is too low.

**Trial $y = 11\%$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= 90 \times \left[\frac{1 - (1.11)^{-6}}{0.11}\right] + \frac{1000}{(1.11)^6} \\
&= 90 \times 4.2305 + 534.64 \\
&= 380.75 + 534.64 = \text{₹915.39}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since ₹915.39 < ₹920, the yield is slightly too high.

**Interpolation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= 10\% + \frac{956.45 - 920}{956.45 - 915.39} \times 1\% \\
y &= 10\% + \frac{36.45}{41.06} \times 1\% \\
y &= 10\% + 0.8877\% \\
y &\approx \mathbf{10.89\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b): Premium Bond at ₹1,050**

$$
\begin{aligned}
YTM &\approx \frac{90 + \dfrac{1000 - 1050}{6}}{\dfrac{1000 + 1050}{2}} \\
&= \frac{90 - 8.33}{1025} \\
&= \frac{81.67}{1025} \\
&\approx 0.0797 = \mathbf{7.97\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** When the bond's price rose from ₹920 (discount) to ₹1,050 (premium), the YTM fell from approximately 10.89% to 7.97%. This demonstrates the **inverse relationship between bond prices and yields**. As the price rises above par, the YTM falls below the coupon rate, and the investor experiences a capital loss at maturity. *(Full 14 marks: a-i=3 marks, a-ii=4 marks, b=5 marks, comment=2 marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern]*

**(a)** Explain the relationship between coupon rate, current yield, and yield to maturity for a **discount bond**, a **par bond**, and a **premium bond**. Provide numerical examples to illustrate each case.

**(b)** A bond has the following characteristics: Face value = **₹5,000**, Annual coupon rate = **12%**, Maturity = **4 years**, Current market price = **₹4,800**. Calculate:
   - (i) Current Yield
   - (ii) Approximate YTM
   - (iii) The capital gain component per year

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a): Yield Relationships**

For each bond type, the ranking of the three yields is determined by the relationship between $P_0$ and $F$.

**Discount Bond** ($P_0 < F$): Example — $F = \text{₹1000}$, Coupon = 8%, $P_0 = \text{₹900}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Coupon Rate} &= 8\% \\
\text{Current Yield} &= \frac{80}{900} = 8.89\% \\
YTM &\approx \frac{80 + 100/5}{(1000+900)/2} = \frac{100}{950} = 10.53\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Order: **Coupon Rate (8%) < Current Yield (8.89%) < YTM (10.53%)**

**Par Bond** ($P_0 = F$): Example — $F = \text{₹1000}$, Coupon = 10%, $P_0 = \text{₹1000}$.

$$
\text{Coupon Rate} = \text{Current Yield} = YTM = 10\%
$$

**Premium Bond** ($P_0 > F$): Example — $F = \text{₹1000}$, Coupon = 12%, $P_0 = \text{₹1100}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Coupon Rate} &= 12\% \\
\text{Current Yield} &= \frac{120}{1100} = 10.91\% \\
YTM &\approx \frac{120 - 100/5}{(1000+1100)/2} = \frac{100}{1050} = 9.52\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Order: **YTM (9.52%) < Current Yield (10.91%) < Coupon Rate (12%)**

**Part (b): Numerical Computation**

Given: $F = \text{₹5000}$, $CR = 12\%$, $n = 4$ years, $P_0 = \text{₹4800}$.

**Sub-part (i): Current Yield**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 0.12 \times 5000 = \text{₹600} \\
CY &= \frac{C}{P_0} = \frac{600}{4800} = \mathbf{12.50\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sub-part (ii): Approximate YTM**

$$
\begin{aligned}
YTM &\approx \frac{C + \dfrac{F - P_0}{n}}{\dfrac{F + P_0}{2}} \\
&= \frac{600 + \dfrac{5000 - 4800}{4}}{\dfrac{5000 + 4800}{2}} \\
&= \frac{600 + 50}{4900} \\
&= \frac{650}{4900} \\
&\approx \mathbf{13.27\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sub-part (iii): Capital Gain Component per Year**

$$
\text{Capital Gain per Year} = \frac{F - P_0}{n} = \frac{5000 - 4800}{4} = \frac{200}{4} = \mathbf{\text{₹50 per year}}
$$

This ₹50 represents the annual accretion of the bond's price from ₹4,800 toward ₹5,000 over 4 years. *(Full 14 marks: a=7 marks with examples, b-i=2 marks, b-ii=3 marks, b-iii=2 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add the final face value** to the last coupon payment when computing trial PVs. The terminal cash flow is $C + F$, not just $C$.
> 2. **Mixing up Current Yield and YTM** in definitions. Current Yield = $C/P_0$. YTM involves the full cash flow equation.
> 3. **Interpolation direction error**: When trial PV > market price, increase $y$; when trial PV < market price, decrease $y$. Many students do this backwards.
> 4. **Using annual compounding blindly**: If coupons are semi-annual, divide the annual coupon rate by 2 and double the number of periods. KTU questions sometimes test this.
> 5. **Confusing face value with market price** in the approximation formula's denominator — the denominator must be $(F + P_0)/2$, not just $F$.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Yield to Maturity**

- ✅ **YTM = Internal Rate of Return** of a bond held to maturity, assuming coupons are reinvested at YTM.
- ✅ **Bond Pricing Identity**: $P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C}{(1+y)^t} + \dfrac{F}{(1+y)^n}$
- ✅ **Approximation Formula**: $YTM \approx \dfrac{C + (F-P_0)/n}{(F+P_0)/2}$ — useful for quick MCQs.
- ✅ **Discount Bond**: $P_0 < F$, **YTM > Coupon Rate**, investor earns capital gain.
- ✅ **Premium Bond**: $P_0 > F$, **YTM < Coupon Rate**, investor incurs capital loss.
- ✅ **Par Bond**: $P_0 = F$, **YTM = Coupon Rate**, no capital gain/loss.
- ✅ **Inverse Price-Yield Relationship**: Price ↑ → Yield ↓ and vice versa.
- ✅ **Trial-and-Error**: Always bracket the YTM between two assumed rates, then use linear interpolation for higher accuracy.
- ✅ **Current Yield** is a partial measure; YTM is the comprehensive measure.
- ✅ **Assumptions**: Reinvestment at YTM, no default, regular coupon intervals, held to maturity.
- ✅ **Capital Gain Component** = $(F - P_0)/n$ — positive for discount bonds, negative for premium bonds, zero for par bonds.
- ✅ **Always add $F$ to the final coupon** when computing present value of the last period.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Macaulay Duration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Macaulay Duration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition
