**Macaulay Duration** is the **weighted-average time** (expressed in years) until a fixed-income investor recovers the entire price of a bond through its scheduled coupon payments and the final principal repayment. The weight assigned to each cash flow is the **share of the bond's total present value** that the cash flow contributes. It is measured in **units of time (years)**.

Mathematically, for a bond paying cash flows $CF_t$ at the end of years $t = 1, 2, \dots, n$, and discounted at the bond's Yield-To-Maturity (YTM) per period $y$:

$$D_{Mac} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} t \cdot w_t \quad \text{where} \quad w_t = \frac{PV(CF_t)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} PV(CF_i)}$$

The concept was introduced by Canadian economist **Frederick Macaulay** in **1938** in his seminal work *"The Movement of Interest Rates, Bond Yields and Stock Prices in the United States Since 1856."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Macaulay Duration is a first-order measure of bond price sensitivity to interest-rate changes. It is the foundation upon which Modified Duration, Effective Duration, and Convexity are built. Expect direct 3-mark questions on definition and 14-mark computational problems.

## Conceptual Analogy — "The Weighted Average Wait"

Imagine you are at a restaurant and you order **three dishes**:
- Dish 1 arrives in **10 minutes** (cost ₹100)
- Dish 2 arrives in **25 minutes** (cost ₹60)
- Dish 3 arrives in **40 minutes** (cost ₹40)

If a friend asked "on average, how long did you wait for the food?", you would not say 40 minutes (that's the longest wait), nor would you say 25 minutes (that's the simple average). Instead, you would give **more importance to the dishes that cost more**, because those represent a larger share of what you paid. So a cheap appetizer arriving quickly matters less than an expensive main course arriving late.

**Macaulay Duration is exactly this idea applied to bonds.**
- The **"cost"** of each dish = the **Present Value of each cash flow** the bond pays.
- The **"waiting time"** for each dish = the **time $t$** until that cash flow arrives.
- The **weighted average wait** = **Macaulay Duration**.

So Macaulay Duration tells you: *"On a present-value-weighted basis, how long, on average, will it take to get my money back from this bond?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insights for First-Time Learners:**
> 1. Duration is **not** the same as maturity. Duration is always **less than or equal to** maturity.
> 2. For a **zero-coupon bond**, Macaulay Duration **equals** the time to maturity, because 100% of the cash flow happens at one point in time.
> 3. Duration measures the bond's **interest-rate risk** in addition to its timing characteristics.

## Standard Metrics and Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Macaulay Duration | $D_{Mac}$ | Years | 0 to n (maturity) |
| Yield to Maturity | $y$ | % per annum | 2% – 15% |
| Coupon Rate | $c$ | % per annum | 0% – 15% |
| Face Value | $F$ | Currency | ₹1,000 (standard) |
| Coupon Payment | $C = c \cdot F / m$ | Currency | Varies |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operational Breakdown of the Macaulay Duration Concept

The calculation proceeds through a logical sequence:

### Step 1 — Identify Every Cash Flow
A bond generates cash flows at each coupon date and the final principal repayment. For an annual-pay bond with face value $F$, coupon rate $c$, and maturity $n$ years:

$$CF_t = \begin{cases} c \cdot F & \text{for } t = 1, 2, \dots, n-1 \\ c \cdot F + F & \text{for } t = n \end{cases}$$

### Step 2 — Discount Each Cash Flow to Present Value
Each future cash flow is brought back to "today's money" using the bond's Yield-To-Maturity $y$ as the discount rate:

$$PV(CF_t) = \frac{CF_t}{(1+y)^t}$$

This step recognizes the **time value of money** — ₹100 received in 5 years is worth less than ₹100 received today.

### Step 3 — Compute the Total Bond Price
The sum of all discounted cash flows equals the bond's current market price:

$$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+y)^t} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} PV(CF_t)$$

This should equal the **observed market price** of the bond (an arbitrage-free valuation).

### Step 4 — Determine the Weight of Each Cash Flow
The weight is the proportion of total price contributed by each discounted cash flow:

$$w_t = \frac{PV(CF_t)}{P_0}$$

Note that $\sum_{t=1}^{n} w_t = 1$ (the weights sum to 100%).

### Step 5 — Compute the Weighted-Average Time
Multiply each weight by its corresponding time $t$, then sum:

$$D_{Mac} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} t \cdot w_t$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step:**
> - *Step 1* captures contractual payment structure.
> - *Step 2* translates future rupee amounts into today's purchasing power.
> - *Step 3* confirms the bond is fairly priced.
> - *Step 4* expresses each cash flow's **economic importance** relative to total cost.
> - *Step 5* delivers the **timing answer** the investor seeks.

## KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **must-memorize cheat sheet** for the KTU University Exam. Every formula below has appeared (or is highly likely to appear) in past papers.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units | Key Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Macaulay Duration (General) | $D_{Mac} = \dfrac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} t \cdot \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+y)^t}}{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+y)^t}}$ | Years | Always valid |
| 2 | Macaulay Duration (Weight form) | $D_{Mac} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} t \cdot w_t$ | Years | $w_t = \dfrac{PV(CF_t)}{P_0}$ |
| 3 | Zero-Coupon Bond Duration | $D_{Mac} = n$ | Years | Single cash flow at maturity |
| 4 | Modified Duration | $D_{Mod} = \dfrac{D_{Mac}}{1+y}$ | Years (per unit yield change) | Used for % price change |
| 5 | % Price Change Approximation | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{P} \approx -D_{Mod} \cdot \Delta y$ | Dimensionless (%) | First-order Taylor expansion |
| 6 | Effective Duration | $D_{Eff} = \dfrac{P_{-} - P_{+}}{2 \cdot P_0 \cdot \Delta y}$ | Years | For bonds with embedded options |
| 7 | Convexity Adjustment | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{P} \approx -D_{Mod} \cdot \Delta y + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot C \cdot (\Delta y)^2$ | Dimensionless | Second-order correction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility in Engineering & Finance Fields:**
> - **Banking & Treasury**: Asset-Liability Management (ALM) teams use duration to immunize interest-rate risk on bond portfolios.
> - **Insurance Companies**: Long-dated liabilities (pension obligations) are matched against duration-equivalent bond assets.
> - **Corporate Finance**: CFOs use duration when deciding whether to issue short-term or long-term debt.
> - **Central Banking**: The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) and global central banks monitor aggregate duration in the banking system as an indicator of systemic interest-rate exposure.
> - **Algorithmic Trading**: Quant funds execute duration-based hedging strategies (e.g., curve trades, DV01-neutral positions).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Annual Coupon Bond

**Problem Statement (Model Q):**
Consider a 3-year annual-pay bond with face value ₹1,000, coupon rate **5% per annum**, and Yield-To-Maturity **6% per annum**.
*(a)* Calculate the bond's current price.
*(b)* Compute the Macaulay Duration of the bond.
*(c)* Compute the Modified Duration.
*(d)* Estimate the new price if YTM rises from 6% to 7%.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Part (a) — Bond Price Calculation:**

The cash flows are: ₹50 at end of Years 1 and 2, and ₹50 + ₹1,000 = ₹1,050 at end of Year 3.

$$P_0 = \frac{50}{(1.06)^1} + \frac{50}{(1.06)^2} + \frac{1050}{(1.06)^3}$$

Evaluating each term:

$$P_0 = \frac{50}{1.06} + \frac{50}{1.1236} + \frac{1050}{1.191016}$$

$$P_0 = 47.1698 + 44.4998 + 881.4985$$

$$\boxed{P_0 = 973.1681}$$

**Part (b) — Macaulay Duration Calculation:**

Using the master formula:

$$D_{Mac} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} t \cdot \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+y)^t}}{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+y)^t}} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} t \cdot PV(CF_t)}{P_0}$$

Computing the numerator — the time-weighted present values:

$$\text{Numerator} = (1)(47.1698) + (2)(44.4998) + (3)(881.4985)$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 47.1698 + 88.9996 + 2644.4955$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 2780.6649$$

Now dividing by the bond price:

$$D_{Mac} = \frac{2780.6649}{973.1681}$$

$$\boxed{D_{Mac} = 2.8573 \text{ years}}$$

**Part (c) — Modified Duration Calculation:**

$$D_{Mod} = \frac{D_{Mac}}{1+y} = \frac{2.8573}{1.06}$$

$$\boxed{D_{Mod} = 2.6956}$$

**Part (d) — Estimated Price Change for 100 bp Rise in YTM:**

$$\Delta y = 0.01$$

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P} \approx -D_{Mod} \cdot \Delta y = -(2.6956)(0.01) = -0.026956$$

$$\Delta P \approx (973.1681)(-0.026956) = -26.235$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{new}} \approx 973.1681 - 26.235 = 946.93}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification — Actual New Price at 7% YTM:**
> 
> $$P_{\text{actual}} = \frac{50}{1.07} + \frac{50}{1.07^2} + \frac{1050}{1.07^3}$$
> 
> $$P_{\text{actual}} = 46.7290 + 43.6729 + 856.8386$$
> 
> $$P_{\text{actual}} = 947.2405$$
> 
> The duration-based estimate (₹946.93) is very close to the actual new price (₹947.24), with a small error of approximately **₹0.31**. This tiny discrepancy is due to **convexity** (second-order effects), which duration alone does not capture.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — How Bond Features Affect Duration

| Bond Feature | Change | Effect on Macaulay Duration | Intuition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coupon Rate ($c$) | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | Larger coupons return cash sooner → shorter average wait |
| Yield to Maturity ($y$) | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | Discounting weights front-end cash flows more heavily |
| Maturity ($n$) | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases (but at a decreasing rate) | Cash flows pushed further into the future |
| Face Value ($F$) | ↑ Increases | → No change | Scales all cash flows proportionally |
| Coupon Frequency ($m$) | ↑ Increases | ↓ Slightly decreases | More frequent cash returns shorten average wait |
| Embedded Call Option | Added | ↓ Decreases | Issuer likely to call when rates fall → truncates cash flows |

## 3.3 Why Duration Is Less Than or Equal to Maturity — Formal Proof

For any bond with at least one intermediate coupon, we can show:

$$D_{Mac} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} t \cdot w_t \le \sum_{t=1}^{n} n \cdot w_t = n \cdot \sum_{t=1}^{n} w_t = n \cdot 1 = n$$

The inequality $D_{Mac} \le n$ holds with **equality** only when $w_n = 1$ and $w_t = 0$ for all $t < n$ — which is precisely the case of a **zero-coupon bond**.

## 3.4 Linkage to Bond Price Sensitivity — First-Order Derivation

Consider the bond price function:

$$P(y) = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+y)^t}$$

Differentiating with respect to $y$ (using the chain rule on $(1+y)^{-t}$):

$$\frac{dP}{dy} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} CF_t \cdot (-t)(1+y)^{-t-1} = -\frac{1}{1+y} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{t \cdot CF_t}{(1+y)^t}$$

Dividing both sides by $P$:

$$\frac{1}{P}\frac{dP}{dy} = -\frac{1}{1+y} \cdot \underbrace{\left[\frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} t \cdot CF_t / (1+y)^t}{P}\right]}_{D_{Mac}}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{D_{Mac}}{1+y} \cdot dy = -D_{Mod} \cdot dy$$

This is the formal derivation of the famous duration-based price sensitivity formula.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1 — Cash Flow Timeline with Present-Value Weights

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t0["Time 0 Today: Pay Bond Price P0"] --> t1["Year 1: Coupon 50 rupees, PV 47.17, Weight 4.85%"]
    t1 --> t2["Year 2: Coupon 50 rupees, PV 44.50, Weight 4.57%"]
    t2 --> t3["Year 3: Coupon plus Principal 1050 rupees, PV 881.50, Weight 90.58%"]
    t3 --> result["Weighted Average Time equals Macaulay Duration equals 2.857 years"]
    classDef timeNode fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    classDef cashNode fill:#059669,stroke:#059669,color:#ffffff
    classDef resultNode fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    class t0 timeNode
    class t1,t2,t3 cashNode
    class result resultNode
```

## Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Duration Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputA["Bond Contract Inputs: Face Value, Coupon Rate, Maturity, Frequency"] --> processA["Process 1: Cash Flow Generator"]
    inputB["Market Input: Yield to Maturity YTM"] --> processB["Process 2: Discount Engine"]
    processA --> processB
    processB --> processC["Process 3: Sum of Present Values equals Bond Price P0"]
    processC --> processD["Process 4: Weight Calculator PV over P0"]
    processD --> processE["Process 5: Time Weighted Sum"]
    processE --> outputA["Output: Macaulay Duration in Years"]
    processE --> outputB["Output: Modified Duration"]
    outputB --> outputC["Output: Estimated Percentage Price Change"]
    classDef ioNode fill:#0f766e,stroke:#0f766e,color:#ffffff
    classDef procNode fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff
    classDef outNode fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    class inputA,inputB ioNode
    class processA,processB,processC,processD,processE procNode
    class outputA,outputB,outputC outNode
```

## Diagram 3 — Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Duration Measure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["Need to Measure Interest Rate Risk"] --> q1{"Does the bond have embedded options call put convertible"}
    q1 -- No --> standard["Use Macaulay Duration then convert to Modified Duration"]
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{"Are option adjusted cash flows available"}
    q2 -- Yes --> effective["Use Effective Duration via finite difference"]
    q2 -- No --> binomial["Use Binomial Tree Model for OAS based duration"]

    effective --> formula2["Formula: D eff equals P down minus P up divided by 2 P0 delta y"]

    class q1,q2 qNode

```

    bondL["Low Coupon 2% Bond, 5 Years: Duration around 4.7 years"]
