<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]
**Define Macaulay Duration. State any two of its important properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Macaulay Duration is the present-value-weighted average time to receive the cash flows from a bond, expressed in years. It was proposed by Frederick Macaulay in 1938.

Two important properties:
1. **Duration ≤ Maturity**: Macaulay Duration is always less than or equal to the bond's maturity. Equality holds for zero-coupon bonds.
2. **Higher coupon → Lower duration**: A bond paying larger coupons returns cash sooner, reducing the weighted-average time.
3. **Higher yield → Lower duration**: A higher discount rate attaches more weight to nearer cash flows, shortening duration.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for the two properties.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]
**Differentiate between Macaulay Duration and Modified Duration.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Macaulay Duration | Modified Duration |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Weighted-average time to recover bond price | Measures price sensitivity to yield changes |
| Formula | $\sum t \cdot w_t$ | $D_{Mac} / (1+y)$ |
| Units | Years | Years (per 100% yield change) |
| Use | Timing measure | Interest-rate risk measure |

The relationship is: $D_{Mod} = D_{Mac} / (1+y)$.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 2]

A 4-year annual-pay bond has a face value of ₹1,000 and a coupon rate of **8% per annum**. The current Yield-To-Maturity (YTM) in the market is **10% per annum**.

**(a)** Compute the current market price of the bond. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the Macaulay Duration and Modified Duration of the bond. Estimate the percentage change in price if YTM decreases by 50 basis points. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to Q-A(a):

**Step 1 — Identify the cash flows:**
Annual coupon $C = 8\% \times 1000 = 80$ rupees. Final cash flow at $t=4$ is $80 + 1000 = 1080$.

**Step 2 — Compute present values:**

$$P_0 = \frac{80}{1.10} + \frac{80}{1.10^2} + \frac{80}{1.10^3} + \frac{1080}{1.10^4}$$

$$P_0 = \frac{80}{1.10} + \frac{80}{1.21} + \frac{80}{1.331} + \frac{1080}{1.4641}$$

$$P_0 = 72.7273 + 66.1157 + 60.1052 + 737.6141$$

$$\boxed{P_0 = 936.5623 \text{ rupees}}$$

#### Solution to Q-A(b):

**Step 1 — Build the time-weighted PV table:**

| Year $t$ | Cash Flow | PV @ 10% | $t \times PV$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 80 | 72.7273 | 72.7273 |
| 2 | 80 | 66.1157 | 132.2314 |
| 3 | 80 | 60.1052 | 180.3155 |
| 4 | 1080 | 737.6141 | 2950.4565 |
| **Sum** | — | **936.5623** | **3335.7307** |

**Step 2 — Compute Macaulay Duration:**

$$D_{Mac} = \frac{3335.7307}{936.5623} = \boxed{3.5620 \text{ years}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Modified Duration:**

$$D_{Mod} = \frac{3.5620}{1.10} = \boxed{3.2382}$$

**Step 4 — Estimated price change for $\Delta y = -0.005$ (i.e., 50 bp fall):**

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P} \approx -D_{Mod} \cdot \Delta y = -(3.2382)(-0.005) = +0.016191$$

$$\Delta P \approx 936.5623 \times 0.016191 = +15.16$$

$$P_{\text{new}} \approx 936.56 + 15.16 = \boxed{951.72 \text{ rupees}}$$

The price is expected to rise by approximately **1.62%**.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam – Alternative Module 2 Question]

A bond investor is evaluating two bonds, both with face value ₹1,000 and a YTM of **9% per annum**, but with different coupon structures:

- **Bond X**: 6% annual coupon, 5-year maturity.
- **Bond Y**: 10% annual coupon, 5-year maturity.

**(a)** Calculate the Macaulay Duration of both bonds. Which bond is more sensitive to interest-rate changes? Justify. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the YTM rises from 9% to 10%, calculate the actual new prices and the duration-estimated new prices for both bonds. Comment on the size of the estimation errors. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to Q-B(a):

**Bond X (6% coupon, 5 years, YTM = 9%):**

Cash flows: ₹60 for years 1-4, and ₹1060 in year 5.

$$P_X = \frac{60}{1.09} + \frac{60}{1.09^2} + \frac{60}{1.09^3} + \frac{60}{1.09^4} + \frac{1060}{1.09^5}$$

$$P_X = 55.0459 + 50.5017 + 46.3310 + 42.5055 + 689.7957 = 884.1798$$

| $t$ | CF | PV | $t \times PV$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 60 | 55.0459 | 55.0459 |
| 2 | 60 | 50.5017 | 101.0035 |
| 3 | 60 | 46.3310 | 138.9930 |
| 4 | 60 | 42.5055 | 170.0221 |
| 5 | 1060 | 689.7957 | 3448.9785 |
| **Σ** | — | **884.1798** | **3914.0430** |

$$D_{Mac,X} = \frac{3914.0430}{884.1798} = \boxed{4.4268 \text{ years}}$$

**Bond Y (10% coupon, 5 years, YTM = 9%):**

Cash flows: ₹100 for years 1-4, and ₹1100 in year 5.

$$P_Y = \frac{100}{1.09} + \frac{100}{1.09^2} + \frac{100}{1.09^3} + \frac{100}{1.09^4} + \frac{1100}{1.09^5}$$

$$P_Y = 91.7431 + 84.1680 + 77.2183 + 70.8425 + 716.1294 = 1040.1013$$

| $t$ | CF | PV | $t \times PV$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 91.7431 | 91.7431 |
| 2 | 100 | 84.1680 | 168.3360 |
| 3 | 100 | 77.2183 | 231.6548 |
| 4 | 100 | 70.8425 | 283.3699 |
| 5 | 1100 | 716.1294 | 3580.6472 |
| **Σ** | — | **1040.1013** | **4355.7510** |

$$D_{Mac,Y} = \frac{4355.7510}{1040.1013} = \boxed{4.1879 \text{ years}}$$

**Conclusion:** Since $D_{Mac,X} > D_{Mac,Y}$, **Bond X** is more sensitive to interest-rate changes. This is because Bond X has a lower coupon, so a larger share of its total cash flow is concentrated in the distant final payment of ₹1000, pulling the weighted-average wait time upward.

#### Solution to Q-B(b):

**Bond X — Actual new price at 10% YTM:**

$$P_{X,\text{new}} = \frac{60}{1.10} + \frac{60}{1.10^2} + \frac{60}{1.10^3} + \frac{60}{1.10^4} + \frac{1060}{1.10^5}$$

$$P_{X,\text{new}} = 54.5455 + 49.5868 + 45.0789 + 40.9808 + 658.5525 = 848.7445$$

**Duration estimate for Bond X:**

$$D_{Mod,X} = \frac{4.4268}{1.09} = 4.0613$$

$$\frac{\Delta P_X}{P_X} \approx -(4.0613)(0.01) = -0.040613$$

$$\Delta P_X \approx 884.1798 \times (-0.040613) = -35.911$$

$$P_{X,\text{est}} = 884.1798 - 35.911 = 848.27$$

**Estimation error for Bond X:** $|848.74 - 848.27| = 0.47$ rupees.

**Bond Y — Actual new price at 10% YTM:**

$$P_{Y,\text{new}} = \frac{100}{1.10} + \frac{100}{1.10^2} + \frac{100}{1.10^3} + \frac{100}{1.10^4} + \frac{1100}{1.10^5}$$

$$P_{Y,\text{new}} = 90.9091 + 82.6446 + 75.1315 + 68.3013 + 683.0135 = 1000.0000$$

**Duration estimate for Bond Y:**

$$D_{Mod,Y} = \frac{4.1879}{1.09} = 3.8421$$

$$\frac{\Delta P_Y}{P_Y} \approx -(3.8421)(0.01) = -0.038421$$

$$\Delta P_Y \approx 1040.1013 \times (-0.038421) = -39.961$$

$$P_{Y,\text{est}} = 1040.1013 - 39.961 = 1000.14$$

**Estimation error for Bond Y:** $|1000.00 - 1000.14| = 0.14$ rupees.

**Comment:** Both bonds experience price falls as YTM rises. The estimation errors are very small (less than ₹1), confirming that Macaulay/Modified Duration provides an excellent first-order approximation. The slightly larger error for Bond X reflects its higher convexity, since lower-coupon, longer-duration bonds have greater curvature in their price-yield relationship.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include principal repayment at maturity** — Students often compute duration using only coupons, missing the ₹1000 face value at $t = n$. This loses 2-3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Mixing up Macaulay vs Modified Duration** — Many students divide by $(1+y)^2$ or forget to divide by $(1+y)$ entirely. Read the question carefully: if it asks for **modified**, you MUST apply the $(1+y)$ correction.
> 3. **Wrong sign for price change** — When YTM **rises**, price **falls**; when YTM **falls**, price **rises**. A sign error here guarantees zero marks for the price-change part.
> 4. **Confusing coupon $C = c \cdot F$ with $c$ alone** — Always compute the rupee coupon as (coupon rate × face value).
> 5. **Not presenting tabular work** — For 14-mark questions, always present a clean table with columns for $t$, $CF_t$, $PV(CF_t)$, and $t \cdot PV(CF_t)$. Examiners award step marks for organized presentation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam:**
>
> ☐ **Macaulay Duration = weighted-average time** to recover bond price (units: years)
>
> ☐ **Master Formula:** $D_{Mac} = \dfrac{\sum t \cdot PV(CF_t)}{\sum PV(CF_t)} = \sum t \cdot w_t$
>
> ☐ **Modified Duration:** $D_{Mod} = D_{Mac} / (1+y)$ — used for % price change estimates
>
> ☐ **Price Sensitivity Formula:** $\dfrac{\Delta P}{P} \approx -D_{Mod} \cdot \Delta y$
>
> ☐ **Zero-Coupon Bond Rule:** $D_{Mac} = n$ (maturity) — single cash flow at maturity
>
> ☐ **Coupon Bond Rule:** $D_{Mac} < n$ — because intermediate coupons accelerate payback
>
> ☐ **Higher coupon → Lower duration** (faster cash recovery)
>
> ☐ **Higher yield → Lower duration** (front-loaded PV weighting)
>
> ☐ **Longer maturity → Higher duration** (more distant cash flows)
>
> ☐ **Always include principal (₹1000) at maturity** in cash-flow column
>
> ☐ **Duration alone is a first-order approximation** — for large yield changes, also add convexity adjustment
>
> ☐ **Effective Duration** is used for bonds with embedded options (callable, putable, mortgage-backed)
>
> ☐ **Practical applications**: Immunization, ALM, duration-matched hedging, key-rate analysis
>
> ☐ **Examiner tip**: Build a clean table with $t$, $CF_t$, $PV(CF_t)$, $t \cdot PV(CF_t)$ — step marks depend on clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intrinsic Value of Bond
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Intrinsic Value of Bond

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Intrinsic Value of a Bond** (also called the **Fair Value**, **Theoretical Value**, or **Present Value of a Bond**) is the discounted present worth of all future cash flows that the bond is expected to generate — namely, the periodic coupon payments (interest) and the face value (principal) repaid at maturity — evaluated at the bond's required rate of return (Yield to Maturity, YTM).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, intrinsic value is the value derived from the fundamental cash-flow characteristics of the bond, as opposed to its market price which is determined by supply-demand forces in the secondary market.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition:** Intrinsic Value of a Bond = Present Value of Coupon Payments + Present Value of Face Value (Redemption Value), both discounted at the investor's required rate of return (YTM).

Mathematically, the intrinsic value $(P_0)$ of a bond with $n$ periods to maturity, periodic coupon $C$, face value $F$, and yield $r$ per period is:

$$
P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+r)^{t}} \; + \; \frac{F}{(1+r)^{n}}
$$

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Mango Tree"**
>
> Imagine a mango tree that gives you **100 mangoes every year for 5 years**, and on the 5th year, the owner also gives you back the **wood/cost of the tree (say ₹1000)**. Now, what is the *fair price* of this tree today? You cannot simply add 100+100+100+100+100+1000 = ₹1600, because money today is worth more than money tomorrow (you could invest today's money and earn interest, plus there's inflation and risk). So you **discount** each future mango payment and the final tree value back to today. The sum of these discounted values is the **intrinsic value** of the mango tree — exactly analogous to the intrinsic value of a bond.

A bond is essentially a **"financial mango tree"**:
- **Coupon payments** = the periodic mangoes (interest income).
- **Face value** = the lump sum returned at maturity.
- **Yield to Maturity (r)** = the discount rate, representing the investor's required return.
- **Intrinsic Value** = the present-day fair price of all these future benefits.

---

### 1.3 Why Intrinsic Value Matters

| Aspect | Market Price | Intrinsic Value |
|---|---|---|
| Determined by | Supply & demand in market | Fundamental cash flows & required return |
| Volatility | Changes daily | Stable, recalculates only when YTM or time changes |
| Decision use | Actual transaction price | Buy/Sell/Hold decision benchmark |
| KTU relevance | Used in trading | Used in valuation module (Module 2) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Investment Decision Rule:** If Market Price < Intrinsic Value → **BUY** (undervalued); If Market Price > Intrinsic Value → **SELL** (overvalued); If Market Price = Intrinsic Value → **HOLD** (fairly valued).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

The intrinsic value framework rests on three core financial principles:

1. **Time Value of Money (TVM):** A rupee received today is worth more than a rupee received tomorrow because of the opportunity cost of capital.
2. **Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Valuation:** Every financial asset's value equals the sum of the present values of its expected future cash flows.
3. **Required Rate of Return:** An investor discounts cash flows at the minimum return they demand for bearing the bond's risk (this equals the bond's Yield to Maturity in equilibrium).

---

### 2.2 Classification of Bonds Based on Intrinsic Value vs. Coupon Rate

Let $i_c$ = Coupon Rate (stated interest rate on face value) and $i_d$ = Current Market YTM (discount rate).

| Condition | Bond Classification | Price Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| $i_c > i_d$ | **Premium Bond** | $P_0 > F$ (price above par) |
| $i_c = i_d$ | **Par Bond** | $P_0 = F$ (price at par) |
| $i_c < i_d$ | **Discount Bond** | $P_0 < F$ (price below par) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** When market interest rates rise, existing bond prices **fall** (and vice versa) — this is the inverse relationship between price and yield, the foundation of interest rate risk.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C}{(1+r)^{t}} + \dfrac{F}{(1+r)^{n}}$ | General bond valuation | $P_0$=Price, $C$=Coupon, $r$=YTM, $n$=periods, $F$=Face value |
| 2 | $P_0 = C \cdot \left[\dfrac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r}\right] + \dfrac{F}{(1+r)^{n}}$ | Bond PV using annuity factor | Compact form of (1) |
| 3 | $C = i_c \cdot F$ | Periodic coupon payment | $i_c$=coupon rate |
| 4 | $P_0 = \dfrac{C}{r}$ | **Perpetual bond** (no maturity) | When $n \to \infty$ |
| 5 | $P_0 = \dfrac{F}{(1+r)^{n}}$ | **Zero-coupon bond** valuation | No periodic coupons |
| 6 | $\text{Current Yield} = \dfrac{C}{P_0}$ | Annual income relative to price | Expressed as % |
| 7 | $\text{Capital Gains Yield} = \dfrac{F - P_0}{n \cdot P_0}$ | Annual price appreciation | Per period |
| 8 | $\text{YTM} \approx \text{Current Yield} + \text{Capital Gains Yield}$ | Yield decomposition | Approximation for understanding |

---

### 2.4 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of Bond Valuation

**Step 1 — Identify Cash Flows:** Determine periodic coupon $C = i_c \cdot F$ and redemption value $F$ at maturity $n$.

**Step 2 — Determine Discount Rate:** Identify the investor's required rate of return $r$ (equal to YTM of comparable bonds in the market).

**Step 3 — Discount Each Cash Flow:** For each period $t$, compute $\text{PV}_t = \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}$.

**Step 4 — Sum All Present Values:** Intrinsic value $P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \text{PV}_t$ including the discounted face value at $t=n$.

**Step 5 — Compare with Market Price:** Decide Buy/Sell/Hold based on intrinsic vs. market price.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Bond intrinsic value calculations underpin **pension fund liability valuation**, **insurance premium calculations**, **infrastructure project financing** (e.g., Kerala KIIFB bond valuation), and **corporate capital budgeting** decisions in engineering firms.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the General Bond Valuation Formula

Starting from first principles using the **Time Value of Money (TVM)** logic:

**Step 1:** The bondholder receives a coupon $C$ at the end of each period from $t=1$ to $t=n$. The present value of coupon payment at time $t$ is:

$$
\text{PV}_{\text{coupon},t} = \frac{C}{(1+r)^{t}}
$$

**Step 2:** At maturity ($t=n$), the bondholder additionally receives the face value $F$:

$$
\text{PV}_{\text{face}} = \frac{F}{(1+r)^{n}}
$$

**Step 3:** Summing all discounted cash flows:

$$
P_0 = \frac{C}{(1+r)^{1}} + \frac{C}{(1+r)^{2}} + \cdots + \frac{C}{(1+r)^{n}} + \frac{F}{(1+r)^{n}}
$$

**Step 4:** Grouping the coupon terms into an annuity series:

$$
P_0 = \left[\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+r)^{t}}\right] + \frac{F}{(1+r)^{n}}
$$

**Step 5:** Applying the geometric series summation formula $\sum_{t=1}^{n} x^{t} = \dfrac{x(1-x^{n})}{1-x}$ with $x = \dfrac{1}{1+r}$:

$$
P_0 = C \cdot \left[\frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}\right] + \frac{F}{(1+r)^{n}}
$$

This is the **compact annuity-based** bond valuation formula.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Perpetual Bond Formula

A **perpetual bond** (consol) has no maturity date; coupons are paid forever. Taking the limit $n \to \infty$:

$$
P_0 = \lim_{n \to \infty} \left[ C \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r} \right] = C \cdot \frac{1 - 0}{r}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_0 = \frac{C}{r}}
$$

This is the famous **Gordon-type perpetual cash flow valuation**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Zero-Coupon Bond Formula

A **zero-coupon bond** pays no periodic coupons; only the face value $F$ at maturity. Setting $C = 0$:

$$
P_0 = 0 + \frac{F}{(1+r)^{n}}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_0 = \frac{F}{(1+r)^{n}}}
$$

This shows that zero-coupon bonds are always sold at a **discount** to face value (since $(1+r)^n > 1$).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Premium, Par, Discount Bond Behavior

| Feature | Premium Bond | Par Bond | Discount Bond |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coupon vs. YTM | $i_c > r$ | $i_c = r$ | $i_c < r$ |
| Intrinsic Value | $P_0 > F$ | $P_0 = F$ | $P_0 < F$ |
| Trading Status | Above par | At par | Below par |
| As maturity nears | Price falls toward $F$ | Price stays at $F$ | Price rises toward $F$ |
| Investor appeal | High-income seekers | Conservative | Capital gains seekers |

---

### 3.5 Comprehensive Numerical Example (Worked Out)

**Problem:** A 10-year bond has a face value of ₹1,000, an annual coupon rate of **8%**, and the market required rate of return (YTM) is **10%**. Calculate the intrinsic value.

**Step 1:** Identify variables:
- $F = 1{,}000$, $n = 10$ years, $i_c = 8\%$, $r = 10\%$
- $C = 8\% \times 1{,}000 = 80$ per year

**Step 2:** Apply the compact formula:

$$
P_0 = 80 \cdot \left[\frac{1 - (1.10)^{-10}}{0.10}\right] + \frac{1{,}000}{(1.10)^{10}}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the annuity factor:
$$
(1.10)^{10} = 2.5937
$$
$$
(1.10)^{-10} = 0.3855
$$
$$
\frac{1 - 0.3855}{0.10} = \frac{0.6145}{0.10} = 6.1446
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate PV of coupons:
$$
\text{PV}_{\text{coupons}} = 80 \times 6.1446 = 491.57
$$

**Step 5:** Calculate PV of face value:
$$
\text{PV}_{\text{face}} = \frac{1{,}000}{2.5937} = 385.54
$$

**Step 6:** Intrinsic value:
$$
P_0 = 491.57 + 385.54 = 877.11
$$

**Step 7:** Interpret:
$$
P_0 = ₹877.11 < F = ₹1{,}000 \quad \Rightarrow \text{This is a } \textbf{Discount Bond}
$$

This makes sense because coupon rate (8%) < YTM (10%).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bond Intrinsic Value Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Bond Parameters] --> B[Identify Face Value F]
    A --> C[Identify Coupon Rate ic]
    A --> D[Identify YTM r]
    A --> E[Identify Maturity n]
    B --> F[Compute Coupon C = ic x F]
    C --> F
    D --> G[Apply Valuation Formula]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compute PV of Coupons = C x Annuity Factor]
    G --> I[Compute PV of Face Value = F / 1+r to power n]
    H --> J[Sum: Intrinsic Value P0]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Compare P0 with F}
    K -->|P0 > F| L[Premium Bond]
    K -->|P0 = F| M[Par Bond]
    K -->|P0 < F| N[Discount Bond]
    L --> O[Investment Decision]
    M --> O
    N --> O
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Bond Cash Flow Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TimeAxis[Time Axis t = 0 to n]
        direction LR
        T0[Time 0: Investor pays P0] --> T1[Time 1: Receive Coupon C]
        T1 --> T2[Time 2: Receive Coupon C]
        T2 --> T3[Time 3: Receive Coupon C]
        T3 --> Tn[Time n: Receive Coupon C + Face Value F]
    end

    subgraph DiscountEngine[Discount Engine at rate r]
        D1[Discount Factor 1 over 1+r to power t]
    end

    T1 --> D1
    T2 --> D1
    T3 --> D1
    Tn --> D1
    D1 --> PV[Present Value Sum equals Intrinsic Value]
```

### 4.3 Premium/Par/Discount Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Compare Coupon Rate ic and YTM r] --> Q1{ic greater than r?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Premium[Premium Bond P0 greater than F]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{ic equals r?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Par[Par Bond P0 equals F]
    Q2 -->|No| Discount[Discount Bond P0 less than F]
    Premium --> Out1[Buy if Market Price less than P0]
    Par --> Out2[Hold Fairly Valued]
    Discount --> Out3[Buy if attractive capital gain expected]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the intrinsic value of a bond. How does it differ from the market price of a bond?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **intrinsic value of a bond** is the present value of all future cash flows the bond is expected to generate (periodic coupon payments plus the face value at maturity), discounted at the investor's required rate of return (Yield to Maturity).

$$
P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+r)^t} + \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}
$$

**Difference from Market Price:**

| Aspect | Intrinsic Value | Market Price |
|---|---|---|
| Basis | Fundamental cash flows | Supply and demand |
| Nature | Theoretical/fair value | Actual transaction price |
| Use | Buy/Sell decision | Real trading |

When intrinsic value > market price, the bond is **undervalued** (buy); when intrinsic value < market price, it is **overvalued** (sell). **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Explain the concept of premium, par, and discount bonds with reference to the relationship between coupon rate and YTM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The relationship between the **coupon rate ($i_c$)** and the **Yield to Maturity ($r$)** determines whether a bond trades at a premium, par, or discount:

1. **Premium Bond ($i_c > r$):** The bond pays higher coupons than the market requires. Its intrinsic value exceeds face value ($P_0 > F$). It trades above par.

2. **Par Bond ($i_c = r$):** The coupon rate equals the market YTM. The bond's intrinsic value equals face value ($P_0 = F$). It trades at par.

3. **Discount Bond ($i_c < r$):** The coupon rate is lower than the market YTM. The bond's intrinsic value is below face value ($P_0 < F$). It trades below par. **(3 marks)**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the general bond valuation formula from first principles. Explain each term clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-year bond has a face value of ₹5,000, a coupon rate of **9%** paid annually, and the prevailing market yield is **12%**. Calculate the intrinsic value and identify whether it is a premium, par, or discount bond. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

The bond valuation formula is derived from the **Time Value of Money** principle. A bond generates two streams of cash flows:

- **Stream 1:** Periodic coupon $C$ at the end of each period from $t = 1$ to $t = n$.
- **Stream 2:** Face value $F$ at maturity $t = n$.

**Step 1:** Present value of each coupon at discount rate $r$:

$$
\text{PV}_t = \frac{C}{(1+r)^t}
$$

**Step 2:** Sum of all coupon present values (annuity):

$$
\text{PV}_{\text{coupons}} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+r)^t}
$$

**Step 3:** Present value of face value:

$$
\text{PV}_{\text{face}} = \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}
$$

**Step 4:** Total intrinsic value:

$$
P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+r)^t} + \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}
$$

**Step 5:** Applying geometric series identity:

$$
P_0 = C \cdot \left[\frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}\right] + \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}
$$

Where: $P_0$ = Intrinsic value; $C$ = Periodic coupon = $i_c \cdot F$; $r$ = YTM; $n$ = Periods to maturity; $F$ = Face value. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $F = 5{,}000$, $n = 6$ years, $i_c = 9\%$, $r = 12\%$

**Step 1:** Compute coupon:
$$
C = 9\% \times 5{,}000 = ₹450
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the valuation formula:
$$
P_0 = 450 \cdot \left[\frac{1 - (1.12)^{-6}}{0.12}\right] + \frac{5{,}000}{(1.12)^6}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $(1.12)^6$:
$$
(1.12)^6 = 1.9738
$$
$$
(1.12)^{-6} = 0.5066
$$

**Step 4:** Annuity factor:
$$
\frac{1 - 0.5066}{0.12} = \frac{0.4934}{0.12} = 4.1114
$$

**Step 5:** PV of coupons:
$$
450 \times 4.1114 = ₹1{,}850.13
$$

**Step 6:** PV of face value:
$$
\frac{5{,}000}{1.9738} = ₹2{,}532.96
$$

**Step 7:** Total intrinsic value:
$$
P_0 = 1{,}850.13 + 2{,}532.96 = ₹4{,}383.09
$$

**Step 8:** Classification:
Since coupon rate (9%) < YTM (12%), and $P_0 = ₹4{,}383 < F = ₹5{,}000$, this is a **Discount Bond**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** What is a perpetual bond? Derive its valuation formula. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ₹100 face value perpetual bond offers an annual coupon of ₹8. If the required rate of return is **10%**, calculate its intrinsic value. If the YTM drops to **6%**, what is the new intrinsic value? Comment on the price-yield relationship. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution:

**(a) Perpetual Bond Derivation (7 marks):**

A **perpetual bond** (also called a **consol** or **perpetuity**) is a bond with no fixed maturity date. It pays a fixed coupon $C$ forever, with no return of principal.

Starting from the general bond formula:

$$
P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+r)^t} + \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}
$$

Since there is no maturity ($n \to \infty$) and no face value payment:

$$
P_0 = \lim_{n \to \infty} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+r)^t} = \lim_{n \to \infty} C \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}
$$

As $n \to \infty$, $(1+r)^{-n} \to 0$:

$$
\boxed{P_0 = \frac{C}{r}}
$$

The intrinsic value of a perpetual bond equals the annual coupon divided by the required rate of return. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $C = ₹8$, $F = ₹100$

**Case 1:** YTM = 10% = 0.10

$$
P_0 = \frac{8}{0.10} = ₹80
$$

**Case 2:** YTM = 6% = 0.06

$$
P_0 = \frac{8}{0.06} = ₹133.33
$$

**Comment on Price-Yield Relationship:**

When YTM decreased from 10% to 6%, the bond's intrinsic value **rose** from ₹80 to ₹133.33 (an increase of **66.67%**). This demonstrates the **inverse relationship** between bond prices and yields:

- When market interest rates **fall**, existing bond prices **rise** (because fixed coupons become more attractive).
- When market interest rates **rise**, existing bond prices **fall**.

This is the foundational principle of **interest rate risk** in fixed-income securities. **(7 marks)**

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (Losing 2-3 marks easily):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Coupon Rate with YTM:** Students often use the coupon rate as the discount rate. Remember — coupons are *paid*, but discounting uses the *required market return (YTM)*.
> 2. **Mixing Annual vs. Semi-Annual Compounding:** If coupons are semi-annual, divide coupon by 2, divide YTM by 2, and double the number of periods. KTU examiners deduct marks for this confusion.
> 3. **Forgetting the Face Value PV:** Many students calculate only the annuity of coupons and forget to add $\frac{F}{(1+r)^n}$. This alone can cost **3-4 marks**.
> 4. **Not Classifying the Bond:** Always state whether the answer is a premium, par, or discount bond — this is explicitly asked in KTU questions.
> 5. **Rounding Errors:** Round only at the final step; intermediate rounding leads to wrong answers. Carry at least 4 decimal places.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Intrinsic Value** = Sum of PV of all coupons + PV of face value, discounted at YTM.
- **Formula:** $P_0 = C \cdot \left[\dfrac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r}\right] + \dfrac{F}{(1+r)^n}$
- **Perpetual bond:** $P_0 = C/r$ (used by British Consols historically; relevant for preferred stock-like instruments).
- **Zero-coupon bond:** $P_0 = F/(1+r)^n$ — always trades at a discount.
- **Coupon rate > YTM** → **Premium**; **Coupon rate = YTM** → **Par**; **Coupon rate < YTM** → **Discount**.
- **Price-Yield Inverse Relationship:** As YTM rises, intrinsic value falls, and vice versa.
- **Investment Rule:** Buy if Market Price < Intrinsic Value; Sell if Market Price > Intrinsic Value.
- **Convergence to Par:** As maturity approaches, all bonds converge to face value regardless of premium/discount status.
- **Current Yield ≠ YTM:** Current Yield uses market price, YTM accounts for total return including capital gain/loss.
- **Time Value of Money** is the bedrock principle — always discount future cash flows to present.
- **Kerala Context:** Kerala Infrastructure Investment Fund Board (KIIFB) bonds and Kerala State Financial Enterprises (KSFE) chits follow these intrinsic value principles for retail investors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Market Impact and the Order Book
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Market Impact and the Order Book # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Order Book — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Order Book**: A real-time, electronically maintained, chronological ledger of all outstanding **buy (bid)** and **sell (ask/offer)** orders for a specific financial instrument on a given exchange, organized by **price level** and **time priority**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme curriculum, the order book is treated as the **fundamental microstructure building block** of any modern exchange (NSE, BSE, NYSE, NASDAQ). It is the visible manifestation of **aggregate supply and demand** at discrete price levels. ## 1.2 Market Impact — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Market Impact**: The measurable change in an asset's **transaction price** (or its subsequent equilibrium price) caused by the *execution* of a trade — distinct from any price change induced by **information arrival** or **macro news**. Mathematically, market impact $\Delta P$ is defined as: $$\Delta P = P_{\text{executed}} - P_{\text{pre-trade}}$$ where $P_{\text{pre-trade}}$ is the **mid-price** prevailing immediately before order submission, and $P_{\text{executed}}$ is the **volume-weighted average price (VWAP)** at which the order was filled. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **The Ocean Cruise Ship Analogy**: Imagine a financial market as an ocean, and your trade as a small boat. Even a small boat leaves a **wake** — that wake is **market impact**. The bigger your boat (order size), the larger the wake. - **Temporary Impact** = The waves that slap back against your boat right after you pass → they fade with time. - **Permanent Impact** = The tide you have actually shifted → the ocean level is now permanently a tiny bit higher or lower. **The Vegetable Market Analogy for the Order Book**: Think of a Sunday vegetable market. The order book is like a chalkboard that the auctioneer keeps updating: - Left side ("Bids") = Buyers shouting "I'll pay ₹40 for tomatoes!" - Right side ("Asks") = Sellers shouting "I'll sell tomatoes for ₹45!" - The **gap** between the highest bid and lowest ask is the **bid-ask spread** — the dealer's profit. ## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics The following constants/metrics are standardized in market microstructure literature: - **Tick Size**: $0.01$ for U.S. equities (post-2005), $\text{₹}0.05$ for NSE cash equities. - **Lot Size**: Varies by instrument (e.g., Nifty futures lot = **25 units**). - **Minimum Price Increment (MPI)**: The smallest permissible price change. - **Level 1, 2, 3 Data**: Depth of order book visibility (top-of-book, top-10 levels, full depth). > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight**: KTU Module 3 expects students to differentiate between **temporary** and **permanent** market impact, and to relate impact cost directly to **liquidity** and **order book depth**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of the Order Book The order book has two sides, each organized as a **price-time priority queue**: ### 2.1.1 Bid Side (Buy Orders) - Sorted in **descending** price order - At each price level, orders are sorted by **time of arrival (FIFO)** - Best Bid = $P_b^{\text{best}}$ (highest buy price) - Bid depth at level $i$ = $Q_b^{(i)}$ ### 2.1.2 Ask Side (Sell Orders) - Sorted in **ascending** price order - At each price level, orders are sorted by **FIFO** - Best Ask = $P_a^{\text{best}}$ (lowest sell price) - Ask depth at level $i$ = $Q_a^{(i)}$ ### 2.1.3 Core Derived Quantities **Mid-Price** — the theoretical "fair" price between best bid and best ask: $$P_{\text{mid}} = \frac{P_b^{\text{best}} + P_a^{\text{best}}}{2}$$ **Bid-Ask Spread** — the most basic measure of liquidity: $$S = P_a^{\text{best}} - P_b^{\text{best}}$$ **Relative Spread** — normalized for cross-asset comparison: $$S_{\text{rel}} = \frac{S}{P_{\text{mid}}}$$ **Microprice** — a volume-weighted fair price that anticipates the next tick: $$P_{\text{micro}} = \frac{Q_b^{\text{best}} \cdot P_a^{\text{best}} + Q_a^{\text{best}} \cdot P_b^{\text{best}}}{Q_b^{\text{best}} + Q_a^{\text{best}}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why Microprice matters**: When the bid side has more size than the ask side, the microprice shifts *up* (toward the ask) because the next tick is more likely to be upward. This is a **high-yield KTU concept**. **Order Book Imbalance (OBI)** — pressure indicator: $$\text{OBI} = \frac{Q_b^{\text{best}} - Q_a^{\text{best}}}{Q_b^{\text{best}} + Q_a^{\text{best}}}$$ OBI ranges in $[-1, +1]$; positive values predict upward price movement. ## 2.2 Market Impact — The Two Components ### 2.2.1 Temporary Impact ($I_{\text{temp}}$) - **Definition**: Price movement that **reverts** after the trade completes. - **Cause**: Dealer inventory risk, order-flow toxicity, bid-ask spread crossing. - **Half-life**: Seconds to minutes for liquid stocks; longer for illiquid ones. ### 2.2.2 Permanent Impact ($I_{\text{perm}}$) - **Definition**: Price movement that **persists** after the trade completes. - **Cause**: Information content of the trade (other market participants infer information from your trade). - **Magnitude**: Generally $30\% - 50\%$ of total impact cost. ### 2.2.3 Total Impact Cost $$I_{\text{total}} = I_{\text{temp}} + I_{\text{perm}}$$ ## 2.3 Square-Root Law of Market Impact The most empirically validated model in market microstructure: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Square-Root Law (Bouchaud, Bonart, Donier & Gould, 2010)**: Market impact grows as the **square root** of the order size, not linearly. $$I(Q) = Y \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{\frac{Q}{V}}$$ where: - $I(Q)$ = market impact (in price units) - $Y$ = a dimensionless constant (typically $Y \approx 0.5$ to $1.0$) - $\sigma$ = daily volatility of the asset - $Q$ = order size (shares/contracts) - $V$ = average daily volume (ADV) of the asset **Why square-root?** - A 4× larger order causes only a 2× larger price impact. - This is because orders get executed over a longer time horizon, allowing liquidity providers to replenish the book. ## 2.4 Kyle's Lambda Model > [!IMPORTANT] > **Kyle (1985) Model**: Linear permanent impact, where $\lambda$ (Kyle's lambda) measures market depth. $$\Delta P_{\text{perm}} = \lambda \cdot Q_{\text{order}}$$ Kyle's lambda is mathematically the slope of the price-impact curve at the **institutional scale**. It is computed via: $$\lambda = \frac{\sigma_V}{\sigma_Q}$$ where $\sigma_V$ is the standard deviation of net order flow and $\sigma_Q$ is the standard deviation of order size. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Meaning | Formula | Units / Range | |:------:|:-------:|:-------:|:-------------:| | $P_{\text{mid}}$ | Mid-price | $(P_b^{\text{best}} + P_a^{\text{best}})/2$ | Currency | | $S$ | Bid-ask spread | $P_a^{\text{best}} - P_b^{\text{best}}$ | Currency | | $S_{\text{rel}}$ | Relative spread | $S / P_{\text{mid}}$ | Dimensionless $\vert$ typically $0.01\%$–$1\%$ | | $P_{\text{micro}}$ | Microprice | $(Q_b P_a + Q_a P_b)/(Q_b + Q_a)$ | Currency | | $\text{OBI}$ | Order book imbalance | $(Q_b - Q_a)/(Q_b + Q_a)$ | $[-1, +1]$ | | $I(Q)$ | Square-root impact | $Y \sigma \sqrt{Q/V}$ | Currency | | $\lambda$ | Kyle's lambda | $\Delta P_{\text{perm}} / Q$ | Currency/share | | VWAP | Volume-weighted avg price | $\sum_i P_i q_i / \sum_i q_i$ | Currency | | Slippage | Execution slippage | $P_{\text{executed}} - P_{\text{expected}}$ | Currency | | $\eta$ | Resilience parameter | Decay rate of temp. impact | $\text{sec}^{-1}$ | ## 2.6 Real-World Utility - **Algorithmic Trading Firms**: Use the square-root law to **slice** large parent orders into smaller child orders to minimize cost. - **Central Banks**: Monitor market impact when conducting open-market operations. - **Exchanges (NSE/BSE)**: Use order book data to compute **real-time liquidity indices** and to design tick-size policies. - **Brokers**: Display **5-level depth** to retail clients (Level 2 data). - **High-Frequency Trading (HFT)**: Microprice is the foundation of statistical-arb strategies. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Microprice from First Principles **Setup**: Let $p_{\uparrow}$ be the probability of the next price tick being upward, and $p_{\downarrow} = 1 - p_{\uparrow}$. The expected next mid-price is: $$E[P_{\text{next}}] = p_{\uparrow} \cdot (P_{\text{mid}} + \text{tick}) + p_{\downarrow} \cdot (P_{\text{mid}} - \text{tick})$$ **Step 1**: Expand the expectation: $$E[P_{\text{next}}] = P_{\text{mid}} + (p_{\uparrow} - p_{\downarrow}) \cdot \text{tick}$$ **Step 2**: Substitute $p_{\downarrow} = 1 - p_{\uparrow}$: $$p_{\uparrow} - p_{\downarrow} = 2 p_{\uparrow} - 1$$ **Step 3**: Assume the upward-tick probability is **proportional to the bid-side depth** and the downward-tick probability is **proportional to the ask-side depth** (a standard microstructural assumption): $$p_{\uparrow} = \frac{Q_b^{\text{best}}}{Q_b^{\text{best}} + Q_a^{\text{best}}}, \quad p_{\downarrow} = \frac{Q_a^{\text{best}}}{Q_b^{\text{best}} + Q_a^{\text{best}}}$$ **Step 4**: Substitute back: $$p_{\uparrow} - p_{\downarrow} = \frac{Q_b^{\text{best}} - Q_a^{\text{best}}}{Q_b^{\text{best}} + Q_a^{\text{best}}} = \text{OBI}$$ **Step 5**: The microprice is defined as the **expected next mid-price**, so we replace $P_{\text{mid}} + (p_{\uparrow} - p_{\downarrow}) \cdot \text{tick}$ with the volume-weighted combination of $P_b^{\text{best}}$ and $P_a^{\text{best}}$: $$P_{\text{micro}} = \frac{Q_b^{\text{best}} \cdot P_a^{\text{best}} + Q_a^{\text{best}} \cdot P_b^{\text{best}}}{Q_b^{\text{best}} + Q_a^{\text{best}}}$$ **Step 6**: Final rearrangement, multiplying numerator and denominator by $-1$ in the second term to match the standard form: $$P_{\text{micro}} = P_{\text{mid}} + \frac{Q_b^{\text{best}} - Q_a^{\text{best}}}{2(Q_b^{\text{best}} + Q_a^{\text{best}})} \cdot S$$ This confirms that **microprice = mid-price + (half of spread) × OBI**. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Square-Root Law (Heuristic Argument) **Step 1**: Suppose a trader must execute order $Q$ over time horizon $T$. The average rate of execution is $v = Q/T$. **Step 2**: The instantaneous temporary impact is empirically linear in the **trading rate**: $$I_{\text{temp}}(t) = \kappa \cdot v(t)$$ **Step 3**: Assume the trader uses a constant-rate execution strategy: $v(t) = Q/T$ for $t \in [0, T]$. **Step 4**: The price impact is the **time-integrated** effect. If impact decays exponentially with rate $\eta$ (resilience), then a unit impulse at time $\tau$ contributes $e^{-\eta(t-\tau)}$ to the impact at time $t > \tau$. **Step 5**: The total impact at time $t$ due to a continuous stream of trades from $0$ to $T$ is: $$I(t) = \kappa \int_0^{\min(t,T)} v(\tau) \, e^{-\eta(t-\tau)} \, d\tau$$ **Step 6**: Substituting $v(\tau) = Q/T$: $$I(t) = \frac{\kappa Q}{T} \int_0^{\min(t,T)} e^{-\eta(t-\tau)} \, d\tau$$ **Step 7**: For $t \geq T$ (after execution is complete), the upper limit is $T$: $$I(T) = \frac{\kappa Q}{T} \int_0^{T} e^{-\eta(T-\tau)} \, d\tau = \frac{\kappa Q}{T} \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-\eta T}}{\eta}$$ **Step 8**: As $T \to \infty$ (very long execution), $1 - e^{-\eta T} \to 1$, and we get $I \to \kappa Q / (\eta T)$. But total trade size is $Q = vT$, so substituting: $$I \propto \frac{1}{T} = \frac{v}{Q}$$ This gives an alternative form: $I \propto \sqrt{v}$ (and hence $\propto \sqrt{Q/T}$) when combined with volatility scaling, recovering the empirical square-root form: $$I(Q, T) = Y \sigma \sqrt{\frac{Q}{V}} \cdot f(T)$$ ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Order Book Impact > [!NOTE] > **Numerical Case Study** — typical KTU 14-mark problem pattern. **Given**: - Best Bid: $P_b^{\text{best}} = \text{₹}1{,}000.00$, Quantity = $Q_b^{\text{best}} = 500$ shares - Best Ask: $P_a^{\text{best}} = \text{₹}1{,}000.50$, Quantity = $Q_a^{\text{best}} = 200$ shares - Second-level Bid: $P_b^{(2)} = \text{₹}999.50$, Quantity = $Q_b^{(2)} = 1{,}000$ shares - Daily volatility: $\sigma = 0.02$ (i.e., $2\%$) - Average daily volume: $V = 1{,}000{,}000$ shares - Order to execute: $Q = 100{,}000$ shares (a buy) - $Y = 0.6$ **Required**: Compute mid-price, microprice, OBI, total cost of walking the book, and expected market impact. **Solution**: **Step 1 — Mid-price**: $$P_{\text{mid}} = \frac{1000.00 + 1000.50}{2} = \text{₹}1{,}000.25$$ **Step 2 — Order Book Imbalance**: $$\text{OBI} = \frac{500 - 200}{500 + 200} = \frac{300}{700} \approx 0.4286$$ Since OBI is positive, the book is **bid-heavy**, predicting upward price pressure. The order to buy adds to this pressure. **Step 3 — Microprice**: $$P_{\text{micro}} = \frac{(500)(1000.50) + (200)(1000.00)}{500 + 200}$$ $$= \frac{500250 + 200000}{700} = \frac{700250}{700} = \text{₹}1{,}000.3571$$ **Step 4 — Spread**: $$S = 1000.50 - 1000.00 = \text{₹}0.50$$ **Step 5 — Cost of "Walking the Book"** (naive market order of 100,000 shares): - First, consume all 200 shares at ₹1,000.50 → cost = $200 \times 1000.50 = \text{₹}200{,}100$ - Then, consume all 1,000 shares at level 2 (₹999.50, but we are buying, so we take the *next* ask level — assume it's also ₹1,000.50 + tick sizes — but for simplicity, take the second ask at ₹1,001.00): - Cost = $99{,}000 \times 1001.00 = \text{₹}99{,}099{,}000$ Total naive cost $\approx \text{₹}99.30$ lakhs vs. ideal cost of $100{,}000 \times 1000.25 = \text{₹}10.025$ crores. Slippage $\approx \text{₹}0.275$ per share $\approx 0.0275\%$. **Step 6 — Square-Root Market Impact**: $$I(Q) = 0.6 \times 0.02 \times \sqrt{\frac{100000}{1000000}} = 0.012 \times \sqrt{0.1}$$ $$= 0.012 \times 0.3162 = 0.00379 = 0.379\%$$ **Step 7 — Total expected cost per share** (impact in ₹): $$I_{\text{rupees}} = 0.00379 \times 1000.25 = \text{₹}3.79 \text{ per share}$$ So the trader pays an extra $\text{₹}3.79$ per share due to market impact. The total cost beyond the mid-price is: $$C_{\text{impact}} = 100000 \times 3.79 = \text{₹}3{,}79{,}000$$ ## 3.4 Resolving Permanent vs Temporary Impact — Algebraic Split For a meta-order of size $Q$, define total impact $I_{\text{tot}}(Q)$. The empirical decomposition is: $$I_{\text{tot}}(Q) = \underbrace{a \cdot \sigma \sqrt{Q/V}}_{I_{\text{temp}}} + \underbrace{b \cdot \sigma \sqrt{Q/V}}_{I_{\text{perm}}}$$ with empirically $b/a \approx 0.5$ to $0.7$, so permanent impact is typically **half** of total impact. The **information content** of the trade is captured by the permanent part; the temporary part is the **dealer mark-up** for providing immediacy. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Order Book Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph EXCH["EXCHANGE MATCHING ENGINE"] direction TB nodeOB["CENTRAL ORDER BOOK"] end subgraph BID["BID SIDE - BUY ORDERS"] direction TB nodeB1["Level 1: Price 1000.00 - Qty 500"] nodeB2["Level 2: Price 999.50 - Qty 1000"] nodeB3["Level 3: Price 999.00 - Qty 1500"] nodeB4["Level N: ... - Qty ..."] end subgraph ASK["ASK SIDE - SELL ORDERS"] direction TB nodeA1["Level 1: Price 1000.50 - Qty 200"] nodeA2["Level 2: Price 1001.00 - Qty 800"] nodeA3["Level 3: Price 1001.50 - Qty 1200"] nodeA4["Level N: ... - Qty ..."] end subgraph TRADER["TRADER / ALGO AGENT"] nodeT1["Submits Market Order Q = 100000"] nodeT2["Reads Best Bid / Best Ask"] nodeT3["Estimates Impact via Square Root Law"] nodeT4["Slices into Child Orders"] end nodeT1 --> nodeOB nodeOB --> nodeB1 nodeOB --> nodeA1 nodeT2 --> nodeT3 nodeT3 --> nodeT4 nodeT4 --> nodeOB nodeB1 --> nodeOB nodeB2 --> nodeOB nodeB3 --> nodeOB nodeB4 --> nodeOB nodeA1 --> nodeOB nodeA2 --> nodeOB nodeA3 --> nodeOB nodeA4 --> nodeOB ``` ## 4.2 Market Impact Cost Decomposition (Schematic Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeStart["Pre-Trade Mid-Price P_mid = 1000.25"] nodeTemp["Temporary Impact
I_temp = 0.20 percent
Dealer spread + Inventory"] nodePerm["Permanent Impact
I_perm = 0.18 percent
Information signal"] nodeFinal["Executed VWAP = 1001.05"] nodeRevert["Post-Trade Re-equilibration
Spreads narrow, depth restores"] nodeStart --> nodeTemp nodeTemp --> nodePerm nodePerm --> nodeFinal nodeFinal --> nodeRevert ``` ## 4.3 Order Routing & Impact Minimization Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeQ["Parent Order Q = 500000 shares"] nodeDecide{"Is ADV
2 percent ADV ?"} nodeSlice["Use TWAP / VWAP slicer
Over T = 30 minutes"] nodeAggressive["Use Liquidity-Seeking Algo
Is / Iceberg orders"] nodeMonitor["Monitor Slippage Real-Time
Compare to I_predicted"] nodeEnd1["End of Execution
Record Shortfall"] nodeQ --> nodeDecide nodeDecide -- Yes --> nodeSlice nodeDecide -- No --> nodeAggressive nodeSlice --> nodeMonitor nodeAggressive --> nodeMonitor nodeMonitor --> nodeEnd1 ``` ## 4.4 Bid-Ask Spread & Microprice Geometry ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SPREAD["SPREAD STRUCTURE"] nodeMidA["P_mid = 1000.25
Midpoint"] nodeBidA["P_b_best = 1000.00
500 shares waiting"] nodeAskA["P_a_best = 1000.50
200 shares waiting"] nodeMicroA["P_micro = 1000.36
Shifted toward ask"] end nodeBidA --> nodeMidA nodeMidA --> nodeAskA nodeMidA --> nodeMicroA nodeAskA -.-> nodeMicroA nodeBidA -.-> nodeMicroA ``` > [!NOTE] > The microprice sits *closer to the ask* than the mid-price because the ask side has fewer shares (200) than the bid side (500), implying a greater probability of an upward price move. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]** **Define market impact and distinguish between temporary and permanent impact.** **Model Answer** (3 marks — KTU expects 1.5 marks for definition, 1.5 marks for distinction): Market impact is the change in an asset's price caused by the execution of a trade, measured as the difference between the executed price and the pre-trade mid-price. It has two components: - **Temporary Impact**: The price concession required by liquidity providers (dealers/market makers) for **immediacy**; it **decays** after trade completion as new liquidity replenishes the order book. - **Permanent Impact**: The persistent price shift that reflects the **information content** of the trade; other market participants update their valuations after observing the trade, and the price does not revert. (3 marks) ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]** **What is the bid-ask spread and why is it considered a measure of liquidity?** **Model Answer** (3 marks): The bid-ask spread is the difference between the **best ask (lowest sell price)** and the **best bid (highest buy price)** in the order book: $S = P_a^{\text{best}} - P_b^{\text{best}}$. It is a **liquidity measure** because: 1. A **narrow spread** indicates abundant liquidity — many buyers and sellers are competing at similar prices, so trades can be executed cheaply. 2. A **wide spread** indicates illiquidity — few participants, high risk for dealers, hence a larger compensation is required for providing immediacy. 3. The spread represents the **minimum transaction cost** for a round-trip trade (buy + sell). (3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A — 14 Marks #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]** **Explain the structure of the limit order book with a suitable diagram. Define mid-price, microprice, and order book imbalance. Using the data below, compute all three quantities.** - Best Bid: $P_b = \text{₹}500.00$, $Q_b = 800$ shares - Best Ask: $P_a = \text{₹}500.20$, $Q_a = 400$ shares **Model Solution**: **Step 1 — Define the limit order book (3 marks)**: A limit order book is a real-time, two-sided queue of buy (bid) and sell (ask) limit orders, sorted by **price-time priority**. Bids are arranged in descending price order; asks in ascending price order. At each price level, orders follow FIFO (First-In-First-Out) priority. A limit order at price $P_{\text{limit}}$ rests on the book and earns the bid-ask spread, while a market order executes immediately against the best available counterparty. **Step 2 — Mid-price (1 mark)**: $$P_{\text{mid}} = \frac{P_b + P_a}{2} = \frac{500.00 + 500.20}{2} = \text{₹}500.10$$ **Step 3 — Order book imbalance (1 mark)**: $$\text{OBI} = \frac{Q_b - Q_a}{Q_b + Q_a} = \frac{800 - 400}{800 + 400} = \frac{400}{1200} = 0.3333$$ **Step 4 — Microprice (2 marks)**: $$P_{\text{micro}} = \frac{Q_b \cdot P_a + Q_a \cdot P_b}{Q_b + Q_a} = \frac{(800)(500.20) + (400)(500.00)}{1200}$$ $$= \frac{400160 + 200000}{1200} = \frac{600160}{1200} = \text{₹}500.1333$$ **Interpretation (1 mark)**: The microprice is shifted *upward* (toward the ask) relative to the mid-price because the bid side has more depth. This predicts a higher probability of an upward tick in the next price update. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **State and explain the Square-Root Law of market impact. A trader wants to buy $Q = 50{,}000$ shares of a stock whose daily volatility is $\sigma = 0.025$ and average daily volume is $V = 5{,}00{,}000$ shares. Assuming $Y = 0.7$, compute the expected market impact. Comment on the practical implications.** **Model Solution**: **Step 1 — Statement of the Law (2 marks)**: The Square-Root Law (Bouchaud et al., 2010) states that market impact grows as the **square root** of the order size, normalized by average daily volume: $$I(Q) = Y \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{\frac{Q}{V}}$$ **Step 2 — Explanation (2 marks)**: The square-root behavior arises because: - Large orders must be spread over longer time horizons, allowing liquidity providers to replenish the order book. - The impact-per-share therefore decreases with order size (economies of scale in execution). - The empirical constants $Y$ are typically in the range $0.5$–$1.0$ for liquid equities. **Step 3 — Computation (2 marks)**: $$I(Q) = 0.7 \times 0.025 \times \sqrt{\frac{50000}{500000}}$$ $$= 0.0175 \times \sqrt{0.1} = 0.0175 \times 0.3162 = 0.005534$$ So the expected market impact is approximately **$0.553\%$** of the asset price. If the current mid-price is, say, ₹200, the impact cost per share is: $$I_{\text{rupees}} = 0.005534 \times 200 = \text{₹}1.11 \text{ per share}$$ Total impact cost = $50{,}000 \times 1.11 = \text{₹}55{,}500$. **Step 4 — Practical implications (1 mark)**: - A 10× larger order ($Q = 5{,}00{,}000$) would cause only $\sqrt{10} \approx 3.16 \times$ more impact (not 10×). - This motivates **algorithmic slicing** — splitting parent orders into smaller child orders across time to reduce cost. - More volatile stocks ($\sigma$ larger) have proportionally larger impact cost. --- ### Question B — 14 Marks *(Alternative Choice)* #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]** **Discuss Kyle's Lambda model. What does it measure, and how is it estimated empirically?** **Model Solution**: **Step 1 — Setup (2 marks)**: Kyle (1985) developed a model in which a single informed trader submits a market order of size $Q$, and a market maker sets the execution price to break even. The model yields a **linear** relationship: $$\Delta P = \lambda \cdot Q$$ where $\lambda$ is called **Kyle's Lambda** (or "price impact coefficient"). **Step 2 — Interpretation (2 marks)**: - $\lambda$ measures the **permanently-permanent price impact** of one additional share of net order flow. - Higher $\lambda$ → less depth → less liquid market. - Lower $\lambda$ → more depth → more liquid market. - For liquid U.S. equities, $\lambda$ is typically in the range $0.0001$ to $0.001$ USD per share per 1000 shares traded. **Step 3 — Empirical Estimation (2 marks)**: Regressing price changes on signed order flow: $$\Delta P_t = \lambda \cdot Q_t^{\text{net}} + \epsilon_t$$ The OLS estimator $\hat{\lambda}$ is obtained. An alternative formula uses: $$\lambda = \frac{\sigma_{\Delta P}}{\sigma_Q}$$ where $\sigma_{\Delta P}$ is the standard deviation of price changes and $\sigma_Q$ is the standard deviation of order flow. **Step 4 — KTU-Requisite Discussion (1 mark)**: Kyle's Lambda is widely used by regulators to assess market quality, by exchanges to design maker-taker fee structures, and by algorithmic traders to estimate the cost of liquidity-taking. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **A stock has the following order book snapshot:** - Bid: $\text{₹}200.00 \times 1{,}000$ and $\text{₹}199.90 \times 2{,}000$ - Ask: $\text{₹}200.10 \times 500$ and $\text{₹}200.20 \times 1{,}500$ **Compute (i) the mid-price, (ii) the bid-ask spread, (iii) the cost of a market buy order of 1,500 shares, (iv) the VWAP of that execution.** **Model Solution**: **Step 1 — Mid-price (1.5 marks)**: $$P_{\text{mid}} = \frac{200.00 + 200.10}{2} = \text{₹}200.05$$ **Step 2 — Bid-ask spread (1.5 marks)**: $$S = 200.10 - 200.00 = \text{₹}0.10$$ **Step 3 — Cost of market buy of 1,500 shares (2.5 marks)**: - 500 shares at ₹200.10 = $\text{₹}100{,}050$ - 1,000 shares at ₹200.20 = $\text{₹}200{,}200$ - **Total cost** = $\text{₹}300{,}250$ **Step 4 — VWAP (1.5 marks)**: $$\text{VWAP} = \frac{(500)(200.10) + (1000)(200.20)}{1500} = \frac{100050 + 200200}{1500}$$ $$= \frac{300250}{1500} = \text{₹}200.1667$$ **Slippage relative to mid** = $200.1667 - 200.05 = \text{₹}0.1167$ per share. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make on This Topic**: > 1. **Confusing VWAP with simple average price** — VWAP must be *volume-weighted*. Marks are lost if you compute $(P_1 + P_2)/2$ instead of $\sum P_i q_i / \sum q_i$. > 2. **Forgetting the square-root in the impact law** — using $I = Y \sigma (Q/V)$ (linear form) instead of $I = Y \sigma \sqrt{Q/V}$ is a **3-mark deduction** in 14-mark questions. > 3. **Mixing up bid-ask notation** — $P_a$ is the *ask* (sell side), not "the price at which you ask." Examiners are strict about this. > 4. **Ignoring units** — If volatility is in percent (e.g., $2\%$), the impact will also be in percent; convert appropriately. > 5. **Missing the time dimension** — Kyle's Lambda is *per-share*; the impact in ₹ requires multiplying by $Q$. > 6. **In 7-mark sub-parts**, do not write paragraphs without formulas — KTU values **equations** above prose. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [ ] **Order Book**: Real-time, two-sided queue of limit orders; sorted by **price priority**, then by **time priority (FIFO)**. - [ ] **Best Bid / Best Ask**: Highest buy price / Lowest sell price visible in the order book. - [ ] **Mid-Price**: Arithmetic average of best bid and best ask — used as the "fair" pre-trade reference. - [ ] **Microprice**: Volume-weighted fair price that anticipates the next tick; incorporates order book imbalance. - [ ] **Order Book Imbalance (OBI)**: $(Q_b - Q_a)/(Q_b + Q_a)$; positive values predict upward price pressure. - [ ] **Bid-Ask Spread**: $S = P_a - P_b$; the minimum round-trip transaction cost; **narrower = more liquid**. - [ ] **Market Impact**: $\Delta P = P_{\text{executed}} - P_{\text{pre-trade}}$; has temporary and permanent components. - [ ] **Temporary Impact**: Decays after trade; reflects **dealer compensation for immediacy**. - [ ] **Permanent Impact**: Persists; reflects **information content** of the trade. - [ ] **Square-Root Law**: $I(Q) = Y \sigma \sqrt{Q/V}$ — empirically validated across asset classes. - [ ] **Kyle's Lambda**: $\lambda$ measures market depth; higher $\lambda$ → less liquid market. - [ ] **VWAP**: Volume-weighted average execution price — standard benchmark for execution quality. - [ ] **Slippage**: $P_{\text{executed}} - P_{\text{expected}}$ — the hidden cost of market orders. - [ ] **High-Yield Tip for KTU**: Always **state the formula first**, then substitute numerical values. Examiners allocate 1–2 marks purely for the correct formula even if the arithmetic goes wrong. - [ ] **Pitfall to Avoid**: Confusing *microprice* with *microstructure* — these are distinct concepts.

topics.title: Optimal Execution - Sell Side Perspective
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Optimal Execution - Sell Side Perspective

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimal Execution (Sell-Side Perspective):** A market microstructure framework that defines the strategic process by which **sell-side intermediaries** (broker-dealers, market makers, and execution desks) **minimize the total transaction cost** of liquidating or acquiring large asset positions on behalf of their **buy-side clients** (institutional investors, mutual funds, hedge funds, pension funds), while simultaneously managing **inventory risk, adverse selection, information leakage, and market impact** within the constraints of regulatory best-execution mandates.

From the KTU 2024 Scheme standpoint, the **sell side** of a financial market consists of entities that **create, promote, price, and execute** transactions, and they earn revenue through **bid-ask spreads, commissions, principal trading profits, and liquidity provision rebates**. The "optimal" in optimal execution refers to the **cost-minimizing equilibrium** that balances the **urgency of execution** against the **market impact cost** of trading.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Mapping (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> - **Sell-Side Intermediary** = Broker-Dealer Firm / Execution Desk
> - **Buy-Side Client** = Institutional Investor placing the order
> - **Parent Order** = The total quantity the client wishes to trade
> - **Child Orders** = The slices into which the parent order is broken
> - **Benchmarks** = VWAP, TWAP, Arrival Price, Implementation Shortfall

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Grocery Store Liquidity Vendor

Imagine a wholesale fruit vendor (the **sell-side broker**) who has been asked by a large hotel chain (the **buy-side client**) to procure **50,000 kilograms of apples** over the course of one day.

If the broker walks into the local fruit market and shouts, *"I want 50,000 kg of apples RIGHT NOW!"*, three things will happen almost immediately:

1. The price of apples will **shoot up** because the sudden demand has overwhelmed the supply on display (this is **temporary market impact**).
2. Other apple merchants, sensing a big buyer is in town, will **raise their quoted prices** for the rest of the day (this is **permanent market impact / information leakage**).
3. The broker will be **forced to pay more** than if the order had been spread out.

A **smart (optimal) broker**, instead, will:
- Buy **smaller baskets** every few minutes (this is **order slicing / child order placement**).
- Use **disguised orders** so other merchants don't realize the hotel is the ultimate buyer (this is **using dark pools / iceberg orders**).
- Carefully **track the average price** paid to ensure it is close to the day's market average (this is **VWAP benchmarking**).
- **Adjust the pace** of buying based on how much apple volume is naturally flowing in the market (this is **Percentage-of-Volume (POV) algorithms**).

This is **optimal execution from the sell-side perspective**: the broker is the **strategic agent** of execution, balancing speed, stealth, and cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Trade-Off (The Fundamental Equation of Execution):**
> $$\text{Total Execution Cost} \;=\; \underbrace{\text{Market Impact Cost}}_{\text{The faster you trade, the higher this is}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{Timing Risk (Opportunity Cost)}}_{\text{The slower you trade, the higher this is}}$$
> The sell-side desk's job is to find the **sweet spot** in the middle, known as the **optimal trading trajectory**.

### 1.3 The Sell-Side Ecosystem at a Glance

| Sell-Side Entity | Primary Revenue Source | Role in Optimal Execution |
|---|---|---|
| **Investment Banks (Bulge Bracket)** | Commissions, Advisory Fees, Prop Trading | Run large institutional execution desks |
| **Broker-Dealers** | Bid-Ask Spread, Commissions | Provide direct market access |
| **Market Makers / Liquidity Providers** | Spread Capture, Rebates | Post continuous two-sided quotes |
| **Electronic Communication Networks (ECNs)** | Access Fees, Rebates | Match orders algorithmically |
| **Dark Pool Operators** | Subscriber Fees, Access Fees | Match large blocks anonymously |
| **Algorithmic Trading Vendors** | Software Licensing | Provide execution algorithms (VWAP, TWAP, IS) |

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics Used by Sell-Side Desks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Pillars of Execution Quality Measurement:**
> 1. **Implementation Shortfall (IS)** — measures the cost of execution vs. the price at the moment the decision to trade was made (the "arrival price"). This is the **gold-standard** performance metric.
> 2. **VWAP (Volume-Weighted Average Price)** — measures the average price of the asset over a benchmark period, weighted by volume.
> 3. **TWAP (Time-Weighted Average Price)** — the simple average price over a benchmark period.
> 4. **Slippage** — the difference between the expected fill price and the actual fill price for each child order.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Anatomy of an Execution Cost — Theoretical Decomposition

A sell-side desk models the **total transaction cost** of a large order as the sum of several rigorously defined components. Each component has a behavioral cause and a mathematical representation.

#### Step 1: Fee and Commission Component (Explicit Cost)
This is the **direct, visible cost** charged by the broker for executing the trade. It is a function of the notional value traded and the brokerage fee structure.

$$C_{\text{commission}} \;=\; \phi \cdot \lvert Q \rvert \cdot P_{\text{avg}}$$

where $\phi$ is the commission rate, $Q$ is the order quantity, and $P_{\text{avg}}$ is the average execution price.

#### Step 2: Spread Cost Component (Implicit Cost — Half-Spread Capture)
When a market order is executed, the buy-side crosses the **bid-ask spread**, paying the offer (ask) when buying or selling at the bid. The half-spread is therefore the **baseline implicit cost** of demanding immediate liquidity.

$$C_{\text{spread}} \;=\; \frac{S_0}{2} \cdot \lvert Q \rvert$$

where $S_0$ is the quoted bid-ask spread at the time of order arrival.

#### Step 3: Temporary Market Impact (Price Pressure from the Order)
This is the **transient price movement** caused by the order's own demand for liquidity. It disappears shortly after the order is completed. It is modeled as proportional to the **rate of trading**.

$$C_{\text{temp}} \;=\; \eta \cdot \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{q_t^{2}}{V_t}$$

where $\eta$ is the temporary impact coefficient, $q_t$ is the quantity traded in child order $t$, and $V_t$ is the market volume during the same interval. The **quadratic form** $q_t^2$ reflects the convexity of impact: doubling the child order size **quadruples** the impact.

#### Step 4: Permanent Market Impact (Information Leakage)
This is the **persistent price shift** caused by the market's inference of information from the trade flow. It is modeled linearly in the order flow imbalance.

$$C_{\text{perm}} \;=\; \gamma \cdot \lvert Q \rvert \cdot \Delta P_{\text{permanent}}$$

where $\gamma$ is the permanent impact coefficient.

#### Step 5: Timing Risk (Opportunity Cost of Delayed Execution)
The risk that the price will move adversely during the execution window. It is measured by the **variance of the price path** during the trading horizon.

$$\sigma_{\text{timing}}^{2} \;=\; \sigma^{2} \cdot T_{\text{horizon}}$$

where $\sigma$ is the daily volatility and $T$ is the remaining trading time in days.

#### Step 6: Missed Trade Cost (Non-Execution Cost)
If a limit order is not filled, the opportunity cost equals the price movement during the unfilled window.

### 2.2 The Almgren-Chriss Optimal Execution Framework (Cornerstone Model)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Almgren-Chriss (2000) model is the foundational theoretical framework for sell-side optimal execution, widely used in industry and taught across KTU-aligned syllabi.**

The model defines an **efficient frontier** of execution strategies, and the sell-side desk chooses a point on this frontier based on the **client's risk aversion**.

**Assumptions of the model:**
- The price evolves with **Brownian motion** plus **linear permanent impact**.
- Temporary impact is a **convex function** of the trading rate.
- The trader's utility is **mean-variance** (mean return minus $\lambda$ times variance).

**The Optimal Trading Trajectory:**

For a liquidation of $X$ shares over a horizon $T$ divided into $N$ discrete intervals, the optimal number of shares to trade in interval $k$ is:

$$x_{k}^{*} \;=\; \frac{\sinh\!\left(\kappa \cdot (N - k)\right)}{\sinh(\kappa \cdot N)} \cdot X$$

where:

$$\kappa \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\lambda \cdot \sigma^{2}}{\eta}}$$

- $\lambda$ = **risk aversion parameter** of the client (set by the sell-side desk in consultation).
- $\sigma^{2}$ = variance of the price process.
- $\eta$ = temporary impact coefficient.
- $\sinh(\cdot)$ = hyperbolic sine function.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Boundary Cases of the Almgren-Chriss Trajectory:**
> - When $\lambda \to 0$ (risk-neutral trader): the trajectory becomes a **flat TWAP** (uniform slice over time).
> - When $\lambda \to \infty$ (extremely risk-averse trader): the trajectory concentrates trading in the **very first interval** (essentially an immediate market order).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference Table — Optimal Execution (Sell Side)**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Variables | Engineering/Finance Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Implementation Shortfall (bps) | $\text{IS} = \frac{P_{\text{decision}} - P_{\text{executed}}}{P_{\text{decision}}} \times 10{,}000$ | bps (basis points) | Gold-standard performance metric for sell-side TCA reports |
| 2 | VWAP Benchmark | $\text{VWAP} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{i} \cdot V_{i}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} V_{i}}$ | Price unit (e.g., ₹, $) | Used as benchmark for agency execution quality |
| 3 | TWAP Benchmark | $\text{TWAP} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{i}$ | Price unit | Used for orders with no volume signal |
| 4 | Permanent Impact | $\Delta P_{\text{perm}} = \gamma \cdot v$ | ₹/share | Models information leakage cost |
| 5 | Temporary Impact | $\Delta P_{\text{temp}} = \eta \cdot \frac{v}{V}$ | ₹/share | Models price pressure from child orders |
| 6 | Almgren-Chriss Trajectory | $x_{k}^{*} = X \cdot \frac{\sinh(\kappa(N-k))}{\sinh(\kappa N)}$ | Number of shares | Optimal slicing of parent order |
| 7 | Almgren-Chriss $\kappa$ | $\kappa = \sqrt{\frac{\lambda \sigma^{2}}{\eta}}$ | Dimensionless | Controls urgency of execution |
| 8 | Total Expected Cost (AC) | $E[C] = \eta \sum \frac{q_{k}^{2}}{V_{k}} + \gamma \sum q_{k}\Delta P$ | ₹ | Direct + indirect execution cost |
| 9 | Cost Variance | $\text{Var}(C) = \sigma^{2} X^{2} \tau$ | ₹² | Timing risk for a remaining time $\tau$ |
| 10 | Bid-Ask Spread | $S = P_{\text{ask}} - P_{\text{bid}}$ | ₹/share | Minimum implicit cost of immediacy |
| 11 | Effective Spread | $S_{\text{eff}} = 2 \cdot \lvert P_{\text{exec}} - M_{\text{mid}} \rvert$ | ₹/share | Realized spread after execution |
| 12 | Realized Spread | $S_{\text{real}} = 2 \cdot (P_{\text{exec}} - M_{\text{mid},\,T})$ | ₹/share | Profit retained by market maker after T periods |
| 13 | Kyle's Lambda | $\lambda = \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta(\text{signed order flow})}$ | ₹ per ₹ of order flow | Price impact per unit of net buying |
| 14 | Order-to-Trade Ratio | $\text{OTR} = \frac{\text{Orders Submitted}}{\text{Trades Executed}}$ | Ratio | Market quality / liquidity measure |
| 15 | Market Depth | $D = \sum_{i} \left( V_{i}^{\text{ask}} \cdot P_{i}^{\text{ask}} + V_{i}^{\text{bid}} \cdot P_{i}^{\text{bid}} \right)$ | ₹ | Liquidity available at top-of-book |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always express execution cost in **basis points (bps)** for board exams, as KTU examiners award full marks only when the unit is explicitly stated. **1 bps = 0.01%**.

### 2.4 Why This Matters in Real Engineering / Finance Practice

The sell-side optimal execution problem is mathematically equivalent to an **optimal control problem** in control systems engineering:

- **State variable** = remaining inventory to liquidate.
- **Control variable** = trading rate at each time step.
- **Objective function** = minimize expected cost + $\lambda \cdot$ variance.
- **Constraint** = complete the trade within horizon $T$.

This is why quantitative analysts (quants) on sell-side desks often have **engineering, physics, or applied mathematics backgrounds** rather than pure finance training. The KTU 2024 syllabus consciously includes this topic to bridge **engineering optimization** with **financial application**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Almgren-Chriss Optimal Trading Trajectory

We will derive the optimal schedule for liquidating $X$ shares over $N$ discrete time steps, with each step of length $\tau = T/N$.

**Step 1 — Define the State Variables**

Let $x_k$ be the number of shares **remaining** to be sold at the beginning of interval $k$, and let $n_k$ be the number of shares sold in interval $k$ (the control variable). The inventory dynamics are:

$$x_{k} \;=\; x_{k-1} \;-\; n_{k}, \qquad x_{0} \;=\; X, \qquad x_{N} \;=\; 0$$

**Step 2 — Specify the Price Evolution**

The mid-price $P_k$ evolves as a random walk plus a **permanent impact** term proportional to the trade size:

$$P_{k} \;=\; P_{k-1} \;+\; \sigma \sqrt{\tau} \cdot \varepsilon_{k} \;-\; \gamma \cdot n_{k}$$

where $\varepsilon_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ is the standard normal innovation, $\sigma$ is the daily volatility scaled to the interval, and $\gamma$ is the permanent impact coefficient.

The **execution price** in interval $k$ (the price actually received when the child order is filled) is below the mid-price by the temporary impact:

$$\tilde{P}_{k} \;=\; P_{k-1} \;-\; \eta \cdot \frac{n_{k}}{\tau} \;-\; \gamma \cdot n_{k}$$

The factor $\frac{n_k}{\tau}$ is the trading rate, and $\eta$ is the temporary impact coefficient (in units of ₹ per share per (shares/second)).

**Step 3 — Express the Total Cost of Liquidation**

The total cost is the difference between the initial paper value $X \cdot P_0$ and the actual cash received:

$$C \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{N} n_{k} \cdot \tilde{P}_{k} \;-\; X \cdot P_0$$

Substituting the execution price and using the inventory dynamics, the expected cost simplifies to:

$$\mathbb{E}[C] \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{N} \eta \cdot \frac{n_{k}^{2}}{\tau} \;+\; \gamma \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{N} n_{k}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Express the Cost Variance (Timing Risk)**

The variance arises from the random-walk component of the price evolution, accumulating as the trade progresses. For a remaining inventory $x_k$, the variance of the price change over the remaining horizon is $\sigma^{2} \cdot (N - k) \cdot \tau$. Therefore:

$$\text{Var}(C) \;=\; \sigma^{2} \cdot \tau \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{N} x_{k}^{2}$$

This is the **timing risk** — the longer the trade is spread out, the larger the price variance the trader is exposed to.

**Step 5 — Write the Mean-Variance Objective Function**

The trader (with risk-aversion $\lambda$) minimizes the **certainty equivalent cost**:

$$\min_{\{n_k\}} \; \mathbb{E}[C] \;+\; \lambda \cdot \text{Var}(C)$$

Substituting:

$$\min_{\{n_k\}} \; \sum_{k=1}^{N} \left( \eta \frac{n_{k}^{2}}{\tau} + \gamma n_{k}^{2} + \lambda \sigma^{2} \tau \, x_{k}^{2} \right)$$

subject to $x_k = x_{k-1} - n_k$, $x_0 = X$, $x_N = 0$.

**Step 6 — Set Up the Lagrangian**

Form the Lagrangian with multiplier $\mu_k$ for the inventory constraint:

$$\mathcal{L} \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{N} \left[ \eta \frac{n_{k}^{2}}{\tau} + \gamma n_{k}^{2} + \lambda \sigma^{2} \tau \, x_{k}^{2} + \mu_{k} (x_{k} - x_{k-1} + n_{k}) \right]$$

**Step 7 — Take the First-Order Condition (FOC)**

Differentiate with respect to $n_k$ (note $n_k = x_{k-1} - x_k$):

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial n_{k}} \;=\; 2 \eta \frac{n_{k}}{\tau} \;+\; 2 \gamma n_{k} \;+\; \mu_{k} \;=\; 0$$

Solving for $n_k$ in terms of $\mu_k$:

$$n_{k} \;=\; -\frac{\mu_{k}}{2(\eta/\tau + \gamma)}$$

**Step 8 — Derive the Recursion for Inventory**

Substitute $n_k = x_{k-1} - x_k$ into the FOC and eliminate $\mu_k$ by differencing adjacent equations. The result is a **second-order linear difference equation**:

$$x_{k+1} \;-\; \left( 2 + \frac{\lambda \sigma^{2} \tau^{2}}{\eta + \gamma \tau} \right) x_{k} \;+\; x_{k-1} \;=\; 0$$

**Step 9 — Solve the Difference Equation**

The characteristic equation gives the parameter:

$$\kappa \;=\; \cosh^{-1}\!\left( 1 + \frac{\lambda \sigma^{2} \tau^{2}}{2(\eta + \gamma \tau)} \right)$$

For small $\tau$ (continuous-time limit), this reduces to:

$$\kappa \;\approx\; \sqrt{\frac{\lambda \sigma^{2}}{\eta}}$$

The general solution satisfying $x_0 = X$ and $x_N = 0$ is:

$$\boxed{\, x_{k}^{*} \;=\; \frac{\sinh\!\left(\kappa \cdot (N - k)\right)}{\sinh(\kappa \cdot N)} \cdot X \,}$$

This is the **Almgren-Chriss optimal trading trajectory**, the cornerstone formula of sell-side optimal execution.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A sell-side desk must liquidate $X = 100{,}000$ shares of an illiquid stock over $T = 5$ days, with $N = 5$ daily intervals. Given $\sigma = 0.02$ (daily), $\eta = 2.5 \times 10^{-6}$, and a risk aversion of $\lambda = 2 \times 10^{-6}$, compute the optimal daily liquidation schedule.

**Step 1 — Compute $\kappa$:**

$$\kappa \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\lambda \sigma^{2}}{\eta}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{(2 \times 10^{-6})(0.02)^{2}}{2.5 \times 10^{-6}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 10^{-10}}{2.5 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$\kappa \;=\; \sqrt{3.2 \times 10^{-4}} \;\approx\; 0.01789$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Trajectory Formula:**

For $k = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$:

$$x_{k}^{*} \;=\; 100{,}000 \cdot \frac{\sinh(0.01789 \cdot (5 - k))}{\sinh(0.01789 \cdot 5)}$$

Computing numerator and denominator:

- $\sinh(0.08945) \approx 0.08953$
- $\sinh(0.01789 \cdot 5) = \sinh(0.08945) \approx 0.08953$

| Day $k$ | $\sinh(0.01789 \cdot (5-k))$ | $x_k^*$ (shares remaining) | $n_k = x_{k-1} - x_k$ (shares to sell) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $\sinh(0.08945) = 0.08953$ | 100,000 | — |
| 1 | $\sinh(0.07156) = 0.07164$ | 80,003 | 19,997 |
| 2 | $\sinh(0.05367) = 0.05372$ | 60,001 | 20,002 |
| 3 | $\sinh(0.03578) = 0.03580$ | 39,994 | 20,007 |
| 4 | $\sinh(0.01789) = 0.01789$ | 19,989 | 20,005 |
| 5 | $\sinh(0) = 0$ | 0 | 19,989 |

**Observation:** With this risk-aversion level, the strategy is close to **TWAP** (uniform slicing of ~20,000 shares per day).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Sell-Side Execution Algorithms

| Algorithm | Benchmark | Strategy Logic | When Used | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **TWAP** | Time | Uniform slicing over time | Low-volume, no volume signal | Predictable, low complexity | Ignores market volume profile |
| **VWAP** | Volume | Match historical volume curve | Liquid stocks, benchmarked orders | Disciplined participation | Front-/back-loading risk |
| **POV (Percentage of Volume)** | Live Volume | Maintain % of market volume | Momentum periods | Adapts to live conditions | Can under-execute if volume is low |
| **Implementation Shortfall (IS)** | Arrival Price | Front-load to minimize IS | Urgent orders, informed flow | Minimizes opportunity cost | Higher market impact |
| **Liquidity Seeking** | Various | Hunt for hidden liquidity | Large blocks, illiquid names | Minimizes impact | Information leakage risk |
| **Dark Aggregator** | Mid | Sweep dark pools first | Sensitive orders | Reduces footprint | Adverse selection risk |
| **Iceberg / Native** | VWAP / Mid | Show only small portion | Retail/medium institutional | Hides true size | Free option given to market |
