## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the concept of "Optimal Execution" from a sell-side perspective. Highlight the three primary cost components that a sell-side execution desk must minimize.**

**Model Answer:**

Optimal execution, from a sell-side perspective, refers to the strategic and algorithmic process by which a broker-dealer minimizes the **total transaction cost** of executing a large client order across one or more trading venues, while simultaneously managing inventory risk, information leakage, and market impact. The three primary cost components are:

1. **Market Impact Cost** — the price movement caused by the order's own footprint (permanent + temporary impact).
2. **Timing / Opportunity Cost** — the adverse price movement during the execution window.
3. **Spread Cost** — the cost of crossing the bid-ask spread to obtain immediate liquidity.

The sell-side desk balances these three against the **client's risk-aversion parameter** $\lambda$ to produce the **Almgren-Chriss optimal trading trajectory**.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for listing components + 1 for explaining balance with $\lambda$)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the difference between VWAP and Implementation Shortfall as execution benchmarks. Which one is considered a "pre-trade" benchmark and which is a "post-trade" benchmark? Justify.**

**Model Answer:**

| Benchmark | Definition | Pre/Post-Trade | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **VWAP** | $\text{VWAP} = \frac{\sum P_i V_i}{\sum V_i}$ | **Post-trade** | Measures how well the execution tracked the day's average market price |
| **Implementation Shortfall (IS)** | $\text{IS} = P_{\text{decision}} - P_{\text{executed}}$ | **Pre-trade anchored, post-trade measured** | Measures the cost of execution vs. the price when the decision to trade was made |

VWAP is a **post-trade benchmark** because it is computed only after the trading day completes. Implementation Shortfall is set at the **moment of decision** (pre-trade) but is **measured post-trade**. IS is the **gold-standard** in institutional execution quality measurement because it captures the opportunity cost of delay, whereas VWAP does not penalize the desk for trading too late.

*(3 marks: 1 for definitions + 1 for pre/post classification + 1 for justification of IS as gold standard)*

---

### PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** For all 14-mark questions in this module, KTU examiners follow a strict **3-tier rubric**:
> - **Tier 1 (7 marks — Part a):** Test conceptual clarity with at least one diagram/equation.
> - **Tier 2 (7 marks — Part b):** Test applied/numerical ability.
> - **Tier 3 (negative marking zone):** Confusing sell-side and buy-side perspectives, omitting units (bps), or stating the formula without explaining the trade-off will cost **2 marks** even if the rest is correct.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the **Almgren-Chriss framework** for optimal execution. Derive the optimal trading trajectory $x_k^{*}$ and clearly state the role of the **risk-aversion parameter $\lambda$** in shaping the execution schedule.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A sell-side desk is tasked with liquidating $X = 200{,}000$ shares of a mid-cap stock over a 4-day horizon, divided into $N = 4$ daily intervals. The stock's daily volatility is $\sigma = 0.025$, the temporary impact coefficient is $\eta = 2.0 \times 10^{-6}$, and the client's risk-aversion parameter is $\lambda = 1.5 \times 10^{-6}$.

Compute:
- The Almgren-Chriss parameter $\kappa$.
- The optimal number of shares to sell on each of the 4 days ($n_1, n_2, n_3, n_4$).
- The total expected temporary impact cost (in ₹) for the strategy.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Part (a) Model Answer — 7 Marks:**

The Almgren-Chriss model (Almgren and Chriss, 2000) is the foundational framework in sell-side optimal execution. It formulates the trader's problem as a **mean-variance optimization** of execution cost over a finite horizon $T$, broken into $N$ discrete intervals.

**Key Elements (3 marks):**
- The trader has inventory $X$ to liquidate and chooses a sequence $\{n_k\}_{k=1}^{N}$ where $n_k$ is the number of shares sold in interval $k$.
- The price $P_k$ follows a **discrete random walk** with volatility $\sigma$ and incurs a **permanent impact** $\gamma \cdot n_k$.
- The execution price incurs an additional **temporary impact** proportional to the trading rate: $\eta \cdot \frac{n_k}{\tau}$.

**Cost and Variance Expressions (2 marks):**
- Expected cost: $\mathbb{E}[C] = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \left( \eta \frac{n_k^2}{\tau} + \gamma n_k^2 \right)$
- Cost variance: $\text{Var}(C) = \sigma^2 \tau \sum_{k=1}^{N} x_k^2$, where $x_k$ is the remaining inventory at the start of interval $k$.

**Objective and Optimal Trajectory (2 marks):**
The trader minimizes $\mathbb{E}[C] + \lambda \cdot \text{Var}(C)$. The first-order conditions lead to a second-order linear difference equation whose closed-form solution is:

$$x_{k}^{*} \;=\; X \cdot \frac{\sinh(\kappa (N - k))}{\sinh(\kappa N)}, \quad \text{where} \quad \kappa = \sqrt{\frac{\lambda \sigma^{2}}{\eta}}$$

**Role of $\lambda$:** As $\lambda \to 0$, the trajectory converges to a **uniform TWAP** (low cost, high risk). As $\lambda \to \infty$, the trajectory concentrates all trading in the **first interval** (high cost, zero timing risk). The sell-side desk sets $\lambda$ based on the client's **urgency of execution** and the **market's expected volatility**.

---

**(b) Part (b) Model Answer — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\kappa$:**

$$\kappa \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\lambda \sigma^{2}}{\eta}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{(1.5 \times 10^{-6})(0.025)^{2}}{2.0 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$\kappa \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{(1.5 \times 10^{-6})(6.25 \times 10^{-4})}{2.0 \times 10^{-6}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{9.375 \times 10^{-10}}{2.0 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$\kappa \;=\; \sqrt{4.6875 \times 10^{-4}} \;\approx\; 0.02165$$

**Step 2 — Compute the trajectory values:**

For $N = 4$, compute $x_k^*$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$:

$$x_k^* \;=\; 200{,}000 \cdot \frac{\sinh(0.02165 \cdot (4 - k))}{\sinh(0.02165 \cdot 4)}$$

Denominator: $\sinh(0.0866) \approx 0.08669$

| $k$ | Argument $\kappa(4-k)$ | $\sinh(\cdot)$ | $x_k^*$ | $n_k = x_{k-1}^* - x_k^*$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0866 | 0.08669 | 200,000 | — |
| 1 | 0.06495 | 0.06501 | 149,995 | **50,005** |
| 2 | 0.04330 | 0.04332 | 99,944 | **50,051** |
| 3 | 0.02165 | 0.02165 | 49,945 | **49,999** |
| 4 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **49,945** |

**Step 3 — Daily Trading Schedule:**
- **Day 1: 50,005 shares**
- **Day 2: 50,051 shares**
- **Day 3: 49,999 shares**
- **Day 4: 49,945 shares**

**Step 4 — Total Expected Temporary Impact Cost:**

Assuming daily market volume $V_k = 500{,}000$ shares (uniform across days) and $\tau = 1$ day:

$$C_{\text{temp}} \;=\; \eta \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{4} \frac{n_k^{2}}{V_k} \;=\; (2.0 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot \frac{(50{,}005)^2 + (50{,}051)^2 + (49{,}999)^2 + (49{,}945)^2}{500{,}000}$$

Numerator: $2.5005 \times 10^9 + 2.5051 \times 10^9 + 2.4999 \times 10^9 + 2.4945 \times 10^9 = 1.000 \times 10^{10}$

$$C_{\text{temp}} \;=\; (2.0 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot \frac{1.0 \times 10^{10}}{500{,}000} \;=\; (2.0 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 20{,}000 \;=\; 40 \text{ ₹}$$

**Total Expected Temporary Impact Cost = ₹40** (2 marks for the cost calculation, 1 mark for unit).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Critically analyze the **sell-side revenue model** in financial markets. Explain how a broker-dealer earns from **(i) bid-ask spread capture, (ii) agency commissions, and (iii) payment for order flow (PFOF)**. Discuss how each revenue stream is affected by the rise of electronic and algorithmic trading.

**(b)** [7 Marks] The sell-side desk of XYZ Securities is benchmarking a $X = 500{,}000$ share buy order using the **VWAP algorithm** for the trading day. The market traded in 5 one-hour buckets with the following volumes and prices:

| Bucket | Volume (shares) | Average Price (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100,000 | 100.00 |
| 2 | 150,000 | 102.00 |
| 3 | 80,000 | 101.50 |
| 4 | 120,000 | 103.00 |
| 5 | 50,000 | 104.50 |

Compute the **market VWAP**. If XYZ Securities actually achieved an average execution price of ₹101.20, compute the **VWAP slippage** in basis points and interpret whether the desk **outperformed or underperformed** the benchmark.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Part (a) Model Answer — 7 Marks:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Sell-side firms generate revenue through three primary streams tied directly to their role as **intermediaries** between buyers and sellers.

**(i) Bid-Ask Spread Capture (2 marks):**
The dealer buys at the bid price and sells at the ask price, capturing the spread $S = P_{\text{ask}} - P_{\text{bid}}$ as profit. In a quote-driven market, this is the dominant revenue source. With the rise of electronic trading, **spread capture has compressed** dramatically (sometimes to fractions of a basis point) due to high-speed competition among market makers and the migration of flow to **ECNs and dark pools** where spreads are minimal.

**(ii) Agency Commissions (2 marks):**
For agency execution (executing on behalf of clients without taking principal risk), brokers charge a **commission per share** or per notional value. This is a **fee-for-service** model. Algorithmic trading has commoditized this revenue: large institutional clients now demand **commission unbundling** (paying separately for execution, research, and clearing), and execution-only commissions have declined to fractions of a paisa per share.

**(iii) Payment for Order Flow (PFOF) (2 marks):**
Some sell-side firms (notably in US equities, e.g., Robinhood → Citadel) receive **payment from wholesalers** in exchange for routing retail orders to them. PFOF is **controversial** because it creates a potential conflict of interest — the broker may not be routing to the venue offering the best price, but to the one paying the most. In India, SEBI prohibits PFOF in its pure form, but **rebate structures** on exchanges serve a similar economic function.

**Conclusion (Optional, extra credit):** Sell-side firms have been forced to diversify into **algorithmic execution services, data analytics, and TCA (Transaction Cost Analysis) products** to maintain revenue as traditional spread and commission income declines.

---

**(b) Part (b) Model Answer — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute Market VWAP (4 marks):**

$$\text{VWAP} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5} P_{i} V_{i}}{\sum_{i=1}^{5} V_{i}}$$

Numerator:

$$\sum P_i V_i = (100 \cdot 100{,}000) + (102 \cdot 150{,}000) + (101.5 \cdot 80{,}000) + (103 \cdot 120{,}000) + (104.5 \cdot 50{,}000)$$

Computing each term:
- $100 \times 100{,}000 = 10{,}000{,}000$
- $102 \times 150{,}000 = 15{,}300{,}000$
- $101.5 \times 80{,}000 = 8{,}120{,}000$
- $103 \times 120{,}000 = 12{,}360{,}000$
- $104.5 \times 50{,}000 = 5{,}225{,}000$

Sum of $P_i V_i$:

$$10{,}000{,}000 + 15{,}300{,}000 + 8{,}120{,}000 + 12{,}360{,}000 + 5{,}225{,}000 = 51{,}005{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

Denominator:

$$\sum V_i = 100{,}000 + 150{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 120{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 500{,}000 \text{ shares}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{VWAP} \;=\; \frac{51{,}005{,}000}{500{,}000} \;=\; 102.01 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 2 — Compute VWAP Slippage (2 marks):**

Since the order is a **buy** order and the actual execution price (₹101.20) is **lower** than the VWAP (₹102.01), the desk **paid less than the market average**, which is favorable for a buy order.

$$\text{Slippage} \;=\; P_{\text{executed}} - P_{\text{VWAP}} \;=\; 101.20 - 102.01 \;=\; -0.81 \text{ ₹}$$

In basis points:

$$\text{Slippage (bps)} \;=\; \frac{-0.81}{102.01} \times 10{,}000 \;\approx\; -79.40 \text{ bps}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation (1 mark):**

The desk **outperformed** the VWAP benchmark by approximately **79.4 bps**. For a buy order, paying less than the market's volume-weighted average is a sign of superior execution quality — the desk successfully acquired shares at a discount to the average market price, saving approximately **₹0.81 per share** × 500,000 shares = **₹4,05,000** in implicit cost.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating buy-side and sell-side:** The sell-side is the **broker/dealer** (the execution agent), and the buy-side is the **client placing the order**. Confusing these two perspectives will result in zero credit for the question.
> 2. **Forgetting units in bps:** Always express slippage and IS in **basis points** explicitly.
> 3. **Omitting the trade-off:** Stating "minimize cost" without mentioning the **cost-risk trade-off** (market impact vs. timing risk) costs at least 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing up benchmarks:** VWAP, TWAP, and IS are **not interchangeable**; explicitly identify which is being used.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential Points to Remember (Must-Memorize for KTU 2024 Exams):**

- **Sell-side** = broker-dealers, market makers, execution desks; **Buy-side** = mutual funds, hedge funds, pension funds, insurance companies (the *clients* of sell-side).
- The **fundamental trade-off** in optimal execution is **Market Impact (↑ with speed) vs. Timing Risk (↑ with delay)**.
- The **Almgren-Chriss optimal trajectory** is $x_k^* = X \cdot \frac{\sinh(\kappa (N-k))}{\sinh(\kappa N)}$, with $\kappa = \sqrt{\frac{\lambda \sigma^2}{\eta}}$.
- **Risk-aversion $\lambda$** is the dial: high $\lambda$ → front-loaded execution; low $\lambda$ → uniform TWAP-like execution.
- **VWAP** is **volume-weighted**; **TWAP** is **time-weighted**; **IS** is anchored at the **decision price** (arrival price).
- **Implementation Shortfall** is the **gold-standard** sell-side performance metric, always quoted in **basis points (bps)**.
- **Temporary impact** is **quadratic** in trading rate; **permanent impact** is **linear** in trade size.
- **Smart Order Routers (SOR)** dynamically choose between lit exchanges, ECNs, and dark pools to minimize cost.
- Sell-side revenue streams: **spread capture, agency commissions, PFOF (regulated in India), algorithmic execution fees, and data analytics services**.
- **Best execution** is a **regulatory mandate** (MiFID II in Europe; SEBI's 2018 circular in India) requiring sell-side firms to demonstrate they acted in the client's best interest.
- **Dark pools** reduce information leakage but introduce **adverse selection** risk.
- **Almgren-Chriss $\kappa$** has **no units** (dimensionless) and is the central numerical parameter for the trajectory.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Impact Models - Buy Side Perspective

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Market Impact (MI)** is defined as the measurable change in the price of a financial asset directly attributable to the act of trading in that asset. From the **Buy Side perspective** (institutional investors such as mutual funds, pension funds, insurance funds, and hedge funds), impact models are quantitative frameworks used to *estimate*, *predict*, and *minimize* the implicit transaction cost incurred when executing large orders in the market.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Buy Side vs. Sell Side**:
> - **Buy Side** = The *purchaser* of securities (institutional asset managers, asset owners). They consume liquidity.
> - **Sell Side** = The *vendor* of securities (broker-dealers, investment banks, market makers). They provide liquidity.
> 
> The Buy Side perspective is *aggressor-centric*: how much does my demand push the price against me?

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Wedding Venue Problem"

Imagine you want to book a massive 5,000-guest wedding venue in a small town. The town only has a few venues with ~200 capacity each.

- **Small order (200 guests)** → You book one venue. Price is stable. No one else competes with you. **Zero impact.**
- **Medium order (1,000 guests)** → You need 5 venues. The 4th and 5th owners see your desperation and raise prices. You pay *more* than the first guest would have.
- **Large order (5,000 guests)** → You have to book *every single venue in the region*. You literally create a *local shortage*, and prices across the board spike. Some venues sell out (permanent scarcity), and you even pushed the next wedding to next year (permanent price effect).

The "extra price" you paid because of the *sheer size of your demand* — that is **Market Impact**. The buy side wants to know this number *before* trading, so they can slice the order smartly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Board Favourite)**:
> *"Market Impact is the difference between the actual execution price of a trade and the price that would have prevailed had the trade not occurred (the 'arrival price')."*
> 
> $$\text{Impact} = P_{executed} - P_{arrival}$$

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bid-Ask Spread** | $s$ | 1–10 bps (liquid), 50+ bps (illiquid) | Cost of demanding *immediate* liquidity |
| **Daily Volatility** | $\sigma$ | **0.5% – 3%** (equities) | Inherent price uncertainty |
| **Participation Rate** | $\phi$ | 5% – 30% | Buy side's share of total volume |
| **Average Daily Volume** | $ADV$ | Asset-dependent | Liquidity benchmark |
| **Order Size Ratio** | $Q/ADV$ | 0.01 – 1.0+ | How "large" the order is |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Structural Components of Market Impact

Every impact model decomposes price movement into exactly **two temporal layers**:

### Layer 1 — **Temporary Impact (TI)**
- The price moves *against* the trader **during execution** but **reverts** shortly after the trade is complete.
- Caused by: paying the spread, demanding liquidity, short-term inventory pressure on the market maker.
- **Analogy**: The premium surge you paid for the 4th wedding venue; once you walk away, that premium fades for the next person.

### Layer 2 — **Permanent Impact (PI)**
- The price moves and **stays moved**; the new price level becomes the consensus market price.
- Caused by: information leakage (market senses a large buyer → re-prices the stock upward because future demand is anticipated).
- **Analogy**: If you publicly announced you wanted to book 5,000 guest slots, the *next* wedding customer would pay the higher rate — the price has permanently shifted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Buy Side's nightmare is Permanent Impact.** It is unrecoverable. Temporary impact is the "rental cost" of urgency; permanent impact is a "wealth transfer" to the market.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Buy-Side Impact Models

### Pillar 1 — **The Square-Root Law (Empirical Baseline)**

Empirically validated across equities, FX, and futures by Barra, Bloomberg, and ITG:

$$I = Y \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{\frac{Q}{ADV}}$$

Where:
- $I$ = Market impact in **basis points (bps)**
- $Y$ = Broker-dealer specific scaling constant (typically **0.10 to 0.314**)
- $\sigma$ = Daily price volatility (in decimal, e.g., 0.02 = 2%)
- $Q$ = Order size (shares or contracts)
- $ADV$ = Average daily volume

**Why square-root?** Because impact is *sub-linear*. Doubling the order size does NOT double the impact. It increases by only $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414\times$. This is due to:
- Liquidity replenishment during execution
- Information diffusion over time
- Smart order routing

### Pillar 2 — **The Almgren–Chriss Model (Optimal Execution Framework)**

A continuous-time mean-variance framework that gives the **trajectory** of execution.

**Permanent Impact** (linear, irreversible):

$$h(v_t) = \gamma \cdot v_t$$

**Temporary Impact** (transient, function of trading rate):

$$g(v_t) = \eta \cdot v_t + \text{sgn}(v_t) \cdot \eta\beta$$

**Cost of trading trajectory** $x(t)$:

$$\text{TC} = \int_0^T \left[ \eta \cdot v_t^2 + (\lambda + \gamma \cdot v_t) \cdot x_t \right] dt + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \theta \cdot S_j \cdot \text{sgn}(v_j)$$

The **optimal trajectory** minimizing expected cost + risk:

$$x^*(t) = X_0 - \frac{\sinh(\kappa(T-t))}{\sinh(\kappa T)} \cdot X_0$$

where the urgency parameter:

$$\kappa = \sqrt{\frac{\lambda \cdot \sigma^2}{\eta}}$$

### Pillar 3 — **The I-Star Model (Kissell, Glantz & Malamut)**

A practitioner-favourite model used by buy-side traders:

$$I^* = I \cdot \sqrt{\frac{Q}{ADV}}$$

Where $I$ is the *percentage of ADV* traded per hour. The $I^*$ is interpreted as:

> "The number of days of average volume required to absorb this order without impact."

**Strength:** Adjusts for volatility, spread, and trade direction simultaneously.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = Y \sigma \sqrt{Q/ADV}$ | Square-root market impact | **bps** |
| 2 | $h(v) = \gamma v$ | Permanent impact (Almgren) | price per share |
| 3 | $g(v) = \eta v + \eta\beta \cdot \text{sgn}(v)$ | Temporary impact (Almgren) | price per share |
| 4 | $x^*(t) = X_0 \cdot \frac{\sinh(\kappa(T-t))}{\sinh(\kappa T)}$ | Optimal execution schedule | shares |
| 5 | $\kappa = \sqrt{\lambda \sigma^2 / \eta}$ | Urgency parameter | $\text{time}^{-1}$ |
| 6 | $I^* = I \cdot \sqrt{Q/ADV}$ | I-Star impact magnitude | days |
| 7 | $\text{IS} = P_{decision} - P_{executed}$ | Implementation Shortfall | currency |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Pipe Rule**: In all tables, absolute value is written as $\vert x \vert$ (using `\vert`), never $\vert x \vert$ with a raw pipe — this prevents markdown table breakage during KTU PDF rendering.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Impact models are not abstract — they are deployed in:
- **Algorithmic trading systems** (VWAP, TWAP, POV, IS algorithms).
- **Transaction Cost Analysis (TCA)** post-trade reports.
- **Smart Order Routers (SORs)** that fragment orders across 50+ venues.
- **Best Execution compliance** under MiFID II (Europe) and SEC Rule 606 (USA).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Square-Root Market Impact

We start with the empirical observation that large orders cause *less-than-linear* price drift. We model the temporary price impact as an integral over the trading rate.

### Step 1 — Define the Price Dynamics

The mid-price $S_t$ evolves as:

$$S_t = S_0 + \int_0^t g(v_u) \, du + \int_0^t \sigma \, dW_u$$

Here $g(v_u)$ is the temporary impact function at trading rate $v_u$, and $dW_u$ is Brownian motion noise.

### Step 2 — Assume Linear Temporary Impact

Following the convention:

$$g(v) = \eta \cdot v$$

Substituting:

$$S_t = S_0 + \eta \int_0^t v_u \, du + \sigma \sqrt{t}$$

### Step 3 — Total Shares Executed

If the trader spreads $Q$ shares uniformly over time $T$:

$$v = \frac{Q}{T}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Total Cost of Impact

The total cost $C$ is the area under the impact curve:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \int_0^T g(v) \cdot v \, dt \\[4pt]
  &= \int_0^T \eta \cdot v \cdot v \, dt \\[4pt]
  &= \eta \cdot v^2 \cdot T
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute $v = Q/T$ and Express in bps

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \eta \cdot \left( \frac{Q}{T} \right)^2 \cdot T \\[4pt]
  &= \eta \cdot \frac{Q^2}{T}
\end{aligned}
$$

The cost is **inversely proportional to execution time** $T$. A trader who needs to finish in less time pays *quadratically* more.

### Step 6 — Convert to Basis Points

Using the convention that impact is normalized by notional $\sigma \sqrt{T \cdot ADV}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{bps} &= \frac{C}{\sigma \sqrt{T \cdot ADV}} \\[4pt]
       &= \frac{\eta Q^2 / T}{\sigma \sqrt{T \cdot ADV}} \\[4pt]
       &= \frac{\eta}{\sigma} \cdot \frac{Q^2}{T \cdot \sqrt{T \cdot ADV}} \\[4pt]
       &= Y \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{\frac{Q}{ADV}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This recovers the **square-root law**, with $Y$ absorbing the $\eta/\sigma^2$ scaling.

## 3.2 Derivation: Almgren–Chriss Optimal Trajectory

### Step 1 — Define Variables

- $X_0$ = total shares to liquidate
- $x(t)$ = shares remaining at time $t$
- $T$ = execution horizon
- $v(t) = -\dot{x}(t)$ = trading rate

### Step 2 — Expected Cost

$$
E[\text{Cost}] = \int_0^T \left( \lambda \cdot x(t) + \eta \cdot v(t)^2 \right) dt
$$

Where $\lambda$ is the risk aversion coefficient (the trader fears price uncertainty).

### Step 3 — Variance Penalty (Risk)

$$
\text{Var}(\text{Cost}) = \sigma^2 \int_0^T x(t)^2 \, dt
$$

### Step 4 — Combined Objective

$$
U = E[\text{Cost}] + \lambda \cdot \text{Var}(\text{Cost})
$$

### Step 5 — Euler–Lagrange Equation

Applying the calculus of variations:

$$
\eta \cdot \ddot{x}(t) = \lambda \sigma^2 \cdot x(t)
$$

### Step 6 — Solve the ODE

The general solution is:

$$
x(t) = A \cdot e^{\kappa t} + B \cdot e^{-\kappa t}
$$

with boundary conditions $x(0) = X_0$ and $x(T) = 0$.

### Step 7 — Apply Boundary Conditions

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(0) &= A + B = X_0 \\
x(T) &= A e^{\kappa T} + B e^{-\kappa T} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving the system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{X_0 \cdot e^{-\kappa T}}{e^{\kappa T} - e^{-\kappa T}} = -\frac{X_0}{2 \sinh(\kappa T)} \\[4pt]
B &= \frac{X_0 \cdot e^{\kappa T}}{e^{\kappa T} - e^{-\kappa T}} = +\frac{X_0}{2 \sinh(\kappa T)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8 — Final Optimal Trajectory

$$
\boxed{\,x^*(t) = X_0 \cdot \frac{\sinh\!\left(\kappa(T - t)\right)}{\sinh(\kappa T)}\,}
$$

where:

$$
\kappa = \sqrt{\frac{\lambda \sigma^2}{\eta}}
$$

**Interpretation**:
- If $\lambda \to 0$ (risk neutral) → $\kappa \to 0$ → trade uniformly (TWAP).
- If $\lambda \to \infty$ (very risk averse) → $\kappa \to \infty$ → trade immediately at the open (market order).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem**: A buy-side fund needs to buy $Q = 500{,}000$ shares of a stock. $ADV = 2{,}000{,}000$ shares. Daily volatility $\sigma = 2\%$. Broker parameter $Y = 0.20$.

**Step 1**: Compute the size ratio.

$$
\frac{Q}{ADV} = \frac{500{,}000}{2{,}000{,}000} = 0.25
$$

**Step 2**: Compute the square root.

$$
\sqrt{0.25} = 0.50
$$

**Step 3**: Plug into the square-root law.

$$
I = 0.20 \times 0.02 \times 0.50
$$

**Step 4**: Convert to bps.

$$
I = 0.002 = 20 \text{ bps}
$$

**Interpretation**: The buy-side fund will pay an extra **20 basis points** (i.e., 0.20% of notional) in market impact. If the stock is at ₹1,000, that's **₹2 per share** of extra cost — totaling **₹10,00,000** in implicit cost.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define "Market Impact" from the buy-side perspective. Distinguish between temporary and permanent impact with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Market impact is the movement in an asset's price directly attributable to a buy-side order, measured as the difference between the executed price and the arrival price. **(1 mark)**

- **Temporary Impact**: A short-lived price concession to obtain liquidity; reverts after execution. *Example*: paying a higher bid when sweeping a thin order book. **(1 mark)**
- **Permanent Impact**: A lasting price shift caused by information leakage from the large order. *Example*: a 10-lakh-share buy in a small-cap stock re-prices the equilibrium higher. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the "Square-Root Law" of market impact? State the formula and explain the role of the $Y$ parameter.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The square-root law states that market impact grows with the square root of the order size relative to ADV. **(1 mark)**

$$I = Y \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{Q/ADV}$$

**(1 mark)** $Y$ is a broker- or venue-specific scaling constant capturing spread depth, queue priority, and the algo's sophistication. It typically lies between **0.10 and 0.314**. **(1 mark)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Almgren–Chriss optimal execution model**, including its assumptions on permanent and temporary impact functions. Derive the optimal trajectory $x^*(t)$ for liquidating $X_0$ shares over horizon $T$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A buy-side fund must purchase $Q = 200{,}000$ shares. $ADV = 1{,}000{,}000$, $\sigma = 0.025$, broker parameter $Y = 0.15$. Compute the expected market impact in basis points. If the trader splits the order into 4 equal blocks executed at 25%, 50%, 75%, and 100% of $ADV$, recompute the impact and comment. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution — 7 marks

**Assumptions** (1 mark):
1. Security price follows arithmetic Brownian motion with volatility $\sigma$.
2. Permanent impact is **linear** in trading rate: $h(v) = \gamma v$.
3. Temporary impact is **linear+fixed** in trading rate: $g(v) = \eta v + \eta\beta \, \text{sgn}(v)$.
4. Trader has mean-variance utility with risk aversion $\lambda$.

**Cost Function** (2 marks):

$$
E[C] + \lambda \, \text{Var}(C) = \int_0^T \left( \eta v^2 + \lambda \sigma^2 x^2 \right) dt
$$

**Euler–Lagrange Equation** (1 mark):

$$
\eta \ddot{x}(t) = \lambda \sigma^2 x(t)
$$

**Solving with boundary conditions** $x(0) = X_0$, $x(T) = 0$ (2 marks):

$$
x^*(t) = X_0 \cdot \frac{\sinh\left(\kappa(T - t)\right)}{\sinh(\kappa T)}
$$

with $\kappa = \sqrt{\lambda \sigma^2 / \eta}$. (1 mark)

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — 7 marks

**Step 1**: Single-order impact (1 mark):

$$
I = 0.15 \times 0.025 \times \sqrt{200{,}000 / 1{,}000{,}000} = 0.15 \times 0.025 \times 0.4472 = 1.677 \text{ bps}
$$

**Step 2**: Each block has $Q_i = 50{,}000$ shares; participation rate is 25% of ADV per block (1 mark).

**Step 3**: Impact per block (1 mark):

$$
I_{block} = 0.15 \times 0.025 \times \sqrt{50{,}000 / 1{,}000{,}000} = 0.15 \times 0.025 \times 0.2236 = 0.838 \text{ bps}
$$

**Step 4**: Total impact across 4 blocks (1 mark):

$$
I_{total} = 4 \times 0.838 = 3.35 \text{ bps}
$$

**Step 5**: Comment (3 marks):

- Single order → 1.68 bps.
- Split into 4 blocks → 3.35 bps.
- **Counter-intuitive result**: Splitting *increased* impact. This is because each block's $Q_i/ADV$ is still substantial, and 4 blocks mean **4× the spread cost** + market moves against the trader between blocks.
- **Lesson**: Splitting is beneficial *only* if the participation rate $Q_i/ADV$ falls low enough that $\sqrt{Q_i/ADV}$ reduction dominates the linear cost multiplier. Otherwise, use a single VWAP.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Describe the **I-Star model** of buy-side impact. How does it differ from the static square-root model in handling volatility, spread, and order direction? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of **Transaction Cost Analysis (TCA)** in closing the buy-side feedback loop. Why is TCA a regulatory requirement under MiFID II? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution — 7 marks

**I-Star definition** (2 marks):

$$
I^* = I \cdot \sqrt{Q/ADV}
$$

Where $I$ = participation rate (% of ADV traded per hour), $Q$ = order size, $ADV$ = average daily volume. $I^*$ is interpreted as the number of days of volume required to absorb the order impact-free.

**Differences from the static square-root** (3 marks):
- The I-Star model **separates participation rate $I$ from the size ratio**, letting traders tune them independently.
- It incorporates **spread and volatility adjustments** via the multiplicative constant — the static model uses a fixed $Y$, while I-Star uses a multi-factor regression.
- It accounts for **order direction** (buy vs. sell) and **market regime** (trending vs. mean-reverting).

**Practical use** (2 marks):
- Buy-side traders compute $I^*$ pre-trade to estimate days-to-execute.
- Real-time I-Star tracking allows dynamic algo adjustment.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — 7 marks

**What is TCA?** (2 marks): Post-trade measurement of execution cost vs. multiple benchmarks (arrival, VWAP, TWAP, interval, close).

**Why TCA closes the feedback loop** (3 marks):
- Provides objective data on whether the algo outperformed the impact model.
- Reveals information leakage (e.g., front-running by HFTs).
- Calibrates the $Y$ parameter in future impact estimates.
- Helps PMs re-evaluate broker performance.

**MiFID II / Regulatory angle** (2 marks):
- MiFID II RTS 27 and RTS 28 require firms to publish execution quality data.
- "Best execution" is a fiduciary duty; without TCA, the firm cannot prove it met this duty.
- SEC Rule 606 in the US mandates order routing disclosure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**:
> - **Common mistake 1**: Writing "market impact = spread cost". Impact includes spread but is *additionally* the price concession beyond the spread. **(−1 mark)**
> - **Common mistake 2**: Confusing the square-root law (empirical) with the Almgren model (theoretical). They are *complementary*, not interchangeable.
> - **Common mistake 3**: In numerical problems, students forget to convert decimals to basis points (×10,000). Always write units in your final answer.
> - **Common mistake 4**: In part (b) "comment" sections, students skip the *qualitative* insight. KTU examiners award marks for **interpretation**, not just arithmetic.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Market Impact** = price drift caused by the trade itself; difference between executed and arrival price.
- ✅ **Temporary vs Permanent** — temporary reverts; permanent is the information-leakage footprint.
- ✅ **Square-Root Law** is the *industry standard empirical benchmark*: $I = Y \sigma \sqrt{Q/ADV}$.
- ✅ **Almgren–Chriss** gives the *optimal trajectory* balancing impact cost vs. timing risk.
- ✅ **Urgency parameter** $\kappa = \sqrt{\lambda \sigma^2 / \eta}$ — higher = front-loaded, lower = uniform.
- ✅ **I-Star** $I^* = I \sqrt{Q/ADV}$ — measures days of volume needed.
- ✅ **Buy-Side is the demand side**; it consumes liquidity and pays impact.
- ✅ **Implementation Shortfall** is the *total cost* — not just impact.
- ✅ **TCA** is the feedback loop — it makes the impact model *calibrate itself*.
- ✅ **Splitting orders** reduces impact only if the resulting $\sqrt{Q_i/ADV}$ drop is large enough; otherwise it amplifies spread costs.
- ✅ The $Y$ parameter is *not universal*; it must be calibrated per broker, per venue, per asset class.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Popular Algorithmic Trading Strategies - Volume Weighted Average Price
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Popular Algorithmic Trading Strategies - Volume Weighted Average Price

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Volume Weighted Average Price (VWAP)** is an intraday trading benchmark that represents the ratio of the total value traded (Price × Volume) to the total volume traded, over a specified time horizon (typically a single trading session). It is mathematically expressed as:

$$
VWAP = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot V_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i}
$$

Where:
- $P_i$ = Typical Price (often the average of High, Low, and Close) of the $i^{th}$ tick or candle
- $V_i$ = Volume traded during that interval
- $n$ = Number of intervals (ticks/minutes/candles) within the session

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** VWAP belongs to the family of *execution algorithms* and *technical benchmarks* in algorithmic trading. It simultaneously serves two functions: (a) a **trading benchmark** for institutional performance evaluation, and (b) a **technical indicator** for retail and prop-desk traders.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Mango Market

Imagine a busy fruit market where mangoes are sold throughout the day at varying prices and in varying quantities:

| Time Slot | Price per Mango (₹) | Mangoes Sold (Qty) | Revenue (₹) |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|--------------|
| 8–10 AM   | 100                 | 50                  | 5,000        |
| 10–12 PM  | 120                 | 30                  | 3,600        |
| 12–2 PM   | 90                  | 70                  | 6,300        |
| 2–4 PM    | 110                 | 40                  | 4,400        |

At the end of the day, what is the **true average price** of a mango? It is *not* the simple arithmetic mean of ₹105. Instead, it is the **total money collected divided by total mangoes sold**:

$$
Average = \frac{19000}{190} = ₹100
$$

This is the **Volume Weighted Average Price**. In equities, the mangoes are *shares*, the price is the *trade price*, and the quantity is the *traded volume*. VWAP tells us the *fair average price* of a security, weighted by how many shares actually changed hands at each price.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Weighted"?** Because high-volume trades have a greater say in the day's true average price than low-volume trades. A trade of 10,000 shares at ₹500 must dominate over a trade of 100 shares at ₹495 — and VWAP mathematically enforces this dominance.

## 1.3 The Two Personas of VWAP

1. **Institutional Persona — Execution Benchmark:** Hedge funds, mutual funds, and pension funds are mandated by regulators (SEBI in India, SEC in the US) and internal compliance to **not move the market adversely**. They measure their own execution quality against VWAP. Buying *below* VWAP = good execution. Buying *above* VWAP = poor execution.

2. **Retail Persona — Trading Signal:** Day traders use VWAP as a dynamic support/resistance line. The relationship "price relative to VWAP" is a powerful, regime-defining indicator.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — How VWAP Behaves Intraday

VWAP is a **cumulative** indicator. It is recalculated from the market open (9:15 AM IST for NSE) and continues to update until the close (3:30 PM IST). It is reset every trading day — there is no carry-over.

### Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown:

- **Step 1 — Tick Aggregation:** Incoming market data is bucketed into intervals (1-minute, 5-minute, or 15-minute candles).
- **Step 2 — Typical Price Computation:** For each interval, compute $TP_i = \frac{High_i + Low_i + Close_i}{3}$.
- **Step 3 — Money Flow Computation:** Multiply $TP_i$ by the corresponding volume $V_i$ to obtain **Price × Volume** (also called *money flow*).
- **Step 4 — Cumulative Sums:** Maintain running totals: $\sum PV$ and $\sum V$ from the start of the day.
- **Step 5 — Ratio Update:** VWAP for interval $k$ = $\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} P_i V_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} V_i}$.

### Why Volume Weighting Matters

A simple arithmetic mean of prices treats all price points equally. But a security that traded 1 million shares at ₹100 and 100 shares at ₹101 should *not* be reported as averaging ₹100.50. VWAP correctly identifies the **volume-supported fair value**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready reference** for any VWAP-related numerical or theoretical question.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Key Boundary Condition / Unit |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | **VWAP (Core)** | $VWAP = \dfrac{\sum (P_i \cdot V_i)}{\sum V_i}$ | Unit: ₹ (price unit). Resets daily. |
| 2 | **Typical Price** | $TP_i = \dfrac{High_i + Low_i + Close_i}{3}$ | Used when OHLC data is available. |
| 3 | **VWAP using Tick Price** | $VWAP = \dfrac{\sum (Trade\ Price \cdot Trade\ Volume)}{\sum Trade\ Volume}$ | Used with raw tick data. |
| 4 | **Relative VWAP Deviation** | $D_t = \dfrac{P_t - VWAP_t}{VWAP_t} \times 100$ | Expressed as %. Above 0 = bullish; below 0 = bearish. |
| 5 | **TWAP (Comparison)** | $TWAP = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | Time-weighted (ignores volume). |
| 6 | **Cumulative Volume** | $CumV_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t} V_i$ | Monitors intraday participation. |
| 7 | **% of Session Volume** | $POV_t = \dfrac{CumV_t}{ADV}$ | ADV = Average Daily Volume. |
| 8 | **VWAP Band** | $\pm k\sigma$ around VWAP (typically $k = 1$ or $2$) | $\sigma$ = intraday standard deviation of price. |
| 9 | **VWAP Anchor Rule** | Buy if $P_t < VWAP_t$; Sell if $P_t > VWAP_t$ | Intraday mean-reversion strategy. |
| 10 | **Slippage vs VWAP** | $Slip = \dfrac{Execution\ Price - VWAP}{VWAP}$ | Negative = favorable execution. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbolic Convention:** In KTU exam scripts, $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) must be written as `mod x` or `$\vert x \vert$` — **never** with the pipe symbol inside markdown tables, to avoid parsing errors in PDF converters.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Financial Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Brokerage Tech** | Smart Order Routers (SOR) slice large parent orders into child orders using VWAP schedules. |
| **Risk Management** | Compliance teams compare trader execution vs. arrival price and VWAP to detect *market abuse* and *front-running*. |
| **Algorithmic Execution** | VWAP algo (e.g., Bloomberg VWAP, Goldman Sigma X) attempts to *match* the market VWAP while executing large orders. |
| **Retail Trading Platforms** | Charting tools (TradingView, Zerodha Kite, MetaTrader) plot VWAP as a single line overlay. |
| **Mutual Fund Disclosures** | SEBI mandates that AMCs disclose portfolio buy/sell weighted average prices. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Numerical Worked Example — VWAP Calculation from Trade Data

**Problem (Board-style):** The following trades occurred in RELIANCE stock during the first 30 minutes of a trading session. Compute the VWAP.

| Trade # | Price (₹) | Volume (Shares) |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| 1       | 2,500     | 200             |
| 2       | 2,505     | 500             |
| 3       | 2,498     | 300             |
| 4       | 2,510     | 1,000           |
| 5       | 2,495     | 400             |

### Step 1 — Compute Price × Volume for each trade

$$
PV_1 = 2500 \times 200 = 500{,}000
$$
$$
PV_2 = 2505 \times 500 = 1{,}252{,}500
$$
$$
PV_3 = 2498 \times 300 = 749{,}400
$$
$$
PV_4 = 2510 \times 1000 = 2{,}510{,}000
$$
$$
PV_5 = 2495 \times 400 = 998{,}000
$$

### Step 2 — Sum the numerator and denominator

$$
\sum (P_i V_i) = 500{,}000 + 1{,}252{,}500 + 749{,}400 + 2{,}510{,}000 + 998{,}000
$$

$$
\sum (P_i V_i) = 6{,}009{,}900
$$

$$
\sum V_i = 200 + 500 + 300 + 1000 + 400 = 2{,}400
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the VWAP formula

$$
VWAP = \frac{6{,}009{,}900}{2{,}400}
$$

$$
VWAP = 2{,}504.125
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

The volume-weighted average price of RELIANCE for this window is **₹2,504.13**. Note that even though the arithmetic mean of the five trade prices is:

$$
AM = \frac{2500 + 2505 + 2498 + 2510 + 2495}{5} = \frac{12508}{5} = 2501.60
$$

… the VWAP (₹2,504.13) is **higher** because the largest trade (1,000 shares at ₹2,510) pulled the average upward. This is exactly what volume-weighting captures.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common VWAP-Based Strategy Variants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following strategies are **exam-favorite** content. KTU frequently tests (a) the strategy logic, (b) the conditions for triggering, and (c) the rationale.

### Strategy 1: VWAP Mean Reversion

- **Hypothesis:** Price tends to revert to VWAP intraday.
- **Entry Long:** When price closes **below** VWAP and RSI < 30 (oversold).
- **Exit:** When price re-crosses VWAP upward.
- **Risk:** Trending days where VWAP keeps shifting — false signals.

### Strategy 2: VWAP Trend Confirmation

- **Hypothesis:** Sustained trading above VWAP = institutional accumulation.
- **Entry Long:** Price holds above VWAP for 3+ consecutive candles.
- **Confirmation:** Volume > 20-period average volume.
- **Exit:** Price closes below VWAP with rising volume.

### Strategy 3: VWAP Breakout (Opening Range Breakout hybrid)

- **Setup:** First 30-min high and low define a range; VWAP sits inside.
- **Entry Long:** Break above opening range high **AND** price > VWAP.
- **Entry Short:** Break below opening range low **AND** price < VWAP.

### Strategy 4: VWAP Execution Algorithm (Institutional)

- **Goal:** Execute a 500,000-share buy order without disturbing price.
- **Method:** Slice order into child orders sized in proportion to expected volume curve (U-shape: high at open/close, low at midday).
- **Benchmark:** Final execution price should be **at or below** session VWAP.

### Strategy 5: VWAP + Standard Deviation Bands

- **Logic:** Plot $\pm 1\sigma$ and $\pm 2\sigma$ bands around VWAP.
- **Use:** Mean reversion from outer band; momentum from band break.

## 3.3 Mathematical Justification — Why VWAP Resets Daily

VWAP is a **non-stationary cumulative indicator**. After market close, all running sums are reset. The rationale is twofold:

1. **Information Decay:** Yesterday's volume structure is irrelevant to today's supply/demand.
2. **Benchmark Integrity:** Institutional performance measurement requires daily comparability across sessions.

$$
VWAP_t^{session} = \frac{\sum_{i=open}^{t} P_i V_i}{\sum_{i=open}^{t} V_i}, \quad \forall t \in [open,\ close]
$$

For $t = close$, this represents the **closing VWAP** — the official benchmark for the entire session.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 VWAP Calculation Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Market Data Feed (Tick Stream)"] --> B["Tick Aggregator / Candle Builder"]
    B --> C["Bucket: 1-min or 5-min intervals"]
    C --> D["Compute Typical Price TP = H + L + C / 3"]
    D --> E["Compute PV = TP x Volume"]
    E --> F["Cumulative Sum: SumPV = Sigma PV"]
    F --> G["Cumulative Sum: SumV = Sigma Volume"]
    G --> H["VWAP Calculation: SumPV / SumV"]
    H --> I{"Reset at Market Close?"}
    I -- Yes --> J["Reset to NaN for Next Session"]
    I -- No --> K["Plot VWAP Line on Chart"]
    K --> L["Trading Strategy Layer"]
    L --> M1["Mean Reversion Signal"]
    L --> M2["Trend Confirmation Signal"]
    L --> M3["Institutional Execution Algo"]
```

## 4.2 VWAP Trading Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Intraday Candle Closes"] --> Q1{"Price above VWAP?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{"Volume greater than 20-period avg?"}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{"RSI less than 30?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4["Bullish Bias: Look for Long Entries on Pullback to VWAP"]
    Q2 -- No --> Q5["Weak Rally: Avoid / Wait"]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q6["Oversold below VWAP: Mean Reversion Long Candidate"]
    Q3 -- No --> Q7["Bearish Continuation Likely"]
    Q4 --> Exit1["Target: Resistance / 2-sigma band"]
    Q6 --> Exit2["Target: VWAP re-cross / Stop below swing low"]
    Q7 --> Exit3["Target: VWAP magnet / Stop above swing high"]
```

## 4.3 VWAP vs TWAP — Functional Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TWAP["TWAP Engine"]
        T1["Equal Time Slices"] --> T2["Constant Order Size"]
        T2 --> T3["Ignores Volume Pattern"]
        T3 --> T4["Output: Time-weighted avg"]
    end
    subgraph VWAP["VWAP Engine"]
        V1["Read Intraday Volume Curve"] --> V2["Variable Order Size"]
        V2 --> V3["Matches Volume Profile"]
        V3 --> V4["Output: Volume-weighted avg"]
    end
    T4 --> Compare["Benchmark Comparison"]
    V4 --> Compare
    Compare --> Use1["Suitable for Low-volume / Illiquid stocks"]
    Compare --> Use2["Suitable for High-volume / Liquid stocks"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip:** Notice that VWAP engines *adapt* to volume profile (U-shape on normal days, J-shape on trending days), while TWAP engines are *rigid*. This adaptability is why VWAP is the gold-standard institutional benchmark.

---

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Volume Weighted Average Price (VWAP). State its formula and explain the significance of volume-weighting.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** VWAP is a trading benchmark that represents the average price of a security over a given time horizon, weighted by the volume traded at each price level.

**Formula (1 Mark):**

$$
VWAP = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot V_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i}
$$

**Significance (1 Mark):** Volume-weighting ensures that prices at which higher volumes were traded have a proportionally greater influence on the average. This reflects the *true* fair value at which the market actually transacted, rather than a simple arithmetic mean that treats every price tick equally.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between TWAP and VWAP as execution benchmarks.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | TWAP | VWAP |
|--------|------|------|
| Weighting | Equal weight per time interval | Weighted by traded volume |
| Formula | Arithmetic mean of prices | Ratio of $\sum PV$ to $\sum V$ |
| Order Slicing | Uniform child orders | Variable child orders matching volume profile |
| Best Use | Illiquid, low-volume stocks | Liquid, high-volume stocks |
| Drawback | Ignores volume surges | Susceptible to volume manipulation at end-of-day |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "TWAP is better than VWAP" or vice-versa. The correct KTU-acceptable answer is **context-dependent** — neither is universally superior. Always state conditions of applicability.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the formula for Volume Weighted Average Price from first principles. Using a hypothetical dataset of 5 trades, demonstrate the step-by-step computation of VWAP. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of VWAP as an institutional execution benchmark. Explain with a diagram how a VWAP execution algorithm slices a large parent order into child orders. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Derivation (3 Marks):**
VWAP is derived from the weighted arithmetic mean concept. For $n$ trades with prices $P_i$ and volumes $V_i$, the weighted mean with weights $w_i = V_i$ is:

$$
VWAP = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot P_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i \cdot P_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i}
$$

When OHLC candle data is available:

$$
VWAP_{candle} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \frac{H_i + L_i + C_i}{3} \right) \cdot V_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i}
$$

**Numerical Demonstration (4 Marks):** Using the dataset from Section 3.1 of these notes, the answer is **₹2,504.13**, with detailed computation already shown in Section 3.1.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Role as Institutional Benchmark (3 Marks):**

1. **Regulatory Compliance:** SEBI and global regulators require institutions to demonstrate best execution. VWAP serves as an objective, audit-friendly benchmark.
2. **Performance Attribution:** Portfolio managers are evaluated on whether their buys/sells beat or miss the VWAP.
3. **Market Impact Minimization:** Trading too aggressively creates *market impact* (price moves against the trader). VWAP algorithms spread execution to mimic natural volume flow, thereby minimizing footprint.

**VWAP Algo — Parent-to-Child Order Slicing (4 Marks):**

A typical institutional order of 500,000 shares is broken down based on the *expected intraday volume curve*:

| Time Slot | Expected % Volume | Child Order Size |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------|
| 9:15–10:00 | 15% | 75,000 |
| 10:00–12:00 | 25% | 125,000 |
| 12:00–14:00 | 20% | 100,000 |
| 14:00–15:00 | 25% | 125,000 |
| 15:00–15:30 | 15% | 75,000 |

The algo dynamically adjusts child order prices and sizes based on real-time volume data, ensuring the final execution tracks the session VWAP within a tolerance band (typically 5–10 basis points).

---

### Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the VWAP mean-reversion trading strategy. What are its strengths and weaknesses? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast VWAP with Simple Moving Average (SMA) and Exponential Moving Average (EMA) as technical indicators. Under what market conditions does each perform best? **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**VWAP Mean Reversion Logic (3 Marks):**
The strategy assumes that intraday price action oscillates around VWAP, and deviations from VWAP are temporary. Entry signals:

- **Long Entry:** Price closes below VWAP AND a confirming indicator (e.g., RSI < 30 or price touches lower Bollinger Band).
- **Exit:** Price re-crosses VWAP upward; target = VWAP.
- **Stop Loss:** Recent swing low or fixed % below entry.

**Strengths (2 Marks):**
1. Robust in range-bound, sideways markets.
2. Provides clear, objective entry/exit levels.
3. Combines well with momentum filters (RSI, MACD).

**Weaknesses (2 Marks):**
1. **Catastrophic failure in trending markets** — a strong downtrend keeps price below VWAP, leading to repeated stop-loss hits.
2. Sensitive to *volume anomalies* (e.g., one large block trade can skew VWAP significantly).
3. Intraday-only — cannot be used for swing or positional trades.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

| Feature | VWAP | SMA | EMA |
|---------|------|-----|-----|
| Weighting | Volume-weighted | Equal-weight over period | Recency-weighted (exponential decay) |
| Reset | Daily | None (continuous) | None (continuous) |
| Lag | Moderate (volume-smoothed) | High (equal weights) | Low (recent prices weighted more) |
| Best Market | Intraday ranging | Swing trading, long trends | Short-term momentum, breakouts |
| Weak Market | Strong trends | Sudden reversals | Choppy, sideways markets |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** VWAP is the *only* one of the three that incorporates volume information, making it uniquely informative for intraday liquidity analysis. SMA is best for trend following over longer horizons; EMA is best for responsive short-term signals.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Formula Recall Error (–2 Marks):** Students frequently write VWAP as $\frac{\sum P_i}{\sum V_i}$ (missing the multiplication). Always show $P_i \cdot V_i$ explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing VWAP with Weighted Average Price (WAP):** WAP uses time-windows or bars; VWAP is session-anchored. Examiners will deduct marks for misuse.
> 3. **No mention of daily reset (–1 Mark):** Always state that VWAP resets at market open.
> 4. **Strategy explanations without entry/exit logic (–2 Marks):** Vague answers like "buy when price is below VWAP" lose marks. Specify the *full conditional*: indicator confirmation, stop-loss, target.
> 5. **Missing units in numerical answers (–1 Mark):** Always write VWAP = ₹2,504.13, not just "2504.13".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this section as your last-day revision sheet.**

- ✅ **Definition:** VWAP = total value traded ÷ total volume traded, intraday.
- ✅ **Core Formula:** $VWAP = \dfrac{\sum (P_i V_i)}{\sum V_i}$
- ✅ **Typical Price Variant:** $TP = \dfrac{H + L + C}{3}$
- ✅ **VWAP is cumulative** — running total from market open.
- ✅ **Resets daily** at the start of each trading session.
- ✅ **Institutional Use:** Execution benchmark, performance measurement, compliance.
- ✅ **Retail Use:** Intraday support/resistance, mean reversion, trend confirmation.
- ✅ **Key Strategies:** Mean Reversion, Trend Confirmation, Breakout Confirmation, Execution Algo, VWAP Bands (±1σ, ±2σ).
- ✅ **Advantages:** Volume-aware, objective benchmark, intraday-relevant.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** Lagging indicator, weak in trends, vulnerable to large block trades.
- ✅ **TWAP vs VWAP:** TWAP ignores volume; VWAP weights by volume.
- ✅ **Common Mistake:** Forgetting the multiplication $P_i \cdot V_i$ in the numerator.
- ✅ **Best Paired With:** RSI, MACD, Bollinger Bands, Volume Oscillator.
- ✅ **NOT a Standalone Signal:** Always use with a momentum filter to avoid trend-day whipsaws.

---
