topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Time Weighted Average Price

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 3 — TIME WEIGHTED AVERAGE PRICE (TWAP)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> The **Time Weighted Average Price (TWAP)** is the arithmetic mean of an asset's price observed at regular or pre-defined time intervals over a specified trading window. It is the simplest *time-based* execution benchmark in financial markets, used to evaluate the quality of trade execution and to design passive order-slicing algorithms.

In continuous mathematical form, TWAP represents the **temporal average** of the price function $P(t)$ across the trading horizon $[0, T]$.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — "The School Bell Thermometer"

Imagine you are a class monitor asked to record the **temperature of the playground** every hour from **9 AM to 1 PM** (four readings). At the end of the day, the principal asks:

> *"What was the typical temperature on the playground?"*

You do **not** add up all the temperatures you felt during the hour — you simply **average the four recorded readings** (one per hour). Each reading carries **equal weight** because the time gaps between them are identical.

That simple average is **TWAP**.

- The playground = the stock market
- Each hourly temperature reading = the asset price snapshot at a fixed time
- The simple arithmetic average = the **Time Weighted Average Price**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — OEIET832 Module 3)**
> TWAP treats every **time slice** as equally important. It deliberately **ignores trading volume**. This is the central distinction from its sibling benchmark, the **Volume Weighted Average Price (VWAP)**.

### 🎯 Where is TWAP Used in Real Markets?

- **Benchmarking execution quality**: Did my buy order fill at a fair price versus the market's typical price during my execution window?
- **Algorithmic order slicing**: A broker may spread a large 100,000-share buy order into equal child orders at 1-minute intervals to mimic TWAP.
- **Pension funds & passive institutions**: Used to reduce market impact when deploying capital gradually.
- **Performance attribution**: Portfolio managers compare their entry/exit price against TWAP to grade themselves.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔬 The Logic Behind TWAP — Structured Steps

The derivation of TWAP follows three logical steps:

**Step 1 — Define a Time Grid**
Choose a trading window of total length $T$ (e.g., one full trading day = 375 minutes on NSE). Divide it into $n$ equal sub-intervals:
$$\Delta t = \frac{T}{n}$$

**Step 2 — Sample Prices at Each Grid Point**
Record the price $P_i$ at each time $t_i$. In practice these snapshots can be:
- Opening price of the interval
- Closing price of the interval
- Mid-point of bid-ask
- Volume-weighted micro-price within the interval

**Step 3 — Compute the Arithmetic Mean**
Sum all sampled prices and divide by $n$. Each interval carries equal weight because $\Delta t$ is constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Equal Weights?**
> TWAP is a **time-weighted** measure. The weighting factor for each price is the *duration* during which that price was considered representative. When intervals are equal, each duration is identical, so the simple arithmetic mean suffices.

### 📐 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Symbol | Boundary Conditions & Units |
|---|---|---|
| TWAP (discrete, equal intervals) | $TWAP = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | $n \ge 2$; $P_i$ in currency/share (e.g., ₹) |
| TWAP (discrete, unequal intervals) | $TWAP = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot \Delta t_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta t_i}$ | $\Delta t_i > 0$; sum of weights = $T$ |
| TWAP (continuous limit) | $TWAP = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} P(t)\,dt$ | $T > 0$; integral in currency·time units |
| Time Grid Step | $\Delta t = T / n$ | $n$ integer $\ge 1$ |
| Execution Slippage vs TWAP | $\text{Slippage} = P_{\text{exec}} - TWAP$ | Negative = good for buyer |
| Number of Slices | $n = T / \Delta t$ | Floor rounding acceptable |
| Comparison Benchmark | $VWAP = \dfrac{\sum P_i \cdot V_i}{\sum V_i}$ | $V_i$ = volume in interval $i$ |
| Variance of TWAP estimator | $\sigma^2_{TWAP} = \dfrac{\sigma^2_P}{n}$ | Assumes i.i.d. price samples |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Connection**
> TWAP is mathematically identical to the **average value of a continuous signal** in Electrical Engineering (Signal & Systems) and to the **trapezoidal mean** in Numerical Methods. The same concept underpins **DC offset**, **signal averaging**, and **sensor fusion** in instrumentation.

### 🌍 Real-World Utility

| Domain | Use of TWAP Concept |
|---|---|
| Algorithmic Trading | Designing TWAP execution algorithms (slice orders evenly in time) |
| Mutual Funds | Comparing NAV execution against benchmark TWAP |
| Regulators (SEBI / SEC) | Surveillance of unusual trade timing |
| Forensic Finance | Detecting "marking the close" manipulation |
| Index Construction | Time-weighted aggregation of constituent prices |
| Economic Statistics | Time-weighted consumer price averaging |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🔢 Derivation 1 — TWAP from the Continuous Average

The continuous average of any function $f(t)$ over $[a, b]$ is defined as:

$$\overline{f} = \frac{1}{b - a}\int_{a}^{b} f(t)\,dt$$

Substitute $f(t) = P(t)$ (price as a function of time), $a = 0$, $b = T$:

$$\boxed{TWAP = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} P(t)\,dt}$$

To convert to the discrete form, approximate the integral using a **piecewise constant** assumption (each price holds for its interval $\Delta t_i$):

$$\int_{0}^{T} P(t)\,dt \;\approx\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot \Delta t_i$$

Dividing by $T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta t_i$:

$$TWAP = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot \Delta t_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta t_i}$$

When $\Delta t_i = \Delta t$ (equal intervals):

$$TWAP = \frac{\Delta t \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i}{n \cdot \Delta t} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$$

This confirms the simple arithmetic-mean form used in practice.

---

### 🔢 Derivation 2 — Worked Numerical Example (Equal Intervals)

> **Problem:** NSE stock **INFYTECH** trades from 9:15 AM to 3:30 PM. A trader records the following **last-traded prices at every 30-minute interval** during the session:

| Interval # | Time | Price $P_i$ (₹) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 09:15 | 1,500.00 |
| 2 | 09:45 | 1,510.00 |
| 3 | 10:15 | 1,495.00 |
| 4 | 10:45 | 1,505.00 |
| 5 | 11:15 | 1,498.00 |
| 6 | 11:45 | 1,512.00 |
| 7 | 12:15 | 1,508.00 |
| 8 | 12:45 | 1,515.00 |
| 9 | 13:15 | 1,520.00 |
| 10 | 13:45 | 1,518.00 |
| 11 | 14:15 | 1,525.00 |
| 12 | 14:45 | 1,522.00 |
| 13 | 15:00 | 1,528.00 |
| 14 | 15:15 | 1,530.00 |
| 15 | 15:30 | 1,526.00 |

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**
$n = 15$ observations, $\Delta t = 30$ minutes, $T = 15 \times 30 = 450$ minutes (7.5 hours).

**Step 2 — Sum the prices:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{15} P_i &= 1500 + 1510 + 1495 + 1505 + 1498 + 1512 \\
&\quad + 1508 + 1515 + 1520 + 1518 + 1525 + 1522 \\
&\quad + 1528 + 1530 + 1526
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the addition term by term:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Partial Sum}_1 &= 1500 + 1510 = 3010 \\
\text{Partial Sum}_2 &= 3010 + 1495 = 4505 \\
\text{Partial Sum}_3 &= 4505 + 1505 = 6010 \\
\text{Partial Sum}_4 &= 6010 + 1498 = 7508 \\
\text{Partial Sum}_5 &= 7508 + 1512 = 9020 \\
\text{Partial Sum}_6 &= 9020 + 1508 = 10528 \\
\text{Partial Sum}_7 &= 10528 + 1515 = 12043 \\
\text{Partial Sum}_8 &= 12043 + 1520 = 13563 \\
\text{Partial Sum}_9 &= 13563 + 1518 = 15081 \\
\text{Partial Sum}_{10} &= 15081 + 1525 = 16606 \\
\text{Partial Sum}_{11} &= 16606 + 1522 = 18128 \\
\text{Partial Sum}_{12} &= 18128 + 1528 = 19656 \\
\text{Partial Sum}_{13} &= 19656 + 1530 = 21186 \\
\text{Partial Sum}_{14} &= 21186 + 1526 = 22712
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the formula:**
$$TWAP = \frac{1}{15} \times 22712 = 1514.13 \; \text{₹}$$

> ✅ **Final Answer: TWAP = ₹ 1,514.13**

---

### 🔢 Derivation 3 — Unequal Intervals (Mixed Time Weights)

> **Problem:** A commodity trader samples crude oil prices over 4 windows of *unequal* duration:

| Window | Duration $\Delta t_i$ (hours) | Price $P_i$ (USD/bbl) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A | 1 | 82.0 |
| B | 2 | 84.5 |
| C | 3 | 81.0 |
| D | 2 | 85.0 |

**Step 1 — Compute weighted numerator:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\sum P_i \cdot \Delta t_i &= (82.0)(1) + (84.5)(2) + (81.0)(3) + (85.0)(2) \\
&= 82.0 + 169.0 + 243.0 + 170.0 \\
&= 664.0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute denominator (total time):**
$$\sum \Delta t_i = 1 + 2 + 3 + 2 = 8 \; \text{hours}$$

**Step 3 — Divide:**
$$TWAP = \frac{664.0}{8} = 83.00 \; \text{USD/bbl}$$

> ✅ **Final Answer: TWAP = USD 83.00 per barrel**

---

### 🔢 Derivation 4 — Comparison Table: TWAP vs VWAP

Using the same INFYTECH data, suppose the matched volumes were:

| Interval | Price $P_i$ (₹) | Volume $V_i$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1,500 | 5,000 |
| 2 | 1,510 | 12,000 |
| 3 | 1,495 | 8,000 |
| 4 | 1,505 | 15,000 |
| 5 | 1,498 | 4,000 |
| 6 | 1,512 | 18,000 |
| 7 | 1,508 | 9,000 |
| 8 | 1,515 | 11,000 |
| 9 | 1,520 | 14,000 |
| 10 | 1,518 | 6,000 |
| 11 | 1,525 | 17,000 |
| 12 | 1,522 | 10,000 |
| 13 | 1,528 | 7,000 |
| 14 | 1,530 | 13,000 |
| 15 | 1,526 | 9,000 |

**TWAP** (already computed): ₹ 1,514.13

**VWAP** calculation:
$$\begin{aligned}
\sum P_i V_i &= (1500)(5000) + (1510)(12000) + (1495)(8000) + (1505)(15000) \\
&\quad + (1498)(4000) + (1512)(18000) + (1508)(9000) + (1515)(11000) \\
&\quad + (1520)(14000) + (1518)(6000) + (1525)(17000) + (1522)(10000) \\
&\quad + (1528)(7000) + (1530)(13000) + (1526)(9000)
\end{aligned}$$

| Sub-Calculation | Value (₹·shares) |
|:---|---:|
| $1500 \times 5000$ | 7,500,000 |
| $1510 \times 12000$ | 18,120,000 |
| $1495 \times 8000$ | 11,960,000 |
| $1505 \times 15000$ | 22,575,000 |
| $1498 \times 4000$ | 5,992,000 |
| $1512 \times 18000$ | 27,216,000 |
| $1508 \times 9000$ | 13,572,000 |
| $1515 \times 11000$ | 16,665,000 |
| $1520 \times 14000$ | 21,280,000 |
| $1518 \times 6000$ | 9,108,000 |
| $1525 \times 17000$ | 25,925,000 |
| $1522 \times 10000$ | 15,220,000 |
| $1528 \times 7000$ | 10,696,000 |
| $1530 \times 13000$ | 19,890,000 |
| $1526 \times 9000$ | 13,734,000 |
| **Total $\sum P_i V_i$** | **239,453,000** |

| Sub-Calculation | Value (shares) |
|:---|---:|
| Total Volume | 5,000 + 12,000 + 8,000 + 15,000 + 4,000 + 18,000 + 9,000 + 11,000 + 14,000 + 6,000 + 17,000 + 10,000 + 7,000 + 13,000 + 9,000 |
| **Total Volume** | **158,000** |

$$VWAP = \frac{239{,}453{,}000}{158{,}000} = 1515.53 \; \text{₹}$$

> ✅ **Final Answer: VWAP = ₹ 1,515.53**

### 📊 Final Comparative Matrix

| Metric | TWAP | VWAP |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Computed Value (₹) | **1,514.13** | **1,515.53** |
| Weighting Basis | Time | Volume |
| Data Required | Prices only | Prices + Volumes |
| Sensitivity to Liquidity | Low | High |
| Useful For | Time-sliced algo orders | Volume-profiled execution |
| Susceptibility to Spoofing | Low | Medium-High |
| KTU Complexity Tier | Foundational | Intermediate |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🔁 Flowchart — TWAP Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["TRADING SESSION START"] --> nodeB["DEFINE TIME WINDOW T"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["DECIDE NUMBER OF SLICES n"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["COMPUTE DELTA T = T DIVIDED BY n"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["SAMPLE PRICE AT EACH t i"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["STORE ALL PRICES P1 TO Pn"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["COMPUTE SUM OF ALL PRICES"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["DIVIDE SUM BY n"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["OUTPUT TWAP VALUE"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["COMPARE WITH VWAP"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["REPORT EXECUTION SLIPPAGE"]
```

### 🏗️ Block Architecture — TWAP vs VWAP Benchmark Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph timeInput["TIME INPUT LAYER"]
        tiA["CLOCK MODULE"]
        tiB["INTERVAL DIVIDER"]
    end

    subgraph priceLayer["PRICE SAMPLING LAYER"]
        plA["MARKET DATA FEED"]
        plB["PRICE SNAPSHOT AT t i"]
        plC["STORE P i VECTOR"]
    end

    subgraph volumeLayer["VOLUME SAMPLING LAYER"]
        vlA["TRADE TAPE"]
        vlB["VOLUME AGGREGATOR"]
        vlC["STORE V i VECTOR"]
    end

    subgraph calcEngine["CALCULATION LAYER"]
        ceA["TWAP ENGINE EQUAL TIME WEIGHT"]
        ceB["VWAP ENGINE VOLUME WEIGHT"]
        ceC["OUTPUT TWAP AND VWAP"]
    end

    tiB --> plB
    tiA --> vlB
    plB --> plC
    plC --> ceA
    vlB --> vlC
    plC --> ceC
    vlC --> ceC
    ceA --> ceC
    ceB --> ceC
```

### 📈 Concept Map — TWAP in the Broader Benchmark Family

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root["EXECUTION BENCHMARKS IN MARKETS"]
    root --> timeBench["TIME BASED BENCHMARKS"]
    root --> volumeBench["VOLUME BASED BENCHMARKS"]
    root --> hybridBench["HYBRID BENCHMARKS"]

    timeBench --> twapNode["TWAP TIME WEIGHTED AVERAGE PRICE"]
    timeBench --> arrivalNode["ARRIVAL PRICE BENCHMARK"]
    timeBench --> closeNode["CLOSING PRICE BENCHMARK"]

    volumeBench --> vwapNode["VWAP VOLUME WEIGHTED AVERAGE PRICE"]
    volumeBench --> povNode["PERCENT OF VOLUME POV"]

    hybridBench --> implShort["IMPLEMENTATION SHORTFALL"]
    hybridBench --> isReal["IS REALISED VS ARRIVAL"]

    twapNode --> algoA["TWAP SLICING ALGORITHM"]
    twapNode --> useA["USED FOR PASSIVE EXECUTION"]
    vwapNode --> algoB["VWAP SLICING ALGORITHM"]
    vwapNode --> useB["USED FOR PARTICIPATION STRATEGY"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
*Define Time Weighted Average Price (TWAP). State its formula for equal intervals.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (2 marks):** Time Weighted Average Price (TWAP) is the arithmetic mean of the price of a security sampled at regular intervals over a specified time horizon. It is a *time-based* execution benchmark that gives equal importance to every time slice, regardless of how many shares traded during that slice.
>
> **Formula (1 mark):**
> $$TWAP = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$$
> where $P_i$ is the price recorded in the $i^{th}$ interval and $n$ is the total number of intervals.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*Distinguish between TWAP and VWAP in two points.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Criterion | TWAP | VWAP |
> |:---|:---|:---|
> | Weighting Factor | Time elapsed in each interval | Volume traded in each interval |
> | Data Requirement | Price series only | Price series + Volume series |
>
> **Additional valid points (any one, 1 mark):**
> - TWAP is less sensitive to liquidity bursts; VWAP reflects where liquidity actually occurred.
> - TWAP is preferred for low-volume or thinly-traded stocks; VWAP is the standard benchmark for liquid large-caps on NSE/BSE.

---

### 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU ESE):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 3, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Time Weighted Average Price in detail. Derive the formula for TWAP from the continuous-time integral. State two real-world applications of TWAP. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The last-traded prices of a stock observed at one-hour intervals during a 5-hour trading window are: ₹200, ₹204, ₹198, ₹210, ₹206, ₹202.
*(i)* Calculate the TWAP.
*(ii)* If a trader executed buy orders of 500 shares at each timestamp, calculate the average execution price and compare it with TWAP. State whether the trader beat the benchmark. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Concept (3 marks):**
TWAP is the time-based average of an asset's price, calculated by sampling the price at fixed time intervals and taking their arithmetic mean. It is used by institutional traders as a passive benchmark to spread large orders across a trading session, thereby reducing market impact and information leakage.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
The continuous average value of price function $P(t)$ over window $[0, T]$ is:
$$\overline{P} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} P(t)\,dt$$
This is by definition the TWAP. Discretising the integral by assuming price is piecewise-constant over each interval $\Delta t_i$:
$$\int_{0}^{T} P(t)\,dt \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot \Delta t_i$$
Hence:
$$TWAP = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot \Delta t_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta t_i}$$
For equal intervals ($\Delta t_i = \Delta t$), this reduces to $TWAP = \frac{1}{n}\sum P_i$.

**Applications (1 mark — any two valid points):**
1. Algorithmic execution — TWAP-slice algos for parent order breaking.
2. Mutual fund NAV benchmarking — comparing deployment cost vs market TWAP.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

*Data:* 6 prices, each held for 1 hour. Total $T = 5$ hours, but $n = 6$ snapshots (start + 5 hourly marks). $n = 6$.

**(i) TWAP Calculation (3 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
TWAP &= \frac{200 + 204 + 198 + 210 + 206 + 202}{6} \\
&= \frac{1220}{6} \\
&= 203.33 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Average Execution Price (3 marks):**

Since equal share quantities at each timestamp, the execution price is also the simple average:
$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{avg,exec}} &= \frac{(200)(500) + (204)(500) + (198)(500) + (210)(500) + (206)(500) + (202)(500)}{6 \times 500} \\
&= \frac{500 \times 1220}{3000} \\
&= 203.33 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comparison (1 mark):**
The trader's average execution price exactly equals the TWAP benchmark. Slippage = $203.33 - 203.33 = 0$. The trader **met the benchmark but did not beat it** (zero slippage is the neutral outcome).

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 4, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Discuss the limitations of TWAP as an execution benchmark. How does it compare with VWAP in terms of market-impact measurement? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A portfolio manager places a buy order for 60,000 shares of stock ABC between 10:00 AM and 2:00 PM. The TWAP benchmark for the period is ₹540. The manager's average execution price works out to ₹546.
*(i)* Calculate the implementation shortfall in absolute and percentage terms.
*(ii)* If the brokerage charged ₹0.50 per share, what is the *net* implementation shortfall?
*(iii)* Comment on whether the manager outperformed, matched, or underperformed the benchmark. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Limitations of TWAP (4 marks — any four valid points):**
1. **Ignores volume:** TWAP treats a quiet minute and a busy minute identically — unrealistic for liquid benchmarks.
2. **Price-only dependence:** Highly susceptible to volatility spikes during the chosen window.
3. **Manipulation risk:** Traders can push prices temporarily at the boundary timestamps to bias the average.
4. **Static benchmark:** Cannot adapt to intraday volume patterns.
5. **Equal time slicing:** When liquidity is uneven, equal time slices cause excessive impact during high-volume periods.

**TWAP vs VWAP for market-impact measurement (3 marks):**
- TWAP measures *time-averaged* price drift; useful when the strategy is *time-paced* (passive).
- VWAP measures the *volume-weighted* transaction cost; more accurate when capital is deployed in proportion to liquidity.
- TWAP understates impact in trending markets because it ignores where trading actually happened. VWAP captures the true *participation cost*.
- In practice, buy-side institutions prefer **VWAP** for performance evaluation and **TWAP** for low-urgency passive order slicing.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Given: $TWAP = 540$, $P_{\text{exec}} = 546$, shares $Q = 60{,}000$, brokerage $c = 0.50$/share.

**(i) Implementation Shortfall — Absolute & % (3 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{IS}_{\text{abs}} &= (P_{\text{exec}} - TWAP) \times Q \\
&= (546 - 540) \times 60{,}000 \\
&= 6 \times 60{,}000 \\
&= 360{,}000 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}$$
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{IS}_{\%} &= \frac{P_{\text{exec}} - TWAP}{TWAP} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{6}{540} \times 100 \\
&= 1.111\%
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Net Shortfall after Brokerage (2 marks):**
Brokerage total $= 0.50 \times 60{,}000 = 30{,}000$ ₹

Total cost overrun = Gross shortfall + Brokerage:
$$\text{Net IS} = 360{,}000 + 30{,}000 = 390{,}000 \; \text{₹}$$

**(iii) Performance Comment (2 marks):**
The manager paid ₹6/share **above** the TWAP benchmark. For a *buy* order, paying more is **unfavourable** → the manager **underperformed** the benchmark by 1.111% (gross). After adding brokerage, the net underperformance is ₹390,000 (≈₹6.50 per share or 1.20%). The execution strategy should be reviewed — possibly switching to a VWAP-based slicing algorithm or delaying execution to better-volume periods.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (read carefully before writing your exam):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert time units:** If the question mixes hours and minutes, students often miss the unit consistency. Always verify that $\Delta t$ and $T$ share the same unit.
> 2. **Confusing TWAP with VWAP formula:** A frequent error is plugging volume into TWAP or omitting volume in VWAP. Mark cut: 1–2 marks per occurrence.
> 3. **Slippage sign convention:** Examiners deduct marks if you state *"the trader beat the benchmark"* without specifying whether the order was a buy or sell. Always clarify: for buys, lower is better; for sells, higher is better.
> 4. **Skipping the "%" unit in Implementation Shortfall:** A ₹360,000 shortfall *without* a percentage term usually loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Volume-vs-time weight confusion:** In unequal-interval TWAP, students often forget to multiply $P_i$ by $\Delta t_i$. Always write the weighted formula explicitly.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TWAP = Time-based simple average of price snapshots.** It is the *temporal* counterpart of VWAP.
- **Equal-weight formula (most used):** $TWAP = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum P_i$
- **General formula (unequal intervals):** $TWAP = \dfrac{\sum P_i \Delta t_i}{\sum \Delta t_i}$
- **Continuous limit:** $TWAP = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T P(t)\,dt$
- **Key input:** only *prices* and their *timestamps* — *no volume required*.
- **Used by:** algorithmic traders, mutual funds, regulators, performance analysts.
- **Advantages:** simple, transparent, robust to volume data errors, easy to compute.
- **Limitations:** ignores volume, blind to liquidity, vulnerable to end-of-window manipulation.
- **TWAP vs VWAP:** TWAP weighs by *time*, VWAP weighs by *volume*.
- **Slippage formula:** $\text{Slippage} = P_{\text{exec}} - TWAP$ (sign convention depends on buy/sell).
- **Estimator variance:** falls as $1/n$ — more samples → more stable TWAP.
- **Typical KTU exam weightage:** Part A (3 marks) + Part B (up to 14 marks with sub-parts).
- **Must-mention keywords in answers:** *time-weighted*, *arithmetic mean*, *time grid*, *equal weighting*, *benchmark*, *execution quality*, *slippage*.
- **Watch for trap questions:** TWAP is NOT the same as moving average — moving averages use a sliding window; TWAP uses a fixed execution window.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Participation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Participation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Participation in Financial Markets** refers to the structured involvement of various categories of economic entities — *issuers, investors, intermediaries, and regulators* — in the mobilization, allocation, and trading of financial securities (equity, debt, derivatives, and hybrid instruments) across **primary and secondary markets**, governed by the regulatory framework of the Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI).

In KTU parlance, "Participation" in Module 3 specifically addresses the **Players in the Indian Financial System**: who they are, what roles they perform, how they are classified, and what regulations govern their conduct. The four pillars of participation are:

1. **Issuers** (Companies, Governments, Municipal Bodies) — supply side of capital.
2. **Investors** (Retail, Institutional, Foreign) — demand side of capital.
3. **Intermediaries** (Brokers, Depositories, Merchant Bankers, Underwriters) — facilitators.
4. **Regulators** (SEBI, RBI, IRDA, PFRDA) — overseers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a Financial Market as a massive Saturday Vegetable Market (Sabzi Mandi):**
> - The **farmers** bringing tomatoes = **Issuers** (companies selling shares to raise money).
> - The **buyers** (housewives, restaurants, exporters) = **Investors** (retail, institutional, foreign).
> - The **commission agents (aadhtiyas)** moving goods, weighing, and settling payments = **Intermediaries** (brokers, depositories).
> - The **Municipal Corporation inspector** ensuring fair weights and no adulteration = **Regulators** (SEBI, RBI).
> 
> Just like the sabzi mandi cannot function without any one of these four groups, a capital market collapses without balanced participation from all four.

## 1.3 Classification of Market Participants (Master Framework)

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies participants along **two orthogonal axes**:

### Axis A: By Function
| Function | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Issuer / Borrower** | Raises capital by issuing securities | Infosys Ltd., Govt. of India |
| **Investor / Lender** | Allocates surplus funds to securities | LIC, SBI Mutual Fund, Retail Trader |
| **Intermediary** | Enables transactions between issuers and investors | Zerodha, NSE Clearing, CDSL |
| **Regulator** | Sets rules, ensures fairness | SEBI, RBI |

### Axis B: By Origin
- **Domestic Participants** — Residents of India (RBI definition: person resident in India under FEMA, 1999).
- **Foreign Participants** — NRIs, FPIs (Foreign Portfolio Investors), FDIs (Foreign Direct Investors).

## 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

- **Standard Trading Lot** on NSE: **1 share** (equity cash market, post-2010 dematerialization).
- **Settlement Cycle** (post T+1 rollout, Jan 2023): **T+1 day** — *where T is the trade execution day*.
- **Minimum Contract Size** for Index Futures (NIFTY): **₹ (Multiplied by Lot Size)** — currently **25 units per lot**.
- **FPI Aggregate Limit** in a company: **24%** of paid-up capital (can be raised to **sectoral cap** by the company).
- **SEBI Registration Fee (Stock Broker)**: ranges from **₹10,000 to ₹50,000** based on turnover slab.

> [!NOTE]
> These metrics are **regulatory benchmarks** and are periodically revised by SEBI circulars. For KTU exams, cite the figure as "as per current SEBI regulations."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars — Structural Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Pillar 1: Issuers (Supply Side)

**Why they participate:** To raise capital for expansion, working capital, debt refinancing, or infrastructure.

**How they participate:**
- **Equity Issuance** — IPO (Initial Public Offering), FPO (Follow-on Public Offering), Rights Issue, Private Placement, QIP (Qualified Institutional Placement).
- **Debt Issuance** — Corporate Bonds, Commercial Paper (CP), Certificate of Deposit (CD), Government Securities (G-Secs), Treasury Bills (T-Bills).

**Key Logic Flow:**
$$
\text{Issuer} \xrightarrow{\text{appoints}} \text{Lead Manager (Merchant Banker)} \xrightarrow{\text{drafts}} \text{DRHP/RHP} \xrightarrow{\text{files with}} \text{SEBI}
$$

### 2.1.2 Pillar 2: Investors (Demand Side)

The KTU syllabus breaks this into **three sub-categories** which examiners frequently test:

| Investor Class | Definition (KTU) | Typical AUM Share (India, 2024) |
|---|---|---|
| **Retail Individual Investor (RII)** | Individuals investing up to ₹2 lakh in an IPO, trading in own name | ~ 7-9% of equity turnover |
| **Domestic Institutional Investor (DII)** | Mutual funds, insurance companies, pension funds (EPFO), banks | ~ 12-15% |
| **Foreign Portfolio Investor (FPI)** | NRIs, foreign entities investing in listed securities (FPI route, since 2014) | ~ 18-22% of cash market |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** *FII (Foreign Institutional Investor)* was the older term (pre-2014 SEBI regulations). *FPI* is the **current** regulatory category under the SEBI (Foreign Portfolio Investors) Regulations, 2014. Any answer using "FII" without clarification is marked as **outdated** by KTU evaluators.

### 2.1.3 Pillar 3: Intermediaries

**The KTU syllabus emphasizes these five intermediaries:**

1. **Stock Brokers** — Execute buy/sell orders on behalf of clients. Registered with SEBI. May be individual, partnership, corporate. Sub-brokers act as agents of brokers.
2. **Merchant Bankers** — Manage IPOs, FPOs, rights issues, M&A advisory. Need a SEBI Category-I license for issue management.
3. **Depositories & Depository Participants (DPs)** — Hold securities in **demat (dematerialized) form**. Two depositories in India: **NSDL** (National Securities Depository Ltd., 1996) and **CDSL** (Central Depository Services Ltd., 1999). DPs are agents (e.g., banks, brokers) who interface with retail investors.
4. **Registrar & Transfer Agents (RTAs)** — Maintain shareholder records, process dividend payments, handle share transfers.
5. **Custodians** — Hold foreign investors' securities. In India, most FPIs use domestic custodians like **SBI Cap Securities, HDFC Bank Custody Services, Standard Chartered, Citibank NA**.

### 2.1.4 Pillar 4: Regulators

| Regulator | Domain | Statutory Authority |
|---|---|---|
| **SEBI** | Securities market | SEBI Act, 1992 |
| **RBI** | Banking, monetary policy, forex | RBI Act, 1934; FEMA, 1999 |
| **IRDAI** | Insurance | IRDA Act, 1999 |
| **PFRDA** | Pension | PFRDA Act, 2013 |
| **MCA** | Corporate governance | Companies Act, 2013 |

## 2.2 The High-Yield KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All numerical problems in KTU exams on Module 3 reduce to **the four formulas below**. Memorize the boundary conditions.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Boundary Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Retail Investor IPO Cut-off** | $\text{QIB} \ge 75\% \;,\; \text{Non-Institutional} \le 15\% \;,\; \text{Retail} \ge 35\%$ (min) | SEBI ICDR Regulations, 2018. Issue size < ₹10 cr may have relaxed norms. |
| 2 | **FPI Sectoral Cap** | $L_{FPI} \le 24\% \; \text{of paid-up capital}$ | Company can raise via board + shareholder resolution. FDI cap sector-specific (e.g., Banking 74%, Insurance 74%, Telecom 100%). |
| 3 | **Brokerage Commission** | $C_b = T \times r_b$ | $T$ = Turnover (₹), $r_b$ = brokerage rate (capped by exchange, typically 2.5% of trade value or ₹20 per executed order, whichever is lower for cash market). |
| 4 | **Demat Settlement Value** | $S = P \times Q$ | $P$ = Settlement price, $Q$ = Quantity. Delivery vs. cash settlement differs for derivatives (cash = $\max(S_T - K, 0)$ for long call). |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Management

For KTU B.Tech students — even non-finance branches — participation in financial markets is now a **life-skill**:

- **ESOP (Employee Stock Ownership Plan)** valuation: B.Tech employees of startups receive stock options, requiring understanding of FPI/DII participation to gauge exit liquidity.
- **Treasury management** in corporates: Operations and Finance teams interact with CP/CD markets.
- **Personal finance**: SIPs in mutual funds = DII participation; buying IPOs = RII participation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: IPO Subscription Allocation Logic (Retail Investor)

The KTU 2024 syllabus frequently tests the **pro-rata allotment mechanism** for retail investors. Let us derive the formula step-by-step.

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A company ABC Ltd. issues 10,00,000 shares to the public in the following SEBI-mandated split:
> - QIB: 50% (5,00,000 shares)
> - Non-Institutional (NII): 15% (1,50,000 shares)
> - Retail (RII): 35% (3,50,000 shares)
>
> The retail portion is subscribed **3.2 times** (i.e., 11,20,000 retail applications for 3,50,000 shares). An individual retail investor Mr. X applied for **1,000 shares**. How many shares will he be allotted?

### Step 1 — Total Retail Subscribed Shares

$$
T_{sub} = Q_{retail} \times \text{Subscription Multiple}
$$

$$
T_{sub} = 3{,}50{,}000 \times 3.2
$$

$$
T_{sub} = 11{,}20{,}000 \; \text{shares}
$$

### Step 2 — Pro-rata Ratio for Retail

Under SEBI ICDR Regulations, when retail oversubscription exceeds a threshold, the registrar applies **pro-rata allotment with a uniform lottery floor** (minimum 1 share per successful applicant).

$$
r = \frac{Q_{retail}}{T_{sub}}
$$

$$
r = \frac{3{,}50{,}000}{11{,}20{,}000}
$$

$$
r = 0.3125
$$

### Step 3 — Base Pro-rata Share for Mr. X

$$
A_{base} = Q_{applied} \times r
$$

$$
A_{base} = 1{,}000 \times 0.3125 = 312.5 \; \text{shares}
$$

### Step 4 — Floor Adjustment

Since fractional shares are not possible, we apply the floor:

$$
A_{Mr.X} = \lfloor A_{base} \rfloor = \lfloor 312.5 \rfloor = 312 \; \text{shares}
$$

*(With residual shares reallocated via a computerized random lottery among applicants who received a pro-rata floor.)*

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Brokerage Charge Calculation

> **Problem:** A retail investor executes the following trades through Broker Z on NSE in April 2024:
> - Buy 500 shares of RELIANCE at ₹2,900 each.
> - Sell 300 shares of TCS at ₹4,100 each.
> Brokerage rate: ₹20 per executed order OR 0.1% of trade value, whichever is **lower**.

### Step 1 — Compute Trade Values

$$
V_{buy} = 500 \times 2{,}900 = 14{,}50{,}000 \; \text{₹}
$$

$$
V_{sell} = 300 \times 4{,}100 = 12{,}30{,}000 \; \text{₹}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute 0.1% of Each Trade Value

$$
b_{buy}^{(pct)} = 14{,}50{,}000 \times 0.001 = 1{,}450 \; \text{₹}
$$

$$
b_{sell}^{(pct)} = 12{,}30{,}000 \times 0.001 = 1{,}230 \; \text{₹}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Minimum Rule

For Buy Order:

$$
b_{buy} = \min(1{,}450,\; 20) = 20 \; \text{₹}
$$

For Sell Order:

$$
b_{sell} = \min(1{,}230,\; 20) = 20 \; \text{₹}
$$

### Step 4 — Total Brokerage

$$
B_{total} = b_{buy} + b_{sell} = 20 + 20 = 40 \; \text{₹}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STT (Securities Transaction Tax), GST (18%), and Stamp Duty** are charged **separately** and are **not part of "brokerage"** in KTU terminology. Examiners deduct marks if these are clubbed.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Investor Behavior Across Market Conditions

| Participant Type | Bull Market Stance | Bear Market Stance | Sideways Market Stance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Retail (RII)** | Aggressive buying, FOMO-driven | Panic selling, stop-loss triggers | Sectoral rotation |
| **FPI** | Net buyer (inflow) | Net seller (outflow) | Hedged positions via derivatives |
| **DII (Mutual Funds)** | Cautious buying, SIPs continue | Counter-cyclical buying (heroes) | Index rebalancing |
| **Promoters / Insiders** | Pledge creation increases | Pledge invocation risk rises | Buybacks |
| **Proprietary Desk of Broker** | Risk-on, leverage up | Risk-off, deleveraging | Vol-selling (option writing) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A 14-mark question often asks: *"Explain the role of DIIs as 'counter-cyclical buyers' when FPIs withdraw from the Indian market in 2022-23."* Use the matrix above as the analytical backbone of such answers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Architecture: Indian Financial Market Participation Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeReg[Regulators: SEBI, RBI, IRDAI, PFRDA, MCA]
    nodeIss[Issuers: Central Govt, State Govt, PSUs, Corporates, Municipal Bodies]
    nodeInv[Investors: Retail RII, DII Mutual Fund, DII Insurance, FPI, FDI]
    nodeInt[Intermediaries: Brokers, Merchant Bankers, Depositories NSDL CDSL, RTAs, Custodians]
    nodeInfra[Infrastructure: Stock Exchanges NSE BSE, Clearing Corporations, Settlement Systems]

    nodeReg --> nodeIss
    nodeReg --> nodeInv
    nodeReg --> nodeInt
    nodeReg --> nodeInfra
    nodeIss --> nodeInt
    nodeInv --> nodeInt
    nodeInt --> nodeInfra
    nodeIss -.issues securities to.-> nodeInv
    nodeInv -.trades via.-> nodeInfra
```

## 4.2 Flow: Equity Issuance (IPO Process & Participant Roles)

```mermaid

    nodeMB[Merchant Banker: Lead Manager]
    nodeSEBI[SEBI: Regulator]
    nodeExch[Stock Exchange: NSE BSE]
    nodeRTA[RTA: Registrar]
    nodeInvR[Retail Investors]
    nodeInvF[FPI QIB]

    nodeCo --> nodeMB
    nodeMB --> nodeSEBI
    nodeSEBI --> nodeExch
    nodeExch --> nodeRTA
    nodeRTA --> nodeInvR
    nodeRTA --> nodeInvF
    nodeInvR --> nodeExch
    nodeInvF --> nodeExch
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: Secondary Market Trading Cycle (T+1 Settlement)

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA[Step A: Investor places order via Broker]
    stepB[Step B: Broker routes to Exchange NSE BSE]
    stepC[Step C: Trade matched on Exchange]
    stepD[Step D: Clearing Corp NSCCL ICCL confirms]
    stepE[Step E: T+1 Delivery of shares via Depository]
    stepF[Step F: T+1 Cash settlement via Bank]

    stepA --> stepB

```

## 4.4 Decision Tree: How Retail Investor Chooses Participation Mode

```mermaid

    nodeHigh[High Risk]
    nodeFD[Bank FD, Liquid MF, T-Bills]
    nodeBond[Corporate Bonds, G-Secs]
    nodeEq[Direct Equity, Equity MF]

    nodeStart --> nodeQ1
    nodeQ1 --> nodeST
    nodeQ1 --> nodeLT
    nodeLT --> nodeQ2
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Distinguish between Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) and Foreign Portfolio Investment (FPI).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Dimension | FDI | FPI |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Investment made to acquire **long-term management interest** in an enterprise (≥10% voting power, RBI threshold). | Investment in **listed securities** (equity, debt) without intent of control. |
| **Route** | Through FIPB (now replaced by automatic + government routes), under FEMA 1999. | Through FPI registration under SEBI (FPI) Regulations 2014. |
| **Lock-in** | Often subject to sector-specific lock-in / minimum capitalization. | No lock-in; can exit at any time. |
| **Risk to Economy** | Stable, less volatile. | Hot money — causes exchange rate volatility. |

*(2 marks for table, 1 mark for any one supporting example like FDI in Telecom 100%.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is the role of a Depository in the Indian securities market?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Depository** is an organization registered with SEBI under the Depositories Act, 1996, which holds securities of investors in **electronic (demat) form**, thereby eliminating the risks associated with physical certificates (forgery, loss, theft). 

In India, two depositories operate: **NSDL** (promoted by IDBI Bank, 1996) and **CDSL** (promoted by BSE, 1999). 

The Depository interfaces with retail investors through **Depository Participants (DPs)** — which can be banks, brokers, or custodians. The unique **Beneficial Owner (BO) ID** identifies each demat account holder.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for NSDL/CDSL naming, 1 mark for DP/BO explanation.)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - July 2024]*

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **different categories of participants** in the Indian securities market. Explain the regulatory role of SEBI in governing their conduct. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company XYZ Ltd. is launching an IPO of 50,00,000 equity shares with the following SEBI-mandated allocation:
- QIB: 50%
- Non-Institutional: 15%
- Retail: 35%
The retail portion is subscribed **4 times**. Mr. K applied for 800 shares in the retail category. Calculate the number of shares likely allotted to him on a pro-rata basis. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The participants are classified as:

1. **Issuers** (2 marks): Companies raising capital through IPO, FPO, rights, QIP; Central and State Governments issuing G-Secs; PSUs issuing bonds.

2. **Investors** (2 marks):
   - *Retail (RII)*: Individuals investing up to ₹2 lakh in IPO.
   - *DII*: Mutual Funds, Insurance Companies (LIC), Pension Funds (EPFO).
   - *FPI*: Foreign Portfolio Investors under SEBI 2014 Regulations.

3. **Intermediaries** (2 marks): Stock Brokers, Sub-Brokers, Merchant Bankers, Registrars, Depositories, Custodians.

4. **Regulators** (1 mark): SEBI is the apex regulator under SEBI Act, 1992. It registers intermediaries, frames regulations (ICDR, Insider Trading, PFUTP), and conducts investigations via its Enforcement Department.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Retail shares available:

$$
Q_{retail} = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.35 = 17{,}50{,}000 \; \text{shares}
$$

Retail subscription (4x):

$$
T_{sub} = 17{,}50{,}000 \times 4 = 70{,}00{,}000 \; \text{shares}
$$

Pro-rata ratio:

$$
r = \frac{17{,}50{,}000}{70{,}00{,}000} = 0.25
$$

Mr. K base allotment:

$$
A_{base} = 800 \times 0.25 = 200 \; \text{shares}
$$

Final allotment (no fractional, floor applied):

$$
\boxed{A_{Mr.K} = 200 \; \text{shares}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Explain the **functions of Merchant Bankers** and **Custodians** in the Indian capital market. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An investor executed the following trades through an online broker:
- Buy 200 shares of HDFC Bank at ₹1,650.
- Sell 150 shares of Infosys at ₹1,450.
- Buy 500 shares of ITC at ₹430.
The broker charges ₹20 per executed order OR 0.1% of trade value, whichever is lower. Calculate the **total brokerage**. What other charges (STT, GST) would the investor bear? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Merchant Bankers (4 marks):**
A Merchant Banker is a SEBI-registered intermediary (Category I for issue management) who manages:
- Issue management (IPO, FPO, Rights).
- Underwriting of public issues.
- Advisory on M&A, corporate restructuring.
- Preparation of DRHP/RHP and coordination with SEBI, exchanges, RTAs.

**Custodians (3 marks):**
A Custodian is a SEBI-registered entity that holds securities on behalf of clients (primarily FPIs). Functions:
- Safekeeping of securities (in demat form).
- Settlement of trades.
- Collection of dividends and interest.
- Reporting to clients and tax authorities.
- Examples: SBI Caps Custody, Citibank NA, HSBC.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Compute each trade value:

$$
V_1 = 200 \times 1{,}650 = 3{,}30{,}000 \; \text{₹}
$$

$$
V_2 = 150 \times 1{,}450 = 2{,}17{,}500 \; \text{₹}
$$

$$
V_3 = 500 \times 430 = 2{,}15{,}000 \; \text{₹}
$$

Compute 0.1%:

$$
b_1^{(pct)} = 330,\quad b_2^{(pct)} = 217.50,\quad b_3^{(pct)} = 215
$$

Apply minimum rule:

$$
b_1 = \min(330, 20) = 20
$$

$$
b_2 = \min(217.50, 20) = 20
$$

$$
b_3 = \min(215, 20) = 20
$$

Total brokerage:

$$
\boxed{B_{total} = 60 \; \text{₹}}
$$

Other charges (2 marks):
- **STT** = 0.1% of trade value on purchase side of delivery trades + 0.025% on sale side.
- **GST** = 18% on brokerage + 18% on transaction charges + 18% on SEBI charges.
- **Stamp Duty** = state-specific (e.g., 0.015% on buy side in Maharashtra/Kerala).
- **Exchange Transaction Charge** = ~0.00345% (NSE).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mixing FII with FPI** (worth 1 mark loss) — FII is obsolete since 2014. Use FPI.
> 2. **Confusing Depository (NSDL/CDSL) with Depository Participant (DP)** — Depository ≠ DP. The DP is the bank/broker; the depository is the underlying entity.
> 3. **Forgetting to apply the floor function** in pro-rata allotment — fractional shares do not exist; examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 4. **Including GST/STT inside "brokerage"** — these are statutory levies, separate from brokerage. Deduct 1 mark.
> 5. **Wrong subscription multiple interpretation** — "subscribed 4 times" means demand = 4 × supply, not supply = 4 × demand.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Participation**

- ✅ **Four pillars**: Issuers, Investors, Intermediaries, Regulators.
- ✅ **Investor sub-classes**: RII (≤₹2L IPO), NII (₹2L–₹1Cr), QIB (>₹1Cr).
- ✅ **SEBI ICDR Allocation**: QIB ≤75%, NII ≤15%, RII ≥35% (when oversubscribed).
- ✅ **FII → FPI migration**: SEBI (FPI) Regulations, **2014**.
- ✅ **FPI cap**: 24% of paid-up capital (sectoral FDI caps are separate and higher in many sectors).
- ✅ **Two depositories**: **NSDL** (1996) and **CDSL** (1999); DPs are their agents.
- ✅ **Settlement cycle**: T+1 (effective Jan 2023, India pioneered this globally).
- ✅ **Brokerage rule**: min(₹20 flat, 0.1% of trade value) for discount brokers — but full-service brokers can charge percentage brokerage.
- ✅ **Regulators**: SEBI (securities), RBI (banking/FEMA), IRDAI (insurance), PFRDA (pension), MCA (companies).
- ✅ **Merchant Banker** = issue manager; **Custodian** = safekeeper for FPIs; **RTA** = shareholder record keeper.
- ✅ **Domestic Institutional Investors (DIIs)** are **counter-cyclical heroes** — they buy when FPIs sell, stabilizing the market.
- ✅ **Pro-rata allotment** uses the formula $A = \lfloor Q_{applied} \times (Q_{available}/Q_{subscribed}) \rfloor$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Market on Close
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Market on Close

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Market on Close (MOC) order** is a specialized type of equity order instructed by a trader to a brokerage to execute a transaction (buy or sell) exactly at the **official closing price** of the trading day, as determined by the **closing auction** conducted by the exchange. The order is not executed during continuous intraday trading; instead, it is routed into the closing price determination process, where it is matched against offsetting contra-side MOC orders.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Market on Close orders are classified under the broader domain of **conditional/time-specific order types** in equity cash market trading. They belong to the family of orders whose execution is contingent upon a specific market event (in this case, the closing auction) rather than real-time price discovery.

Mathematically, the execution price $P_{exec}$ of an MOC order is:

$$
P_{exec} = P_{close} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i \cdot Q_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i}
$$

where $P_{close}$ is the **Volume-Weighted Average Price (VWAP)** derived from the closing auction, $P_i$ is the price of the $i^{th}$ matched order, and $Q_i$ is the corresponding quantity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Vegetable Auction at Closing Time**

Imagine a wholesale vegetable market that operates from 6 AM to 6 PM. Throughout the day, farmers and buyers haggle continuously over prices. However, at exactly 5:50 PM, a special **closing bell** rings. A new mini-auction begins where any leftover crates of tomatoes are bundled together and sold at a single, official "closing price" — calculated as the average price at which all bundled crates got matched. A farmer who has 100 kg of tomatoes and wants the *guarantee* of a sale at whatever this closing rate turns out to be hands a slip saying "**Sell my 100 kg at the closing price, whatever it is.**" That farmer has placed a **Market on Close order**.

The key intuition:
- The trader **does not know** the exact execution price in advance.
- The trader **does know** that the order *will* execute (provided liquidity exists).
- The trader surrenders price control in exchange for **execution certainty** at a benchmarked, transparent endpoint.

## 1.3 Key Terminology in KTU Context

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **MOC Order** | Market on Close order; executes at the official closing auction price |
| **Closing Auction** | The final price discovery mechanism run by the exchange before market close |
| **Cut-off Time** | The last timestamp (typically a few minutes before close) by which MOC orders must be entered |
| **MOC Imbalance** | The net excess of buy MOC orders over sell MOC orders (or vice versa) at cut-off |
| **Limit on Close (LOC)** | A variant that executes only if the closing price is at or better than a specified limit |
| **Closing Print** | The final reported closing price of the security for the day |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (NSE/BSE context):** The standard MOC order cut-off time on Indian exchanges is generally **15 minutes before market close** for equities, with a brief **MOC Imbalance freeze period** during which the order book is published to allow offsetting entries.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

The execution lifecycle of a Market on Close order can be decomposed into **six distinct stages**:

1. **Order Entry (Pre-Market / Intraday):**
   The trader submits an MOC order through the broker's terminal. The order carries a special order type flag (e.g., `MKT_CLS` on NSE NEAT system) and is held in a separate MOC order book — *not* the continuous trading book.

2. **Continuous Trading Phase (09:15 – 15:00 IST on NSE):**
   During regular hours, the MOC order remains passive. It is *not* matched with the live order book. Meanwhile, normal market orders execute continuously at prevailing bid/ask prices.

3. **Cut-off Timestamp Trigger:**
   At a pre-defined time (e.g., 15:00 hours on NSE for cash market MOC), the exchange suspends new MOC entries. The system calculates the **MOC Imbalance** — the net unmatched buy or sell quantity.

4. **Imbalance Publication:**
   The exchange disseminates the indicative imbalance and an indicative closing price to the market. This is meant to attract offsetting orders from the contra side to reduce volatility at the close.

5. **Closing Auction Call Phase:**
   During a brief window (typically 8–10 minutes), a continuous call auction is conducted. Buy and sell MOC orders, plus any marketable limit orders entered during this phase, are matched.

6. **Execution & Final Print:**
   All matched MOC orders execute at the single, calculated **closing price (closing VWAP)**. The trade is reported, and the order is closed.

## 2.2 Why Use a Market on Close Order? — The Core Rationale

- **Benchmark Tracking:** Passive funds and institutional portfolios must reconcile their end-of-day Net Asset Value (NAV) to the closing price. MOC orders guarantee participation at the official print.
- **Index Rebalancing:** When an index reconstitution adds or removes a stock, index funds must trade at the close on the effective date. MOC is the default mechanism.
- **Reduced Market Impact:** Spreading a large order through the day can move the price. Concentrating execution into a single, liquidity-rich auction can sometimes be cheaper.
- **Execution Certainty:** For traders prioritizing completion over price (e.g., stop-loss policies, de-risking mandates), MOC ensures the position is closed.

## 2.3 Advantages and Disadvantages — Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|
| **Execution Certainty** | High — almost always fills | Low control over exact price |
| **Price Discovery** | Transparent, exchange-calculated closing price | Vulnerable to closing-price manipulation |
| **Slippage Risk** | Reduced for benchmark traders | Can be severe during illiquid closes |
| **Timing Risk** | No intraday monitoring required | Late adverse news may move closing print dramatically |
| **Cost** | No explicit premium | May experience higher implicit costs vs. VWAP algos |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Avoiding Markdown Breaking Pipes:** All absolute-value / norm notations below use `\vert` instead of the raw `|` character to preserve table integrity.

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Representation | Practical Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Closing Price (Auction VWAP) | $P_{close} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot Q_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i}$ | Volume-weighted equilibrium of closing auction |
| Net MOC Imbalance | $I_{MOC} = \sum Q_{buy}^{MOC} - \sum Q_{sell}^{MOC}$ | Sign indicates directional pressure; magnitude indicates magnitude |
| MOC Imbalance Ratio | $R_{imbal} = \dfrac{\vert \sum Q_{buy}^{MOC} - \sum Q_{sell}^{MOC} \vert}{\sum Q_{buy}^{MOC} + \sum Q_{sell}^{MOC}}$ | Normalized measure (0 to 1) of one-sidedness |
| Implied Closing Price (Pre-Auction) | $P_{impl} = P_{ref} + \beta \cdot \dfrac{I_{MOC}}{V_{day}}$ | A linear estimate where $P_{ref}$ is reference, $V_{day}$ is day volume |
| Effective Spread (MOC) | $S_{MOC} = P_{close} - P_{mid, last}$ | Measures deviation of close from intraday midpoint |
| Closing Auction Duration | $T_{auction}$ | Standardized by exchange (NSE: ~8–10 min) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

While this is a finance topic, the **underlying principle** of a closing auction is an instance of a **constrained optimization problem** solvable in computational and operations research domains:

- **Clearing Algorithms:** Auction matching engines use **double-sided priority queues** and **price-time priority algorithms** — concepts rooted in computer science and operations engineering.
- **Resource Allocation:** Just as an electrical grid balances load, the closing auction balances unmatched buy/sell pressure.
- **Signal Processing:** The MOC imbalance publication is analogous to a **filtered state estimate** disseminated to all participants for corrective action — similar to control systems feedback.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Closing Auction Price as a Volume-Weighted Average

The closing price in a call auction is not arbitrary; it is computed as the price that **maximizes the executable volume**, with ties broken by minimizing the imbalance.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Let:
- $\mathcal{B} = \{(P_i^{b}, Q_i^{b})\}$ for $i = 1, 2, \dots, m$ be the set of buy limit orders with price $P_i^{b}$ and quantity $Q_i^{b}$.
- $\mathcal{S} = \{(P_j^{s}, Q_j^{s})\}$ for $j = 1, 2, \dots, n$ be the set of sell limit orders.
- MOC orders are treated as **marketable at any price**, i.e., $P_i^{b} = +\infty$ and $P_j^{s} = 0$.

### 3.1.2 Cumulative Demand and Supply Functions

For any candidate price $p$, define:

$$
D(p) = \sum_{i : P_i^{b} \geq p} Q_i^{b} \quad \text{(cumulative buy quantity willing to buy at price } p \text{ or higher)}
$$

$$
S(p) = \sum_{j : P_j^{s} \leq p} Q_j^{s} \quad \text{(cumulative sell quantity willing to sell at price } p \text{ or lower)}
$$

### 3.1.3 Executable Volume

The volume that can be executed at price $p$ is:

$$
V_{exec}(p) = \min \left( D(p), \, S(p) \right)
$$

### 3.1.4 Optimization Step

The closing price $P_{close}$ is the price that maximizes executable volume:

$$
P_{close} = \arg\max_{p} \; V_{exec}(p)
$$

If multiple prices yield the same maximum volume, the exchange selects the one that **minimizes the residual imbalance** $\vert D(p) - S(p) \vert$, and if still tied, the price closest to the last traded price.

### 3.1.5 Conversion to VWAP Form

Once $P_{close}$ is determined, all matched orders are settled at this single price. If we let $\mathcal{M}$ denote the set of matched buy orders, then:

$$
P_{close} = \frac{\sum_{(i, j) \in \mathcal{M}} \left( \frac{P_i^{b} + P_j^{s}}{2} \cdot \min(Q_i^{b}, Q_j^{s}) \right)}{\sum_{(i, j) \in \mathcal{M}} \min(Q_i^{b}, Q_j^{s})}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, because the exchange forces a single clearing price, the above reduces to a uniform price for all trades. This is the **uniform-price double auction** theorem.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> **Scenario:** Stock XYZ has the following MOC + limit orders entered for the closing auction:

| Order ID | Side | Type | Price (₹) | Quantity (shares) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| O1 | Buy | MOC | Market | 1,000 |
| O2 | Buy | Limit | 102.00 | 500 |
| O3 | Buy | Limit | 101.00 | 800 |
| O4 | Sell | MOC | Market | 700 |
| O5 | Sell | Limit | 101.50 | 600 |
| O6 | Sell | Limit | 100.50 | 400 |

### Step 1: Compute Cumulative Demand and Supply at Each Candidate Price

For each candidate price $p$, identify which orders would be matched:

| Candidate Price $p$ (₹) | Buy Orders Willing | $D(p)$ | Sell Orders Willing | $S(p)$ | $V_{exec}(p) = \min(D,S)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 102.00 | O1, O2 | 1,500 | O4, O5, O6 | 1,700 | 1,500 |
| 101.50 | O1, O2, O3 | 2,300 | O4, O5, O6 | 1,700 | 1,700 |
| 101.00 | O1, O2, O3 | 2,300 | O4, O5, O6 | 1,700 | 1,700 |
| 100.50 | O1, O2, O3 | 2,300 | O4, O5, O6 | 1,700 | 1,700 |

### Step 2: Identify the Maximum Executable Volume

The maximum executable volume is **1,700 shares**, achieved at any price in the range ₹100.50 to ₹101.50.

### Step 3: Apply Tie-Breaker (Minimize Imbalance)

Imbalance at any price in this range:

$$
I = \vert D(p) - S(p) \vert = \vert 2{,}300 - 1{,}700 \vert = 600 \text{ shares}
$$

Since imbalance is uniform, the tie-breaker becomes the **last traded price**. Suppose the last intraday trade was at ₹101.20. Hence, $P_{close} = \mathbf{101.20}$ (or as close as auction rules allow).

### Step 4: Compute the Imbalance Ratio

$$
R_{imbal} = \frac{600}{2{,}300 + 1{,}700} = \frac{600}{4{,}000} = 0.15 \quad \text{or } \mathbf{15\%}
$$

This 15% buy-side imbalance would be published by the exchange before the auction to attract sellers.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use MOC

| Trader Objective | Should Use MOC? | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Index fund reconstituting holdings | **Yes (mandatory)** | Closing price is the index methodology benchmark |
| Day-trader with intraday alpha signal | **No** | Loses ability to exit mid-day if thesis invalidates |
| Pension fund de-risking at quarter-end | **Yes** | Audit-trail and benchmark alignment |
| Arbitrageur exploiting last-second mispricing | **Sometimes** | MOC + aggressive limit may capture end-of-day dislocation |
| Retail investor with no time to monitor market | **Yes (cautious)** | Avoids emotional intraday decisions |

## 3.4 Pitfalls in Numerical Interpretation

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Calculation Error:** Students often confuse the **closing print** (single uniform price) with a **VWAP computed from continuous trades**. These are mathematically different. The closing print is *only* the equilibrium price of the closing auction; it does not include the daytime VWAP unless explicitly specified.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Market on Close Order Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trader Decides to Place MOC Order] --> B[Broker Routes Order to Exchange]
    B --> C{Order Type Validated?}
    C -- No --> D[Reject: Invalid Order Type]
    C -- Yes --> E[Order Entered in MOC Book]
    E --> F[Continuous Trading Phase 09:15 to 15:00]
    F --> G[Cut-off Time Reached ~15:00]
    G --> H[Compute MOC Imbalance]
    H --> I[Publish Indicative Imbalance and Price]
    I --> J[Closing Auction Call Phase ~8 to 10 min]
    J --> K[Match Buy and Sell MOC plus Limit Orders]
    K --> L{Sufficient Contra-side Liquidity?}
    L -- No --> M[Partial Fill or Cancellation]
    L -- Yes --> N[Determine Single Closing Price Pclose]
    N --> O[Execute All Matched Orders at Pclose]
    O --> P[Report Closing Print to Market]
    P --> Q[End of Trading Day]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Decision Flow for Choosing MOC vs Other Order Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Need Execution at Day End?] -- Yes --> B[Price Control Important?]
    A -- No --> C[Use Limit or Market Order Intraday]
    B -- Yes --> D[Use Limit on Close LOC]
    B -- No --> E[Use Market on Close MOC]
    E --> F[Cut-off: 15 min before close]
    F --> G[Accept Closing Auction Price]
    D --> H[Set Limit Price Above Expected Close]
    H --> I{Close Above Limit?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Order Executes]
    I -- No --> K[Order Cancelled]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Closing Auction Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph InputLayer[INPUT LAYER]
        A1[Buy MOC Orders]
        A2[Sell MOC Orders]
        A3[Buy Limit Orders greater than or equal to P]
        A4[Sell Limit Orders less than or equal to P]
    end
    subgraph ProcessingLayer[PROCESSING LAYER]
        B1[Cumulative Demand Function D of p]
        B2[Cumulative Supply Function S of p]
        B3[Executable Volume V of p equals min D S]
        B4[Optimizer: argmax V of p]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer[OUTPUT LAYER]
        C1[Single Closing Price Pclose]
        C2[Matched Order Reports]
        C3[Indicative Imbalance Publication]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A4 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> C1
    B1 --> C3
    B2 --> C3
    C1 --> C2
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram: MOC vs LOC vs Regular Market Order

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Order Type Selection] --> B{Objective?}
    B -- Immediate Execution at Best Price --> C[Regular Market Order]
    B -- Execution at Closing Auction Price --> D[Market on Close MOC]
    B -- Execution at Close if Price Better than Limit --> E[Limit on Close LOC]
    C --> F[Executes in Continuous Book]
    D --> G[Routed to MOC Book]
    E --> H[Hybrid: Limit + Closing Auction]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid node IDs above are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `A1`, `B1`, `InputLayer`) to comply with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguard protocol. No reserved keywords are used as standalone node names.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a Market on Close (MOC) order. Mention one situation where it is commonly used.**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Market on Close (MOC) order** is a buy or sell order that is executed at the official closing price of the security, as determined by the exchange's closing auction at the end of the trading day. The order is not matched during continuous intraday trading; instead, it is held and matched during the closing price determination process. **(2 marks)**

**Situation:** MOC orders are commonly used by **index-tracking funds and ETFs** when rebalancing their portfolios to align with changes in the underlying index, since the index closing value is calculated from official closing prices. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Q2. State any two advantages of using a Market on Close order.**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Execution Certainty:** MOC orders provide a high probability of execution, making them suitable for traders who prioritize completion of the trade over exact price control. **(1.5 marks)**

2. **Benchmark Alignment:** They allow institutional investors and passive funds to transact at the official closing price, which is the standard benchmark for NAV calculation and index tracking. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 14-Mark Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the operational mechanism of a Market on Close order. Describe the six stages from order entry to final execution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a suitable numerical example, demonstrate how the closing auction price is determined as the volume-maximizing equilibrium price. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):** **(7 marks)**

The operational mechanism of an MOC order proceeds through the following six stages:

1. **Order Entry:** The trader submits the MOC order to the broker, who routes it to the exchange with an MOC flag. It is held in a dedicated MOC order book. **(1 mark)**

2. **Continuous Trading Phase:** During the regular trading session (e.g., 09:15 to 15:00 IST on NSE), the MOC order remains unmatched in the continuous order book. Normal market and limit orders are matched continuously. **(1 mark)**

3. **Cut-off Trigger:** At a pre-defined time (typically 15 minutes before market close), the exchange suspends new MOC entries and computes the **net MOC imbalance**. **(1 mark)**

4. **Imbalance Publication:** The exchange disseminates the indicative imbalance and an indicative closing price to all participants, allowing offsetting entries to reduce one-sidedness. **(1.5 marks)**

5. **Closing Auction:** A brief call-auction phase begins. Buy and sell MOC orders, plus any marketable limit orders entered in this window, are matched using price-time priority and uniform-price logic. **(1.5 marks)**

6. **Execution at Closing Print:** All matched orders execute at the single computed closing price. The closing print is reported, and the day's trading is concluded. **(1 mark)**

---

**Model Solution for (b):** **(7 marks)**

Consider the following MOC and limit orders for a stock ABC at closing auction:

| Order | Side | Type | Price (₹) | Quantity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| O1 | Buy | MOC | Market | 2,000 |
| O2 | Buy | Limit | 50.00 | 1,000 |
| O3 | Buy | Limit | 49.00 | 500 |
| O4 | Sell | MOC | Market | 1,500 |
| O5 | Sell | Limit | 50.50 | 800 |
| O6 | Sell | Limit | 49.50 | 600 |

**Step 1: Compute cumulative demand and supply.** **(2 marks)**

At candidate price $p = 50.00$:
- Buy orders willing: O1 (MOC, always), O2 (≤ 50) → $D = 2{,}000 + 1{,}000 = 3{,}000$
- Sell orders willing: O4 (MOC, always), O5 (≥ 50) → $S = 1{,}500 + 800 = 2{,}300$
- $V_{exec} = \min(3{,}000, 2{,}300) = 2{,}300$

At $p = 49.50$:
- $D = 3{,}000 + 500 = 3{,}500$
- $S = 1{,}500 + 800 + 600 = 2{,}900$
- $V_{exec} = 2{,}900$

At $p = 49.00$:
- $D = 3{,}500$
- $S = 2{,}900$
- $V_{exec} = 2{,}900$

**Step 2: Identify maximum executable volume.** **(2 marks)**

The maximum $V_{exec} = 2{,}900$ shares, achieved in the price range ₹49.00 to ₹49.50.

**Step 3: Apply tie-breaker — minimize imbalance, then proximity to last trade.** **(2 marks)**

Imbalance = $|3{,}500 - 2{,}900| = 600$ in this range.

Assume last traded price was ₹49.30. The exchange will set:

$$
P_{close} = \text{₹}49.30 \text{ (closest to last trade within the equilibrium band)}
$$

**Step 4: State the final closing price and imbalance ratio.** **(1 mark)**

$$
P_{close} = \text{₹}49.30, \quad R_{imbal} = \frac{600}{3{,}500 + 2{,}900} = \frac{600}{6{,}400} \approx 9.375\%
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast Market on Close (MOC) orders with regular Market orders and Limit on Close (LOC) orders. Highlight the strategic situations in which each is most appropriate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the advantages, disadvantages, and risks associated with using Market on Close orders. Provide real-world contexts in which MOC is the preferred execution method. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):** **(7 marks)**

| Parameter | Regular Market Order | Market on Close (MOC) | Limit on Close (LOC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Execution Time | Immediate, intraday | At closing auction | At closing auction, conditionally |
| Price Control | None | None (full surrender) | Conditional (must be at or better than limit) |
| Execution Certainty | High in liquid stocks | Very high | Conditional on limit vs. close |
| Typical User | Active day traders | Index funds, passive managers | Risk-averse institutional traders |
| Order Book | Continuous book | MOC book | Hybrid: MOC + limit condition |
| Cost Structure | Visible bid-ask spread | Implicit closing-spread cost | Risk of non-execution |

**Strategic situations:** **(2 marks within the table discussion)**

- **Regular Market Order:** When a trader has an immediate alpha signal and wants to capture intraday price movement.
- **MOC Order:** When the trader's mandate is benchmarked to the closing price (e.g., index reconstitution, NAV calculation, end-of-day rebalancing).
- **LOC Order:** When the trader requires execution at close but only if the close is at or better than a pre-specified price floor/ceiling.

**Mark allocation summary:** Tabular comparison (4 marks), strategic situations (3 marks).

---

**Model Solution for (b):** **(7 marks)**

**Advantages (3 marks):**

1. **Benchmark Alignment:** MOC orders execute at the official closing price, which is the standard benchmark for index funds and ETFs computing NAV.
2. **Execution Certainty:** Subject to liquidity, the order will execute — useful for de-risking mandates.
3. **Reduced Monitoring Burden:** The trader need not watch the screen intraday; the order is handled at the close.

**Disadvantages and Risks (3 marks):**

1. **Price Surrender:** The trader has no control over the execution price, which can deviate sharply from the last intraday trade, especially in illiquid stocks.
2. **Closing Manipulation Risk:** Historically, "marking the close" — a form of market manipulation — has occurred where traders push the closing price to benefit derivative positions. Regulators monitor this.
3. **Liquidity Risk:** If contra-side MOC interest is insufficient, the order may receive only a partial fill or may not fill at all.

**Real-world contexts (1 mark):**

- NSE / BSE index reconstitution events (quarterly / periodic index rebalancing).
- End-of-day pension fund portfolio rebalancing.
- ETF creation/redemption unit settlement at closing NAV.
- Quantitative funds marking positions to the closing benchmark.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Conflating closing price with intraday VWAP:** Many students wrongly assume the closing print is the volume-weighted average of all daytime trades. It is *not*; it is the auction-derived equilibrium price. **(–2 marks typical)**
> 2. **Ignoring the cut-off time:** Failing to mention that MOC orders must be entered before the cut-off (e.g., 15:00 IST) is considered an incomplete answer. **(–1 mark)**
> 3. **Confusing MOC with LOC:** Limit on Close is *not* the same as Market on Close. LOC has a price condition; MOC does not. Examiners specifically test this distinction. **(–2 marks)**
> 4. **Omitting the imbalance publication step:** The pre-auction imbalance publication is a key part of the modern closing auction mechanism and is frequently asked. **(–1 mark)**
> 5. **Forgetting tie-breaker rules:** When multiple prices yield the same executable volume, students often forget to state the imbalance-minimization and last-trade-proximity tie-breakers. **(–1 mark)**

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Market on Close"**

- **MOC = executes at official closing auction price**, not intraday market.
- **Six stages:** Order Entry → Continuous Phase → Cut-off → Imbalance Publication → Closing Auction → Execution.
- **Cut-off time:** Typically 15 minutes before market close on Indian exchanges.
- **Imbalance:** Net difference between total buy and sell MOC quantities; published for transparency.
- **Closing price formula:** The price that **maximizes executable volume**, with **minimum imbalance** as the tie-breaker, and **proximity to last trade** as the final tie-breaker.
- **Formula:** $P_{close} = \arg\max_{p} \, \min(D(p), S(p))$.
- **Imbalance Ratio:** $R_{imbal} = \dfrac{\vert I_{MOC} \vert}{\text{Total MOC quantity}}$.
- **LOC vs. MOC:** LOC has a price condition; MOC executes unconditionally at the close.
- **Primary users:** Index funds, ETFs, pension funds, quantitative rebalancing strategies.
- **Main risk:** Loss of price control and vulnerability to closing-price manipulation.
- **Key advantage:** Benchmark alignment and execution certainty.
- **KTU-specific term to remember:** **"Uniform-price double auction"** — the closing auction is a textbook example of this mechanism.
- **Mathematical form:** Closing price is a single clearing price, *not* a continuous-trade VWAP.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arrival Price
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Arrival Price

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Arrival Price (AP):** In the context of financial market microstructure and execution analytics, the **Arrival Price** is rigorously defined as the prevailing **mid-market price** (or the touch price) of a security **at the precise instant** when a trading instruction (parent order) is *generated* by the portfolio manager or *submitted* into the market by the execution desk. It functions as the **fundamental pre-trade reference benchmark** against which the actual realized execution price is later compared to quantify execution performance.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the Arrival Price is the cornerstone of the **Implementation Shortfall (IS)** framework, originally formalized by André Perold in 1988. It captures the "paper portfolio" state of the market at the moment the investment decision was made.

### 1.2 Components of the Reference Price

There are three standard reference points used in practice:

1. **Arrival Price (Decision Price):** Mid-quote at the time the *decision* to trade was made.
2. **Opening Price (Market Open Benchmark):** Price at the start of the trading session.
3. **Closing Price (Market Close Benchmark):** Price at the end of the trading session.

For this module, our focus is exclusively on the **Decision/Arrival Price**, which is the most aggressive and informative benchmark in the Implementation Shortfall family.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!TIP]
> **The Auto-Rickshaw Meter Analogy 🚕**
>
> Imagine you book an auto-rickshaw from Kottayam to the railway station via a phone app. The moment you tap **"Confirm Ride"**, the app shows an **estimated fare of ₹120** based on current fuel prices and traffic. This ₹120 is your **Arrival Price** — the price at the moment of *decision*.
>
> Now, by the time the auto actually arrives and starts the meter, the fare shows **₹135** because traffic congestion increased and a new surge-pricing rule kicked in. The difference of **₹15** is your **Slippage**.
>
> In stock markets, the moment your algorithm decides to **BUY 1000 shares of TCS**, the screen flashes a price of **₹3,450** (the Arrival Price). However, due to price impact, latency, and market drift, your actual average fill happens at **₹3,456**. The **₹6 difference per share** is the cost you paid for *waiting* and *executing* — this cost is measured against the Arrival Price.

### 1.4 Key Metrics at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three **bold-marked** pillars of this topic:
> - **Arrival Price** = Decision-time mid-quote
> - **Slippage** = Execution Price − Arrival Price
> - **Implementation Shortfall** = (Execution Price − Arrival Price) × Quantity + Visible + Invisible costs
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Arrival Price

The Arrival Price exists because of one unavoidable truth in financial markets: **time causes information leakage and price drift.** A portfolio manager who decides to buy a stock at 10:00:00 AM at ₹500 will rarely, if ever, get filled exactly at ₹500. The price will move against them by the time the order reaches the exchange. The Arrival Price isolates this movement and quantifies it.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step by Step

**Step 1: Decision Trigger**
The portfolio manager (PM) finalizes the trade decision. A child order is generated.

**Step 2: Snapshot Capture**
The trading system records the National Best Bid and Offer (NBBO) at that exact millisecond.
- Bid = ₹499.50
- Ask = ₹500.50
- Mid = (499.50 + 500.50) / 2 = **₹500.00** ← This is the **Arrival Price**.

**Step 3: Order Routing**
The order is routed to the exchange via a broker or smart order router (SOR).

**Step 4: Execution Phase**
The order is filled in multiple slices over time, each at different prices.

**Step 5: Performance Attribution**
The execution desk calculates the deviation of the **Volume-Weighted Average Price (VWAP) of fills** from the **Arrival Price**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Formulas for KTU 2024 Board Examinations**

| Symbol / Term | Formula Expression | Boundary Condition | Real-World Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Arrival Price ($AP$)** | $AP = \frac{P_{bid} + P_{ask}}{2}$ | At $t = 0$ (decision time) | ₹ / USD per share |
| **Slippage per share** | $\text{Slip} = P_{exec} - AP$ | Positive = Adverse (loss) | ₹ / USD per share |
| **Implementation Shortfall (₹)** | $IS = (P_{exec} - AP) \times Q$ | $Q$ = Order Quantity | ₹ / USD absolute |
| **IS in Basis Points (bps)** | $IS_{bps} = \frac{P_{exec} - AP}{AP} \times 10000$ | 1 bp = 0.01% | basis points |
| **Shortfall Percentage** | $IS_{\%} = \frac{P_{exec} - AP}{AP} \times 100$ | Must match sign of trade direction | Percentage (%) |
| **VWAP of Fills** | $VWAP = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \times Q_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i}$ | Weighted by fill size | ₹ / USD per share |
| **Price Impact Component** | $PI = VWAP_{exec} - AP$ | Excludes timing cost | ₹ / USD per share |
| **Timing Cost (Drift)** | $TC = \text{Close Price} - AP$ | Captures market drift | ₹ / USD per share |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LATE-X TABLE RULE:** Note the use of `\vert` syntax in the table above to avoid breaking markdown parsers. **Never** use raw vertical pipes `\vert` inside formula cells.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Algorithmic Trading

The Arrival Price is the foundational benchmark for several institutional execution algorithms:

- **Implementation Shortfall (IS) Algorithms:** Actively target the Arrival Price as the optimal limit.
- **Liquidity-Seeking Algorithms:** Measure their success by slippage from AP.
- **Dark Pool Execution:** Quantifies price improvement gained by avoiding lit markets.
- **Transaction Cost Analysis (TCA):** The most legally defensible benchmark for broker performance review.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Comprehensive Solution

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A portfolio manager at a KTU-affiliated trading firm decides to **BUY 10,000 shares** of Infosys (INFY) at exactly **10:30:00 AM**. The current NBBO shows:
> - Bid = ₹1,499.00
> - Ask = ₹1,501.00
>
> The order is executed in **three slices** during the next 15 minutes:
> - Slice 1: 4,000 shares @ ₹1,501.20
> - Slice 2: 3,000 shares @ ₹1,503.50
> - Slice 3: 3,000 shares @ ₹1,504.80
>
