### 4.3 Arbitrage Mechanism — Cash-and-Carry vs Reverse Cash-and-Carry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Observed F0 vs Fair Value"] --> Check{"F0 greater than Fair Value?"}
    Check -->|Yes: Mispricing| Carry["CASH-AND-CARRY ARBITRAGE"]
    Check -->|No| Check2{"F0 less than Fair Value?"}
    Check2 -->|Yes: Mispricing| Reverse["REVERSE CASH-AND-CARRY ARBITRAGE"]
    Check2 -->|No| Equilibrium["EQUILIBRIUM<br/>No arbitrage opportunity"]

    Carry --> C1["Borrow at risk-free rate r"]
    C1 --> C2["Buy underlying at S0"]
    C2 --> C3["Sell futures at F0 overvalued"]
    C3 --> C4["At T: Deliver at F0<br/>Repay loan S0 e to the rT<br/>Profit = F0 minus S0 e to the rT"]
    C4 --> Profit1["Riskless Profit"]
    Profit1 --> Equalize["Price pressure pushes F0 down<br/>and S0 up"]

    Reverse --> R1["Short sell the underlying at S0"]
    R1 --> R2["Invest proceeds at r"]
    R2 --> R3["Buy futures at F0 undervalued"]
    R3 --> R4["At T: Receive asset<br/>Cost = F0 e to the -rT<br/>Profit = S0 e to the rT minus F0"]
    R4 --> Profit2["Riskless Profit"]
    Profit2 --> Equalize

    Equalize --> Equilibrium
```

**Explanation:** This is the **core arbitrage engine** that enforces the cost-of-carry formula. If $F_0$ deviates from its fair value in *either* direction, riskless arbitrage is possible, and arbitrage activity drives prices back to equilibrium.

---

### 4.4 Term Structure — Contango vs Backwardation Visually

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CONTANGO ["CONTANGO: Future Spot Expected to Rise OR Storage Cost Dominant"]
    P1["F1 = 100"] --> P2["F2 = 105"] --> P3["F3 = 110"] --> P4["F4 = 115"]
    end

    subgraph BACK ["BACKWARDATION: Near-Term Convenience OR High Yield"]
    Q1["F1 = 100"] --> Q2["F2 = 95"] --> Q3["F3 = 92"] --> Q4["F4 = 90"]
    end

    S0["Spot S0 = 98"]
    S0 -.-> P1
    S0 -.-> Q1
```

**Explanation:** Two distinct futures curve shapes. Contango (rising curve) reflects the market pricing in a *cost of carry* that exceeds any income. Backwardation (falling curve) reflects either (a) Keynes's *normal backwardation* where hedgers pay a premium for insurance, or (b) high convenience yield for a commodity with current scarcity.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*Define the term "futures price" and explain how it differs from the "spot price" of an asset.*

**Model Answer:**

> A **futures price** is the price agreed upon at the inception of a futures contract for delivery of the underlying asset at a specified future date. It is determined by the **no-arbitrage condition** in the market, equating the cost of two strategies that deliver the same asset at maturity.

> The **spot price** is the prevailing market price for immediate delivery and settlement of the asset. Key differences:

| Feature | Spot Price ($S_0$) | Futures Price ($F_0$) |
|---|---|---|
| Delivery | Immediate | Future date $T$ |
| Cash flow | Paid now | Paid at $T$ (initially zero) |
| Determines | Current demand-supply | Expectation + cost-of-carry |
| Daily settlement | No | Yes (MTM) |

> Mathematically, the relationship is $F_0 = S_0 e^{cT}$ where $c$ is the **net cost of carry**. At maturity, $F_T = S_T$ (convergence theorem).

**Marks Distribution (KTU pattern):** Definition (1), Difference (1), Example/equation (1).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

*What is "contango"? Under what market conditions does it typically arise?*

**Model Answer:**

> **Contango** is a market condition in which the futures price $F_0$ exceeds the spot price $S_0$ for a given maturity ($F_0 > S_0$), causing the futures curve to slope upward.

> It typically arises under the following conditions:
>
> 1. **Positive cost of carry:** When financing cost $r$ plus storage cost $u$ exceeds income $q$ and convenience yield $y$.
> 2. **Expectation of rising spot prices:** Market participants anticipate future scarcity.
> 3. **Low convenience yield:** The asset has no significant non-monetary benefit from physical possession (e.g., financial assets like stock indices).
> 4. **Excess supply in spot markets:** For commodities, immediate oversupply drives $S_0$ down relative to expected future $S_T$.

> **Example:** Crude oil markets often exhibit contango when inventory levels are high and storage tanks are full, since holding physical oil becomes costly.

**Marks Distribution (KTU pattern):** Definition (1), Conditions (1), Example (1).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign errors in storage cost vs convenience yield:** Students often confuse $u$ (cost, positive) with $y$ (benefit, subtracts). Always remember: $\text{cost adds, yield subtracts}$.
> 2. **Compounding convention mix-ups:** If the problem says "8% p.a. simple interest" but the formula uses $e^{rT}$, marks are lost. Verify before substituting.
> 3. **Forgetting PV of income:** When a discrete dividend is given, many students ignore it, leading to an *overstated* $F_0$.
> 4. **No-arbitrage argument reversed:** Some students write $F_0 = S_0 e^{-rT}$ instead of $F_0 = S_0 e^{rT}$. Memorize the direction.
> 5. **Currency misquote:** USD/INR vs INR/USD — the formula inverts. Always verify the quote convention.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** *Explain the no-arbitrage principle used to determine the futures price of an investment asset with no income. Derive the corresponding formula.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *The current spot price of a stock is ₹500. The expected dividend yield is 3% per annum. The risk-free interest rate is 6% per annum (continuous compounding). Find the futures price for delivery in 9 months.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a):**

**No-arbitrage principle** (definition): Two portfolios producing identical future cash flows must sell at the same price today. If they don't, arbitrageurs will trade to exploit the price difference, pushing prices back to equilibrium.

**Construction of replicating portfolios:**

- **Strategy 1 (Cash-and-Carry):** Borrow $S_0$ at rate $r$, buy the asset, hold it to maturity $T$, deliver it against a forward contract at price $F_0$.
- **Strategy 2 (Reverse Cash-and-Carry):** Short-sell the asset, invest the proceeds, buy a forward to cover the short position.

**Cash flow table:**

| Time | Strategy 1 | Strategy 2 |
|---|---|---|
| $t = 0$ | $-S_0$ (borrow) + $S_0$ (buy asset) = $0$ | $+S_0$ (short sale proceeds) - $S_0$ (enter forward) = $0$ |
| $t = T$ | $-S_0 e^{rT}$ (repay loan) + $F_0$ (deliver at $F_0$) | $+S_0 e^{rT}$ (from investment) $-F_0$ (receive under forward) |

**Setting NPV = 0:**

$$-S_0 e^{rT} + F_0 = 0 \implies F_0 = S_0 e^{rT}$$

**Final formula:**

$$\boxed{F_0 = S_0 e^{rT}}$$

**Interpretation:** The futures price grows at the risk-free rate because the cost of "storing" a financial asset is simply the financing cost.

---

**Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- $S_0 = 500$
- $q = 0.03$
- $r = 0.06$
- $T = 9/12 = 0.75$ years

**Formula (asset with continuous dividend yield):**

$$F_0 = S_0 \, e^{(r-q)T}$$

**Substitution:**

\begin{aligned}
F_0 &= 500 \cdot e^{(0.06 - 0.03) \times 0.75} \\
&= 500 \cdot e^{0.03 \times 0.75} \\
&= 500 \cdot e^{0.0225} \\
&= 500 \cdot 1.02276 \\
&\approx 511.38
\end{aligned}

**Answer:** $F_0 \approx \text{₹}511.38$.

**Interpretation:** The futures price (₹511.38) exceeds the spot price (₹500) because the net cost of carry $(r - q) = 3\%$ is positive. The market is in **mild contango**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** *Explain the concepts of "cost of carry", "convenience yield" and "backwardation" with reference to commodity futures pricing.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *The spot price of crude oil is \$85 per barrel. The storage cost is 2% per annum, the convenience yield is 3% per annum, and the risk-free rate is 5% per annum (continuous compounding). Find the 6-month futures price. Comment on whether the market is in contango or backwardation.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Cost of carry** is the net cost incurred (or benefit received) by holding a physical asset from time $t$ to time $T$. It includes:
- **Financing cost** $r$ (interest on money tied up),
- **Storage cost** $u$ (warehousing, insurance, spoilage),
- **Minus convenience yield** $y$ (non-monetary benefit of physical possession).

Mathematically:

$$c = r + u - y$$

**Convenience yield** is the implicit benefit enjoyed by the holder of the physical commodity that is *not* available to holders of futures contracts. It includes:
- Ability to quickly meet customer demand,
- Maintaining production runs during supply disruptions,
- Avoiding stock-out costs.

It is highest when inventories are low and the commodity is in production use.

**Backwardation** is a market condition where $F_0 < S_0$. It typically arises when:
1. **Convenience yield exceeds total cost** ($y > r + u$).
2. **Keynes's normal backwardation:** Hedgers (e.g., oil producers) are net short and willing to pay speculators a premium (i.e., accept a lower futures price) to insure against price drops.
3. **Current scarcity:** Spot prices are elevated due to immediate demand, but expected to normalize.

The full pricing formula incorporating all three concepts:

$$F_0 = S_0 \, e^{(r + u - y)T}$$

---

**Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- $S_0 = 85$
- $u = 0.02$
- $y = 0.03$
- $r = 0.05$
- $T = 0.5$ years

**Compute net cost of carry:**

$$c = r + u - y = 0.05 + 0.02 - 0.03 = 0.04$$

**Apply formula:**

\begin{aligned}
F_0 &= S_0 \, e^{cT} \\
&= 85 \cdot e^{0.04 \times 0.5} \\
&= 85 \cdot e^{0.02} \\
&= 85 \cdot 1.02020 \\
&\approx 86.72
\end{aligned}

**Answer:** $F_0 \approx \$86.72$ per barrel.

**Comment:** Although the futures price (₹86.72) is *slightly above* the spot price (\$85), the situation is **near equilibrium**. Since $c = 4\% > 0$, technically the market is in **mild contango**, but the convenience yield (3%) is *almost* offsetting the financing + storage cost (5% + 2% = 7%). If convenience yield were to rise (e.g., a geopolitical shock reduces inventory), the market would tip into backwardation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this checklist in the last 30 minutes before your KTU exam.**

- [ ] **Futures Price Definition:** Agreed price today for delivery at future date $T$, set by **no-arbitrage**.
- [ ] **Convergence Theorem:** $F_T = S_T$ at maturity — guaranteed by arbitrage.
- [ ] **Master Formula:** $F_0 = S_0 \, e^{cT}$ where $c = r + u - y - q$.
- [ ] **Investment Asset (No Income):** $F_0 = S_0 e^{rT}$.
- [ ] **Investment Asset (Known Income $I$ at $t_I$):** $F_0 = (S_0 - I e^{-r t_I}) e^{rT}$.
- [ ] **Investment Asset (Continuous Yield $q$):** $F_0 = S_0 e^{(r-q)T}$.
- [ ] **Commodity Asset:** $F_0 = S_0 e^{(r+u-y)T}$.
- [ ] **Currency:** $F_0 = S_0 e^{(r - r_f) T}$.
- [ ] **Contango:** $F_0 > S_0$ → curve slopes upward.
- [ ] **Backwardation:** $F_0 < S_0$ → curve slopes downward.
- [ ] **Cost-of-Carry Components:** Financing + storage minus yield/income.
- [ ] **Compounding Convention:** Continuous ($e^{rT}$) vs discrete ($(1+r)^T$) — never mix.
- [ ] **Arbitrage Engine:** Cash-and-carry when $F_0$ overpriced; reverse cash-and-carry when $F_0$ underpriced.
- [ ] **Risk-free rate $r$** is the *annualized* rate; multiply by $T$ in years before exponentiating.
- [ ] **PV vs FV:** Discount known income using $e^{-r t}$ (present value), then compound $F_0$ using $e^{rT}$.
- [ ] **Tick Size & Lot Size:** Exchange-specific (NIFTY = 25 units, MCX crude = 100 barrels); not needed in theory but useful in problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Mantra for KTU Valuation:** The examiner will award full marks only if you (i) state the **no-arbitrage principle** explicitly, (ii) derive the formula with **intermediate algebraic steps**, and (iii) interpret the result in terms of contango/backwardation. Always include a closing sentence interpreting the answer.

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hedging** is a risk management strategy employed in financial markets that involves taking an offsetting position in a related security, derivative instrument, or asset to reduce or neutralize the risk of adverse price movements in an existing investment. The primary objective of hedging is not to generate profit but to **protect against potential losses** arising from exposure to market volatility in equities, commodities, currencies, or interest rates.

In the formal language of modern portfolio theory and derivatives pricing, hedging is the construction of a portfolio consisting of the original risky asset and one or more derivative instruments whose combined exposure results in a **reduced, statistically minimized, or zero variance** of the underlying risk factor.

### Key Terminological Anchors

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Spot Price ($S$)** | The current market price at which an asset can be bought or sold for immediate delivery. |
| **Futures Price ($F$)** | The pre-agreed price at which an asset will be bought or sold at a specified future date. |
| **Long Hedge** | Taking a long position in futures to protect against a future rise in the price of an asset the hedger plans to buy. |
| **Short Hedge** | Taking a short position in futures to protect against a future fall in the price of an asset the hedger already owns or will produce. |
| **Basis ($b$)** | The difference between spot price and futures price: $b = S - F$. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Hedging is positioned as a foundational concept in Module 4, typically discussed in conjunction with derivatives, forwards, futures, options, and swaps. Examiners frequently test the ability to distinguish between speculation and hedging, identify the type of hedge required, and compute the optimal number of contracts.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **farmer in Wayanad** who has cultivated pepper on 5 acres of land. Your crop will be ready for harvest in **6 months**, and you are worried that the global pepper price might crash before then, leaving you unable to recover your cultivation cost of approximately **₹2,00,000**.

You have **two choices**:

1. **Do nothing (Unhedged):** Hope the price stays high. If it crashes from ₹500/kg to ₹300/kg, you lose ₹2 lakh in revenue.

2. **Hedge:** Today, you contact a buyer and sign a contract to sell your entire pepper harvest at **₹500/kg** in 6 months. This contract is essentially a **futures/forwards contract**.

By locking in the price today, you have transferred the price risk to someone else. You now **know exactly** how much you will earn, regardless of how the market moves. You have sacrificed the possibility of a windfall gain (if prices rose) in exchange for **certainty**.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Summary:** Hedging is the financial equivalent of buying **insurance for your investments**. Just as you pay a premium for car insurance to protect against accidents, you pay a small cost (or accept a small opportunity cost) to protect your financial position from adverse market moves. Insurance does not prevent the accident, but it covers the financial damage — similarly, hedging does not stop prices from falling, but it prevents financial loss.

---

## 1.3 Why Hedging Matters — The Core Economic Rationale

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of Hedging Justification:**

1. **Risk Reduction for Producers & Consumers:** Farmers (producers), manufacturers (consumers), airlines (fuel consumers), and exporters (currency receivers) all face price uncertainty. Hedging converts this uncertainty into predictable costs and revenues.

2. **Cash Flow Stability:** Corporations use hedging to stabilize earnings, satisfy debt covenants, and protect planned capital expenditure programs.

3. **Information Asymmetry & Agency Costs:** Managers hedge to reduce the volatility of firm value, thereby reducing the cost of financial distress and aligning with shareholder interest.

---

## 1.4 Hedging vs. Speculation vs. Arbitrage

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU examiner **frequently** asks students to differentiate between these three fundamental trading motives. Memorize the distinction below.

| Activity | Objective | Position Taken | Risk Profile |
|----------|-----------|----------------|--------------|
| **Hedging** | Reduce existing risk | Offsetting to existing exposure | Risk-reducing (protective) |
| **Speculation** | Profit from price movement | Directional bet (no underlying exposure) | Risk-increasing (leveraged) |
| **Arbitrage** | Lock in riskless profit | Simultaneous buy/sell in different markets | Zero-risk profit (theoretical) |

---

## 1.5 Categories of Risk Commonly Hedged

> [!TIP]
> A KTU-favorite 3-mark question: *"List the types of risks that can be hedged."* Use the mnemonic **"FCIE"** to remember:

1. **F**oreign Exchange (Currency) Risk
2. **C**ommodity Price Risk
3. **I**nterest Rate Risk
4. **E**quity Price Risk

Additional risks include **credit risk** (via credit derivatives), **inflation risk**, and **volatility risk** (via options).

---

## 1.6 Hedging Instruments — The Four Pillars

| Instrument | Mechanism | Best Use Case |
|------------|-----------|---------------|
| **Forwards** | Customized OTC agreement to buy/sell at a future date | Tailored corporate hedging |
| **Futures** | Standardized exchange-traded contracts | Liquid, mark-to-market hedging |
| **Options** | Right (not obligation) to buy/sell at strike price | Asymmetric protection (keep upside) |
| **Swaps** | Exchange of cash flows (e.g., fixed-for-floating) | Long-term interest rate/currency hedging |

---

## 1.7 The Four Common Hedging Strategies (Module 4 Anchor Topics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Four Strategies — They Are KTU Board Exam Favorites:**

### Strategy 1: **Long Hedge**
- **Scenario:** You need to buy an asset in the future (e.g., a flour mill needing wheat).
- **Action:** Buy futures today.
- **Logic:** If spot price rises, the gain on futures offsets the higher purchase cost.

### Strategy 2: **Short Hedge**
- **Scenario:** You currently own an asset (e.g., a stock portfolio, or commodity inventory).
- **Action:** Sell futures today.
- **Logic:** If spot price falls, the gain on short futures offsets the loss on the asset.

### Strategy 3: **Cross Hedge**
- **Scenario:** The asset you want to hedge has no identical futures contract.
- **Action:** Use a futures contract on a **correlated but not identical** asset (e.g., hedging jet fuel with crude oil futures).
- **Logic:** Relies on the correlation coefficient $\rho$ between the two price series.

### Strategy 4: **Hedging with Options**
- **Scenario:** You want downside protection but want to retain upside potential.
- **Action:** Buy a **put option** (right to sell at strike price).
- **Logic:** Maximum loss = premium paid; unlimited upside retained.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics of a Perfect Hedge

A **perfect hedge** is one in which the loss on the underlying asset is **exactly offset** by the gain on the hedging instrument, leaving the hedger with a riskless position.

Let us formalize this for a **short futures hedge** (the most common corporate scenario):

> **Setup:** A hedger owns an asset currently priced at $S_0$ and expects to sell it at time $T$.
> **Action:** Sells $N$ futures contracts at price $F_0$.
> **At time $T$:** Asset is worth $S_T$, futures settles at $F_T$.

The **total payoff** to the hedger at time $T$ is:

$$ \Pi_T = S_T + N \cdot (F_0 - F_T) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** If $S_T$ falls (loss on asset), then $F_T$ also tends to fall, making $(F_0 - F_T)$ positive (gain on futures). The two effects cancel if the hedge ratio is set correctly.

---

## 2.2 The Optimal Hedge Ratio ($h^\star$) — The Core Derivation

The **optimal hedge ratio** is the proportion of the exposure that should be hedged to **minimize the variance** of the hedged portfolio.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is one of the **most frequently tested 14-mark derivations** in KTU ESE for Module 4. Master it step-by-step.

### Setup

Let:
- $\Delta S$ = change in spot price of the asset being hedged
- $\Delta F$ = change in futures price of the hedging instrument
- $h$ = hedge ratio (number of futures contracts per unit of spot exposure)

The **change in value of the hedged portfolio** is:

$$ \Delta V = \Delta S - h \cdot \Delta F $$

(The hedger loses on the spot if $S$ falls but gains on the short futures position.)

### Variance Minimization

We wish to find $h$ that minimizes $\text{Var}(\Delta V)$:

$$ \text{Var}(\Delta V) = \text{Var}(\Delta S) + h^2 \cdot \text{Var}(\Delta F) - 2h \cdot \text{Cov}(\Delta S, \Delta F) $$

Taking the derivative with respect to $h$ and setting it to zero:

$$ \frac{\partial \, \text{Var}(\Delta V)}{\partial h} = 2h \cdot \text{Var}(\Delta F) - 2 \cdot \text{Cov}(\Delta S, \Delta F) = 0 $$

Solving for $h$:

$$ h^\star = \frac{\text{Cov}(\Delta S, \Delta F)}{\text{Var}(\Delta F)} $$

Using the correlation coefficient $\rho_{SF}$:

$$ h^\star = \rho_{SF} \cdot \frac{\sigma_S}{\sigma_F} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $h^\star = \rho_{SF} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_S}{\sigma_F}$
>
> - If $\rho_{SF} = 1$ (perfect correlation) and $\sigma_S = \sigma_F$ (equal volatility), then $h^\star = 1$ → **perfect hedge**.
> - If $\rho_{SF} < 1$ or $\sigma_S \neq \sigma_F$, the hedge is **imperfect** and basis risk remains.

---

## 2.3 Hedge Effectiveness ($HE$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Hedge effectiveness measures the proportion of the variance of the unhedged position that has been eliminated by the hedge.

$$ HE = 1 - \frac{\text{Var}(\Delta V_{\text{hedged}})}{\text{Var}(\Delta S)} $$

Substituting the optimal hedge ratio:

$$ HE^\star = \rho_{SF}^2 $$

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** The **squared correlation coefficient** $\rho_{SF}^2$ is the maximum achievable hedge effectiveness. A correlation of $0.9$ yields $HE^\star = 0.81$ (81% of risk eliminated).

---

## 2.4 Number of Futures Contracts ($N^\star$)

To translate the optimal hedge ratio into actual contracts, we use:

$$ N^\star = h^\star \cdot \frac{Q_A}{Q_F} $$

Where:
- $Q_A$ = size of position being hedged (units of the asset)
- $Q_F$ = size of **one** futures contract

For equity portfolio hedging using index futures:

$$ N^\star = h^\star \cdot \frac{\beta_{\text{portfolio}} \cdot V_{\text{portfolio}}}{V_{\text{futures}}} $$

Where:
- $\beta_{\text{portfolio}}$ = beta of the portfolio relative to the index
- $V_{\text{portfolio}}$ = market value of the portfolio
- $V_{\text{futures}}$ = value of one futures contract = $\text{Index Level} \times \text{Multiplier}$

---

## 2.5 Cost of Carry Model for Futures Pricing

The theoretical relationship between spot and futures prices is:

$$ F_0 = S_0 \cdot e^{(r + u - y)(T - t)} $$

For assets with no income (e.g., commodities in storage with cost $u$, no yield $y$):

$$ F_0 = S_0 \cdot e^{r(T-t)} \quad \text{(No income, no storage cost case)} $$

For equity indices with continuous dividend yield $q$:

$$ F_0 = S_0 \cdot e^{(r - q)(T-t)} $$

For currencies with foreign interest rate $r_f$:

$$ F_0 = S_0 \cdot e^{(r - r_f)(T-t)} $$

> [!NOTE]
> Where $r$ is the domestic risk-free rate, $u$ is the storage cost (as a percentage), $y$ is the convenience yield, and $q$ is the dividend yield. For currencies, $r_f$ is the foreign risk-free rate.

---

## 2.6 Basis Risk — The Residual Risk

> [!WARNING]
> **Basis risk is what makes a hedge "imperfect."** It arises whenever the hedge ratio is not exactly 1 or the assets are not perfectly correlated.

$$ \text{Basis at time } t: \quad b_t = S_t - F_t $$

**Change in basis:**

$$ \Delta b = \Delta S - \Delta F $$

- If $b$ is **constant** over the hedging horizon → perfect hedge.
- If $b$ **fluctuates randomly** → imperfect hedge; basis risk exists.

**Final value of hedged position (short hedge):**

$$ \Pi_T = S_T + (F_0 - F_T) = F_0 + (S_T - F_T) = F_0 + b_T $$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** For a perfect hedge, the hedger locks in the **initial futures price** $F_0$ plus the **final basis** $b_T$. Uncertainty arises only because $b_T$ is random.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet — Complete Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Print this table. It covers **every formula** the KTU examiner can ask in Module 4 — Hedging.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Application |
|---|-------------------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Basis** | $b_t = S_t - F_t$ | Tracks hedging residual |
| 2 | **Hedged Position Payoff (Short)** | $\Pi_T = S_T + N(F_0 - F_T)$ | Final wealth with hedge |
| 3 | **Hedged Position Payoff (Long)** | $\Pi_T = -S_T + N(F_T - F_0)$ | Buyer hedging future purchase |
| 4 | **Optimal Hedge Ratio** | $h^\star = \rho_{SF} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_S}{\sigma_F}$ | Minimizes portfolio variance |
| 5 | **Hedge Ratio (Covariance form)** | $h^\star = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(\Delta S, \Delta F)}{\text{Var}(\Delta F)}$ | Direct estimation form |
| 6 | **Number of Contracts** | $N^\star = h^\star \cdot \dfrac{Q_A}{Q_F}$ | Translates ratio into contracts |
| 7 | **Portfolio Hedge Contracts** | $N^\star = \dfrac{\beta_P \cdot V_P}{V_F}$ | Index futures for equity portfolio |
| 8 | **Hedge Effectiveness** | $HE = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Var}(\text{hedged})}{\text{Var}(\text{unhedged})} = \rho_{SF}^2$ | Quality of the hedge |
| 9 | **Cost of Carry (General)** | $F_0 = S_0 \cdot e^{(r+u-y)(T-t)}$ | Theoretical futures price |
| 10 | **Cost of Carry (No income/cost)** | $F_0 = S_0 \cdot e^{r(T-t)}$ | Commodities, currencies |
| 11 | **Cost of Carry (Index)** | $F_0 = S_0 \cdot e^{(r-q)(T-t)}$ | Equity index futures |
| 12 | **Cost of Carry (Currency)** | $F_0 = S_0 \cdot e^{(r-r_f)(T-t)}$ | FX forwards/futures |
| 13 | **Cost of Carry (Discrete, simplified)** | $F_0 = S_0 (1 + r)^{T-t}$ | Short-dated approximations |
| 14 | **Lock-in Price (Short Hedge)** | $K_{\text{lock}} = F_0 + \mathbb{E}[b_T]$ | Expected effective selling price |
| 15 | **Lock-in Price (Long Hedge)** | $K_{\text{lock}} = F_0 - \mathbb{E}[b_T]$ | Expected effective purchase price |
| 16 | **Put-Call Parity (no dividend)** | $C + K e^{-rT} = P + S_0$ | Validates option hedges |
| 17 | **Covered Interest Parity** | $F = S \cdot \dfrac{(1+r)^T}{(1+r_f)^T}$ | Currency forward pricing |
| 18 | **Protective Put Payoff** | $\Pi_T = S_T + \max(K - S_T, 0) - P_0$ | Floor with upside retention |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Units of measure — $S$, $F$, $V$ are in **currency units (₹, $, etc.)**; $\sigma$ is in **percentage returns**; $h^\star$ and $N^\star$ are **dimensionless** (contracts). The optimal hedge ratio $h^\star$ is between 0 and 1 for typical hedges.

## 2.8 Engineering / Real-World Utility of Hedging Models

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters beyond the exam hall:** The variance-minimization framework for hedging is structurally identical to **signal-processing filter design** in electrical engineering, where $h^\star$ is the optimal filter coefficient that minimizes output noise power given correlated input and reference signals. Airline CFOs using fuel hedging apply the same logic that electrical engineers use for active noise cancellation.

| Engineering Domain | Hedging Analogue |
|--------------------|-------------------|
| **Noise cancellation (DSP)** | Optimal hedge ratio as Wiener filter coefficient |
| **Structural engineering** | Counterweights absorb vibration (basis risk absorption) |
| **Control systems** | Feedback loop stabilizes output (delta-hedging stabilizes option delta) |
| **Thermodynamics** | Insulator reduces heat transfer variance (hedge reduces return variance) |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation #1: Optimal Hedge Ratio ($h^\star$)

**Problem Statement:** Given that the change in the spot price $\Delta S$ and futures price $\Delta F$ are jointly distributed random variables with means $\mu_S$, $\mu_F$ and variances $\sigma_S^2$, $\sigma_F^2$, derive the hedge ratio that minimizes the variance of a hedged position.

**Step 1 — Define the hedged portfolio payoff:**

The hedger holds $\Delta S$ (the spot position) and shorts $h$ units of futures.

$$ \Delta V = \Delta S - h \cdot \Delta F $$

> Here, $\Delta S$ is the gain/loss on the underlying asset, and $-h \cdot \Delta F$ is the gain/loss on the short futures position (profit when futures price falls).

**Step 2 — Compute the variance of the hedged portfolio:**

$$ \text{Var}(\Delta V) = \text{Var}(\Delta S - h \Delta F) $$

Applying the variance operator:

$$ \text{Var}(\Delta V) = \text{Var}(\Delta S) + h^2 \cdot \text{Var}(\Delta F) - 2h \cdot \text{Cov}(\Delta S, \Delta F) $$

> **Reasoning:** $\text{Var}(A - B) = \text{Var}(A) + \text{Var}(B) - 2\,\text{Cov}(A,B)$. Note the minus sign from squaring the negative coefficient of $h$.

**Step 3 — Take the first derivative with respect to $h$:**

$$ \frac{d\, \text{Var}(\Delta V)}{dh} = 2h \cdot \text{Var}(\Delta F) - 2 \cdot \text{Cov}(\Delta S, \Delta F) $$

> **Reasoning:** $\dfrac{d}{dh}[h^2 \cdot \text{Var}(\Delta F)] = 2h \cdot \text{Var}(\Delta F)$, and $\dfrac{d}{dh}[-2h \cdot \text{Cov}(\Delta S, \Delta F)] = -2 \cdot \text{Cov}(\Delta S, \Delta F)$.

**Step 4 — Set the derivative equal to zero (first-order condition):**

$$ 2h \cdot \text{Var}(\Delta F) - 2 \cdot \text{Cov}(\Delta S, \Delta F) = 0 $$

**Step 5 — Solve for $h$:**

$$ 2h \cdot \text{Var}(\Delta F) = 2 \cdot \text{Cov}(\Delta S, \Delta F) $$

$$ h^\star = \frac{\text{Cov}(\Delta S, \Delta F)}{\text{Var}(\Delta F)} $$

**Step 6 — Express in correlation form:**

Recall the identity $\text{Cov}(\Delta S, \Delta F) = \rho_{SF} \cdot \sigma_S \cdot \sigma_F$ and $\text{Var}(\Delta F) = \sigma_F^2$:

$$ h^\star = \frac{\rho_{SF} \cdot \sigma_S \cdot \sigma_F}{\sigma_F^2} $$

$$ \boxed{h^\star = \rho_{SF} \cdot \frac{\sigma_S}{\sigma_F}} $$

**Step 7 — Verify second-order condition (minimum):**

$$ \frac{d^2\, \text{Var}(\Delta V)}{dh^2} = 2 \cdot \text{Var}(\Delta F) > 0 \quad \checkmark $$

Since variance is always positive, the critical point is indeed a minimum.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation #2: Hedged Portfolio Variance

Substituting $h^\star$ back into the variance equation:

$$ \text{Var}(\Delta V^\star) = \text{Var}(\Delta S) + (h^\star)^2 \cdot \text{Var}(\Delta F) - 2 h^\star \cdot \text{Cov}(\Delta S, \Delta F) $$

**Step 1 — Substitute $h^\star$:**

$$ \text{Var}(\Delta V^\star) = \sigma_S^2 + \left[\frac{\text{Cov}(\Delta S, \Delta F)}{\sigma_F^2}\right]^2 \cdot \sigma_F^2 - 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Cov}(\Delta S, \Delta F)}{\sigma_F^2} \cdot \text{Cov}(\Delta S, \Delta F) $$

**Step 2 — Simplify term by term:**

$$ \text{Var}(\Delta V^\star) = \sigma_S^2 + \frac{[\text{Cov}(\Delta S, \Delta F)]^2}{\sigma_F^2} - \frac{2 \cdot [\text{Cov}(\Delta S, \Delta F)]^2}{\sigma_F^2} $$

$$ \text{Var}(\Delta V^\star) = \sigma_S^2 - \frac{[\text{Cov}(\Delta S, \Delta F)]^2}{\sigma_F^2} $$

**Step 3 — Express in correlation form:**

$$ \text{Var}(\Delta V^\star) = \sigma_S^2 - \frac{(\rho_{SF} \cdot \sigma_S \cdot \sigma_F)^2}{\sigma_F^2} $$

$$ \text{Var}(\Delta V^\star) = \sigma_S^2 - \rho_{SF}^2 \cdot \sigma_S^2 $$

$$ \boxed{\text{Var}(\Delta V^\star) = \sigma_S^2 (1 - \rho_{SF}^2)} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is the **conditional variance** of $S$ given $F$ — the portion of spot variance not "explained" by futures. Hedging cannot eliminate the orthogonal noise component.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation #3: Hedged Portfolio Mean Return

The expected return of the hedged portfolio is:

**Step 1 — Compute expected change in hedged position:**

$$ \mathbb{E}[\Delta V] = \mathbb{E}[\Delta S] - h^\star \cdot \mathbb{E}[\Delta F] $$

**Step 2 — Substitute the optimal hedge ratio:**

$$ \mathbb{E}[\Delta V^\star] = \mu_S - \frac{\text{Cov}(\Delta S, \Delta F)}{\sigma_F^2} \cdot \mu_F $$

**Step 3 — For unbiased forward/futures markets**, $\mu_F \approx \mu_S$ (no risk premium), and for $\rho \approx 1$:

$$ \mathbb{E}[\Delta V^\star] \approx \mu_S - \frac{\sigma_S \cdot \mu_S}{\sigma_F} \cdot \rho_{SF} \approx \mu_S (1 - \rho_{SF}) \approx 0 $$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** Under efficient market assumptions, optimal hedging reduces expected return close to zero — you trade away expected upside for risk reduction. This is the **risk-return tradeoff in action**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example #1: Commodity Hedging

**Problem:** A tea plantation company expects to harvest **10,000 kg** of tea in **3 months**. The current spot price is **₹200/kg**. Tea futures (3-month contract) trade at **₹210/kg**. Each futures contract covers **1,000 kg**. The standard deviation of spot price changes is $\sigma_S = 0.10$ and for futures is $\sigma_F = 0.12$. The correlation coefficient is $\rho = 0.85$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the hedge type:** Short hedge (hedger owns the commodity, fears price decline).

**Step 2 — Compute the optimal hedge ratio:**

$$ h^\star = \rho_{SF} \cdot \frac{\sigma_S}{\sigma_F} = 0.85 \times \frac{0.10}{0.12} = 0.7083 $$

**Step 3 — Determine the total quantity to hedge:**

$$ Q_{\text{hedge}} = h^\star \times Q_A = 0.7083 \times 10{,}000 = 7{,}083 \text{ kg} $$

**Step 4 — Convert to number of futures contracts:**

$$ N^\star = \frac{Q_{\text{hedge}}}{Q_F} = \frac{7{,}083}{1{,}000} = 7.08 \approx 7 \text{ contracts} $$

**Step 5 — Compute hedge effectiveness:**

$$ HE = \rho_{SF}^2 = (0.85)^2 = 0.7225 = 72.25\% $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** With 7 short futures contracts, the company eliminates approximately **72.25% of the spot price variance**. About 27.75% of the price risk remains due to imperfect correlation (basis risk).

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example #2: Equity Portfolio Hedging with Index Futures

**Problem:** A mutual fund holds an equity portfolio worth **₹5 crore** with a beta of **1.25** relative to the Nifty 50. The Nifty 50 spot index is at **20,000**, and the 1-month Nifty futures trade at **20,400**. The futures contract multiplier is **25**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the value of one futures contract:**

$$ V_F = \text{Index} \times \text{Multiplier} = 20{,}000 \times 25 = \text{₹}5{,}00{,}000 $$

**Step 2 — Compute the optimal number of contracts using the beta-adjusted formula:**

$$ N^\star = \frac{\beta_P \cdot V_P}{V_F} = \frac{1.25 \times 5{,}00{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} $$

**Step 3 — Solve:**

$$ N^\star = \frac{6{,}25{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = 125 \text{ contracts (short)} $$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

Selling 125 Nifty futures contracts protects the portfolio against a market downturn. If Nifty falls by 10%, the futures gain offsets a corresponding portion of the portfolio's loss.

**Step 5 — Locked-in portfolio value approximation:**

$$ V_{\text{locked}} \approx V_P + N^\star \cdot (F_0 - F_T) = V_P + 125 \cdot 25 \cdot (20{,}400 - F_T) $$

$$ V_{\text{locked}} \approx \text{₹}5 \text{ crore} + 3{,}125 \cdot (20{,}400 - F_T) $$

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example #3: Currency Hedging (Forward Cover)

**Problem:** An Indian IT exporter will receive a payment of **$1,00,000** in **6 months**. The current spot exchange rate is **₹83/\$**. The 6-month forward rate is **₹84.50/\$**. The Indian 6-month risk-free rate is **6% p.a.** and the US 6-month rate is **5% p.a.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the locked-in INR value using forward contract:**

$$ \text{INR received} = \$1{,}00{,}000 \times \text{₹}84.50/\$ = \text{₹}84{,}50{,}000 $$

**Step 2 — Verify using cost of carry formula:**

$$ F = S \cdot e^{(r - r_f)(T-t)} = 83 \cdot e^{(0.06 - 0.05) \times 0.5} $$

$$ F = 83 \cdot e^{0.005} \approx 83 \times 1.00501 \approx \text{₹}83.42/\$ $$

> [!NOTE]
> The forward rate of ₹84.50 in the market is higher than the theoretical ₹83.42, suggesting the forward is trading at a **premium to interest rate parity**. The exporter benefits by locking in the higher forward.

**Step 3 — Hedge effectiveness:** Since forwards are firm obligations, the hedge is **perfect** (no basis risk) if the exporter delivers exactly the dollar amount on the agreed date.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Selecting the Right Hedge Instrument

| Hedger Profile | Best Instrument | Rationale |
|----------------|-----------------|-----------|
| Wheat farmer (sell commodity) | Short futures | Standardized, liquid, low cost |
| Airline (buy jet fuel) | Long futures or swap | Long-term exposure needs swap |
| IT exporter (receive USD) | Forward contract | Customizable amount and date |
| Equity fund manager | Index futures | High correlation, deep liquidity |
| Company worried about stock crash, wants to keep upside | Protective put | Asymmetric payoff |
| Company wants to lock in borrowing cost | Interest rate swap | Convert floating to fixed |
| MNC with multi-currency exposure | Currency swap | Long-dated multi-period hedge |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram #1: Hedging Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hedger has market exposure] --> B{What is the direction of risk?}
    B -- Risk of price RISE --> C[Need LONG hedge]
    B -- Risk of price FALL --> D[Need SHORT hedge]
    C --> E{Is there a futures market for the exact asset?}
    D --> F{Is there a futures market for the exact asset?}
    E -- YES --> G[Buy futures directly]
    E -- NO --> H[Use CROSS HEDGE with correlated asset]
    F -- YES --> I[Sell futures directly]
    F -- NO --> J[Use CROSS HEDGE with correlated asset]
    G --> K{Do you want to retain upside?}
    I --> L{Do you want to retain upside?}
    K -- YES --> M[Buy CALL option protective hedge]
    K -- NO --> N[Futures sufficient]
    L -- YES --> O[Buy PUT option protective hedge]
    L -- NO --> P[Futures sufficient]
    H --> Q[Compute optimal hedge ratio h star]
    J --> Q
    Q --> R[Apply formula N star equals h star times QA divided by QF]
    R --> S[Execute hedge and monitor basis]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram #2: Anatomy of a Short Hedge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T0[Time Zero: Today]
        S0[Spot Price S0]
        F0[Futures Price F0]
        S0 --> BASIS[Initial Basis b0 equals S0 minus F0]
        F0 --> BASIS
    end
    subgraph T1[Time T: Hedge Expiry]
        ST[Spot Price ST]
        FT[Futures Price FT]
        ST --> FBASIS[Final Basis bT equals ST minus FT]
        FT --> FBASIS
    end
    BASIS -.-> FBASIS
    F0 -.->|Locked Price for Hedger| PROFIT[Hedger locks in approximately F0 plus bT]
    FBASIS -.-> PROFIT
    PROFIT --> RESULT{Risk Reduced?}
    RESULT -- YES --> SUCCESS[Hedge Successful]
    RESULT -- NO --> REVIEW[Re-evaluate correlation and hedge ratio]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram #3: Hedging Instruments Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Hedging Instruments] --> P1[Forwards]
    R --> P2[Futures]
    R --> P3[Options]
    R --> P4[Swaps]
    P1 --> C1[OTC Customized Counterparty Risk]
    P2 --> C2[Exchange Traded Standardized Mark to Market]
    P3 --> C3[Right but not Obligation Premium Cost]
    P4 --> C4[Exchange of Cash Flows Long Tenor]
    C1 --> U1[Use for Tailored Corporate Deals]
    C2 --> U2[Use for Liquid Hedging of Equities and Commodities]
    C3 --> U3[Use for Asymmetric Protection Retain Upside]
    C4 --> U4[Use for Long Term Interest and Currency Exposures]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram #4: Optimal Hedge Ratio Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Collect historical price data of asset and futures] --> B[Compute daily changes Delta S and Delta F]
    B --> C[Calculate mean and standard deviations]
    C --> D[Calculate correlation coefficient rho SF]
    D --> E[Compute covariance matrix]
    E --> F[Apply formula h star equals rho SF times sigma S over sigma F]
    F --> G[Verify using covariance form]
    G --> H[Compute number of contracts N star]
    H --> I[Compute hedge effectiveness HE equals rho squared]
    I --> J[Execute hedge and monitor basis risk weekly]
```

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram #5: Risk Reduction Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EXPOSURE[Original Exposure] -->|Identified| CHOICE[Select Hedge Type]
    CHOICE -->|Long or Short| INSTRUMENT[Choose Instrument]
    INSTRUMENT -->|Futures Options Swap Forward| EXECUTE[Execute Hedge]
    EXECUTE -->|Monitor Daily| BASISM[Track Basis Movements]
    BASISM -->|High Variance| REBALANCE[Adjust Hedge Ratio]
    REBALANCE -->|Updated N star| EXECUTE
    BASISM -->|Stable Low Variance| MAINTAIN[Maintain Current Hedge]
    MAINTAIN -->|Until Maturity| SETTLE[Settle at Expiry]
    SETTLE -->|Compare to Unhedged| EVALUATE[Evaluate Performance]
    EVALUATE --> EXPOSURE
```

---
