# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define hedging. Distinguish between hedging and speculation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Hedging** is a risk management strategy in which an investor or business takes an offsetting position in a derivative instrument to protect an existing exposure against adverse price movements. The objective is to **reduce or transfer risk**, not to earn profit.
>
> | Aspect | Hedging | Speculation |
> |--------|---------|-------------|
> | **Objective** | Risk reduction | Profit from price movement |
> | **Underlying exposure** | Exists | None (pure directional bet) |
> | **Risk profile** | Lowers risk | Increases risk (often leveraged) |
> | **Net position** | Offsetting | Directional |
> | **Example** | Farmer selling wheat futures | Trader buying wheat futures hoping for a rise |
>
> *(Approx. word count suitable for 3 marks: 120–150 words with one comparison table.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the concept of basis and basis risk in hedging.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Basis** is defined as the difference between the spot price and the futures price of an asset at any given point in time:
>
> $$ b_t = S_t - F_t $$
>
> **Basis risk** is the risk that the basis $b_t$ will change unpredictably between the time the hedge is initiated and the time it is lifted. If the basis were constant, the hedger could lock in the futures price $F_0$ exactly. However, since $b_T$ is random, the final hedged position value $F_0 + b_T$ fluctuates.
>
> Basis risk arises primarily in **cross hedging** situations, where the asset being hedged is not identical to the underlying of the futures contract used. Higher basis volatility $\sigma_b$ → lower hedge effectiveness.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has **internal choice** between two options (A or B). Each option has sub-parts typically worth **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Adapted]`

**A.** *(a)* Explain the different types of hedging strategies with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**A.** *(b)* An exporter expects to receive a payment of €50,000 in 4 months. The spot rate is ₹90/€, and the 4-month forward rate is ₹91.20/€. The exporter is worried about INR appreciation against the euro. Recommend a suitable hedging strategy and compute the locked-in INR revenue. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to A(a): Types of Hedging Strategies (7 marks)**

The four principal hedging strategies are:

**1. Long Hedge (7-Mark Content):**
- **Definition:** Taking a long position in futures to protect against a future price increase when the hedger plans to **buy** an asset in the future.
- **Example:** A bakery knows it will need 10 tonnes of wheat in 3 months. To protect against wheat price rise, it buys wheat futures today. If wheat price rises, the gain on long futures offsets the higher spot purchase cost.
- **Payoff:** $\Pi_T = -S_T + N(F_T - F_0) = F_0 + (\text{basis variation})$.

**2. Short Hedge (7-Mark Content):**
- **Definition:** Taking a short position in futures to protect against a future price decline when the hedger **already owns** or **will produce** the asset.
- **Example:** A farmer with 5,000 kg of pepper in storage fears a price drop before sale. He sells pepper futures. If prices fall, the gain on short futures offsets the spot price loss.
- **Payoff:** $\Pi_T = S_T + N(F_0 - F_T)$.

**3. Cross Hedge (7-Mark Content):**
- **Definition:** Hedging an asset using futures on a **different but correlated** asset when no direct futures contract exists.
- **Example:** An airline hedging jet fuel prices using crude oil futures (since no jet fuel futures exist). Effectiveness depends on the correlation coefficient.
- **Formula:** $h^\star = \rho_{SF} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_S}{\sigma_F}$, where $\rho_{SF}$ is the correlation between jet fuel and crude oil prices.

**4. Hedging with Options (Protective Put / Call):**
- **Definition:** Buying a put option (to protect against price decline while retaining upside) or a call option (to protect against price rise).
- **Example:** An equity investor holding ₹10 lakh of stocks buys Nifty 20,000 strike puts to insure against a crash.
- **Maximum loss:** Limited to the **premium paid**.

> Each strategy worth approximately **1.75 marks** with definition + example + formula/payoff.

---

#### **Model Solution to A(b): Currency Hedging of Exporter Receivable (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify the exposure:** The exporter will **receive €50,000 in 4 months**. If the euro depreciates against the rupee (i.e., ₹/€ rate falls), the INR realization decreases.

> **Wait — the problem says the exporter is worried about INR appreciation. INR appreciation means € becomes cheaper in rupee terms.** So yes, the exporter faces a downside risk.

**Step 2 — Select the strategy:** The exporter should **sell € forward** (enter a 4-month EUR/INR forward sale contract) to lock in the current forward rate.

**Step 3 — Compute the locked-in INR revenue:**

$$ \text{INR Revenue}_{\text{locked}} = €50{,}000 \times \text{₹}91.20/\text{€} = ₹45{,}60{,}000 $$

**Step 4 — Compare with unhedged scenario (sensitivity analysis):**

| Scenario | Spot Rate at Maturity | INR Realization |
|----------|----------------------|-----------------|
| Euro appreciates to ₹92.50 | ₹92.50 | ₹46,25,000 (unhedged gain) |
| Euro depreciates to ₹88.00 | ₹88.00 | ₹44,00,000 (unhedged loss) |
| Hedged (forward) | ₹91.20 (locked) | **₹45,60,000 (certain)** |

**Step 5 — Justify the hedge:**

The exporter sacrifices the upside of € appreciation (₹46,25,000) but eliminates the downside risk (₹44,00,000). Given the **certainty equivalent** principle and the exporter's likely need for predictable cash flows, the hedge is recommended.

**Step 6 — Alternative using options:**
The exporter could instead **buy a put option on EUR/INR** (right to sell € at ₹91.20). This retains upside if € appreciates beyond ₹91.20 but costs the option premium.

> **Final recommendation:** Forward sale for certainty; protective put if exporter wants to retain upside.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Adapted]`

**B.** *(a)* Derive the optimal hedge ratio that minimizes the variance of a hedged portfolio. Explain all the terms used. *(7 marks)*

**B.** *(b)* A portfolio manager holds a portfolio worth ₹8 crore with a beta of 1.15 relative to the BSE Sensex. The Sensex spot is at 72,000 and the 2-month futures trade at 73,200. The futures multiplier is ₹50. Compute the number of futures contracts required to fully hedge the portfolio. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to B(a): Derivation of Optimal Hedge Ratio (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Define the hedged portfolio value change:**

Let $\Delta S$ be the change in spot price and $\Delta F$ the change in futures price. With hedge ratio $h$:

$$ \Delta V = \Delta S - h \cdot \Delta F $$

**Step 2 — Variance of the hedged position:**

$$ \text{Var}(\Delta V) = \sigma_S^2 + h^2 \sigma_F^2 - 2h \cdot \rho_{SF} \sigma_S \sigma_F $$

**Step 3 — First-order condition:**

$$ \frac{d \, \text{Var}(\Delta V)}{dh} = 2h \sigma_F^2 - 2 \rho_{SF} \sigma_S \sigma_F = 0 $$

**Step 4 — Solve:**

$$ h^\star = \frac{\rho_{SF} \sigma_S \sigma_F}{\sigma_F^2} = \rho_{SF} \cdot \frac{\sigma_S}{\sigma_F} $$

**Step 5 — Alternative covariance form:**

$$ h^\star = \frac{\text{Cov}(\Delta S, \Delta F)}{\text{Var}(\Delta F)} $$

**Step 6 — Interpretation of terms:**

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| $\rho_{SF}$ | Correlation coefficient between spot and futures price changes |
| $\sigma_S$ | Standard deviation of spot price changes |
| $\sigma_F$ | Standard deviation of futures price changes |
| $\text{Cov}(\Delta S, \Delta F)$ | Joint movement of spot and futures |
| $\text{Var}(\Delta F)$ | Dispersion of futures price changes |

> **Step 7 — Special case:** If $\rho_{SF} = 1$ and $\sigma_S = \sigma_F$, then $h^\star = 1$ → perfect hedge with 1:1 futures-to-spot coverage.

---

#### **Model Solution to B(b): Equity Portfolio Hedging (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Portfolio value $V_P$ | ₹8,00,00,000 |
| Portfolio beta $\beta_P$ | 1.15 |
| Spot index level | 72,000 |
| Futures price $F_0$ | 73,200 |
| Multiplier | 50 |

**Step 2 — Compute value of one futures contract:**

$$ V_F = \text{Index} \times \text{Multiplier} = 72{,}000 \times 50 = ₹36{,}00{,}000 $$

**Step 3 — Apply beta-adjusted hedging formula:**

$$ N^\star = \frac{\beta_P \cdot V_P}{V_F} $$

$$ N^\star = \frac{1.15 \times 8{,}00{,}00{,}000}{36{,}00{,}000} $$

**Step 4 — Solve:**

$$ N^\star = \frac{9{,}20{,}00{,}000}{36{,}00{,}000} = 255.56 \approx 256 \text{ contracts} $$

> The manager should **sell (short) 256 BSE Sensex futures contracts** to hedge the portfolio against market decline.

**Step 5 — Verification and explanation:**

- **Direction:** Short futures (since we own the portfolio and fear a market fall).
- **Why beta-adjust?** The portfolio's beta of 1.15 means it is 15% more volatile than the index. Therefore, we need a larger futures position than a 1:1 hedge would suggest.
- **Why 256 (not 256.56)?** Futures contracts are indivisible; rounding to the nearest whole contract introduces minor tracking error.

**Step 6 — Residual risk:** The hedge is imperfect because (a) beta is an estimate, (b) correlation is not 1, and (c) basis risk exists between the portfolio and the index.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks — Read This Carefully:**

1. **Confusing Long and Short Hedge Direction (–2 marks):**
   Many students write "long hedge to protect against falling prices." This is **wrong**. A long hedge protects against **rising** prices (you lock in a purchase price); a short hedge protects against **falling** prices (you lock in a sale price). Use a clear sentence: *"Since the hedger owns the asset and fears a price decline, a SHORT hedge is appropriate."*

2. **Forgetting the Multiplier in Index Futures Contracts (–1 to –2 marks):**
   When hedging with Nifty or Sensex futures, remember that one contract = Index × Multiplier. Forgetting to multiply by the multiplier (25 for Nifty, 50 for Sensex) leads to wildly incorrect contract counts.

3. **Mixing Spot and Futures Formulas (–1 mark):**
   Cost of carry formula $F_0 = S_0 \cdot e^{(r+u-y)T}$ is for futures pricing. It is **not** the hedge payoff formula. Keep these separate.

4. **Not Mentioning Basis Risk (–1 mark):**
   Any hedging answer that does not mention **basis risk** and **imperfect correlation** is incomplete. Examiners expect at least one sentence on the limitations of hedging.

5. **Forgetting the Beta in Portfolio Hedging (–2 marks):**
   Equity portfolio hedging requires the **beta-adjusted** formula $N^\star = \beta_P \cdot V_P / V_F$. Using the unadjusted formula loses 2 marks.

6. **Unit Inconsistency in Volatility (–0.5 to –1 mark):**
   If volatility is given as a percentage (e.g., 10%), convert to decimal (0.10) before applying the formula. Similarly, ensure time periods match (annual vs. daily vs. monthly).

7. **Omitting Hedging Limitations (–1 mark):**
   Always end your answer with a brief note on: (a) basis risk, (b) opportunity cost of giving up upside, (c) margin/cost requirements, and (d) counterparty risk in OTC hedges.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Hedging**

### **Core Definitions**
- ☐ Hedging = offsetting position to reduce risk (NOT to earn profit)
- ☐ Speculation = directional bet for profit (no underlying exposure)
- ☐ Arbitrage = simultaneous buy-sell for riskless profit
- ☐ Basis $b_t = S_t - F_t$ (spot minus futures)
- ☐ Basis risk = uncertainty in basis at hedge maturity

### **Four Hedge Types**
- ☐ **Long hedge:** protect against future price rise (buyer's hedge)
- ☐ **Short hedge:** protect against future price fall (seller's/producer's hedge)
- ☐ **Cross hedge:** use correlated but non-identical instrument
- ☐ **Option-based (protective put/call):** asymmetric hedge, retain upside

### **Critical Formulas to Memorize**
- ☐ $h^\star = \rho_{SF} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_S}{\sigma_F}$ — optimal hedge ratio
- ☐ $h^\star = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(\Delta S, \Delta F)}{\text{Var}(\Delta F)}$ — alternative form
- ☐ $N^\star = h^\star \cdot \dfrac{Q_A}{Q_F}$ — number of contracts
- ☐ $N^\star = \dfrac{\beta_P \cdot V_P}{V_F}$ — for index futures portfolio hedge
- ☐ $HE = \rho_{SF}^2$ — maximum hedge effectiveness
- ☐ $\Pi_T^{\text{short}} = S_T + N(F_0 - F_T)$ — short hedge payoff
- ☐ $\Pi_T^{\text{long}} = -S_T + N(F_T - F_0)$ — long hedge payoff
- ☐ $F_0 = S_0 \cdot e^{(r+u-y)(T-t)}$ — cost of carry

### **Real-World Applications**
- ☐ Farmers hedging crop prices (commodity short hedge)
- ☐ Airlines hedging fuel costs (long hedge with crude oil)
- ☐ IT exporters hedging USD/INR (currency forward sale)
- ☐ Mutual funds hedging equity portfolio (short index futures)
- ☐ Companies hedging interest rates (interest rate swaps)
- ☐ Investors hedging against market crash (protective puts)

### **Common KTU Exam Traps**
- ☐ "Long hedge" = fear of **price rise**, not fall
- ☐ Hedging reduces risk but **eliminates upside**
- ☐ Forwards = OTC (counterparty risk); Futures = exchange (no counterparty risk)
- ☐ Beta must be used for equity portfolio hedging
- ☐ Hedge effectiveness cannot exceed $\rho_{SF}^2$
- ☐ Mention **basis risk** in every hedging answer
- ☐ The futures multiplier differs across contracts (Nifty = 25, Sensex = 50)

### **One-Line Exam Summary**
> *"Hedging minimizes variance, not profit; its effectiveness is bounded above by the squared correlation between the hedged asset and the hedging instrument, and residual basis risk is unavoidable in practice."*

---

**End of Module 4 — Hedging Notes**
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Pattern (ESE)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Speculation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Speculation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Speculation** is the act of engaging in a financial transaction that involves substantial risk of losing capital, in the hope of realizing a substantial gain. It is the practice of buying and selling financial assets (such as stocks, bonds, commodities, currencies, or derivatives) with the primary objective of profiting from short-term price fluctuations rather than holding them for long-term intrinsic value, dividends, or interest income.

A **Speculator** is therefore a market participant who willingly assumes calculated risk by taking a position in an asset, expecting to profit from anticipated changes in its market price, without any intention of using the underlying asset for productive or consumption purposes.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Distinction (KTU Board Standard)**: Speculation differs from investment in the **time horizon** and **risk appetite**. An investor seeks steady, long-term returns; a speculator seeks high, short-term gains and accepts correspondingly high risk. Speculation also differs from gambling because it is based on **systematic analysis, market information, and risk management** rather than pure chance.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a fisherman in coastal Kerala. An ordinary fisherman (the **investor**) goes to sea daily, fishes consistently, and earns a stable income over years — predictable but modest. A **speculator**, on the other hand, is like a trader who studies the tides, weather satellites, and monsoon patterns, sails out precisely when a storm surge is predicted, catches a high-value haul, and returns quickly — earning a fortune on a single trip but also risking his boat.

The fisherman-speculator is not gambling randomly; he uses **data, timing, and calculated risk**. If his forecast is wrong, he loses his boat (capital). This is exactly how a stock speculator operates in the **NSE/BSE markets** — using technical charts, news flows, and macroeconomic signals to predict short-term price direction.

### 1.3 Core Characteristics of Speculation (Bullet-Frame)

- **Time Horizon**: Short-term — typically days, weeks, or a few months. *(Bold: < 12 months in most cases)*
- **Risk Level**: High — the possibility of losing the entire invested capital is real.
- **Primary Motive**: Capital gain from price differentials, **not** income (dividends/interest).
- **Information Dependence**: Heavy reliance on market data, charts, news, and sentiment.
- **Liquidity Requirement**: High — speculators need markets where they can enter and exit positions swiftly.
- **Leverage Use**: Frequently uses borrowed capital (margin trading) to amplify potential returns.
- **Acceptable Loss Tolerance**: Willing to absorb a loss as a "cost of doing business" if the thesis proves wrong.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024)**: Module 4 explicitly tests the difference between **Speculation, Investment, Hedging, and Gambling**. Master the comparison table in Section 2 — it is a guaranteed question topic.

### 1.4 Standard Market Metrics in Speculation

- **Margin**: The portion of the trade value that the speculator must fund with his own capital, expressed as a percentage of the total contract value.
- **Leverage Ratio**: The multiple by which the speculator's exposure exceeds his actual capital outlay.
- **Volatility**: The statistical measure of price dispersion — higher volatility implies higher speculative opportunity (and risk).
- **Bid-Ask Spread**: The cost of immediacy — tighter spreads favor active speculators.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Operational Mechanism of Speculation

A speculator's workflow follows a disciplined sequence:

1. **Market Scanning**: Identify an asset whose price is expected to move significantly in the near future. The basis may be **technical analysis** (chart patterns, moving averages, RSI) or **fundamental analysis** (earnings, macroeconomic data).
2. **Directional Forecast**: Decide on a directional bet — either **price will rise (Bullish)** or **price will fall (Bearish)**.
3. **Position Entry**: Execute the trade by either buying the asset (long position) or selling borrowed asset (short selling).
4. **Position Monitoring**: Continuously track price action, news, and stop-loss triggers.
5. **Position Exit**: Close the trade once the target price is achieved, the stop-loss is hit, or the thesis is invalidated.
6. **Settlement**: Realize the profit or loss and return the asset (in the case of short selling).

### 2.2 Types of Speculators (KTU Board Favourite)

| Speculator Type | Market View | Action | Profit Source |
|-----------------|-------------|--------|---------------|
| **Bull** | Price will rise | Buys now, sells later at a higher price | Price appreciation |
| **Bear** | Price will fall | Sells borrowed asset now, buys back later at a lower price | Price depreciation |
| **Stag** | IPO/Issue underpriced | Applies for new issues intending to sell on listing day | Listing-day premium |
| **Arbitrageur** | Same asset, different prices | Simultaneous buy-cheap / sell-dear across markets | Price differential |
| **Scalper** | Tiny price moves | Multiple intraday trades, holding for minutes | Bid-ask spread capture |

### 2.3 Speculation vs Investment vs Hedging vs Gambling (Master Comparison Table)

> [!NOTE]  
> This table is the single most important comparative chart for KTU Module 4. Memorize it.

| Parameter | Speculation | Investment | Hedging | Gambling |
|-----------|-------------|------------|---------|----------|
| **Time Horizon** | Short-term | Long-term | Variable | Instant |
| **Primary Motive** | Capital gain from price change | Wealth creation, income | Risk reduction | Entertainment/chance |
| **Risk Level** | High | Moderate | Low (transfers risk) | Pure chance |
| **Information Use** | Heavy | Heavy | Heavy | None / minimal |
| **Underlying Asset Use** | Never used | Used productively | Owned or related | Notional |
| **Knowledge Required** | Moderate-High | High | High | Low |
| **Returns Profile** | Asymmetric (high win or high loss) | Steady compounding | Offsetting losses | Zero-sum by design |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> Although Speculation is qualitative, KTU frequently tests **profit/loss arithmetic**. Memorize the four formulas below.

| Formula Name | Equation | Variables Explained | Real-World Use |
|--------------|----------|---------------------|----------------|
| **Long Position P/L** | $P/L = (S - B) \times N$ | $S$ = Selling price, $B$ = Buying price, $N$ = Number of units | Equity bull speculation |
| **Short Position P/L** | $P/L = (S_{\text{init}} - S_{\text{exit}}) \times N$ | $S_{\text{init}}$ = Initial short-sale price, $S_{\text{exit}}$ = Buy-back price | Equity bear speculation |
| **Leverage Ratio** | $L = \dfrac{V_{\text{exposure}}}{V_{\text{equity}}}$ | $V_{\text{exposure}}$ = Total position value, $V_{\text{equity}}$ = Own capital | Margin trading, F&O |
| **Return on Speculation (%)** | $R\% = \dfrac{P/L}{M} \times 100$ | $P/L$ = Profit or Loss, $M$ = Margin paid | Performance benchmarking |
| **Margin Requirement** | $M = V_{\text{exposure}} \times m\%$ | $m\%$ = Margin % prescribed by exchange/broker | F&O trading |
| **Payoff Diagram (Long Call)** | $\text{Payoff} = \max(S_T - K,\, 0) - C$ | $S_T$ = Spot at expiry, $K$ = Strike, $C$ = Premium | Options speculation |

> [!WARNING]  
> **Critical Board-Exam Tip**: When asked to differentiate Speculation from Gambling, ALWAYS mention that **speculation involves economic purpose and risk analysis**, while gambling creates no economic value and is purely redistributive. Examiners award full marks only when this distinction is explicit.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of Speculation in Financial Markets

- **Price Discovery**: Aggregated speculative activity helps markets arrive at the "correct" price for an asset.
- **Market Liquidity**: Speculators add volume to the order book, reducing bid-ask spreads for all participants.
- **Risk Transfer**: Speculators absorb risks that producers, manufacturers, or investors wish to offload.
- **Capital Formation**: Healthy speculation channels savings into productive enterprises via secondary-market activity.
- **Information Efficiency**: Speculators rapidly incorporate new information into prices, supporting the **Efficient Market Hypothesis (EMH)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Long Speculation in Stocks

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
Mr. Rahul, a speculator, buys 500 shares of Infosys at ₹1,500 per share on 1st March 2024, paying a 40% margin. He sells all 500 shares on 28th March 2024 at ₹1,650 per share. Brokerage is 0.1% on each leg. Calculate:
- (a) Total exposure and margin paid
- (b) Gross profit
- (c) Net profit after brokerage
- (d) Percentage return on margin

**Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the variables.**
Number of shares $N = 500$
Buying price $B = 1500$
Selling price $S = 1650$
Margin rate $m = 40\%$
Brokerage rate $b = 0.1\%$ per transaction leg

**Step 2 — Calculate total exposure (face value of the position).**

$$
V_{\text{exposure}} = B \times N
$$

$$
V_{\text{exposure}} = 1500 \times 500 = 7{,}50{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the margin (own capital deployed).**

$$
M = V_{\text{exposure}} \times m
$$

$$
M = 7{,}50{,}000 \times 0.40 = 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the leverage ratio.**

$$
L = \frac{V_{\text{exposure}}}{V_{\text{equity}}} = \frac{7{,}50{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} = 2.5
$$

So Mr. Rahul is trading with **2.5x leverage**.

**Step 5 — Calculate gross profit (price difference only).**

$$
P_{\text{gross}} = (S - B) \times N
$$

$$
P_{\text{gross}} = (1650 - 1500) \times 500 = 150 \times 500 = 75{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate brokerage on both legs.**

$$
\text{Buy leg brokerage} = B \times N \times b = 1500 \times 500 \times 0.001 = 750 \text{ INR}
$$

$$
\text{Sell leg brokerage} = S \times N \times b = 1650 \times 500 \times 0.001 = 825 \text{ INR}
$$

$$
\text{Total brokerage} = 750 + 825 = 1575 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 7 — Calculate net profit.**

$$
P_{\text{net}} = P_{\text{gross}} - \text{Total brokerage}
$$

$$
P_{\text{net}} = 75{,}000 - 1575 = 73{,}425 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 8 — Calculate return on margin.**

$$
R\% = \frac{P_{\text{net}}}{M} \times 100
$$

$$
R\% = \frac{73{,}425}{3{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 24.475\%
$$

**Final Answer Block:**

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Total Exposure | ₹7,50,000 |
| Margin Paid | ₹3,00,000 |
| Leverage | 2.5x |
| Gross Profit | ₹75,000 |
| Total Brokerage | ₹1,575 |
| Net Profit | ₹73,425 |
| Return on Margin | 24.475% |

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Short Selling (Bear Speculation)

**Problem Statement:**
Ms. Priya expects the price of Tata Steel to fall. She shortsells 1,000 shares at ₹120 each, with a margin requirement of 50%. After three weeks, the price falls to ₹105 and she buys back the shares. Brokerage is 0.05% per leg. Calculate:
- (a) Initial margin
- (b) Gross profit
- (c) Net profit after brokerage
- (d) Return on margin

**Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify variables.**
$N = 1000$, $S_{\text{init}} = 120$, $S_{\text{exit}} = 105$, $m = 50\%$, $b = 0.05\%$

**Step 2 — Initial margin.**

$$
M = S_{\text{init}} \times N \times m
$$

$$
M = 120 \times 1000 \times 0.50 = 60{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 3 — Gross profit.**

$$
P_{\text{gross}} = (S_{\text{init}} - S_{\text{exit}}) \times N
$$

$$
P_{\text{gross}} = (120 - 105) \times 1000 = 15 \times 1000 = 15{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 4 — Brokerage.**

$$
\text{Sell leg} = 120 \times 1000 \times 0.0005 = 60 \text{ INR}
$$

$$
\text{Buy-back leg} = 105 \times 1000 \times 0.0005 = 52.5 \text{ INR}
$$

$$
\text{Total brokerage} = 112.5 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 5 — Net profit.**

$$
P_{\text{net}} = 15{,}000 - 112.5 = 14{,}887.5 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 6 — Return on margin.**

$$
R\% = \frac{14{,}887.5}{60{,}000} \times 100 = 24.81\%
$$

**Final Answer Block:**

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Initial Margin | ₹60,000 |
| Gross Profit | ₹15,000 |
| Total Brokerage | ₹112.50 |
| Net Profit | ₹14,887.50 |
| Return on Margin | 24.81% |

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Payoff Scenarios in Speculation

> [!NOTE]  
> The matrix below is an essential exam-ready artifact. It illustrates **asymmetric risk-reward** in speculation.

| Market Outcome | Long Position P/L | Short Position P/L | Risk Profile |
|----------------|-------------------|--------------------|--------------|
| Price rises by 20% | **+20% × Leverage** | −20% × Leverage | Bull wins, Bear loses |
| Price flat | Loss = Brokerage + Opportunity cost | Loss = Brokerage + Loan cost | Both lose |
| Price falls by 20% | −20% × Leverage | **+20% × Leverage** | Bear wins, Bull loses |
| Price falls by 50% | **Margin wiped out, margin call triggered** | **+50% × Leverage** | Forced liquidation risk |

The **asymmetric** nature of speculation — where a small capital outlay controls a large position — is precisely what makes it both lucrative and dangerous.

### 3.4 Margin Call Demonstration (Why Speculation is Risky)

Suppose a speculator with ₹1,00,000 capital takes a 5x leveraged long position in an asset valued at ₹5,00,000. The maintenance margin is 25%.

- If the asset falls by 10% → loss = ₹50,000 (50% of capital) → **Margin call issued**.
- If the asset falls by 20% → loss = ₹1,00,000 → **Total capital wiped out**.
- If the asset falls by 21% → speculator's debt begins exceeding equity → **Broker liquidates position**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> This is why KTU 2024 examiners love asking: *"Why is speculation riskier than investment?"* The answer is **leverage-induced loss magnification**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Speculation Workflow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Market Scanning and Asset Identification] --> nodeB[Directional Forecast using Technical or Fundamental Analysis]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Direction?}
    nodeC -- Bullish --> nodeD[Long Position - Buy Asset on Margin]
    nodeC -- Bearish --> nodeE[Short Position - Borrow and Sell Asset]
    D1[Monitor Price Action and News] --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF{Target Hit or Stop Loss Triggered}
    nodeF -- Target Achieved --> nodeG[Close Trade and Realize Profit]
    nodeF -- Stop Loss Hit --> nodeH[Close Trade and Absorb Loss]
    nodeF -- Thesis Invalidated --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeI[Reinvest or Withdraw Capital]
    nodeH --> nodeI
```

### 4.2 Comparative Block Diagram: Speculation vs Investment vs Hedging vs Gambling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPE["Speculation Block"]
        SP1[Short Term Horizon]
        SP2[High Risk Acceptance]
        SP3[Profit from Price Change]
        SP4[Uses Leverage Frequently]
    end
    subgraph INV["Investment Block"]
        IV1[Long Term Horizon]
        IV2[Moderate Risk Appetite]
        IV3[Profit from Income and Growth]
        IV4[Uses Diversification]
    end
    subgraph HED["Hedging Block"]
        HD1[Variable Horizon]
        HD2[Risk Reduction Motive]
        HD3[Profit from Offsetting Loss]
        HD4[Uses Derivatives]
    end
    subgraph GAM["Gambling Block"]
        GM1[Instant Horizon]
        GM2[Pure Chance Risk]
        GM3[No Economic Value Created]
        GM4[Zero Sum Redistribution]
    end
```

### 4.3 Bull and Bear Market Mechanics Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph BULL["Bull Speculator Mechanics"]
        B1[Step 1 - Buy at Low Price] --> B2[Step 2 - Hold for Upside]
        B2 --> B3[Step 3 - Sell at Higher Price]
        B3 --> B4[Realize Profit equals SP minus BP times N]
    end
    subgraph BEAR["Bear Speculator Mechanics"]
        R1[Step 1 - Borrow Shares from Broker] --> R2[Step 2 - Sell at Market Price]
        R2 --> R3[Step 3 - Wait for Price Drop]
        R3 --> R4[Step 4 - Buy Back at Lower Price]
        R4 --> R5[Step 5 - Return Shares to Broker]
        R5 --> R6[Realize Profit equals SS minus BP times N]
    end
```

### 4.4 Margin Call and Leverage Risk Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeX[Speculator Deposits Margin M] --> nodeY[Broker Allows Exposure E equals M times Leverage]
    nodeY --> nodeZ{Price Movement}
    nodeZ -- Adverse Move --> nodeA[Loss equals E times Percent Drop]
    nodeA --> nodeB{Loss exceeds M minus Maintenance Margin}
    nodeB -- Yes --> nodeC[Broker Issues Margin Call]
    nodeB -- No --> nodeD[Continue Holding]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Speculator Must Top Up or Close]
    nodeE --> nodeF[If Ignored - Forced Liquidation]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Capital Erosion or Wipeout]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Diagram Reading Tip (KTU 2024)**: When a question says "Explain the working of speculation with a diagram", reproduce a simplified version of the **Speculation Workflow Architecture** above. Examiners reward visual clarity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]  
*Define speculation. How is it different from investment?*

**Model Answer (3-Mark Standard):**

Speculation is the practice of buying or selling financial assets with the primary objective of profiting from short-term price movements, accepting high risk in the process. A speculator does not intend to use the underlying asset for productive purposes; he merely bets on the price direction.

It differs from investment in three key ways:
1. **Time Horizon**: Speculation is short-term; investment is long-term.
2. **Risk Level**: Speculation carries high risk; investment carries moderate risk.
3. **Motive**: Speculation seeks capital gain from price change; investment seeks wealth creation, dividends, and interest income.

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mistake**: Writing only the definition without the three-point comparison. You will lose 2 out of 3 marks. Always include the comparative table or bullet points.

---

**Question 2** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]  
*List and briefly explain the different types of speculators.*

**Model Answer (3-Mark Standard):**

The major types of speculators are:
1. **Bull**: A speculator who anticipates a price rise and takes a long position. Profits from price appreciation.
2. **Bear**: A speculator who anticipates a price fall and shortsells the asset. Profits from price depreciation.
3. **Stag**: A speculator who applies for underpriced IPOs or new issues and sells them on the listing day at a premium.
4. **Arbitrageur**: Exploits simultaneous price differences of the same asset across two markets.
5. **Scalper / Day Trader**: Executes multiple rapid intraday trades to capture small price moves.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]  
> KTU Part B 14-mark questions always have an internal choice (either-or). Below are TWO alternatives, exactly as they would appear in the question paper.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of speculation in detail. Discuss its characteristics and the various types of speculators. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Speculation is the act of undertaking a financial transaction that involves significant risk of capital loss, with the expectation of substantial gain arising from short-term fluctuations in the market price of an asset. The speculator does not have a long-term commitment to the asset; he only seeks to profit from price volatility.

**Characteristics of Speculation:**
- Short time horizon (days to weeks).
- High risk-return profile.
- Heavy reliance on market information, charts, and sentiment.
- Frequent use of leverage and margin trading.
- The underlying asset is rarely used for productive or consumption purposes.
- Speculators are willing to be wrong and absorb the loss as a business cost.

**Types of Speculators:**
- **Bulls** — optimistic about price, take long positions.
- **Bears** — pessimistic about price, take short positions.
- **Stags** — operate in primary markets (IPOs).
- **Arbitrageurs** — exploit price differentials across markets.
- **Scalpers** — exploit minute price moves through high-frequency trading.

**Economic Functions of Speculation:**
- Provides liquidity to the market.
- Aids in price discovery.
- Facilitates risk transfer from risk-averse to risk-tolerant participants.
- Enhances market efficiency by rapidly incorporating new information.

**(b)** A speculator purchases 1,200 shares of Reliance Industries at ₹2,800 per share, paying a margin of 35%. He sells them three weeks later at ₹3,050 per share. Brokerage is 0.1% on both buy and sell sides. Calculate the total exposure, margin paid, gross profit, net profit, and percentage return on margin. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given: $N = 1200$, $B = 2800$, $S = 3050$, $m = 35\%$, $b = 0.1\%$

**Step 1 — Total Exposure:**

$$
V_{\text{exposure}} = B \times N = 2800 \times 1200 = 33{,}60{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 2 — Margin Paid:**

$$
M = V_{\text{exposure}} \times m = 33{,}60{,}000 \times 0.35 = 11{,}76{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 3 — Leverage:**

$$
L = \frac{33{,}60{,}000}{11{,}76{,}000} \approx 2.86
$$

**Step 4 — Gross Profit:**

$$
P_{\text{gross}} = (S - B) \times N = (3050 - 2800) \times 1200 = 250 \times 1200 = 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 5 — Brokerage:**

$$
\text{Buy leg} = 2800 \times 1200 \times 0.001 = 3360 \text{ INR}
$$

$$
\text{Sell leg} = 3050 \times 1200 \times 0.001 = 3660 \text{ INR}
$$

$$
\text{Total brokerage} = 3360 + 3660 = 7020 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 6 — Net Profit:**

$$
P_{\text{net}} = 3{,}00{,}000 - 7020 = 2{,}92{,}980 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 7 — Return on Margin:**

$$
R\% = \frac{2{,}92{,}980}{11{,}76{,}000} \times 100 \approx 24.91\%
$$

**Final Tabulation:**

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Total Exposure | ₹33,60,000 |
| Margin Paid | ₹11,76,000 |
| Leverage | 2.86x |
| Gross Profit | ₹3,00,000 |
| Total Brokerage | ₹7,020 |
| Net Profit | ₹2,92,980 |
| Return on Margin | 24.91% |

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — INTERNAL CHOICE [KTU University Exam - Sample]

**(a)** Distinguish between speculation, investment, hedging, and gambling. Why is speculation considered riskier than investment? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Speculation | Investment | Hedging | Gambling |
|-------|-------------|------------|---------|----------|
| Time horizon | Short-term | Long-term | Variable | Instant |
| Motive | Capital gains from price change | Income and growth | Risk mitigation | Entertainment |
| Risk | High | Moderate | Low (transfers risk) | Pure chance |
| Information use | Heavy | Heavy | Heavy | None |
| Asset use | Never used | Used productively | Owned/related | Notional |
| Economic value | Positive (liquidity, price discovery) | Positive (capital formation) | Positive (risk transfer) | Zero-sum |

**Why Speculation is Riskier than Investment:**
1. **Leverage-induced loss magnification**: Speculators use borrowed capital; small adverse moves wipe out equity quickly.
2. **Short time horizon reduces recovery**: Investments recover over years; speculative losses realize immediately.
3. **Higher volatility exposure**: Short-term price swings are larger and more random.
4. **Margin calls can force liquidation at the worst possible moment**.
5. **No income cushion**: Unlike dividend-paying investments, pure speculation offers no cash flow during the holding period.

**(b)** Mr. Anoop shortsells 800 shares of HDFC Bank at ₹1,650 per share, with an initial margin of 45%. After 25 days, the price drops to ₹1,580, and he squares off the position. Brokerage is 0.08% on each leg. Calculate the initial margin, gross profit, net profit, and return on margin. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given: $N = 800$, $S_{\text{init}} = 1650$, $S_{\text{exit}} = 1580$, $m = 45\%$, $b = 0.08\%$

**Step 1 — Initial Margin:**

$$
M = S_{\text{init}} \times N \times m = 1650 \times 800 \times 0.45 = 5{,}94{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 2 — Gross Profit:**

$$
P_{\text{gross}} = (S_{\text{init}} - S_{\text{exit}}) \times N = (1650 - 1580) \times 800 = 70 \times 800 = 56{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 3 — Brokerage:**

$$
\text{Sell leg} = 1650 \times 800 \times 0.0008 = 1056 \text{ INR}
$$

$$
\text{Buy-back leg} = 1580 \times 800 \times 0.0008 = 1011.2 \text{ INR}
$$

$$
\text{Total brokerage} = 2067.2 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 4 — Net Profit:**

$$
P_{\text{net}} = 56{,}000 - 2067.2 = 53{,}932.8 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 5 — Return on Margin:**

$$
R\% = \frac{53{,}932.8}{5{,}94{,}000} \times 100 \approx 9.08\%
```

**Final Tabulation:**

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Initial Margin | ₹5,94,000 |
| Gross Profit | ₹56,000 |
| Total Brokerage | ₹2,067.20 |
| Net Profit | ₹53,932.80 |
| Return on Margin | 9.08% |

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**:  
> 1. **Forgetting brokerage** is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks in numericals. ALWAYS deduct it.  
> 2. **Using selling price as buying price** in short-selling problems is the #2 error. Remember: in a short sale, the *first* price is the *sale* price.  
> 3. **Not stating the leverage ratio** in long-position problems is a minor deduction. Examiners like to see it.  
> 4. **Writing "speculation is gambling"** in theory answers is a strict penalty. Always clarify that speculation is *economic* and *analytical*.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Speculation**

- **Definition**: Speculation = profiting from short-term price movements with high risk.
- **Core Motive**: Capital gain, NOT income.
- **Time Horizon**: Short-term (days to weeks).
- **Three Pillars of Speculation**: Information, Timing, Risk Acceptance.
- **Five Speculator Types**: Bull, Bear, Stag, Arbitrageur, Scalper.
- **Bull = Long, Bear = Short, Stag = IPO, Arbitrageur = Cross-Market, Scalper = Intraday**.
- **Long P/L formula**: $P/L = (S - B) \times N$.
- **Short P/L formula**: $P/L = (S_{\text{init}} - S_{\text{exit}}) \times N$.
- **Margin formula**: $M = V_{\text{exposure}} \times m\%$.
- **Leverage**: $L = V_{\text{exposure}} / V_{\text{equity}}$ — never trade without understanding it.
- **Speculation ≠ Gambling** — speculation is analytical and economically useful.
- **Speculation ≠ Investment** — investment is long-term and income-oriented.
- **Speculation ≠ Hedging** — hedging reduces risk; speculation embraces it.
- **Risk Magnifier**: Leverage and short time horizon together make speculation the riskiest market activity for retail participants.
- **Always deduct brokerage** in numericals — both buy and sell legs.
- **Always state the leverage ratio** in long-position problems.
- **Real-World Anchors to Mention in Answers**: NSE F&O traders, crude oil speculators, forex traders, IPO stags, George Soros / Warren Buffett (long-term investor, NOT speculator — a good contrast example).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Options Theory - Mechanics of Options Market
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Options Theory - Mechanics of Options Market

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of an Option

An **option** is a **derivative contract** that confers upon its holder (the buyer) the **right, but not the obligation**, to buy or sell an underlying asset (such as a stock, index, commodity, or currency) at a **predetermined price** (called the **strike price** or **exercise price**) on or before a specified **expiration date** (or **maturity date**). In exchange for this right, the buyer pays a non-refundable fee known as the **option premium** to the seller (the *writer*).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> "An option is a financial contract that gives the holder the right, but not the obligation, to transact (buy or sell) an underlying asset at a set price on or before a future date."

## 1.2 The Two Fundamental Option Types

| Option Type | Right Conferred | Action at Exercise |
|-------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **Call Option** | Right to **BUY** the underlying asset | Holder purchases the asset at the strike price |
| **Put Option** | Right to **SELL** the underlying asset | Holder sells the asset at the strike price |

## 1.3 Two Styles of Exercise

1. **American Options**: Can be exercised **any time** on or before the expiration date.
2. **European Options**: Can be exercised **only on** the expiration date itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Despite the names "American" and "European," these terms **do not** refer to geographical location — they are global designations describing exercise flexibility. In Indian markets (NSE/BSE), **index options are European-style**, while **stock options are American-style**.

## 1.4 Real-World Analogy — The "Insurance Premium" Intuition

Imagine you wish to buy a flat in a city for **₹50,00,000**, but the builder's rate may rise in **3 months**. You pay the builder a token **"token advance"** of **₹1,00,000** to **lock the price at ₹50,00,000** for 3 months.

- After 3 months:
  - If prices **rose** → You happily buy at ₹50,00,000 (you saved money).
  - If prices **fell** → You simply **walk away**; the ₹1,00,000 is the cost of the *option* you held.

> **Mapping to Options Theory:**
> - The **token advance** = Option Premium
> - The **locked price** = Strike Price (K)
> - The **3 months** = Time to Expiration (T)
> - Your **right (not duty) to buy** = Call Option
> - **Walking away** = Letting the option expire worthless

This is precisely the foundational principle of an **option**: a **bounded loss** (the premium) for an **unlimited (or large) upside potential** — a hallmark of asymmetric risk-reward.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The premium is always a **sunk cost** — it is never refundable, regardless of whether the option is exercised or not. This is a frequent 2-mark question.

## 1.5 Key Market Participants in the Options Market

| Participant | Role |
|-------------|------|
| **Buyer / Holder** | Pays premium, holds the right |
| **Writer / Seller** | Receives premium, bears the obligation |
| **Options Clearing Corporation (OCC)** | Intermediary; guarantees performance |
| **Regulator (SEBI in India)** | Oversees market integrity |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Essential Option Terminology

- **Underlying Asset**: The financial instrument (stock, index, currency) on which the option is based.
- **Strike Price (K)**: The fixed price at which the underlying can be bought/sold.
- **Spot Price (S)**: The current market price of the underlying.
- **Premium (c or p)**: The price paid by the buyer to the writer.
- **Expiration Date (T)**: The last date the option can be exercised.
- **Intrinsic Value (IV)**: The value the option would have **if exercised immediately**.
- **Time Value (TV)**: The portion of premium attributable to the possibility of future favorable price movement.
- **Moneyness**: The relationship between the spot price and the strike price.

## 2.2 Moneyness — The Heart of Option Classification

| Moneyness Status | Call Option Condition | Put Option Condition | Intrinsic Value |
|------------------|-----------------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| **In-the-Money (ITM)** | S > K | S < K | Positive |
| **At-the-Money (ATM)** | S = K | S = K | Zero |
| **Out-of-the-Money (OTM)** | S < K | S > K | Zero |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick:** "ITM for Calls = In a position to **C**ash in (S > K)". "ITM for Puts = In a position to **P**ocket (S < K)".

## 2.3 Intrinsic Value Formulas

$$
\text{IV}_{\text{Call}} = \max(S - K, \, 0)
$$

$$
\text{IV}_{\text{Put}} = \max(K - S, \, 0)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Intrinsic value can **never be negative** — it is floored at zero. This is the most common mathematical trap in KTU exams.

## 2.4 Premium Decomposition

$$
\text{Option Premium} = \text{Intrinsic Value (IV)} + \text{Time Value (TV)}
$$

> An **ATM** option has **zero IV** but the **maximum TV** (because future movement can push it in either direction).
> A **deeply ITM** option has near-zero TV (the outcome is almost certain).
> A **deeply OTM** option has **zero IV** and low TV (low probability of finishing ITM).

## 2.5 Payoff and Profit Functions — The Four Basic Positions

### 2.5.1 Long Call (Buy a Call)

- **Payoff at expiration:** $C_T = \max(S_T - K, \, 0)$
- **Net Profit:** $\pi_C^{\text{Long}} = \max(S_T - K, \, 0) - c_0$

### 2.5.2 Short Call (Write a Call)

- **Payoff at expiration:** $-C_T = -\max(S_T - K, \, 0) = \min(K - S_T, \, 0)$
- **Net Profit:** $\pi_C^{\text{Short}} = c_0 - \max(S_T - K, \, 0)$

### 2.5.3 Long Put (Buy a Put)

- **Payoff at expiration:** $P_T = \max(K - S_T, \, 0)$
- **Net Profit:** $\pi_P^{\text{Long}} = \max(K - S_T, \, 0) - p_0$

### 2.5.4 Short Put (Write a Put)

- **Payoff at expiration:** $-P_T = \min(S_T - K, \, 0)$
- **Net Profit:** $\pi_P^{\text{Short}} = p_0 - \max(K - S_T, \, 0)$

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb for Examiner:** The buyer's profit formula = Payoff − Premium Paid. The seller's profit = Premium Received − Payoff.

## 2.6 Break-Even Points (BEP)

| Position | Break-Even Spot Price Formula |
|----------|-------------------------------|
| **Long Call** | $S_{\text{BE}} = K + c_0$ |
| **Short Call** | $S_{\text{BE}} = K + c_0$ |
| **Long Put** | $S_{\text{BE}} = K - p_0$ |
| **Short Put** | $S_{\text{BE}} = K - p_0$ |

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Boundary |
|--------|---------|---------------------|
| $c_0$ | Call premium at $t=0$ | $c_0 \geq 0$ always |
| $p_0$ | Put premium at $t=0$ | $p_0 \geq 0$ always |
| $S_T$ | Spot price at expiry | $S_T > 0$ |
| $K$ | Strike price | Fixed by contract |
| $IV_C$ | Call intrinsic value | $\max(S_T - K, 0)$ |
| $IV_P$ | Put intrinsic value | $\max(K - S_T, 0)$ |
| $\pi_{\text{Buyer}}$ | Profit for buyer | Payoff $-$ Premium Paid |
| $\pi_{\text{Writer}}$ | Profit for writer | Premium Received $-$ Payoff |
| Call Upper Bound | $c_0 \leq S_0$ | Otherwise arbitrage |
| Call Lower Bound | $c_0 \geq \max(S_0 - K e^{-rT}, 0)$ | No-arbitrage |
| Put-Call Parity | $c + K e^{-rT} = p + S_0$ | Holds for European options |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Put-Call Parity** is a favorite KTU question. Memorize: $C + PV(K) = P + S$. The signature rule: *Long Call + Risk-free Bond = Long Put + Underlying Stock*.

## 2.8 Mechanics of the Trading Process

1. **Order Placement** — Buyer places a "buy" order; seller places a "sell" order.
2. **Order Matching** — Exchange's electronic system matches compatible orders.
3. **Premium Settlement** — The premium changes hands on the trade date ($T_0$).
4. **Margin Posting** — The **writer** must deposit initial margin with the clearinghouse.
5. **Daily Mark-to-Market (MTM)** — The clearinghouse settles the writer's account daily.
6. **Exercise / Expiry** — On or before expiration, the buyer decides whether to exercise.
7. **Final Settlement** — Cash or physical delivery of the underlying.

## 2.9 Margins in Options Markets

> Unlike futures (which require margins from both parties), options require margins **only from the writer**.

| Position | Margin Required |
|----------|-----------------|
| Long Call / Long Put | **No margin** — only premium is paid upfront |
| Short Call / Short Put | **Initial Margin** + Mark-to-Market settlement |

> [!NOTE]
> The margin for a naked short option is typically calculated as the larger of: (a) **20% of the underlying market value minus the OTM amount**, or (b) **8% of the underlying market value**, subject to a minimum.

## 2.10 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Management

- **Corporate Treasury Hedging** — Companies hedge currency exposure using currency options.
- **Project Risk Management** — Oil & gas companies hedge commodity price risk.
- **Portfolio Insurance** — Fund managers buy puts to limit downside loss.
- **Speculation** — Traders use low-premium OTM options for leveraged bets.
- **Employee Stock Options (ESOs)** — Tech firms compensate engineers with call options on company stock.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Payoff Function of a Long Call

We start from the contract definition: a long call holder receives $\max(S_T - K, 0)$ at expiry.

**Step 1: Definition of Payoff at Expiry**

$$
C_T^{\text{Long}} = 
\begin{cases}
S_T - K, & \text{if } S_T > K \\
0, & \text{if } S_T \leq K
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 2: Compact max() Representation**

$$
C_T^{\text{Long}} = \max(S_T - K, 0)
$$

**Step 3: Add Net Profit (subtract premium paid $c_0$)**

$$
\pi_C^{\text{Long}} = \max(S_T - K, 0) - c_0
$$

**Step 4: Solve for Break-Even Point**

At the break-even, $\pi_C^{\text{Long}} = 0$:

$$
\max(S_T - K, 0) - c_0 = 0
$$

Assume $S_T > K$ (otherwise max is 0 and the equation cannot hold since $c_0 > 0$):

$$
S_T - K - c_0 = 0 \implies S_{\text{BE}} = K + c_0
$$

**Step 5: Maximum Loss**

If $S_T \to 0$, the call expires worthless. The loss is the premium paid:

$$
\pi_{\text{max loss}} = -c_0
$$

**Step 6: Maximum Profit**

Unbounded above (theoretically infinite as $S_T \to \infty$):

$$
\pi_{\text{max profit}} \to \infty \text{ as } S_T \to \infty
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Long Call

**Given:**
- Spot price today: $S_0 = ₹100$
- Strike price: $K = ₹105$
- Call premium: $c_0 = ₹4$
- Time to expiry: $T = 1$ month

**Case A: At expiry, $S_T = ₹110$ (ITM)**

$$
\pi = \max(110 - 105, 0) - 4 = 5 - 4 = +₹1
$$

**Case B: At expiry, $S_T = ₹103$ (OTM)**

$$
\pi = \max(103 - 105, 0) - 4 = 0 - 4 = -₹4
$$

**Case C: At expiry, $S_T = ₹105$ (ATM)**

$$
\pi = \max(105 - 105, 0) - 4 = 0 - 4 = -₹4
$$

**Break-Even Spot Price:**

$$
S_{\text{BE}} = K + c_0 = 105 + 4 = ₹109
$$

> The position is profitable only if $S_T > ₹109$ at expiry.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Payoff & Profit for a Short Call

**Step 1: Payoff Mirror**

The short call is the **exact opposite** of the long call. If the long call receives $\max(S_T - K, 0)$, the short call writer is obligated to pay the same amount.

$$
C_T^{\text{Short}} = -\max(S_T - K, 0)
$$

**Step 2: Add Net Profit (premium received $c_0$)**

$$
\pi_C^{\text{Short}} = c_0 - \max(S_T - K, 0)
$$

**Step 3: Maximum Profit**

If $S_T \leq K$, the call expires worthless and the writer keeps the entire premium:

$$
\pi_{\text{max profit}} = c_0
$$

**Step 4: Maximum Loss**

Unbounded below as $S_T \to \infty$:

$$
\pi_{\text{max loss}} \to -\infty \text{ as } S_T \to \infty
$$

**Step 5: Break-Even**

Setting $\pi_C^{\text{Short}} = 0$:

$$
c_0 - (S_T - K) = 0 \implies S_{\text{BE}} = K + c_0
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Payoff & Profit for a Long Put

**Step 1: Payoff at Expiry**

A put gives the right to sell at $K$. If $S_T < K$, exercise; otherwise walk away.

$$
P_T^{\text{Long}} = \max(K - S_T, 0)
$$

**Step 2: Add Net Profit (subtract put premium $p_0$)**

$$
\pi_P^{\text{Long}} = \max(K - S_T, 0) - p_0
$$

**Step 3: Maximum Profit**

If $S_T \to 0$, the holder sells at $K$ for a profit of $K - p_0$ per share:

$$
\pi_{\text{max profit}} = K - p_0
$$

**Step 4: Maximum Loss**

If $S_T \to \infty$, the put is worthless; loss is the premium:

$$
\pi_{\text{max loss}} = -p_0
$$

**Step 5: Break-Even**

Setting $\pi_P^{\text{Long}} = 0$, with $S_T < K$:

$$
(K - S_T) - p_0 = 0 \implies S_{\text{BE}} = K - p_0
$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Long Put

**Given:**
- $S_0 = ₹200$, $K = ₹210$, $p_0 = ₹6$, $T = 1$ month

**Case A: $S_T = ₹195$ (ITM for put)**

$$
\pi = \max(210 - 195, 0) - 6 = 15 - 6 = +₹9
$$

**Case B: $S_T = ₹220$ (OTM for put)**

$$
\pi = \max(210 - 220, 0) - 6 = 0 - 6 = -₹6
$$

**Break-Even:**

$$
S_{\text{BE}} = K - p_0 = 210 - 6 = ₹204
$$

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Payoff & Profit for a Short Put

**Step 1: Mirror of Long Put**

$$
P_T^{\text{Short}} = -\max(K - S_T, 0)
$$

**Step 2: Add Net Profit**

$$
\pi_P^{\text{Short}} = p_0 - \max(K - S_T, 0)
$$

**Step 3: Maximum Profit**

If $S_T \geq K$, the put expires worthless. Writer keeps the premium:

$$
\pi_{\text{max profit}} = p_0
$$

**Step 4: Maximum Loss**

If $S_T \to 0$:

$$
\pi_{\text{max loss}} = p_0 - K
$$

---

## 3.7 Summary Analytical Matrix — All Four Basic Positions

| Position | Premium Direction | Max Profit | Max Loss | Break-Even ($S_{\text{BE}}$) |
|----------|-------------------|------------|----------|------------------------------|
| **Long Call** | Pay $c_0$ | $\infty$ | $-c_0$ | $K + c_0$ |
| **Short Call** | Receive $c_0$ | $c_0$ | $-\infty$ | $K + c_0$ |
| **Long Put** | Pay $p_0$ | $K - p_0$ | $-p_0$ | $K - p_0$ |
| **Short Put** | Receive $p_0$ | $p_0$ | $p_0 - K$ | $K - p_0$ |

---

## 3.8 Derivation: Put-Call Parity (European Options)

**Step 1: Construct Two Portfolios**

- **Portfolio A:** One European Call + Cash of $K e^{-rT}$ (the present value of strike)
- **Portfolio B:** One European Put + One share of the underlying stock

**Step 2: Value of Portfolio A at Expiry**

If $S_T > K$: Call exercised → Portfolio A worth $S_T - K + K = S_T$
If $S_T \leq K$: Call expires worthless → Portfolio A worth $0 + K = K \geq S_T$ (i.e., $K$)

$$
V_A(T) = \max(S_T, K)
$$

**Step 3: Value of Portfolio B at Expiry**

If $S_T < K$: Put exercised → Portfolio B worth $K - S_T + S_T = K$
If $S_T \geq K$: Put expires worthless → Portfolio B worth $S_T$

$$
V_B(T) = \max(S_T, K)
$$

**Step 4: Equate and Discount to Present**

Since $V_A(T) = V_B(T)$ in all states, by **No-Arbitrage**:

$$
c_0 + K e^{-rT} = p_0 + S_0
$$

This is the celebrated **Put-Call Parity Theorem**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This relation holds strictly for **European** options on **non-dividend-paying** stocks. For dividend-paying stocks, $S_0$ is replaced by $S_0 - PV(\text{dividends})$.

---

## 3.9 Worked Example — Put-Call Parity

**Given:**
- $S_0 = ₹100$, $K = ₹100$, $r = 10\%$, $T = 0.5$ year, $c_0 = ₹10$

**Find $p_0$:**

$$
p_0 = c_0 + K e^{-rT} - S_0
$$

$$
p_0 = 10 + 100 \cdot e^{-0.10 \times 0.5} - 100
$$

$$
p_0 = 10 + 100 \cdot e^{-0.05} - 100
$$

$$
p_0 = 10 + 100 \cdot (0.9512) - 100
$$

$$
p_0 = 10 + 95.12 - 100 = ₹5.12
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Option Market Architecture & Trade Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Investor Decision: Buy or Sell] --> B[Place Order via Broker]
    B --> C[Exchange Order Matching Engine]
    C --> D{Order Matched?}
    D -- No --> E[Order Rests in Book]
    E --> D
    D -- Yes --> F[Trade Executed]
    F --> G[Premium Settled T0]
    F --> H[Writer Posts Initial Margin]
    H --> I[Clearinghouse Takes Position]
    I --> J[Daily Mark to Market MTM]
    J --> K{Approaching Expiry}
    K --> L[Buyer Decision: Exercise or Lapse]
    L -- Exercise --> M[Final Settlement Cash or Physical]
    L -- Lapse --> N[Option Expires Worthless]
    M --> O[Position Closed]
    N --> O
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Option Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Option Contract] --> B[Call Option Right to Buy]
    A --> C[Put Option Right to Sell]
    B --> D{Compare S vs K}
    C --> E{Compare S vs K}
    D -- S greater than K --> F[Call is In The Money ITM]
    D -- S equals K --> G[Call is At The Money ATM]
    D -- S less than K --> H[Call is Out of The Money OTM]
    E -- S less than K --> I[Put is In The Money ITM]
    E -- S equals K --> J[Put is At The Money ATM]
    E -- S greater than K --> K[Put is Out of The Money OTM]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Four Basic Option Positions & Risk Profile

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Buyers
        LC[Long Call: Limited Loss Unlimited Gain]
        LP[Long Put: Limited Loss Large Gain capped at K minus premium]
    end
    subgraph Writers
        SC[Short Call: Limited Gain Unlimited Loss]
        SP[Short Put: Limited Gain Large Loss capped at K minus premium]
    end
    LC -.Pay Premium.-> SC
    LP -.Pay Premium.-> SP
    SC -.Margin Posted.-> M[Clearinghouse]
    SP -.Margin Posted.-> M
    M --> GS[Daily MTM Settlement]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Option Premium Decomposition Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Option Premium c0 or p0] --> IV[Intrinsic Value Component]
    P --> TV[Time Value Component]
    IV --> IV1[Call IV = max S minus K, 0]
    IV --> IV2[Put IV = max K minus S, 0]
    TV --> TV1[Decreases as Time to Expiry Shrinks]
    TV --> TV2[Higher when Volatility is High]
    TV --> TV3[Maximum when ATM]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid:** All the above diagrams are pure block-level functional flow representations. They map the interactions between market participants, clearing infrastructure, classification logic, and premium decomposition. These schematics directly correspond to questions on "Explain the mechanics of options trading" commonly asked in KTU Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
**Define an option. Distinguish between a call option and a put option with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

An **option** is a derivative contract giving the holder the right — but not the obligation — to buy or sell an underlying asset at a predetermined strike price on or before a specified expiration date, in exchange for a premium.

- **Call Option** confers the right to **buy**. *Example:* A call on Reliance with strike ₹2500 allows the holder to buy Reliance at ₹2500 regardless of the market price.
- **Put Option** confers the right to **sell**. *Example:* A put on TCS with strike ₹3800 allows the holder to sell TCS at ₹3800 even if the market price falls to ₹3500.

> **(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for call example + 1 for put example)**

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**
**Explain the concept of "Intrinsic Value" and "Time Value" of an option.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Intrinsic Value (IV)** is the value an option would have **if exercised immediately**. It is calculated as $\max(S - K, 0)$ for calls and $\max(K - S, 0)$ for puts. It is never negative.
- **Time Value (TV)** is the portion of the premium attributable to the possibility that the option will become more valuable before expiry due to favorable price movement.

$$
\text{Premium} = \text{Intrinsic Value} + \text{Time Value}
$$

Time value is **highest when the option is ATM** and **erodes to zero at expiration**. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 4 carries two 14-mark questions. Either Q1 or Q2 must be answered. Here we provide both options for full preparation.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**(a) Explain the mechanics of an options market in detail, including the roles of buyer, writer, clearinghouse, and the margin system.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The mechanics of an options market involve the following sequential steps:

1. **Contract Initiation:** A standardized option contract is created by the exchange specifying: underlying asset, strike price, expiry date, lot size, and premium.
2. **Buyer's Role:** The buyer (long position holder) pays the premium upfront. The buyer's risk is **strictly limited to the premium paid**. The buyer holds the right, not the obligation.
3. **Writer's Role:** The writer (short position) receives the premium and assumes the obligation to deliver (for calls) or take delivery (for puts) if the buyer exercises.
4. **Exchange & Clearinghouse:** The exchange provides the trading platform. The clearinghouse (e.g., **OCC** in the US, **NSCCL** in India) becomes the **counterparty to every trade**, guaranteeing settlement and eliminating default risk.
5. **Margin System:**
   - **Buyers** pay only the premium — **no margin** required.
   - **Writers** must post **initial margin** (calculated using SPAN or VaR methodology).
   - **Mark-to-Market (MTM)** is performed daily; losses are debited and gains credited to the writer's account.
6. **Exercise & Settlement:** At expiry (or earlier for American options), the buyer decides whether to exercise. Settlement is either **cash-settled** (for index options) or **physically-delivered** (for stock options).

> **(7 marks: 2 for buyer/writer roles + 2 for clearinghouse + 3 for margin & settlement process)**

---

**(b) With suitable payoff diagrams, explain the payoffs and profits of a Long Call and a Long Put position. Use an example to compute break-even points.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Long Call Payoff Formula:**

$$
\text{Payoff}_C^{\text{Long}} = \max(S_T - K, 0)
$$

$$
\text{Profit}_C^{\text{Long}} = \max(S_T - K, 0) - c_0
$$

**Long Call Behaviour:**
- For $S_T \leq K$: Payoff = 0, Profit = $-c_0$ (loss capped at premium)
- For $S_T > K$: Payoff = $S_T - K$, Profit = $S_T - K - c_0$
- **Break-Even:** $S_{\text{BE}} = K + c_0$

**Long Put Payoff Formula:**

$$
\text{Payoff}_P^{\text{Long}} = \max(K - S_T, 0)
$$

$$
\text{Profit}_P^{\text{Long}} = \max(K - S_T, 0) - p_0
$$

**Long Put Behaviour:**
- For $S_T \geq K$: Payoff = 0, Profit = $-p_0$
- For $S_T < K$: Payoff = $K - S_T$, Profit = $K - S_T - p_0$
- **Break-Even:** $S_{\text{BE}} = K - p_0$

**Numerical Example:**

Let $K = ₹100$, $c_0 = ₹5$, $p_0 = ₹4$.

| Position | Break-Even Spot Price |
|----------|----------------------|
| Long Call | $₹100 + ₹5 = ₹105$ |
| Long Put | $₹100 - ₹4 = ₹96$ |

If $S_T = ₹110$:
- Long Call Profit = $10 - 5 = +₹5$
- Long Put Profit = $0 - 4 = -₹4$

If $S_T = ₹90$:
- Long Call Profit = $0 - 5 = -₹5$
- Long Put Profit = $10 - 4 = +₹6$

> **(7 marks: 2 for long call formulas + 2 for long put formulas + 3 for numerical example with break-even computation)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) Derive the Put-Call Parity theorem. State and explain its significance in option pricing.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

**Step 1: Construct Portfolio A and Portfolio B.**
- **Portfolio A** = Long European Call (cost $c_0$) + Risk-free Bond paying $K$ at $T$ (cost $K e^{-rT}$)
- **Portfolio B** = Long European Put (cost $p_0$) + One share of underlying (cost $S_0$)

**Step 2: Value at Expiry for Portfolio A.**

If $S_T > K$: Call is exercised, paying $S_T - K$. The bond pays $K$. Total = $S_T$.
If $S_T \leq K$: Call lapses. The bond pays $K$. Total = $K \geq S_T$.
Thus, $V_A(T) = \max(S_T, K)$.

**Step 3: Value at Expiry for Portfolio B.**

If $S_T < K$: Put is exercised, paying $K - S_T$. The stock is worth $S_T$. Total = $K$.
If $S_T \geq K$: Put lapses. The stock is worth $S_T$. Total = $S_T$.
Thus, $V_B(T) = \max(S_T, K)$.

**Step 4: Apply No-Arbitrage Principle.**

Since $V_A(T) \equiv V_B(T)$ for all $S_T$:

$$
c_0 + K e^{-rT} = p_0 + S_0
$$

**Significance:**
1. Provides a **theoretical relationship** between call and put prices.
2. Enables **arbitrage detection** — any deviation creates a riskless profit opportunity.
3. Helps in **pricing exotic options** and **hedging strategies**.
4. Serves as the **foundation of the Binomial and Black-Scholes models**.

> **(7 marks: 3 for construction + 2 for derivation + 2 for significance)**

---

**(b) An investor buys a call option with strike ₹150 at a premium of ₹8. Compute the profit at expiry for spot prices of ₹140, ₹150, ₹160, and ₹170. Also determine the break-even point.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given: $K = ₹150$, $c_0 = ₹8$.

**Break-Even Point:**

$$
S_{\text{BE}} = K + c_0 = 150 + 8 = ₹158
$$

**Profit Computation Using $\pi = \max(S_T - K, 0) - c_0$:**

**Case 1: $S_T = ₹140$ (OTM)**

$$
\pi = \max(140 - 150, 0) - 8 = \max(-10, 0) - 8 = 0 - 8 = -₹8
$$

**Case 2: $S_T = ₹150$ (ATM)**

$$
\pi = \max(150 - 150, 0) - 8 = 0 - 8 = -₹8
$$

**Case 3: $S_T = ₹160$ (ITM)**

$$
\pi = \max(160 - 150, 0) - 8 = 10 - 8 = +₹2
$$

**Case 4: $S_T = ₹170$ (ITM)**

$$
\pi = \max(170 - 150, 0) - 8 = 20 - 8 = +₹12
$$

**Summary Table:**

| Spot Price ($S_T$) | Moneyness | Payoff ($\max(S_T - K, 0)$) | Profit |
|---------------------|-----------|------------------------------|--------|
| ₹140 | OTM | ₹0 | -₹8 |
| ₹150 | ATM | ₹0 | -₹8 |
| ₹160 | ITM | ₹10 | +₹2 |
| ₹170 | ITM | ₹20 | +₹12 |

**Observations:**
- Maximum loss is **-₹8** (the premium).
- Profit grows **1-for-1** with spot price above the break-even.
- The position is profitable **only when $S_T > ₹158$**.

> **(7 marks: 2 for BEP + 4 for four cases + 1 for summary/observation)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Payoff with Profit** — Payoff is **before** premium; Profit is **after** premium. Always read the question carefully: "compute the payoff" vs. "compute the profit."
> 2. **Sign Error in Moneyness** — A call is **ITM when S > K**, NOT when S < K. This is the single most common error. Memorize: "**C**all = **C**ompare upward."
> 3. **Forgetting the Max() Function** — Intrinsic value is **never negative**. Writing $S_T - K$ directly (without max) for cases where $S_T < K$ will cost you 2 marks.
> 4. **Mismatched Break-Even Formulas** — Long Call BEP = $K + c$, Long Put BEP = $K - p$. Do not invert them.
> 5. **Applying Put-Call Parity to American Options** — Parity holds strictly only for **European** options. The examiner will deduct 1 mark if you ignore this caveat.
> 6. **Confusing Writer's Position with Buyer's Position** — When asked "compute the profit for the *writer*," remember the writer **receives** the premium, not pays.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Option** = Right (not obligation) + Premium + Strike + Expiry.
- **Call** = Right to **Buy**; **Put** = Right to **Sell**.
- **American** = Anytime before expiry; **European** = Only at expiry.
- **Intrinsic Value**: Call = $\max(S - K, 0)$; Put = $\max(K - S, 0)$.
- **Time Value**: Always $\geq 0$; maximum when ATM; zero at expiry.
- **Moneyness**:
  - Call ITM $\Leftrightarrow S > K$; OTM $\Leftrightarrow S < K$.
  - Put ITM $\Leftrightarrow S < K$; OTM $\Leftrightarrow S > K$.
- **Premium Decomposition**: Premium = IV + TV.
- **Profit for Buyer** = Payoff $-$ Premium Paid.
- **Profit for Writer** = Premium Received $-$ Payoff.
- **Break-Even (Long Call)** = $K + c_0$.
- **Break-Even (Long Put)** = $K - p_0$.
- **Maximum Loss** for buyers = Premium paid.
- **Maximum Profit** for call buyers = Unlimited.
- **Maximum Profit** for put buyers = $K - p_0$.
- **Margin** is required **only from writers**; buyers pay only premium.
- **Put-Call Parity**: $c_0 + K e^{-rT} = p_0 + S_0$ (European, no dividends).
- **Clearinghouse** is the central counterparty — guarantees all trades.
- **Mark-to-Market (MTM)** is done **daily** for the writer's account.
- **NSE Index Options** are **European**; **NSE Stock Options** are **American**.
- **Settlement**: Index options = Cash; Stock options = Physical delivery (in India).

> **Final Memory Hook:** *"Options are insurance policies on prices. You pay a small premium for peace of mind — and the worst that can happen is you lose the premium, never more."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Properties of Stock Options

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Stock Options — The Academic Definition

A **stock option** is a contractual financial derivative that grants the holder (buyer) the **right, but not the obligation**, to buy or sell a specified quantity of the underlying stock at a pre-determined price (known as the **strike price** or **exercise price**, denoted $K$) on or before a specified expiration date (denoted $T$).

The two principal types of stock options are:

- **Call Option ($C$)**: Gives the holder the right to **buy** the underlying stock at strike price $K$.
- **Put Option ($P$)**: Gives the holder the right to **sell** the underlying stock at strike price $K$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** The phrase **"right but not the obligation"** is the defining feature of every option. Examiners frequently test this single line — learn it verbatim. The seller (writer) of the option, however, **assumes the obligation** when the buyer exercises.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Tag — Module 4:** Properties of stock options include factors like option price determinants, time value, intrinsic value, leverage, limited risk, and the symmetry between calls and puts. The KTU 2024 Scheme expects you to list, explain, and compute these properties in both Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

## 1.2 Intuition: A Real-World Analogy

Imagine you walk into a car dealership and **fall in love** with a brand-new SUV priced today at **₹20,00,000**. You are also shopping for a house and won't have the cash for three months. The dealer offers you a deal:

> *"Pay me ₹50,000 today. If, in three months, you still want the SUV at the listed price of ₹20,00,000, you may buy it. If you change your mind, walk away — but the ₹50,000 is mine forever."*

This is exactly how a **call option** works:

| Real-World Element | Options Market Counterpart |
|---|---|
| ₹50,000 paid today | **Option Premium** ($C$ or $P$) |
| Three-month window | **Time to Expiration** ($T - t$) |
| ₹20,00,000 locked price | **Strike Price** ($K$) |
| The SUV | **Underlying Stock** |
| Right to buy (not forced) | **Right, Not Obligation** |

If the SUV's market price skyrockets to ₹24,00,000, you happily exercise the option — buying at ₹20,00,000 and pocketing the difference. If the price crashes to ₹16,00,000, you simply walk away; your maximum loss is the ₹50,000 premium.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "option" is the perfect word:** Just like ordering food from a menu (you *can* order, you are not *forced* to), an option gives you a *choice*. The premium is the "price of choice."

## 1.3 Position Roles in Every Option Contract

Every option contract has **two sides**:

- **Long Position (Holder / Buyer)**: Pays the premium; has the **right** to exercise.
- **Short Position (Writer / Seller)**: Receives the premium; has the **obligation** to fulfill if exercised.

This is the foundational **contract symmetry** that drives all option properties.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Core Properties of Stock Options

### Property 1 — Limited Liability for the Buyer
The maximum loss for the **buyer (long)** of either a call or a put is **strictly limited to the premium paid**. No matter how badly the trade goes, the buyer cannot lose more than the initial outflow.

$$
\text{Max Loss}_{\text{Buyer}} = \text{Premium Paid}
$$

### Property 2 — Unlimited / Substantial Downside for the Seller
The **seller (writer)** of a call option has **theoretically unlimited loss potential** (since stock price can rise indefinitely). The seller of a put has substantial loss bounded by the maximum possible stock decline.

$$
\text{Max Loss}_{\text{Call Writer}} \to \infty \quad ; \quad \text{Max Loss}_{\text{Put Writer}} = K - 0 = K
$$

### Property 3 — Leverage
Options provide **tremendous leverage**. A small premium controls a large notional position. If stock price moves favourably by 20%, the option premium may move by 200% or more.

$$
\text{Leverage Ratio} = \frac{\Delta \text{Stock Price}}{\Delta \text{Option Price}}
$$

### Property 4 — Time Decay (Theta $\theta$)
An option's value **erodes with time** as expiration approaches (all else equal). This is measured by **Theta** — the time-decay rate.

$$
\theta = \frac{\partial V}{\partial t}
$$

- $\theta < 0$ for a long option position (time hurts the buyer).
- $\theta > 0$ for a short option position (time helps the seller).

### Property 5 — Intrinsic Value vs. Time Value
The total option price is decomposed as:

$$
V = \text{Intrinsic Value} + \text{Time Value}
$$

- **Call Intrinsic Value** $= \max(S - K, 0)$
- **Put Intrinsic Value** $= \max(K - S, 0)$
- **Time Value** = Premium $-$ Intrinsic Value (always $\geq 0$).

### Property 6 — Put-Call Parity (The Foundational Identity)
A landmark property linking calls, puts, the stock, and a bond:

$$
C + PV(K) = P + S
$$

Or equivalently:

$$
C - P = S - PV(K) = S - Ke^{-rT}
$$

where $PV(K) = K e^{-rT}$ is the present value of the strike price at risk-free rate $r$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** Put-call parity is tested in both 3-mark and 14-mark questions. Memorize both forms.

### Property 7 — Moneyness Classification
Every option can be classified by its moneyness relative to the underlying price $S$:

| Moneyness | Call Condition | Put Condition |
|---|---|---|
| **In-the-Money (ITM)** | $S > K$ | $S < K$ |
| **At-the-Money (ATM)** | $S = K$ | $S = K$ |
| **Out-of-the-Money (OTM)** | $S < K$ | $S > K$ |

### Property 8 — No Voting / Dividend Rights
Options are **derivatives** — they confer no ownership. Holding a call does **not** entitle you to dividends, voting rights, or any shareholder privileges. The underlying stockholder retains all such rights.

## 2.2 Real-World Utility in Finance & Engineering

- **Risk Management (Hedging):** Manufacturers use currency options to hedge import costs; tech firms use stock options in employee compensation (ESOPs) to align incentives.
- **Speculation:** Traders use leverage to amplify directional bets.
- **Income Generation:** Covered-call writers earn premium income.
- **Engineering Project Finance:** Long-gestation engineering projects (e.g., shipbuilding, power plants) use commodity options to lock in future input costs.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Call Payoff | $\max(S_T - K, 0)$ | Profit = Payoff $-$ Premium |
| 2 | Put Payoff | $\max(K - S_T, 0)$ | Profit = Payoff $-$ Premium |
| 3 | Call Profit | $\max(S_T - K, 0) - C$ | Net P&L to holder |
| 4 | Put Profit | $\max(K - S_T, 0) - P$ | Net P&L to holder |
| 5 | Intrinsic Value (Call) | $\max(S - K, 0)$ | Live, before expiration |
| 6 | Intrinsic Value (Put) | $\max(K - S, 0)$ | Live, before expiration |
| 7 | Put-Call Parity | $C + Ke^{-rT} = P + S$ | European options, no dividends |
| 8 | Upper Bound (Call) | $C \leq S$ | Otherwise arbitrage |
| 9 | Lower Bound (Call) | $C \geq \max(S - K, 0)$ | Non-negative time value |
| 10 | Upper Bound (Put) | $P \leq K$ | Otherwise arbitrage |
| 11 | Lower Bound (Put) | $P \geq \max(K - S, 0)$ | Non-negative time value |
| 12 | Breakeven Call | $K + C$ | Stock price at expiry = BE |
| 13 | Breakeven Put | $K - P$ | Stock price at expiry = BE |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-Specific Notation:** $S_T$ = stock price at expiration $T$; $S$ = current stock price; $K$ = strike price; $C$ = call premium; $P$ = put premium; $r$ = risk-free rate (annualized).
