# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Payoff and Profit for a Long Call

**Step 1 — Define the Payoff Function**
At expiration $T$, the holder of a European call option with strike $K$ receives:

$$
\text{Payoff}_{\text{Call}} = \max(S_T - K, 0)
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the Premium to Get Profit**
The buyer paid premium $C$ at time $t = 0$. Profit (P&L) at expiration is:

$$
\pi_{\text{Call, Long}} = \max(S_T - K, 0) - C
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Two Regimes**
- **Regime A**: $S_T \leq K$ (Option expires worthless) → $\pi = 0 - C = -C$ (loss limited to premium).
- **Regime B**: $S_T > K$ (Option exercised) → $\pi = S_T - K - C$ (unlimited profit upside).

**Step 4 — Solve for the Breakeven Point**
Set $\pi = 0$ in Regime B:

$$
S_T - K - C = 0 \implies S_T^{*} = K + C
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Demonstration**
Let $K = 100$, $C = 5$.

| $S_T$ | $\max(S_T - K, 0)$ | $\pi = \text{Payoff} - C$ | Region |
|---|---|---|---|
| 80 | 0 | $-5$ | OTM (loss capped) |
| 100 | 0 | $-5$ | ATM (loss capped) |
| 105 | 5 | $0$ | **Breakeven** |
| 120 | 20 | $+15$ | ITM (profit) |
| 150 | 50 | $+45$ | ITM (large profit) |

## 3.2 Derivation: Payoff and Profit for a Long Put

**Step 1 — Define the Put Payoff**

$$
\text{Payoff}_{\text{Put}} = \max(K - S_T, 0)
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the Premium**

$$
\pi_{\text{Put, Long}} = \max(K - S_T, 0) - P
$$

**Step 3 — Two Regimes**
- **Regime A**: $S_T \geq K$ → $\pi = 0 - P = -P$ (loss limited to premium).
- **Regime B**: $S_T < K$ → $\pi = K - S_T - P$ (profit as $S_T$ falls).

**Step 4 — Breakeven Point**

$$
K - S_T - P = 0 \implies S_T^{*} = K - P
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Demonstration**
Let $K = 100$, $P = 5$.

| $S_T$ | $\max(K - S_T, 0)$ | $\pi$ | Region |
|---|---|---|---|
| 80 | 20 | $+15$ | ITM |
| 95 | 5 | $0$ | **Breakeven** |
| 100 | 0 | $-5$ | ATM |
| 120 | 0 | $-5$ | OTM |
| 60 | 40 | $+35$ | Deep ITM |

## 3.3 Derivation: Put-Call Parity (The No-Arbitrage Argument)

**Step 1 — Construct Two Portfolios**

**Portfolio A:** One European call $C$ + a zero-coupon bond paying $K$ at $T$.
**Portfolio B:** One European put $P$ + one share of stock $S$ today.

**Step 2 — Evaluate Both at Expiration $T$**

*Portfolio A at $T$*:
- Call payoff: $\max(S_T - K, 0)$
- Bond payoff: $K$
- Total: $\max(S_T - K, 0) + K$

*Portfolio B at $T$*:
- Put payoff: $\max(K - S_T, 0)$
- Stock value: $S_T$
- Total: $\max(K - S_T, 0) + S_T$

**Step 3 — Verify They Are Identical**

**Case 1:** $S_T \geq K$

$$
\text{Portfolio A} = (S_T - K) + K = S_T \quad ; \quad \text{Portfolio B} = 0 + S_T = S_T
$$

**Case 2:** $S_T < K$

$$
\text{Portfolio A} = 0 + K = K \quad ; \quad \text{Portfolio B} = (K - S_T) + S_T = K
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the No-Arbitrage Principle**
If two portfolios have identical payoffs in all states of the world, they **must have identical prices today**:

$$
C + PV(K) = P + S
$$

$$
C + Ke^{-rT} = P + S
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange the Two Standard Forms**

$$
C - P = S - Ke^{-rT}
$$

$$
P - C = Ke^{-rT} - S
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Note:** This derivation assumes **European options on a non-dividend-paying stock**. For dividend-paying stocks, replace $S$ with $S - PV(\text{Dividends})$ in the parity equation.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Four Basic Positions

| Property | Long Call | Long Put | Short Call | Short Put |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Initial Cash Flow | $-C$ (outflow) | $-P$ (outflow) | $+C$ (inflow) | $+P$ (inflow) |
| Max Profit | $\infty$ | $K - P$ | $C$ (premium) | $P - 0$ adjusted |
| Max Loss | $-C$ | $-P$ | $-\infty$ | $K + P$? No, $-(K - P)$ |
| Breakeven | $K + C$ | $K - P$ | $K + C$ | $K - P$ |
| Outlook | Bullish | Bearish | Bearish | Bullish |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Notation Pitfall:** A negative number means the position holder is in loss. Always specify the sign convention used.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Anatomy of a Stock Option

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stock Option Contract] --> B[Type Selection]
    B --> C[Call Option: Right to BUY]
    B --> D[Put Option: Right to SELL]
    C --> E[Holder: Pays Premium, Limited Loss = C]
    C --> F[Writer: Receives Premium, Unlimited Loss Potential]
    D --> G[Holder: Pays Premium, Max Loss = P, Max Profit = K - P]
    D --> H[Writer: Receives Premium, Max Loss = K - P]
    E --> I[Property Set]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Intrinsic Value = max S minus K or K minus S, 0]
    I --> K[Time Value = Premium minus Intrinsic Value]
    I --> L[Put Call Parity: C plus K e to power minus rT equals P plus S]
    I --> M[Moneyness: ITM, ATM, OTM]
    I --> N[Greeks: Theta, Delta, Gamma, Vega, Rho]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Payoff Logic for a Long Call

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[At Expiration: Compare S sub T to K] --> decision1{Is S sub T greater than K?}
    decision1 -->|Yes| pathA[Call is ITM]
    decision1 -->|No| pathB[Call is OTM or ATM]
    pathA --> payoffA[Payoff equals S sub T minus K]
    pathB --> payoffB[Payoff equals 0]
    payoffA --> pnlA[Profit equals S sub T minus K minus C]
    payoffB --> pnlB[Profit equals minus C, full premium lost]
    pnlA --> resultA[Unlimited profit potential]
    pnlB --> resultB[Loss capped at premium C]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Properties Interlinkage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    propA[Property: Limited Risk for Buyer] --> core[Core Option Mechanics]
    propB[Property: Leverage] --> core
    propC[Property: Time Decay Theta] --> core
    propD[Property: Put Call Parity] --> core
    propE[Property: Moneyness] --> core
    propF[Property: Intrinsic plus Time Value Decomposition] --> core
    core --> market[Market Application: Hedging, Speculation, ESOPs, Project Finance]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. List any **six properties** of stock options. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (6 points, ~50 words each):**

1. **Right, Not Obligation:** A stock option grants the holder the right to buy (call) or sell (put) the underlying stock at a pre-agreed strike price, but the holder is **not obligated** to exercise.
2. **Limited Liability for Buyer:** The maximum loss for the option buyer is restricted to the **premium paid**, regardless of how adverse price movement becomes.
3. **Leverage:** A small investment in premium controls a large notional exposure, magnifying percentage returns on favourable moves.
4. **Time Decay (Theta):** Option value erodes as expiration approaches, even when the underlying price is unchanged — time is "against" the buyer.
5. **No Ownership / No Voting Rights:** Options are derivative contracts; they convey no dividend, voting, or shareholder privileges of the underlying stock.
6. **Put-Call Parity:** The relationship $C + Ke^{-rT} = P + S$ holds for European options, ensuring no-arbitrage pricing.

---

### Question A2. Distinguish between a **Call Option** and a **Put Option** with an example. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Call Option | Put Option |
|---|---|---|
| Right Conferred | To **buy** the stock at strike $K$ | To **sell** the stock at strike $K$ |
| Buyer Outlook | **Bullish** (expects price to rise) | **Bearish** (expects price to fall) |
| Payoff at $T$ | $\max(S_T - K, 0)$ | $\max(K - S_T, 0)$ |
| Breakeven | $K + C$ | $K - P$ |
| Max Loss (Buyer) | Premium $C$ | Premium $P$ |
| Max Profit (Buyer) | Unbounded | $K - P$ |
| Example | Buying a 1-month call on Infosys at $K$ = ₹1,500 | Buying a 1-month put on Tata Steel at $K$ = ₹120 |

**Example (Call):** You buy a call on Reliance with $K = ₹2,500$, paying $C = ₹40$. If at expiry $S_T = ₹2,600$, profit $= 2600 - 2500 - 40 = ₹60$.

**Example (Put):** You buy a put on HDFC Bank with $K = ₹1,600$, paying $P = ₹30$. If at expiry $S_T = ₹1,500$, profit $= 1600 - 1500 - 30 = ₹70$.

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern Note:** Part B questions in Module 4 typically allow internal choice. Both options below are independent — students answer **one**.

### Question B1(a) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**"Explain the concept of Put-Call Parity. A stock is trading at ₹500. A 3-month European call with strike ₹520 trades at ₹18. The risk-free rate is 8% per annum. Find the price of the corresponding put using put-call parity."**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State Put-Call Parity**

$$
C + Ke^{-rT} = P + S
$$

**Step 2 — List the Given Variables**

- $S = 500$ (current stock price)
- $K = 520$ (strike price)
- $T = 3/12 = 0.25$ years
- $r = 0.08$ (risk-free rate)
- $C = 18$ (call premium)
- $P = ?$ (put premium to find)

**Step 3 — Compute $Ke^{-rT}$**

$$
Ke^{-rT} = 520 \times e^{-0.08 \times 0.25} = 520 \times e^{-0.02}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Exponential**

$$
e^{-0.02} \approx 0.9802
$$

$$
Ke^{-rT} = 520 \times 0.9802 = 509.70
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $P$**

$$
P = C + Ke^{-rT} - S
$$

$$
P = 18 + 509.70 - 500
$$

$$
P = 27.70
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the Answer**

The put option is worth approximately **₹27.70**. Since $K > S$, the put is **in-the-money**, so a non-trivial positive value is consistent with the theory.

**Step 7 — Sanity Check**

- $C + PV(K) = 18 + 509.70 = 527.70$
- $P + S = 27.70 + 500 = 527.70$ ✓ Parity holds.

### Question B1(b) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**"A call option has strike price ₹200 and premium ₹15. Construct a payoff table and profit table for the long call holder for the following stock prices at expiration: ₹170, ₹190, ₹200, ₹210, ₹230, ₹250."**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Formulas**

$$
\text{Payoff} = \max(S_T - K, 0) = \max(S_T - 200, 0)
$$

$$
\text{Profit} = \text{Payoff} - C = \text{Payoff} - 15
$$

**Step 2 — Construct the Table Row-by-Row**

| Stock Price $S_T$ (₹) | $\max(S_T - 200, 0)$ (₹) | Profit (₹) | Moneyness |
|---|---|---|---|
| 170 | $\max(-30, 0) = 0$ | $0 - 15 = -15$ | OTM |
| 190 | $\max(-10, 0) = 0$ | $0 - 15 = -15$ | OTM |
| 200 | $\max(0, 0) = 0$ | $0 - 15 = -15$ | ATM |
| 210 | $\max(10, 0) = 10$ | $10 - 15 = -5$ | ITM (small) |
| 230 | $\max(30, 0) = 30$ | $30 - 15 = +15$ | ITM |
| 250 | $\max(50, 0) = 50$ | $50 - 15 = +35$ | ITM (large) |

**Step 3 — Identify Key Observations**

- **Breakeven Price** $= K + C = 200 + 15 = \mathbf{₹215}$.
- For $S_T \leq 200$, the option is **unexercised**, and the loss is the constant **₹15** premium.
- For $S_T > 200$, the option becomes **profitable** and gains ₹1 for every ₹1 rise in $S_T$ beyond 200.
- **Maximum Loss** = ₹15 (the premium). **Maximum Profit** = Unbounded.

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

This table perfectly demonstrates the **limited-risk, unlimited-upside** property of the long call holder.

### Question B2(a) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**"Explain the properties of stock options in detail. Why is the loss of a call option buyer limited while that of a put option writer is substantial?"**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — The Eight Properties (Tabular Form)**

| # | Property | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Right, Not Obligation** | Holder can choose to exercise or let expire worthless. |
| 2 | **Limited Risk for Buyer** | Maximum loss = premium paid. |
| 3 | **Leverage** | Small premium controls large notional value. |
| 4 | **Time Decay** | Theta erodes option value as $T$ approaches. |
| 5 | **Intrinsic + Time Value** | Total premium splits into these two components. |
| 6 | **Put-Call Parity** | $C + Ke^{-rT} = P + S$ |
| 7 | **Moneyness** | ITM / ATM / OTM classification. |
| 8 | **No Voting Rights** | Options confer no ownership benefits. |

**Step 2 — Why Call Buyer's Loss Is Limited**

The call buyer's maximum loss occurs when the call expires **out-of-the-money** ($S_T \leq K$). In that case:

$$
\pi_{\text{Call, Long}} = \max(S_T - K, 0) - C = 0 - C = -C
$$

The buyer simply does not exercise; the loss is **exactly equal to the premium paid**. Since the option grants a *right* (not an obligation) and the buyer can walk away, the loss is **capped**.

**Step 3 — Why Put Writer's Loss Is Substantial**

The put writer's profit function is:

$$
\pi_{\text{Put, Short}} = P - \max(K - S_T, 0) = P - (K - S_T)^{+}
$$

The maximum loss occurs when $S_T \to 0$ (stock price crashes to zero):

$$
\text{Max Loss}_{\text{Put Writer}} = P - (K - 0) = P - K \approx -K
$$

The loss is approximately equal to the **strike price**, which is a large, substantial amount compared to the modest premium received. Unlike a call writer (who faces unlimited loss), the put writer's loss is bounded — but bounded at a *very high* value.

**Step 4 — Comparative Summary**

| Position | Max Loss | Capped? |
|---|---|---|
| Long Call | $C$ (premium) | Yes — fully capped |
| Long Put | $P$ (premium) | Yes — fully capped |
| Short Call | $\infty$ (unlimited) | No — uncapped |
| Short Put | $K - P \approx K$ | Capped but large |

**Step 5 — Real-World Implication**

This asymmetry is why **margin requirements** are imposed on option writers but not buyers. Exchanges demand collateral from sellers to protect against this substantial downside.

---

### Question B2(b) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**"A stock trades at ₹80. A European call with strike ₹85 and 6 months to expiration is priced at ₹6. The risk-free rate is 10% per annum. Using put-call parity, find the price of the European put with the same strike and maturity. Verify your answer."**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State Put-Call Parity**

$$
C + Ke^{-rT} = P + S
$$

**Step 2 — Given Values**

- $S = 80$
- $K = 85$
- $T = 6/12 = 0.5$
- $r = 0.10$
- $C = 6$
- $P = ?$

**Step 3 — Compute $Ke^{-rT}$**

$$
Ke^{-rT} = 85 \times e^{-0.10 \times 0.5} = 85 \times e^{-0.05}
$$

$$
e^{-0.05} \approx 0.9512
$$

$$
Ke^{-rT} = 85 \times 0.9512 = 80.85
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $P$**

$$
P = C + Ke^{-rT} - S
$$

$$
P = 6 + 80.85 - 80 = 6.85
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

The put is **in-the-money** because $K > S$ ($85 > 80$). Its value is the **intrinsic value plus time value**:
- Intrinsic Value: $K - S = 85 - 80 = ₹5$
- Time Value: $6.85 - 5 = ₹1.85$
- Total: ₹6.85 ✓

**Step 6 — Verification (Both Sides of Parity)**

- LHS: $C + Ke^{-rT} = 6 + 80.85 = 86.85$
- RHS: $P + S = 6.85 + 80 = 86.85$ ✓

Parity holds exactly.
