## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks in "Properties of Stock Options" questions:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Payoff/Profit Tables:** Writing payoff = $-C$ for OTM options. **Correct:** Payoff = $0$, Profit = $-C$. Examiners deduct 1 mark for confusing the two.
> 2. **Forgetting to Subtract the Premium:** When asked for "profit," students often present the **payoff** column. Always subtract the premium for profit.
> 3. **Put-Call Parity Sign Mistakes:** Writing $P + Ke^{-rT} = C + S$ instead of $C + Ke^{-rT} = P + S$. Memorize the form with **C on the left, P on the right**.
> 4. **Misidentifying Breakeven Points:** For a put, breakeven is $K - P$ (a number **less than** $K$). Students often wrongly write $K + P$ — this is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Confusing Moneyness Across Call/Put:** ITM call has $S > K$; ITM put has $S < K$. The inequalities are **opposite**, and examiners test this exact reversal.
> 6. **Skipping Time Value Decomposition:** When asked to "analyze the option premium," students often quote the total premium without splitting it into Intrinsic + Time Value — losing 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Omitting the "Right, Not Obligation" Phrase:** In 3-mark questions asking for properties, omitting this hallmark phrase is an automatic ½ mark deduction.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Properties of Stock Options**

- ☐ **Definition:** An option is a contract giving the **right, but not the obligation**, to buy (call) or sell (put) at strike $K$ by expiration $T$.
- ☐ **Call Payoff:** $\max(S_T - K, 0)$ | **Put Payoff:** $\max(K - S_T, 0)$
- ☐ **Call Profit:** $\max(S_T - K, 0) - C$ | **Put Profit:** $\max(K - S_T, 0) - P$
- ☐ **Call Breakeven:** $K + C$ | **Put Breakeven:** $K - P$
- ☐ **Buyer's Max Loss = Premium Paid (always).**
- ☐ **Call Writer's Loss = Unlimited; Put Writer's Loss = Substantial (capped near $K$).**
- ☐ **Premium = Intrinsic Value + Time Value.**
- ☐ **Intrinsic Value (Call) = $\max(S - K, 0)$**, **Intrinsic Value (Put) = $\max(K - S, 0)$.**
- ☐ **Time Value ≥ 0 always; zero only at expiration for ATM/OTM options.**
- ☐ **Put-Call Parity:** $C + Ke^{-rT} = P + S$ (European, no dividends).
- ☐ **Moneyness:** ITM / ATM / OTM — opposite inequalities for calls vs. puts.
- ☐ **Options are derivatives; they confer NO voting, dividend, or ownership rights.**
- ☐ **Leverage:** Small premium controls large notional exposure.
- ☐ **Time Decay (Theta):** Erodes option value as $T$ approaches.
- ☐ **Upper Bound (Call): $C \leq S$**; **Lower Bound (Call): $C \geq \max(S - K, 0)$.**
- ☐ **Upper Bound (Put): $P \leq K$**; **Lower Bound (Put): $P \geq \max(K - S, 0)$.**
- ☐ **Greeks to remember for Part B:** $\theta$ (time), $\Delta$ (delta, price sensitivity), $\Gamma$ (gamma, convexity), $\nu$ (vega, volatility), $\rho$ (rho, interest rate).
- ☐ **Memorize the SUV / car-dealer analogy** for viva voce or interview questions.
- ☐ **Always verify put-call parity** by checking both sides are equal in numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nature of Option Values
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Nature of Option Values # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of financial derivatives under the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, the **Nature of Option Values** refers to the systematic decomposition of an option's market price (premium) into two fundamental economic components: **Intrinsic Value** and **Time Value**. An option's total premium is the sum of these two values, representing the equilibrium price at which the option trades in a competitive market. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > The **Option Premium (C or P)** is the price paid by the buyer to the seller (writer) for acquiring the rights embedded in the option contract. This premium is mathematically expressed as: > $$\text{Option Premium} = \text{Intrinsic Value} + \text{Time Value}$$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **Real-World Analogy — The Rain Insurance Policy:** Imagine you are planning an outdoor wedding in Kochi in June (monsoon season). You approach an insurance company and buy a "Rain Insurance" policy that pays you ₹1,00,000 if it rains on your wedding day. You pay a premium of ₹15,000 upfront. - **Intrinsic Value** = The amount you are *certain* to receive right now if the event were to happen today. If today is sunny, this is ₹0. If it's already pouring outside, the policy is worth exactly ₹1,00,000. - **Time Value** = The *extra* amount you pay because there's still uncertainty (it *might* rain on the actual day). The further away the wedding day, the higher this uncertainty component. - **Total Premium (₹15,000)** = Intrinsic Value + Time Value. An **option** works the same way! It's a "contract insurance" giving you the *right* (not obligation) to buy or sell a stock at a fixed price in the future. The price you pay for this right is split between *what it's worth right now* (intrinsic) and *what it might be worth in the future* (time value). ## 1.3 Types of Option Contracts (Foundation Recap) > [!NOTE] > Before diving deeper, recall the two foundational option types: > - **Call Option (C):** Gives the holder the right to **BUY** the underlying asset at the strike price. > - **Put Option (P):** Gives the holder the right to **SELL** the underlying asset at the strike price. > > The premium formulas differ based on type: > $$\text{Call Premium } (C) = \max(S - K, 0) + \text{Time Value}$$ > $$\text{Put Premium } (P) = \max(K - S, 0) + \text{Time Value}$$ > where **S** = Current Spot Price of underlying and **K** = Strike Price. ## 1.4 Key Terminology Standardised | Term | Symbol | Standard Description | |------|--------|----------------------| | Current Stock Price | **S** | The prevailing market price of the underlying asset | | Strike / Exercise Price | **K** | The pre-agreed price at which the option can be exercised | | Option Premium | **C / P** | Market price of the call/put option | | Intrinsic Value | **IV** | The immediate exercise value of the option | | Time Value | **TV** | Premium attributable to remaining time until expiry | | Time to Expiration | **T** | Time (in years) remaining until option maturity | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Option Value ### 2.1.1 Intrinsic Value (IV) The **Intrinsic Value** is the *immediate* gain the option holder would realize if the option were exercised at this very moment. It is a *deterministic* function of the current stock price and the strike price. **Why it exists:** An option has intrinsic value only when exercising it today would generate a positive cash flow. If exercising today yields no profit, an economically rational trader would not exercise, making the intrinsic value zero. **How it is computed:** $$\text{IV}_{\text{Call}} = \max(S - K,\ 0)$$ $$\text{IV}_{\text{Put}} = \max(K - S,\ 0)$$ The `max(·, 0)` operator is critical — it represents the **rational exercise boundary**. An option holder will *never* exercise at a loss, so intrinsic value is floored at zero. ### 2.1.2 Time Value (TV) The **Time Value** is the portion of the option premium *above and beyond* intrinsic value. It exists because of the **uncertainty** about the future price of the underlying asset. **Why it exists:** The further the expiration date, the greater the possibility that the option will finish in-the-money. This uncertainty has a price — and that price is the time value. **How it is computed:** $$\text{Time Value} = \text{Option Premium} - \text{Intrinsic Value}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** Time value is **always ≥ 0**. An option will never trade below its intrinsic value in an efficient market, because arbitrageurs would immediately buy it and exercise it for a risk-free profit. ## 2.2 Concept of "Moneyness" Moneyness classifies an option based on the relationship between **S** (spot) and **K** (strike). This is a **high-frequency** KTU examination topic. | Classification | Call Option Condition | Put Option Condition | Intrinsic Value | |----------------|----------------------|----------------------|-----------------| | **In-The-Money (ITM)** | S > K | S < K | Positive | | **At-The-Money (ATM)** | S ≈ K | S ≈ K | ≈ 0 | | **Out-of-The-Money (OTM)** | S < K | S > K | Zero | > [!NOTE] > **Memory Trick for KTU Exams:** "**ITM = Intrinsic value Today is Money (positive)**". ATM options have *near-zero* intrinsic value, and OTM options have *exactly zero* intrinsic value. ## 2.3 Behaviour of Option Values as Spot Price Changes For a **Call Option** (Strike = K): - When **S < K** (OTM): IV = 0, Total Premium = Time Value only - When **S = K** (ATM): IV = 0, Total Premium = Time Value only (but maximum time value) - When **S > K** (ITM): IV = S − K, Total Premium = (S − K) + Time Value For a **Put Option** (Strike = K): - When **S > K** (OTM): IV = 0, Total Premium = Time Value only - When **S = K** (ATM): IV = 0, Total Premium = Time Value only - When **S < K** (ITM): IV = K − S, Total Premium = (K − S) + Time Value ## 2.4 The Six Determinants of Option Value > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Frequently Tested Concept:** The Black-Scholes framework identifies six variables that influence option premiums. The examiner expects students to know the **direction** of impact (positive/negative) for each. | # | Variable | Effect on Call Premium | Effect on Put Premium | |---|----------|----------------------|----------------------| | 1 | **Increase in Spot Price (S)** | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | | 2 | **Increase in Strike Price (K)** | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases | | 3 | **Increase in Time to Expiry (T)** | ↑ Increases (American) | ↑ Increases (American) | | 4 | **Increase in Volatility (σ)** | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | | 5 | **Increase in Risk-Free Rate (r)** | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | | 6 | **Increase in Dividends** | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases | > [!TIP] > **Valuation Note:** Volatility is the *only* variable that *increases the value of both calls and puts*. This is why options are popular hedging tools during turbulent markets. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Expression | Boundary Condition | |---------|----------------------|---------------------| | Call Intrinsic Value | $\max(S - K,\ 0)$ | $\geq 0$ always | | Put Intrinsic Value | $\max(K - S,\ 0)$ | $\geq 0$ always | | Total Call Premium (C) | $C = \max(S - K,\ 0) + TV$ | $C \geq \max(S-K,0)$ | | Total Put Premium (P) | $P = \max(K - S,\ 0) + TV$ | $P \geq \max(K-S,0)$ | | Time Value Extraction | $TV = C - \max(S - K,\ 0)$ | $TV \geq 0$ | | Lower Bound (European Call, no divs) | $C \geq \max(S - K e^{-rT},\ 0)$ | **No negative time value** | | Lower Bound (European Put, no divs) | $P \geq \max(K e^{-rT} - S,\ 0)$ | **No negative time value** | | Put-Call Parity | $C + K e^{-rT} = P + S$ | European options only | ## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Finance > [!NOTE] > Although this is a finance course, the concepts connect to engineering via: > - **Risk Management in Infrastructure Projects:** Construction firms use real options to value the *option to abandon* or *expand* a project. > - **Energy Sector Hedging:** Power-grid engineers use option pricing models (Black-Scholes) to value generation flexibility. > - **R&D Investment Decisions:** Tech firms treat R&D projects as "options on future products" — time value represents the value of waiting for more information. > - **Startup Valuation:** Early-stage startups are valued using option-pricing logic — the equity is essentially a call option on the firm's assets. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Call Option Payoff and Profit Functions Let us derive the **profit diagrams** for the four basic option positions. This is a **regularly repeated 14-mark KTU question**. ### 3.1.1 Long Call (Buying a Call) A long call holder pays premium **C** upfront and gains the right to buy at strike **K**. **Step 1: Payoff at expiration T** The holder will exercise only if it is profitable (i.e., $S_T > K$). Therefore: $$ \text{Payoff}_{\text{Long Call}} = \begin{cases} S_T - K & \text{if } S_T > K \\ 0 & \text{if } S_T \leq K \end{cases} $$ This can be compactly written as: $$\text{Payoff}_{\text{Long Call}} = \max(S_T - K,\ 0)$$ **Step 2: Profit = Payoff − Premium Paid** $$\text{Profit}_{\text{Long Call}} = \max(S_T - K,\ 0) - C$$ **Step 3: Breakeven Point** Breakeven occurs when Profit = 0: $$\max(S_T - K,\ 0) - C = 0$$ For the option to be exercised ($S_T > K$): $$S_T - K = C \implies S_T^{\text{BE}} = K + C$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** A long call has *limited loss* (max loss = C) and *unlimited profit potential*. The breakeven is K + C. ### 3.1.2 Short Call (Writing/Selling a Call) The seller receives premium **C** but has the obligation to deliver at **K** if exercised. **Step 1: Payoff for Short Call** $$\text{Payoff}_{\text{Short Call}} = -\max(S_T - K,\ 0)$$ **Step 2: Profit for Short Call** $$\text{Profit}_{\text{Short Call}} = C - \max(S_T - K,\ 0)$$ **Step 3: Breakeven Point** $$C = \max(S_T - K,\ 0) \implies S_T^{\text{BE}} = K + C$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Short call has *limited profit* (max = C) and *unlimited loss potential* (as $S_T \to \infty$). This is why uncovered call writing is extremely risky. ### 3.1.3 Long Put (Buying a Put) The holder pays premium **P** and gains the right to *sell* at **K**. **Step 1: Payoff** $$\text{Payoff}_{\text{Long Put}} = \max(K - S_T,\ 0)$$ **Step 2: Profit** $$\text{Profit}_{\text{Long Put}} = \max(K - S_T,\ 0) - P$$ **Step 3: Breakeven** $$K - S_T = P \implies S_T^{\text{BE}} = K - P$$ ### 3.1.4 Short Put (Writing a Put) **Step 1: Payoff** $$\text{Payoff}_{\text{Short Put}} = -\max(K - S_T,\ 0)$$ **Step 2: Profit** $$\text{Profit}_{\text{Short Put}} = P - \max(K - S_T,\ 0)$$ **Step 3: Breakeven** $$P = K - S_T \implies S_T^{\text{BE}} = K - P$$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) > **Problem:** > A call option on a stock has a strike price **K = ₹100** and a current premium **C = ₹15**. The current stock price **S = ₹110**. Calculate: > (a) Intrinsic Value > (b) Time Value > (c) Determine if the option is ITM, ATM, or OTM. > (d) What is the breakeven stock price at expiration? ### Solution **Given:** - $S = 110$, $K = 100$, $C = 15$ **Step (a): Intrinsic Value of Call** Since this is a call option, we apply: $$IV_{\text{Call}} = \max(S - K,\ 0)$$ Substituting the values: $$IV_{\text{Call}} = \max(110 - 100,\ 0) = \max(10,\ 0) = ₹10$$ **Step (b): Time Value of Option** Using the decomposition formula: $$TV = C - IV = 15 - 10 = ₹5$$ **Step (c): Moneyness Classification** Since $S = 110 > K = 100$, the call option is **In-The-Money (ITM)**. The intrinsic value is positive (₹10), confirming ITM status. **Step (d): Breakeven Price** For a long call: $$S_T^{\text{BE}} = K + C = 100 + 15 = ₹115$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Verification:** If at expiration $S_T = ₹115$: > $$\text{Profit} = \max(115 - 100,\ 0) - 15 = 15 - 15 = 0 \checkmark$$ > If $S_T = ₹120$: Profit = $20 - 15 = +₹5$ (profit). > If $S_T = ₹90$: Profit = $0 - 15 = -₹15$ (maximum loss, equals premium). ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Payoff Table | Position | Maximum Profit | Maximum Loss | Breakeven | |----------|---------------|--------------|-----------| | **Long Call** | **Unlimited** | Limited (Premium C) | $K + C$ | | **Short Call** | Limited (Premium C) | **Unlimited** | $K + C$ | | **Long Put** | $K - P$ (when $S_T = 0$) | Limited (Premium P) | $K - P$ | | **Short Put** | Limited (Premium P) | $K - P$ (when $S_T = 0$) | $K - P$ | ## 3.4 Boundary Conditions for Option Premiums (Derivation Outline) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Module 4 Expected Question:** "Derive the lower bound of a European call option." **Step 1:** Consider two portfolios at time $t = 0$: - **Portfolio A:** One European call option + cash equal to $K e^{-rT}$ - **Portfolio B:** One share of stock (price S) **Step 2:** At expiration T: - If $S_T > K$: Call is exercised. Portfolio A pays $S_T - K + K = S_T$. Portfolio B pays $S_T$. **Equal.** - If $S_T \leq K$: Call expires worthless. Portfolio A pays $K$. Portfolio B pays $S_T \leq K$. **Portfolio A ≥ Portfolio B.** **Step 3:** Since Portfolio A dominates Portfolio B at T, by **no-arbitrage**, Portfolio A must cost at least as much today: $$C + K e^{-rT} \geq S$$ Rearranging: $$C \geq S - K e^{-rT}$$ Combining with the non-negativity of the call (you would never pay negative premium): $$C \geq \max(S - K e^{-rT},\ 0)$$ This is the **lower bound for a European call option on a non-dividend-paying stock**. Similarly for a European put: $$P \geq \max(K e^{-rT} - S,\ 0)$$ --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Decomposition of Option Value (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Option Premium C or P] --> B{Decomposition} B --> C1[Intrinsic Value IV] B --> C2[Time Value TV] C1 --> D1[max S minus K, 0 for Call] C1 --> D2[max K minus S, 0 for Put] C2 --> E1[Function of T] C2 --> E2[Function of volatility sigma] C2 --> E3[Function of r and dividends] D1 --> F1{ITM check} D1 --> F2{ATM check} D1 --> F3{OTM check} F1 --> G1[IV greater than 0] F2 --> G2[IV approx 0] F3 --> G3[IV equals 0] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Payoff Architecture: Four Basic Option Positions ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LONG_CALL [Long Call Position] LC1[Premium Paid: minus C] --> LC2[Payoff: max S minus K, 0] LC2 --> LC3[Profit: max S minus K, 0 minus C] LC3 --> LC4[Breakeven: K plus C] end subgraph SHORT_CALL [Short Call Position] SC1[Premium Received: plus C] --> SC2[Payoff: minus max S minus K, 0] SC2 --> SC3[Profit: C minus max S minus K, 0] SC3 --> SC4[Breakeven: K plus C] end subgraph LONG_PUT [Long Put Position] LP1[Premium Paid: minus P] --> LP2[Payoff: max K minus S, 0] LP2 --> LP3[Profit: max K minus S, 0 minus P] LP3 --> LP4[Breakeven: K minus P] end subgraph SHORT_PUT [Short Put Position] SP1[Premium Received: plus P] --> SP2[Payoff: minus max K minus S, 0] SP2 --> SP3[Profit: P minus max K minus S, 0] SP3 --> SP4[Breakeven: K minus P] end ``` ## 4.3 Moneyness Decision Tree (Flowchart Logic) ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Start: Compare S and K] --> CHECK{Is S greater than K} CHECK -->|Yes| CALL1[For Call: ITM, IV = S minus K] CHECK -->|Yes| PUT1[For Put: OTM, IV = 0] CHECK -->|No| CALL2[For Call: OTM, IV = 0] CHECK -->|No| PUT2[For Put: ITM, IV = K minus S] CHECK -->|Equal| CALL3[For Call: ATM, IV approx 0] CHECK -->|Equal| PUT3[For Put: ATM, IV approx 0] CALL1 --> RESULT[Premium = IV + Time Value] CALL2 --> RESULT CALL3 --> RESULT PUT1 --> RESULT PUT2 --> RESULT PUT3 --> RESULT RESULT --> KEY[Time Value is Maximum at ATM] style START fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style CHECK fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style RESULT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style KEY fill:#FF69B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Option Value Behaviour as Spot Price Varies (Block Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Stock Price S increases from 0 to infinity] --> B{S relative to K} B -->|Deep OTM| C[Call Premium equals Time Value only
Put Premium approaches 0] B -->|ATM region| D[Call Premium equals max Time Value
Put Premium equals max Time Value] B -->|Deep ITM| E[Call Premium equals S minus K plus small TV
Put Premium approaches K minus S] B -->|S very high| F[Call Premium approx S minus K
Intrinsic dominates] B -->|S approaches 0| G[Put Premium approx K
Intrinsic dominates] style A fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **"Distinguish between Intrinsic Value and Time Value of an option with suitable examples."** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Intrinsic Value (IV) | Time Value (TV) | |--------|---------------------|-----------------| | **Definition** | The immediate exercise value if exercised today | The premium above intrinsic value | | **Formula (Call)** | $\max(S - K,\ 0)$ | $C - \max(S - K,\ 0)$ | | **Formula (Put)** | $\max(K - S,\ 0)$ | $P - \max(K - S,\ 0)$ | | **Sign** | Always $\geq 0$ | Always $\geq 0$ | | **Example** | If $S = 110$, $K = 100$: IV = ₹10 | If premium = ₹15, TV = ₹5 | The **Intrinsic Value** represents the *realised* gain from immediate exercise, while the **Time Value** represents the *potential* gain from price movements before expiration. --- ### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **"Explain the concept of 'Moneyness' of an option with reference to call and put options."** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Moneyness** describes the relationship between the current stock price (S) and strike price (K), and determines whether an option has positive intrinsic value. **For a Call Option:** - **In-The-Money (ITM):** $S > K$ — Intrinsic Value = $S - K$ - **At-The-Money (ATM):** $S = K$ — Intrinsic Value $\approx 0$ - **Out-of-The-Money (OTM):** $S < K$ — Intrinsic Value = 0 **For a Put Option:** - **In-The-Money (ITM):** $S < K$ — Intrinsic Value = $K - S$ - **At-The-Money (ATM):** $S = K$ — Intrinsic Value $\approx 0$ - **Out-of-The-Money (OTM):** $S > K$ — Intrinsic Value = 0 > [!NOTE] > Moneyness is a *static* measure at a point in time, while the *option value* (premium) changes continuously as S changes. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice > **Note:** KTU 2024 ESE pattern requires Module Internal Choice. Both alternatives below are equivalent 14-mark full questions. ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **"Explain the nature of option values in detail. With the help of suitable payoff diagrams, describe the profit and loss profile of:** **(a) A long call position** **(b) A long put position** **Also determine the break-even points for each."** #### Model Solution #### **(a) Long Call Position (7 Marks)** **Concept:** A long call position is created by *buying* a call option. The holder pays a premium **C** and obtains the right (not obligation) to purchase the underlying asset at strike price **K** on expiration. **Step 1: Payoff at Expiration** The holder will exercise only when profitable, i.e., $S_T > K$: $$\text{Payoff}_{\text{Long Call}} = \max(S_T - K,\ 0)$$ **Step 2: Profit Function** Subtracting the premium paid: $$\text{Profit}_{\text{Long Call}} = \max(S_T - K,\ 0) - C$$ **Step 3: Maximum Loss and Maximum Profit** - **Maximum Loss** = C (occurs when $S_T \leq K$, the option expires worthless) - **Maximum Profit** = Unlimited (as $S_T \to \infty$) **Step 4: Breakeven Point** Setting profit to zero: $$\max(S_T - K,\ 0) - C = 0 \implies S_T^{\text{BE}} = K + C$$ **Step 5: Payoff Diagram Description** ``` Profit | / | / | / | / 0|-------X-----------------> S_T | K+C | / | / | / (slope = 1) | / | / -C|/ +-----------------> 0 K K+C ``` **Conclusion (1 Mark):** Long call is a *bullish* strategy with limited risk (₹C) and unlimited upside. #### **(b) Long Put Position (7 Marks)** **Concept:** A long put is created by *buying* a put option. The holder pays premium **P** and gains the right to sell at strike **K**. **Step 1: Payoff at Expiration** The holder exercises when $S_T < K$: $$\text{Payoff}_{\text{Long Put}} = \max(K - S_T,\ 0)$$ **Step 2: Profit Function** $$\text{Profit}_{\text{Long Put}} = \max(K - S_T,\ 0) - P$$ **Step 3: Maximum Loss and Maximum Profit** - **Maximum Loss** = P (occurs when $S_T \geq K$) - **Maximum Profit** = $K - P$ (occurs when $S_T \to 0$) **Step 4: Breakeven Point** $$K - S_T = P \implies S_T^{\text{BE}} = K - P$$ **Step 5: Payoff Diagram Description** ``` Profit K-P|\ \ \ \ \ \ 0--------X-----------------> S_T K-P | -P ------| (max loss) | +-----------------> 0 K-P K ``` **Conclusion (1 Mark):** Long put is a *bearish* hedging strategy — the maximum loss is limited to the premium paid, while profit caps at $K - P$ when the stock falls to zero. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **"With reference to financial options:** **(a) Define Intrinsic Value and Time Value. Compute these for a call option with Strike Price = ₹200, Spot Price = ₹230, and Premium = ₹40.** **(b) What is 'Moneyness'? Explain the three categories with their intrinsic value formulas for both call and put options. Also state the lower bound for a European call option."** #### Model Solution #### **(a) Intrinsic Value, Time Value & Numerical Computation (7 Marks)** **Definition (2 Marks):** **Intrinsic Value (IV):** The intrinsic value of an option is the *gain that would be realised* if the option were exercised immediately at the current market price. It is a deterministic function of spot price **S** and strike price **K**. **Time Value (TV):** Time value is the *additional premium* that an option commands beyond its intrinsic value, attributable to the remaining time until expiration and the uncertainty of future price movements. **Step-by-step Computation (5 Marks):** **Given:** $S = ₹230$, $K = ₹200$, $C = ₹40$ **Step 1: Identify Option Type & Moneyness** Since $S = 230 > K = 200$, the call option is **In-The-Money (ITM)**. **Step 2: Calculate Intrinsic Value** $$IV_{\text{Call}} = \max(S - K,\ 0) = \max(230 - 200,\ 0) = \max(30,\ 0) = ₹30$$ **Step 3: Calculate Time Value** $$TV = C - IV = 40 - 30 = ₹10$$ **Step 4: Verification** $$C = IV + TV = 30 + 10 = ₹40 \checkmark$$ **Step 5: Interpretation** - The option has ₹30 of *certain* value (immediate exercise). - The remaining ₹10 is paid for the *possibility* that the stock may rise further before expiry. --- #### **(b) Moneyness and Lower Bound (7 Marks)** **Definition of Moneyness (2 Marks):** **Moneyness** is a classification of options based on the relationship between the spot price **S** and the strike price **K**. It indicates whether an option has intrinsic value (ITM), zero intrinsic value (OTM), or is at the boundary (ATM). **Three Categories with Formulas (4 Marks):** | Category | Condition (Call) | Condition (Put) | IV Call | IV Put | |----------|------------------|-----------------|---------|--------| | **ITM** | $S > K$ | $S < K$ | $S - K$ | $K - S$ | | **ATM** | $S = K$ | $S = K$ | $0$ | $0$ | | **OTM** | $S < K$ | $S > K$ | $0$ | $0$ | **Lower Bound for European Call (1 Mark):** For a European call option on a non-dividend-paying stock: $$C \geq \max(S - K e^{-rT},\ 0)$$ This ensures no arbitrage — the call must be worth at least the *present value* of the exercise benefit. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Forgetting the `max(·, 0)`:** Many students write $IV = S - K$ for calls, even when $S < K$. This is **wrong** because exercising an out-of-the-money option would incur a loss, and rational agents never do this. Always use $\max(S - K,\ 0)$. > > 2. **Confusing profit with payoff:** *Payoff* ignores the premium paid; *Profit* includes it. KTU exam questions explicitly ask for profit functions, and skipping the premium term costs 2-3 marks. > > 3. **Mismeasuring Moneyness for Puts:** A common error is stating that puts are ITM when $S > K$. **This is wrong for puts** — puts are ITM when $S < K$ (the holder benefits from selling high when the market is low). > > 4. **Ignoring Time Value Sign:** Time value is **always ≥ 0**. If your calculation gives a negative TV, you've made a sign error. > > 5. **Confusing American vs. European:** American options can be exercised anytime before expiration (so they always have ≥ European value). KTU 2024 Module 4 focuses mostly on **European** options for valuation formulas. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:** - ☐ **Option Premium = Intrinsic Value + Time Value** — this is the central identity. - ☐ **Call IV** $= \max(S - K,\ 0)$ and **Put IV** $= \max(K - S,\ 0)$ — never negative. - ☐ **Time Value** $= \text{Premium} - \text{Intrinsic Value} \geq 0$ — never negative in efficient markets. - ☐ **Moneyness ITM** — Calls: $S > K$ | Puts: $S < K$ (opposite conditions!). - ☐ **ATM options have maximum Time Value** because uncertainty is highest when $S \approx K$. - ☐ **Long Call:** Max Loss = C, Max Profit = Unlimited, Breakeven = $K + C$. - ☐ **Long Put:** Max Loss = P, Max Profit = $K - P$, Breakeven = $K - P$. - ☐ **Short Call:** Max Profit = C, Max Loss = **Unlimited** (dangerous!). - ☐ **Short Put:** Max Profit = P, Max Loss = $K - P$ (occurs at $S_T = 0$). - ☐ **Volatility (σ)** is the only variable that *increases* both call and put values. - ☐ **Lower Bound (European Call, no divs):** $C \geq \max(S - K e^{-rT},\ 0)$. - ☐ **Lower Bound (European Put, no divs):** $P \geq \max(K e^{-rT} - S,\ 0)$. - ☐ **Put-Call Parity:** $C + K e^{-rT} = P + S$ (European options, no dividends). - ☐ **Time value decays to zero** at expiration (a key reason for early exercise considerations). - ☐ **At expiration, an option's value = its intrinsic value only** (TV = 0 at T). --- **End of Module 4 Notes — Nature of Option Values** *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) examination standards and Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels (L1–L4).*

topics.title: Put-Call Parity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Put-Call Parity # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Put-Call Parity (PCP)** is a foundational no-arbitrage principle in options pricing theory that establishes a deterministic, equilibrium relationship between the prices of European call options and European put options written on the same underlying asset, with identical strike price and expiration date. Mathematically, Put-Call Parity states: $$C + \frac{X}{(1+r)^T} = P + S_0$$ For continuous compounding, this becomes: $$C + X \cdot e^{-rT} = P + S_0$$ Where: - **C** = Price (premium) of the European **Call** option - **P** = Price (premium) of the European **Put** option - **S₀** = Current spot price of the underlying stock/asset - **X** (or **K**) = Strike (exercise) price - **r** = Risk-free rate of interest (annualized) - **T** = Time to expiration (in years) - **e^(-rT)** = Continuous discount factor > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Put-Call Parity is a **CO2 (Apply)** and **CO3 (Analyze)** level topic. Students must be able to (a) state the parity equation, (b) derive it using a two-portfolio replication argument, and (c) compute any of the four variables given the other three. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Twin Portfolios" Story Imagine you are at a marketplace, and a shopkeeper sells two items: 1. A **"Rain Insurance Policy"** (Put option) — pays you if it rains. 2. A **"Umbrella Bundle"** (Call option + a fixed deposit of money) — pays you if it doesn't rain. The clever observation: **both items give you the exact same end-state value** regardless of whether it rains or shines, IF you combine the right things. - **Portfolio A (Umbrella side):** Buy a Call Option *and* lock away enough cash today to pay the strike price tomorrow. Total cost today = **C + PV(X)**. - **Portfolio B (Insurance side):** Buy a Put Option *and* own the stock today. Total cost today = **P + S₀**. No matter what happens to the stock price at expiration, both portfolios are worth the **same maximum** of (S_T, X). Since they produce identical future payoffs, they **must** cost the same today — otherwise a risk-free arbitrage profit would exist. > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Takeaway:** Put-Call Parity is essentially the financial-market equivalent of the **law of one price**: two ways of constructing the same payoff must have the same price. Violating it is like finding a ₹100 note selling for ₹50 — a free arbitrage opportunity that the market immediately closes. --- ## 1.3 Why Put-Call Parity Matters in Finance Put-Call Parity is not merely an academic curiosity; it is the **engine** behind: | Application | Real-World Use | |---|---| | **Arbitrage Detection** | Identifying mispriced options on exchanges | | **Synthetic Options** | Replicating puts/calls using other instruments | | **Risk Management** | Hedging exposure without directly trading the option | | **Valuation** | Pricing American options, exotics, and dividend-paying stocks | | **Capital Markets** | Reverse-engineering implied volatility | > [!TIP] > The **risk-free rate (r)** typically refers to the yield on government Treasury bills or central-bank repo rates. For KTU numerical problems, the rate is assumed constant, continuously compounded unless otherwise stated. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Underlying Logic — A Step-by-Step Argument ### Step 1: Construct Two Portfolios with Identical Terminal Payoffs **Portfolio A — Protective Cash + Call:** - Long position in one European call option (cost = **C**) - Long position in a zero-coupon bond maturing at T with face value **X** (cost = **PV(X) = X·e^(-rT)**) - Total initial cost: $A_0 = C + X \cdot e^{-rT}$ **Portfolio B — Protective Put + Stock:** - Long position in one European put option (cost = **P**) - Long position in one share of the underlying stock (cost = **S₀**) - Total initial cost: $B_0 = P + S_0$ --- ### Step 2: Evaluate Terminal Payoff at Expiration (T) The terminal value of each portfolio depends on whether the stock ends **above** or **below** the strike. **Case 1: S_T > X (Stock finishes in-the-money for call holder)** Portfolio A payoff at T: $$A_T = \max(S_T - X, 0) + X = S_T - X + X = S_T$$ Portfolio B payoff at T: $$B_T = \max(X - S_T, 0) + S_T = 0 + S_T = S_T$$ **Case 2: S_T ≤ X (Stock finishes out-of-the-money for call holder)** Portfolio A payoff at T: $$A_T = \max(S_T - X, 0) + X = 0 + X = X$$ Portfolio B payoff at T: $$B_T = \max(X - S_T, 0) + S_T = (X - S_T) + S_T = X$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** In *both* terminal scenarios, $A_T = B_T = \max(S_T, X)$. The two portfolios are **perfect economic substitutes** at maturity. --- ### Step 3: Apply the Law of One Price (No-Arbitrage) If two portfolios yield the same payoff at the same future time under **every possible state of the world**, they must have identical present-day prices. Otherwise, an arbitrageur could: - **Buy the cheaper portfolio**, **short the costlier one**, and pocket a **risk-free profit** at time zero that grows risk-free until maturity (when both portfolios cancel out exactly). Therefore: $$C + X \cdot e^{-rT} = P + S_0$$ --- ## 2.2 Boundary Conditions & Validity Constraints The parity relationship holds **strictly** under the following assumptions (standard Black-Scholes framework): | Assumption | Description | |---|---| | **European Exercise** | Options exercisable only at expiration T | | **No Dividends** | Underlying stock pays no dividends during option life | | **Frictionless Markets** | No transaction costs, bid-ask spreads, or taxes | | **Continuous Trading** | Portfolio can be rebalanced continuously | | **Constant Risk-Free Rate** | Borrowing and lending rate **r** is identical and deterministic | | **No Early Exercise Premium** | American options deviate due to early-exercise optionality | > [!WARNING] > For **American options**, strict equality does *not* hold. Instead, inequality bounds apply: > $$S_0 - X \le C - P \le S_0 - X \cdot e^{-rT}$$ --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Quick Reference) | # | Formula | Description | |---|---|---| | 1 | $C + X \cdot e^{-rT} = P + S_0$ | **Put-Call Parity (continuous compounding)** | | 2 | $C + \dfrac{X}{(1+r)^T} = P + S_0$ | **Put-Call Parity (discrete compounding)** | | 3 | $C - P = S_0 - X \cdot e^{-rT}$ | **Rearranged form — call minus put equals forward value** | | 4 | $C = P + S_0 - X \cdot e^{-rT}$ | **Synthetic Call** (replicate a call using put + stock − bond) | | 5 | $P = C + X \cdot e^{-rT} - S_0$ | **Synthetic Put** (replicate a put using call + bond − stock) | | 6 | $\text{Forward Price } F_0 = S_0 \cdot e^{rT}$ | Forward price from spot using no-arbitrage | | 7 | $\text{Upper Bound: } C \le S_0$ | Call price cannot exceed spot price | | 8 | $\text{Lower Bound: } C \ge \max(S_0 - X \cdot e^{-rT}, 0)$ | Call must be worth at least intrinsic discounted value | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Financial Utility Although introduced in finance, Put-Call Parity is the conceptual backbone of: - **Real Options Analysis in Engineering:** Evaluating R&D projects, patent abandonment decisions, infrastructure investment deferrals. - **Corporate Finance:** Pricing convertible bonds and employee stock options (ESOPs). - **Algorithmic Trading:** Statistical-arbitrage engines detect and exploit parity violations in milliseconds. - **Insurance & Reinsurance:** Catastrophe bonds (CAT bonds) are essentially exotic put options on disasters. > [!TIP] > For KTU 2024 ESE problems, always **draw the payoff table** (two columns × two rows) before writing the parity equation. Examiners reward the *tabulation logic*, not just the formula. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Parity Equation We begin with a **formal no-arbitrage proof** using replication. ### Setup Let: - $S_0$ = Current stock price - $X$ = Strike price - $T$ = Time to expiration (years) - $r$ = Constant risk-free rate - $C$ = European call price - $P$ = European put price ### Step 1: Define Portfolio A Portfolio A consists of: - One European call option (long) - A zero-coupon risk-free bond with face value $X$ maturing at $T$ (long) Initial cost of Portfolio A at time $t = 0$: $$A_0 = C + \underbrace{X \cdot e^{-rT}}_{\text{PV of bond}}$$ ### Step 2: Define Portfolio B Portfolio B consists of: - One European put option (long) - One share of the underlying stock (long) Initial cost of Portfolio B at time $t = 0$: $$B_0 = P + S_0$$ ### Step 3: Compute Terminal Payoff — State 1 Assume the stock price at expiration satisfies $S_T > X$ (stock has risen above strike). Terminal payoff of Portfolio A: $$ \begin{aligned} A_T &= \max(S_T - X, 0) + X \\ &= (S_T - X) + X \\ &= S_T \end{aligned} $$ Terminal payoff of Portfolio B: $$ \begin{aligned} B_T &= \max(X - S_T, 0) + S_T \\ &= 0 + S_T \\ &= S_T \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4: Compute Terminal Payoff — State 2 Assume the stock price at expiration satisfies $S_T \le X$ (stock has fallen to or below strike). Terminal payoff of Portfolio A: $$ \begin{aligned} A_T &= \max(S_T - X, 0) + X \\ &= 0 + X \\ &= X \end{aligned} $$ Terminal payoff of Portfolio B: $$ \begin{aligned} B_T &= \max(X - S_T, 0) + S_T \\ &= (X - S_T) + S_T \\ &= X \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5: Unified Terminal Payoff Combining both states: $$A_T = B_T = \max(S_T, X) \quad \forall \, S_T \ge 0$$ ### Step 6: Apply the No-Arbitrage Principle If $A_0 \neq B_0$, a risk-free profit exists. Therefore: $$A_0 = B_0$$ Substituting: $$ \begin{aligned} C + X \cdot e^{-rT} &= P + S_0 \\ \therefore \quad C - P &= S_0 - X \cdot e^{-rT} \end{aligned} $$ **This is the Put-Call Parity relation. Q.E.D.** --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 (Basic) > **Problem (KTU-Style):** A stock trades at **₹100** today. A European call with strike **₹105** and 6-month maturity is priced at **₹8**. The risk-free rate is **6%** per annum (continuous compounding). Find the price of the corresponding European put. **Solution:** Given: $S_0 = 100$, $X = 105$, $T = 0.5$, $r = 0.06$, $C = 8$. Step 1 — Compute the discount factor: $$e^{-rT} = e^{-0.06 \times 0.5} = e^{-0.03}$$ $$e^{-0.03} \approx 0.9704$$ Step 2 — Compute the present value of the strike: $$X \cdot e^{-rT} = 105 \times 0.9704 \approx 101.90$$ Step 3 — Apply Put-Call Parity to solve for $P$: $$ \begin{aligned} P &= C + X \cdot e^{-rT} - S_0 \\ &= 8 + 101.90 - 100 \\ &= 9.90 \end{aligned} $$ **∴ The European put price = ₹9.90** --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 (Arbitrage Detection) > **Problem:** Stock $S_0$ = ₹50, Strike $X$ = ₹50, $T$ = 1 year, $r$ = 10%, Call price ₹6, Put price ₹4. Determine whether an arbitrage opportunity exists. **Step 1 — Theoretical put price via Parity:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{theoretical}} &= C + X \cdot e^{-rT} - S_0 \\ &= 6 + 50 \cdot e^{-0.10} - 50 \\ &= 6 + 50 \cdot 0.9048 - 50 \\ &= 6 + 45.24 - 50 \\ &= 1.24 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compare with market price:** Market put price = ₹4.00, but theoretical price = ₹1.24. Since $P_{\text{market}} > P_{\text{theoretical}}$, the put is **overpriced**. **Step 3 — Construct arbitrage strategy:** | Action | Cash Flow Today | Cash Flow at T | |---|---|---| | Sell (write) the put | **+₹4.00** | If $S_T < 50$: pay $(50 - S_T)$ | | Buy the call | **−₹6.00** | If $S_T > 50$: receive $(S_T - 50)$ | | Buy the stock | **−₹50.00** | Receive $S_T$ | | Borrow PV(50) at 10% | **+₹45.24** | Repay ₹50 | **Net cash flow today:** $$+4.00 - 6.00 - 50.00 + 45.24 = -6.76 \text{ (cost)}$$ Wait — let us invert. The put is overpriced, so **sell** it, and **buy** the replicating portfolio (call + stock − bond). **Corrected Arbitrage:** | Action | Cash Today | Cash at T | |---|---|---| | Sell put | **+₹4.00** | −max(50 − S_T, 0) | | Buy call | **−₹6.00** | +max(S_T − 50, 0) | | Buy stock | **−₹50.00** | +S_T | | Lend PV(50) | **−₹45.24** | +₹50.00 | **Net today:** $$+4.00 - 6.00 - 50.00 - 45.24 = -97.24$$ That cannot be right. The correct arbitrage is: **Sell put, buy the replicating portfolio of the put.** The replicating portfolio of a put is: **Long call + Long bond − Short stock.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Buy call} &: \text{Cost } = 6 \\ \text{Buy zero-coupon bond of face 50} &: \text{Cost } = 45.24 \\ \text{Short-sell stock} &: \text{Proceeds } = 50 \\ \text{Sell put} &: \text{Proceeds } = 4 \\ \hline \text{Net initial cash inflow} &= -6 - 45.24 + 50 + 4 = 2.76 \end{aligned} $$ At T, this combination's payoff **exactly cancels** the sold put's payoff under every state. Hence, **risk-free arbitrage profit = ₹2.76 per share** today. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 3 (Discrete Compounding) > **Problem:** $S_0$ = ₹200, $X$ = ₹210, $T$ = 3 months, $r$ = 8% p.a. compounded **quarterly**, $C$ = ₹15. Find $P$. **Step 1 — Identify compounding frequency:** Quarterly compounding → 1 quarter = $T = 3/12 = 0.25$ years, rate per quarter = $0.08/4 = 0.02$. **Step 2 — Discount factor (discrete):** $$\frac{1}{(1+r)^T} = \frac{1}{(1 + 0.02)^1} = \frac{1}{1.02} \approx 0.9804$$ **Step 3 — Present value of strike:** $$X \cdot (1+r)^{-T} = 210 \times 0.9804 \approx 205.88$$ **Step 4 — Solve for put:** $$ \begin{aligned} P &= C + X \cdot (1+r)^{-T} - S_0 \\ &= 15 + 205.88 - 200 \\ &= 20.88 \end{aligned} $$ **∴ Put price ≈ ₹20.88** --- ## 3.5 Sensitivity Matrix — How Parity Variables Interact | Variable | ↑ Increase Effect on C | ↑ Increase Effect on P | Parity Implication | |---|---|---|---| | **S₀ (Spot Price)** | Increases | Decreases | $C - P$ rises by $\Delta S_0$ | | **X (Strike)** | Decreases (slightly) | Increases | $C - P$ falls by $\Delta X \cdot e^{-rT}$ | | **r (Risk-free rate)** | Increases | Decreases | Higher discount → bigger call premium | | **T (Time to Maturity)** | Increases (uncertainty) | Increases (uncertainty) | Both rise, but $C - P$ moves toward $S_0$ | | **σ (Volatility)** | Increases | Increases | Equal absolute effect — $C - P$ unchanged | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight from the matrix:** The variable that **directly affects** the call-minus-put spread is the **spot price $S_0$** and the **discounted strike $X \cdot e^{-rT}$**. Volatility affects both options equally in absolute terms — this is a *deep* consequence of Put-Call Parity. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Put-Call Parity Architecture — The Twin Portfolio Block Diagram The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of Put-Call Parity, showing how two distinct portfolios collapse into a single equivalent payoff. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TODAY["Time T = 0 (Today)"] direction TB PORT_A["PORTFOLIO A
Long Call + Zero-Coupon Bond"] PORT_B["PORTFOLIO B
Long Put + Long Stock"] end subgraph FUTURE["Time T = Expiration"] direction TB STATE1["STATE 1: Stock Up
S sub T greater than X"] STATE2["STATE 2: Stock Down
S sub T less than X"] end subgraph PAYOFFS["Terminal Payoff Computation"] direction LR A_UP["A sub T = S sub T"] B_UP["B sub T = S sub T"] A_DN["A sub T = X"] B_DN["B sub T = X"] end subgraph ARB["No-Arbitrage Conclusion"] EQ["C + X times e power minus rT equals P + S sub 0"] PROF["If violated: Risk-Free Arbitrage Exists"] end PORT_A --> STATE1 PORT_A --> STATE2 PORT_B --> STATE1 PORT_B --> STATE2 STATE1 --> A_UP STATE1 --> B_UP STATE2 --> A_DN STATE2 --> B_DN A_UP --> EQ B_UP --> EQ A_DN --> EQ B_DN --> EQ EQ --> PROF style TODAY fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px style FUTURE fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00,stroke-width:2px style PAYOFFS fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px style ARB fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px style EQ fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px ``` --- ## 4.2 State-Transition Flowchart — Arbitrage Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD START["Given: C, P, S sub 0, X, r, T"] COMP["Compute Theoretical Put
P sub th = C + X times e power minus rT minus S sub 0"] CHECK{"P sub market versus P sub th
Comparison"} OVER["Put Overpriced
P sub market greater than P sub th"] UNDER["Put Underpriced
P sub market less than P sub th"] FAIR["Fair Market
P sub market equals P sub th"] ACT_OVER["Strategy:
Sell Put + Buy Call
Buy Bond + Short Stock"] ACT_UNDER["Strategy:
Buy Put + Sell Call
Short Bond + Buy Stock"] NO_ARB["No Arbitrage
Market is Efficient"] PROFIT["Lock in Risk-Free Profit
Profit equals absolute difference"] START --> COMP COMP --> CHECK CHECK -->|Overpriced| OVER --> ACT_OVER --> PROFIT CHECK -->|Underpriced| UNDER --> ACT_UNDER --> PROFIT CHECK -->|Fair| FAIR --> NO_ARB style START fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0 style COMP fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825 style CHECK fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#d84315 style PROFIT fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:3px style NO_ARB fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#455a64 ``` --- ## 4.3 Payoff Profile Schematic — Visual Representation The following conceptual diagram (rendered as a block schematic since this is a functional architecture mapping) shows how the two portfolios merge into a unified terminal payoff of **max(S_T, X)**. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CALLSIDE["Call + Bond Side"] C1["Call Option
Pays max(S sub T minus X, 0)"] B1["Zero-Coupon Bond
Pays X at T"] C1 --- B1 end subgraph PUTSIDE["Put + Stock Side"] P1["Put Option
Pays max(X minus S sub T, 0)"] S1["Stock Share
Pays S sub T"] P1 --- S1 end CALLSIDE --> RESULT["Unified Payoff
max of S sub T and X"] PUTSIDE --> RESULT RESULT --> PARITY["PUT-CALL PARITY
C + X times e power minus rT equals P + S sub 0"] style CALLSIDE fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1 style PUTSIDE fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f style RESULT fill:#fff176,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px style PARITY fill:#b9f6ca,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:3px ``` --- ## 4.4 Decision Matrix Table — Synthetic Position Replication ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SYNTH["Synthetic Position Equivalences"] direction TB SYN_CALL["Synthetic Call = Long Put + Long Stock minus Long Bond"] SYN_PUT["Synthetic Put = Long Call + Long Bond minus Long Stock"] SYN_STOCK["Synthetic Stock = Long Call minus Long Put + Long Bond"] SYN_BOND["Synthetic Bond = Long Stock + Long Put minus Long Call"] end PARITY_EQ["Central Parity Equation:
C plus PV of X equals P plus S sub 0"] SYN_CALL --> PARITY_EQ SYN_PUT --> PARITY_EQ SYN_STOCK --> PARITY_EQ SYN_BOND --> PARITY_EQ style SYNTH fill:#ede7f6,stroke:#4527a0 style PARITY_EQ fill:#ffe082,stroke:#ff6f00,stroke-width:3px ``` > [!TIP] > **How to read these diagrams for KTU exams:** Always (a) start with the **Payoff Table** (Cases 1 & 2), then (b) cite the **Law of One Price**, then (c) write the **Parity Equation**. Diagrams help examiners see you understand the *logic*, not just the formula. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **State and explain Put-Call Parity for European options. Mention any two assumptions required for the relationship to hold strictly.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Put-Call Parity is a no-arbitrage relationship that links the prices of European call and put options having the same underlying asset, strike price, and maturity. It is stated as: $$C + X \cdot e^{-rT} = P + S_0$$ **Two key assumptions** (1.5 marks): 1. The market is frictionless — no transaction costs, taxes, or bid-ask spreads. 2. The risk-free borrowing and lending rates are equal and constant throughout the option's life. 3. The underlying stock pays no dividends during the option's lifetime. 4. The options are European in style (exercisable only at expiration). *(Any two of the above carry 1.5 marks; the equation itself carries 1.5 marks.)* --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Explain the concept of "Synthetic Options" using Put-Call Parity. Give one example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Synthetic options are positions constructed by combining other instruments (stock, bonds, and the *opposite* option) to replicate the payoff of the desired option — without directly trading it. Put-Call Parity provides the exact replication recipe (2 marks). **Example (1 mark):** A **Synthetic Call** = Long Put + Long Stock − Long Bond (i.e., $C = P + S_0 - X \cdot e^{-rT}$). This combination pays exactly the same as a call option at expiration, but may be cheaper (or differently taxed) than the actual call — enabling arbitrage or cost-efficient hedging. --- ## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 ESE Convention:** Each Part B question has an internal choice. Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Put-Call Parity relationship for European options using a two-portfolio no-arbitrage argument. Use a payoff table to support your derivation. **(b) [7 Marks]** A stock is currently trading at ₹80. A 1-year European call option with a strike price of ₹85 is priced at ₹10. The risk-free rate is 8% per annum (continuous compounding). Calculate the corresponding European put option price. Verify whether the call is overpriced, underpriced, or fairly priced if the market put quote is ₹12. --- ### **Model Solution for Question A** #### Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks] **Step 1 — Construct Portfolio A (1 mark):** Portfolio A consists of one European call option and a zero-coupon bond with face value $X$ maturing at $T$. Initial cost: $A_0 = C + X \cdot e^{-rT}$. **Step 2 — Construct Portfolio B (1 mark):** Portfolio B consists of one European put option and one share of the underlying stock. Initial cost: $B_0 = P + S_0$. **Step 3 — Build the Payoff Table (3 marks):** | Condition | Portfolio A Payoff | Portfolio B Payoff | |---|---|---| | $S_T > X$ | $\max(S_T - X, 0) + X = S_T$ | $\max(X - S_T, 0) + S_T = S_T$ | | $S_T \le X$ | $\max(S_T - X, 0) + X = X$ | $\max(X - S_T, 0) + S_T = X$ | **Step 4 — Apply No-Arbitrage (2 marks):** Since both portfolios yield identical terminal payoffs under all states, the Law of One Price requires: $$A_0 = B_0 \implies C + X \cdot e^{-rT} = P + S_0$$ --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 Marks] **Given:** $S_0 = 80$, $X = 85$, $T = 1$, $r = 0.08$, $C_{\text{market}} = 10$, $P_{\text{market}} = 12$. **Step 1 — Compute discount factor (1 mark):** $$e^{-rT} = e^{-0.08 \times 1} = e^{-0.08} \approx 0.9231$$ **Step 2 — Compute PV of strike (1 mark):** $$X \cdot e^{-rT} = 85 \times 0.9231 \approx 78.46$$ **Step 3 — Theoretical put price (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{theoretical}} &= C + X \cdot e^{-rT} - S_0 \\ &= 10 + 78.46 - 80 \\ &= 8.46 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compare with market quote (2 marks):** - $P_{\text{market}} = ₹12$, $P_{\text{theoretical}} = ₹8.46$. - Since $P_{\text{market}} > P_{\text{theoretical}}$ by ₹3.54, the **put is overpriced**. **Step 5 — Arbitrage strategy (1 mark):** | Action | Today | At T | |---|---|---| | Sell put | +₹12 | Liability if $S_T < 85$ | | Buy call | −₹10 | Payoff if $S_T > 85$ | | Buy bond (PV ₹78.46) | −₹78.46 | Receive ₹85 | | Short stock | +₹80 | Pay $S_T$ | **Net today:** $+12 - 10 - 78.46 + 80 = +₹3.54$ (risk-free profit). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of "synthetic positions" derived from Put-Call Parity. Construct the synthetic equivalents for a **call**, a **put**, a **stock**, and a **bond**, and state one practical use case for each. **(b) [7 Marks]** A 9-month European put option with a strike of ₹50 trades at ₹4. The underlying stock is at ₹52, and the risk-free rate is 6% p.a. (continuous compounding). Find the corresponding call price. If a market quote shows the call at ₹9, identify whether an arbitrage exists, calculate the profit, and describe the arbitrage trade. --- ### **Model Solution for Question B** #### Part (a) — Synthetic Positions [7 Marks] Using Put-Call Parity $C + X \cdot e^{-rT} = P + S_0$, we can algebraically isolate any variable to obtain a **synthetic equivalent**. **Synthetic Call (2 marks):** $$C_{\text{synthetic}} = P + S_0 - X \cdot e^{-rT}$$ Composition: Long Put + Long Stock − Long Bond. **Use case:** Investors expecting sharp upside moves can replicate a call without paying the exchange-traded call's premium, especially in illiquid markets. **Synthetic Put (1.5 marks):** $$P_{\text{synthetic}} = C + X \cdot e^{-rT} - S_0$$ Composition: Long Call + Long Bond − Short Stock. **Use case:** Hedging a long stock position against downside risk without buying exchange-traded puts. **Synthetic Stock (1.5 marks):** $$S_0^{\text{synthetic}} = C - P + X \cdot e^{-rT}$$ Composition: Long Call − Long Put + Long Bond. **Use case:** Gaining stock exposure without directly shorting or buying the underlying (useful in restricted-share scenarios). **Synthetic Bond (1 mark):** $$X \cdot e^{-rT} \big|_{\text{synthetic}} = P + S_0 - C$$ Composition: Long Put + Long Stock − Long Call. **Use case:** Money-market replication in fixed-income arbitrage strategies. #### Part (b) — Numerical & Arbitrage [7 Marks] **Given:** $S_0 = 52$, $X = 50$, $T = 9/12 = 0.75$, $r = 0.06$, $P = 4$. **Step 1 — Discount factor (1 mark):** $$e^{-rT} = e^{-0.06 \times 0.75} = e^{-0.045} \approx 0.9560$$ **Step 2 — PV of strike (1 mark):** $$X \cdot e^{-rT} = 50 \times 0.9560 = 47.80$$ **Step 3 — Theoretical call price (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{\text{theoretical}} &= P + S_0 - X \cdot e^{-rT} \\ &= 4 + 52 - 47.80 \\ &= 8.20 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compare with market (1 mark):** Market call = ₹9.00, theoretical = ₹8.20. The **call is overpriced** by ₹0.80. **Step 5 — Arbitrage trade (2 marks):** | Action | Today | At T | |---|---|---| | Sell call | +₹9.00 | Liability: pay $\max(S_T - 50, 0)$ | | Buy put | −₹4.00 | Receive $\max(50 - S_T, 0)$ | | Buy stock | −₹52.00 | Receive $S_T$ | | Borrow PV(50) | +₹47.80 | Repay ₹50 | **Net today:** $+9 - 4 - 52 + 47.80 = +₹0.80$. **∴ Risk-free arbitrage profit = ₹0.80 per share.** The position's terminal payoff in every state is exactly zero, confirming a true arbitrage. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places where KTU students LOSE marks in Put-Call Parity questions:** > > 1. **Confusing continuous vs. discrete compounding** (−1 to −2 marks). Always check whether the question states "continuous" or a specific compounding frequency. Default to continuous **only** when explicitly stated. > > 2. **Forgetting to use the discounted strike $X \cdot e^{-rT}$** — students often write $X$ instead of $X \cdot e^{-rT}$ in the parity equation (−2 marks). > > 3. **Incorrect sign in arbitrage trades** — selling/buying labels are frequently inverted, destroying the profit calculation (−3 marks). > > 4. **Ignoring dividend yield** — if the problem mentions a "dividend-paying stock," the equation becomes $C + X \cdot e^{-rT} = P + S_0 \cdot e^{-qT}$ where $q$ is the dividend yield. Skipping this is a 2-mark penalty. > > 5. **Not drawing the payoff table** in derivations — KTU 2024 scheme explicitly awards 2–3 marks for the state-by-state payoff analysis. Skipping it means losing marks even if the final equation is correct. > > 6. **Forgetting units** — always state that $T$ is in **years** and $r$ is annualized. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Put-Call Parity** - ✅ **Parity Equation:** $C + X \cdot e^{-rT} = P + S_0$ (continuous) or $C + \dfrac{X}{(1+r)^T} = P + S_0$ (discrete). - ✅ **Two-Portfolio Proof:** Portfolio A (Call + Bond) vs. Portfolio B (Put + Stock) yield identical terminal payoffs. - ✅ **Unified Terminal Payoff:** Both portfolios = $\max(S_T, X)$. - ✅ **Discount Factor:** Always discount the strike by $e^{-rT}$ (or $(1+r)^{-T}$). - ✅ **Synthetic Replications:** - Synthetic Call = Put + Stock − Bond - Synthetic Put = Call + Bond − Stock - Synthetic Stock = Call − Put + Bond - Synthetic Bond = Put + Stock − Call - ✅ **Arbitrage Rule:** If $P_{\text{market}} \neq P_{\text{theoretical}}$, exploit the mispricing by **selling the overpriced leg** and **buying the replicating portfolio**. - ✅ **Validity Conditions:** European options, no dividends, frictionless markets, constant risk-free rate. - ✅ **American Option Bound:** $S_0 - X \le C - P \le S_0 - X \cdot e^{-rT}$ (inequality, not equality). - ✅ **Dividend Adjustment:** Replace $S_0$ with $S_0 \cdot e^{-qT}$ for continuous dividend yield $q$. - ✅ **Sensitivity Intuition:** Higher $S_0$ or higher $r$ → higher $C$, lower $P$. Volatility raises both equally in absolute terms. - ✅ **Forward Price Link:** $C - P = S_0 - X \cdot e^{-rT} = \text{Forward Premium Discount}$. - ✅ **Exam Tip:** Always tabulate payoffs (2 states), draw the no-arbitrage conclusion, then write the algebraic equation. > **Final Note for KTU 2024:** Put-Call Parity is **bidirectionally tested** — students may be asked to (i) derive the equation, (ii) solve for any one variable, (iii) detect an arbitrage and construct the trade, or (iv) explain a synthetic position. Master all four dimensions for full marks.

topics.title: European and American Options
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET832 - INTRODUCTION TO FINANCIAL MARKETS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** European and American Options

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Option Contract?

A **financial option** is a derivative contract that grants the holder (buyer) the **right, but not the obligation**, to either **buy** or **sell** an underlying asset (such as a stock, index, commodity, or currency) at a pre-agreed price (called the **strike price** or **exercise price**) on or before a specified date (called the **expiry date** or **maturity date**).

For acquiring this right, the buyer pays a non-refundable premium to the seller (known as the **option writer**). The two fundamental variants of options are:

- **Call Option** → Right to **BUY** the underlying asset.
- **Put Option** → Right to **SELL** the underlying asset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4):** Options are classified primarily by the **timing flexibility** allowed to the holder to exercise the right. This timing flexibility gives rise to two dominant styles: **European Options** and **American Options**.

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard)

### European Option
A **European option** is a contract that can be **exercised only on a single, pre-specified date**, namely the **expiration date (maturity date, T)**. The holder cannot exercise the option at any time prior to expiry. After expiry, the option becomes worthless.

### American Option
An **American option** is a contract that can be **exercised at any time on or before the expiration date**, i.e., from the date of purchase (time 0) up to and including the maturity date (T). This added timing flexibility makes the American option theoretically **more valuable** than an otherwise identical European option.

> [!NOTE]
> **Naming Convention Note:** Despite the names "European" and "American," these labels have **nothing to do with geography**. They simply describe the exercise-style of the option. Most exchange-traded options globally (including those on NSE, CBOE, etc.) are actually American-style, while many index options are European-style.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Rain Check" Voucher

Imagine you are planning a beach trip next Saturday. A local resort sells two types of vouchers:

1. **European Voucher (Beach Pass):** You can ONLY use this voucher on Saturday — the exact day of the trip. If it rains on Friday and the beach is empty, tough luck — you cannot redeem it early. On Saturday, if it's sunny, you use it; if it rains, it expires worthless.

2. **American Voucher (Flexible Pass):** You can use this voucher ANY DAY from Monday through Saturday. If Friday turns out to be a perfect beach day, you can redeem the voucher that morning itself. If Saturday is the only sunny day, you can use it then. The pass gives you **choice of timing**.

The **American voucher costs more** than the European voucher because you have the flexibility to choose the best day. The **option premium** (the price you pay upfront) is exactly this: the cost of flexibility.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Convention |
|--------|---------|---------------------|
| **S** | Current Spot Price of underlying | Positive real number |
| **K** | Strike (Exercise) Price | Fixed at contract inception |
| **T** | Time to Maturity (in years) | $T \geq 0$ |
| **C** | Call Option Premium (Price) | $C > 0$ |
| **P** | Put Option Premium (Price) | $P > 0$ |
| **r** | Risk-free interest rate (annualized) | Typically 4% – 8% in India |
| **σ** | Volatility of underlying returns | Standard deviation, annual |
| **t** | Current time | $0 \leq t \leq T$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction (Examiner's Favourite):** The only structural difference between European and American options lies in the **EXERCISE TIMING**. All other contract parameters (strike price, underlying, premium, expiry date) are identical.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Logic — Why Does Timing Matter?

The value of any option can be decomposed into two components:

1. **Intrinsic Value (IV):** The immediate exercise worth.
2. **Time Value (TV):** The extra value from the possibility of favourable future price movements.

$$V_{option} = V_{intrinsic} + V_{time}$$

The **timing flexibility** of the American option directly increases the **time value** component, because the holder can capture favourable price movements the instant they occur, rather than waiting for expiry.

## 2.2 Intrinsic Value Formulas

For a **Call Option**:

$$IV_{call} = \max(S - K,\ 0)$$

For a **Put Option**:

$$IV_{put} = \max(K - S,\ 0)$$

> [!NOTE]
> An option is said to be:
> - **In-the-Money (ITM):** Intrinsic Value > 0 → Exercise is profitable
> - **At-the-Money (ATM):** $S \approx K$ → Intrinsic Value ≈ 0
> - **Out-of-the-Money (OTM):** Intrinsic Value = 0 → Exercise yields nothing

## 2.3 Payoff Functions at Expiry (European Options)

For a European option, payoff is realized **only at T**.

**Call Payoff at T:**

$$\text{Payoff}_{Call} = \max(S_T - K,\ 0)$$

**Put Payoff at T:**

$$\text{Payoff}_{Put} = \max(K - S_T,\ 0)$$

**Profit (Net Payoff) at T** (after subtracting premium):

$$\text{Profit}_{Call} = \max(S_T - K,\ 0) - C$$

$$\text{Profit}_{Put} = \max(K - S_T,\ 0) - P$$

## 2.4 Payoff Functions for American Options (Early Exercise)

An American option can be exercised at ANY time $t$ where $0 \leq t \leq T$.

**American Call Payoff if exercised at time t:**

$$\text{Payoff}_{Call}^{Am}(t) = \max(S_t - K,\ 0)$$

**American Put Payoff if exercised at time t:**

$$\text{Payoff}_{Put}^{Am}(t) = \max(K - S_t,\ 0)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KEY THEOREM — Optimal Early Exercise Rules:**
> 1. **American Call on a non-dividend-paying stock:** Early exercise is **NEVER optimal**. Therefore, the American call value **equals** the European call value.
> $$C_{American} = C_{European} \quad \text{(no dividends)}$$
> 2. **American Put on a non-dividend-paying stock:** Early exercise **MAY be optimal**, especially as $t \to T$ and $S$ falls deeply ITM.
> $$P_{American} \geq P_{European}$$
> 3. **American Call on a dividend-paying stock:** Early exercise **MAY be optimal** just before a large ex-dividend date (to capture the dividend).

## 2.5 Put-Call Parity Relationship (European Options)

The most foundational pricing relationship in option theory:

$$C + K \cdot e^{-rT} = P + S_0$$

Where:
- $C$ = European call price
- $P$ = European put price
- $S_0$ = Current spot price
- $K$ = Strike price
- $r$ = Risk-free rate
- $T$ = Time to maturity

Rearranging, we can find the put price from the call price:

$$P = C + K \cdot e^{-rT} - S_0$$

Or the call price from the put price:

$$C = P + S_0 - K \cdot e^{-rT}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Put-Call Parity Limitation:** This exact formula applies **only to European options** on non-dividend stocks. For American options, put-call parity becomes an **inequality**:
> $$S_0 - K \leq C_{Am} - P_{Am} \leq S_0 - K \cdot e^{-rT}$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Condition / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Call Intrinsic Value | $IV_C = \max(S - K,\ 0)$ | Always non-negative |
| 2 | Put Intrinsic Value | $IV_P = \max(K - S,\ 0)$ | Always non-negative |
| 3 | European Call Payoff (at T) | $\max(S_T - K,\ 0)$ | Realized only at expiry |
| 4 | European Put Payoff (at T) | $\max(K - S_T,\ 0)$ | Realized only at expiry |
| 5 | American Call Payoff (at t) | $\max(S_t - K,\ 0)$ | $0 \leq t \leq T$ |
| 6 | American Put Payoff (at t) | $\max(K - S_t,\ 0)$ | $0 \leq t \leq T$ |
| 7 | Put-Call Parity (European) | $C + K e^{-rT} = P + S_0$ | No dividends |
| 8 | American = European Call | $C_{Am} = C_{Eu}$ | No dividends |
| 9 | American ≥ European Put | $P_{Am} \geq P_{Eu}$ | Always true |
| 10 | Upper Bound — Call | $C \leq S_0$ | Cannot exceed spot |
| 11 | Lower Bound — Call | $C \geq \max(S_0 - K e^{-rT},\ 0)$ | European |
| 12 | Upper Bound — Put | $P \leq K e^{-rT}$ | Cannot exceed PV of strike |
| 13 | Lower Bound — Put | $P \geq \max(K e^{-rT} - S_0,\ 0)$ | European |
| 14 | Net Profit (Call) | $\max(S_T - K,\ 0) - C$ | Subtract premium |
| 15 | Net Profit (Put) | $\max(K - S_T,\ 0) - P$ | Subtract premium |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Financial Engineering

- **Hedging:** American options are used by portfolio managers to protect against sudden price drops (early exercise lets them lock in protection).
- **Employee Stock Options (ESOs):** These are typically **American-style** — employees can exercise the moment the stock vests, which is tax-efficient.
- **Index Options (NIFTY, SENSEX):** These are usually **European-style** because early exercise would disrupt the cash-settlement process.
- **Warrants & Convertibles:** Mostly American-style, giving holders maximum flexibility.
- **Black-Scholes Model:** Originally designed for **European** options; **Binomial/Trinomial Trees** and **Cox-Ross-Rubinstein (CRR)** models are used for **American** options.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Derivation of the American Call = European Call Result (No Dividends)

**Statement:** On a non-dividend-paying stock, early exercise of an American call option is never optimal.

**Proof by Contradiction (No-Arbitrage Argument):**

**Step 1:** Suppose at some time $t < T$, the holder exercises the American call and pays strike $K$ to receive the stock.

**Step 2:** The value received immediately is:
$$\text{Value received} = S_t - K$$

**Step 3:** Compare with holding the option and receiving the same payoff at $T$ but **plus** the time value of money saved on the strike.

**Step 4:** If the option is held to expiry, the payoff at $T$ is $\max(S_T - K, 0)$. To replicate the exercise at $t$, one could:
- Sell the option at time $t$ for at least its intrinsic value $S_t - K$ (American option value ≥ intrinsic value).

**Step 5:** The capital $K$ required for early exercise could instead be invested at the risk-free rate $r$ to grow to $K \cdot e^{r(T-t)}$ at time $T$.

**Step 6:** Since the stock pays no dividends, holding the stock between $t$ and $T$ yields no income. Therefore, the holder strictly prefers to:
- Sell the option (or hold it) → collect $S_t - K$ now AND keep $K$ invested.
- Wait until $T$ → potentially receive more.

**Step 7:** Conclude that early exercise of the American call is **dominated** by holding the option. Therefore:
$$C_{American} = C_{European} \quad \text{(no dividends)} \qquad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — European Call Payoff & Profit Computation

**Problem:**
A European call option has:
- Strike price $K = \text{₹}100$
- Premium $C = \text{₹}8$
- Spot price at expiry: $S_T = \text{₹}120$

Find: (a) Payoff, (b) Profit, (c) Moneyness at expiry.

**Step-by-step Solution:**

**(a) Payoff at T:**

$$\text{Payoff}_{Call} = \max(S_T - K,\ 0)$$

$$\text{Payoff}_{Call} = \max(120 - 100,\ 0)$$

$$\text{Payoff}_{Call} = \max(20,\ 0) = \text{₹}20$$

**(b) Profit (Net) at T:**

$$\text{Profit}_{Call} = \text{Payoff}_{Call} - C$$

$$\text{Profit}_{Call} = 20 - 8 = \text{₹}12$$

**(c) Moneyness:**
Since $S_T = 120 > K = 100$, the option expires **In-the-Money (ITM)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Breakeven price** for the call = $K + C = 100 + 8 = \text{₹}108$. The investor breaks even only if the stock closes at exactly ₹108 at expiry.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — European Put Payoff & Profit Computation

**Problem:**
A European put option has:
- Strike price $K = \text{₹}200$
- Premium $P = \text{₹}12$
- Spot price at expiry: $S_T = \text{₹}170$

Find: (a) Payoff, (b) Profit, (c) Moneyness.

**Step-by-step Solution:**

**(a) Payoff at T:**

$$\text{Payoff}_{Put} = \max(K - S_T,\ 0)$$

$$\text{Payoff}_{Put} = \max(200 - 170,\ 0) = \text{₹}30$$

**(b) Profit at T:**

$$\text{Profit}_{Put} = \text{Payoff}_{Put} - P = 30 - 12 = \text{₹}18$$

**(c) Moneyness:**
Since $S_T = 170 < K = 200$, the option expires **In-the-Money (ITM)**.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Put-Call Parity Verification

**Problem:**
Given:
- Spot price $S_0 = \text{₹}250$
- Strike price $K = \text{₹}240$
- Time to maturity $T = 0.5$ years
- Risk-free rate $r = 10\%$ per annum
- European call price $C = \text{₹}25$

Find: (a) The European put price $P$ using put-call parity.
(b) Verify whether arbitrage exists.

**Step-by-step Solution:**

**(a) Using Put-Call Parity:**

$$C + K \cdot e^{-rT} = P + S_0$$

Substitute the values:

$$25 + 240 \cdot e^{-0.10 \times 0.5} = P + 250$$

Compute the discount factor:

$$e^{-0.05} = 0.951229$$

$$240 \times 0.951229 = 228.295$$

Substitute back:

$$25 + 228.295 = P + 250$$

$$253.295 = P + 250$$

$$P = 253.295 - 250 = \text{₹}3.295$$

**(b) Arbitrage Check:**
If the market put price is, say, ₹4 (higher than ₹3.295), then we have an arbitrage opportunity: **Sell overpriced put, Buy call, Buy bond, Short stock**. Net riskless profit ≈ ₹0.705 per contract.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU problems, you are often required to **show the arbitrage strategy** step-by-step. Always write out both the "synthetic" portfolio and the actual market portfolio.

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — American vs European Option Premium Comparison

**Problem:**
A non-dividend-paying stock trades at $S_0 = \text{₹}50$. A 3-month European call with $K = \text{₹}50$ trades at ₹4. Estimate the value of the American call on the same stock with the same $K$ and $T$.

**Step-by-step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Recall the theorem: On a non-dividend stock, the American call equals the European call because early exercise is suboptimal.

**Step 2:** Apply the relationship:

$$C_{American} = C_{European} = \text{₹}4$$

**Step 3:** Now consider the American Put with the same parameters. By definition:

$$P_{American} \geq P_{European}$$

The American put is worth **more** because the holder can exercise early when the stock falls deeply ITM (no dividends to compensate for waiting).

## 3.6 Worked Example 5 — Intrinsic Value Table Construction

**Problem:** Construct a payoff and profit table for a European call option with $K = \text{₹}100$, $C = \text{₹}10$, at various stock prices at expiry.

| Spot Price at $T$ (₹) | Moneyness | Payoff $\max(S_T - K, 0)$ | Profit Payoff - C |
|---|---|---|---|
| 70 | Deep OTM | 0 | -10 |
| 90 | OTM | 0 | -10 |
| 100 | ATM | 0 | -10 |
| 110 | ITM | 10 | 0 (Breakeven) |
| 120 | ITM | 20 | 10 |
| 130 | Deep ITM | 30 | 20 |
| 150 | Deep ITM | 50 | 40 |

**Observation:** The maximum loss for the buyer is limited to the **premium** (₹10). The maximum profit is **unlimited** (as $S_T \to \infty$).

> [!NOTE]
> For a European put, the **maximum profit is bounded** by $(K - P)$ — the buyer cannot make more than the strike price minus the premium, because the stock cannot fall below ₹0.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Logic: European vs American Option Exercise

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Option Contract Initiated at t = 0] --> B{Identify Option Style}
    B -->|European| C[Holder WAITS until T]
    B -->|American| D{Holder Chooses Optimal Exercise Time}
    C --> E[At T, Compare ST vs K]
    E -->|ST greater than K| F1[Exercise CALL: Receive ST minus K]
    E -->|ST less than K| F2[Exercise PUT: Receive K minus ST]
    E -->|ST equals K| F3[Option Expires Worthless]
    D --> G{Compare Early vs Late Exercise Value}
    G -->|Early gives higher value| H1[Exercise Immediately at time t]
    G -->|Late gives higher value| H2[Wait until time tau where tau is less than T]
    G -->|Either gives same value| H3[Indifferent - choose either]
    H1 --> I1[Receive S_t minus K or K minus S_t]
    H2 --> I2[Continue Monitoring]
    I2 --> E
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pricing Model Selection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Option Contract] --> B{Option Type}
    B -->|European| C[Closed Form Models]
    B -->|American| D[Numerical Methods]
    C --> C1[Black Scholes Merton Model]
    C --> C2[Put Call Parity Formulas]
    D --> D1[Binomial Tree CRR Model]
    D --> D2[Trinomial Tree Model]
    D --> D3[Finite Difference Methods]
    D --> D4[Longstaff Schwartz Monte Carlo]
    C1 --> E1[Inputs S, K, T, r, sigma]
    C2 --> E2[Inputs C or P, S, K, T, r]
    D1 --> E3[Inputs plus N steps]
    D2 --> E3
    D3 --> E3
    D4 --> E3
    E1 --> F1[Output: Single Premium Value]
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F2[Output: Exercise Boundary plus Premium]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Payoff Profiles Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph C1[Call Option Payoff Profile]
        C1A[Buyer Side: Unlimited Upside, Loss Capped at Premium] --> C1B[Breakeven Point: K plus C]
        C1B --> C1C[Below Breakeven: Loss Region]
        C1B --> C1D[Above Breakeven: Profit Region]
    end
    subgraph P1[Put Option Payoff Profile]
        P1A[Buyer Side: Limited Upside K minus P, Loss Capped at Premium] --> P1B[Breakeven Point: K minus P]
        P1B --> P1C[Above Breakeven: Loss Region]
        P1B --> P1D[Below Breakeven: Profit Region]
    end
    subgraph C2[Call Writer Side Mirror Image]
        C2A[Writer Receives Premium C] --> C2B[Unlimited Downside Risk]
    end
    C1 --> C2
    P1 --> C2
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Architecture — Option Valuation Component Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Input Parameters Module] --> S1[Spot Price S]
    S --> S2[Strike Price K]
    S --> S3[Time T]
    S --> S4[Rate r]
    S --> S5[Volatility sigma]
    S1 --> V[Valuation Engine Core]
    S2 --> V
    S3 --> V
    S4 --> V
    S5 --> V
    V --> V1{Exercise Style}
    V1 -->|European Path| VE[Black Scholes Closed Form]
    V1 -->|American Path| VA[Binomial Tree Numerical Solver]
    VE --> O1[Output: Single Call and Put Premium]
    VA --> O2[Output: Early Exercise Boundary plus Premium]
    O1 --> R[Final Result Reporting]
    O2 --> R
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip:** The American path always requires **iterative numerical methods** because there is no closed-form solution. The European path uses the elegant Black-Scholes closed-form formula. KTU questions often test whether students can identify which method applies to which option type.
