# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]

**Define a European option. How does it differ from an American option in terms of exercise flexibility?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **European option** is a financial derivative contract that can be exercised **only on the expiration date (maturity date T)**. The holder does not have the right to exercise the option at any point prior to expiry.

An **American option**, in contrast, can be exercised **at any time on or before the expiration date**, giving the holder greater timing flexibility.

**Key Differences:**

| Parameter | European Option | American Option |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Exercise Timing | Only at expiry $T$ | Anytime $0 \leq t \leq T$ |
| Premium | Lower | Higher (due to flexibility) |
| Valuation | Closed-form (Black-Scholes) | Numerical (Binomial/Trinomial Tree) |
| Early Exercise | Not possible | Possible (sometimes optimal) |

The only structural difference is the **timing of exercise**. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for American definition, 1 for the comparison table.)*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample Paper]

**State and explain the Put-Call Parity relationship for European options. Mention the assumption under which it holds.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Put-Call Parity** for European options is:

$$C + K \cdot e^{-rT} = P + S_0$$

Where $C$ = European call premium, $P$ = European put premium, $K$ = strike price, $S_0$ = spot price, $r$ = risk-free rate, $T$ = time to maturity.

**Explanation:** This equation states that a protective put position ($P + S_0$) is exactly equivalent in payoff to a fiduciary call position ($C + K e^{-rT}$) at expiry. Both portfolios yield the same terminal value $\max(S_T, K)$, hence they must have the same cost today.

**Assumption:** The relationship holds strictly for **European options on non-dividend-paying stocks** with no transaction costs and continuous compounding. *(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for explanation, 1 for assumption.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **payoff and profit profiles** of European call and put options for both the buyer and the writer. Use suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A European call option on a stock has the following data:
- Spot price $S_0 = \text{₹}200$
- Strike price $K = \text{₹}210$
- Premium $C = \text{₹}12$
- Time to maturity $T = 3$ months
- Risk-free rate $r = 8\%$ per annum

Compute: (i) Intrinsic Value, (ii) Payoff at expiry if $S_T = \text{₹}230$, (iii) Profit/Loss, (iv) Breakeven price. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Payoff and Profit Profiles (7 Marks)**

**Call Option Buyer:**
- Payoff at $T$: $\max(S_T - K, 0)$
- Profit: $\max(S_T - K, 0) - C$
- Maximum Loss = Premium $C$ (when $S_T \leq K$)
- Maximum Profit = **Unlimited** (as $S_T \to \infty$)
- Breakeven: $S_T = K + C$

**Call Option Writer (Seller):**
- Payoff at $T$: $-\max(S_T - K, 0) = \min(K - S_T, 0)$
- Profit: $C - \max(S_T - K, 0)$
- Maximum Profit = Premium $C$ (when $S_T \leq K$)
- Maximum Loss = **Unlimited** (as $S_T \to \infty$)
- Breakeven: $S_T = K + C$

**Put Option Buyer:**
- Payoff at $T$: $\max(K - S_T, 0)$
- Profit: $\max(K - S_T, 0) - P$
- Maximum Loss = Premium $P$
- Maximum Profit = $K - P$ (when $S_T = 0$)
- Breakeven: $S_T = K - P$

**Put Option Writer (Seller):**
- Payoff at $T$: $-\max(K - S_T, 0) = \min(S_T - K, 0)$
- Profit: $P - \max(K - S_T, 0)$
- Maximum Profit = Premium $P$
- Maximum Loss = $K - P$ (when $S_T = 0$)

**Key Asymmetry:** Buyers have **limited loss / unlimited or large gain**. Writers have **limited gain / large or unlimited loss**. This is why option **writing is riskier** than buying.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

Given: $S_0 = 200$, $K = 210$, $C = 12$, $T = 0.25$ years, $r = 8\%$, $S_T = 230$.

**(i) Intrinsic Value at Current Spot:**
The current spot is ₹200 vs strike ₹210. Since $S_0 < K$, the call is currently **Out-of-the-Money (OTM)**.
$$IV_{current} = \max(S_0 - K, 0) = \max(200 - 210, 0) = \text{₹}0$$

**(ii) Payoff at Expiry when $S_T = 230$:**
$$\text{Payoff} = \max(S_T - K, 0) = \max(230 - 210, 0) = \text{₹}20$$

**(iii) Profit/Loss at Expiry:**
$$\text{Profit} = \text{Payoff} - C = 20 - 12 = \text{₹}8 \text{ (Profit)}$$

**(iv) Breakeven Price:**
$$S_T^{BE} = K + C = 210 + 12 = \text{₹}222$$

At $S_T = 230 > 222$, the option buyer earns a profit of ₹8 (excluding the time value of the premium).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

**(a)** Discuss the **American option pricing** problem. Why is the American call on a non-dividend stock equivalent in value to a European call? Prove this with an arbitrage argument. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using **Put-Call Parity**, find the price of a 6-month European put option on a non-dividend stock when:
- Spot price $S_0 = \text{₹}500$
- Strike price $K = \text{₹}520$
- European call price $C = \text{₹}15$
- Risk-free rate $r = 12\%$ per annum
- Time to maturity $T = 0.5$ years

Also verify whether an arbitrage opportunity exists if the market put price is quoted as ₹45. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — American Option Pricing & Equivalence Theorem (7 Marks)**

The American option pricing problem is fundamentally more complex than the European case because of the **early exercise feature**. Since the holder can choose the optimal exercise time from a continuum of possibilities, the option becomes an **optimal stopping problem**.

**Black-Scholes Cannot Be Applied Directly** to American options. Instead, numerical methods like the **Cox-Ross-Rubinstein (CRR) Binomial Tree**, **Trinomial Trees**, or **Finite Difference Methods** are employed.

**Theorem:** On a non-dividend-paying stock, the value of an American call equals the value of an otherwise identical European call:
$$C_{American} = C_{European}$$

**Proof by No-Arbitrage Argument:**

**Step 1:** Suppose at time $t < T$, the American call holder exercises and pays $K$ to receive the stock.

**Step 2:** The early exercise value is exactly $S_t - K$.

**Step 3:** If instead the holder **sells the option** at time $t$, they receive **at least** $S_t - K$ (since the American option is always worth at least its intrinsic value).

**Step 4:** The $K$ capital saved by selling (instead of exercising) can be invested at the risk-free rate $r$, growing to $K \cdot e^{r(T-t)}$ at time $T$.

**Step 5:** Since the stock pays **no dividends**, holding the stock from $t$ to $T$ yields **no income**. Therefore, the holder loses nothing by waiting.

**Step 6:** The cash amount $S_t - K$ obtained by selling the option PLUS the $K$ invested at risk-free rate is **strictly greater** than the early exercise value of $S_t - K$ alone (due to the accumulated interest).

**Step 7:** Hence early exercise is **dominated** by selling the option. Conclusion: Early exercise is **never optimal**, and $C_{American} = C_{European}$ when no dividends are paid. $\blacksquare$

---

**Part (b) — Put-Call Parity Application (7 Marks)**

Given: $S_0 = 500$, $K = 520$, $C = 15$, $r = 12\%$, $T = 0.5$ years.

**Step 1: Apply the Put-Call Parity Formula:**

$$C + K \cdot e^{-rT} = P + S_0$$

**Step 2: Compute the Discount Factor:**

$$e^{-rT} = e^{-0.12 \times 0.5} = e^{-0.06} = 0.941765$$

**Step 3: Compute the Present Value of Strike:**

$$K \cdot e^{-rT} = 520 \times 0.941765 = 489.718$$

**Step 4: Substitute and Solve for $P$:**

$$15 + 489.718 = P + 500$$

$$504.718 = P + 500$$

$$P_{theoretical} = 504.718 - 500 = \text{₹}4.718$$

**Step 5: Arbitrage Verification:**

The market quote is $P_{market} = \text{₹}45$, which is **significantly higher** than the theoretical $P_{theoretical} = \text{₹}4.718$.

Since $P_{market} > P_{theoretical}$, an **arbitrage opportunity exists**.

**Arbitrage Strategy (Sell High, Buy Low):**

| Action | Cash Flow Today (₹) |
|--------|---------------------|
| Sell overpriced put (write) | +45.000 |
| Buy call (at ₹15) | -15.000 |
| Short the stock (sell) | +500.000 |
| Invest PV of strike $K \cdot e^{-rT}$ into bond (borrow) | -489.718 |
| **Net Initial Cash Flow** | **+40.282** |

The arbitrageur pockets ₹40.282 risk-free per contract today, and the position converges to zero payoff at expiry, locking in the arbitrage profit. **(7 marks: 1 for formula, 2 for substitution, 1 for theoretical put, 1 for arbitrage identification, 2 for arbitrage strategy.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing Payoff vs Profit:** Many students forget to subtract the premium when computing profit. Payoff = $\max(S_T - K, 0)$, but **Profit = Payoff - Premium**. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Misapplying Put-Call Parity to American options:** The exact parity holds **only for European options**. For American options, it becomes an **inequality**: $S_0 - K \leq C_{Am} - P_{Am} \leq S_0 - K e^{-rT}$.
> 3. **Forgetting to use continuous compounding:** Put-Call Parity uses $e^{-rT}$, NOT $\frac{1}{(1+r)^T}$. Mixing up continuous vs discrete discounting is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Claiming American call > European call always:** The equality $C_{Am} = C_{Eu}$ holds **only when no dividends are paid**. With dividends, the American call is strictly more valuable.
> 5. **Neglecting the writer's (seller's) perspective:** KTU often asks for the **buyer AND writer** payoff diagrams. A common mistake is showing only the buyer's profile. A full answer requires both sides.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions:** When applying Black-Scholes or Put-Call Parity, **always list the assumptions** explicitly (no dividends, no transaction costs, no taxes, continuous trading). Examiners allocate 1 mark for assumptions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ **European Option** → Exercise **only at expiry T**.
- ☐ **American Option** → Exercise **anytime from 0 to T**.
- ☐ **Only difference** between European and American = **timing of exercise**.
- ☐ American option premium $\geq$ European option premium (more flexibility = more value).
- ☐ **Call Intrinsic Value** = $\max(S - K, 0)$.
- ☐ **Put Intrinsic Value** = $\max(K - S, 0)$.
- ☐ **Buyer's max loss** = premium paid. **Writers' max profit** = premium received.
- ☐ **Call buyer's max profit** = unlimited. **Put buyer's max profit** = $K - P$.
- ☐ **Call writer's max loss** = unlimited. **Put writer's max loss** = $K - P$.
- ☐ **Breakeven (Call)** = $K + C$. **Breakeven (Put)** = $K - P$.
- ☐ **Put-Call Parity (European)** = $C + K e^{-rT} = P + S_0$ — uses **continuous compounding**.
- ☐ **American call = European call** on a **non-dividend** stock.
- ☐ **American put $\geq$ European put** always.
- ☐ Put-Call Parity for American is an **inequality**, not an equation.
- ☐ **Valuation method:** European → Black-Scholes closed form. American → Binomial/Trinomial tree.
- ☐ **Moneyness:** ITM (exercise profitable) | ATM ($S = K$) | OTM (exercise worthless).
- ☐ **Payoff ≠ Profit.** Always subtract premium to get profit.
- ☐ **Time value** of money uses $e^{-rT}$ for European options, not $(1+r)^{-T}$.
- ☐ Most **index options** (NIFTY, SENSEX) are **European**. Most **stock options** are **American**.
- ☐ **Employee Stock Options (ESOs)** are typically American-style.
- ☐ Dividends can make American call early exercise **optimal** (just before ex-dividend date).
